> - **PSD (Welch's method)**: Standard for biomedical spectral estimation.
> - **LTI Convolution Theorem**: Convolution in time $\Leftrightarrow$ Multiplication in frequency.
> - **Common biomedical bandwidths**: ECG (0.05–150 Hz), EEG (0.5–100 Hz), EMG (20–500 Hz).
> - **Clinical diagnostic uses**: ECG R-peak detection (time), HRV LF/HF (freq), EEG band-power (freq), EMG fatigue via median freq (freq).
> - **Pitfall**: Always apply a **window** (Hann/Hamming) before FFT to reduce spectral leakage.
> - **Pitfall**: $f_N = f_s/2$ is the folding frequency, not $f_s$ itself.
> - **Pitfall**: Aliasing distorts ST-segments and Q-waves — clinically catastrophic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Acquisition Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signal Acquisition Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Biomedical Signal Acquisition System** is an engineered electronic chain designed to detect, transduce, condition, and digitize physiological bioelectric or biophysical events emanating from a living subject, such that the resulting signal is rendered suitable for display, storage, processing, and clinical interpretation.

The canonical acquisition chain comprises, in strict sequential order:
**Subject (Source) → Transducer/Electrode → Amplifier → Filter → Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) → Display/Processor.**

In KTU terminology aligned with the 2024 scheme, the system must satisfy four cardinal specifications simultaneously: **high input impedance ($\geq 10\ \text{M}\Omega$)**, **high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR $\geq 100\ \text{dB}$)**, **adequate gain (typically 100–10,000)**, and **patient safety (leakage current $< 10\ \mu\text{A}$ as per IEC 60601-1)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** The acquisition system bridges the *biological* domain (ions, membranes, potentials) and the *electronic* domain (voltages, currents, bits). Mastering this conversion is foundational to every subsequent module of PEICT633.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stethoscope of Electronics"

Imagine a doctor trying to listen to a patient's heartbeat inside a loud, crowded marketplace. The heartbeat is the **biomedical signal** — extremely faint (often in the **millivolt** range for ECG and **microvolt** range for EEG). The marketplace is the **electrical noise environment** (50 Hz powerline interference, motion artifacts, electromagnetic coupling).

The acquisition system is essentially an **"electronic stethoscope"** that:
1. **Picks up** the faint heartbeat (the **electrode** acts as the pickup microphone).
2. **Boosts** it above the surrounding chatter (the **amplifier** turns the whisper into a clear voice).
3. **Filters out** the unwanted crowd noise (the **bandpass filter** removes the rumble of conversation).
4. **Records** it in a language computers understand (the **ADC** translates voice into digital bits).
5. **Displays** the cleaned heartbeat waveform on a screen for the doctor.

Without any one of these stages, the doctor's diagnosis would be impossible — just as a stethoscope without a chest piece, or with blocked tubing, would be useless.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q = 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at **310 K** (body temperature).
- **Standard biopotential ranges**: ECG **0.5–5 mV**; EEG **5–300 µV**; EMG **50 µV–5 mV**; EOG **50–3500 µV**; ERG **0–900 µV**.
- **Standard bandwidths**: ECG **0.05–150 Hz**; EEG **0.1–100 Hz**; EMG **20–500 Hz**.
- **Electrode half-cell potential** for Ag/AgCl: **+0.223 V** (vs. Standard Hydrogen Electrode).
- **Standard isolation resistance** for patient protection: **$10\ \text{M}\Omega$** minimum.
- **CMRR minimum** for clinical-grade amplifiers: **100 dB** (i.e., a ratio of 100,000:1).

> [!NOTE]
> The acronym **"EMG = $50\ \mu\text{V}$–$5\ \text{mV}$"** can be remembered with the mnemonic: *"Eyes More Gain"* — EMG (eyes, since eye muscles are involved in EOG too) needs *more gain* than ECG because its amplitude is smaller.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Stages — Operational Breakdown

### Stage 1 — The Bioelectric Source (The Patient)
- Cells generate ionic currents governed by the **Nernst Equation**.
- The source can be modelled as a **Thevenin equivalent**: $V_s$ in series with source resistance $R_s$.
- Typical $R_s$ of tissue: **100–5000 $\Omega$**.

### Stage 2 — The Electrode (The Ionic-Electronic Transducer)
- Converts ionic current (in body) into electronic current (in wire).
- Establishes the **electrode-electrolyte interface** — a Helmholtz double layer modelled as a **half-cell potential $E_{hc}$**.
- For differential measurement, **two electrodes are used**, and the difference in their $E_{hc}$ values appears as a **DC offset** that the amplifier must reject.

### Stage 3 — The Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)
- A precision differential amplifier with three op-amp topology.
- Provides: **high differential gain $A_d$**, **unity common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 1$**, **very high input impedance**, and **adjustable gain via a single resistor $R_g$**.
- **Why preferred over simple difference amplifier**: The buffer inputs prevent loading of the high-impedance electrode source.

### Stage 4 — The Band-Pass Filter (Noise Sculptor)
- A **high-pass filter (HPF)** removes DC offsets and baseline drift.
- A **low-pass filter (LPF)** removes high-frequency noise and aliases the spectrum before sampling.
- ECG HPF cutoff: **0.05 Hz**; ECG LPF cutoff: **150 Hz**.

### Stage 5 — The ADC (The Digital Translator)
- Samples the analog waveform at $f_s$ (samples/second) — must satisfy **Nyquist criterion**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **Resolution** is set by the number of bits $n$: $\text{LSB} = V_{FS}/2^n$.
- A 12-bit ADC with $\pm 5\ \text{V}$ range: LSB $= 10\ \text{V} / 4096 = 2.44\ \text{mV}$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Why high input impedance?** Because the electrode-tissue source has a high Thevenin resistance. A low-impedance amplifier would form a voltage divider and attenuate the already-faint biopotential.
- **Why high CMRR?** Because **50 Hz powerline interference** appears as a common-mode signal (same on both inputs). CMRR rejects it; differential biopotential is amplified.
- **Why isolation?** Because the patient must be protected from any fault current originating from the mains-powered equipment. An **isolation amplifier** (using optical or transformer coupling) breaks the galvanic path.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| \# | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Nernst Equation (half-cell potential) | $E_{hc} = E^0 + (RT / zF) \cdot \ln(a_{ox}/a_{red})$ | $E^0$ standard potential, $R$ gas const, $T$ temp, $z$ valence, $F$ Faraday const | $E^0_{Ag/AgCl} = +0.223$ V |
| 2 | Thermal Voltage at body T | $V_T = kT/q$ | $k$ Boltzmann const, $T = 310$ K | $V_T \approx 25.85$ mV |
| 3 | Instrumentation Amplifier Gain | $A_d = (1 + 2R_1 / R_g) \cdot (R_3 / R_2)$ | $R_g$ gain-set resistor, others matched | $A_d = 1$ to $1000$ |
| 4 | CMRR (in dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$ | $A_d$ diff. gain, $A_{cm}$ common-mode gain | $\geq 100$ dB clinical |
| 5 | ADC Resolution (LSB) | $\text{LSB} = V_{FS} / 2^n$ | $V_{FS}$ full-scale voltage, $n$ bits | e.g. 2.44 mV @ 12-bit, $\pm 5$ V |
| 6 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ highest signal frequency | ECG: $f_s \geq 300$ Hz |
| 7 | Skin-Electrode Impedance (RC model) | $Z = R_s + 1/(j \omega C_d)$ | $R_s$ series resist., $C_d$ double-layer cap. | $R_s$ k$\Omega$, $C_d$ µF range |
| 8 | Input-Referred Noise | $v_{n,in} = v_{n,out} / A_d$ | $v_{n,out}$ output noise | $\mu$V range |
| 9 | Common-Mode Input Range | $V_{cm} \leq V_{sup}/2$ | supply rail restriction | 0 to $V_{cc}-1.5$V typical |
| 10 | Patient Leakage Current Limit | $I_{leak} \leq 10\ \mu\text{A}$ (Type CF) | IEC 60601-1 patient aux. current | $< 10$ µA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every modern patient monitor (Philips IntelliVue, GE CARESCAPE, Mindray ePM) implements the above chain. The **CMRR** spec is what allows you to record a clean ECG even inside an ICU full of ventilators, infusion pumps, and fluorescent lights — a fact KTU examiners love to test.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Half-Cell Potential from First Principles (Nernst)

**Goal:** Derive the equilibrium potential developed at a metal-electrolyte interface.

**Step 1 — Free Energy of Oxidation-Reduction Reaction**

For a general redox couple $Ox + ne^- \rightleftharpoons Red$, the change in Gibbs free energy is:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)
$$

where $a_{Ox}$ and $a_{Red}$ are the activities of oxidized and reduced species, $R$ is the universal gas constant ($8.314\ \text{J/mol K}$), and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**Step 2 — Relate Free Energy to Electrical Work**

The electrical work done in transferring $n$ moles of electrons across a potential difference $E$ is:

$$
\Delta G = -n F E
$$

where $F$ is the Faraday constant ($96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$). Equating the two expressions:

$$
-n F E = -n F E^0 + RT \ln\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for E**

Dividing through by $-nF$ and rearranging:

$$
E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)
$$

In base-10 logarithm form (more common in biomedical literature):

$$
E = E^0 + \frac{2.303\, RT}{nF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}}\right)
$$

For an Ag/AgCl electrode, at $T = 310$ K (body temperature) and $n = 1$:

$$
E_{Ag/AgCl} = 0.223 + \frac{0.0617}{1} \log_{10}(a_{Cl^-}) \quad [\text{Volts}]
$$

At physiological chloride concentration, the measured value stabilizes near **+0.223 V** vs. SHE.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Three Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Gain

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form differential gain of the classical instrumentation amplifier.

The three op-amp IA consists of two input buffer stages (op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$) followed by a unity-gain difference amplifier (op-amp $A_3$).

**Step 1 — Define Input Voltages**

Let $V_1$ and $V_2$ be the two input signals applied to the non-inverting terminals of $A_1$ and $A_2$ respectively. The gain-setting resistor $R_g$ is connected between the inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$. Let $R_1$ be the feedback resistor in each input stage.

**Step 2 — Current Through $R_g$**

Since no current flows into the op-amp inputs, the current through $R_g$ must equal the current flowing through each $R_1$ feedback resistor:

$$
I_{Rg} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}
$$

The output voltage of the first stage is the voltage at the output of $A_1$ (and similarly $A_2$):

$$
V_{O1} = V_1 + I_{Rg} \cdot R_1
$$

$$
V_{O2} = V_2 - I_{Rg} \cdot R_1
$$

**Step 3 — Differential Output of First Stage**

Subtracting:

$$
V_{O1} - V_{O2} = (V_1 - V_2) + 2 I_{Rg} R_1
$$

Substituting $I_{Rg}$:

$$
V_{O1} - V_{O2} = (V_1 - V_2) \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Unity Difference Amplifier (Second Stage)**

The difference amplifier with $R_3 = R_2$ gives:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2} (V_{O1} - V_{O2})
$$

**Step 5 — Total Differential Gain**

Combining both stages:

$$
V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)
$$

Thus the closed-form differential gain is:

$$
\boxed{A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** If $R_1 = 25\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, and $R_3/R_2 = 10$, then:

$$
A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 25\,000}{1000}\right) \times 10 = (1 + 50) \times 10 = 510
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CMRR in dB

**Step 1 — Define Common-Mode and Differential Gains**

The output of any differential amplifier can be written as:

$$
V_{out} = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_{cm} \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of CMRR**

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Decibels**

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right)
$$

**Numerical Example:** If $A_d = 1000$ and $A_{cm} = 0.1$:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10000) = 20 \times 4 = 80\ \text{dB}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ADC Quantization and Sampling

**Step 1 — LSB Voltage**

For an $n$-bit bipolar ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS}$:

$$
\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}
$$

**Step 2 — Sampling Frequency (Nyquist)**

To avoid aliasing of a signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

In practice, **oversampling** is used: $f_s \geq 5 f_{max}$ to allow for realizable anti-alias filter roll-off.

**Numerical Example:** For an EMG signal with $f_{max} = 500$ Hz, a sampling rate of $f_s = 2000$ Hz (4× oversampling) is preferred.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Biomedical Signal Acquisition Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BIOLOGICAL SOURCE - Patient Tissue - mV to uV range] --> B[TRANSDUCER - Surface or Needle Electrode - Ag/AgCl preferred]
    B --> C[INPUT PROTECTION - Patient Safety Stage - Current Limiting and Isolation]
    C --> D[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER - High CMRR Three Op-Amp Topology - Gain 100 to 10000]
    D --> E[ANALOG BANDPASS FILTER - High Pass 0.05 Hz to 0.5 Hz - Low Pass 150 Hz to 500 Hz]
    E --> F[NOTCH FILTER - 50 Hz Powerline Rejection - Q factor above 30]
    F --> G[ADC STAGE - 12 bit to 16 bit Resolution - Sampling 250 Hz to 2000 Hz]
    G --> H[DISPLAY OR DSP - Microcontroller or PC - Storage and Analysis]
    H --> I[CLINICAL OUTPUT - Monitor Trace - Diagnostic Report]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f0e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1f5
    style G fill:#ffffe1
    style H fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#d0d0d0
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Electrode-Electrolyte Interface Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Tissue Half Cell - E half cell potential] --> A2[Electrolyte Resistance - R s in series]
    A2 --> A3[Double Layer Capacitance - C d in parallel]
    A3 --> A4[Faradaic Resistance - R ct charge transfer]
    A4 --> A5[Metal Lead Wire Output - V to instrumentation amplifier]
    B1[DC Offset Source] --> A5
    C1[AC Biopotential Source] --> A5
    style A1 fill:#fff0e1
    style A2 fill:#ffe1f0
    style A3 fill:#e1f0ff
    style A4 fill:#f0ffe1
    style A5 fill:#e1ffe1
    style B1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style C1 fill:#e1e1ff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Instrumentation Amplifier Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN1[Input 1 V1] --> BUF1[Buffer Op Amp A1]
    IN2[Input 2 V2] --> BUF2[Buffer Op Amp A2]
    BUF1 --> R1A[Feedback R1]
    BUF2 --> R1B[Feedback R1]
    R1A --> RG[Gain Set Resistor Rg]
    R1B --> RG
    BUF1 --> DIFF[Difference Amplifier A3]
    BUF2 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> OUT[Single Ended Output Vout]
    style IN1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style IN2 fill:#e1f5ff
    style BUF1 fill:#fff4e1
    style BUF2 fill:#fff4e1
    style R1A fill:#ffe1e1
    style R1B fill:#ffe1e1
    style RG fill:#ffe1f5
    style DIFF fill:#e1ffe1
    style OUT fill:#d0d0d0
```

## 4.4 Signal Conditioning Strategy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Raw Biopotential - uV to mV Amplitude - DC Offset Present] --> S2[Step 1 Instrumentation Amplifier - Set gain to bring signal to V range]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2 High Pass Filter - Remove DC electrode offset]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3 Low Pass Filter - Anti-aliasing preparation]
    S4 --> S5[Step 4 Notch Filter - 50 Hz line interference removal]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5 Level Shifter - Match ADC input range]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6 ADC Sampling and Quantization - Digital output stream]
    style S1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style S2 fill:#e1ffe1
    style S3 fill:#fff4e1
    style S4 fill:#f0e1ff
    style S5 fill:#ffe1f5
    style S6 fill:#ffe1e1
    style S7 fill:#d0d0d0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of a biomedical signal acquisition system", always include the **patient safety block (isolation)** and the **CMRR specification on the IA block** — both are favorite evaluation points.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. Define the term 'Common Mode Rejection Ratio' (CMRR) for a differential amplifier. Why is it important in biomedical signal acquisition? [3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential-mode gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_{cm}$ of a differential amplifier, expressed in decibels as:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right)
$$

It is critically important in biomedical signal acquisition because environmental noise — most notably **50 Hz powerline interference** — appears as a **common-mode signal** (present equally and in phase on both input electrodes). A high CMRR (≥ 100 dB for clinical equipment) ensures this interference is rejected while the differential biopotential (ECG, EEG, EMG) is amplified. Without high CMRR, the patient signal would be swamped by 50 Hz hum, making diagnosis impossible.

---

### **Q2. List any three types of biopotential electrodes and state the typical half-cell potential of an Ag/AgCl electrode. [3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Three types of biopotential electrodes:

1. **Surface (Skin) Electrodes** — Ag/AgCl pre-gelled disposable type; used in resting ECG, EEG, EMG.
2. **Needle Electrodes** — fine concentric or monopolar needles inserted into muscle; used in clinical EMG and intra-operative monitoring.
3. **Microelectrodes** — glass micropipettes with tip diameter < 1 µm; used for intracellular recording of single neuron action potentials.

**Half-cell potential of Ag/AgCl electrode: $E_{hc} = +0.223$ V** vs. Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) at body temperature 310 K.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional stages of a biomedical signal acquisition system. Discuss the role of the instrumentation amplifier in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the differential gain expression for a three op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Calculate the gain when $R_1 = 47\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, and $R_3/R_2 = 5$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) [7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1):**

The biomedical signal acquisition system consists of the following functional stages connected in series:

1. **Bioelectric Source (Patient):** Generates ionic potential differences in the range of µV to mV. Modelled as a Thevenin source with $V_s$ and source resistance $R_s$ (100 Ω to 5 kΩ).

2. **Electrode (Transducer):** Converts ionic conduction (in tissue) into electronic conduction (in wire). Establishes the half-cell potential at the metal-electrolyte interface.

3. **Input Protection and Isolation Stage:** Provides patient safety by limiting leakage current to < 10 µA (per IEC 60601-1). Uses optocouplers or transformer isolation to break the galvanic path between the patient and mains-powered circuitry.

4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA):** The most critical stage. Provides high differential gain, very high input impedance, and excellent CMRR (≥ 100 dB). The three op-amp IA is the industry standard.

5. **Band-Pass Filter:** A high-pass filter removes DC electrode offset (cutoff 0.05 Hz for ECG) and a low-pass filter limits bandwidth (cutoff 150 Hz for ECG), preparing the signal for sampling.

6. **Notch Filter:** A 50 Hz twin-T or active notch filter rejects powerline interference.

7. **ADC and Display:** Converts the analog conditioned signal into digital samples for storage, processing, and display on a monitor.

**Role of the Instrumentation Amplifier (Detail):**
The IA must amplify a very small differential biopotential (e.g., 1 mV ECG) sitting on a much larger common-mode offset (e.g., 1.5 V from electrode half-cell potentials). A simple difference amplifier cannot do this because:
- It has **low input impedance** which would load the high-impedance electrode-tissue source.
- It has **unadjustable gain** requiring two precisely matched resistors.
- It provides **insufficient CMRR** for clinical use.

The three op-amp IA overcomes all these limitations: the two input buffers present extremely high input impedance (≥ 10 GΩ), and the single gain-setting resistor $R_g$ allows easy gain adjustment.

**Marks Distribution:** Block diagram — 2 marks; 7 functional stages — 3 marks; Role of IA — 2 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) [7 Marks]**

**Derivation (as per Section 3.2):**

The current flowing through the gain-setting resistor $R_g$ is:

$$
I_{Rg} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}
$$

Since no current flows into the op-amp inverting inputs, the same current flows through the two feedback resistors $R_1$. The output voltages of the two input buffer stages are:

$$
V_{O1} = V_1 + I_{Rg} \cdot R_1
$$

$$
V_{O2} = V_2 - I_{Rg} \cdot R_1
$$

Their difference is:

$$
V_{O1} - V_{O2} = (V_1 - V_2) \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right)
$$

The unity-gain difference amplifier (with $R_3/R_2$) produces the final output:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2} \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right) (V_1 - V_2)
$$

Therefore the differential gain is:

$$
\boxed{A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Substituting $R_1 = 47\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_3/R_2 = 5$:

$$
A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 47}{1}\right) \times 5 = (1 + 94) \times 5 = 95 \times 5 = 475
$$

**Marks Distribution:** First stage output equation — 2 marks; Difference derivation — 2 marks; Final gain formula — 1 mark; Numerical answer — 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice*
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 — Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the electrode-electrolyte interface with a suitable equivalent circuit. Discuss the significance of the half-cell potential. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC is used in an ECG acquisition system with a full-scale input range of ±5 V. Calculate the resolution in mV. If the maximum ECG frequency component is 150 Hz, determine the minimum sampling rate. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) [7 Marks]**

**Equivalent Circuit (refer to Section 4.2):**

When a metal electrode is placed in contact with an electrolyte (e.g., body fluid or electrode gel), a **charge separation** occurs at the interface due to the transfer of metal ions into the solution and the adsorption of ions onto the metal surface. This charge separation forms a **Helmholtz double layer**, which behaves electrically as a capacitor $C_d$ in parallel with a charge-transfer resistance $R_{ct}$ (Faradaic path). The bulk electrolyte has its own resistance $R_s$ in series.

The complete equivalent circuit from the metal to the tissue is:

$$
Z_{electrode} = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + j \omega R_{ct} C_d}
$$

**Significance of the Half-Cell Potential:**

The half-cell potential $E_{hc}$ is the DC potential developed at a single metal-electrolyte interface. It is a direct consequence of the Nernst equation:

$$
E_{hc} = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}}\right)
$$

For Ag/AgCl: $E_{hc} = +0.223$ V vs. SHE. The significance is twofold:
1. **DC offset generator** — When two electrodes are used, the difference of their half-cell potentials (often several mV) appears as a DC offset that must be removed by AC coupling or HPF.
2. **Source of motion artifacts** — Any change in the electrolyte concentration or skin-electrode contact (e.g., patient movement) alters $E_{hc}$ and produces baseline wander.

**Marks Distribution:** Equivalent circuit diagram — 2 marks; Component explanation ($C_d$, $R_{ct}$, $R_s$) — 2 marks; Half-cell potential equation + significance — 3 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) [7 Marks]**

**Part 1 — ADC Resolution:**

Given: $n = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = \pm 5\ \text{V}$ (total span = 10 V).

$$
\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10\ \text{V}}{4096} = 2.4414 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Resolution} = 2.44\ \text{mV}}
$$

**Part 2 — Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist):**

Given: $f_{max} = 150$ Hz (ECG upper cutoff).

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 150 = 300\ \text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{s,min} = 300\ \text{Hz}}
$$

In practice, a sampling rate of **360 Hz** or **500 Hz** is used to provide a margin for the anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

**Marks Distribution:** LSB formula — 1 mark; Calculation — 2 marks; Nyquist formula — 1 mark; Calculation — 2 marks; Practical comment — 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pm$ in full-scale voltage** — Many students write LSB = $5\text{V}/4096$, losing 1 mark. The correct form is $\text{LSB} = 10\text{V}/4096 = 2.44$ mV.
> 2. **Confusing $V_T$ at 27°C vs. 37°C** — Always use **25.85 mV at 310 K (body temperature)**, not 25.7 mV at 300 K. Examiners catch this.
> 3. **Inverting the CMRR formula** — A common error is $20 \log_{10}(A_{cm}/A_d)$ instead of $20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$ — this gives a *negative* dB value which is meaningless.
> 4. **Missing the practical safety stage** — A block diagram without the isolation/patient safety block is considered incomplete in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 5. **Forgetting to write units** in the final numerical answers (mV, Hz, dB) — easy 0.5 to 1 mark lost per question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Acquisition chain (memorize in order):** Source → Electrode → Protection/Isolation → Instrumentation Amplifier → Band-Pass Filter → Notch Filter → ADC → Display.
- **Instrumentation Amplifier Gain Formula:** $A_d = (1 + 2R_1/R_g)(R_3/R_2)$ — this is the *single most tested formula* in Module 1.
- **CMRR Definition:** $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_d \vert / \vert A_{cm} \vert)$ — clinical spec ≥ 100 dB.
- **Half-Cell Potential of Ag/AgCl:** $E_{hc} = +0.223$ V vs. SHE at 310 K — non-polarizable, hence preferred for biopotential recording.
- **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^0 + (2.303 RT / nF) \log_{10}(a_{Ox}/a_{Red})$ — fundamental for any electrode-related derivation.
- **Thermal Voltage at body temperature:** $V_T = kT/q = 25.85$ mV at 310 K.
- **ADC Resolution:** $\text{LSB} = V_{FS}/2^n$ — always use the *total* voltage span (e.g., 10 V for ±5 V range).
- **Nyquist Criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — for ECG with $f_{max} = 150$ Hz, $f_s \geq 300$ Hz.
- **Standard Biopotential Ranges:** ECG 0.5–5 mV, EEG 5–300 µV, EMG 50 µV–5 mV.
- **Patient Safety (IEC 60601-1):** Patient auxiliary current < 10 µA; isolation resistance > 10 MΩ.
- **Why IA over simple difference amp:** Higher input impedance, single-resistor gain control, far superior CMRR.
- **Three types of electrodes:** Surface, Needle, Microelectrode — each with characteristic tip size, application, and impedance.
- **Skin-electrode impedance model:** $R_s$ in series with parallel combination of $C_d$ and $R_{ct}$ — frequency dependent.
- **Ag/AgCl is preferred** because it is non-polarizable (low offset drift, low noise, stable half-cell potential).
- **50 Hz notch filter** is mandatory in Indian/Kerala hospital environments due to the 230 V, 50 Hz mains supply.
- **Input-referred noise** of a clinical ECG amplifier should be < 5 µV RMS for clean recording.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acquisition of Biomedical Signals (e.g., ECG)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Acquisition of Biomedical Signals (e.g., ECG) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition: Biomedical Signal A **Biomedical Signal** is a time-varying or spatially varying quantity that originates from a living biological system and carries information about the structure, function, or pathology of that system. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, a biomedical signal is a measurable electrical, mechanical, chemical, or acoustic response of a biological tissue or organ that can be transduced into a quantifiable electrical signal for clinical analysis, monitoring, and diagnostic purposes. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** > *"A biomedical signal is a time-varying signal that represents a physiological variable of a biological system and is suitable for measurement, analysis, and diagnostic interpretation after appropriate transduction and signal conditioning."* ## 1.2 Classification of Biomedical Signals Biomedical signals are broadly classified into **two super-categories** based on their physical origin: | Origin Type | Examples | Nature | |-------------|----------|--------| | **Bioelectric** | ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, ERG | Electrical potential generated by cellular activity | | **Non-Bioelectric** | PCG (heart sound), Blood Pressure, Body Temperature, Respiration Rate | Mechanically, acoustically, or chemically transduced into electrical form | ### The 6 Sub-Classes (KTU-Mandated Classification) 1. **Bioelectric Signals** – Generated by depolarization of excitable cells (ECG, EEG, EMG) 2. **Bioacoustic Signals** – Vibration/sound produced by body (PCG, Korotkoff sounds) 3. **Biomechanical Signals** – Displacement, force, pressure (Blood pressure, Spirometry) 4. **Biochemical Signals** – Concentration of ions/gases (pH, pO₂, pCO₂) 5. **Bioimpedance Signals** – Impedance variation (Plethysmography, Impedance cardiography) 6. **Biomagnetic Signals** – Magnetic fields from bioelectric activity (MCG, MEG) ## 1.3 The Electrocardiogram (ECG) — Primary Case Study > [!IMPORTANT] > **ECG Definition (KTU Examiner's Language):** > The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is the recording of the electrical activity of the heart over a period of time, measured via electrodes placed on the skin surface. It is a non-invasive representation of the bioelectric potential generated by the depolarization and repolarization of cardiac muscle. ### Conceptual Analogy: ECG as a "Movie of the Heart's Electrical Show" > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor Analogy:** > Imagine your heart is a large orchestra with millions of cells. Just as a conductor's baton movement produces sound, the coordinated **depolarization wave** in the heart's pacemaker (SA node) creates an electrical "wave" that sweeps through the atria and ventricles. The ECG is essentially a **graphic score sheet** of this electrical wave, recorded from the skin's surface using electrodes. When the atria contract → we see the **P wave**; when the ventricles depolarize → the sharp **QRS complex**; when they recover → the **T wave**. A doctor reading an ECG is like a music critic reading a score — deviations from the normal pattern tell a story of disease. ## 1.4 Standard ECG Waveform Components A single normal cardiac cycle of the ECG contains the following waves, complexes, and intervals: | Component | Physiological Meaning | Duration | Amplitude (Lead II) | |-----------|----------------------|----------|---------------------| | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | **80–100 ms** | **0.25 mV** | | **PR Segment** | AV nodal conduction delay | ~50 ms | Isoelectric | | **PR Interval** | Atrial depolarization + AV delay | **120–200 ms** | — | | **QRS Complex** | Ventricular depolarization | **80–120 ms** | **1.0–1.5 mV** | | **ST Segment** | Ventricular repolarization plateau | ~120 ms | Isoelectric | | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | **160 ms** | **0.1–0.5 mV** | | **U wave** | Purkinje repolarization (sometimes) | ~160 ms | Low amplitude | | **QT Interval** | Total ventricular activity | **350–440 ms** | — | | **RR Interval** | One full cardiac cycle | **0.6–1.2 s** | — | ### Standard ECG Signal Characteristics (Board Exam Must-Know) > [!IMPORTANT] > **ECG Key Metrics for Acquisition Design:** > - **Frequency Range:** **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (clinical); up to **500 Hz** for high-resolution > - **Amplitude Range:** **0.5 mV to 4 mV** (typical QRS peak) > - **Standard Calibration:** **10 mm/mV vertical**, **25 mm/s horizontal** > - **Sampling Rate (Clinical):** **250–500 Hz** (minimum 200 Hz per AHA) > - **ADC Resolution:** **≥ 10 bits** (preferably 12 bits) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The ECG Acquisition System — Block Architecture A complete ECG acquisition chain consists of **6 cascaded functional blocks**. Each block performs a non-negotiable transformation required to convert the bioelectric event at the heart into a digital, noise-free, clinically interpretable signal. ### Block 1: Electrodes (Transduction Interface) The electrodes form the **physical interface** between the ionic current inside the body and the electronic current in the measuring instrument. The **electrode-skin interface** is modelled as a half-cell potential (DC offset) in series with a parallel RC network (polarization impedance). > [!NOTE] > **Half-Cell Potential (HCP):** When a metal electrode contacts an electrolyte (skin/electrolyte gel), an equilibrium potential difference develops at the interface, typically **± 200 mV to ± 400 mV** for Ag/AgCl electrodes. This DC offset MUST be removed by the next stages. **Common Electrode Types Used for ECG:** - **Ag/AgCl (Silver/Silver-Chloride) electrodes** — Most popular, low HCP (~220 mV), stable - **Gold-plated electrodes** — Used for long-term monitoring - **Carbon-rubber electrodes** — Disposable, used in stress tests - **Suction electrodes** — Emergency/short-term use - **Needle electrodes** — Invasive, for intracardiac ECG ### Block 2: Isolation Amplifier (Patient Safety & Ground Isolation) A mandatory safety block that provides **galvanic isolation** between the patient and the rest of the circuit. It uses either **transformer, optical, or capacitive coupling** to break any direct conductive path, thereby preventing microshock (< 10 mA) and macroshock (> 100 mA). The **CMRR** (Common Mode Rejection Ratio) here is critical, typically **> 100 dB** at 50/60 Hz. ### Block 3: Preamplifier / Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) The IA is the **heart of the analog front-end**. It must have: - **Very high input impedance** (≥ 10 MΩ at 10 Hz) to prevent signal loading - **High CMRR** (≥ 80 dB) to reject common-mode noise (50 Hz mains hum) - **Low input bias current** (≤ 10 nA) - **Programmable gain** (typically 100 to 1000) The standard 3-op-amp topology (e.g., AD620, INA128) is the de-facto KTU answer. ### Block 4: Signal Conditioning Filters Three filtering stages are mandatory: 1. **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** — fc = **0.05 Hz** to remove DC electrode offset and baseline wander due to respiration 2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — fc = **150 Hz** to limit bandwidth before ADC and reject high-frequency noise (muscle artifact) 3. **Notch Filter (Band-Stop)** — fc = **50 Hz** (India/Asia) or **60 Hz** (US) to suppress mains line interference (Q = 30 typical) ### Block 5: Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) The ADC performs **two operations**: sampling and quantization. A **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** is the most common choice for ECG due to its excellent trade-off between speed, resolution, and power. - **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** holds the analog voltage during conversion - **Quantizer** maps the continuous amplitude into discrete levels ### Block 6: Digital Signal Processing (DSP) Post-digitization, digital filters, baseline correction, beat detection (Pan-Tompkins algorithm), and feature extraction are applied. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Numerical/Unit | Engineering Use | |---|-------------------|---------------|----------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_s$ in Hz | Anti-aliasing constraint | | 2 | **ECG Bandwidth** | $B = f_{max} - f_{min}$ | $150 - 0.05 \approx 150$ Hz | Filter design | | 3 | **Quantization Step (Resolution)** | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Volts per LSB | ADC selection | | 4 | **Quantization SNR (SQNR)** | $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$ | dB, $N$ = bits | ADC resolution planning | | 5 | **Dynamic Range of ADC** | $DR = 20 \log_{10}(2^N)$ | dB | Bit-depth sizing | | 6 | **Total Bits for ECG** | $N \geq \log_2\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{\Delta V}\right)$ | bits | Resolution requirement | | 7 | **CMRR Definition** | $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | dB | Amplifier spec | | 8 | **Half-Cell Potential (Ag/AgCl)** | $E_{HCP} \approx 0.223$ V | Volts | Electrode offset | | 9 | **Heart Rate from RR** | $HR = \dfrac{60}{RR_{seconds}}$ | BPM | Beat-to-beat interval | | 10 | **High-Pass Cutoff (Time Const)** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | Baseline wander filter | | 11 | **Low-Pass Cutoff** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | Anti-aliasing filter | | 12 | **Aliasing Frequency** | $f_{alias} = \vert f_s - f_{in} \vert$ | Hz | Aliasing detection | | 13 | **Notch Quality Factor** | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW}$ | dimensionless | Notch sharpness | | 14 | **Gain of 3-OpAmp IA** | $A = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$ | V/V | Instrumentation amp gain | | 15 | **Storage (Nyquist Memory)** | $Memory = 2 \cdot f_{max} \cdot N \cdot duration$ | bits | Storage planning | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Tip:** For ECG, the typical answer when asked "minimum sampling rate" is **$f_s = 500$ Hz** (twice the clinically recommended upper limit of 150 Hz × 2, then add margin). Use **12-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 5$ V → $\Delta = 1.22$ mV, comfortably resolving 0.5 mV ECG amplitude. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Holter Monitors** (24-h ambulatory ECG): Use **f_s = 128–256 Hz**, **12-bit ADC**, compressed storage - **Patient Monitors (ICU)**: Use **f_s = 500 Hz**, **16-bit ADC**, real-time arrhythmia detection - **Defibrillators / AEDs**: Use **f_s ≥ 1000 Hz** for accurate shock synchronization - **Wearable ECG patches (e.g., Zio)**: Use **f_s = 200 Hz**, **10-bit ADC**, Bluetooth transmission - **Implantable Loop Recorders (ILR)**: **f_s = 200 Hz**, ultra-low power ASIC # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Sampling Rate for ECG ### Given Data - Maximum frequency content of ECG signal: $f_{max} = 150$ Hz (clinical bandwidth) - We want to prevent aliasing in the acquired signal ### Step 1 — State the Nyquist Theorem By the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem, to reconstruct a bandlimited signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least **twice the highest frequency component**. $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the ECG Bandwidth Value $$f_s \geq 2 \times 150 \, \text{Hz} = 300 \, \text{Hz}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Engineering Margin In practice, a 20%–50% safety margin is added to accommodate non-ideal anti-aliasing filters and unforeseen high-frequency content. $$f_s^{practical} = 1.5 \times f_s^{Nyquist} = 1.5 \times 300 = 450 \, \text{Hz}$$ ### Step 4 — Standard Engineering Selection Choose the next standard value: **$f_s = 500 \, \text{Hz}$** > [!NOTE] > **Result:** Minimum sampling rate = **300 Hz**; Recommended clinical rate = **500 Hz**; AHA clinical guideline = **≥ 250 Hz**; Telemetry/Research = **1000–2000 Hz**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: ADC Resolution (Number of Bits) for ECG ### Given Data - ECG signal amplitude: $V_{min} = 0.5$ mV (smallest meaningful feature) - ADC reference voltage: $V_{ref} = 5$ V - We require the quantization step $\Delta$ to be at most $V_{min}/2$ for adequate fidelity. ### Step 1 — Quantization Step Formula $$\Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$$ ### Step 2 — Set the Resolution Constraint For the quantization noise to be at least 2× smaller than the smallest feature: $$\Delta \leq \frac{V_{min}}{2} = \frac{0.5 \, \text{mV}}{2} = 0.25 \, \text{mV}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the Inequality $$\frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} \leq 0.25 \, \text{mV}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $N$ (Number of Bits) $$2^N \geq \frac{V_{ref}}{\Delta} = \frac{5 \, \text{V}}{0.25 \, \text{mV}} = \frac{5}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 20{,}000$$ ### Step 5 — Take $\log_2$ of Both Sides $$N \geq \log_2(20{,}000) = \frac{\log_{10}(20{,}000)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{4.301}{0.3010} \approx 14.29$$ ### Step 6 — Round Up to Nearest Integer $$N \geq 15 \, \text{bits}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical Result:** The minimum theoretical requirement is **15 bits**, but commercial ECGs use **12 bits** (with oversampling) or **16 bits** (premium). Standard KTU board answer: **"12-bit ADC with $f_s = 500$ Hz suffices for clinical ECG."** ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Storage Requirement for 24-Hour Holter Recording ### Given Data - Sampling rate: $f_s = 250$ Hz - Resolution: $N = 12$ bits per sample - Channels: 2 (Holter typically 2-lead) - Duration: 24 hours ### Step 1 — Number of Samples per Second (per channel) $$S_{per\,sec} = f_s = 250 \, \text{samples/sec}$$ ### Step 2 — Bits per Second (per channel) $$B_{per\,sec} = 250 \times 12 = 3000 \, \text{bits/sec}$$ ### Step 3 — Total Duration in Seconds $$T = 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86{,}400 \, \text{seconds}$$ ### Step 4 — Total Bits (Uncompressed, 2-Channel) $$B_{total} = 2 \times 3000 \times 86{,}400 = 518{,}400{,}000 \, \text{bits}$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to Megabytes $$B_{total} = \frac{518.4 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^6} \approx 64.8 \, \text{MB}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** A 24-hour, 2-channel Holter recording at 250 Hz / 12-bit resolution requires **~64.8 MB** of storage. Real systems use compression (LZW on R-R intervals) to bring this down to **5–10 MB**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sizing the Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter ### Given Data - $f_s = 500$ Hz - $f_{max} = 150$ Hz (ECG bandwidth) ### Step 1 — Compute Nyquist Frequency $$f_{Nyq} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \, \text{Hz}$$ ### Step 2 — Select Filter Order Choose a **4th-order Butterworth** filter for maximally flat passband (no ripple, preserves QRS morphology). ### Step 3 — Set Cutoff Frequency The cutoff must be slightly **above** the ECG bandwidth but well **below** $f_{Nyq}$: $$f_c = 150 \, \text{Hz} \, (\text{or } 0.6 \times f_{Nyq} = 150 \, \text{Hz})$$ ### Step 4 — Attenuation at Nyquist For a 4th-order Butterworth, attenuation at $f_{Nyq} = 250$ Hz is: $$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left[1 + \left(\frac{250}{150}\right)^8\right] = 10 \log_{10}[1 + 8.55] \approx 10.6 \, \text{dB}$$ > [!WARNING] > **10.6 dB is INSUFFICIENT** to prevent aliasing. In practice, **8th-order filters** are used, giving ≈ 42 dB attenuation at Nyquist. This is why higher-order anti-aliasing filters are mandatory. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Acquisition Pitfalls | Pitfall | Cause | Effect | Standard Fix | |---------|-------|--------|--------------| | **Baseline Wander** | Respiration, electrode motion | Slow DC drift | HPF with fc = 0.05 Hz | | **Powerline Interference** | 50/60 Hz mains coupling | 50 Hz buzz | Notch filter (Q=30) + driven right leg (DRL) | | **Muscle Artifact (EMG)** | Patient movement | High-freq noise | LPF fc = 35–40 Hz (diagnostic), 150 Hz (research) | | **Aliasing** | Insufficient $f_s$ | Distorted waveform | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ + sharp anti-alias filter | | **Quantization Noise** | Low ADC bits | Stepped waveform | ≥ 12 bits, oversampling | | **Motion Artifact** | Electrode-skin slip | Sharp transients | Adhesive electrodes, skin prep | | **DC Offset** | Half-cell potential | Saturation of amplifier | AC coupling, HPF | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ECG Acquisition — Full Block-Level Architecture (Mermaid) The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **complete signal flow** from the heart's electrical activity to the digital processor, mapping every functional block and the noise/artifact points where each filter must intervene. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["HEART
Bioelectric Source"] --> A2["ELECTRODES
Ag-AgCl x 4 or 10"] A2 --> A3["ELECTRODE-SKIN
INTERFACE
HCP = 223 mV"] A3 --> A4["ISOLATION AMP
Patient Safety
CMRR 100 dB"] A4 --> A5["INSTRUMENTATION AMP
Gain 100 to 1000
3-Opamp Topology"] A5 --> A6["HIGH PASS FILTER
fc = 0.05 Hz
Removes DC Offset"] A6 --> A7["LOW PASS FILTER
fc = 150 Hz
Limits Bandwidth"] A7 --> A8["NOTCH FILTER
fc = 50 Hz
Removes Mains Hum"] A8 --> A9["SAMPLE AND HOLD
S-H Circuit"] A9 --> A10["ADC
12 to 16 bit
SAR Topology"] A10 --> A11["DIGITAL PROCESSOR
DSP Microcontroller
Beat Detection"] A11 --> A12["DISPLAY
Monitor LCD"] A11 --> A13["STORAGE
SD Card Flash"] N1["NOISE SOURCES"] -.->|50 Hz| A8 N1 -.->|DC Drift| A6 N1 -.->|EMG Noise| A7 N1 -.->|Aliasing Risk| A9 N2["DRIVEN RIGHT LEG
DRL Circuit"] -.->|Common Mode Feedback| A4 ``` ## 4.2 ECG Waveform Anatomy — Mermaid Timing Diagram The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **temporal sequence of ECG waves** with their physiological origins, duration ranges, and amplitude bands. This is the **single most-tested concept** in KTU Module 1. ```mermaid flowchart LR T0["t = 0 ms
SA Node Fires"] --> T1["P WAVE
Atrial Depolarization
Duration 80 to 100 ms
Amplitude 0.25 mV"] T1 --> T2["PR SEGMENT
AV Nodal Delay
Duration 50 ms
Isoelectric"] T2 --> T3["QRS COMPLEX
Ventricular Depolarization
Duration 80 to 120 ms
Amplitude 1.0 to 1.5 mV"] T3 --> T4["ST SEGMENT
Repolarization Plateau
Duration 120 ms
Isoelectric"] T4 --> T5["T WAVE
Ventricular Repolarization
Duration 160 ms
Amplitude 0.1 to 0.5 mV"] T5 --> T6["U WAVE
Optional Purkinje Repolarization
Duration 160 ms
Amplitude less than 0.1 mV"] T6 --> T7["RR INTERVAL
One Cardiac Cycle
Duration 0.6 to 1.2 s
HR = 60/RR"] ``` ## 4.3 Lead Configuration — Einthoven's Triangle (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB RARM["RA
Right Arm
Red Electrode"] --> LA["LA
Left Arm
Yellow Electrode"] LA --> LL["LL
Left Leg
Green Electrode"] LL --> RARM L1["LEAD I = LA - RA"] -.-> LA L1 -.-> RARM L2["LEAD II = LL - RA"] -.-> LL L2 -.-> RARM L3["LEAD III = LL - LA"] -.-> LL L3 -.-> LA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Einthoven's Law (Mandatory Equation):** > $$II = I + III$$ > where the Roman numerals denote the lead voltages. This is a board-exam guaranteed question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: List and briefly explain any three types of biomedical signals with examples.** **Model Answer:** Biomedical signals are measurable physiological variables derived from living systems. Three major types are: 1. **Bioelectric Signals:** These are electrical potentials generated by the depolarization of excitable cells such as cardiac myocytes or neurons. The amplitude is in the millivolt range and frequency is sub-Hz to a few kHz. **Examples:** ECG, EEG, EMG. 2. **Bioacoustic Signals:** These are pressure waves (vibrations) generated by the body's mechanical activity, such as valve closure in the heart. The transducer (microphone/piezo) converts acoustic energy to electrical form. **Examples:** Phonocardiogram (PCG), Korotkoff sounds during BP measurement. 3. **Biomechanical Signals:** These arise from physical motion, force, displacement, or pressure within the body. The transducer converts the mechanical quantity into voltage. **Examples:** Blood pressure (arterial line), Spirometry (lung volume), Goniometer (joint angle). > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (Examiner's Pattern):** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for any 2 types explained + 1 mark for examples. Total 3. --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define ECG and state its clinically important frequency and amplitude ranges.** **Model Answer:** The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a non-invasive recording of the electrical activity of the heart, captured via surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest. **Clinical Ranges:** - **Frequency Bandwidth:** **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (clinical). For high-resolution research: up to 500 Hz. - **Signal Amplitude:** **0.5 mV to 4 mV** (QRS peak), with P and T waves in the 0.1–0.5 mV range. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** Always quote the **bandwidth** and **amplitude** as a range, not a single value, and explicitly state the **standard calibration (10 mm/mV and 25 mm/s)** to earn the third mark. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student must answer either **Question A or Question B** (Module-Internal Choice). Both questions below are independent and full-mark. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]` **Q: With a neat block diagram, describe the complete ECG signal acquisition system. Explain the role of each block and justify the choice of sampling rate and ADC resolution.** #### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Function of Each Stage (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** The ECG acquisition system consists of **six cascaded blocks**: 1. **Electrodes (4-10 Ag/AgCl electrodes):** Transduce ionic current in the body to electronic current. They form the physical interface and contribute a half-cell potential of ~223 mV. 2. **Isolation Amplifier:** Provides galvanic isolation (using transformer/optical coupling) to protect the patient from microshock and macroshock. Common-mode rejection is critical here. 3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (3-OpAmp, e.g., AD620):** Provides high gain (100–1000), very high input impedance (≥ 10 MΩ), and high CMRR (≥ 80 dB). This is the **first signal-amplification stage**. 4. **Signal Conditioning Filters:** - **High-Pass Filter (fc = 0.05 Hz)** → removes DC offset and baseline wander - **Low-Pass Filter (fc = 150 Hz)** → limits bandwidth - **Notch Filter (fc = 50 Hz, Q = 30)** → rejects mains interference 5. **ADC (12-bit SAR, 500 Hz sampling):** Converts the analog ECG to digital samples. A Sample-and-Hold circuit precedes the ADC. 6. **Digital Signal Processor:** Performs digital filtering, R-peak detection (Pan-Tompkins), and feature extraction for diagnosis. **Block Diagram Reference:** See SECTION 4.1 above. #### Part (b) — Sampling Rate & ADC Resolution Justification (7 Marks) **Sampling Rate Justification:** By the Nyquist theorem, for $f_{max} = 150$ Hz: $$f_s \geq 2 \times 150 = 300 \, \text{Hz}$$ With a 50% engineering margin, **$f_s = 500$ Hz** is chosen. This is the standard in clinical ECG (AHA recommends ≥ 250 Hz; 500 Hz is comfortable for QRS morphology preservation). **ADC Resolution Justification:** ECG minimum feature size = 0.5 mV. For quantization step $\Delta \leq 0.25$ mV with $V_{ref} = 5$ V: $$N \geq \log_2\left(\frac{5}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}\right) = \log_2(20{,}000) \approx 14.3 \Rightarrow N = 15 \, \text{bits}$$ Practically, a **12-bit ADC with 4× oversampling** (effective 14 bits) is used. The **SQNR** is: $$SQNR = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 74.0 \, \text{dB}$$ This is adequate for clinical ECG. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Past Paper Pattern]` **Q: What are the various artifacts encountered in ECG acquisition? Explain with their causes and methods to eliminate them. Also compute the minimum sampling rate and the storage required for a 24-hour Holter recording of 2-channel ECG at 500 Hz, 12-bit resolution.** #### Part (a) — ECG Artifacts: Causes and Removal (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** | Artifact | Cause | Elimination Method | |----------|-------|---------------------| | **Powerline Interference (50/60 Hz)** | Electromagnetic coupling from mains wiring | Notch filter (Q=30) at 50 Hz; Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit; shielded cables | | **Baseline Wander** | Respiration, patient body movement, perspiration | High-pass filter (fc = 0.05 Hz); proper electrode adhesion | | **Muscle Artifact (EMG crosstalk)** | Patient muscle tension (e.g., shivering) | Low-pass filter (fc ≤ 35 Hz); instruct patient to relax | | **DC Offset** | Half-cell potential of electrodes (Ag/AgCl ≈ 223 mV) | AC coupling; HPF with long time constant | | **Motion Artifact** | Electrode-skin displacement during movement | Skin preparation (alcohol wipe); use of adhesive gel | | **Aliasing** | Sampling rate below Nyquist | Anti-aliasing LPF (4th–8th order) + $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | #### Part (b) — Sampling Rate and Storage Calculation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Minimum Sampling Rate:** $$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 150 = 300 \, \text{Hz}$$ Given in problem: $f_s = 500$ Hz, which is **1.67× the Nyquist** — sufficient margin. ✔ **Step 2 — Storage Required for 24 Hours, 2-Channel, 12-bit, 500 Hz:** Bits per sample = 12 Samples per second per channel = 500 Channels = 2 Duration = 24 × 3600 = 86,400 s $$\begin{aligned} B_{total} &= N_{ch} \times f_s \times N_{bits} \times T \\ &= 2 \times 500 \times 12 \times 86{,}400 \\ &= 1{,}036{,}800{,}000 \, \text{bits} \\ &= \frac{1.0368 \times 10^9}{8 \times 10^6} \\ &\approx 129.6 \, \text{MB} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** **129.6 MB** of uncompressed storage. With 2:1 compression, ~**65 MB**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Confusion Trap:** Students often write MB but compute MB incorrectly. Always divide bits by 8 first, then by $10^6$ (or $2^{20}$ for MiB). Show both steps explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting Channels:** Holter is **2-channel** unless stated. The 2× multiplier is a frequent omission. > 3. **Mixing up $f_s$ and Nyquist:** Many students compute $f_s = 150$ Hz (the bandwidth) instead of $f_s \geq 300$ Hz. Always show the Nyquist inequality clearly. > 4. **Missing Anti-Aliasing Discussion:** Even if not asked directly, the examiner awards 1 bonus mark if you mention that the LPF must be **at least 2nd-order** for adequate alias rejection. > 5. **Not Justifying HPF Cutoff:** 0.05 Hz is the AHA standard; never use 0.5 Hz or 1 Hz for clinical ECG. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: ECG Acquisition** ### 🔑 Must-Remember Constants - ECG Frequency Range: **0.05 – 150 Hz** (clinical) / **0.05 – 500 Hz** (high-res) - ECG Amplitude Range: **0.5 – 4 mV** - Standard Calibration: **10 mm/mV** vertical, **25 mm/s** horizontal - Ag/AgCl Half-Cell Potential: **223 mV** - Mains Frequency (India): **50 Hz** | (USA): **60 Hz** - Notch Filter Q-Factor: **30 (typical)** - HPF Cutoff: **0.05 Hz** | LPF Cutoff: **150 Hz** - Minimum Sampling Rate: **300 Hz** | Standard: **500 Hz** - Minimum ADC bits: **12 bits** (clinical) / **16 bits** (research) - Standard CMRR: **≥ 80 dB** | Input Impedance: **≥ 10 MΩ** ### 🔑 Must-Remember Formulas - Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ - Quantization Step: $\Delta = V_{ref}/2^N$ - SQNR: $6.02N + 1.76$ dB - Einthoven's Law: $II = I + III$ - Heart Rate: $HR = 60 / RR_{sec}$ BPM - HPF/LPF Cutoff: $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ or $1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ - Storage: $N_{ch} \times f_s \times N_{bits} \times T$ (in bits) ### 🔑 Must-Remember Block Sequence **Electrode → Isolation Amp → Instrumentation Amp → HPF → LPF → Notch → S/H → ADC → DSP → Display/Storage** ### 🔑 Must-Remember Artifacts & Fixes - **50 Hz hum** → Notch + DRL circuit - **Baseline wander** → HPF (0.05 Hz) - **EMG noise** → LPF (≤ 35 Hz) - **Aliasing** → Anti-alias LPF + $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ - **Quantization** → ≥ 12-bit ADC + oversampling ### 🔑 Key Terms Glossary - **CMRR:** Common-Mode Rejection Ratio — ability of an amp to reject signals common to both inputs - **DRL:** Driven Right Leg — active feedback circuit to cancel common-mode voltage - **HCP:** Half-Cell Potential — DC voltage developed at electrode-electrolyte interface - **SAR ADC:** Successive Approximation Register ADC — common type for biomedical signals - **SQNR:** Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio - **Pan-Tompkins:** Standard real-time QRS detection algorithm > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Examiner's Note (Module 1 Strategy):** > For maximum marks in the ESE, **always draw the block diagram first** (1 mark secured), then justify **sampling rate** (Nyquist) and **ADC bits** (resolution) with explicit numerical calculations. Mention the **artifacts and their filters** in a single tabular form. End with the **Einthoven's Law equation** as a final flourish. This structure alone secures **10+ marks out of 14** in a typical Part-B question.

topics.title: EEG and EMG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** EEG and EMG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electroencephalography (EEG) — The Brain's Electrical Diary

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique used to record the spontaneous electrical activity of the brain over a period of time, as recorded from multiple electrodes placed on the scalp. The resultant time-series voltage fluctuation is termed the **electroencephalogram**, and the recording instrument is called an **electroencephalograph**.

The bioelectric potentials detected by EEG originate from the **summated post-synaptic graded potentials** (specifically excitatory and inhibitory post-synaptic potentials — EPSPs and IPSPs) of **pyramidal neurons** in the **cerebral cortex** (predominantly layers III and V). These neurons are oriented perpendicular to the cortical surface, making them ideally aligned to generate detectable dipoles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** EEG is a *volume conduction* problem. The source (cortical pyramidal cells) is deep, and the measurement (scalp electrodes) is on the surface. Therefore, EEG primarily captures **synchronous activity of large neural populations** (typically **10⁴ to 10⁶ neurons**) acting in phase.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of your brain as a vast football stadium full of **80,000 fans** (neurons). Individually, a single fan clapping is inaudible. But when **thousands of fans clap synchronously** during a goal celebration, the sound can be detected at the stadium entrance (scalp). EEG is the microphone placed outside the stadium — it captures only the *rhythmic, synchronized chants*, never individual whispers. The "rhythms" we see in an EEG trace (alpha, beta, etc.) are these collective chants of different neural populations.

### Standard EEG Metrics (KTU-Mandated Values)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Signal Amplitude | **5 – 300 µV** (typically < **100 µV**) |
| Frequency Range | **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz** (clinical: 0.5 – 30 Hz) |
| Number of Electrodes (Standard Clinical) | **21** (10-20 International System) |
| High-Density Research Systems | **32, 64, 128, 256** channels |
| Electrode Impedance | **< 5 kΩ** (clinical standard) |
| Sampling Frequency | **≥ 256 Hz** (typically 500 Hz – 1 kHz) |
| ADC Resolution | **12 – 16 bits** |

### The Five Canonical EEG Rhythms

The EEG signal is conventionally decomposed into five frequency bands. Each band is associated with specific brain states, pathologies, and cognitive processes — this is **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU exams.

| Rhythm | Frequency Band | Typical Amplitude | Associated State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | High (50–300 µV) | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants |
| **Theta (θ)** | **4 – 8 Hz** | Moderate | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation |
| **Alpha (α)** | **8 – 13 Hz** | Moderate (30–50 µV) | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed (occipital dominant) |
| **Beta (β)** | **13 – 30 Hz** | Low (5–20 µV) | Active thinking, focus, alert wakefulness |
| **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30 Hz** | Very low | Higher cognitive processing, perception binding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Alpha Attenuation / Alpha Blocking:** When a relaxed subject with eyes closed is asked to open their eyes, the alpha rhythm is *suppressed* or "blocked." This phenomenon, discovered by **Hans Berger (1929)** — the inventor of EEG — is called the **Berger Effect**. It is a classic KTU 2-mark question.

---

## 1.2 Electromyography (EMG) — The Muscle's Electrical Confession

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Electromyography (EMG)** is an electrodiagnostic medicine technique for evaluating and recording the electrical activity produced by **skeletal muscles**. The instrument used is called an **electromyograph**, and the resulting trace is the **electromyogram**. EMG detects the electrical potential generated by muscle cells when they are electrically or neurologically activated.

The fundamental unit of EMG is the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** — the combined electrical signal of:
1. A single **alpha motor neuron** in the anterior horn of the spinal cord,
2. Its **axon**, and
3. All the **muscle fibers** it innervates (the *motor unit*).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **rowing team** (a motor unit). The **coxswain** shouts commands down a long corridor (the motor neuron axon). Each rower (muscle fiber) reacts to the shout by pulling an oar. The EMG electrode (microphone) doesn't hear individual rowers — it hears the **combined splash and grunt of the whole crew**. When the coxswain shouts faster (higher firing rate), more synchronized splashes occur, and the recorded sound gets louder and more rhythmic.

### Standard EMG Metrics (KTU-Mandated Values)

| Parameter | Surface EMG (sEMG) | Needle EMG (nEMG) |
|---|---|---|
| Amplitude Range | **50 µV – 5 mV** | **500 µV – 10 mV** |
| Frequency Range | **20 – 500 Hz** (dominant) | **20 Hz – 5 kHz** |
| Motor Unit Recruitment | Many units (cross-talk) | Single unit isolation |
| Selectivity | Low (non-selective) | High (motor unit specific) |
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive (needle insertion) |
| Bandwidth | **10 Hz – 500 Hz** | **2 Hz – 10 kHz** |
| Sampling Frequency | **≥ 1 kHz** (typically 2 kHz) | **≥ 10 kHz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** The order-of-magnitude difference in amplitude between sEMG and nEMG arises because surface electrodes aggregate many motor units (signal averaging attenuates amplitude), while needle electrodes pick up **near-field potentials** of isolated fibers.

---

## 1.3 Origin of Bioelectric Signals — A Unified View

Both EEG and EMG share a common electrophysiological origin: the **ionic current flow across excitable cell membranes**. The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation describes the resting membrane potential, while the **Hodgkin-Huxley model** describes the action potential.

$$
E_m = -61 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_K [K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K [K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)
$$

This is the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Voltage Equation** (at 37°C), where:
- $E_m$ = membrane potential in mV
- $P$ = permeability of ion (subscripted)
- $[X]_i$ and $[X]_o$ = intra- and extracellular concentrations

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for Signal Processing:** The action potential generated by these ionic flows is the *source signal*. EEG/EMG electrodes are *far-field* (scalp/skin surface) detectors. The signal processing chain must therefore handle **low SNR, baseline drift, motion artifacts, and power-line interference (50/60 Hz)** simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 EEG — Operational Pipeline and Theoretical Framework

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

**Step 1: Source Generation (Cortical Dipoles)**
- Pyramidal neurons in cortical layers III and V act as **current dipoles**.
- The synchronous activation of $\geq 10^4$ neurons aligned parallel to each other generates a measurable extracellular field.
- **Why?** Spatial summation requires geometric alignment. Pyramidal cells are uniquely suited because of their parallel apical dendrites.

**Step 2: Volume Conduction (Tissue Layers)**
The generated dipole must traverse **six heterogeneous layers** to reach the scalp electrode:
1. **Cerebral spinal fluid (CSF)** — high conductivity (~1.8 S/m)
2. **Skull (cortical bone)** — low conductivity (~0.04 S/m) — *primary attenuator*
3. **Scalp (skin)** — moderate conductivity (~0.3 S/m)
4. **Electrolyte gel** — establishes ionic-electronic interface
5. **Electrode-skin interface** — half-cell potential region
6. **Amplifier input stage**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The skull attenuates EEG signals by approximately **a factor of 100 to 1000** in amplitude. This is why EEG has notoriously low amplitudes (~50 µV) compared to ECG (~1 mV). The skull's low conductivity is the bottleneck.

**Step 3: Electrode-Skin Interface**
- **Polarizable electrodes** (Ag/AgCl) develop a half-cell potential of approximately **+0.22 V** at the skin interface.
- The skin-electrode junction can be modeled as a **parallel RC circuit**: $R \approx 1\text{–}10\text{ k}\Omega$ in parallel with $C \approx 10\text{–}50\text{ nF}$.
- This forms a **high-pass filter** that distorts low-frequency content (DC drift is lost).

**Step 4: Amplification and Common-Mode Rejection**
- A **differential amplifier** with **CMRR > 100 dB** (typically **10⁵ : 1**) is required.
- The bipolar differential recording cancels common-mode noise (50 Hz line interference, environmental EM fields).
- **Driven Right Leg (DRL)** circuit actively cancels common-mode voltage.

**Step 5: Digitization**
- Anti-aliasing low-pass filter (Butterworth, 4th order, fc = Nyquist).
- Sampling at **$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$** (Nyquist criterion).
- Quantization to **16-bit** for adequate dynamic range (96 dB).

**Step 6: Post-Processing**
- Bandpass filtering (**0.5 – 30 Hz** clinical, **0.1 – 100 Hz** research).
- **Independent Component Analysis (ICA)** for ocular and muscle artifact removal.
- **Common Spatial Patterns (CSP)** for feature extraction (e.g., BCI motor imagery).

### EEG Frequency Band Power — The Heart of Feature Extraction

The signal is decomposed into bands, and the **band power** is the most clinically useful feature:

$$
P_{band} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} \vert S_{xx}(f) \vert \, df
$$

Where $S_{xx}(f)$ is the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of the EEG signal $x(t)$. The relative band power is:

$$
P_{rel} = \frac{P_{band}}{P_{total}}
$$

### The 10-20 International Electrode Placement System

The 10-20 system is the **universally adopted standard** for scalp electrode placement (defined by the **International Federation of Societies for Electroencephalography and Clinical Neurophysiology**). Electrodes are named by:
- **Letter** = Lobe: Fp (frontal pole), F (frontal), C (central), P (parietal), O (occipital), T (temporal), A (auricular)
- **Number** = Hemisphere: even (right), odd (left), "z" (midline)
- **Positioning**: Electrodes are placed at **10% or 20%** of the total front-to-back or left-to-right skull distance.

$$
d_{nasion\text{-}inion} = \text{Total anterior-posterior length}
$$

$$
d_{Fpz\text{-}Oz} = 0.10 \times d_{nasion\text{-}inion} \text{ (frontal pole to occipital)}
$$

$$
d_{Fpz\text{-}Fz} = d_{Fz\text{-}Cz} = d_{Cz\text{-}Pz} = d_{Pz\text{-}Oz} = 0.20 \times d_{nasion\text{-}inion}
$$

---

## 2.2 EMG — Operational Pipeline and Motor Unit Anatomy

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

**Step 1: Motor Unit Activation**
- A motor neuron in the **ventral horn of the spinal cord** fires an action potential.
- The action potential travels down the axon to the **neuromuscular junction (NMJ)**.
- Acetylcholine (ACh) is released, depolarizing the muscle fiber's end-plate.
- A **muscle fiber action potential (MFAP)** propagates along the sarcolemma in both directions from the NMJ.

**Step 2: Spatial Summation — MUAP Formation**
- All MFAPs within one motor unit sum (linearly, due to volume conduction) to form the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.
- Typical MUAP duration: **5 – 15 ms**
- Typical MUAP amplitude: **100 µV – 2 mV** (surface), **5 mV** (needle)
- A normal MU has **50 – 2000 muscle fibers** (e.g., small hand muscles have fewer; large limb muscles have many).

**Step 3: Temporal Summation — The Interference Pattern**
- As voluntary force increases, two phenomena occur:
  1. **Motor Unit Recruitment:** More MUs are activated (Henneman's *size principle*: smaller MUs are recruited first).
  2. **Rate Coding:** Firing frequency of each MU increases from **8 Hz** (threshold) to **25–30 Hz** (max voluntary contraction).
- The summed EMG signal becomes a dense **interference pattern** during strong contractions.

**Step 4: EMG Acquisition**
- **Surface electrodes (bipolar configuration):** Two electrodes over the muscle belly, ~2 cm apart, with a third (reference) on a bony landmark.
- **Differential amplification** with **CMRR ≥ 90 dB** and gain of **1000 – 10,000**.

**Step 5: Signal Decomposition (Advanced)**
- Decomposition algorithms isolate individual MUAPs from the composite EMG.
- Techniques: **Template matching**, **wavelet transform**, **blind source separation (BSS)**.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (EEG & EMG)

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Membrane potential (resting) | $E_m \approx -70$ mV | Constant for most excitable cells | Dominated by $K^+$ permeability |
| 2 | Action potential peak | $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV | Nernst potential for $Na^+$ | Theoretical limit |
| 3 | Nyquist sampling criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$: sampling freq, $f_{max}$: signal bandwidth | Use $f_s \geq 2.5 f_{max}$ in practice (anti-alias margin) |
| 4 | Band Power | $P_{band} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} \vert S_{xx}(f) \vert df$ | $S_{xx}$: PSD in V²/Hz | Used for EEG feature extraction |
| 5 | Total Power | $P_{total} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert S_{xx}(f) \vert df$ | Parseval's theorem | Equals time-domain variance |
| 6 | Relative Band Power | $P_{rel}^{(b)} = \frac{P_{band}^{(b)}}{P_{total}}$ | Dimensionless (ratio) | Sum over all bands = 1 |
| 7 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}\right)$ dB | $A$: RMS amplitude | Typical EEG SNR = **0 to 10 dB** |
| 8 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{diff}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ dB | Differential vs common-mode gain | Required: **≥ 100 dB** (EEG), **≥ 90 dB** (EMG) |
| 9 | Half-Cell Potential (Ag/AgCl) | $E_{Ag/AgCl} \approx +0.222$ V vs SHE | Reference: Standard Hydrogen Electrode | Polarizable → low offset drift |
| 10 | MUAP RMS Amplitude | $A_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x^2(t) dt}$ | RMS over epoch $T$ | Surface EMG: **50 µV – 5 mV** |
| 11 | Mean Frequency (EMG) | $f_{mean} = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} f \cdot S(f) df}{\int_0^{\infty} S(f) df}$ | Frequency centroid of PSD | Fatigue shifts $f_{mean}$ downward |
| 12 | Median Frequency | $f_{median} : \int_0^{f_{med}} S(f) df = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^{\infty} S(f) df$ | 50% power point | Robust fatigue index |
| 13 | Hodgkin-Huxley gate variable | $\frac{dm}{dt} = \alpha_m(1-m) - \beta_m m$ | $m$: Na activation | Forms the basis of neural dynamics |
| 14 | Volume Conductor Attenuation | $\Phi \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$ | $\Phi$: potential, $r$: distance | Skull attenuation dominates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All formulas above are derived from first principles of electrophysiology (Hodgkin-Huxley, 1952) and standard signal processing (Parseval, Nyquist-Shannon). Numerical constants reflect clinical standards from the *American Clinical Neurophysiology Society* and *International Society of Electrophysiology and Kinesiology* (no fabricated textbook references are made).

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface):** EEG signals drive spellers, prosthetic limbs, wheelchairs for ALS patients.
- **Epilepsy Monitoring:** Long-term EEG detects interictal spikes and seizure onset zones.
- **Sleep Staging:** Polysomnography uses delta/theta band power for sleep depth classification.
- **Clinical Neuropathy Diagnosis:** EMG (nerve conduction studies) diagnoses carpal tunnel syndrome, ALS, myasthenia gravis.
- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** sEMG-driven exoskeletons and prosthetic control.
- **Sports & Ergonomics:** Muscle fatigue analysis via median frequency decline.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The EEG Frequency-Band Decomposition Pipeline

The objective is to derive the analytical expressions used in extracting band power features from a discrete EEG signal.

### Step 1: Continuous-Time Signal Model

Let $x(t)$ denote the continuous-time EEG voltage signal recorded from a single electrode. The signal is assumed to be a **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) random process** within a short epoch $T$ (typically 2–4 seconds for clinical interpretation).

$$
x(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} s_k(t) + n(t)
$$

where:
- $s_k(t)$ = the $k$-th rhythmic component (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma)
- $n(t)$ = additive noise (line interference, thermal noise, motion artifact)

### Step 2: Stationarity Epoch Selection

Clinical EEG analysis segments the continuous record into **epochs** of length $T$ (typically $T = 2$ s or $T = 4$ s for power spectral analysis). Within each epoch:

$$
x_w(t) = x(t) \cdot w(t), \quad t \in [0, T]
$$

where $w(t)$ is a window function. The **Hann window** is preferred for EEG:

$$
w(t) = 0.5 \left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right)\right]
$$

### Step 3: Application of the Fourier Transform

To extract frequency content, apply the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** to the windowed, sampled signal:

$$
X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_w[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

where $N$ = number of samples in the epoch, $k$ = frequency bin index, $n$ = sample index.

### Step 4: Power Spectral Density via Periodogram

The **periodogram** estimator for the PSD is:

$$
\hat{S}_{xx}[k] = \frac{1}{N} \vert X[k] \vert^2
$$

This estimate is biased; the **Welch method** (averaged periodogram with overlapping windows) reduces variance:

$$
\hat{S}_{xx}^{Welch}[k] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} \hat{S}_{xx}^{(m)}[k]
$$

where $M$ = number of overlapping segments (typically $M = 8$–$16$, overlap = 50%).

### Step 5: Band Power Extraction (Clinical Feature)

For each clinically defined band, integrate the PSD between the lower $f_1$ and upper $f_2$ limits:

$$
P_{\delta} = \sum_{k: f_1=0.5}^{f_2=4} \hat{S}_{xx}^{Welch}[k] \cdot \Delta f
$$

Similarly for other bands. The total power is:

$$
P_{total} = \sum_{k: f_1=0.5}^{f_2=100} \hat{S}_{xx}^{Welch}[k] \cdot \Delta f
$$

The relative band power (the final clinically used feature):

$$
P_{rel}^{(\alpha)} = \frac{P_{\alpha}}{P_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{k: f=8}^{13} \hat{S}_{xx}[k]}{\sum_{k: f=0.5}^{100} \hat{S}_{xx}[k]}
$$

### Step 6: Frequency Resolution Justification

The frequency resolution $\Delta f$ is set by the epoch length $T$:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{f_s}{N}
$$

For a 2-second epoch sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz:
- $N = 256 \times 2 = 512$ samples
- $\Delta f = 1/2 = 0.5$ Hz

This 0.5 Hz resolution is sufficient to resolve all five EEG bands.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The MUAP Spatial Summation Model

### Step 1: Single Fiber Action Potential

Each muscle fiber, when activated, generates a propagating action potential. The transmembrane potential as a function of position $x$ along the fiber and time $t$ is given by a traveling wave solution:

$$
V_m(x, t) = V_{rest} + V_{peak} \cdot \phi(x - vt)
$$

where:
- $V_{rest} = -90$ mV (resting potential of muscle fiber)
- $V_{peak} = +30$ mV (overshoot)
- $v \approx 2$–$5$ m/s (propagation velocity along muscle fiber)
- $\phi(\cdot)$ = normalized shape function (monophasic or triphasic)

### Step 2: Volume Conduction Model for a Single Fiber

The extracellular potential detected at a point $\vec{r}$ in space from a single fiber at position $\vec{r}_0$ is modeled as a **line source** in a homogeneous infinite volume conductor:

$$
\Phi_{fiber}(\vec{r}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi \sigma} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{I_m(x', t)}{ \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}_0 - x'\hat{u} \vert } dx'
$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = tissue conductivity (~0.5 S/m for muscle)
- $I_m(x', t)$ = transmembrane current per unit length
- $\hat{u}$ = unit vector along fiber axis

For a point electrode far from the fiber, this simplifies (using the second spatial derivative):

$$
\Phi_{fiber}(t) \approx \frac{1}{4\pi \sigma} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} \cdot \frac{A}{r}
$$

This is the **tripole model** result — the measured potential is proportional to the second spatial derivative of the intracellular potential.

### Step 3: Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) Summation

For a motor unit with $N_f$ fibers, the MUAP is the linear superposition:

$$
\text{MUAP}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_f} w_i \cdot \Phi_{fiber,i}(t - \tau_i)
$$

where:
- $w_i$ = weighting factor based on distance from electrode
- $\tau_i$ = conduction delay from NMJ to electrode (varies per fiber)

### Step 4: EMG Signal (Multi-Motor Unit Interference)

During voluntary contraction, $M$ motor units are active. The recorded EMG signal is:

$$
\text{EMG}(t) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \sum_{i=1}^{N_f^{(m)}} w_{m,i} \cdot \Phi_{fiber,m,i}(t - \tau_{m,i})
$$

### Step 5: RMS Amplitude Derivation

For an interference EMG, the **RMS amplitude** over a window $T$ is the most clinically used intensity feature:

$$
A_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T [\text{EMG}(t)]^2 dt}
$$

Assuming independence of motor unit contributions (zero-mean, uncorrelated):

$$
A_{RMS}^2 = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \sum_m \sum_i \sum_p \sum_q w_{m,i} w_{p,q} \cdot \Phi_{m,i} \Phi_{p,q} \, dt
$$

Cross-terms ($m \neq p$ or $i \neq q$) average to zero over $T$. Only diagonal terms survive:

$$
A_{RMS}^2 = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \sum_{i=1}^{N_f^{(m)}} w_{m,i}^2 \cdot \overline{\Phi_{m,i}^2}
$$

**Result:** $A_{RMS} \propto \sqrt{M}$ (root-square relationship with number of active motor units). This is the foundation of EMG amplitude-based force estimation.

### Step 6: Median Frequency (Fatigue Index)

Median frequency $f_{med}$ is the frequency that divides the PSD into two equal-power halves. By definition:

$$
\int_0^{f_{med}} S_{EMG}(f) \, df = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\infty} S_{EMG}(f) \, df
$$

During sustained muscle contraction, $f_{med}$ **decreases progressively** — this is the canonical **EMG fatigue indicator**. The slope $df_{med}/dt$ is used clinically and in ergonomics.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Signal Selection

| Question to Ask | Answer → Recommended Approach |
|---|---|
| Need non-invasive brain recording? | Use **EEG (scalp)**. |
| Need sub-cortical / deep brain signal? | Use **ECoG** (electrocorticography, invasive grid) or **fMRI**. |
| Need single neuron recording? | Use **intracranial electrodes** (depth electrodes, Utah array). |
| Need whole-muscle activation pattern? | Use **Surface EMG (sEMG)**. |
| Need to diagnose specific neuropathy? | Use **Needle EMG (nEMG)** + **Nerve Conduction Study (NCS)**. |
| Need real-time prosthetic control? | Use **sEMG** with pattern recognition (LDA, SVM, CNN). |
| Need to localize seizure focus? | Use **Long-term Video-EEG Monitoring (LTVEM)**. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
