# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EEG Signal Acquisition Chain (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Brain Source<br/>Cortical Pyramidal Neurons<br/>10^4 to 10^6 sync cells] --> B[Volume Conductor<br/>CSF plus Skull plus Scalp<br/>High Attenuation Factor]
    B --> C[Electrodes<br/>Ag/AgCl Disc<br/>10-20 System Placement]
    C --> D[Electrode Skin Interface<br/>Half Cell Potential 0.22 V<br/>RC Parallel Model]
    D --> E[Differential Amplifier<br/>CMRR greater than 100 dB<br/>Gain 10000 to 50000]
    E --> F[Anti Aliasing Filter<br/>4th Order Butterworth<br/>fc equals Nyquist]
    F --> G[ADC<br/>16 bit Resolution<br/>fs equals 256 to 1000 Hz]
    G --> H[Digital Processing<br/>Bandpass 0.5 to 30 Hz<br/>ICA for Artifact Removal]
    H --> I[Feature Extraction<br/>Band Power, PSD, ERP<br/>CSP for BCI]
    I --> J[Classification<br/>LDA, SVM, Deep Learning<br/>Application Output]
```

## 4.2 EMG Signal Acquisition Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Alpha Motor Neuron<br/>Spinal Cord Ventral Horn<br/>Action Potential Initiator] --> B[Axon Conduction<br/>Peripheral Nerve<br/>Velocity 50 to 80 m per s]
    B --> C[Neuromuscular Junction<br/>ACh Release<br/>Muscle Fiber Activation]
    C --> D[Muscle Fibers in Motor Unit<br/>50 to 2000 Fibers<br/>MUAP Spatial Summation]
    D --> E[Volume Conductor<br/>Skin and Fat Layers<br/>Differential Filtering Effect]
    E --> F[Surface or Needle Electrodes<br/>Bipolar Configuration<br/>Reference Electrode]
    F --> G[Differential Amplifier<br/>CMRR greater than 90 dB<br/>Gain 1000 to 10000]
    G --> H[Bandpass Filter<br/>20 Hz to 500 Hz sEMG<br/>2 Hz to 10 kHz Needle]
    H --> I[Rectification and Smoothing<br/>Full Wave Rectifier<br/>RMS or Moving Average Envelope]
    I --> J[Feature Extraction<br/>RMS, MAV, ZC, WL, MDF<br/>Time and Frequency Domain]
    J --> K[Pattern Recognition<br/>LDA, SVM, CNN<br/>Prosthetic Control]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Which Biosignal Do I Need?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Clinical or Engineering Goal]) --> Q1{Source Organ?}
    Q1 -->|Brain| Q2{Resolution Required?}
    Q1 -->|Muscle| Q3{Depth of Measurement?}
    Q2 -->|High temporal, lower spatial| EEG[Use EEG]
    Q2 -->|High spatial resolution| ECoG[Use ECoG or fMRI]
    Q3 -->|Superficial whole muscle| sEMG[Use Surface EMG]
    Q3 -->|Deep single motor unit| nEMG[Use Needle EMG]
    EEG --> App1[Applications: Epilepsy, BCI, Sleep, Cognition]
    ECoG --> App2[Applications: Surgical Mapping, Research]
    sEMG --> App3[Applications: Prosthetics, Sports, Ergonomics]
    nEMG --> App4[Applications: Neuropathy Diagnosis, ALS, Myasthenia]
```

## 4.4 EEG Frequency Band Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Signal[Raw EEG Signal 0.5 to 100 Hz] --> BPF[Bandpass Filter Bank]
    BPF --> Delta[Delta 0.5 to 4 Hz<br/>Deep Sleep and Infants]
    BPF --> Theta[Theta 4 to 8 Hz<br/>Drowsiness and Meditation]
    BPF --> Alpha[Alpha 8 to 13 Hz<br/>Relaxed Wakefulness]
    BPF --> Beta[Beta 13 to 30 Hz<br/>Active Thinking]
    BPF --> Gamma[Gamma above 30 Hz<br/>Higher Cognition]
    Delta --> Path1[Pathology: Encephalopathy, Tumor]
    Alpha --> Norm1[Normal: Occipital Dominant Eyes Closed]
    Beta --> Norm2[Normal: Frontal During Active Tasks]
```

## 4.5 Motor Unit Recruitment Strategy (Henneman's Size Principle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Force Demand Increase] --> B[Stage 1: Small Motor Units Recruited<br/>Type I Slow Oxidative<br/>Threshold 8 Hz]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Rate Coding of Small Units<br/>Frequency 8 to 25 Hz]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Larger Motor Units Recruited<br/>Type IIa Fast Oxidative]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Largest Motor Units Recruited<br/>Type IIb Fast Glycolytic<br/>Threshold 30 Hz]
    E --> F[Maximum Voluntary Contraction<br/>Full Interference Pattern]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Differentiate between surface EMG and needle EMG. Mention any three clinical applications of EMG.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Feature | Surface EMG (sEMG) | Needle EMG (nEMG) |
|---|---|---|
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Invasive (needle inserted into muscle) |
| **Selectivity** | Picks up many motor units (cross-talk) | Isolates individual or few motor units |
| **Amplitude** | 50 µV – 5 mV | 500 µV – 10 mV |
| **Frequency Range** | 20 Hz – 500 Hz | 20 Hz – 5 kHz |
| **Patient Comfort** | Painless | Mild discomfort, risk of infection |
| **Use Case** | Kinesiology, prosthetics, ergonomics | Clinical neurophysiology diagnosis |

**Three Clinical Applications:**
1. **Diagnosis of peripheral neuropathies** (e.g., carpal tunnel syndrome, diabetic neuropathy) — using nerve conduction velocity (NCV) combined with EMG.
2. **Diagnosis of motor neuron diseases** — such as Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS), where EMG shows fibrillation potentials and chronic denervation.
3. **Myasthenia Gravis diagnosis** — repetitive nerve stimulation reveals decremental responses in compound muscle action potential (CMAP).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **EMG (muscle)** with **ENG (Electroneurography)** — the nerve conduction study. Examiners expect you to clarify that ENG measures nerve conduction velocity, while EMG measures muscle response. Conflating them costs 1 mark.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest]
**Explain the origin of EEG signals. List the five standard EEG frequency bands with their frequency ranges.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

**Origin:** EEG signals originate from the **summated post-synaptic potentials (PSPs)** of **pyramidal neurons** in the cerebral cortex (primarily layers III and V). These neurons have apical dendrites oriented perpendicular to the cortical surface, allowing their synchronized graded potentials (EPSPs and IPSPs) to form detectable dipoles. Approximately **$10^4$ to $10^6$ neurons** must fire synchronously to produce a scalp-detectable EEG signal.

**Five Frequency Bands:**

| Band | Frequency Range | State |
|---|---|---|
| Delta (δ) | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | Deep sleep |
| Theta (θ) | **4 – 8 Hz** | Drowsiness |
| Alpha (α) | **8 – 13 Hz** | Relaxed wakefulness |
| Beta (β) | **13 – 30 Hz** | Active thinking |
| Gamma (γ) | **> 30 Hz** | Cognitive binding |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write "alpha is 8–12 Hz" or "delta is up to 5 Hz" — both are technically imprecise. The **8–13 Hz** (alpha) and **0.5–4 Hz** (delta) ranges are the **International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology** standards. Use exactly these.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the EEG signal acquisition system. Explain the role of the differential amplifier and the significance of the 10-20 electrode placement system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A clinical EEG signal is acquired for 4 seconds at a sampling rate of 256 Hz. Compute (i) the total number of samples, (ii) the frequency resolution, (iii) the Nyquist frequency, and (iv) the number of DFT bins. Justify why the 10-20 system uses even numbers for the right hemisphere and odd numbers for the left hemisphere.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — EEG Acquisition System [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
Brain → Volume Conductor (CSF/Skull/Scalp) → Electrodes (10-20 placement)
       → Electrode-Skin Interface (Half-cell potential)
       → Differential Amplifier (CMRR > 100 dB, Gain ~10⁴)
       → Anti-Aliasing Filter (4th order Butterworth, fc = 128 Hz)
       → ADC (16-bit, 256 Hz)
       → Digital Processing (Bandpass, ICA)
       → Display/Storage
```

**Components Explained:**

1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Convert ionic currents into electronic voltages. Low offset, low polarization. Disc electrodes (~1 cm diameter) are placed on the scalp after skin abrasion to reduce impedance below **5 kΩ**.

2. **Differential Amplifier:** Rejects common-mode noise (50 Hz line interference, ambient EM noise). The CMRR must exceed **100 dB** (ratio of $10^5$ : 1). Two inputs: inverting (active electrode) and non-inverting (reference). Output is proportional to the *difference* of the two inputs.

3. **Driven Right Leg (DRL) Circuit:** Actively drives the patient body to a low-impedance ground reference, reducing common-mode voltage.

4. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A 4th-order **Butterworth** low-pass filter with cutoff at **$f_c = f_s / 2 = 128$ Hz**. This is required before digitization to prevent spectral aliasing.

5. **ADC:** Samples the analog signal at **256 Hz** with **16-bit resolution** (dynamic range ≈ **96 dB**).

6. **DRL + Reference Electrodes:** Ground reference on the forehead or earlobe (A1, A2).

**10-20 Electrode Placement System:**

- Standardized by the **International Federation of Societies for EEG** (now IFCN).
- Electrodes are placed at **10% or 20%** of the measured skull distances (nasion-inion, left-right pre-auricular points).
- **Naming convention:** Letter indicates the *lobe* (F, P, T, O, C, Fp, A), number indicates the *hemisphere*, and "z" indicates the **midline**.

| Hemisphere | Numbering |
|---|---|
| **Right** | Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8, 10) |
| **Left** | Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7, 9) |
| **Midline** | Subscript "z" (Fz, Cz, Pz) |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Epoch duration $T = 4$ s
- Sampling frequency $f_s = 256$ Hz

**(i) Total number of samples:**
$$
N = T \times f_s = 4 \text{ s} \times 256 \text{ samples/s} = \mathbf{1024 \text{ samples}}
$$

**(ii) Frequency resolution:**
$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{256}{1024} = \mathbf{0.25 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Justification:** A resolution of 0.25 Hz is sufficient to resolve all EEG sub-bands (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma) without ambiguity. Higher resolution requires longer epochs (inverse trade-off).

**(iii) Nyquist frequency:**
$$
f_{Nyquist} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{256}{2} = \mathbf{128 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Justification:** The Nyquist frequency is the highest frequency that can be unambiguously represented. Any signal component above 128 Hz will alias into the 0–128 Hz band, corrupting the data.

**(iv) Number of DFT bins:**
$$
N_{bins} = N = \mathbf{1024 \text{ bins}}
$$

(In the standard DFT implementation, the output has $N$ complex bins, of which $N/2 + 1 = 513$ are unique due to conjugate symmetry for real signals.)

**Justification for Even/Odd Numbering:**

The convention ensures:
- **Lateralization consistency:** All right-hemisphere electrodes are immediately identifiable by their even numbers; left-hemisphere by odd. This is critical for clinical interpretation (e.g., identifying lateralized seizure foci).
- **Standardization across institutions:** Any EEG recorded globally using the 10-20 system is directly comparable.
- **Midline symmetry:** The "z" subscript identifies the midline (Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz) which serves as the symmetry axis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** In Part (b), students commonly confuse the **Nyquist frequency** (half the sampling rate) with the **sampling frequency** itself. Also, the DFT for an $N$-point real input gives $N/2 + 1$ *unique* magnitude bins due to Hermitian symmetry — stating "1024 bins" is correct for the full DFT output, but "513 unique bins" is more precise.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the origin of the EMG signal. Define a Motor Unit, Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP), and describe Henneman's size principle of motor unit recruitment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a surface EMG signal recorded over the biceps brachii during a sustained isometric contraction, the median frequency (MDF) was measured at three time points:
- $t_1 = 0$ s: $f_{med} = 110$ Hz
- $t_2 = 30$ s: $f_{med} = 92$ Hz
- $t_3 = 60$ s: $f_{med} = 78$ Hz

Compute (i) the rate of MDF decline (slope) per minute, (ii) the percentage decrease from $t_1$ to $t_3$, and (iii) discuss whether this trend indicates muscle fatigue. Mention at least two physiological reasons for MDF decline during fatigue.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — EMG Origin and Motor Unit Anatomy [7 Marks]

**Origin of EMG Signal:**
EMG signals originate from the **electrical activity of muscle fibers** during voluntary or stimulated contraction. The chain of events:

1. The **central nervous system** (specifically the **primary motor cortex**) sends a command to the **alpha motor neuron** in the ventral horn of the spinal cord.
2. The action potential travels down the **motor axon** to the **neuromuscular junction**.
3. Acetylcholine (ACh) is released, depolarizing the muscle fiber end-plate.
4. A **muscle fiber action potential (MFAP)** propagates along the sarcolemma at **2–5 m/s**.
5. The MFAPs from all fibers in one motor unit sum (volume conduction) to produce the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.

**Definitions:**

- **Motor Unit (MU):** A single **alpha motor neuron** plus all the **muscle fibers** it innervates. Typical motor unit sizes: small muscles (extraocular) = 5–10 fibers; large muscles (gastrocnemius) = 1000–2000 fibers.
- **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP):** The time- and space-averaged extracellular voltage recorded from a single motor unit's activity. Typical MUAP duration: **5–15 ms**; typical MUAP amplitude: **100 µV – 2 mV** (surface).

**Henneman's Size Principle (1957, 1965):**

During voluntary contraction, motor units are recruited in a **predictable order based on size**:

1. **Smallest motor units are recruited first.** They have small-diameter, slowly conducting axons and innervate **Type I (slow-twitch, oxidative)** muscle fibers — suited for low-force, sustained activities.
2. **Larger motor units are recruited later.** They have large-diameter, fast-conducting axons and innervate **Type II (fast-twitch)** fibers — suited for high-force, brief activities.
3. **Rate coding complements recruitment:** Already-active units increase their firing frequency (typically 8–30 Hz) before new units are recruited.

**Consequence:** Smooth, graded force production with minimal metabolic cost.

#### Part (b) — EMG Fatigue Analysis [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**

| Time (s) | $f_{med}$ (Hz) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 110 |
| 30 | 92 |
| 60 | 78 |

**(i) Rate of MDF decline per minute:**

Using linear regression on the data points $(0, 110), (30, 92), (60, 78)$:

**Step 1:** Calculate mean time $\bar{t} = (0 + 30 + 60)/3 = 30$ s.
**Step 2:** Calculate mean frequency $\bar{f} = (110 + 92 + 78)/3 = 93.33$ Hz.
**Step 3:** Compute the slope $m$:

$$
m = \frac{\sum (t_i - \bar{t})(f_i - \bar{f})}{\sum (t_i - \bar{t})^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerator:
- $(0 - 30)(110 - 93.33) = (-30)(16.67) = -500$
- $(30 - 30)(92 - 93.33) = (0)(-1.33) = 0$
- $(60 - 30)(78 - 93.33) = (30)(-15.33) = -460$
- Sum = $-960$

**Step 5:** Denominator:
- $(0 - 30)^2 = 900$
- $(30 - 30)^2 = 0$
- $(60 - 30)^2 = 900$
- Sum = $1800$

**Step 6:** Slope:

$$
m = \frac{-960}{1800} = -0.533 \text{ Hz/s}
$$

Converting to per minute:

$$
m = -0.533 \times 60 = \mathbf{-32 \text{ Hz/min}}
$$

**(ii) Percentage decrease from $t_1$ to $t_3$:**

$$
\% \text{ decrease} = \frac{f_{med}(t_1) - f_{med}(t_3)}{f_{med}(t_1)} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{110 - 78}{110} \times 100 = \frac{32}{110} \times 100 = \mathbf{29.09\%}
$$

**(iii) Interpretation — Does this indicate muscle fatigue?**

**Yes.** A 29% drop in median frequency over 60 seconds is a **clear indicator of muscular fatigue**. The slope of approximately **−32 Hz/min** is consistent with the literature on sustained isometric contractions (typical slopes: **−0.5 to −1.5% per second**, which equals **−30% to −90% per minute** for sustained maximal contractions).

**Two Physiological Reasons for MDF Decline During Fatigue:**

1. **Motor Unit Synchronization and Slowing of Conduction Velocity:** As fatigue develops, the action potential conduction velocity along muscle fibers *decreases* (due to accumulation of metabolic byproducts like $K^+$, inorganic phosphate, and $H^+$). This shifts the EMG spectrum toward lower frequencies.

2. **Selective Dropout of Fast-Twitch (Type II) Motor Units:** Fast-twitch fibers fatigue first and may cease firing, leaving predominantly slow-twitch (Type I) fiber activity. Since Type I fibers have smaller MUAPs with lower-frequency content, the aggregate PSD shifts toward lower frequencies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students wrongly state that "the muscle fibers get smaller" or "the muscle stops firing entirely" — neither is correct. The dominant mechanism is **conduction velocity slowing** and **selective Type II dropout**, both of which preserve the integrity of contraction while reducing frequency content.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> Use this list as a final 5-minute review before entering the KTU examination hall.

- [ ] **EEG = summated PSPs of cortical pyramidal neurons**; minimum ~$10^4$ synchronous cells needed.
- [ ] **Five EEG bands:** Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30). All values in **Hz**.
- [ ] **EEG amplitude range: 5–300 µV**; frequency 0.5–100 Hz; sampling ≥256 Hz.
- [ ] **Alpha rhythm** is dominant occipitally with eyes closed; **suppressed** (Berger Effect) when eyes open.
- [ ] **10-20 system:** Even = right hemisphere; Odd = left hemisphere; "z" = midline.
- [ ] **Skull attenuation** is the dominant attenuator (factor 100–1000) in EEG volume conduction.
- [ ] **CMRR ≥ 100 dB** is mandatory for EEG amplifiers.
- [ ] **EMG = MUAPs summed over multiple active motor units.**
- [ ] **Motor Unit = one alpha motor neuron + all its innervated muscle fibers.**
- [ ] **Surface EMG: 50 µV – 5 mV, 20–500 Hz**; **Needle EMG: 500 µV – 10 mV, 20 Hz – 5 kHz**.
- [ ] **Henneman's size principle:** Smallest MUs (Type I) recruited first; largest MUs (Type II) recruited last.
- [ ] **Rate coding:** Firing rate goes from **8 Hz (threshold) to 25–30 Hz (max voluntary)**.
- [ ] **EMG fatigue indicator:** Median frequency ($f_{med}$) **decreases** progressively during sustained contraction.
- [ ] **$f_{med}$ decline reasons:** (i) Conduction velocity slowing due to $K^+/H^+$ accumulation; (ii) Type II fiber dropout.
- [ ] **Band power feature:** $P_{band} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} \vert S_{xx}(f) \vert df$; **Relative band power** = $P_{band}/P_{total}$.
- [ ] **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — always mention the practical margin ($f_s \geq 2.5 f_{max}$).
- [ ] **Welch's periodogram** is the standard PSD estimator for EEG (averaged, windowed periodograms).
- [ ] **Ag/AgCl electrode half-cell potential:** +0.222 V vs SHE.
- [ ] **EMG frequency-domain features:** MDF (median frequency), MNF (mean frequency), spectral entropy.
- [ ] **EMG time-domain features:** RMS amplitude, MAV (mean absolute value), ZC (zero crossings), WL (waveform length).
- [ ] **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz voltage equation** defines the resting membrane potential from ionic permeabilities.
- [ ] **Hodgkin-Huxley model** describes the action potential via gate variables ($m, h, n$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When answering Part A (3-mark) questions, always quantify your answer with **numbers** (e.g., frequency ranges, amplitudes, slopes) — vague qualitative answers are penalized. When answering Part B (14-mark) questions, always include a **diagram** (even a simple block diagram) — this guarantees at least 1–2 marks even if your explanation is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** is the electronic process of translating a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal (e.g., ECG, EEG, EMG, PPG) into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital signal that can be stored, processed, and analyzed by a digital computer or microprocessor. In biomedical engineering, ADC is the critical bridge between the physiological world (analog) and the computational world (digital). The ADC operation is mathematically modeled as two sequential subprocesses: 1. **Sampling** — Discretization of the continuous-time axis (t → nT). 2. **Quantization & Encoding** — Discretization of the continuous amplitude axis into a finite set of binary codes. > [!NOTE] > **Core KTU Terminology:** An ADC is characterized by its **resolution (n bits)**, **sampling frequency (fs in Hz)**, **dynamic range**, **signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR)**, and **aperture time**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine a long, smooth staircase railing (the analog signal). Now imagine you press your hand against it at **fixed regular intervals** — say once every second (this is **sampling**). Each time you press, you also **round your palm's height** to the nearest whole number of stairs (this is **quantization**). The result is a sequence of whole numbers representing approximate heights — your hand movements produce a *digital* record of the railing's curve. In biomedical terms: - The **ECG waveform** (analog, ~mV range, continuous) → becomes a sequence of numbers like $\{521, 528, 540, 555, \ldots\}$ → stored in memory as binary codes. - The number of unique "heights" you can record is determined by how many bits you use — more bits = finer height resolution. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Benchmarks:** > - **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (where $f_m$ is the highest frequency component of the input signal) > - **Standard ECG Sampling Rate:** **360 Hz to 1000 Hz** (clinical standard) > - **Standard EEG Sampling Rate:** **256 Hz to 512 Hz** > - **Standard EMG Sampling Rate:** **1000 Hz to 2000 Hz** > - **Typical Biomedical ADC Resolution:** **12-bit to 24-bit** > - **Quantization Step (LSB) Size:** $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Three Pillars of ADC Operation The ADC pipeline for a biomedical signal (e.g., ECG) consists of three sequential stages. Each stage introduces its own error/noise mechanism, and KTU examiners love testing the limits of each. ### Pillar 1 — Anti-Aliasing Filtering (Pre-Sampling Stage) - **Why:** Real biomedical signals (ECG, EMG) contain noise and frequency components above the Nyquist limit. Sampling without filtering causes **aliasing** (irreversible distortion). - **How:** A low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ attenuates high-frequency noise *before* the sampler. - **Engineering Reality:** Practical filters have a finite roll-off (e.g., Butterworth 4th-order, 0.5 dB ripple), so $f_c$ is often chosen slightly below $f_s/2$. ### Pillar 2 — Sampling (Sample-and-Hold Circuit) - **Why:** The quantizer needs a stable voltage during the conversion interval (typically nanoseconds to microseconds). Without a hold capacitor, the input may change mid-conversion. - **How:** A switch (MOSFET) closes briefly to charge a hold capacitor $C_H$ to the input voltage. It then opens, "freezing" the voltage for quantization. - **Key Metrics:** - **Aperture Time ($t_{ap}$):** Time for the switch to fully open (delay uncertainty). - **Acquisition Time ($t_{acq}$):** Time to charge the capacitor to within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB accuracy. - **Droop Rate:** Leakage-induced voltage decay during hold mode. ### Pillar 3 — Quantization & Encoding - **Why:** Computers work with finite bits; we must map a continuous voltage range to discrete binary codes. - **How:** The full-scale voltage range $[-V_{FS}, +V_{FS}]$ is divided into $2^n$ equal sub-ranges of width $\Delta$ (the **Least Significant Bit, LSB**). - **Error Introduced:** **Quantization Error** $e_q$, bounded by $-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below is the **High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet** — memorize every row. KTU 2024 ESE problems are frequently solved using 2–3 of these combined. | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|-------------------|------------|---------------| | 1 | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz | | 2 | Sampling Interval | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds | | 3 | LSB / Quantization Step Size | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Volts | | 4 | Quantization Error Bound | $-\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Volts (uniform) | | 5 | RMS Quantization Noise | $e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Volts | | 6 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB | | 7 | Aperture Uncertainty Error | $\Delta V = \left(\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$ | Volts | | 8 | Alias Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$, $k$ chosen to bring into $[0, f_s/2]$ | Hz | | 9 | Dynamic Range | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2^n) = 6.02n$ | dB | | 10 | Effective Number of Bits | $ENOB = \dfrac{SQNR_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits | | 11 | Resolution (relative) | $\dfrac{1}{2^n}$ | dimensionless | | 12 | Acquisition Time (approx.) | $t_{acq} \approx R_s C_H \cdot \ln(2^n)$ | seconds | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In the **biomedical industry**, ADC selection is mission-critical: - **Portable ECG Holter Monitors** require **24-bit sigma-delta ADCs** at $f_s = 500$ Hz to capture mV-level signals with high dynamic range and low power. - **MRI Receivers** use **16-bit, 100 MHz ADCs** to digitize high-bandwidth RF signals. - **Implantable Pacemakers** use **12-bit successive approximation ADCs** at low sampling rates (~200 Hz) to minimize power. - **EEG/BCI Systems** need **antialiasing filters** matched precisely to half the sampling rate to avoid distortion of neural oscillations. > [!TIP] > **KTU Frequently Tested Insight:** The trade-off between **resolution (bits)** and **sampling speed** — High-resolution ADCs are slow (sigma-delta), while high-speed ADCs have lower resolution (flash). Biomedical applications typically favor resolution over speed because physiological signals have low bandwidths. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantization Step Size ($\Delta$) and Resolution The ADC accepts an input voltage range $[V_{min}, V_{max}]$. The full-scale voltage span is: $$ V_{FS} = V_{max} - V_{min} $$ An $n$-bit ADC partitions this span into $2^n$ equally spaced intervals. Each interval width is the **quantization step** or **LSB**: $$ \Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} $$ **Resolution** as a percentage of full-scale: $$ \text{Resolution (\%)} = \dfrac{\Delta}{V_{FS}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100\% $$ ### Numerical Example (KTU-Style) For an 8-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 10$ V: $$ \Delta = \dfrac{10}{2^8} = \dfrac{10}{256} = 0.0390625 \text{ V} = 39.0625 \text{ mV} $$ For a 12-bit ADC with the same $V_{FS}$: $$ \Delta = \dfrac{10}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{10}{4096} = 0.002441 \text{ V} = 2.441 \text{ mV} $$ > **Step Conversion Logic:** Each additional bit **halves** the quantization step — this is why 12-bit ECG ADCs are vastly superior to 8-bit ones for capturing small QRS complexes. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) Assume the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $\left[-\dfrac{\Delta}{2}, +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}\right]$. **Step 1 — Probability Density Function of $e_q$:** $$ p(e_q) = \dfrac{1}{\Delta}, \quad -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2} $$ **Step 2 — Mean Square Value of Quantization Noise:** $$ \overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, de_q $$ $$ \overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \dfrac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\dfrac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 \right] $$ $$ \overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 2 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta^3}{8} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} $$ **Step 3 — RMS Quantization Noise Voltage:** $$ e_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\overline{e_q^2}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} $$ **Step 4 — Signal Power for Full-Scale Sinusoid:** For a sinusoid of peak amplitude $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2}$: $$ P_{signal} = \dfrac{(V_{FS}/2)^2}{2} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{8} $$ **Step 5 — SQNR in Linear Scale:** $$ SQNR = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^2}{8} \cdot \dfrac{12}{\Delta^2} = \dfrac{12 \cdot V_{FS}^2}{8 \Delta^2} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$:** $$ SQNR = \dfrac{12 \cdot V_{FS}^2}{8} \cdot \dfrac{2^{2n}}{V_{FS}^2} = \dfrac{12 \cdot 2^{2n}}{8} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to Decibels:** $$ SQNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20n \log_{10}(2) $$ $$ SQNR_{dB} = 1.7609 + 20n \cdot 0.30103 $$ $$ \boxed{SQNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}} $$ > **Interpretation:** Every additional bit of resolution adds **6.02 dB** of dynamic range. This is the famous **"6 dB per bit" rule** that KTU examiners expect students to derive, not just memorize. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aperture Uncertainty Error If the analog input is changing at rate $\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ and the aperture switch has timing uncertainty $\Delta t_{ap}$, the voltage error introduced is: $$ \Delta V = \left(\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot \Delta t_{ap} $$ For a full-scale sinusoid $V(t) = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2} \sin(2\pi f_m t)$: $$ \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \pi f_m V_{FS} \cos(2\pi f_m t) $$ Maximum slope occurs at zero crossings: $$ \left(\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right)_{max} = \pi f_m V_{FS} $$ Therefore: $$ \Delta V = \pi f_m V_{FS} \cdot \Delta t_{ap} $$ For the error to remain within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB: $$ \pi f_m V_{FS} \cdot \Delta t_{ap} \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{2} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}} $$ Solving for the maximum tolerable aperture uncertainty: $$ \Delta t_{ap} \leq \dfrac{1}{\pi f_m \cdot 2^{n+1}} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** This is why high-frequency, high-resolution ADCs are physically impossible — aperture uncertainty grows with both bandwidth and resolution. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Aliasing Frequency A sinusoid of frequency $f$ sampled at $f_s$ appears at an apparent frequency: $$ f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert $$ where $k$ is an integer chosen such that $0 \leq f_{alias} \leq \dfrac{f_s}{2}$. **Example (KTU-Style):** Input signal $f = 350$ Hz, sampling rate $f_s = 200$ Hz. Since $f > f_s/2 = 100$ Hz, **aliasing occurs**. $$ k = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{f}{f_s}\right) = \text{round}(1.75) = 2 $$ $$ f_{alias} = \vert 350 - 2 \cdot 200 \vert = \vert 350 - 400 \vert = 50 \text{ Hz} $$ The 350 Hz signal **falsely appears** as a 50 Hz signal — a critical error in EEG/ECG analysis. --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Biomedical ADC System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BIOLOGICAL SOURCE

Derive its RMS value.** **Model Answer:** Quantization error $e_q$ is the difference between the actual analog input voltage and its discretized digital representation. For a uniform $n$-bit ADC with step size $\Delta$, the error is bounded as: $$ -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2} $$ Assuming $e_q$ is uniformly distributed, its RMS value is derived as: $$ e_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, de_q} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} $$ Substituting $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$: $$ \boxed{e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n \sqrt{12}}} $$ **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 for definition, 2 for derivation steps.]** --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. What is the significance of the Nyquist rate in biomedical signal acquisition?** **Model Answer:** The Nyquist Sampling Theorem states that a bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies: $$ \boxed{f_s \geq 2 f_m} $$ The minimum sampling rate $f_N = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**. **Significance in Biomedical Signal Acquisition:** - It sets the **lower bound** for sampling rate to prevent irreversible aliasing of physiological signals (ECG, EEG, EMG). - ECG ($f_m \approx 150$ Hz) → $f_s \geq 300$ Hz (clinical standard: 360–500 Hz). - EEG ($f_m \approx 100$ Hz) → $f_s \geq 200$ Hz (clinical standard: 256–512 Hz). - EMG ($f_m \approx 500$ Hz) → $f_s \geq 1000$ Hz. **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 for statement, 1 for formula, 1 for biomedical significance.]** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the working of a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** with a neat block diagram. Define **aperture time** and **acquisition time**. Derive the relation between the maximum input frequency and aperture uncertainty for an $n$-bit ADC. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An ECG signal has a maximum frequency component of 150 Hz. It is sampled by a 10-bit ADC with a full-scale voltage of 5 V. - (i) Calculate the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. - (ii) Determine the quantization step size (LSB). - (iii) Find the maximum quantization error. - (iv) Calculate the SQNR in dB. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B-A #### Part (a) — Sample-and-Hold Circuit **Working Principle:** A Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit freezes a rapidly changing analog voltage at a constant level for the duration of the A/D conversion. It consists of: - An **MOSFET switch** (or JFET) controlled by a sampling clock. - A **hold capacitor** $C_H$. - A **buffer amplifier** (high input impedance, low output impedance) to prevent capacitor discharge. **Operating Phases:** 1. **Sample Mode:** Switch closed → $C_H$ charges to input voltage $V_{in}$ through the switch ON-resistance $R_{on}$. 2. **Hold Mode:** Switch opens → $C_H$ retains the last sampled voltage. **Key Timing Parameters:** - **Aperture Time ($t_{ap}$):** Delay between the hold command and the actual opening of the switch. - **Acquisition Time ($t_{acq}$):** Time required for the capacitor to charge to within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB of the input voltage after the switch closes. $$ t_{acq} \approx R_{on} C_H \cdot \ln(2^n) $$ **Derivation of Maximum Input Frequency vs Aperture Uncertainty:** The maximum rate of change of a sinusoid $V(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_m t)$ is: $$ \left(\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2\pi f_m A $$ For an $n$-bit ADC, the aperture error must be less than $\pm \dfrac{1}{2}$ LSB: $$ \Delta V_{ap} \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{2} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}} $$ The aperture-induced error is: $$ \Delta V_{ap} = \left(\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot \Delta t_{ap} = 2\pi f_m A \cdot \Delta t_{ap} $$ For a full-scale input ($A = V_{FS}/2$): $$ 2\pi f_m \cdot \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2} \cdot \Delta t_{ap} \leq \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}} $$ Solving for $f_m$: $$ \boxed{f_{m,max} = \dfrac{1}{\pi \cdot \Delta t_{ap} \cdot 2^{n+1}}} $$ **[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 for block diagram/working, 2 for definitions, 3 for derivation.]** --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem on ECG Sampling **Given:** $f_m = 150$ Hz, $n = 10$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V. **(i) Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist Rate):** $$ f_s \geq 2 f_m = 2 \times 150 = \boxed{300 \text{ Hz}} $$ **(ii) Quantization Step Size (LSB):** $$ \Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{5}{1024} = \boxed{4.8828 \text{ mV}} $$ **(iii) Maximum Quantization Error:** $$ e_{q,max} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \dfrac{4.8828}{2} = \boxed{\pm 2.4414 \text{ mV}} $$ **(iv) Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio:** $$ SQNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 = 6.02(10) + 1.76 = 60.2 + 1.76 = \boxed{61.96 \text{ dB}} $$ **[7 Marks Breakdown: 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2 marks for each subpart.]** --- ### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** **(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of a **12-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages for biomedical applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A biomedical amplifier outputs a signal with $V_{max} = +4$ V and $V_{min} = -4$ V. This signal is sampled at $f_s = 2$ kHz using a 16-bit ADC. - (i) Compute the quantization step size. - (ii) Determine the RMS quantization noise voltage. - (iii) Calculate the SQNR in dB. - (iv) If a 50 Hz powerline interference (with amplitude 1 V) is present in the input, will the ADC be able to digitize both the 50 Hz interference and the desired biomedical signal (band 0–100 Hz) without aliasing? Justify. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B-B #### Part (a) — SAR ADC Operation **Working Principle of SAR ADC:** A Successive Approximation Register ADC uses a **binary search algorithm** to converge on the digital representation of the analog input. It consists of: 1. **Sample-and-Hold circuit** — to freeze the input. 2. **Comparator** — compares $V_{in}$ with the DAC output. 3. **DAC** (n-bit, resistor-ladder or switched-capacitor) — generates the trial analog voltage. 4. **SAR Logic** — performs the binary search. 5. **Output Register** — holds the final n-bit code. **Step-by-Step Operation (for 12-bit ADC):** 1. On START signal, the SAR sets the MSB (bit 11) to 1 → trial code = $100\ldots 0$ (binary) = 2048. 2. DAC outputs $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2}$ = 2.5 V (assuming $V_{FS} = 5$ V). 3. Comparator decides: if $V_{in} > 2.5$ V, MSB is kept at 1; else set to 0. 4. SAR then tests bit 10 → trial code updated based on previous decision. 5. Process continues for all 12 bits in **12 clock cycles**. 6. After 12 cycles, the EOC (End of Conversion) flag is set. **Total Conversion Time:** $T_{conv} = n \cdot T_{clk}$ **Advantages for Biomedical Applications:** - ✅ **Medium resolution (8–16 bits)** — ideal for ECG, EMG, body temperature. - ✅ **Low power consumption** — suitable for portable/ implantable devices. - ✅ **Predictable conversion time** — deterministic behavior. - ✅ **Compact IC design** — fits in small form-factor wearables. **Disadvantages for Biomedical Applications:** - ❌ **Speed limitation** — $n$ clock cycles needed; not suitable for very high-frequency signals (ultrasound, MRI). - ❌ **Requires precise DAC** — adds cost and complexity. - ❌ **Susceptible to noise** during conversion — needs good PCB layout and shielding. **[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 for block diagram, 3 for step-by-step operation, 2 for advantages/disadvantages.]** --- #### Part (b) — Biomedical Signal with Powerline Interference **Given:** $V_{max} = +4$ V, $V_{min} = -4$ V, $f_s = 2$ kHz, $n = 16$ bits. **(i) Quantization Step Size:** $$ V_{FS} = V_{max} - V_{min} = 4 - (-4) = 8 \text{ V} $$ $$ \Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{8}{2^{16}} = \dfrac{8}{65536} = \boxed{1.2207 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 122.07 \text{ μV}} $$ **(ii) RMS Quantization Noise Voltage:** $$ e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{1.2207 \times 10^{-4}}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{1.2207 \times 10^{-4}}{3.4641} $$ $$ \boxed{e_{q,rms} \approx 3.5238 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V} = 35.24 \text{ μV}} $$ **(iii) SQNR in dB:** $$ SQNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 = 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = \boxed{98.08 \text{ dB}} $$ **(iv) Aliasing Analysis:** - Desired biomedical signal band: 0–100 Hz → $f_{m1} = 100$ Hz. - Powerline interference: $f_2 = 50$ Hz, amplitude 1 V. - Maximum frequency present: $f_{max} = \max(100, 50) = 100$ Hz. - Nyquist criterion check: $$ f_{Nyquist} = \dfrac{f_s}{2} = \dfrac{2000}{2} = 1000 \text{ Hz} $$ Since both $f_1 = 100$ Hz and $f_2 = 50$ Hz are well below 1000 Hz: $$ f_1, f_2 \ll f_{Nyquist} $$ **Conclusion:** ✅ **Yes**, the ADC can digitize **both** the 50 Hz interference and the 0–100 Hz biomedical signal **without aliasing**, because the sampling rate (2 kHz) is significantly higher than the Nyquist rate (200 Hz minimum). **Note for Practical Implementation:** Although the ADC itself does not cause aliasing, the 50 Hz interference will corrupt the desired signal. An analog notch filter at 50 Hz (or digital notch filter in post-processing) should be used **before** the ADC to remove it. **[7 Marks Breakdown: 1 + 1 + 1.5 + 3.5 marks for subparts.]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MARKS-LOSS PITFALLS — READ CAREFULLY:** > > 1. **Confusing $V_{FS}$ with $V_{max}$:** Many students write $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{max}}{2^n}$ instead of $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{V_{max}-V_{min}}{2^n}$. This costs **2 full marks** in Part B numericals. > > 2. **Forgetting the constant 1.76 dB in SQNR:** Writing $SQNR = 6.02n$ (without +1.76) is incomplete. Examiner deducts **0.5–1 mark**. > > 3. **Mixing up Nyquist rate vs Sampling rate:** Nyquist rate = $2 f_m$ (minimum); Sampling rate = $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (chosen value). Students often swap them — **1 mark loss**. > > 4. **Aliasing derivation missing direction folding:** The alias frequency formula $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ requires explicitly stating which $k$ is chosen. Examiners penalize vague answers. > > 5. **Aperture Time vs Acquisition Time confusion:** Aperture = uncertainty in switch opening; Acquisition = time to charge capacitor. Swap them and lose **1 mark** in Part A. > > 6. **Unit conversions:** $2^{10} = 1024$ (not 1000), $2^{12} = 4096$, $2^{16} = 65536$ — handwritten mistakes cost easy marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1: ADC** - ⭐ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ — non-negotiable foundation. - ⭐ **LSB Formula:** $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ — appears in 80% of KTU problems. - ⭐ **Max Quantization Error:** $\pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$. - ⭐ **RMS Quantization Noise:** $\dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}}$. - ⭐ **SQNR Rule:** $6.02n + 1.76$ dB — **6 dB per bit**. - ⭐ **Dynamic Range:** $6.02n$ dB. - ⭐ **Aperture Error:** $\Delta V = \pi f_m V_{FS} \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$. - ⭐ **Alias Frequency:** $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$. - ⭐ **Resolution (%):** $\dfrac{100}{2^n}$ %. - ⭐ **ENOB:** $\dfrac{SQNR_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}$. - ⭐ **SAR ADC:** $n$ clock cycles per conversion; medium resolution; low power. - ⭐ **Flash ADC:** $2^n - 1$ comparators; ultra-fast; low resolution. - ⭐ **Sigma-Delta ADC:** oversampling + noise shaping; high resolution (16–24 bit); used in EEG, audiometry. - ⭐ **Antialiasing Filter:** MUST be analog, placed **before** the sampler, with cutoff at $f_s/2$. - ⭐ **Sample-and-Hold** is mandatory before quantization for fast-changing signals. - ⭐ **Biomedical Sampling Standards:** ECG → 360–500 Hz; EEG → 256–512 Hz; EMG → 1–2 kHz; ECG resolution → 12–24 bits. > 🎯 **HIGH-YIELD TAKEAWAY FOR KTU 2024 ESE:** Master the numerical derivations for **SQNR**, **quantization step**, and **alias frequency**. These three alone account for ~60% of marks in any ADC-related question. Always state the **assumptions** (e.g., uniform quantization, full-scale sinusoidal input, uniformly distributed error) before starting any derivation — KTU examiners award marks for explicit assumption statements. ---

topics.title: Nyquist Theorem and Its Importance in Signal Acquisition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nyquist Theorem and Its Importance in Signal Acquisition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** (often called the Nyquist Theorem in the KTU syllabus) is the foundational result of digital signal processing that links a continuous-time (analog) signal to its discrete-time (sampled) representation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Statement:** A continuous-time, bandlimited signal $x(t)$ whose highest frequency component is $f_m$ Hertz can be completely and uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$
> The minimum sampling rate, $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and the corresponding sampling interval is $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$.

The boundary frequency $f_s / 2$ is called the **Nyquist Frequency** (or folding frequency). The original analog signal must be strictly limited to the range $\left[ -f_s/2,\; +f_s/2 \right]$ in the frequency domain for the theorem to hold without loss of information.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wagon-Wheel" Effect

Imagine a movie camera filming the spokes of a rotating wheel:

- If the camera captures **more than 2 frames per spoke-passing** (i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_m$), the wheel appears to rotate **forward** — the true motion is captured faithfully.
- If the camera captures **fewer than 2 frames per spoke-passing** ($f_s < 2 f_m$), the wheel appears to rotate **backward** or stand still — this perceptual illusion is exactly **aliasing**.

> [!NOTE]
> In biomedical signal processing, the same effect appears in MRI and ultrasound when sampling is too slow. ECG monitors, if undersampled, can show a flatline where high-frequency QRS complexes actually existed — a clinically dangerous error.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The theorem introduces three standard metrics that every KTU student must memorize:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Frequency** | $f_s$ | **Hz (samples/sec)** | Rate at which ADC acquires samples |
| **Maximum Signal Frequency** | $f_m$ | **Hz** | Highest frequency present in bandlimited signal |
| **Sampling Period** | $T_s$ | **seconds (s)** | Time interval between two consecutive samples |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course outcome CO1 maps directly to this topic — students must demonstrate the ability to apply the Nyquist criterion while designing any biomedical data-acquisition system (ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Nyquist Theorem can be decomposed into the following logical steps that govern **every** signal acquisition chain:

1. **Bandlimiting the input signal:** Apply an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** (AAF) to the analog signal to ensure no frequency component exists above $f_m$.
   - *Why:* Any frequency component above $f_s/2$ will reflect back (fold) into the baseband upon sampling and corrupt the signal permanently.
2. **Choosing the sampling frequency:** Select $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, ideally with a **safety margin of 10 %–20 %** in practice.
   - *Why:* Practical analog filters do not have brick-wall cutoffs; they roll off gradually.
3. **Sampling (A/D conversion):** Acquire discrete samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ at uniform time intervals.
4. **Reconstruction (D/A conversion):** Low-pass filter the impulse-train of samples with a cutoff at $f_s/2$ to recover $x(t)$ exactly (ideal case).
5. **Verification:** The spectrum of the sampled signal consists of periodically replicated copies of the original spectrum, separated by $f_s$. Reconstruction is possible only if these copies do not overlap.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Aliasing

When a sinusoid of frequency $f_0$ is sampled at $f_s$, the observed (apparent) frequency becomes:

$$f_{apparent} = \left| f_0 - k f_s \right|$$

for the integer $k$ that places $f_{apparent}$ inside the principal band $\left[ 0, f_s/2 \right]$. This is the **aliasing equation** — the same physical frequency can be misrepresented as a completely different frequency if the theorem is violated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aliasing is irreversible.** Once an analog signal is undersampled and digitized, the aliased frequencies cannot be separated from genuine low-frequency content, no matter how sophisticated the digital filter.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Nyquist Criterion | $f_s$ in Hz |
| 2 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m$ | Minimum sampling rate | Hz |
| 3 | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | Sampling period | seconds |
| 4 | $T_s \leq 1 / (2 f_m)$ | Maximum allowable sampling interval | seconds |
| 5 | $f_{alias} = \vert f - f_s \vert$ | Apparent frequency after aliasing (single fold) | Hz |
| 6 | $f_{apparent} = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$ | General aliasing equation | Hz |
| 7 | $f_{Nyquist\;Freq} = f_s / 2$ | Folding frequency (half sampling rate) | Hz |
| 8 | $BW = f_s / 2$ | Maximum usable analog bandwidth | Hz |
| 9 | $R_{data} = N \cdot f_s \cdot B$ | Data rate for $N$ channels, $B$ bits/sample | bits/sec |
| 10 | $\Delta t = 1 / f_s$ | Time resolution of acquisition | seconds |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG Acquisition (KTU Frequently Asked):** QRS complex extends to ~150 Hz → $f_s$ should be at least **360 Hz** (clinical standard: 500 Hz).
- **EEG Acquisition:** Bandwidth typically 0.5–70 Hz → $f_s$ of **200 Hz** suffices.
- **EMG Acquisition:** Bandwidth 20–500 Hz → $f_s$ must be at least **1000 Hz** (clinical: 2000 Hz).
- **MRI / fMRI:** Spatial sampling is governed by the same $k$-space Nyquist criterion.
- **Ultrasound:** Time-gated sampling at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max\;transducer}$ is essential for accurate RF echo reconstruction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Impulse Sampling to Spectral Replication

**Goal:** Show mathematically that sampling a bandlimited signal in the time domain produces periodic replication in the frequency domain, and that reconstruction is only possible when these replicas do not overlap.

**Step 1 — Represent sampling as multiplication by an impulse train.**

Let the sampling impulse train be:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

The sampled signal is therefore:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(n T_s)\, \delta(t - n T_s)$$

This is the *ideal sampled representation* of $x(t)$.

**Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform.**

Using the **multiplication-in-time ↔ convolution-in-frequency** property of the Fourier Transform:

$$\mathcal{F}\{x_s(t)\} = X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

That is, the spectrum of the sampled signal is a **periodic replication** of the original spectrum $X(f)$, scaled by $1/T_s$ and shifted by integer multiples of $f_s$.

**Step 3 — Impose the no-overlap (no-aliasing) condition.**

The original spectrum $X(f)$ is non-zero only over the band $\left[ -f_m, +f_m \right]$. For the shifted copies (centered at $\pm f_s, \pm 2 f_s, \dots$) not to overlap, the *first* shifted copy on the right (centered at $+f_s$) must start *at or beyond* the end of the original band on the right ($+f_m$).

This is written as:

$$f_s - f_m \geq f_m$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the sampling frequency.**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_s - f_m &\geq f_m \\
f_s &\geq 2 f_m \\
\therefore \quad f_{Nyquist} &= 2 f_m
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Nyquist Criterion**.

## 3.2 Derivation — Reconstruction via the Sinc Interpolator

If the sampling criterion is satisfied, $X(f)$ can be recovered from $X_s(f)$ by an ideal low-pass filter $H(f)$ with gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_s/2$:

$$H(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_s / 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The inverse Fourier transform of this ideal filter is the **sinc kernel**:

$$\mathcal{F}^{-1}\{H(f)\} = h(t) = \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s}$$

Applying this filter to $x_s(t)$ (convolution in time) yields the **Shannon–Whittaker reconstruction formula**:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(n T_s)\; \frac{\sin\!\left(\pi (t - n T_s) / T_s\right)}{\pi (t - n T_s) / T_s}$$

This is the **exact, perfect reconstruction** formula — provided $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ and $x(t)$ is strictly bandlimited.

## 3.3 Analytical Example — Finding the Aliased Frequency

**Problem:** A pure sinusoid at $f_0 = 380$ Hz is sampled at $f_s = 250$ Hz. What is the observed (apparent) frequency?

**Solution:**

First, check Nyquist: $2 f_m = 2 \times 380 = 760$ Hz. Since $f_s = 250 < 760$, the theorem is violated, and aliasing occurs.

Find $k$ such that $f_{apparent} = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$ lies in the range $\left[ 0, f_s/2 \right] = \left[ 0, 125 \right]$ Hz.

Test $k = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{apparent} &= \vert 380 - 1 \times 250 \vert \\
&= \vert 380 - 250 \vert \\
&= 130 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $130 > 125$, fold again with $k = 2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{apparent} &= \vert 380 - 2 \times 250 \vert \\
&= \vert 380 - 500 \vert \\
&= 120 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

$120 \in [0, 125]$ ✓ → **Observed frequency is 120 Hz** (a true 380 Hz tone appears as a 120 Hz tone).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Signal Acquisition Block Diagram (Anti-Aliasing + Sampling Pipeline)

The figure below shows the canonical acquisition chain that enforces the Nyquist criterion in any real biomedical instrument.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Analog Biomedical Signal x of t] --> aaf[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter Cutoff fm]
    aaf --> sdh[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    sdh --> adc[Analog to Digital Converter]
    adc --> buf[Digital Buffer Memory]
    buf --> proc[Digital Signal Processor]

    clk1[Clock fs] --> sdh
    clk1 --> adc

    classDef io fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#003366
    classDef proc fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,color:#663300
    classDef clk fill:#E6F9E6,stroke:#009933,color:#003300

    class src,buf,proc io
    class aaf,sdh,adc proc
    class clk1 clk
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **anti-aliasing filter** is the *gatekeeper* — it ensures the signal is strictly bandlimited to $f_m$ before sampling.
- The **sample-and-hold** circuit freezes the voltage for the ADC to digitize (governed by clock $f_s$).
- The **ADC** produces discrete samples $x(nT_s)$ stored in the buffer.
- The **DSP** performs further analysis (filtering, feature extraction, QRS detection, etc.).

## 4.2 Spectral Replication — Visual Logic of the Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Before Sampling]
        A1[Original Spectrum X of f centered at 0 Hz]
    end

    subgraph B[After Sampling at fs Greater Than 2fm]
        B1[Replica 1 centered at 0 Hz]
        B2[Replica 2 centered at plus fs]
        B3[Replica 3 centered at minus fs]
        B1 --- B2
        B2 --- B3
    end

    subgraph C[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]
        C1[Pass band 0 to fs over 2]
        C2[Extract original X of f]
        C1 --> C2
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
```

**Logic of the diagram:**

- **A** shows the original bandlimited spectrum.
- **B** shows that after sampling, the spectrum is replicated at every integer multiple of $f_s$. Because $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, the replicas do **not** overlap.
- **C** shows the ideal low-pass reconstruction filter that isolates the central replica and recovers the original signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the spectral replicas in box **B** overlap (which happens when $f_s < 2 f_m$), the system enters the **aliasing regime** — and no filter, digital or analog, can separate the overlapped regions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Nyquist Sampling Theorem states that a continuous-time, bandlimited signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its discrete samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

The minimum sampling rate $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m$ is called the Nyquist rate, and the corresponding sampling interval is $T_s = 1 / (2 f_m)$. (Any one equivalent restatement earns full marks.)

---

### Q2. Define aliasing. How is it prevented in biomedical signal acquisition? [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Aliasing is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a signal, when sampled below the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2 f_m$), appear as spurious lower-frequency components in the digitized signal, causing irreversible distortion.

**Prevention methods (any two for full marks):**

1. Apply a sharp **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** before the ADC to bandlimit the signal to $f_m < f_s/2$.
2. Choose a **higher sampling rate** with a practical safety margin of 10–20 %.
3. Use **oversampling** (e.g., $f_s = 4 f_m$ or higher) followed by digital decimation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the Nyquist sampling criterion $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ for a bandlimited signal. State the conditions under which perfect reconstruction is possible. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 60 Hz power-line interference signal is to be filtered from an ECG signal using a digital notch filter. If the ADC samples the ECG at 500 Hz, determine whether 60 Hz will alias into the baseband, and find the apparent frequency. Comment on the design choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. Let $x(t)$ be bandlimited to $f_m$ Hz. Its Fourier Transform $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$. **(1 mark)**
2. Sampling is modelled as multiplication by an impulse train $p(t) = \sum \delta(t - nT_s)$ with $T_s = 1/f_s$. **(1 mark)**
3. Sampled signal: $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)$. **(1 mark)**
4. Fourier Transform: $X_s(f) = (1/T_s) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - k f_s)$. **(1 mark)**
5. This is a periodic replication of $X(f)$ at multiples of $f_s$. **(0.5 mark)**
6. For non-overlapping replicas: $f_s - f_m \geq f_m \implies f_s \geq 2 f_m$. **(2 marks)**
7. **Conditions for perfect reconstruction:** (i) the signal must be strictly bandlimited to $f_m$, (ii) sampling rate must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, (iii) reconstruction uses an ideal low-pass filter with cutoff $f_s/2$. **(0.5 mark)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $f_0 = 60$ Hz, $f_s = 500$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Check if 60 Hz aliases:** The Nyquist frequency is $f_s/2 = 250$ Hz. Since $f_0 = 60 < 250$, **60 Hz does NOT alias** in the strict mathematical sense — it remains at 60 Hz in the digital domain. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Find the apparent (sampled) frequency:** $f_{apparent} = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$ for the integer $k$ such that the result lies in $[0, 250]$ Hz. For $k=0$: $f_{apparent} = 60$ Hz. ✓ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Design comment:** The notch filter should be designed at the **discrete normalized frequency** $\omega_0 = 2 \pi f_0 / f_s = 2 \pi (60/500) = 0.24 \pi$ rad/sample. The 500 Hz sampling rate is comfortably above $2 \times 60 = 120$ Hz, so no aliasing of the interference occurs. The design is robust. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the components of a biomedical signal acquisition system and state the role of the anti-aliasing filter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EMG signal has a usable bandwidth of 20 Hz to 500 Hz. **(i)** Calculate the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. **(ii)** If a clinician chooses a sampling rate of 1 kHz, what is the maximum allowable analog input frequency? **(iii)** Calculate the data rate (in kbps) for a 16-bit ADC with two channels. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The biomedical signal acquisition chain consists of: **(1 mark each for naming, 1 mark for explanation**

1. **Transducer / Electrode** — converts physiological variable (e.g., muscle action potential) into electrical voltage.
2. **Preamplifier** — boosts the typically low-amplitude ($\mu$V–mV) bioelectric signal; provides high CMRR to reject 50/60 Hz interference.
3. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filter** — bandlimits the signal to $f_m$ before the ADC.
4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — freezes the signal value during the ADC conversion time.
5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — quantizes and encodes samples.
6. **Digital Signal Processor / Microcontroller** — performs filtering, feature extraction, storage, display.

**Role of the anti-aliasing filter (1 mark extra):** It ensures that no frequency component above $f_s/2$ reaches the ADC, preventing irreversible aliasing. In practice, a 4th-order Butterworth or Bessel filter is used.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Minimum sampling rate:** $f_m = 500$ Hz

$$f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Maximum allowable analog input frequency for $f_s = 1$ kHz:** **(2 marks)**

$$f_{max} = f_s / 2 = 1000 / 2 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Data rate for 16-bit, 2-channel ADC at 1 kHz:** **(3 marks)**

$$R_{data} = N \times f_s \times B = 2 \times 1000 \times 16 = 32{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 1 — Nyquist Theorem):**
> 1. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency.** *Nyquist Rate = $2 f_m$* (sampling rate). *Nyquist Frequency = $f_s / 2$* (folding frequency). Examiners deduct 1 mark per instance of this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter in block diagrams.** Drawing a block diagram without the AAF is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Unit errors** — $f_s$ in kHz vs. Hz; $T_s$ in ms vs. μs. Always write units explicitly.
> 4. **Incorrect aliasing computation** — using $f_{apparent} = f_s - f_0$ for *all* cases. The correct form is the modular form $\vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$; for higher folds, multiple subtractions may be needed.
> 5. **"Sampling rate" vs. "Sampling period" mix-up** in derivations — keep them strictly separate; use $f_s = 1/T_s$ as the conversion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nyquist Criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ is the *minimum* rate; in practice use 10–20 % margin.
- **Nyquist Rate** = $2 f_m$ (sampling rate). **Nyquist Frequency** = $f_s/2$ (signal-band cutoff).
- **Aliasing** is *irreversible*; prevention is by **anti-aliasing analog filter**, not digital.
- **Aliasing equation:** $f_{apparent} = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$, fold into $[0, f_s/2]$.
- **Reconstruction formula:** $x(t) = \sum x(nT_s) \mathrm{sinc}\left((t - nT_s)/T_s\right)$ — ideal, infinite-sum interpolation.
- **Data rate:** $R = N \cdot f_s \cdot B$ bits/sec.
- **Biomedical benchmarks (memorize for KTU):** ECG $f_s \geq 360$ Hz; EEG $\geq 200$ Hz; EMG $\geq 1$ kHz.
- **Spectral replication:** Sampling in time ↔ periodic replication in frequency (multiplication ↔ convolution property of FT).
- **No-overlap condition** $f_s - f_m \geq f_m$ is the geometric origin of the $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ rule.
- **Perfect reconstruction requires:** strictly bandlimited signal + rate $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ + ideal low-pass filter with cutoff $f_s/2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aliasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aliasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which a continuous-time (analog) signal is sampled at a sampling frequency that is **insufficient** to capture its true frequency content, causing high-frequency components to **fold back** into the lower frequency spectrum and become **indistinguishable** (aliases) from genuine low-frequency components in the digitized signal.

In formal mathematical terms, when a band-limited signal $x(t)$ possessing a maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ is sampled at a sampling rate $f_s$, aliasing occurs if:

$$f_s < 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

The minimum sampling rate required to avoid aliasing is termed the **Nyquist Rate** ($f_{Nyquist}$), and its corresponding frequency is the **Nyquist Frequency** ($f_N = f_s / 2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of Biomedical Signal Processing (e.g., ECG, EEG, EMG), aliasing is a *critical artifact source*. Diagnostic features such as the QRS complex, P-waves, and sharp EMG spikes contain high-frequency transients that are extremely vulnerable to aliasing distortion if not filtered and sampled properly.

## Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **wagon wheel** spinning in a Western movie, captured by a film camera:

- If the camera captures **enough frames per second** (e.g., 60 fps for a wheel spinning at 30 revolutions per second), the wheel appears to rotate **forward** correctly.
- However, if the camera captures **too few frames per second** (e.g., 20 fps for that same wheel), the wheel appears to rotate **backward** — even though it is physically moving forward!

This is aliasing. The camera (our **sampler**) cannot resolve the true motion, so the high-speed rotation **falsely appears** as a low-speed rotation in the opposite direction.

> [!TIP]
> **Biomedical Intuition:** In ECG, the true QRS complex contains frequencies up to ~150 Hz. If a poor acquisition system samples at 200 Hz (only 1.33× the maximum frequency), the sharp R-peak appears as a **distorted, rounded, low-frequency blob** — clinically dangerous, since ST-elevation and Q-wave morphology may be misrepresented.

## Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (units: **Hz**)
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N = f_s / 2$ (units: **Hz**)
- **Typical ECG Sampling Rate (Clinical):** **500 Hz to 1000 Hz** (per AHA recommendations)
- **Typical EEG Sampling Rate:** **256 Hz to 512 Hz** (clinical); up to **20 kHz** (research)
- **Aliasing Frequency Formula:** $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - f_s \cdot \text{round}(f_{in}/f_s) \vert$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Underlying Logic — Why Aliasing Occurs

Aliasing arises from a fundamental conflict between the **continuous nature** of analog signals and the **discrete nature** of digital sampling. Here is the structured reasoning:

1. **Spectrum Replication by Sampling:** When a continuous signal is sampled, its frequency spectrum becomes **periodically replicated** in the frequency domain, with each replica centered at integer multiples of $f_s$ (i.e., $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$).

2. **Spectral Overlap Condition:** If the original signal's spectrum extends **beyond** $f_s/2$, adjacent spectral replicas will **overlap** with each other.

3. **Loss of Information:** Once overlap occurs, there is **no mathematical method** to recover the original signal — the information is permanently lost. This is a one-way destructive process.

4. **Folding Mechanism:** The overlapping high-frequency components **fold back** symmetrically around the Nyquist frequency $f_s/2$, creating fictitious low-frequency artifacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Aliasing cannot be corrected by post-processing. It can *only* be **prevented at the acquisition stage** through:
> - Adequate sampling rate (satisfying Nyquist criterion), and
> - **Anti-aliasing analog filters** (low-pass filters with cutoff $\leq f_s/2$) placed *before* the ADC.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | Hz |
| 2 | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | Nyquist (folding) frequency | Hz |
| 3 | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - f_s \cdot k \vert$ | Aliased frequency, where $k = \text{round}(f_{in}/f_s)$ | Hz |
| 4 | $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ | Angular sampling frequency | rad/s |
| 5 | $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$ | Spectrum of sampled signal (replicas) | — |
| 6 | $\Delta t = 1 / f_s$ | Sampling interval | seconds |
| 7 | $f_{max} = \omega_{max} / (2\pi)$ | Conversion from angular to linear frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $\text{SNR}_{alias} \propto -20 \log_{10}(f_s / f_{Nyquist})$ | Approx. SNR penalty due to undersampling | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In the table above, the absolute value is written as `\vert \cdot \vert` (i.e., $\vert \cdot \vert$) — **NOT** with vertical pipe symbols `\vert` inside the table markdown. This prevents syntax corruption in the KTU markdown renderer.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MRI & CT Imaging:** Aliasing causes **wrap-around artifacts** at the edges of the field-of-view when the imaged object extends beyond the receiver coil's receptive region.
- **Doppler Ultrasound:** Aliasing occurs when blood flow velocity exceeds the Nyquist limit, causing flow direction to appear **reversed** — a critical diagnostic pitfall.
- **ECG/EEG Acquisition Systems:** Front-end analog anti-aliasing filters are mandatory; modern ADCs in patient monitors have integrated digital filters post-ADC as a secondary safeguard.
- **Audio CD Standardization:** The 44.1 kHz sampling rate was specifically chosen (twice the ~22.05 kHz human hearing limit) to prevent aliasing in commercial audio.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Spectral Replication upon Sampling

**Starting Point:** Consider a band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ with Fourier Transform $X(f)$, where $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Model the Sampling Process:** The sampled signal is the product of $x(t)$ and an impulse train $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$, where $T_s = 1/f_s$.

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Fourier Transform:** By the **Multiplication-in-Time Property** (which corresponds to **Convolution-in-Frequency**), the spectrum of the sampled signal is:

$$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Result:** The original spectrum $X(f)$ is replicated at every integer multiple of $f_s$. The replicas are scaled by $1/T_s = f_s$.

**Step 4 — Identify the Overlap Condition:** If the bandwidth of $X(f)$ (i.e., $f_{max}$) is less than $f_s/2$, adjacent replicas **do not overlap**, and $x(t)$ can be perfectly reconstructed using a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_s/2$.

**Step 5 — State the Failure Condition:** If $f_{max} > f_s/2$, the replicas **overlap**, and information is irreversibly corrupted. This is the **mathematical origin of aliasing**.

## Derivation 2: Deriving the Aliased Frequency (Worked Example)

**Problem Setup:** A biomedical instrument samples an EMG signal containing a motor unit action potential with dominant frequency $f_{in} = 145$ Hz. The sampling rate is $f_s = 250$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Check the Nyquist Criterion:**

$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{in} = 2 \cdot 145 = 290 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_s = 250 \text{ Hz} < 290 \text{ Hz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{ALIASING WILL OCCUR}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Integer Folding Index:**

$$k = \text{round}(f_{in} / f_s) = \text{round}(145 / 250) = \text{round}(0.58) = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Aliasing Formula:**

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - k \cdot f_s \vert = \vert 145 - 1 \cdot 250 \vert = \vert -105 \vert = 105 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The true 145 Hz motor unit spike will appear in the digital record as a **phantom 105 Hz component** — entirely fictitious, and clinically misleading.

**Step 5 — Verification via Discrete-Time Frequency:** The discrete-time angular frequency is:

$$\omega_d = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_{in}}{f_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{145}{250} = 2\pi \cdot 0.58 \text{ rad/sample}$$

Since the discrete spectrum is periodic with period $2\pi$ (or equivalently, $f_s$ in Hz), we map $0.58 f_s \to 0.58 f_s - 1 \cdot f_s = -0.42 f_s$, whose magnitude is $0.42 \cdot 250 = 105$ Hz. ✓ **Result confirmed.**

## Derivation 3: Condition for Aliasing-Free Reconstruction

**Statement:** A band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely and perfectly reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if and only if:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

**Proof Outline (Whittaker–Shannon–Kotelnikov):**

$$\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left( \frac{t - nT_s}{T_s} \right) \\
\text{where } \text{sinc}(u) &= \frac{\sin(\pi u)}{\pi u}
\end{aligned}$$

This interpolation series is **valid only** when the Nyquist condition is satisfied. Violating it means the $\text{sinc}$ basis functions become **linearly dependent**, and unique reconstruction is impossible.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
