# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define aliasing. State the Nyquist criterion for aliasing-free sampling of a band-limited signal.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Aliasing (1 Mark):** Aliasing is the phenomenon in which a continuous-time signal, when sampled at a rate insufficient to capture its highest frequency component, produces a digital signal whose frequency content is a corrupted (folded) version of the original. High-frequency components masquerade as low-frequency artifacts, rendering the original signal irrecoverable.

> **Nyquist Criterion (2 Marks):** For a band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$, aliasing-free sampling requires the sampling frequency $f_s$ to satisfy:
> $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
> The threshold $2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and the corresponding half-sampling frequency $f_s/2$ is the **Nyquist Frequency** — the maximum representable frequency in the digitized signal.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the role of an anti-aliasing filter in a biomedical signal acquisition system? Why must it be an analog filter placed *before* the ADC?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Role (2 Marks):** An anti-aliasing filter is a low-pass analog filter placed at the front-end of the acquisition system with a cutoff frequency at or below the Nyquist frequency ($f_c \leq f_s/2$). It eliminates any signal component whose frequency exceeds $f_s/2$, preventing such components from folding back into the desired baseband during sampling. In biomedical systems, it is critical for preserving diagnostic features (e.g., QRS morphology in ECG) and suppressing high-frequency noise (e.g., 50/60 Hz line interference harmonics, EMG cross-talk).

> **Why Analog and Pre-ADC (1 Mark):** The filter **must precede the ADC** because once a high-frequency component is sampled by the ADC, aliasing has already occurred and **cannot be undone** by any subsequent digital filter. By the Sampling Theorem, the analog input to the ADC must be strictly band-limited to $[0, f_s/2]$, which is guaranteed only by an analog filter operating on the continuous signal *before* discretization.

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)**

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a)** A sinusoidal EMG interference signal $x(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 420 \cdot t)$ volts is inadvertently picked up by an ECG acquisition system sampling at $f_s = 500$ Hz. **(i)** Determine whether aliasing will occur. **(ii)** Calculate the alias frequency that will appear in the digital record. **(iii)** Sketch (or describe) the frequency spectrum before and after sampling, clearly indicating the aliased component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the complete anti-aliasing strategy used in a modern ECG acquisition system. Discuss filter order selection, cutoff frequency choice, and the consequence of violating the Nyquist criterion on ST-segment and QRS-complex morphology. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**(i) Nyquist Check (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= 420 \text{ Hz} \\
f_{Nyquist} &= 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \cdot 420 = 840 \text{ Hz} \\
f_s &= 500 \text{ Hz} \\
\text{Since } f_s &= 500 < 840 = f_{Nyquist} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Aliasing WILL occur}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Alias Frequency Calculation (3 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
k &= \text{round}(f_{in} / f_s) = \text{round}(420 / 500) = \text{round}(0.84) = 1 \\
f_{alias} &= \vert f_{in} - k \cdot f_s \vert = \vert 420 - 1 \cdot 500 \vert = 80 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

The 420 Hz EMG interference will appear as an **80 Hz phantom component** in the digitized ECG.

**(iii) Spectral Description (2 Marks):**
- **Before Sampling:** A single spectral line at $\pm 420$ Hz with amplitude 2.5 V (using cosine convention).
- **After Sampling:** Replicas appear at $\pm 420, \pm 80, \pm 920, \pm 1420$ Hz, etc. Within the baseband $[-250, +250]$ Hz, we observe the original alias at $\pm 80$ Hz — the corrupted version of the true 420 Hz tone.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):** The acquisition chain is: **Electrode → Preamplifier (instrumentation amp) → Analog Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter → ADC → Digital Filter (optional) → Microprocessor/Display**.

**Filter Order Selection (2 Marks):** Higher-order filters (e.g., **4th-order Butterworth** or **Bessel**) provide steeper roll-off beyond $f_s/2$, maximizing attenuation of biomedical high-frequency interference. Butterworth is preferred for maximally flat passband; Bessel is preferred for linear phase (preserving waveform shape of QRS complex).

**Cutoff Frequency Choice (1 Mark):** Typically set at **$f_c = 0.45 \cdot f_s$ to $0.49 \cdot f_s$** to balance alias rejection and preservation of useful signal bandwidth (e.g., ECG clinical bandwidth is 0.05–150 Hz).

**Consequence of Violation (1 Mark):** If Nyquist is violated, the R-peak's high-frequency harmonics fold back, producing a **rounded, widened QRS** that mimics bundle branch block; P-waves may vanish (low-amplitude, high-frequency content is destroyed), and ST-segment elevation may be underestimated — leading to **misdiagnosis of myocardial infarction**.

---

### **QUESTION B — [Alternative Choice for Q.A]**
**(a)** Starting from the continuous-time Fourier transform of a sampled signal, derive the condition under which a band-limited signal can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples. State and prove the Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An EEG amplifier samples a signal at $f_s = 256$ Hz. A 50 Hz power-line interference is present. **(i)** Will it alias? **(ii)** If yes, what frequency will it appear at? **(iii)** Suggest two practical engineering solutions to suppress this interference without losing EEG information. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Derivation (5 Marks):** [Refer to Derivation 1 and 3 in Section 3 above.] The sampled spectrum is:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

If $f_{max} < f_s/2$, the replicas do not overlap, and a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_s/2$ and gain $T_s$ perfectly recovers $X(f)$. The interpolation formula in time domain is:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left( \frac{t - nT_s}{T_s} \right)$$

**Condition Statement (2 Marks):** Perfect reconstruction requires $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion). If violated, the sinc basis functions become linearly dependent, and reconstruction is non-unique — this is the mathematical proof of why aliasing is irreversible.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

**(i) Aliasing Check (2 Marks):**

$$f_{Nyquist} = f_s / 2 = 256 / 2 = 128 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_{in} = 50 \text{ Hz} < 128 \text{ Hz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{No aliasing of the fundamental.}$$

However, the **harmonics** of the 50 Hz interference (100 Hz, 150 Hz, 200 Hz, ...) may exceed 128 Hz and alias. Specifically, the 3rd harmonic (150 Hz) aliases to:

$$f_{alias} = \vert 150 - 256 \cdot 1 \vert = 106 \text{ Hz} \quad \text{(appears as 106 Hz artifact)}$$

**(ii) Solution Recommendations (3 Marks):**

1. **Narrow-band Notch Filter (50 Hz):** Insert a high-Q analog notch filter centered at 50 Hz (e.g., twin-T topology) at the preamplifier output. This removes the fundamental and a narrow band around it without attenuating adjacent EEG frequencies (alpha rhythm at 8–13 Hz, beta at 13–30 Hz remain intact).

2. **Right-Leg Driven Common-Mode Feedback (in Driven Right Leg, DRL circuit):** Actively cancel the 50 Hz common-mode voltage by inverting and feeding it back to the patient's right leg, reducing interference at the source rather than filtering it out post-hoc.

3. **(Optional) Increase $f_s$:** If a higher sampling rate (e.g., 512 Hz) is used, then $f_N = 256$ Hz, accommodating the 200 Hz 4th harmonic without aliasing — at the cost of greater data storage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mark Loss #1:** Many students write "$f_s > 2f_{max}$" using **strict inequality**. The correct statement is $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (equality is allowed and corresponds to critical sampling).
> - **Mark Loss #2:** Students often forget to take the **absolute value** when computing $f_{alias}$ — sign matters! Always report the magnitude, since frequency is non-negative.
> - **Mark Loss #3:** Confusing **Nyquist Rate** ($2 f_{max}$) with **Nyquist Frequency** ($f_s/2$). These are different quantities — KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> - **Mark Loss #4:** Stating that aliasing "can be removed using a digital filter" — this is **FALSE**. Aliasing is irreversible; only prevention is possible via analog anti-aliasing filters.
> - **Mark Loss #5:** Omitting units (Hz) in numerical answers. Always include units in KTU Board examinations.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Aliasing** = irreversible corruption of high-frequency signal content due to insufficient sampling.
- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$ (minimum sampling rate).
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N = f_s/2$ (maximum representable frequency).
- **Aliasing Formula:** $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - k \cdot f_s \vert$, where $k = \text{round}(f_{in}/f_s)$.
- **Sampling Theorem:** Perfect reconstruction via sinc interpolation **requires** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** must be **analog**, placed **before** the ADC, with cutoff $\leq f_s/2$.
- **Spectral replication** upon sampling creates replicas at every integer multiple of $f_s$.
- **Overlap** of replicas (when $f_{max} > f_s/2$) **folds** high frequencies into the baseband.
- **Biomedical Relevance:** ECG requires $f_s \geq 500$ Hz; EEG requires $f_s \geq 256$ Hz; EMG requires $f_s \geq 1000$ Hz.
- **Clinical Dangers:** Misdiagnosis of MI, epilepsy, and neuromuscular disorders due to aliased ECG/EEG/EMG.
- **No Post-Processing Fix:** Once aliased, the information is **permanently lost**.
- **Real-World Aliases:** Wagon wheel effect, helicopter blades, stroboscopic lighting, MRI wrap-around, Doppler ultrasound flow reversal.
- **Filter Types:** Butterworth (maximally flat), Bessel (linear phase), Chebyshev (steep roll-off with ripple) — choose based on biomedical signal shape preservation needs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Conversion Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Signal Conversion Circuits # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Signal Conversion Circuits? **Signal Conversion Circuits** are specialized electronic building blocks that transform biomedical signals from one physical/electrical domain into another, making them suitable for acquisition, processing, storage, and display by digital systems. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT633)**, these circuits form the critical *front-end* and *back-end* interfaces between the human body (biological source) and the digital signal processor (DSP/Microcontroller). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Signal conversion circuits refer to the ensemble of analog signal conditioning circuits (amplifiers, filters, isolation), data conversion circuits (ADC, DAC, Sample-and-Hold), and transducer interfacing circuits required to translate bioelectric events (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) into machine-readable digital codes and vice versa. The biomedical signal chain can be abstracted as: $$\text{Biological Source} \rightarrow \text{Transducer} \rightarrow \text{Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Filter} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{DSP} \rightarrow \text{DAC} \rightarrow \text{Display/Actuator}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator Booth" of a Hospital Imagine a busy international hospital where doctors from 50 different countries arrive speaking different languages. Before any consultation, every conversation must pass through a **translator booth**. The translator: - **Amplifies** the soft whisper of a patient, - **Filters** out the background noise of hospital corridors, - **Converts** spoken language into written text (analog → digital), - **Displays** the doctor's prescription on a screen (digital → analog). Signal conversion circuits are *exactly this translator booth* for biomedical signals. The **ECG signal** is too weak (typically **0.5 mV to 5 mV**), buried in noise (50 Hz power-line interference, muscle artifacts), and incompatible with digital processors. Without conversion circuits, a DSP chip cannot make sense of a heartbeat. ## 1.3 Key Sub-Circuits Classified by Function > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight — Three Functional Tiers of Signal Conversion:** | Tier | Function | Typical Circuits | |------|----------|------------------| | **Front-End (Analog In)** | Amplification & Isolation | Instrumentation Amplifier (INA), Isolation Amplifier, Charge Amplifier | | **Middle (Conditioning)** | Filtering & Sampling | Active Filters (LPF, HPF, BPF, Notch), Sample-and-Hold (S/H) | | **Back-End (Conversion)** | A/D and D/A | ADC (Flash, SAR, Dual-slope, Sigma-Delta), DAC (R-2R Ladder, Weighted Resistor) | ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard ECG amplitude:** **0.5 mV – 5 mV** (require gain of ~1000) - **EEG amplitude:** **10 µV – 100 µV** (require gain of ~10,000) - **Body impedance:** **~50 Ω to 5 kΩ** (varies with electrode type) - **Standard supply rails for biomedical op-amps:** **±5 V** or **±12 V** - **ADC resolution for clinical use:** **12-bit minimum** (4096 levels) - **Knee frequency / –3 dB point:** **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** for diagnostic ECG # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) — The Universal Building Block An op-amp is a **differential amplifier** with two inputs (inverting `−`, non-inverting `+`) and one output. In an *ideal* op-amp (used for theoretical derivations): - Open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$ - Input impedance $Z_{in} \to \infty$ - Output impedance $Z_{out} \to 0$ - Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) $\to \infty$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** In biomedical applications, the op-amp is rarely used open-loop. It is always used with **negative feedback** to set a precise, stable gain. ### 2.1.1 Inverting Amplifier The input signal is applied to the inverting terminal through $R_1$, and feedback is provided by $R_f$. **Closed-loop gain:** $$A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$ The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output. ### 2.1.2 Non-Inverting Amplifier The input is applied to the non-inverting terminal; the inverting terminal forms a voltage divider with $R_1$ and $R_f$. $$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$ ### 2.1.3 Voltage Follower (Unity-Gain Buffer) A special case where $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$, giving $A_v = 1$. Used for **impedance matching** between a high-impedance source (e.g., electrodes) and a low-impedance load. ## 2.2 Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) — The Heart of Biomedical Front-End The instrumentation amplifier is a **three-op-amp topology** that offers: - Extremely high input impedance (suitable for high-impedance bioelectrodes) - High **CMRR** (> **100 dB** for medical-grade INAs like AD620, INA128) - Precisely adjustable differential gain via a single external resistor $R_G$ **Differential gain of the standard 3-op-amp INA:** $$A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}$$ For a symmetric design where $R_1 = R_2$ and $R_3 = R_f$, this simplifies to: $$A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why INA over standard op-amp?** ECG/EEG signals are *floating* differential signals sitting on a large common-mode DC offset (half-cell potential of ~±300 mV). The INA rejects this common-mode voltage while amplifying only the differential component — a feat impossible with a single-ended op-amp. ## 2.3 Active Filters Passive filters (using R, L, C) are unsuitable for biomedical work because **inductors are bulky, lossy, and pick up 50 Hz hum**. Active filters use op-amps + RC networks to provide: - **Sharp roll-off** (high Q-factor) - **Gain** (no signal attenuation) - **No loading effect** (high input impedance, low output impedance) ### 2.3.1 First-Order Low-Pass Filter (LPF) — Sallen-Key Topology Used to remove high-frequency noise (e.g., muscle artifact above 150 Hz in ECG). $$H(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + sRC}$$ **Cut-off frequency (−3 dB point):** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ ### 2.3.2 First-Order High-Pass Filter (HPF) Removes baseline wander and DC offset (e.g., 0.05 Hz cutoff in ECG). $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ ### 2.3.3 Notch Filter (Band-Reject) — 50 Hz Power-Line Rejection Q-factor and notch depth: $$\text{Notch frequency } f_N = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}, \quad Q = \frac{1}{2(2 - A_0)}$$ ## 2.4 Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit Required between the analog signal and the ADC to **freeze** the analog voltage during conversion. A typical S/H uses a **MOSFET switch + holding capacitor + buffer op-amp**. **Acquisition time:** $$t_{acq} \approx \tau \ln\left(\frac{1}{1 - \epsilon}\right), \quad \text{where } \tau = R_{ON} \cdot C_H$$ ## 2.5 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) The ADC converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal into a discrete digital code. **Resolution / Quantization Step (LSB size):** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$ where $n$ = number of bits. **Quantization Error (maximum):** $$e_q = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}$$ **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB:** $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite:** A 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\,V$ has LSB = **1.22 mV** and maximum quantization error of **0.61 mV**. This is why clinical ADCs are at least 12-bit. ## 2.6 Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) ### 2.6.1 Weighted-Resistor DAC Output voltage for an $n$-bit DAC: $$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$$ ### 2.6.2 R-2R Ladder DAC (Most widely used) Output for an $n$-bit R-2R DAC: $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}, \quad D = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why R-2R is preferred:** Only two resistor values (R and 2R) are needed, making it **monolithic IC friendly** and avoiding the huge range of resistor values (R, 2R, 4R, 8R, …) required in the weighted-resistor design. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|----------------| | 1 | Inverting Amp Gain | $A_v = -R_f / R_1$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | Non-Inverting Amp Gain | $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_1$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | INA Differential Gain | $A_d = 1 + 2R_1 / R_G$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | 1st-Order LPF Cut-off | $f_c = 1 / (2\pi RC)$ | Hz | | 5 | 1st-Order HPF Cut-off | $f_c = 1 / (2\pi RC)$ | Hz | | 6 | Notch Filter Frequency | $f_N = 1 / (2\pi RC)$ | Hz | | 7 | S/H Acquisition Time | $t_{acq} = \tau \ln[1/(1-\epsilon)]$ | seconds | | 8 | ADC LSB (Resolution) | $\Delta = V_{ref} / 2^n$ | Volts | | 9 | ADC Quantization Error | $e_q = \pm V_{ref} / 2^{n+1}$ | Volts | | 10 | SQNR | $6.02n + 1.76$ | dB | | 11 | R-2R DAC Output | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^n$ | Volts | | 12 | CMRR | $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ | dB | | 13 | Slew Rate Limit | $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_p)$ | Hz | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Gain of a 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier **Problem Setup:** Derive the closed-loop differential gain $A_d = V_{out} / (V_1 - V_2)$ of the standard 3-op-amp INA shown below, where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are the inputs to op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$, and $R_G$ is the gain-setting resistor between them. ### Step 1 — Apply the Virtual Short Concept For op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$, the inverting input tracks the non-inverting input: $$V_{-}(A_1) = V_1, \quad V_{-}(A_2) = V_2$$ ### Step 2 — Voltage Across $R_G$ The two inverting inputs are connected across $R_G$. The voltage drop across $R_G$ is: $$V_{R_G} = V_1 - V_2$$ ### Step 3 — Current Through $R_G$ Since the op-amp input impedance is infinite, no current flows into the inputs. All current flowing through $R_G$ must also flow through the two feedback resistors $R_1$ of $A_1$ and $A_2$: $$I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$ ### Step 4 — Output Voltages of $A_1$ and $A_2$ The output of $A_1$ is lower than $V_1$ by the drop across its $R_1$: $$V_{o1} = V_1 + I \cdot R_1 = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$ The output of $A_2$ is higher than $V_2$: $$V_{o2} = V_2 - I \cdot R_1 = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$ ### Step 5 — Difference Stage (Op-Amp $A_3$) The third stage is a standard difference amplifier with $R_2$ and $R_3$: $$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o1} - V_{o2})$$ ### Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify Compute $V_{o1} - V_{o2}$: $$\begin{aligned} V_{o1} - V_{o2} &= \left[V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)\right] - \left[V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)\right] \\ &= (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2) \\ &= \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 7 — Final Closed-Form Result $$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ $$\boxed{A_d = \frac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)}$$ For a symmetric design ($R_2 = R_3$): $$A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Design Insight:** Only $R_G$ needs to be changed to set the gain. This makes the INA *single-resistor programmable*, which is why it is the de-facto standard for ECG/EEG front-ends. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Quantization Error and SQNR of an Ideal $n$-bit ADC ### Step 1 — Quantization Step Definition An ideal ADC uniformly divides its full-scale input range $V_{FS}$ into $2^n$ discrete levels. The size of one LSB is: $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$ ### Step 2 — Maximum Quantization Error The analog input can lie anywhere within a quantization interval. The maximum deviation from the true value is half an LSB: $$e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$ ### Step 3 — RMS Quantization Noise Assuming the error is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$: $$\begin{aligned} e_{q,rms}^2 &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, de \\ &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[\frac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} \\ &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2(\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} \end{aligned}$$ $$e_{q,rms} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n \sqrt{12}}$$ ### Step 4 — RMS Signal Power For a full-scale sinusoidal input with peak $V_{FS}/2$: $$P_{signal} = \frac{(V_{FS}/2)^2}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$ ### Step 5 — SQNR The ratio of signal power to quantization noise power is: $$\begin{aligned} \text{SQNR} &= \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{V_{FS}^2 / (12 \cdot 2^{2n})} \\ &= \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2n}}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 6 — Convert to Decibels $$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20n \log_{10}(2)$$ $$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Pattern:** A 12-bit ADC → SQNR ≈ **73.84 dB**. A 16-bit ADC → SQNR ≈ **98.08 dB**. Every additional bit adds ~6 dB of dynamic range. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Output Voltage of an R-2R Ladder DAC ### Step 1 — Binary-Weighted Current In an R-2R ladder, each bit $b_i$ controls a switch that connects node $i$ to either ground or $V_{ref}$. The Thevenin equivalent at the summing junction yields a binary-weighted current contribution: $$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{i+1} \cdot R}$$ ### Step 2 — Summation The total current entering the op-amp virtual ground is: $$I_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} I_i = \frac{V_{ref}}{R} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{i+1}}$$ ### Step 3 — Output via Op-Amp With feedback resistor $R_f = R$: $$V_{out} = -I_{total} \cdot R_f = -V_{ref} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{i+1}}$$ ### Step 4 — Final Form Define the digital word $D = \sum b_i 2^{n-1-i}$. Then: $$\boxed{V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}}$$ The sign inversion is removed by adding an inverting buffer for unipolar outputs. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: ECG Front-End Sizing > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Pattern (Dec 2023):** A 14-year-old patient's ECG has peak amplitude **3 mV** with a common-mode 50 Hz interference of **1.2 V**. The ADC accepts 0–5 V and has 10-bit resolution. Design the INA gain. **Step 1 — Required Input Range to ADC** $$V_{ADC,FS} = 5\,V$$ **Step 2 — ECG Peak After Amplification** $$V_{out,peak} = 0.9 \times V_{FS} = 4.5\,V \quad \text{(leave 10% headroom)}$$ **Step 3 — Required Gain** $$A_d = \frac{4.5\,V}{3\,mV} = 1500$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $R_G$** (with $R_1 = 25\,k\Omega$, $R_2 = R_3$) $$1500 = 1 + \frac{2(25\,k)}{R_G} \implies R_G = \frac{50\,k}{1499} \approx 33.4\,\Omega$$ **Step 5 — Verify CMRR Constraint** For 1.2 V common-mode to be reduced below the LSB: $$\text{LSB} = \frac{5\,V}{2^{10}} = 4.88\,mV$$ $$A_{cm} \leq \frac{4.88\,mV}{1.2\,V} = 4.07 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\text{CMRR} \geq 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1500}{4.07 \times 10^{-3}}\right) \approx 111.3\,dB$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A medical-grade INA such as **AD620** (CMRR ~ 100 dB at 1 kHz) or **INA128** (CMRR ~ 120 dB) is mandatory. General-purpose op-amps (LM741, ~70 dB) will *fail* this specification. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Biomedical Signal Conversion Chain The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a typical ECG acquisition system, from biological source to digital storage. Each op-amp and gain-setting resistor is shown explicitly. ```mermaid flowchart TB V1[Input V1] --> R1a[Resistor R1] R1a --> A1[Op-Amp A1] RG[Gain Resistor RG] --> A1 RG --> A2[Op-Amp A2] R1b[Resistor R1] --> A2 V2[Input V2] --> R1b A1 --> R2a[Resistor R2] A2 --> R2b[Resistor R2] R2a --> A3[Op-Amp A3 Difference Stage] R2b --> A3 R3a[Feedback R3] --> A3 R3b[Feedback R3] --> A3 A3 --> VOUT[Output Vout Differential] style V1 fill:#e0ffff style V2 fill:#e0ffff style A1 fill:#ffe4b5 style A2 fill:#ffe4b5 style A3 fill:#ffe4b5 style VOUT fill:#90ee90 style RG fill:#ffb6c1 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: R-2R Ladder DAC Architecture This diagram shows how each bit $b_i$ switches a node between $V_{ref}$ and ground, with the binary-weighted current summed at the op-amp virtual ground. ```mermaid flowchart LR B3[Bit b3 MSB] --> S3[Switch S3] B2[Bit b2] --> S2[Switch S2] B1[Bit b1] --> S1[Switch S1] B0[Bit b0 LSB] --> S0[Switch S0] VREF[Vref] --> S3 VREF --> S2 VREF --> S1 VREF --> S0 GND[GND] --> S3 GND --> S2 GND --> S1 GND --> S0 S3 --> R3[Resistor 2R] R3 --> R2X[Resistor 2R] R2X --> R1X[Resistor 2R] R1X --> R0X[Resistor 2R] R0X --> VIRT[Virtual Ground] S2 --> R2X S1 --> R1X S0 --> R0X VIRT --> OP[Op-Amp Summing] RFB[Feedback R] --> OP OP --> VDAC[Analog Output Vout] style B3 fill:#ffb6c1 style B2 fill:#ffb6c1 style B1 fill:#ffb6c1 style B0 fill:#ffb6c1 style VREF fill:#ffe4b5 style OP fill:#e0ffff style VDAC fill:#90ee90 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: SAR ADC Functional Flow The **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** is the most common ADC architecture for biomedical applications due to its excellent trade-off between speed, resolution, and power consumption. ```mermaid flowchart LR VIN[Analog Input Vin] --> SH[Sample and Hold] SH --> CMP[Comparator] CMP --> SAR[SAR Logic] SAR --> DACINT[Internal DAC] DACINT --> CMP SAR --> DOUT[Digital Output N bits] style VIN fill:#ffe4e1 style SH fill:#fff0f5 style CMP fill:#e0ffff style SAR fill:#fafad2 style DACINT fill:#e6e6fa style DOUT fill:#90ee90 ``` ## 4.5 Functional Comparison: ADC Architectures ```mermaid flowchart TB ADC[ADC Classification] --> FLASH[Flash ADC] ADC --> SAR[SAR ADC] ADC --> DUAL[Dual-Slope ADC] ADC --> SIGMA[Sigma-Delta ADC] FLASH --> F1[Speed: Fastest] FLASH --> F2[Resolution: 4-8 bits] FLASH --> F3[Use: Video, Oscilloscopes] SAR --> S1[Speed: Medium] SAR --> S2[Resolution: 12-18 bits] SAR --> S3[Use: ECG, Data Acquisition] DUAL --> D1[Speed: Slow] DUAL --> D2[Resolution: 16-24 bits] DUAL --> D3[Use: Digital Multimeters] SIGMA --> SG1[Speed: Medium-Slow] SIGMA --> SG2[Resolution: 16-24 bits] SIGMA --> SG3[Use: Audio, High-Precision Bio] style ADC fill:#ffe4b5 style FLASH fill:#ffe4e1 style SAR fill:#e0ffff style DUAL fill:#fff0f5 style SIGMA fill:#f0fff0 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between an instrumentation amplifier and a general-purpose operational amplifier in the context of biomedical signal acquisition.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | General-Purpose Op-Amp | Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) | |-----------|------------------------|----------------------------------| | Input configuration | Single-ended or differential (low $Z_{in}$) | True differential with very high $Z_{in}$ | | CMRR | ~70–90 dB | ~100–140 dB | | Gain setting | Two resistors (matched requirement) | Single external resistor $R_G$ | | Suitable for | General amplification | Biomedical front-ends (ECG, EEG) | The INA is **preferred** for biomedical use because of its high CMRR (essential to reject the half-cell potential of electrodes), high input impedance (essential to not load high-impedance electrode-tissue interfaces), and single-resistor gain programmability. *(3 marks — Full marks for tabular comparison + one-line justification)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - July 2023]` **What is the resolution of a 12-bit ADC with a reference voltage of 5 V? Also compute the maximum quantization error.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Resolution (LSB):** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} = 1.22 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum Quantization Error:** $$e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm 0.61 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3 — SQNR (bonus):** $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 73.84 \text{ dB}$$ *(3 marks — 1.5 marks for LSB, 1.5 marks for quantization error)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Module 1 Choice A **Q.A (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Derive its closed-loop differential gain. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (2 marks)** A 3-op-amp INA consists of: - **First stage:** Two op-amps ($A_1$, $A_2$) configured as a high-impedance differential buffer with gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs. - **Second stage:** A unity-gain difference amplifier ($A_3$) that subtracts the two buffer outputs and provides the single-ended output. **Step 2 — Gain Derivation (5 marks)** Using the virtual short principle at $A_1$ and $A_2$: $$V_{-}(A_1) = V_1, \quad V_{-}(A_2) = V_2$$ Current through $R_G$: $$I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$ Outputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$: $$V_{o1} = V_1 + I \cdot R_1 = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$ $$V_{o2} = V_2 - I \cdot R_1 = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$ Difference: $$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ Final output from difference stage $A_3$: $$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o1} - V_{o2}) = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ $$\boxed{A_d = \frac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)}$$ --- **Q.A (b) Explain the working of a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC with a block diagram. A 10-bit SAR ADC has a reference of 4.096 V. Determine its resolution and the digital output for an analog input of 2.5 V. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — SAR ADC Working (4 marks)** A SAR ADC contains: - A **Sample-and-Hold** circuit - A **Comparator** - An **internal DAC** (typically R-2R ladder) - **SAR logic** register **Working principle:** 1. The analog input is sampled and held. 2. The SAR sets the MSB to 1, all other bits 0. The internal DAC converts this trial code to an analog voltage $V_{DAC}$. 3. The comparator checks if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$. 4. If **YES**, the MSB remains 1; if **NO**, the MSB is reset to 0. 5. The process repeats for the next bit. After $n$ cycles, all $n$ bits are resolved. 6. Total conversion time = $n \times T_{clock}$ (where $T_{clock}$ is one bit-trial period). **Step 2 — Resolution Calculation (1.5 marks)** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{4.096}{2^{10}} = \frac{4.096}{1024} = 4 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3 — Digital Output for $V_{in} = 2.5\,V$ (1.5 marks)** $$D = \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta} = \frac{2.5}{0.004} = 625_{10}$$ Convert 625 to 10-bit binary: $$625 = 512 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 1 = 2^9 + 2^6 + 2^5 + 2^4 + 2^0$$ $$D = 1001110001_2$$ --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - July 2023]` — Module 1 Choice B **Q.B (a) With neat diagrams, explain the working of a Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit. Why is it necessary in a data acquisition system? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Block Diagram (2 marks)** A typical S/H circuit consists of: - A **MOSFET switch** (or JFET) - A **Hold capacitor** $C_H$ (10 pF to 1 nF) - A **High-input-impedance buffer amplifier** (typically a JFET op-amp like LF398) **Step 2 — Two Operating Modes (3 marks)** **Sample Mode (Switch ON):** The MOSFET conducts, connecting the input signal to $C_H$. The capacitor charges to $V_{in}$ through the on-resistance $R_{ON}$ of the switch. The buffer outputs the input voltage directly. $$\tau = R_{ON} \cdot C_H$$ **Hold Mode (Switch OFF):** The MOSFET opens. The capacitor retains its charge (no discharge path due to the high-impedance buffer). The output remains frozen at the last sampled value. **Acquisition time** (to settle within $\epsilon$ of final value): $$t_{acq} = \tau \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-\epsilon}\right)$$ For $\epsilon = 0.1\%$: $t_{acq} \approx 7\tau$. **Step 3 — Necessity in Data Acquisition (2 marks)** - ADCs require a **constant input voltage** during the conversion window (which can be microseconds for SAR ADCs). - If the input changes during conversion, the output code is **erroneous** (aperture error). - The S/H "freezes" the rapidly varying biomedical signal (e.g., ECG with sharp QRS complexes) so that the ADC sees a stable voltage. - It also enables **multiplexing**: multiple channels can share one ADC by sequentially sampling and holding each. --- **Q.B (b) Explain the architecture and working of an R-2R ladder DAC. A 8-bit R-2R DAC has $V_{ref} = 5\,V$. Calculate the output voltage for the digital input `10110100`. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Architecture (2.5 marks)** The R-2R ladder uses only two resistor values (**R** and **2R**). Each bit $b_i$ controls a switch that connects the corresponding node to either $V_{ref}$ (bit = 1) or ground (bit = 0). **Key property:** Looking into any node of the ladder, the Thevenin resistance is always **R**, regardless of switch positions. This makes the binary-weighted current contribution simple. **Step 2 — Working (2.5 marks)** For bit $b_i$, the current contribution is: $$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{i+1} \cdot R}$$ Total current: $$I_{total} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{i+1}}$$ The summing op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f = R$ produces: $$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{i+1}} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical Calculation (2 marks)** For input `10110100` (MSB to LSB): $$D = 1 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 1 \cdot 2^4 + 0 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0$$ $$D = 128 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 180$$ Output voltage: $$V_{out} = -5 \cdot \frac{180}{256} = -3.516 \text{ V}$$ If unipolar output is required, the sign inversion is removed by a final inverting buffer, giving **+3.516 V**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign of output:** Many students forget the negative sign in the DAC output equation. The R-2R ladder is an *inverting* configuration. (-1 mark typical) > 2. **Bit ordering:** When computing $D$ from binary, always read **MSB to LSB** from left to right. Reversed bit order gives a wrong answer. (-1 mark) > 3. **R-2R vs Weighted:** Don't use the weighted-resistor formula for an R-2R DAC. They are different architectures. (-2 marks if confused) > 4. **INA gain formula:** Many students write $A_d = 1 + R_f/R_1$ (the non-inverting amp formula) for the INA. The correct formula is $A_d = 1 + 2R_1/R_G$. (-2 marks) > 5. **ADC resolution units:** Always state LSB in **Volts** with the correct prefix (mV, µV). Forgetting units loses 0.5 mark. > 6. **S/H hold droop:** A real S/H has capacitor leakage; mention this in the answer for full marks in 'necessity' part. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Signal Conversion Circuits** - **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** Three op-amp topology, $A_d = 1 + 2R_1/R_G$. Use for all differential biomedical signals. - **CMRR:** Must be > 100 dB for medical-grade. Rejects 50 Hz common-mode and DC half-cell potentials. - **Active Filters > Passive Filters:** Provide gain, sharp roll-off, no inductors. Sallen-Key and Multiple-Feedback (MFB) are standard topologies. - **Cutoff Frequency:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ for both LPF and HPF (first-order). - **Notch Filter:** Specifically designed at 50 Hz / 60 Hz for power-line rejection in ECG/EEG. - **Sample-and-Hold:** Required between filter output and ADC. Uses MOSFET switch + hold capacitor + buffer. Aperture time must be smaller than ADC conversion time. - **ADC Resolution:** $\Delta = V_{ref}/2^n$. More bits → finer resolution, higher SQNR. - **SQNR Formula:** $6.02n + 1.76$ dB — every extra bit adds ~6 dB of dynamic range. - **SAR ADC:** Most common in biomedical; $n$ clock cycles for $n$-bit conversion; medium speed, 12–18 bits. - **Flash ADC:** Fastest, but limited to 4–8 bits due to exponential resistor count. - **Dual-Slope ADC:** Slow but very accurate (16–24 bits); used in digital multimeters. - **Sigma-Delta ADC:** High resolution (16–24 bits), used in audio and high-precision bio signals. - **R-2R Ladder DAC:** Only two resistor values; ideal for IC integration. $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot D/2^n$ (with sign inversion). - **Weighted-Resistor DAC:** Simple but impractical for high resolution due to huge resistor spread ($R$ to $2^{n-1}R$). - **Slew Rate:** Limits the maximum frequency of an op-amp: $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_p)$. - **Typical ECG front-end gains:** ~1000 (ECG), ~10,000 (EEG), ~100 (EMG). - **Standard medical ADC:** Minimum **12 bits** for diagnostic ECG; **16 bits** preferred for research-grade EEG. - **Safety:** Always include **isolation amplifiers** (AD210, ISO124) between the patient and the mains-powered equipment to prevent microshock hazards.

topics.title: Principle of Noise Canceller Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principle of Noise Canceller Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC) Model:** An adaptive noise cancelling system is a dual-input closed-loop signal processing architecture that uses an adaptive filter driven by a *reference* noise input to estimate and subtract the correlated noise component contaminating a *primary* input, thereby recovering the underlying desired biomedical signal. It was formally introduced by **Widrow and Glover (1975)** as an alternative to fixed filtering when the noise spectrum is unknown, non-stationary, or time-varying.

In biomedical signal processing, the **Principle of Noise Canceller Model** is the foundational block on which virtually every modern biosignal denoising subsystem (fetal ECG extraction, mains interference rejection, EMG artifact removal, etc.) is built.

### 1.2 The Two Canonical Inputs

| Input | Notation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Input** | $d(n)$ | Desired signal $s(n)$ corrupted by additive noise $n_0(n)$ |
| **Reference Input** | $x(n)$ | Noise $n_1(n)$ correlated with $n_0(n)$ but uncorrelated with $s(n)$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Active Headphones" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of the **active noise-cancelling headphones** you wear on an aeroplane. The cabin engine noise enters your ear as the *primary input* (signal + noise). Tiny microphones on the earcup pick up *only* that engine rumble — this is the *reference input*. A tiny processor calculates an **anti-noise** wave that is the mirror image (180° out of phase) of the engine noise and plays it through the speaker. The two waves collide in your ear canal and **cancel each other**, leaving the pilot's announcement (the "desired signal") crystal-clear. The adaptive noise canceller does the *exact same thing* in software, for ECG, EEG, and EMG signals.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** improvement is typically in the range of **20 – 40 dB** for well-tuned biomedical ANC systems.
- The convergence parameter (step size) $\mu$ must satisfy the **stability bound**: $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$ where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the reference autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$.
- The output error $e(n)$ is simultaneously the **system output** and the **adaptation error** — this duality is the genius of the model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How the Model Works

The Adaptive Noise Canceller is structured as a **two-channel adaptive filter** with the following logical flow:

1. **Primary Channel Reception** — The corrupted signal $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ enters the summer junction.
2. **Reference Channel Sensing** — A second sensor picks up noise $x(n) = n_1(n)$ that is *spatially* or *temporally* correlated with $n_0(n)$.
3. **Adaptive Filtering** — The reference $x(n)$ is convolved with the adaptive filter's impulse response $w(n)$ to yield an estimate $y(n)$ of the contaminating noise $n_0(n)$.
4. **Subtractive Cancellation** — The summer computes $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
5. **Weight Update via LMS** — The error $e(n)$ is fed back to the LMS adaptation engine, which updates filter coefficients to **minimise the mean square error (MSE)**.
6. **Convergence to Clean Signal** — At steady state, $y(n) \approx n_0(n)$, so $e(n) \approx s(n)$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why two inputs?** Because if noise were *known*, we could simply subtract it. The reference provides a *proxy* for the unknown noise.
- **Why adapt?** Because biomedical noise (motion artifact, baseline wander, mains hum) is **non-stationary** — its spectrum drifts with patient movement, respiration, electrode impedance changes, etc.
- **Why subtract rather than filter?** Because fixed bandpass filters would also destroy legitimate signal content overlapping the noise band (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum sits inside the QRS spectrum).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All high-yield formulas for the **Principle of Noise Canceller Model** are consolidated below. This is the single most important reference block for KTU ESE preparation.

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary input | $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ | Corrupted signal at summer input |
| 2 | Reference input | $x(n) = n_1(n)$ | Noise-only correlated reference |
| 3 | Filter output | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Estimate of $n_0(n)$ |
| 4 | System output (error) | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | Recovered signal approximation |
| 5 | LMS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Coefficient adaptation law |
| 6 | Mean square error | $J(n) = E\!\left[\,e^{2}(n)\,\right]$ | Cost function minimised by LMS |
| 7 | Optimal Wiener solution | $\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\,\mathbf{r}_{xd}$ | Closed-form (theoretical) weights |
| 8 | LMS stability bound | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$ | Convergence condition |
| 9 | Normalised LMS (NLMS) step | $\mu(n) = \dfrac{\tilde{\mu}}{\epsilon + \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)}$ | Variable step size for stability |
| 10 | Decorrelation requirement | $E[s(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0 \quad \forall k$ | Reference must be signal-uncorrelated |
| 11 | Noise correlation requirement | $E[n_0(n)\,x(n-k)] \neq 0$ | Reference must be noise-correlated |
| 12 | Excess MSE (Widrow) | $J_{\mathrm{ex}} \approx \dfrac{\mu}{2}\,\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})\,J_{\min}$ | Misadjustment due to gradient noise |
| 13 | Time constant of LMS | $\tau_{i} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\mu\lambda_{i}}$ | Mode-$i$ convergence speed |
| 14 | Misadjustment | $\mathcal{M} = \dfrac{J_{\mathrm{ex}}}{J_{\min}} \approx \dfrac{\mu}{2}\,\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})$ | Steady-state normalised error |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This model is the backbone of clinical-grade **fetal ECG monitors** (cancel maternal ECG from abdominal lead), **pacemaker artefact suppression**, **electrosurgical unit (ESU) interference rejection**, and **wearable Holter pre-processors**. The same architecture, with a *speech* reference input, is used in **aircraft cockpit communications** and **hands-free car kits**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LMS Adaptation Law for the Noise Canceller

We begin by stating the **cost function** that the adaptive filter must minimise — the instantaneous squared error. The derivation is presented exhaustively so every algebraic transition is auditable for KTU valuation purposes.

**Step 1 — Define the primary signal entering the summer.**

The primary input received by the noise canceller is the desired biomedical signal buried in additive environmental noise:

$$
d(n) \;=\; s(n) \;+\; n_0(n)
$$

where $s(n)$ is the clean signal of interest (e.g., fetal ECG) and $n_0(n)$ is the unwanted noise (e.g., maternal ECG leakage).

**Step 2 — Define the adaptive filter output.**

The adaptive FIR filter of order $N$ takes the reference noise vector $\mathbf{x}(n)$ and produces a weighted sum:

$$
\mathbf{x}(n) \;=\; \bigl[\,x(n),\; x(n-1),\; \dots,\; x(n-N+1)\,\bigr]^{T}
$$

$$
\mathbf{w}(n) \;=\; \bigl[\,w_0(n),\; w_1(n),\; \dots,\; w_{N-1}(n)\,\bigr]^{T}
$$

$$
y(n) \;=\; \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n)\,x(n-k)
$$

This is the *best linear estimate* of $n_0(n)$ given the reference noise.

**Step 3 — Form the system output as the cancellation error.**

The summer in the noise canceller subtracts the filter output from the primary input:

$$
e(n) \;=\; d(n) \;-\; y(n) \;=\; s(n) \;+\; n_0(n) \;-\; \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

Crucially, **$e(n)$ is BOTH the system output AND the error signal used for adaptation**. This dual role is the architectural cleverness of the model.

**Step 4 — Define the instantaneous mean-square cost function.**

To drive the adaptation, we define the squared error as our cost:

$$
J(n) \;=\; e^{2}(n)
$$

The LMS algorithm uses this *instantaneous* estimate (dropping the expectation operator of the true MSE) to perform stochastic gradient descent.

**Step 5 — Compute the gradient of the cost with respect to the weight vector.**

Differentiating $J(n) = e^{2}(n)$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}(n)$:

$$
\frac{\partial J(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} \;=\; 2\,e(n)\,\frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)}
$$

Now, since $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$, we have:

$$
\frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} \;=\; -\,\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) \;=\; -\,2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the steepest-descent update rule.**

The standard gradient-descent weight update is:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) \;=\; \mathbf{w}(n) \;-\; \mu\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n)
$$

Inserting the LMS gradient approximation:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) \;=\; \mathbf{w}(n) \;-\; \mu\!\left(-\,2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Final LMS adaptation law for the noise canceller.**

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) \;=\; \mathbf{w}(n) \;+\; 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic summary:** The reference $x(n)$ is correlated with $n_0(n)$ but not with $s(n)$. Therefore, the LMS algorithm — driven by the *combined* error $e(n) \approx s(n) + \text{residual noise}$ — adjusts the weights to **minimise only the noise component**, because the signal component $s(n)$ is statistically invisible to the reference and hence does not bias the weight solution. This is the mathematical *why* the noise canceller works.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Optimum Wiener Weights (Theoretical Limit)

If the autocorrelation $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{T}]$ and cross-correlation $\mathbf{r}_{xd} = E[\mathbf{x}\,d]$ were known exactly, the optimum weights would satisfy the **Wiener-Hopf equation**:

$$
\mathbf{R}_{xx}\,\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}} \;=\; \mathbf{r}_{xd}
$$

Solving:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}} \;=\; \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\,\mathbf{r}_{xd}
$$

The minimum MSE achieved is:

$$
J_{\min} \;=\; \sigma_{d}^{2} \;-\; \mathbf{r}_{xd}^{T}\,\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}}
$$

LMS asymptotically approaches this Wiener optimum if the step-size condition holds.

## 3.3 Summary of Key Assumptions in the Model

| Assumption | Mathematical Statement | Real-World Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Signal–reference uncorrelated | $E[s(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0$ | Reference sensor must not "see" the desired signal |
| Noise–reference correlated | $E[n_0(n)\,x(n-k)] \neq 0$ | The two noise sources must share a physical coupling |
| Wide-sense stationarity (local) | $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ slowly varying | LMS tracks slow drifts; RLS needed for faster non-stationarity |
| Zero-mean reference | $E[x(n)] = 0$ | AC-coupling of the reference channel assumed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Adaptive Noise Canceller

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete signal-flow and feedback interaction of the **Principle of Noise Canceller Model**, in full alignment with the architecture introduced by Widrow & Glover.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcS["Desired Signal s(n)"] --> sumA{"Primary Summer"}
    srcN0["Primary Noise n0(n)"] --> sumA
    sumA --> primIn["Primary Input d(n) = s(n) + n0(n)"]
    primIn --> sumB{"Cancellation Summer"}
    sumB --> errOut["Error Output e(n)"]
    errOut --> outTerm["Recovered Signal Estimate"]
    errOut --> adaptEng["LMS Adaptation Engine"]
    adaptEng -->|"Updates weights w(n+1)"| adaptFilt["Adaptive FIR Filter w(n)"]
    srcN1["Reference Noise n1(n)"] --> refIn["Reference Input x(n)"]
    refIn --> adaptFilt
    adaptFilt --> filtOut["Filter Output y(n) = wT x(n)"]
    filtOut --> sumB
    adaptFilt -.->|"w(n) feedforward"| adaptEng
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**

- The **Primary Summer** combines the clean biosignal with environmental noise.
- The **Cancellation Summer** performs the critical subtraction that yields the recovered signal.
- The **LMS Adaptation Engine** is the *brain* — it observes $e(n)$, correlates it with $x(n)$, and nudges the weights.
- The dashed *feedforward* arrow from filter to engine represents the *coefficient readout* required for the update equation.

## 4.2 Sequential Operation Flowchart (Algorithm Pseudocode in Words)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA["Initialise w(0) = 0, choose mu"] --> initB["Set filter order N"]
    initB --> readX["Read reference x(n)"]
    readX --> compY["Compute y(n) = wT x(n)"]
    compY --> compE["Compute e(n) = d(n) - y(n)"]
    compE --> emit["Emit e(n) as clean-signal estimate"]
    emit --> updW["Update w(n+1) = w(n) + 2 mu e(n) x(n)"]
    updW --> chkConv{"Converged or max-iter?"}
    chkConv -->|"No"| readX
    chkConv -->|"Yes"| stopOp["Stop adaptation, lock weights"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram rationale:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard, all node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `initA`, `compY`, `updW`) and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords. Labels use only raw uppercase text — no bold/italic markup — ensuring clean compilation in any Mermaid renderer (GitHub, VSCode, Obsidian).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1.** Define the *Principle of the Adaptive Noise Canceller Model*. State the two fundamental requirements on the reference input for proper cancellation to occur.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC) is a dual-input adaptive filtering system in which a *primary* input $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ containing the desired signal $s(n)$ corrupted by noise $n_0(n)$ is cleaned by subtracting a filtered *reference* noise $x(n)$ that is correlated with $n_0(n)$. The adaptive filter adjusts its weights using the LMS rule $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ so that the output $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ approaches $s(n)$.

**Two requirements on the reference:**

1. **Uncorrelated with the desired signal:** $E[s(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0$ for all lags $k$.
2. **Correlated with the primary noise:** $E[n_0(n)\,x(n-k)] \neq 0$ for some lag $k$.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — requirement 1, 1 mark — requirement 2)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]

State the LMS algorithm update equation and the **stability bound** on the step size $\mu$ for the Adaptive Noise Canceller. What happens if $\mu$ is too large?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The LMS weight update is:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) \;=\; \mathbf{w}(n) \;+\; 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

**Stability bound:**

$$
0 \;<\; \mu \;<\; \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}
$$

where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{T}]$.

If $\mu$ is too large (exceeds the bound), the filter weights **diverge**, the mean-square error **grows unbounded**, and the system becomes **unstable** — the noise canceller injects *more* noise than it removes.

*(1 mark — equation, 1 mark — bound, 1 mark — instability consequence)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Principle of the Adaptive Noise Canceller Model**. Derive the LMS weight-update equation from the instantaneous squared-error cost function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fetal ECG monitor uses an ANC to cancel the maternal ECG from an abdominal lead. The maternal ECG is the *primary noise* $n_0(n)$ and a chest-lead recording of the mother's heartbeat serves as the *reference* $x(n)$. The filter is a 2-tap adaptive FIR with $w_0 = 0.4$, $w_1 = 0.2$, and reference samples $x(n) = 0.5$, $x(n-1) = 0.3$. If the abdominal signal $d(n) = 1.2$, compute the noise estimate $y(n)$, the system output $e(n)$, and the updated weights using $\mu = 0.05$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the standard Widrow-Glover ANC block diagram with *primary input* entering one port of a summer, *reference input* entering the adaptive filter, filter output entering the *other* port of the summer, and the error fed back to the LMS engine. (See Section 4.1 of these notes for the reference schematic.)

2. **Derivation (4 marks):** State $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$, define $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$, form $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$, take $J(n) = e^{2}(n)$, compute gradient $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$, and apply steepest descent to obtain $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$. (Full derivation appears in Section 3.1 above.)

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute the filter output $y(n)$:**

$$
y(n) \;=\; w_0\,x(n) \;+\; w_1\,x(n-1) \;=\; (0.4)(0.5) \;+\; (0.2)(0.3)
$$

$$
y(n) \;=\; 0.20 \;+\; 0.06 \;=\; 0.26
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the system output $e(n)$:**

$$
e(n) \;=\; d(n) \;-\; y(n) \;=\; 1.2 \;-\; 0.26 \;=\; 0.94
$$

**Step 3 — Update weight $w_0$:**

$$
w_0(n+1) \;=\; w_0(n) \;+\; 2\mu\,e(n)\,x(n) \;=\; 0.4 \;+\; 2(0.05)(0.94)(0.5)
$$

$$
w_0(n+1) \;=\; 0.4 \;+\; 0.047 \;=\; 0.447
$$

**Step 4 — Update weight $w_1$:**

$$
w_1(n+1) \;=\; w_1(n) \;+\; 2\mu\,e(n)\,x(n-1) \;=\; 0.2 \;+\; 2(0.05)(0.94)(0.3)
$$

$$
w_1(n+1) \;=\; 0.2 \;+\; 0.0282 \;=\; 0.2282
$$

**Final Answer:** $y(n) = 0.26$, $e(n) = 0.94$, $w_0 = 0.447$, $w_1 = 0.2282$. *(1 mark each for filter output, system output, both updated weights, plus 3 marks for showing all working)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **optimum Wiener weight vector** $\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}}$ for the Adaptive Noise Canceller from the mean-square error cost function $J = E[e^{2}(n)]$. Explain why the LMS algorithm approaches this optimum asymptotically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **LMS** and the **Normalised LMS (NLMS)** algorithms for the noise canceller with respect to: (i) stability, (ii) convergence speed for coloured reference inputs, and (iii) computational complexity. State the NLMS weight update. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Express MSE in matrix form:**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) \;=\; E\!\left[\bigl(d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)\bigr)^{2}\right] \;=\; E[d^{2}] \;-\; 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{xd} \;+\; \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\,\mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 2 — Set gradient to zero:**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J \;=\; -\,2\,\mathbf{r}_{xd} \;+\; 2\,\mathbf{R}_{xx}\,\mathbf{w} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Wiener-Hopf equation:**

$$
\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}} \;=\; \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\,\mathbf{r}_{xd}
$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic optimality of LMS (3 marks):** The LMS algorithm uses the *stochastic* gradient $-2 e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$, which is an unbiased estimate of the true gradient (since $E[-2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] = -2\mathbf{r}_{xd} + 2\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}$). By the *Robbins-Monro stochastic approximation theorem*, provided the step size satisfies $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$ and decays slowly ($\sum \mu = \infty$, $\sum \mu^{2} < \infty$), $\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{LMS}}(n) \to \mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}}$ in the mean-square sense as $n \to \infty$.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Stability (2 marks):** LMS stability depends on the absolute step size $\mu$ relative to $\lambda_{\max}$, which is *unknown* for non-stationary biomedical signals. NLMS normalises the step size by the instantaneous reference power $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{x}$, making it **inherently stable** for any $\tilde{\mu} \in (0, 2)$.

**(ii) Convergence for coloured inputs (2 marks):** LMS convergence speed is governed by $\tau_i \approx 1/(2\mu\lambda_i)$. For coloured references (typical in ECG, EEG), the eigenvalue spread $\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ is large, causing **slow modes** in the LMS transient. NLMS normalises out this eigenvalue spread, giving **uniformly faster convergence** for coloured biomedical references.

**(iii) Complexity (1 mark):** LMS requires $2N+1$ multiplications per sample. NLMS requires $3N+2$ (extra division and energy computation), a marginal increase.

**NLMS weight update (2 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) \;=\; \mathbf{w}(n) \;+\; \frac{\tilde{\mu}}{\epsilon + \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)}\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small positive constant to prevent division by zero.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in Noise Canceller questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the reference requirement** — Many students write that the reference must be "uncorrelated with the noise." This is the *opposite* of the truth. The reference **MUST** be correlated with the primary noise. Marks lost: **1–2**.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the LMS update — Writing $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ instead of $+ 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ is a common transcription slip. Marks lost: **1**.
> 3. **Omitting the derivation steps** — KTU 2024 scheme values *worked derivations* over final answers. Skipping the gradient computation $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} e^{2}(n) = -2 e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ forfeits **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating the stability bound** explicitly — A bare LMS equation without $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$ is considered incomplete by KTU examiners. Marks lost: **1**.
> 5. **Mixing up $e(n)$ as output vs. error** — The duality that $e(n)$ is BOTH the system output AND the adaptation error is the central insight; students who don't articulate this lose conceptual marks. Marks lost: **1**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-revision checklist for the Principle of Noise Canceller Model:**

- ⭐ The ANC is a **dual-input** adaptive filter: *primary* (signal + noise) and *reference* (noise only).
- ⭐ Primary input: $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$. Reference: $x(n) = n_1(n)$.
- ⭐ Reference MUST be **correlated with $n_0(n)$** AND **uncorrelated with $s(n)$**.
- ⭐ Filter output: $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$. Cancellation error (system output): $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
- ⭐ LMS update: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- ⭐ Stability bound: $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$.
- ⭐ NLMS update: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \dfrac{\tilde{\mu}}{\epsilon + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{x}}\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ — preferred for biomedical signals.
- ⭐ Wiener optimum: $\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{xd}$.
- ⭐ Time constant: $\tau_i \approx 1/(2\mu\lambda_i)$ — slow for small $\lambda_i$ (coloured inputs).
- ⭐ Misadjustment: $\mathcal{M} \approx (\mu/2)\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})$.
- ⭐ Applications: **fetal ECG** (cancel maternal ECG), **50/60 Hz mains hum** rejection, **motion artifact** removal, **EMG-ECG crosstalk** suppression, **pacemaker spike** detection.
- ⭐ Original reference: **Widrow & Glover (1975)** — adaptive noise cancelling; do **not** confuse with Widrow-Hoff LMS (1960).
- ⭐ For Part B derivations, always show the **gradient step** and the **steepest-descent substitution** explicitly — KTU examiners award full credit only for complete working.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 60 Hz Adaptive Cancelling Using a Sine Wave Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 60 Hz Adaptive Cancelling Using a Sine Wave Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**60 Hz Adaptive Cancelling Using a Sine Wave Model** is a classical adaptive noise-cancellation (ANC) technique used in biomedical signal processing to remove powerline interference (PLI) — specifically the **50 Hz / 60 Hz** sinusoidal contamination from physiological recordings such as **ECG, EMG, and EEG**.

The fundamental idea is to represent the interfering sinusoid as a parametric model of known angular frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / F_s$, where $f_0 = \mathbf{60\ Hz}$ and $F_s$ is the sampling frequency. Two reference sinusoids — one in-phase (sine) and one quadrature-phase (cosine) — are generated internally and passed through a 2-tap adaptive FIR filter whose weights adapt via the **Least Mean Squares (LMS)** algorithm to estimate and subtract the interference from the corrupted primary signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Syllabus Highlight):**
> A 60 Hz adaptive canceller using a sine wave model is a 2-weight adaptive FIR filter that takes internally generated $\sin(\omega_0 k)$ and $\cos(\omega_0 k)$ as reference inputs and updates its weights using the LMS rule to synthesize a replica of the interfering sinusoid, which is then subtracted from the noise-corrupted biomedical signal.

The mathematical signal model is:

$$x(k) = s(k) + A\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi)$$

where $s(k)$ is the clean biomedical signal, $A$ is the unknown amplitude, $\phi$ is the unknown phase, and $\omega_0$ is the known normalized angular frequency of the line interference.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Echo-Removal Headphones" Metaphor**

Imagine you are a musician recording vocals in a small studio, and a constant low-pitch hum from a faulty air-conditioner (the 60 Hz mains hum) keeps leaking into your microphone. You cannot stop the air-conditioner, but you know **exactly** what pitch it hums at — just the letter "C" below middle C, which is **60 Hz**. So you install a small smart speaker in the room that listens to the hum and produces an *anti-hum* — a sound wave of the **same pitch and loudness** but **inverted in phase** (peaks where the hum has troughs and vice versa). When this anti-hum is mixed with the contaminated recording, the two identical-but-opposite waves cancel each other out, leaving only your clean vocals.

In this analogy:
- The **musical recording + AC hum** = primary input $x(k) = s(k) + n(k)$
- The **known pitch of the AC** = known frequency $\omega_0$
- The **smart speaker's adjustable anti-hum** = adaptive filter output $\hat{n}(k)$
- The **loudness and timing knobs on the speaker** = adaptive weights $w_1(k), w_2(k)$
- The **clean vocals** = the error $e(k)$ after subtraction
- The **smart auto-adjusting circuit in the speaker** = the LMS algorithm

The beauty of the sine wave model is that because we **already know the frequency**, we don't need a separate physical reference sensor picking up the hum — we can **synthesize** the reference entirely in software using $\sin(\omega_0 k)$ and $\cos(\omega_0 k)$, making this an *internally-referenced* adaptive canceller.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Powerline frequency (US/Japan part) | $f_0$ | **60 Hz** |
| Powerline frequency (Europe/India) | $f_0$ | **50 Hz** |
| Normalized angular frequency | $\omega_0$ | $2\pi f_0 / F_s$ (radians/sample) |
| Sampling frequency (ECG) | $F_s$ | **500 Hz** or **360 Hz** typical |
| ECG signal bandwidth | — | 0.05 – 150 Hz |
| EEG signal bandwidth | — | 0.5 – 40 Hz |
| Required notch depth | — | > **40 dB** at $f_0$ |
| LMS step-size range | $\mu$ | $0.01$ to $0.5$ (dimensionless) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Adaptive" and not a Fixed Notch Filter?**
> A traditional IIR notch filter at 60 Hz has a **fixed bandwidth**. If the line frequency drifts by $\pm 0.5$ Hz (common in real grids), a fixed notch will leave residual hum, and widening the notch to track drift will remove legitimate biomedical signal content. The **adaptive** sine-wave model *tracks* the precise frequency, amplitude, and phase of the interference continuously, achieving sharper and self-adjusting cancellation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of the 60 Hz Adaptive Canceller

The system consists of **four conceptual blocks**:

1. **Primary Input Channel** — delivers the contaminated signal $d(k) = s(k) + n(k)$ (also called the *desired response* in adaptive-filter nomenclature because the canceller wants to *destroy* $n(k)$).
2. **Internal Reference Generator** — produces two pure sinusoids at the known $\omega_0$:
   - $x_1(k) = \sin(\omega_0 k)$
   - $x_2(k) = \cos(\omega_0 k)$
3. **2-Tap Adaptive FIR Filter** — combines the references using two real weights $w_1(k), w_2(k)$ to synthesize the noise estimate $\hat{n}(k)$.
4. **Subtractor** — outputs the error $e(k) = d(k) - \hat{n}(k)$, which is the cleaned signal. $e(k)$ is also fed back to drive the LMS update.

### 2.2 Why Two References (Sine AND Cosine)?

A single sinusoid $\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi)$ has **two unknowns**: amplitude $A$ and phase $\phi$. Even though the frequency is known, the phase of the contaminating sinusoid depends on the *unknown* relative coupling between the mains wiring and the electrode lead, and it drifts with grid loading.

Using the trigonometric identity:

$$A\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi) = \underbrace{A\cos\phi}_{w_1^*}\sin(\omega_0 k) + \underbrace{A\sin\phi}_{w_2^*}\cos(\omega_0 k)$$

The canceller uses *two* references to span the entire 2-D subspace of all possible 60 Hz sinusoids. After convergence, the weights converge to $w_1^* = A\cos\phi$ and $w_2^* = A\sin\phi$, and the amplitude and phase can be recovered as $A = \sqrt{(w_1^*)^2 + (w_2^*)^2}$ and $\phi = \arctan(w_2^*/w_1^*)$.

### 2.3 The LMS Update Equations

The adaptive filter output is:

$$\hat{n}(k) = w_1(k)\,x_1(k) + w_2(k)\,x_2(k)$$

The error (cleaned signal) is:

$$e(k) = d(k) - \hat{n}(k)$$

The LMS algorithm updates each weight by stepping in the direction of the negative instantaneous gradient of $e^2(k)$:

$$w_i(k+1) = w_i(k) - \mu \frac{\partial e^2(k)}{\partial w_i(k)} = w_i(k) + 2\mu\, e(k)\, x_i(k), \quad i = 1, 2$$

In **vector-matrix form**, defining the reference vector $\mathbf{x}(k) = [x_1(k),\ x_2(k)]^T$ and weight vector $\mathbf{w}(k) = [w_1(k),\ w_2(k)]^T$:

$$\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + 2\mu\, e(k)\, \mathbf{x}(k)$$

### 2.4 Convergence Analysis (Why This Works So Well)

The beauty of the sine-wave reference is the **autocorrelation structure** of $\mathbf{x}(k)$:

$$\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(k)\mathbf{x}^T(k)] = \begin{bmatrix} E[\sin^2(\omega_0 k)] & E[\sin(\omega_0 k)\cos(\omega_0 k)] \\ E[\sin(\omega_0 k)\cos(\omega_0 k)] & E[\cos^2(\omega_0 k)] \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_2$$

The cross-correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ is **diagonal and equal-eigenvalue** ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 1/2$). This is a remarkable property of orthogonal sinusoids — it means:

- **The convergence rate is the same in both weight dimensions** (no slow-mode / fast-mode mismatch).
- **The Wiener optimum** is simply $\mathbf{w}^* = 2\mathbf{p}$, where $\mathbf{p} = E[d(k)\mathbf{x}(k)]$ is the cross-correlation vector.
- **The time constant** of convergence for each weight is $\tau \approx \dfrac{1}{2\mu \lambda_i} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ samples.
- **Stability bound** is $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{max}} = 2$, but in practice $\mu$ is kept small ($\le 0.5$) for low misadjustment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> This exact architecture is the basis of commercial pacemaker sense-amplifier PLI rejection, ICU patient monitor ECG front-ends, Holter recorders, polysomnography EEG systems, and evoked-potential averagers. Modern variants replace LMS with Normalized LMS (NLMS) or Recursive Least Squares (RLS) for faster tracking, but the 2-tap sine-model structure remains.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / F_s$ | Normalized angular frequency of the line interference |
| 2 | $x_1(k) = \sin(\omega_0 k)$ | In-phase reference |
| 3 | $x_2(k) = \cos(\omega_0 k)$ | Quadrature reference |
| 4 | $\hat{n}(k) = w_1(k) x_1(k) + w_2(k) x_2(k)$ | Adaptive filter output (noise estimate) |
| 5 | $e(k) = d(k) - \hat{n}(k)$ | Error / cleaned signal |
| 6 | $w_i(k+1) = w_i(k) + 2\mu e(k) x_i(k)$ | LMS weight update |
| 7 | $\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + 2\mu e(k) \mathbf{x}(k)$ | LMS update in vector form |
| 8 | $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T] = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_2$ | Autocorrelation of sine-cosine pair |
| 9 | $\mathbf{w}^* = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} = 2\mathbf{p}$ | Wiener optimal weight |
| 10 | $\tau \approx 1/\mu$ | Convergence time constant (samples) |
| 11 | $0 < \mu < 2$ | Stability bound for this $\mathbf{R}$ |
| 12 | $\mathcal{M} \approx \mu \cdot \text{tr}(\mathbf{R}) = \mu$ | Misadjustment |
| 13 | $A = \sqrt{w_1^{*2} + w_2^{*2}}$ | Recovered interference amplitude |
| 14 | $\phi = \arctan(w_2^*/w_1^*)$ | Recovered interference phase |
| 15 | $n(k) = A\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi) = w_1^*\sin(\omega_0 k) + w_2^*\cos(\omega_0 k)$ | Phase-to-weights identity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** The diagonality of $\mathbf{R} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_2$ is the **single most examinable fact** about this topic. Examiners love asking *"Why does the sine-cosine model converge without a slow mode?"* — the answer is always the equal-eigenvalue property of orthogonal sinusoids.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Phase-Shifted Sinusoid to Two-Tap Filter

**Statement to Prove:** A pure sinusoid of unknown amplitude $A$ and unknown phase $\phi$ at known frequency $\omega_0$ can be exactly synthesized as a linear combination of $\sin(\omega_0 k)$ and $\cos(\omega_0 k)$ with two real weights $w_1, w_2$.

**Step 1 — Start with the physical model of the powerline interference:**

$$n(k) = A \sin(\omega_0 k + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sine-of-a-sum trigonometric expansion:**

$$n(k) = A \left[ \sin(\omega_0 k)\cos\phi + \cos(\omega_0 k)\sin\phi \right]$$

This step uses the identity $\sin(\alpha + \beta) = \sin\alpha\cos\beta + \cos\alpha\sin\beta$.

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the two reference sinusoids:**

$$n(k) = (A\cos\phi)\sin(\omega_0 k) + (A\sin\phi)\cos(\omega_0 k)$$

**Step 4 — Define the optimal weights as the projection coefficients:**

$$w_1^* = A\cos\phi, \quad w_2^* = A\sin\phi$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite in the standard 2-tap filter form:**

$$n(k) = w_1^* \cdot x_1(k) + w_2^* \cdot x_2(k) = \mathbf{w}^{*T}\mathbf{x}(k)$$

This proves the 2-tap structure is **sufficient** (not just necessary) to span the subspace of all 60 Hz sinusoids. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — LMS Update Rule from Gradient Descent

**Step 1 — Define the instantaneous cost function:**

$$J(k) = e^2(k)$$

The LMS algorithm uses the *instantaneous* (stochastic) squared error rather than the true mean-squared error $E[e^2(k)]$.

**Step 2 — Express the error in terms of the weights:**

$$e(k) = d(k) - w_1(k)x_1(k) - w_2(k)x_2(k)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to weight $w_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial w_1(k)} = -x_1(k)$$

$$\frac{\partial e^2(k)}{\partial w_1(k)} = 2 e(k) \cdot \frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial w_1(k)} = -2 e(k) x_1(k)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the steepest-descent update with step-size $\mu$:**

$$w_1(k+1) = w_1(k) - \mu \frac{\partial e^2(k)}{\partial w_1(k)} = w_1(k) + 2\mu\, e(k)\, x_1(k)$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for $w_2$:**

$$w_2(k+1) = w_2(k) - \mu \frac{\partial e^2(k)}{\partial w_2(k)} = w_2(k) + 2\mu\, e(k)\, x_2(k)$$

The factor of $2$ comes from the $\frac{\partial}{\partial w} e^2 = 2e \frac{\partial e}{\partial w}$ differentiation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Autocorrelation Matrix of the Sine-Cosine Reference

**Step 1 — Define the four elements of $\mathbf{R}$:**

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} E[\sin^2(\omega_0 k)] & E[\sin(\omega_0 k)\cos(\omega_0 k)] \\ E[\sin(\omega_0 k)\cos(\omega_0 k)] & E[\cos^2(\omega_0 k)] \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the diagonal element $r_{11}$:**

Using the identity $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$E[\sin^2(\omega_0 k)] = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} E[\cos(2\omega_0 k)] = \frac{1}{2} - 0 = \frac{1}{2}$$

The expectation of the cosine over a complete period is zero.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the diagonal element $r_{22}$:**

Using $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$E[\cos^2(\omega_0 k)] = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}E[\cos(2\omega_0 k)] = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the off-diagonal element $r_{12}$:**

Using $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$:

$$E[\sin(\omega_0 k)\cos(\omega_0 k)] = \frac{1}{2} E[\sin(2\omega_0 k)] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the final matrix:**

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_2$$

This proves the diagonality and equal-eigenvalue property. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time Constant of Convergence

**Step 1 — Use the standard LMS convergence theory for the mean-weight vector:**

$$E[\mathbf{w}(k)] = (\mathbf{I} - 2\mu \mathbf{R})^k \mathbf{w}(0) + 2\mu \sum_{i=0}^{k-1}(\mathbf{I} - 2\mu \mathbf{R})^i \mathbf{p}$$

**Step 2 — Since $\mathbf{R} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}$, the matrix $(\mathbf{I} - 2\mu \mathbf{R}) = (1 - \mu)\mathbf{I}$.**

The eigen-modes decouple trivially.

**Step 3 — Each weight converges geometrically with ratio $(1 - \mu)$:**

The time constant (samples for the error to decay to $1/e$) is obtained by setting $(1-\mu)^{\tau} = e^{-1}$:

$$(1-\mu)^\tau = e^{-1}$$

$$\tau \ln(1-\mu) = -1$$

**Step 4 — For small $\mu$ (typical operating regime $\mu \ll 1$), use the approximation $\ln(1-\mu) \approx -\mu$:**

$$\tau \approx \frac{1}{\mu} \text{ samples}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to seconds by dividing by $F_s$:**

$$\tau_{sec} = \frac{1}{\mu F_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example (ECG, $F_s = 500$ Hz, $\mu = 0.05$):**
> $$\tau \approx \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ samples} = 40 \text{ ms}$$
> So the canceller converges within roughly one cardiac cycle — fast enough to track realistic grid-frequency drift.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Recovery of Amplitude and Phase from Converged Weights

After convergence, $\mathbf{w}(k) \to \mathbf{w}^* = [w_1^*, w_2^*]^T$. By the derivation in §3.1, the optimal weights encode the original sinusoid's amplitude and phase:

**Step 1 — Form the squared-weight sum:**

$$w_1^{*2} + w_2^{*2} = (A\cos\phi)^2 + (A\sin\phi)^2 = A^2(\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi) = A^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for amplitude:**

$$A = \sqrt{w_1^{*2} + w_2^{*2}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the weight ratio:**

$$\frac{w_2^*}{w_1^*} = \frac{A\sin\phi}{A\cos\phi} = \tan\phi$$

**Step 4 — Solve for phase:**

$$\phi = \arctan\left(\frac{w_2^*}{w_1^*}\right)$$

This is a powerful feature for *diagnostic* applications: by inspecting the converged weights, one can quantify the line-interference amplitude and coupling phase in real time. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete 60 Hz Adaptive Sine-Wave Canceller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Primary Input d of k = s of k + n of k"]
    refA["Reference Sine x1 of k = sin w0 k"]
    refB["Reference Cosine x2 of k = cos w0 k"]
    wA["Adaptive Weight w1 of k"]
    wB["Adaptive Weight w2 of k"]
    mulA["Multiplier x x1 of k"]
    mulB["Multiplier x x2 of k"]
    addA["Adder: y of k = w1 x1 + w2 x2"]
    subA["Subtractor: e of k = d of k - y of k"]
    outA["Cleaned Output e of k"]
    lmsA["LMS Update Engine mu step size"]
    fbA["Feedback of e of k to LMS"]

    srcA --> subA
    refA --> mulA
    refB --> mulB
    wA --> mulA
    wB --> mulB
    mulA --> addA
    mulB --> addA
    addA --> subA
    subA --> outA
    subA --> fbA
    fbA --> lmsA
    lmsA --> wA
    lmsA --> wB
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Per-Iteration Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Iteration Start: Time Index k"]
    step2["Read primary sample d of k"]
    step3["Generate x1 = sin w0 k"]
    step4["Generate x2 = cos w0 k"]
    step5["Compute y of k = w1 x1 + w2 x2"]
    step6["Compute e of k = d of k - y of k"]
    step7["Output e of k as cleaned sample"]
```
