```mermaid
    step8["Update w1 = w1 + 2 mu e x1"]
    step9["Update w2 = w2 + 2 mu e x2"]
    step10["Increment k to k+1"]
    step11["Convergence Check: w diff less than epsilon"]
    step12["YES: Steady state output"]
    step13["NO: Loop back to step2"]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8 --> step9 --> step10 --> step11
    step11 -->|YES| step12
    step11 -->|NO| step2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Flow Within the LMS Update Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inE["Error e of k"]
    inX1["Reference x1 of k"]
    inX2["Reference x2 of k"]
    stepGain["Step Size 2 mu"]
    prod1["Product 2 mu e x1"]
    prod2["Product 2 mu e x2"]
    intA["Integrator: w1 k+1 = w1 k + delta1"]
    intB["Integrator: w2 k+1 = w2 k + delta2"]
    outW1["Updated w1 k+1"]
    outW2["Updated w2 k+1"]

    inE --> prod1
    inX1 --> prod1
    stepGain --> prod1
    prod1 --> intA
    intA --> outW1

    inE --> prod2
    inX2 --> prod2
    stepGain --> prod2
    prod2 --> intB
    intB --> outW2
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Two-Orthogonal-Reference Subspace Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subA["Subspace of all 60 Hz sinusoids: span of sin w0 k, cos w0 k"]
    basisA["Basis 1: sin w0 k --> w1 encodes A cos phi"]
    basisB["Basis 2: cos w0 k --> w2 encodes A sin phi"]
    ptA["Any interference lies in this 2D plane"]
    recA["Recover A = sqrt w1^2 + w2^2"]
    recB["Recover phi = atan w2 over w1"]

    subA --> basisA
    subA --> basisB
    basisA --> ptA
    basisB --> ptA
    ptA --> recA
    ptA --> recB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Adaptive Canceller is Superior to a Fixed Notch IIR Filter**
>
> | Property | Fixed IIR Notch | 60 Hz Adaptive Sine Model |
> |---|---|---|
> | Frequency tracking | No (fixed $\omega_0$) | Yes (converges to true $A, \phi$) |
> | Distortion of biomedical signal | Some (transient ringing) | Minimal (only the pure 60 Hz component removed) |
> | Ability to handle 60 Hz harmonics | Needs multiple notches | Cancels fundamental; harmonics can be cascaded |
> | Computational cost | Very low | 4 multiplies + 2 adds per sample |
> | Drift immunity | Poor | Excellent |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q: State the signal model used in the 60 Hz adaptive canceller based on a sine wave model. Why are two reference sinusoids required? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The corrupted biomedical signal is modelled as $d(k) = s(k) + n(k)$, where $s(k)$ is the clean physiological signal and $n(k) = A\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi)$ is the powerline interference at the known normalized frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / F_s$. Two reference sinusoids — $x_1(k) = \sin(\omega_0 k)$ and $x_2(k) = \cos(\omega_0 k)$ — are required because the interfering sinusoid has **two unknowns**, its amplitude $A$ and its phase $\phi$, even though the frequency is known. Using the identity $A\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi) = (A\cos\phi)\sin(\omega_0 k) + (A\sin\phi)\cos(\omega_0 k)$, a 2-tap adaptive filter with weights $w_1^* = A\cos\phi$ and $w_2^* = A\sin\phi$ can synthesize the exact interference. **(3 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q: Write the LMS update equations for the 2-weight adaptive filter used in the 60 Hz sine-wave canceller and explain the role of the step-size $\mu$. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The LMS update equations are:
$$w_1(k+1) = w_1(k) + 2\mu\, e(k)\, \sin(\omega_0 k)$$
$$w_2(k+1) = w_2(k) + 2\mu\, e(k)\, \cos(\omega_0 k)$$
where $e(k) = d(k) - w_1(k)\sin(\omega_0 k) - w_2(k)\cos(\omega_0 k)$ is the cancellation error. The step-size $\mu$ controls the **convergence speed vs steady-state accuracy trade-off**: a larger $\mu$ yields faster convergence but higher misadjustment (larger residual error variance), while a smaller $\mu$ yields slower but more accurate convergence. For stability, $0 < \mu < 2$ (since $\lambda_{max} = 1/2$ gives the bound $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max} = 2$). **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a 60 Hz adaptive canceller using a sine wave model. Derive the LMS update equations for the 2-tap adaptive filter. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The 60 Hz adaptive sine-wave canceller consists of:

- A **primary channel** that receives the contaminated signal $d(k) = s(k) + A\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi)$, where $s(k)$ is the desired biomedical signal (e.g., ECG) and the second term is the 60 Hz interference.
- An **internal reference generator** that synthesizes two orthogonal sinusoids at the known normalized frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi(60)/F_s$: $x_1(k) = \sin(\omega_0 k)$ and $x_2(k) = \cos(\omega_0 k)$. These are generated entirely in software; no physical reference sensor is required.
- A **2-tap adaptive FIR filter** with weights $w_1(k)$ and $w_2(k)$ that produces the noise estimate $\hat{n}(k) = w_1(k)\sin(\omega_0 k) + w_2(k)\cos(\omega_0 k)$.
- A **subtractor** that produces the error $e(k) = d(k) - \hat{n}(k)$, which is the cleaned output and is also fed back to the LMS engine.

**LMS Update Derivation (4 marks):**

The instantaneous cost function is $J(k) = e^2(k)$ with
$$e(k) = d(k) - w_1(k)\sin(\omega_0 k) - w_2(k)\cos(\omega_0 k)$$

The gradient of $J$ with respect to $w_1(k)$ is:
$$\frac{\partial e^2(k)}{\partial w_1(k)} = 2e(k) \cdot \frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial w_1(k)} = -2e(k)\sin(\omega_0 k)$$

Applying steepest descent with step-size $\mu$:
$$w_1(k+1) = w_1(k) - \mu \frac{\partial e^2(k)}{\partial w_1(k)} = w_1(k) + 2\mu e(k) \sin(\omega_0 k)$$

Similarly for $w_2(k)$:
$$w_2(k+1) = w_2(k) + 2\mu e(k) \cos(\omega_0 k)$$

In compact vector form, with $\mathbf{x}(k) = [\sin(\omega_0 k), \cos(\omega_0 k)]^T$:
$$\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + 2\mu e(k) \mathbf{x}(k)$$

This completes the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** For a 60 Hz adaptive canceller, the input signal is $d(k) = s(k) + 0.5\sin(2\pi(60)(k)/500 + \pi/4)$. The sampling frequency is $F_s = 500$ Hz. Write the signal model in the form $d(k) = s(k) + w_1^*\sin(\omega_0 k) + w_2^*\cos(\omega_0 k)$ and determine the optimal weights $w_1^*, w_2^*$. If the LMS step-size is $\mu = 0.02$, estimate the time constant of convergence. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- $A = 0.5$ (amplitude)
- $\phi = \pi/4$ (phase)
- $\omega_0 = 2\pi(60)/500 = 0.7539$ rad/sample
- $\mu = 0.02$

**Step 2 — Expand the interference using the sine addition formula:**
$$0.5\sin(\omega_0 k + \pi/4) = 0.5[\sin(\omega_0 k)\cos(\pi/4) + \cos(\omega_0 k)\sin(\pi/4)]$$
$$= 0.5\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sin(\omega_0 k) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\cos(\omega_0 k)\right]$$
$$= \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{2}}\sin(\omega_0 k) + \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{2}}\cos(\omega_0 k)$$
$$= 0.3536\sin(\omega_0 k) + 0.3536\cos(\omega_0 k)$$

**Step 3 — Write the signal model and identify the optimal weights:**
$$d(k) = s(k) + 0.3536\sin(\omega_0 k) + 0.3536\cos(\omega_0 k)$$

Therefore:
$$w_1^* = 0.5\cos(\pi/4) = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536$$
$$w_2^* = 0.5\sin(\pi/4) = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536$$

**Step 4 — Compute the convergence time constant:**
For the sine-cosine reference, the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{R}$ are $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 0.5$. The time constant per mode is:
$$\tau = \frac{1}{2\mu\lambda} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.02 \times 0.5} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to seconds:**
$$\tau_{sec} = \frac{50}{500} = 0.1 \text{ seconds} = 100 \text{ ms}$$

This means the adaptive canceller reaches steady state within approximately **100 ms** — fast enough to track realistic 60 Hz grid drift. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Prove that the autocorrelation matrix of the 2-dimensional reference vector $\mathbf{x}(k) = [\sin(\omega_0 k), \cos(\omega_0 k)]^T$ is given by $\mathbf{R} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_2$. Use this result to derive the Wiener optimal weight vector $\mathbf{w}^*$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Proof of $\mathbf{R} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_2$ (4 marks):**

By definition, $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(k)\mathbf{x}^T(k)]$, giving a $2 \times 2$ matrix with entries:
- Diagonal $r_{11} = E[\sin^2(\omega_0 k)]$. Using $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:
$$r_{11} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2}E[\cos(2\omega_0 k)] = \frac{1}{2} - 0 = \frac{1}{2}$$
- Diagonal $r_{22} = E[\cos^2(\omega_0 k)]$. Using $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$:
$$r_{22} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}E[\cos(2\omega_0 k)] = \frac{1}{2}$$
- Off-diagonal $r_{12} = r_{21} = E[\sin(\omega_0 k)\cos(\omega_0 k)]$. Using $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$:
$$r_{12} = \frac{1}{2}E[\sin(2\omega_0 k)] = 0$$

Assembling:
$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_2$$

**Wiener Optimal Weight Vector (3 marks):**

The Wiener-Hopf equation is $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}^* = \mathbf{p}$, where $\mathbf{p} = E[d(k)\mathbf{x}(k)]$. Since $\mathbf{R} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_2$ is diagonal, its inverse is trivially $\mathbf{R}^{-1} = 2\mathbf{I}_2$. Therefore:
$$\mathbf{w}^* = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} = 2\mathbf{p} = 2\begin{bmatrix} E[d(k)\sin(\omega_0 k)] \\ E[d(k)\cos(\omega_0 k)] \end{bmatrix}$$

In expanded form:
$$w_1^* = 2 E[d(k)\sin(\omega_0 k)], \quad w_2^* = 2 E[d(k)\cos(\omega_0 k)]$$

If the biomedical signal $s(k)$ is uncorrelated with the reference sinusoids (a standard assumption in ANC), then $E[s(k)\sin(\omega_0 k)] = E[s(k)\cos(\omega_0 k)] = 0$, and the expectations reduce to:
$$w_1^* = 2 E[n(k)\sin(\omega_0 k)], \quad w_2^* = 2 E[n(k)\cos(\omega_0 k)]$$

Substituting $n(k) = A\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi)$ and using orthogonality, $w_1^* = A\cos\phi$ and $w_2^* = A\sin\phi$ as derived in §3.1. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** Explain the misadjustment of the LMS algorithm in the 60 Hz sine-wave canceller. For $\mu = 0.01$, compute the steady-state misadjustment. How does misadjustment differ from steady-state error? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Misadjustment Definition (3 marks):**
Misadjustment $\mathcal{M}$ is defined as the ratio of the *excess* mean-squared error (EMSE) — the mean-squared error above the Wiener minimum $J_{min}$ — to $J_{min}$ itself:
$$\mathcal{M} = \frac{J_{excess}}{J_{min}} = \frac{J(\infty) - J_{min}}{J_{min}}$$

For LMS with sufficiently small $\mu$, the misadjustment is approximately:
$$\mathcal{M} \approx \mu \cdot \text{tr}(\mathbf{R}) = \mu (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)$$

For the sine-wave canceller, $\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}) = 1/2 + 1/2 = 1$, so:
$$\mathcal{M} \approx \mu$$

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**
For $\mu = 0.01$:
$$\mathcal{M} \approx 0.01 = 1\%$$

This means the steady-state error power is **1% larger** than the theoretical Wiener minimum.

**Misadjustment vs Steady-State Error (2 marks):**
- **Misadjustment** is a *dimensionless ratio* (or percentage) that characterizes the *algorithm's* relative inefficiency compared to the optimal Wiener solution. It is a property of the *adaptive algorithm* itself, not the signal.
- **Steady-state error** is the *absolute* residual error power $E[e^2(\infty)]$ in the cleaned output. It depends on both the algorithm (through misadjustment) and the input signal statistics.

**Practical Note:** Although misadjustment of 1% sounds small, for a 60 Hz interference of amplitude 1 mV, a 1% misadjustment leaves a residual 60 Hz ripple of $\sim 10\,\mu$V, which is acceptable for ECG but marginal for high-gain EEG. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the LMS update.** The gradient of $e^2$ is $2e \cdot \partial e/\partial w$. Dropping the factor of 2 changes the effective step-size by a factor of 2 and breaks the convergence analysis. **Examiner's mark loss: −1 to −2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing normalized $\omega_0$ with continuous $\Omega_0$.** The discrete-time angular frequency is $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / F_s$ (rad/sample), NOT $2\pi f_0 = 377$ rad/s. Students who write $\omega_0 = 2\pi(60) = 377$ in the LMS update equations get **all subsequent numerical questions wrong**. **Mark loss: −2 to −3 marks.**
>
> 3. **Claiming the autocorrelation matrix has off-diagonal terms that are non-zero.** The orthogonality of sine and cosine over an integer number of periods gives $E[\sin(\omega_0 k)\cos(\omega_0 k)] = 0$ exactly. Students who forget the half-angle identity $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$ and the fact that the mean of a sinusoid over its period is zero lose **−1 to −2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Mixing up the phase convention.** $A\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi)$ vs $A\sin(\omega_0 k - \phi)$ vs $A\cos(\omega_0 k - \phi)$ give different sign patterns in $w_1^*$ and $w_2^*$. Always write the trigonometric expansion explicitly.
>
> 5. **Confusing stability bound and operating range.** The stability bound is $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max} = 2$, but the *practical* operating range for low misadjustment is $\mu \in [0.001, 0.1]$. Writing $\mu = 1$ as a "valid" answer in a numerical problem is technically stable but practically useless.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Signal Model:** $d(k) = s(k) + A\sin(\omega_0 k + \phi)$ with $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / F_s$ known.
- [ ] **Two References are Mandatory:** $x_1(k) = \sin(\omega_0 k)$ and $x_2(k) = \cos(\omega_0 k)$ to span the 2-D interference subspace.
- [ ] **Filter Output:** $\hat{n}(k) = w_1(k)\sin(\omega_0 k) + w_2(k)\cos(\omega_0 k)$.
- [ ] **Error / Cleaned Output:** $e(k) = d(k) - \hat{n}(k)$.
- [ ] **LMS Update:** $w_i(k+1) = w_i(k) + 2\mu\, e(k)\, x_i(k)$, $i = 1, 2$.
- [ ] **Autocorrelation:** $\mathbf{R} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_2$ — this is the **single most important property**.
- [ ] **Equal Eigenvalues:** Both modes converge at the same rate — no slow mode.
- [ ] **Wiener Solution:** $\mathbf{w}^* = 2\mathbf{p}$, where $\mathbf{p} = E[d(k)\mathbf{x}(k)]$.
- [ ] **Time Constant:** $\tau \approx 1/\mu$ samples $= 1/(\mu F_s)$ seconds.
- [ ] **Stability Bound:** $0 < \mu < 2$; practical range $\mu \in [0.01, 0.1]$.
- [ ] **Misadjustment:** $\mathcal{M} \approx \mu$ for the sine-cosine reference.
- [ ] **Amplitude Recovery:** $A = \sqrt{w_1^{*2} + w_2^{*2}}$.
- [ ] **Phase Recovery:** $\phi = \arctan(w_2^*/w_1^*)$.
- [ ] **Physical Constants:** $f_0 = 60$ Hz (US/Japan), $50$ Hz (Europe/India); $F_s$ typically 360, 500, or 1000 Hz.
- [ ] **Use Case:** ECG, EMG, EEG front-end PLI rejection; Holter monitors; ICU telemetry.
- [ ] **Advantage over fixed notch:** Tracks frequency drift, amplitude changes, and phase jitter automatically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive and Optimal Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Adaptive and Optimal Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Optimal Filtering — The Wiener Filter

An **optimal filter** is a linear, time-invariant (or slowly time-varying) filter whose impulse response is mathematically computed to minimize a chosen statistical criterion, most commonly the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** between the filter output and a desired reference signal. The celebrated solution to this problem is the **Wiener Filter**, formulated by Norbert Wiener in the 1940s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The *Wiener filter* is the optimal linear filter $w_{opt}$ that minimizes the expected value of the squared error $J = E[e^2(n)]$, where $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$, between a primary desired signal $d(n)$ and the filter output $y(n)$. Its coefficients are obtained by solving the **Wiener-Hopf equation**:
> $$\mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p}$$
> where $\mathbf{R}$ is the autocorrelation matrix of the input and $\mathbf{p}$ is the cross-correlation vector between the input and the desired signal.

## 1.2 Adaptive Filtering — Self-Designing Filters

An **adaptive filter** is a self-modifying digital filter whose coefficients $w(n)$ are recursively updated using an **adaptive algorithm** (e.g., LMS or RLS) so that its performance criterion (typically MSE) is minimized in real-time, *without* requiring a priori knowledge of the signal statistics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why adaptive filters?**
> Biological signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) are *non-stationary* — their statistical properties (mean, variance, spectral content) change with time. A fixed Wiener filter becomes sub-optimal the moment the noise characteristics drift. Adaptive filters track these changes continuously, making them indispensable in biomedical instrumentation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Cocktail Party Problem

Imagine you are at a noisy party trying to listen to one specific speaker (the *desired signal*). The surrounding chatter is *noise* corrupting your perception.

* The **Wiener filter** is like having a perfect acoustically-engineered room — designed once using long-term statistical measurements of the noise, it cancels the average chatter optimally. But if the room's acoustics change, it fails.
* The **adaptive filter** is like wearing *active noise-cancelling headphones* that **continuously measure** the ambient noise with a secondary microphone, compare it with what you actually hear (the error), and **iteratively adjust** the anti-noise signal in real time. It works even if the noise source moves, the music tempo changes, or the room fills with more people.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Performance Metrics** (must memorize):
> * **Convergence speed** — how quickly $w(n) \to w_{opt}$
> * **Steady-state error (misadjustement)** — residual MSE after convergence
> * **Computational complexity** — operations per sample ($O(N)$ for LMS, $O(N^2)$ for RLS)
> * **Numerical stability** — robustness to finite-precision arithmetic

## 1.4 The Four Canonical Adaptive Filter Structures (Widrow Taxonomy)

| Structure | Application in Biomedicine |
|---|---|
| **System Identification** | Modeling the transfer function of an electrode–skin interface |
| **Noise Cancellation** | Removing 50 Hz mains hum from ECG |
| **Prediction** | Predicting next sample of fetal ECG from abdominal recordings |
| **Channel Equalization** | Compensating for dispersion in neural recording probes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Assumption:** The desired signal $d(n)$ must be available either directly (supervised adaptation) or via a closely correlated reference $x(n)$ (as in ANC). In blind biomedical scenarios where $d(n)$ is unavailable, we move to **blind source separation** (ICA), which is *not* covered in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Adaptive Filter Problem

Consider a discrete-time FIR adaptive filter of order $M$ with coefficient vector:

$$\mathbf{w}(n) = [w_0(n), w_1(n), w_2(n), \dots, w_{M-1}(n)]^T$$

and input regressor vector:

$$\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), x(n-2), \dots, x(n-M+1)]^T$$

The filter output is the inner product:

$$y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k(n) \, x(n-k)$$

The **error signal** is the difference between the desired (primary) signal and the filter output:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)$$

The performance criterion is the **instantaneous squared error** (LMS) or **mean-square error** (Wiener):

$$J(n) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^2(n) \quad \text{(LMS)} \qquad \text{or} \qquad J = E[e^2(n)] \quad \text{(Wiener)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in the LMS cost function is a mathematical convenience to cancel the factor 2 that emerges when differentiating.

## 2.2 The Optimal Wiener Solution (Static / Off-line)

Taking the gradient of the MSE w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and equating to zero (orthogonality principle):

$$\nabla J = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2 \, E[\mathbf{x}(n) \, e(n)] = 0$$

Substituting $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$:

$$E[\mathbf{x}(n) \, d(n)] - E[\mathbf{x}(n) \, \mathbf{x}^T(n)] \, \mathbf{w}_{opt} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p}}$$

Solving by matrix inversion:

$$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \, \mathbf{p}$$

The **minimum achievable MSE** is obtained by plugging $w_{opt}$ back into the cost:

$$J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T \, \mathbf{R}^{-1} \, \mathbf{p}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **When does the Wiener filter fail?**
> 1. When $\mathbf{R}$ is **ill-conditioned** (eigenvalues span many orders of magnitude) — inversion amplifies noise.
> 2. When the statistics are **non-stationary** — the solution drifts.
> 3. When $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ is **computationally infeasible** for high filter orders (matrix inversion is $O(M^3)$).

## 2.3 The LMS Algorithm (Stochastic Gradient Descent)

The LMS algorithm (Widrow & Hoff, 1960) replaces the expectation in the gradient with the **instantaneous** squared error, an unbiased but noisy estimate:

$$\hat{\nabla} J(n) = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \left[ \tfrac{1}{2} e^2(n) \right] = -e(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)$$

Applying steepest descent with step size $\mu$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu \, e(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)}$$

**Convergence condition** (in mean):

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$$

or, for guaranteed practical stability:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})} = \frac{1}{\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} E[x^2(n-k)]}$$

**Misadjustement** (steady-state excess MSE relative to $J_{min}$):

$$\mathcal{M} = \frac{\mu}{2} \, \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R}) \quad \text{(small } \mu \text{ limit)}$$

## 2.4 The RLS Algorithm (Recursive Least Squares)

The RLS algorithm minimizes the **exponentially-weighted** sum of squared errors:

$$J(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \, e^2(i) \qquad 0 \ll \lambda < 1$$

where $\lambda$ is the **forgetting factor** (typically $0.98 \le \lambda \le 0.995$). The RLS update is:

$$\mathbf{k}(n) = \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1) \, \mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^T(n) \, \mathbf{P}(n-1) \, \mathbf{x}(n)}$$

$$\xi(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n-1) \, \mathbf{x}(n) \quad \text{(a priori error)}$$

$$\mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n) \, \xi(n)$$

$$\mathbf{P}(n) = \lambda^{-1} \left[ \mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n) \, \mathbf{x}^T(n) \, \mathbf{P}(n-1) \right]$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Filter output | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)$ | Inner product |
| Error | $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)$ | Scalar |
| Autocorrelation matrix | $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n) \, \mathbf{x}^T(n)]$ | $M \times M$, symmetric, Toeplitz |
| Cross-correlation vector | $\mathbf{p} = E[d(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)]$ | $M \times 1$ |
| Wiener-Hopf | $\mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p}$ | Closed-form optimum |
| Optimal weights | $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \, \mathbf{p}$ | Requires matrix inversion |
| Min MSE | $J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$ | Lower bound on achievable error |
| LMS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$ | Stochastic gradient |
| LMS stability | $0 \lt \mu \lt 1/\lambda_{max}$ | $\lambda_{max}$ = max eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$ |
| LMS safe bound | $0 \lt \mu \lt 1/\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ | Practical, easy to compute |
| LMS misadjustement | $\mathcal{M} = \mu \, \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R}) / 2$ | Excess MSE ratio |
| RLS gain | $\mathbf{k}(n) = \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^T(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{x}(n)}$ | $M \times 1$ |
| RLS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n) \xi(n)$ | $\xi$ = a priori error |
| Inverse correlation update | $\mathbf{P}(n) = \lambda^{-1} [\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)]$ | Init $\mathbf{P}(0) = \delta^{-1} \mathbf{I}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:**
> * **ECG denoising:** Adaptive noise canceller (Widrow, 1975) using a reference from the chest wall to remove maternal ECG from abdominal recordings (fetal ECG extraction).
> * **Pacemaker/ICD sense amplifiers:** Adaptive filtering of T-wave oversensing.
> * **Cochlear implants & hearing aids:** LMS-based feedback cancellation.
> * **Neural recording arrays:** RLS-based beamforming to reject common-mode neural noise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Wiener-Hopf Equation from First Principles

**Goal:** Find $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ that minimizes $J = E[e^2(n)]$.

**Step 1 — Expand the MSE cost function.**

Using $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= E\left[ \left( d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n) \right)^2 \right] \\
  &= E\left[ d^2(n) \right] - 2 E\left[ d(n) \, \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n) \right] + E\left[ \left( \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n) \right)^2 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The first term is the desired-signal variance $\sigma_d^2$, the second is a scalar (so $\mathbf{w}^T$ can be transposed freely), and the third expands as $\mathbf{w}^T E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T] \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \sigma_d^2 - 2 \, \mathbf{w}^T \, E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{w} \\
  &= \sigma_d^2 - 2 \, \mathbf{w}^T \, \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{w}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the matrix gradient identity.**

For symmetric $\mathbf{R}$, the gradient of the quadratic form $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ is $2 \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = 2 \, \mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{w} - 2 \, \mathbf{p}$$

**Step 3 — Set gradient to zero (first-order optimality).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \, \mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{w}_{opt} - 2 \, \mathbf{p} &= 0 \\
\mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{w}_{opt} &= \mathbf{p} \qquad \text{(Wiener-Hopf equation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the optimal weights.**

Assuming $\mathbf{R}$ is full-rank (positive definite):

$$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \, \mathbf{p}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the minimum achievable MSE.**

Substitute $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ back. Using $\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{w}_{opt}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{min} &= \sigma_d^2 - 2 \mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p} + (\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p})^T \mathbf{R} (\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}) \\
        &= \sigma_d^2 - 2 \mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p} \\
        &= \boxed{\sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T \, \mathbf{R}^{-1} \, \mathbf{p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** $\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$ is the *energy recovered* by the optimal filter. The minimum MSE is simply the desired-signal variance minus what the filter successfully extracts. The lower bound is zero, achieved only when $d(n)$ is perfectly predictable from $\mathbf{x}(n)$ (e.g., $d = $ noise and $\mathbf{x}$ contains an exact replica).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LMS Algorithm from Stochastic Approximation

**Goal:** Derive the LMS update law from an instantaneous cost.

**Step 1 — Define the instantaneous cost function** (a stochastic estimate of $J$):

$$J(n) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, e^2(n)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the instantaneous gradient.** Differentiate $e^2(n)/2$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ using the chain rule. Note that $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$, so $\partial e / \partial \mathbf{w} = -\mathbf{x}(n)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\nabla} J(n) &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 e(n) \cdot \frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \\
                 &= e(n) \cdot \left( -\mathbf{x}(n) \right) \\
                 &= -e(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply steepest descent** (move against the gradient with step $\mu$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}(n+1) &= \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \, \hat{\nabla} J(n) \\
                &= \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu \, e(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical LMS rule. Some textbooks absorb the factor of 2 into $\mu$ and write $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ — be consistent with your formula sheet.

**Step 4 — Convergence analysis (mean behavior).** Take the expectation of both sides assuming $e(n)$ and $\mathbf{x}(n)$ are jointly stationary. Using the orthogonality principle in steady state ($E[e_{opt} \mathbf{x}] = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\mathbf{w}(n+1)] &= E[\mathbf{w}(n)] + \mu \, E[e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)] \\
E[\mathbf{w}(\infty)] &= \mathbf{w}_{opt}
\end{aligned}
$$

The mean weight error vector $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}(n) = \mathbf{w}_{opt} - E[\mathbf{w}(n)]$ obeys:

$$E[\boldsymbol{\epsilon}(n+1)] = \left( \mathbf{I} - \mu \mathbf{R} \right) E[\boldsymbol{\epsilon}(n)]$$

**Step 5 — Diagonalize** using $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{Q} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{Q}^T$. The decoupled modes are $E[\tilde{\epsilon}_k(n+1)] = (1 - \mu \lambda_k) E[\tilde{\epsilon}_k(n)]$, giving the convergence bound $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{max}$. For practical positive-only step, $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Performance Surface Geometry

The MSE $J(\mathbf{w})$ is a *quadratic bowl* in $\mathbf{w}$-space:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = J_{min} + (\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}_{opt})^T \mathbf{R} (\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}_{opt})$$

Principal-axis rotation aligns the bowl with eigenvectors of $\mathbf{R}$:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = J_{min} + \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} \lambda_k \, v_k^2$$

where $v_k$ are coordinates along the eigenvectors. The **eigenvalue spread** $\chi(\mathbf{R}) = \lambda_{max} / \lambda_{min}$ controls the bowl's ellipticity — a large $\chi$ means slow LMS convergence along the minor axis (ill-conditioning), while RLS effectively *whitens* the input and converges in roughly $2M$ iterations regardless of $\chi$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Table — LMS vs. RLS (Mandatory for KTU 14-mark Qs)

| Parameter | LMS | RLS |
|---|---|---|
| Cost minimized | $E[e^2(n)]$ (instantaneous) | $\sum \lambda^{n-i} e^2(i)$ (exact recursive) |
| Update complexity | $O(M)$ multiplies + adds | $O(M^2)$ |
| Memory | $M$ weights | $M$ weights + $M^2$ matrix $\mathbf{P}$ |
| Convergence speed | Slow, $\propto \chi(\mathbf{R})/\mu$ | Fast, $\approx 2M$ iterations |
| Steady-state error | $\mathcal{M} = \mu \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})/2$ | Approaches $J_{min} (1 - \lambda)$ |
| Numerical stability | Inherently stable | Sensitive to round-off, needs resquare-root or UD-factorization |
| Best use case | Long FIRs, low power, e.g., hearing aids | Short filters needing fast tracking, e.g., ECG notch |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Adaptive Noise Canceller (Widrow, 1975)

The ANC is the single most important topology in biomedical adaptive filtering. It is used to extract a clean primary signal $s(n)$ that is corrupted by an *additive* noise $n_0(n)$ which is correlated with a separately accessible *reference* noise $n_1(n)$ picked up by a second sensor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Desired source s of n] --> sumA((Sum +))
    noiseA[Primary noise n0] --> sumA
    sumA --> prim[Primary signal d of n]
    noiseB[Reference noise n1 correlated with n0] --> fadapt[Adaptive filter W of n]
    fadapt --> yhat[Anti noise y of n]
    prim --> sumB((Sum -))
    yhat --> sumB
    sumB --> err[Error e of n drives adaptation]
    err --> ctrl[Weight update algorithm LMS or RLS]
    ctrl --> fadapt
    err --> out[Clean output e approximates s of n]
```

**How to read the diagram:**
* The *primary* input $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ contains the ECG plus 50 Hz mains hum.
* The *reference* input $n_1(n)$ is a second electrode picking up mostly the hum (placed near the body, away from the heart).
* The adaptive filter shapes $n_1(n)$ into $y(n) \approx n_0(n)$.
* The error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n) \approx s(n)$ — the clean ECG — is fed back to update $W$.

> [!TIP]
> **Critical requirement for ANC to work:** The reference noise $n_1(n)$ must be correlated with the corrupting noise $n_0(n)$, but *uncorrelated* with the desired signal $s(n)$. Otherwise, the filter will cancel $s$ as well, causing signal distortion.

## 4.2 Performance Surface and LMS Trajectory

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axisX[w1 axis]
    axisY[w2 axis]
    bowl[Quadratic MSE bowl]
    opt[Optimum w star at bottom of bowl]
    start[Initial weights w 0]
    step1[Step 1 down the gradient]
    step2[Step 2]
    step3[Step 3 zigzag toward optimum]
    start --> step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> opt
    bowl -.- opt
```

> [!NOTE]
> The zigzag trajectory in the LMS plot is the visual fingerprint of stochastic gradient descent. The size of the zigzags is controlled by $\mu$ — small $\mu$ gives slow, smooth convergence; large $\mu$ gives fast but noisy convergence, eventually diverging past the stability bound.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of an Adaptive Filtering System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputBlock[Input conditioning]
        in1[Analog front end amplifier and anti alias filter] --> in2[ADC at Fs greater than 2W]
    end
    subgraph algoBlock[Adaptive algorithm core]
        buf1[Tap delay line x n x n-1 to x n-M+1] --> mac[MAC unit wT x]
        wmem[Weight memory w0 to wM-1] --> mac
        mac --> yout[Filter output y n]
        dref[Desired signal d n] --> summer((Subtract))
        yout --> summer
        summer --> errout[Error e n]
        errout --> update[Weight update equation LMS or RLS]
        update --> wmem
    end
    in2 --> buf1
    in2 --> dref
    errout --> outport[Clean output to display or DSP]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-level summary of signal flow:** Anti-aliased input $\to$ tapped delay line $\to$ MAC (multiply-accumulate) with current weights $\to$ produce estimate $y(n)$ $\to$ subtract from desired $d(n)$ to get $e(n)$ $\to$ update weights using LMS/RLS rule $\to$ loop back. The output of interest is $e(n)$ itself, *not* $y(n)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*State and explain the orthogonality principle associated with the Wiener filter.*

**Model Answer:**
The orthogonality principle states that *at the optimum weight setting, the error signal $e(n)$ is statistically orthogonal (uncorrelated) to every tap input $x(n-k)$, $k = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$*. Mathematically:
$$E[e_{opt}(n) \, x(n-k)] = 0 \quad \text{for all } k$$
In vector form: $E[e_{opt}(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)] = \mathbf{0}$. This principle is the foundation for deriving the Wiener-Hopf equation $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p}$, because if any further weight change could reduce MSE, $e$ would still be correlated with some $x(n-k)$ — a contradiction at the optimum.

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Define the step size $\mu$ in the LMS algorithm. What is its effect on convergence speed and steady-state error?*

**Model Answer:**
The step size $\mu$ is a positive scalar that controls the magnitude of weight correction at each iteration: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$. A **larger $\mu$** gives faster convergence but larger steady-state misadjustement and risk of divergence; a **smaller $\mu$** gives slower convergence but lower residual MSE. The trade-off is fundamental and the practitioner must choose $0 < \mu \ll 1/\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ for stable operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### **QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) Derive the Wiener-Hopf equation for the optimal FIR filter. Clearly state the orthogonality principle and the physical meaning of $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{p}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. Define the filter output $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$, error $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$, and cost $J = E[e^2(n)]$.
2. Expand the cost using $J = \sigma_d^2 - 2 \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}$, where:
   * $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n) \mathbf{x}^T(n)]$ is the $M \times M$ input autocorrelation matrix — it encodes the second-order statistics of the input process. Element $R_{ij} = E[x(n-i) x(n-j)]$.
   * $\mathbf{p} = E[d(n) \mathbf{x}(n)]$ is the $M \times 1$ cross-correlation vector between the desired signal and each input tap. Element $p_i = E[d(n) x(n-i)]$.
3. Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$: $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = 2 \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w} - 2 \mathbf{p}$.
4. Setting $\nabla J = \mathbf{0}$ gives the **Wiener-Hopf equation**:
   $$\mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p} \implies \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$$
5. **Orthogonality principle:** $E[e_{opt}(n) \mathbf{x}(n)] = \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{0}$. The error at optimum is uncorrelated with every input tap.

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an adaptive noise canceller (ANC) used for removing 50 Hz mains interference from an ECG signal. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

* **Primary input** $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$, where $s(n)$ is clean ECG and $n_0(n)$ is 50 Hz hum plus amplifier drift.
* **Reference input** $n_1(n)$: a second electrode placed close to the body but away from the heart picks up mostly mains interference, with negligible ECG content.
* The **adaptive FIR filter** $W(z)$ processes $n_1(n)$ to produce an estimate $y(n) \approx n_0(n)$.
* The summer computes $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
* The **LMS algorithm** updates $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) n_1(n)$.
* At convergence, $y(n) \approx n_0(n)$ and $e(n) \approx s(n)$, the clean ECG, available at the output.
* **Advantages over fixed 50 Hz notch filter:** (i) Tracks frequency drift of mains, (ii) preserves ECG spectral content near 50 Hz, (iii) cancels harmonics, (iv) adapts to electrode impedance changes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> * Do not confuse the ANC's output with the filter output. **$e(n)$ is the output**, *not* $y(n)$. Writing "the output of the adaptive filter is the clean ECG" loses 2 marks immediately.
> * Forgetting to justify the choice of reference location (uncorrelated with $s$, correlated with $n_0$) loses 1 mark in the application explanation.

### **QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) Derive the LMS weight update equation starting from the instantaneous squared error. State the convergence condition. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. Instantaneous cost: $\hat{J}(n) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^2(n)$.
2. Differentiate: $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{J}(n) = e(n) \cdot \frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$.
3. Steepest descent update: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{J}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$.
4. Convergence condition: $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{max}$. For practical use, $0 < \mu < 1/\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})$, ensuring all modes of the weight-error vector decay geometrically.

**(b) Compare the LMS and RLS algorithms in terms of computational complexity, convergence speed, steady-state error, and stability. State suitable biomedical applications for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | LMS | RLS |
|---|---|---|
| Complexity | $O(M)$ per sample | $O(M^2)$ per sample |
| Convergence | Slow, depends on $\chi(\mathbf{R})$ | Fast, $\sim 2M$ iterations |
| Steady-state error | $\mathcal{M} = \mu \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})/2$ | Approaches $J_{min} (1-\lambda)$ |
| Stability | Very robust, self-stabilizing | Needs square-root or UD factorization for long filters |
| Biomedical use | Hearing aids, cochlear implants, wearable ECG monitors | Fetal ECG extraction, EEG artifact removal, MRI gradient de-noising |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> * Always quote the *exact* form of the LMS update used in the question. Mixing $2\mu$ and $\mu$ conventions loses 1 mark.
> * The RLS update involves a matrix $\mathbf{P}(n)$ — the inverse of the exponentially-weighted input correlation — do *not* call it simply "the autocorrelation". The qualifier "exponentially-weighted" is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

* **Wiener filter** is *static*; **adaptive filter** is *dynamic* and tracks non-stationarity.
* The **Wiener-Hopf equation** is $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p}$; solution $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$; minimum MSE $J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$.
* The **orthogonality principle**: $E[e_{opt} \mathbf{x}] = \mathbf{0}$ at the optimum.
* **LMS update:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$, with $0 < \mu < 1/\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ for stable operation.
* **RLS** uses forgetting factor $\lambda \in [0.98, 0.995]$ and the matrix $\mathbf{P}(n)$ for fast convergence at $O(M^2)$ cost.
* **ANC** output is the *error* $e(n)$, not the filter output $y(n)$.
* **Reference noise** for ANC must be correlated with primary noise and uncorrelated with the desired signal.
* **Eigenvalue spread** $\chi(\mathbf{R})$ dictates LMS convergence speed; RLS is insensitive to it.
* **Misadjustement** $\mathcal{M} = \mu \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})/2$ is the trade-off knob in LMS design.
* **Key biomedical applications:** 50 Hz mains removal in ECG, fetal ECG extraction, EEG ocular-artifact cancellation, hearing aid acoustic-feedback cancellation, neural-recording common-mode rejection.
* **Convergence speed ranking** (fastest to slowest for correlated input): RLS $\gg$ NLMS $\approx$ VSLMS $\gg$ LMS $\gg$ Sign-LMS.
* **Stability rule of thumb:** If you cannot compute $\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ on-line, set $\mu \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$ normalized by input power and tune empirically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection of Biomedical Signals in Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Detection of Biomedical Signals in Noise ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Signal Detection in Noise** is the statistical and deterministic process of identifying the presence (or estimating the parameters) of a desired biomedical signal—such as the **QRS complex of ECG**, the **alpha rhythm of EEG**, or the **evoked response (EP)**—that is buried in additive random noise, interference (e.g., **50 Hz powerline interference**, baseline wander), or competing physiological artifacts. Mathematically, the received observation is modeled as a **binary hypothesis testing** problem: $$ x(t) = \begin{cases} n(t), & H_0 \quad \text{(signal-absent hypothesis)} \\ s(t) + n(t), & H_1 \quad \text{(signal-present hypothesis)} \end{cases} $$ where $s(t)$ is the deterministic (or stochastic) biomedical signal of interest and $n(t)$ is the corrupting **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** with two-sided Power Spectral Density $\mathbf{N_0/2}$ (W/Hz). ### 1.2 The Detection Goal The detector must decide between two competing hypotheses: - **$H_0$:** Only noise is present → Output "No Signal". - **$H_1$:** Signal + noise is present → Output "Signal Detected". This decision is governed by a **decision rule** that minimizes a chosen cost function (Bayesian) or maximizes a chosen probability (Neyman-Pearson). ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "Hearing Your Name in a Noisy Market"** > Imagine standing in a crowded fish market where hundreds of people are speaking. Your brain is performing **real-time signal detection in noise**: > - **Signal (s(t))** = your friend's voice saying your name. > - **Noise (n(t))** = the chorus of other voices, vehicle honks, and background chatter. > - **Matched Filter** = your brain's tuned auditory filter that recognizes the spectral signature and rhythm of your friend's voice. > - **Decision Threshold** = the loudness level above which your brain confidently says "Yes, that was my name." > - **Probability of Detection ($P_D$)** = correctly hearing your name when it is called. > - **Probability of False Alarm ($P_{FA}$)** = wrongly turning around when a stranger says something phonetically similar ("James" vs. "Jane"). The brain accomplishes this by correlating the incoming sound against a **stored template (memory)** and integrating over time — the exact strategy used by a **Matched Filter** in DSP. ### 1.4 Why Detection is Hard in Biomedical Signals Biomedical signals pose a unique challenge compared to radar/comm signals: | Property | Biomedical Signal | Consequence | |----------|-------------------|-------------| | **SNR** | Often $\le \mathbf{0\ dB}$ | Signal is weaker than noise | | **Bandwidth** | Narrow (e.g., ECG QRS $\approx 40\ Hz$) | Allows narrowband filtering | | **Stationarity** | Quasi-stationary only | Classical assumptions partially violated | | **Variability** | Morphology changes between patients/conditions | Fixed matched filter is suboptimal | | **Artifacts** | Motion, EMG, baseline wander | Non-Gaussian, colored noise | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Detection Framework — Step-by-Step Logic The generic detection chain in a biomedical instrument is: 1. **Acquisition**: Electrodes/transducers pick up $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$. 2. **Pre-filtering**: Analog anti-aliasing filter restricts bandwidth to $[0, B]$ Hz. 3. **A/D Conversion**: Sampling at $f_s \ge 2B$ (Nyquist criterion). 4. **Detection Operation**: Apply either a **matched filter**, **correlator**, or **Wiener filter**. 5. **Threshold Comparison**: Compare the test statistic $\Lambda(x)$ against a threshold $\eta$. 6. **Decision**: Declare $H_1$ if $\Lambda > \eta$, else $H_0$. ### 2.2 Matched Filter — The Optimal Linear Detector The **Matched Filter** is the linear time-invariant filter that **maximizes the output Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the decision instant $t = T$ for a known signal $s(t)$ in AWGN. **Derivation Philosophy** (Why?): We want to design an impulse response $h(t)$ such that the output $y(T)$ contains the largest possible signal component relative to the noise variance. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they form the spine of KTU Module-2 questions.** | # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | |---|---------|----------|-------------------| | 1 | Observation model | $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ | $s(t)$: signal (V), $n(t)$: noise (V) | | 2 | Signal Energy | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} s^2(t)\, dt$ | Joules (or V²·s) | | 3 | Matched Filter Impulse Response | $h(t) = K \cdot s(T - t)$ | $K$: arbitrary gain constant | | 4 | Matched Filter Output SNR | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{2E}{N_0}$ | Dimensionless (ratio) | | 5 | Matched Filter Transfer Function | $H(f) = K \cdot S^*(f)\, e^{-j2\pi f T}$ | $S^*(f)$: complex conjugate of signal spectrum | | 6 | Autocorrelation of $s(t)$ | $r_{ss}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} s(t)\, s(t+\tau)\, dt$ | V²·s | | 7 | Cross-correlation detector | $r_{xs}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, s(t+\tau)\, dt$ | V²·s | | 8 | Noise PSD at output | $S_{n_o}(f) = \vert H(f)\vert^2 \cdot \dfrac{N_0}{2}$ | W/Hz | | 9 | Output Noise Variance | $\sigma_n^2 = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^2 df$ | V² | | 10 | Output Signal at $t=T$ | $y(T) = K \cdot E_s$ | V | | 11 | Wiener–Khinchin Theorem | $S_{ss}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{r_{ss}(\tau)\}$ | W/Hz | | 12 | Probability of False Alarm | $P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\eta}{\sigma_n}\right)$ | $Q(\cdot)$: complementary Gaussian CDF | | 13 | Probability of Detection | $P_D = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\eta - E_s/\sigma_n}{1}\right)$ | — | | 14 | Likelihood Ratio Test | $\Lambda(x) = \dfrac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)} \gtrless \eta$ | — | | 15 | Log-Likelihood Ratio (Gaussian) | $\ln\Lambda(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\int \!\! \left[2x(t)s(t) - s^2(t)\right] dt$ | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Reminder for Tables:** Vertical bars are written using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert H(f)\vert$) to prevent markdown table breakage. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Holter Monitors & AEDs (Automated External Defibrillators):** Use matched-filter-based QRS detectors (Pan-Tompkins algorithm) to identify ventricular fibrillation reliably in noisy ambulatory ECG. - **Hearing Aids:** Implement matched filtering-like processing to enhance speech against background chatter. - **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Use correlation detectors to detect P300 evoked potentials buried in ongoing EEG noise (SNR $\approx$ -10 dB). - **Fetal ECG Extraction:** Matched filters isolate fetal QRS from maternal ECG + uterine noise. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Matched Filter (Complete, Exam-Ready) We are given an LTI filter with impulse response $h(t)$ acting on the input $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$. **Step 1 — Output of the LTI System:** The filter output is the convolution: $$ y(t) = h(t) * x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\, x(t - \tau)\, d\tau $$ **Step 2 — Signal Component at the Decision Instant $t = T$:** Setting $t = T$ and substituting $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$: $$ y(T) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\, s(T - \tau)\, d\tau \; + \; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\, n(T - \tau)\, d\tau $$ Define the **signal component** $y_s(T)$ and **noise component** $y_n(T)$: $$ y_s(T) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\, s(T - \tau)\, d\tau $$ $$ y_n(T) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\, n(T - \tau)\, d\tau $$ **Step 3 — Output Noise Variance (using white-noise property):** For AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$, the autocorrelation is $r_{nn}(\tau) = \dfrac{N_0}{2}\, \delta(\tau)$. The output noise variance is: $$ \sigma_n^2 = E\!\left[y_n^2(T)\right] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int h(\tau_1)h(\tau_2)\, r_{nn}(\tau_2 - \tau_1)\, d\tau_1 d\tau_2 $$ Since $r_{nn}(\tau_2 - \tau_1) = \dfrac{N_0}{2}\, \delta(\tau_2 - \tau_1)$, the integration collapses: $$ \sigma_n^2 = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h^2(\tau)\, d\tau $$ **Step 4 — Define Output SNR:** $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{y_s^2(T)}{\sigma_n^2} = \dfrac{\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\, s(T - \tau)\, d\tau\right]^2}{\dfrac{N_0}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h^2(\tau)\, d\tau} $$ **Step 5 — Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality:** For real-valued functions $u(\tau)$ and $v(\tau)$: $$ \left[\int u(\tau)v(\tau)\, d\tau\right]^2 \le \left[\int u^2(\tau)\, d\tau\right] \cdot \left[\int v^2(\tau)\, d\tau\right] $$ Let $u(\tau) = h(\tau)$ and $v(\tau) = s(T-\tau)$: $$ \left[\int h(\tau)\, s(T-\tau)\, d\tau\right]^2 \le \int h^2(\tau)\, d\tau \;\cdot\; \int s^2(T-\tau)\, d\tau $$ By the change of variable $\tau' = T - \tau$, the second integral equals the **signal energy** $E_s = \int s^2(t)\, dt$. **Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify:** $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{out}} \le \dfrac{\int h^2(\tau)\, d\tau \cdot E_s}{\dfrac{N_0}{2}\int h^2(\tau)\, d\tau} = \dfrac{2 E_s}{N_0} $$ **Step 7 — Equality Condition of Cauchy–Schwarz:** Equality holds iff $h(\tau) = K \cdot s(T - \tau)$ for some constant $K$. This is the **Matched Filter**! $$ \boxed{\,h_{\text{MF}}(t) = K \cdot s(T - t)\,} $$ **Step 8 — Maximum Achievable SNR:** $$ \boxed{\,\text{SNR}_{\text{out, max}} = \dfrac{2 E_s}{N_0}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The maximum SNR depends **only on the signal energy and noise PSD**, not on the signal's waveform shape. This is a famous result credited to North (1943) and Van Vleck & Middleton. ### 3.2 Frequency-Domain Interpretation Taking the Fourier Transform of $h(t) = K\, s(T - t)$ and using the time-reversal & delay properties: $$ H_{\text{MF}}(f) = K \cdot \mathcal{F}\{s(T - t)\} = K \cdot S^*(f)\, e^{-j2\pi f T} $$ The magnitude response matches the signal spectrum, while the phase aligns all frequency components to add constructively at $t = T$. ### 3.3 Derivation of the Correlation Detector Setting $K = 1$ in the matched filter output: $$ y(T) = \int h(\tau)\, x(T-\tau)\, d\tau = \int s(\tau)\, x(T - \tau)\, d\tau $$ Substituting $t = T - \tau$ (i.e., $\tau = T - t$, $d\tau = -dt$): $$ y(T) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} s(T - t)\, x(t)\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, s(T - t)\, dt $$ This is precisely the **cross-correlation** between $x(t)$ and $s(t)$, evaluated at lag $\tau = T$: $$ y(T) = r_{xs}(T) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, s(t + T)\, dt \bigg\vert_{\tau = T} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Hence, the matched filter and the correlator are mathematically identical implementations of the optimal detector.** ### 3.4 Probability of Detection and False Alarm (Gaussian Case) For the matched filter output $y(T) = \pm E_s + n$: - Under $H_0$: $y(T) \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, \sigma_n^2)$. - Under $H_1$: $y(T) \sim \mathcal{N}(E_s,\, \sigma_n^2)$. Decision rule: Declare $H_1$ if $y(T) > \eta$. $$ P_{FA} = P\!\left(y(T) > \eta \mid H_0\right) = \int_{\eta}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_n} e^{-y^2 / 2\sigma_n^2}\, dy = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\eta}{\sigma_n}\right) $$ $$ P_D = P\!\left(y(T) > \eta \mid H_1\right) = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\eta - E_s}{\sigma_n}\right) $$ where $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\, du$ is the **Gaussian tail function**. ### 3.5 Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) For discrete observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$ under AWGN: $$ \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} = \dfrac{\prod_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x_i - s_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]}{\prod_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{x_i^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]} $$ Taking the logarithm and simplifying: $$ \ln\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[2 x_i s_i - s_i^2\right] $$ The optimal detector decides $H_1$ when: $$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i s_i \gtrless \dfrac{E_s}{2} + \sigma^2 \ln\eta $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notice**: $\sum x_i s_i$ is the **discrete correlator output** — again confirming that correlation/matched-filter is the optimal detector. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Optimal Biomedical Signal Detection Chain The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full functional flow of detecting a known biomedical signal (e.g., QRS complex) buried in noise. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["BIOELECTRIC SOURCE
ECG EEG EMG EP"] --> B["INSTRUMENTATION AMP
Gain 1000 CMRR 100dB"] B --> C["ANALOG BANDPASS FILTER
0.05 to 150 Hz"] C --> D["ADC
Sampling Rate 500 Hz"] D --> E["DIGITAL PREPROCESSING
Baseline Removal 50Hz Notch"] E --> F["MATCHED FILTER
h t equals K times s of T minus t"] F --> G["THRESHOLD COMPARATOR
Decision Threshold eta"] G --> H{Output Decision} H -- y greater than eta --> I["H1 DETECTED
QRS or EP Present"] H -- y less than eta --> J["H0 NO SIGNAL
Noise only"] F -.feedback path.-> K["TEMPLATE STORAGE
Reference QRS waveform"] K --> F G --> L["PERFORMANCE METRICS
Pd and Pfa and ROC"] ``` ### 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy of Detection Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD D["SIGNAL DETECTION IN NOISE"] --> D1["LINEAR DETECTORS"] D --> D2["STATISTICAL DETECTORS"] D --> D3["ADAPTIVE DETECTORS"] D1 --> D1a["Matched Filter"] D1 --> D1b["Wiener Filter MMSE"] D1 --> D1c["Correlator"] D2 --> D2a["Likelihood Ratio Test"] D2 --> D2b["Bayes Criterion"] D2 --> D2c["Neyman Pearson Criterion"] D3 --> D3a["Adaptive Matched Filter"] D3 --> D3b["LMS / RLS based Detector"] D3 --> D3c["Template Updating QRS"] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Correlator Realization ```mermaid flowchart LR X["x of t equals s of t plus n of t"] --> MUL["MULTIPLIER
x t times s t"] ST["TEMPLATE
REFERENCE s of t"] --> MUL MUL --> INT["INTEGRATOR
0 to T"] INT --> COMP["THRESHOLD
COMPARATOR"] COMP --> DEC["DECISION
H1 or H0"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Figure 4.3 Insight:** This correlator is mathematically equivalent to the matched filter; both produce the same SNR at $t = T$. ### 4.4 Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR R["ROC CURVE Pd vs Pfa"] --> R1["High Pd and Low Pfa
GOOD DETECTOR
ROC bows toward top left"] R --> R2["Pd equals Pfa
RANDOM GUESS
Diagonal line y equals x"] R --> R3["As threshold eta increases
Pfa decreases Pd decreases"] R --> R4["As SNR increases
ROC bows further upward"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1.** Define the **matched filter**. Write its impulse response for detecting a known signal $s(t)$ in additive white Gaussian noise. Why is it called "matched"? **Model Answer:** A matched filter is an LTI filter whose impulse response is matched (or tuned) to the known signal $s(t)$ to maximize the output SNR. Its impulse response is: $$ h(t) = K\, s(T - t) $$ It is called "matched" because its time-reversed, delayed replica of the signal template is convolved with the input — effectively aligning the signal with itself for maximum correlation. The maximum SNR achieved is $\mathbf{SNR_{\max} = 2E_s/N_0}$. > **Examiner's Note:** Students often forget the delay $T$. Always include it. **(Lose 1 mark if missing.)** --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2.** Distinguish between **matched filter** and **Wiener filter** in the context of biomedical signal detection. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Matched Filter | Wiener Filter | |--------|---------------|---------------| | **Goal** | Maximize output SNR | Minimize MSE | | **Signal knowledge** | Requires known template $s(t)$ | Requires PSDs $S_{ss}(f)$, $S_{nn}(f)$ | | **Noise assumption** | White Gaussian | Can be colored | | **Optimality criterion** | Deterministic SNR maximization | Statistical MSE minimization | | **Application** | QRS detection, EP averaging | Denoising ECG, image restoration | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the impulse response of the **matched filter** for detecting a known deterministic signal $s(t)$ in AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. Show that the maximum achievable output SNR is $\dfrac{2 E_s}{N_0}$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A known ECG QRS-like pulse $s(t) = A \cdot \Pi\!\left(\dfrac{t - T/2}{T}\right)$ (rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and duration $T$) is observed in AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. Compute: (i) the signal energy $E_s$, (ii) the matched filter impulse response $h(t)$, and (iii) the output SNR in dB given $A = 1\ mV$, $T = 100\ ms$, $N_0 = 10^{-8}\ V^2/Hz$. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** **(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:** *(Provide the full derivation from Section 3.1 above, with the following sub-step scoring:)* 1. Setup of $y(t) = h(t) * x(t)$ and signal/noise decomposition at $t = T$ — **(2 marks)** 2. Computation of output noise variance $\sigma_n^2 = \dfrac{N_0}{2}\int h^2(\tau)\, d\tau$ — **(1 mark)** 3. Definition of SNR and application of Cauchy–Schwarz inequality — **(2 marks)** 4. Equality condition yielding $h(t) = K\, s(T-t)$ and $\text{SNR}_{\max} = 2E_s/N_0$ — **(2 marks)** **(b) Numerical Problem [7 Marks]:** **(i) Signal Energy:** $$ E_s = \int_{0}^{T} A^2\, dt = A^2 T = (10^{-3})^2 \times (100 \times 10^{-3}) = 10^{-7}\ V^2\cdot s $$ **(ii) Matched Filter Impulse Response:** Since $s(t) = A \cdot \Pi\!\left(\dfrac{t - T/2}{T}\right)$ is a rectangle from $0$ to $T$: $$ h(t) = K \cdot s(T - t) = K A \cdot \Pi\!\left(\dfrac{T - t - T/2}{T}\right) = K A \cdot \Pi\!\left(\dfrac{T/2 - t}{T}\right) $$ That is, a rectangle of amplitude $KA$ from $0$ to $T$ (time-reversed, but symmetric since $s(t)$ is symmetric). **(iii) Output SNR (in dB):** $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{out, max}} = \dfrac{2 E_s}{N_0} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-8}} = 20 $$ Converting to decibels: $$ \text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(20) = 10 \times 1.301 = \mathbf{13.01\ dB} $$ > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > > [!WARNING] > > **Common Mistake:** Students forget the factor of **2** in $\text{SNR} = 2E_s/N_0$ and write only $E_s/N_0$. This costs **1 full mark**. Also, ensure unit consistency: $A$ in Volts, $T$ in seconds, $N_0$ in V²/Hz — so $E_s$ must come out in V²·s. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** With the necessary mathematical expressions, explain the **likelihood ratio test (LRT)** for detecting a known signal in Gaussian noise. State the **Neyman-Pearson criterion** and discuss its relevance to ECG QRS detection. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A cardiac monitor uses a correlator to detect the QRS template $s(t)$ of duration $T = 250\ ms$ from noisy ECG. The template energy is $E_s = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ V^2\cdot s$ and the noise PSD is $N_0/2 = 2 \times 10^{-9}\ V^2/Hz$. If the threshold is set to achieve $P_{FA} = 10^{-3}$, find the corresponding probability of detection $P_D$. Use $Q^{-1}(10^{-3}) = 3.09$. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** **(a) LRT and Neyman-Pearson [7 Marks]:** The likelihood ratio is defined as: $$ \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} $$ where $p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_i)$ is the conditional probability density of observation vector $\mathbf{x}$. **Decision Rule:** Declare $H_1$ if $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) > \eta$, else $H_0$. For AWGN, expanding gives: $$ \ln\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[2 x_i s_i - s_i^2\right] \gtrless \ln\eta $$ **Neyman-Pearson Criterion:** For a fixed, tolerable probability of false alarm $P_{FA} = \alpha$, the LRT maximizes the probability of detection $P_D$. This is the most relevant criterion for **medical diagnostics**, where clinicians want to avoid missing a critical event (e.g., ventricular fibrillation), so $P_D$ must be high while bounding $P_{FA}$ to prevent false alarms. **(b) Numerical Problem [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — Compute SNR:** $$ \text{SNR}_{\max} = \dfrac{2 E_s}{N_0} = \dfrac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-9}) \times 2} $$ Wait — careful: $N_0/2 = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ implies $N_0 = 4 \times 10^{-9}\ V^2/Hz$. So: $$ \text{SNR}_{\max} = \dfrac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-9}} = 2500 $$ **Step 2 — Threshold from $P_{FA}$:** $$ P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\eta}{\sigma_n}\right) = 10^{-3} $$ So $\dfrac{\eta}{\sigma_n} = Q^{-1}(10^{-3}) = 3.09$, hence $\eta = 3.09\, \sigma_n$. **Step 3 — Compute $P_D$:** $$ P_D = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\eta - E_s/\sigma_n}{1}\right) \quad \text{(using normalized SNR)} $$ The **deflection coefficient** $d^2 = 2 E_s/N_0 = 2500$. The detection probability is: $$ P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(P_{FA}) - \sqrt{d^2}\right) = Q\!\left(3.09 - 50\right) = Q(-46.91) \approx 1.0 $$ That is, $\mathbf{P_D \approx 1}$ — practically perfect detection, because the SNR is very high. > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > > [!WARNING] > > **Two common errors:** > > 1. Confusing $\sigma_n$ (noise **standard deviation** at output) with $N_0$ (two-sided PSD). Always link them via $\sigma_n^2 = (N_0/2) \int \vert H(f)\vert^2 df$. > > 2. Misreading "noise PSD $= N_0/2$" as $N_0/2$ being the **total**, not the **two-sided** PSD. Read the problem statement literally. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Facts to Memorize Before the Exam:** - ⭐ The **matched filter** maximizes output SNR for known signals in AWGN. - ⭐ Impulse response: $h(t) = K \cdot s(T - t)$ — **time-reversed + delayed** version of the signal. - ⭐ Maximum output SNR: $\mathbf{SNR_{\max} = 2 E_s / N_0}$ — independent of signal shape. - ⭐ Transfer function: $H(f) = K \cdot S^*(f)\, e^{-j2\pi f T}$. - ⭐ Matched filter and **correlator** are mathematically equivalent realizations. - ⭐ Output noise variance: $\sigma_n^2 = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \int h^2(t)\, dt$. - ⭐ $P_{FA}$ and $P_D$ are linked via the **Gaussian tail function $Q(\cdot)$**. - ⭐ **Neyman-Pearson** = fix $P_{FA}$, maximize $P_D$ (most common in biomedical). - ⭐ **LRT** = optimal statistical test (Bayes reduces to LRT for symmetric costs). - ⭐ **Wiener filter** differs in that it minimizes **MSE** and uses PSDs. - ⭐ Real-world example: **Pan-Tompkins QRS detector** = matched-filter + adaptive threshold. - ⭐ For EEG EP detection: SNR $\sim$ -10 dB → requires **signal averaging** (coherent integration) to push effective SNR above detection threshold. - ⭐ ROC curve plots $P_D$ vs $P_{FA}$; better detectors bow toward the **top-left corner**. > **Time-Saver Tip:** For any KTU 14-mark derivation, always structure as: > *(i) define $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ → (ii) write $y(T)$ decomposition → (iii) compute $\sigma_n^2$ → (iv) define SNR → (v) Cauchy–Schwarz → (vi) equality condition → (vii) final $\text{SNR}_{\max}$ formula.* > > Following this 7-step structure consistently guarantees full marks even with minor arithmetic slips.

topics.title: Removal of Artifacts of One Signal Embedded in Another
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Removal of Artifacts of One Signal Embedded in Another

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing**, the **"Removal of Artifacts of One Signal Embedded in Another"** refers to the systematic process of identifying, isolating, and cancelling an undesired physiological or non-physiological component (the *artifact* or *interference signal*) that has been superimposed upon a desired biomedical signal of interest during acquisition.

Mathematically, the corrupted observed signal is modelled as:

$$x[n] = s[n] + \eta[n] + v[n]$$

where:
- $s[n]$ = the desired clean physiological signal (e.g., fetal ECG, true EEG rhythm)
- $\eta[n]$ = the unwanted artifact signal embedded within $x[n]$ (e.g., maternal ECG, powerline hum, motion artifact)
- $v[n]$ = additive broadband random noise (sensor noise, thermal noise)

The objective of artifact removal is to recover an estimate $\hat{s}[n]$ that is maximally free from $\eta[n]$ while preserving the morphology and diagnostic information of $s[n]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU PEICT633 Module 2 framework, this topic is closely associated with the **Adaptive Filtering** paradigm, particularly the **Widrow-Hoff LMS algorithm** and the **Wiener filter** applied to cancellation of one signal from another (e.g., maternal ECG cancellation from abdominal recordings).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Two Voices in One Room" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are recording your friend's voice (the **desired signal**, say fetal heartbeat) using a microphone placed near a loud TV playing a news channel (the **artifact**, say maternal heartbeat). Your recording contains BOTH voices mixed together — your friend's quiet voice is *embedded* inside the TV's loud voice. If you have a SECOND microphone placed near ONLY the TV (capturing the artifact alone as a **reference**), you can mathematically "subtract" the TV's contribution from your main recording using an *adaptive noise canceller* — leaving only your friend's voice.
>
> In biomedical terms: the **primary input** is the chest/abdominal electrode, the **reference input** is a second electrode that picks up ONLY the artifact (e.g., maternal thoracic ECG), and an adaptive filter continuously learns how the artifact maps from reference into the primary input and cancels it.

The two classical biomedical scenarios where this concept is rigorously applied are:

| Scenario | Desired Signal $s[n]$ | Artifact $\eta[n]$ | Reference Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fetal ECG extraction | Fetal ECG (fECG) | Maternal ECG (mECG) | Thoracic maternal ECG lead |
| Powerline interference | ECG / EEG waveform | 50 Hz / 60 Hz mains hum | Mains voltage tap |
| Motion artifact removal | EEG | Electrode motion artefact | Accelerometer placed on head |
| EMG contamination | ECG | Muscle electrical activity | Auxiliary EMG lead |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Numerical Parameters to Memorise for KTU Exams:**
>
> - **Mains Powerline Frequency:** **50 Hz (India/Europe)** or **60 Hz (USA)** — the notch filter target
> - **Sample Rate for ECG processing:** typically **$\mathbf{f_s = 500\,\text{Hz}}$** or **$\mathbf{1000\,\text{Hz}}$**
> - **Maternal ECG R-peak amplitude:** **0.5 mV – 2 mV** (dominant)
> - **Fetal ECG R-peak amplitude:** **10 μV – 100 μV** (very weak, often buried)
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** improvement goal: typically **20 dB – 40 dB** after cancellation

---

## 1.4 Classification of Embedded Artifacts

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite:** Students are often asked to *classify* artifacts. Use this taxonomy:

1. **Physiological Artifacts** — originate from the body's own electrical activity.
   - Examples: Maternal ECG in fetal recordings; EMG interference in ECG; EOG (eye-blink) in EEG.
2. **Non-Physiological / External Artifacts** — originate from external sources.
   - Examples: 50 Hz powerline interference; electrode motion; instrumentation noise.

The removal strategy differs fundamentally:
- For **deterministic periodic** artifacts (e.g., 50 Hz hum, maternal ECG) → **adaptive filtering / template subtraction**.
- For **random/stochastic** artifacts (e.g., thermal noise) → **Wiener filtering / ensemble averaging**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Model of the Artifact-Embedded Signal

The general observed model in discrete time is:

$$x[n] = s[n] + \sum_{k=1}^{K} a_k \cdot \eta[n - \Delta_k] + v[n]$$

where $a_k$ is the attenuation coefficient and $\Delta_k$ is the propagation delay through tissue between the artifact source and the recording electrode. For a **single artifact** with delay and attenuation:

$$x[n] = s[n] + a \cdot \eta[n - \Delta]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** The artifact is rarely a clean copy of the reference — it is **filtered, attenuated, and delayed** by the body tissues. This is exactly why a *fixed* filter fails and an *adaptive* filter is required: the filter must *learn* the unknown path $\mathcal{H}\{.\}$ that maps $\eta[n] \rightarrow a \cdot \eta[n-\Delta]$.

---

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Artifact Removal Techniques

### Pillar 1 — **Wiener Filter (Statistical / Optimal Linear Filter)**

The Wiener filter provides the **mean-square-error optimal** linear filter coefficients assuming stationary signals with known second-order statistics (autocorrelation $r_{xx}[\tau]$ and cross-correlation $r_{dx}[\tau]$).

In the frequency domain:

$$W(z) = \frac{P_{xd}(z)}{P_{xx}(z)}$$

where $P_{xd}(z)$ is the cross-power spectral density and $P_{xx}(z)$ is the auto-power spectral density.

**Limitation:** Requires stationary signals and known statistics — rarely true in biomedical contexts.

---

### Pillar 2 — **Adaptive Filter (The Workhorse of Biomedical Artifact Removal)**

An adaptive filter $\mathbf{w}[n]$ of order $M$ processes a reference input $r[n]$ to produce an output $y[n]$ that estimates the artifact:

$$y[n] = \sum_{i=0}^{M-1} w_i[n] \cdot r[n-i] = \mathbf{w}^T[n] \cdot \mathbf{r}[n]$$

The **error signal** is:

$$e[n] = x[n] - y[n]$$

The adaptive algorithm iteratively adjusts $\mathbf{w}[n]$ to minimise $E\{e^2[n]\}$ — under the assumption that $r[n]$ is correlated with the artifact but uncorrelated with $s[n]$, minimising the error yields the cleanest possible estimate of $s[n]$.

---

### Pillar 3 — **Template Subtraction (Used for Maternal ECG Cancellation)**

1. Detect each maternal R-peak in the abdominal signal.
2. Extract a window around each beat (e.g., 200 ms before to 400 ms after the R-peak).
3. Compute an ensemble average across all detected beats → *maternal ECG template* $T[n]$.
4. Subtract the scaled, aligned template from each individual beat:

$$\hat{s}[n] = x[n] - \alpha \cdot T[n - n_{\text{R-peak}}]$$

where $\alpha$ is a scale factor (often $\alpha \approx 1$) and $n_{\text{R-peak}}$ is the location of the R-peak.

---

## 2.3 The LMS (Least Mean Squares) Algorithm — Widrow-Hoff

This is the **most frequently examined adaptive algorithm** in KTU papers.

**Weight Update Equation:**

$$\mathbf{w}[n+1] = \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu \cdot e[n] \cdot \mathbf{r}[n]$$

**Filter Output:**

$$y[n] = \mathbf{w}^T[n] \cdot \mathbf{r}[n]$$

**Error Signal:**

$$e[n] = x[n] - y[n]$$

where:
- $\mu$ = step-size (learning rate), constrained to $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$ where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix
- $e[n]$ is treated as the cleaned estimate of $s[n]$ (i.e., $\hat{s}[n] = e[n]$)
- $\mathbf{r}[n] = [r[n], r[n-1], \ldots, r[n-M+1]]^T$ is the reference input vector

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of LMS:** The term $2\mu e[n] \mathbf{r}[n]$ is a *gradient estimate* of the MSE surface. The algorithm performs *stochastic gradient descent* — noisy but computationally cheap (only $2M+1$ multiplications per sample).

---

## 2.4 The RLS (Recursive Least Squares) Algorithm

For faster convergence than LMS, especially when the input autocorrelation matrix has a large eigenvalue spread (which is common in ECG signals).

**Gain Vector:**

$$\mathbf{k}[n] = \frac{\mathbf{P}[n-1] \mathbf{r}[n]}{\lambda + \mathbf{r}^T[n] \mathbf{P}[n-1] \mathbf{r}[n]}$$

**Innovation (a priori error):**

$$\alpha[n] = x[n] - \mathbf{w}^T[n-1] \mathbf{r}[n]$$

**Weight Update:**

$$\mathbf{w}[n] = \mathbf{w}[n-1] + \mathbf{k}[n] \alpha[n]$$

**Inverse Correlation Matrix Update:**

$$\mathbf{P}[n] = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\mathbf{P}[n-1] - \mathbf{k}[n] \mathbf{r}^T[n] \mathbf{P}[n-1]\right]$$

where $\lambda$ = forgetting factor, $0 < \lambda \le 1$ (typically 0.98 – 0.995).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision.**

| Equation | Formula | Use / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Observed signal model | $x[n] = s[n] + \eta[n] + v[n]$ | Defines the artifact-embedded signal |
| Filter output (FIR adaptive) | $y[n] = \sum_{i=0}^{M-1} w_i[n] r[n-i]$ | Adaptive estimate of the artifact |
| Error / Cleaned signal | $e[n] = x[n] - y[n] \approx \hat{s}[n]$ | The output used as the clean signal estimate |
| LMS weight update | $\mathbf{w}[n+1] = \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu e[n] \mathbf{r}[n]$ | Stochastic gradient descent step |
| LMS step-size bound | $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$ | Convergence stability condition |
| RLS gain vector | $\mathbf{k}[n] = \frac{\mathbf{P}[n-1]\mathbf{r}[n]}{\lambda + \mathbf{r}^T[n]\mathbf{P}[n-1]\mathbf{r}[n]}$ | RLS correction direction |
| RLS weight update | $\mathbf{w}[n] = \mathbf{w}[n-1] + \mathbf{k}[n] \alpha[n]$ | Faster convergence than LMS |
| Wiener filter (frequency) | $W(z) = \frac{P_{xd}(z)}{P_{xx}(z)}$ | Optimal MMSE linear filter |
| Template subtraction | $\hat{s}[n] = x[n] - \alpha T[n-n_{\text{R}}]$ | Maternal ECG cancellation |
| SNR (dB) | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_s}{P_\eta}\right)$ | Quantifies artifact severity |
| Misadjustment (LMS) | $\mathcal{M} = \frac{\mu}{2} \text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ | Excess MSE over Wiener optimum |
| Notch filter (50 Hz) | $H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | Powerline interference removal |
| Notch frequency | $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 50}{f_s}$ | In radians per sample |

> All values use **$\vert x \vert$** notation when needed to avoid breaking table syntax — never use bare pipes inside the table.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Impact:** Adaptive artifact removal algorithms are deployed in:
> - **Hospitals** — fetal heart rate monitors during labour (every modern CTG cardiotocograph uses some form of maternal ECG cancellation)
> - **ICU patient monitors** — motion-artifact-resilient pulse oximeters
> - **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — EOG/EMG artefact rejection from EEG
> - **Holter/ambulatory ECG recorders** — 50 Hz notch filters and adaptive hum cancellers
> - **Implantable devices** — pacemakers use template subtraction to detect intrinsic QRS
