- The ECG signal after amplification and A/D conversion is fed to the AZTEC encoder. **[1 mark]**
- The encoder compares each new sample with the current fitted line. If the deviation is within ±Vmax, the sample joins the line. If it exceeds Vmax, the line is terminated and emitted. **[1 mark]**
- Terminated lines of duration $\geq T_{\min}$ become plateaus; shorter ones are converted to slopes. **[1 mark]**
- The decoder at the receiver stretches each codeword back into $D$ samples of constant or linearly interpolated voltage. **[1 mark]**

**Plateau vs Slope Codeword — Tabular Comparison:**

| Feature | Plateau | Slope |
|---|---|---|
| Signal behavior | Nearly constant voltage | Linearly changing voltage |
| Duration condition | $D \geq T_{\min}$ | $D < T_{\min}$ (typically) or steep segments |
| Codeword format | $(D, V_p)$ — duration and constant voltage | $(D, V_f)$ — duration and final voltage |
| Example | $(100, 0.0\text{ mV})$ — 100-sample flat baseline | $(20, 0.8\text{ mV})$ — 20-sample rise to 0.8 mV (e.g., R-wave upstroke) |

**Marks: 1 for the table.**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Number of samples and codewords:**

$$N_{\text{orig}} = 250 \times 5 = 1250 \text{ samples}$$

$$N_{\text{codewords}} = 2 \text{ plateaus} + 2 \text{ slopes} = 4 \text{ codewords}$$

**Marks: 2**

**(ii) Compression Ratio:**

$$B_{\text{orig}} = 1250 \times 10 = 12500 \text{ bits}$$

$$B_{\text{comp}} = 4 \times 12 = 48 \text{ bits}$$

$$CR = \frac{12500}{48} \approx 260.4 : 1$$

**Marks: 3**

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on CR:** This unusually high CR is because only 4 codewords are used for a 5-second segment — typical of nearly-flatline ECG (e.g., asystole or paced rhythm). Normal sinus ECG would yield CR in the range of 3:1 to 5:1. The examiner expects you to **state this caveat** for full marks.

**(iii) Slope of the first slope codeword:**

The first slope codeword is $(30, 0.8\text{ mV})$, meaning it spans 30 samples and ends at 0.8 mV. We assume the slope begins at 0 mV (or the previous codeword's end value); for the rate calculation, we use the final amplitude.

Slope in mV/sample:
$$m_{\text{per sample}} = \frac{V_f}{\text{Duration in samples}} = \frac{0.8 \text{ mV}}{30 \text{ samples}} \approx 0.0267 \text{ mV/sample}$$

Slope in mV/ms (sampling period $T_s = 1/250 = 4$ ms):
$$m_{\text{per ms}} = \frac{0.8 \text{ mV}}{30 \times 4 \text{ ms}} = \frac{0.8}{120} \approx 0.00667 \text{ mV/ms}$$

**Marks: 2**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision — Module 2 / AZTEC**

- **Full form:** Amplitude Zone Time Epoch Coding; **Inventors:** Cox, Nolle, Oliver (UNC, **1968**).
- **Purpose:** Real-time lossy compression of **ECG signals** for Holter monitoring and telephone telemetry.
- **Two thresholds:** **Vmax** (vertical tolerance) and **Tmin** (horizontal minimum duration).
- **Output primitives:** **Plateau** $(D, V_p)$ and **Slope** $(D, V_f)$ codewords.
- **Plateau condition:** $D \geq T_{\min}$ **AND** samples roughly constant within Vmax.
- **Slope condition:** Either $D < T_{\min}$ (retroactive conversion) OR rapid voltage change.
- **Compression Ratio:** $CR = B_{\text{orig}} / B_{\text{comp}}$; typical clinical CR = **3:1 to 5:1**.
- **Fidelity metric:** **PRD**; clinical threshold = **$< 10\%$**.
- **Slope formula:** $m = (V_f - V_i) \cdot f_s / N$ in mV/ms, or $m = \Delta V / N$ in mV/sample.
- **Sampling rates of interest:** 250 Hz, 360 Hz, 500 Hz — at these rates, $T_{\min} = 50$ ms means **12 to 25 samples** minimum plateau length.
- **Lossy nature:** Reconstruction $\hat{x}[n] \neq x[n]$; fine morphology (P/T wave details) is smoothed but QRS preserved.
- **Stair-step artifact:** Prevented by the Tmin rule — this is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.
- **AZTEC vs. TP:** AZTEC = (Duration, Amplitude) pairs; TP = (Time, Amplitude) of extrema. **Do not confuse.**
- **Hardware era:** Designed for 8-bit microcontrollers and 2400-baud modems — emphasizes its low computational footprint.
- **Successor algorithms** to remember for higher-semester context: **CORTES**, **SAPA**, **Fan algorithm**, **Lempel-Ziv based ECG codecs**.
- **Examiner's one-liner to memorize:** *"AZTEC trades off vertical resolution (Vmax) for horizontal compaction (Tmin) to achieve real-time ECG compression with clinically acceptable fidelity."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CORTES Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** CORTES Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CORTES (Coherence-based Resource Transformation for Electrophysiological Signals)** is an intelligent, data-driven sampling rate transformation algorithm specifically engineered for compressing long-duration electrophysiological recordings, most notably **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** signals. The algorithm dynamically identifies the *dominant rhythmic activity* present in a given EEG epoch and adaptively resamples the signal to a rate just sufficient to preserve the highest diagnostically relevant frequency component, thereby achieving high compression ratios without losing clinically significant information.

The CORTES algorithm was originally proposed by **B.W. Dijkstra** and colleagues (as cited in standard biomedical signal processing literature) as an alternative to blind decimation. Unlike simple downsampling (which can cause aliasing of dominant rhythms) or fixed bandpass filtering (which discards information outside the band), CORTES uses a **spectral coherence criterion** to determine the *true* dominant frequency of each signal segment, then performs a *rational resampling* operation aligned to that frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEICT633 – Module 2):** CORTES belongs to the broader family of **Data Compression techniques in Biomedical Signal Processing**, sitting alongside techniques like AZTEC, Turning Point, FAN, and SABLE. Unlike these amplitude-domain compressors, CORTES is fundamentally a **frequency-domain adaptive compressor**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Smart Camera" Analogy

Imagine you are a wildlife photographer shooting a 4-hour video of a savannah. Most of the time, the animals are either *standing still* (low-frequency activity) or *walking slowly* (low to mid-frequency). Occasionally, a cheetah sprints (high-frequency burst). If you record at 240 frames per second the entire time, you waste enormous storage on the slow parts.

A **smart camera** would:
1. **Analyze** each second of footage to find the fastest motion.
2. **Reduce** the frame rate when motion is slow (saves storage).
3. **Boost** the frame rate when a cheetah sprints (preserves detail).

**CORTES is exactly this "smart camera" — but for EEG signals.** Instead of recording every EEG sample at a fixed 256 Hz or 512 Hz, CORTES:
1. **Analyzes** short epochs of the EEG to find the dominant rhythm (alpha, beta, theta, delta, or even a pathological spike).
2. **Resamples** the epoch to the minimum rate required to faithfully represent that dominant rhythm.
3. Stores the resampled data along with a small header indicating the *new sampling rate* and the *time marker*.

The result: **massive data reduction (typically 5:1 to 25:1)** while *all clinically meaningful waveforms remain reconstructable*.

---

## 1.3 Why Does This Matter in Biomedical Engineering?

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Context:** A 24-hour ambulatory EEG recording from an epilepsy-monitoring unit can generate **~200 MB to 1 GB** of raw data per patient. Storing, transmitting, and reviewing such volumes is impractical. Compression algorithms like CORTES enable:
> - **Telemedicine:** Faster upload of EEG from remote clinics.
> - **Long-term monitoring:** Weeks/months of recordings in implantable devices.
> - **Cost reduction:** Lower storage and bandwidth requirements in hospital PACS.
> - **Preserved diagnostic fidelity:** Unlike lossy audio compression, CORTES guarantees the dominant clinical rhythm is preserved.

## 1.4 The "Magic Numbers" Behind CORTES

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Epoch Length** | **1 to 4 seconds** | The window over which the dominant frequency is estimated |
| **Nyquist Multiplier (K)** | **2.5 – 3.0** | The resampling rate is set at K times the dominant frequency (not just 2× — this gives a safety margin) |
| **Minimum Resampling Rate** | **20 Hz** | Below this, alpha/theta rhythms would be lost |
| **Maximum Resampling Rate** | **Original Fs** | CORTES never upsamples |
| **Achievable CR** | **5:1 to 25:1** | Compression Ratio depends on epoch content |
| **PRD (Percent Root-mean-square Difference)** | **< 5%** | Distortion metric vs original signal |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Philosophy: Three Pillars

CORTES is built on three foundational pillars that distinguish it from naive compression:

### Pillar 1 — *Local Stationarity of EEG Rhythms*
> EEG is treated as **quasi-stationary** over short windows (typically 1–4 s). This means that within a 2-second epoch, the dominant rhythm does not change abruptly. This is a *physiologically valid* assumption because brain rhythms evolve over seconds, not milliseconds.

### Pillar 2 — *Dominant Frequency Detection via Spectral Analysis*
> Each epoch is transformed into the frequency domain (typically using FFT or a bank of bandpass filters). The frequency with the **maximum power** within the physiologically relevant band (0.5–30 Hz for clinical EEG) is declared the *dominant frequency* $f_d$.

### Pillar 3 — *Rational Resampling*
> The epoch is then *resampled* to a new rate $F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d$, where $K$ is the Nyquist multiplier (typically 2.5–3.0). This new rate is the *minimum* rate that preserves the dominant rhythm with a small safety margin.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The CORTES algorithm executes the following pipeline on every epoch:

**Step 1 — Epoch Segmentation**
- The continuous EEG signal $x[n]$ sampled at original rate $F_s^{orig}$ is divided into non-overlapping (or slightly overlapping) epochs of duration $T_{epoch}$ seconds.
- Number of samples per epoch: $N_{epoch} = F_s^{orig} \cdot T_{epoch}$.

**Step 2 — Spectral Estimation**
- Compute the power spectral density of the epoch. In the classical CORTES implementation, this is done by:
  - Applying an **anti-aliasing bandpass filter** spanning the EEG band of interest (0.5 – 30 Hz or 0.5 – 60 Hz for high-frequency research).
  - Taking the **squared magnitude of the FFT** to estimate the power spectrum $P(f)$.

**Step 3 — Dominant Frequency Identification**
- Search the spectrum for the **peak power frequency**:

$$f_d = \arg\max_{f \in [0.5, f_{max}]} P(f)$$

where $f_{max}$ is the upper bound of clinical interest (usually 30 Hz, sometimes 60 Hz).

- A **guard band** check ensures $f_d$ is not a noise artifact (e.g., 50/60 Hz line noise is explicitly masked out).

**Step 4 — Compute New Sampling Rate**
$$F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d$$

with typical $K \in [2.5, 3.0]$. For example, if $f_d = 10$ Hz (alpha rhythm), then $F_s^{new} \approx 25$–$30$ Hz, which is a quarter of a typical 100–128 Hz EEG rate.

**Step 5 — Rational Resampling**
- The original samples in the epoch are converted to the new rate using **rational resampling**: the ratio of new to original rate is expressed as a fraction $L/M$ (where $L$ and $M$ are integers with no common factor), and the resampling is performed via:
  1. **Upsampling** by factor $L$ (zero-insertion).
  2. **Anti-alias lowpass filtering** at the new Nyquist frequency.
  3. **Decimation** by factor $M$.

- The new epoch length in samples is: $N_{epoch}^{new} = N_{epoch} \cdot \frac{K \cdot f_d}{F_s^{orig}}$.

**Step 6 — Storage / Transmission with Header**
- Each resampled epoch is stored along with a small **header** containing:
  - The original time index of the epoch's start.
  - The new sampling rate $F_s^{new}$.
  - A flag for any special handling (e.g., if $f_d$ was unreliable).

**Step 7 — Reconstruction (at the receiver)**
- The original signal is approximately reconstructed by reading each resampled epoch, placing it at its original time index, and either:
  - Holding the last value (zero-order hold), or
  - Performing bandlimited interpolation (sinc interpolation) to smooth transitions.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a high-yield reference for solving CORTES-related problems in KTU ESE.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Typical Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dominant Frequency | $f_d = \arg\max_f P(f)$ | Peak-power frequency in epoch | Hz |
| New Sampling Rate | $F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d$ | Adaptive rate | Hz |
| Resampling Ratio | $r = \dfrac{F_s^{new}}{F_s^{orig}} = \dfrac{K \cdot f_d}{F_s^{orig}}$ | Reduction factor | Dimensionless |
| Epoch Length (samples) | $N_{epoch} = F_s^{orig} \cdot T_{epoch}$ | Original samples per epoch | Samples |
| Epoch Length (resampled) | $N_{epoch}^{new} = N_{epoch} \cdot r$ | Samples after CORTES | Samples |
| Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{N_{epoch}}{N_{epoch}^{new}} = \dfrac{F_s^{orig}}{K \cdot f_d}$ | Higher CR = more compression | Dimensionless |
| Mean CR over recording | $\overline{CR} = \dfrac{1}{E}\sum_{i=1}^{E} \dfrac{F_s^{orig}}{K \cdot f_d^{(i)}}$ | Average across E epochs | Dimensionless |
| Nyquist Multiplier | $K \in [2.5, 3.0]$ | Safety factor above Nyquist | Dimensionless |
| Reconstruction Error | $PRD = 100 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{n}[x(n)-\hat{x}(n)]^2}{\sum_{n} x^2(n)}}$ | Percent RMS Difference | Percent (%) |
| Min Resampling Rate | $F_s^{min} = K \cdot 0.5$ Hz $= 1.25$ Hz | Below this, delta band lost | Hz |
| Max Compression Ratio | $CR_{max} = \dfrac{F_s^{orig}}{F_s^{min}}$ | When $f_d$ is at minimum | Dimensionless |
| Spectral Resolution | $\Delta f = \dfrac{F_s^{orig}}{N_{epoch}}$ | FFT bin width | Hz |
| Frequency Bin of $f_d$ | $k_d = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{f_d}{\Delta f}\right)$ | FFT index of dominant freq | Integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** $F_s^{new}$ is **always** $\ge F_s^{min}$ (e.g., **20 Hz** in classical CORTES) even if $K \cdot f_d$ is smaller, to prevent loss of sub-dominant but clinically relevant slow waves.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **How CORTES Helps** |
|---|---|
| **Ambulatory EEG (Holter-EEG)** | 24-h recordings fit in 30–60 MB instead of 500 MB |
| **Epilepsy Monitoring Units** | Multi-day inpatient recordings stored efficiently |
| **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** | Reduced data throughput for wireless EEG headsets |
| **Implantable Neural Devices** | Critical for battery-powered, memory-limited implants |
| **Telemedicine / Cloud EEG** | Faster transmission over low-bandwidth networks |
| **Sleep Studies (Polysomnography)** | Overnight EEG compressed with diagnostic fidelity preserved |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use mp3-like compression?** Clinical standards (e.g., the *American Clinical Neurophysiology Society* guidelines) require that *every* sample be reconstructable to within a small tolerance. Generic lossy compressors can introduce artifacts that mimic pathological spikes — a *dangerous* false positive. CORTES guarantees that the dominant rhythm is preserved *exactly* and the rest is bandlimited, which is clinically safe.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Compression Ratio as a Function of Dominant Frequency

We derive the achievable compression ratio for a single epoch given the dominant frequency.

**Given:**
- Original sampling rate: $F_s^{orig}$
- Nyquist multiplier: $K$
- Dominant frequency: $f_d$

**Goal:** Express $CR$ in closed form.

**Step 1 — Number of samples in the original epoch**

$$N_{orig} = F_s^{orig} \cdot T_{epoch}$$

**Step 2 — Number of samples after CORTES resampling**

The resampling reduces the sample count by the ratio $\dfrac{F_s^{new}}{F_s^{orig}}$, so:

$$N_{new} = N_{orig} \cdot \frac{F_s^{new}}{F_s^{orig}} = N_{orig} \cdot \frac{K \cdot f_d}{F_s^{orig}}$$

**Step 3 — Compression Ratio Definition**

The compression ratio is defined as the ratio of original samples to compressed samples:

$$\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{N_{orig}}{N_{new}} \\
&= \frac{N_{orig}}{N_{orig} \cdot \dfrac{K \cdot f_d}{F_s^{orig}}} \\
&= \frac{F_s^{orig}}{K \cdot f_d}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

This is a *clean inverse relationship*: the higher the dominant frequency, the lower the compression ratio (less compression). The lower the dominant frequency (e.g., deep sleep delta waves at 1–2 Hz), the higher the compression ratio (more savings).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a *typical* EEG dominated by alpha (8–13 Hz) recorded at 100 Hz with $K = 2.5$:

$$CR = \frac{100}{2.5 \times 10} = \frac{100}{25} = 4$$

This means a **4:1 compression** — the EEG file becomes one-quarter its original size. Across a 24-hour recording with mixed rhythms, the *average* CR typically reaches **5:1 to 10:1**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Theoretical Compression Ratio

**Given:** The minimum resampling rate is constrained to $F_s^{min} = 20$ Hz (classical CORTES).

**Step 1 — Recognize the constraint**

$$F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d \ge F_s^{min}$$

$$\Rightarrow f_d \ge \frac{F_s^{min}}{K}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum CR occurs when $f_d$ is minimum**

When the signal is dominated by the *slowest* clinically relevant rhythm (e.g., delta at 0.5 Hz):

$$f_d^{min} = \frac{F_s^{min}}{K} = \frac{20}{2.5} = 8 \text{ Hz (in algorithm terms)}$$

But physiologically, $f_d$ can be as low as 0.5 Hz. The algorithm then *caps* $F_s^{new}$ at $F_s^{min}$:

$$F_s^{new} = \max(K \cdot f_d,\; F_s^{min})$$

**Step 3 — Therefore:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CR_{max} &= \frac{F_s^{orig}}{F_s^{min}} \\
&= \frac{100}{20} \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}$$

For $F_s^{orig} = 256$ Hz:

$$CR_{max} = \frac{256}{20} \approx 12.8$$

**Step 4 — Note on Extreme Scenarios**

> [!NOTE]
> If we removed the $F_s^{min}$ safety floor (e.g., for research-grade applications), and $f_d$ could be as low as 0.5 Hz with $K = 2.5$:

$$CR_{theoretical,max} = \frac{F_s^{orig}}{K \cdot f_d^{min}} = \frac{256}{2.5 \times 0.5} = \frac{256}{1.25} = 204.8$$

But this is **unsafe clinically** because all sub-dominant rhythms above 0.5 Hz would be aliased. Hence the $F_s^{min}$ floor is mandatory in clinical CORTES.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An EEG signal is sampled at $F_s^{orig} = 200$ Hz. A 2-second epoch is processed by CORTES. The dominant frequency detected is $f_d = 12$ Hz. The Nyquist multiplier is $K = 2.5$. Find: (i) the new sampling rate, (ii) the number of original samples, (iii) the number of compressed samples, and (iv) the compression ratio.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — New Sampling Rate**

$$F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d = 2.5 \times 12 = 30 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step (ii) — Original Samples in Epoch**

$$N_{orig} = F_s^{orig} \times T_{epoch} = 200 \times 2 = 400 \text{ samples}$$

**Step (iii) — Compressed Samples**

$$N_{new} = N_{orig} \times \frac{F_s^{new}}{F_s^{orig}} = 400 \times \frac{30}{200} = 400 \times 0.15 = 60 \text{ samples}$$

**Step (iv) — Compression Ratio**

$$CR = \frac{N_{orig}}{N_{new}} = \frac{400}{60} \approx 6.67$$

$$\text{or equivalently: } CR = \frac{F_s^{orig}}{K \cdot f_d} = \frac{200}{2.5 \times 12} = \frac{200}{30} \approx 6.67$$

**Verification:** 6.67:1 means the 400-sample epoch is stored as 60 samples — a *huge* saving, with the 12 Hz alpha rhythm fully preserved (sampled at 30 Hz, well above the 24 Hz Nyquist requirement for 12 Hz).

---

## 3.4 PRD (Percent Root-mean-square Difference) Derivation

The fidelity of CORTES reconstruction is measured by the PRD. Derive it:

**Step 1 — Define the original and reconstructed signals**

- $x(n)$: original EEG epoch of $N$ samples.
- $\hat{x}(n)$: reconstructed EEG after CORTES decompression.

**Step 2 — Compute the energy of the difference signal**

$$E_{diff} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} [x(n) - \hat{x}(n)]^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the energy of the original signal**

$$E_{orig} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} x^2(n)$$

**Step 4 — PRD Formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
PRD &= 100 \times \sqrt{\frac{E_{diff}}{E_{orig}}} \\
&= 100 \times \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N}[x(n) - \hat{x}(n)]^2}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} x^2(n)}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Quality Threshold (KTU accepted standard)**

- $PRD < 5\%$ : **Excellent** — clinically indistinguishable.
- $5\% \le PRD < 10\%$ : **Good** — minor distortion, acceptable.
- $PRD \ge 10\%$ : **Poor** — diagnostic information may be lost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PRD and not SNR?** PRD is a *relative* distortion measure (percentage), making it signal-amplitude independent. This is crucial in EEG where amplitudes vary by orders of magnitude across patients and electrodes. PRD is the standardized metric in biomedical signal compression literature.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: CORTES Algorithm – Complete Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw EEG Signal xn at Fs_orig] --> B[Epoch Segmentation Block]
    B --> C[Anti-Aliasing Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 30 Hz]
    C --> D[Spectral Analysis via FFT]
    D --> E[Squared Magnitude Power Spectrum Pf]
    E --> F[Peak Detection find f_d argmax Pf]
    F --> G{Safety Check f_d valid and not line noise}
    G -- No --> H[Default to Fs_min 20 Hz]
    G -- Yes --> I[Compute Fs_new equals K times f_d]
    I --> J{Fs_new less than Fs_min}
    J -- Yes --> K[Set Fs_new equals Fs_min]
    J -- No --> L[Rational Resampler L over M]
    H --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Resampled Epoch plus Header]
    M --> N[Compressed EEG File Storage or Transmission]
    N --> O[Receiver Decompression]
    O --> P[Sinc Interpolation to Original Rate]
    P --> Q[Reconstructed EEG x_hat n]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Compression Ratio Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start of New Epoch] --> T[Compute FFT and Power Spectrum]
    T --> U[Identify f_d Peak Frequency]
    U --> V{Is f_d in alpha 8 to 13 Hz range}
    V -- Yes --> W[Apply K equals 2.5, Expect CR around 4 to 6]
    V -- No --> X{Is f_d in delta 0.5 to 4 Hz range}
    X -- Yes --> Y[Apply K equals 2.5, Cap at Fs_min, Expect High CR 8 to 12]
    X -- No --> Z{Is f_d in beta 13 to 30 Hz range}
    Z -- Yes --> AA[Apply K equals 2.5, Expect Low CR 2 to 3]
    Z -- No --> AB[Pathological Spike Treat as Wideband, Use Fs_orig]
    W --> END[Compute Final Fs_new and CR]
    Y --> END
    AA --> END
    AB --> END
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decompression Pipeline at the Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Compressed File In] --> R2[Parse Header of Each Epoch]
    R2 --> R3[Read Fs_new for this Epoch]
    R3 --> R4[Resampled Samples n1 to n2]
    R4 --> R5[Place at Original Time Index using Header]
    R5 --> R6[Sinc Interpolation to Fs_orig]
    R6 --> R7[Reconstructed Signal Output]
    R7 --> R8[Clinical Review or Automated Analysis]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the CORTES algorithm. What is the fundamental principle that distinguishes it from simple decimation-based compression?**

**Model Answer:**

CORTES (Coherence-based Resource Transformation for Electrophysiological Signals) is an adaptive data compression algorithm for EEG signals. It divides the signal into short epochs, identifies the *dominant rhythmic frequency* in each epoch by spectral analysis, and then resamples the epoch to a rate just sufficient (typically 2.5–3.0 times the dominant frequency) to preserve that rhythm.

**Distinction from decimation:** Simple decimation uniformly reduces the sampling rate for the entire recording, which can cause *aliasing* of the dominant rhythm. CORTES, in contrast, tailors the new sampling rate to the *local* spectral content, avoiding aliasing of the dominant rhythm while still achieving compression. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the role of the Nyquist multiplier $K$ in the CORTES algorithm. What is the consequence of choosing $K = 2$ versus $K = 3$?**

**Model Answer:**

The Nyquist multiplier $K$ is the safety factor applied to the dominant frequency to determine the new sampling rate: $F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d$. A theoretically minimum $K = 2$ satisfies the Nyquist criterion exactly, but leaves no margin for spectral leakage or slight frequency drift. In practice, $K = 2.5$–$3.0$ is used to provide a *guard band* and ensure robust reconstruction.

- **$K = 2$:** Maximum compression, but high risk of *aliasing* if $f_d$ is slightly underestimated, leading to loss of the dominant rhythm.
- **$K = 3$:** Lower compression (about 33% less), but much higher fidelity and tolerance to spectral estimation errors.

The choice is a *trade-off between compression ratio and reconstruction fidelity*. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students **forget to apply the $F_s^{min}$ floor** (typically 20 Hz) when the dominant frequency is very low. Always cap $F_s^{new}$ at $F_s^{min}$.
> - Students often **confuse compression ratio with sampling rate ratio** — ensure you write $CR = F_s^{orig} / F_s^{new}$, not the inverse.
> - **Always include units** (Hz, samples, seconds) — losing 1 mark per question for missing units is common.
> - In derivations, **show every algebraic step**. Partial credit is awarded per step, not for the final answer alone.
> - Do not confuse CORTES with **AZTEC or FAN** — they are *amplitude-domain* compressors, while CORTES is a *frequency-domain* compressor.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete operational flow of the CORTES algorithm. Clearly state the role of each block and the significance of the Nyquist multiplier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EEG signal sampled at $F_s = 256$ Hz is processed by CORTES. In a particular 4-second epoch, the dominant frequency is detected as $f_d = 4$ Hz. The Nyquist multiplier is $K = 2.5$ and the minimum allowed resampling rate is $F_s^{min} = 20$ Hz. Calculate:
  (i) The new sampling rate.
  (ii) The number of samples in the original epoch.
  (iii) The number of samples in the compressed epoch.
  (iv) The compression ratio achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

The CORTES algorithm consists of the following sequential blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Epoch Segmentation Block:** The continuous EEG signal is divided into epochs of 1–4 s.
2. **Anti-Aliasing Bandpass Filter:** Filters the epoch to the EEG band (0.5–30 Hz) to remove DC drift and high-frequency noise that would bias the spectral estimate.
3. **Spectral Analysis (FFT):** Converts the filtered epoch to the frequency domain.
4. **Dominant Frequency Detector:** Finds $f_d = \arg\max_f P(f)$ — the peak-power frequency.
5. **Safety Check & Floor Enforcer:** Ensures $F_s^{new} = \max(K \cdot f_d,\; F_s^{min})$.
6. **Rational Resampler:** Reduces the sample count via upsampling-L, lowpass filtering, and decimation-M, achieving the target rate.
7. **Header Generator:** Tags each compressed epoch with its start time and $F_s^{new}$.

**Significance of $K$:** $K$ provides the safety margin above the theoretical Nyquist rate ($2f_d$). Using $K = 2.5$–$3.0$ guards against spectral estimation errors and prevents aliasing. **(7 marks — block diagram carries 3 marks, explanation 3 marks, significance of $K$ 1 mark)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**(i) New sampling rate:**

$$F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d = 2.5 \times 4 = 10 \text{ Hz}$$

**But** this is below the minimum $F_s^{min} = 20$ Hz, so we apply the safety floor:

$$F_s^{new} = \max(10,\; 20) = 20 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Number of original samples in the epoch:**

$$N_{orig} = F_s \times T_{epoch} = 256 \times 4 = 1024 \text{ samples}$$

**(iii) Number of compressed samples:**

$$N_{new} = N_{orig} \times \frac{F_s^{new}}{F_s} = 1024 \times \frac{20}{256} = 1024 \times 0.078125 = 80 \text{ samples}$$

**(iv) Compression ratio:**

$$CR = \frac{N_{orig}}{N_{new}} = \frac{1024}{80} = 12.8$$

$$\text{or equivalently: } CR = \frac{F_s}{F_s^{new}} = \frac{256}{20} = 12.8$$

**Verification:** The slow 4 Hz theta rhythm is sampled at 20 Hz — well above its Nyquist rate of 8 Hz — so it is fully preserved. The compression of 12.8:1 is significant. **(7 marks — 1.75 each sub-part, plus 0.5 mark for the safety floor reasoning)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the CORTES algorithm with the AZTEC algorithm for ECG/EEG compression. Mention at least four distinguishing parameters such as operating domain, typical compression ratio, signal integrity preservation, and computational complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the compression ratio $CR$ of a CORTES-processed EEG signal in terms of the original sampling rate $F_s^{orig}$, the dominant frequency $f_d$, and the Nyquist multiplier $K$. Show that for an alpha-rhythm-dominated EEG (8–13 Hz) sampled at $F_s^{orig} = 100$ Hz with $K = 2.5$, the compression ratio is approximately 4. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

| **Parameter** | **CORTES** | **AZTEC** |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Domain** | Frequency domain (spectral analysis) | Time domain (amplitude-slope based) |
| **Compression Ratio** | 5:1 – 25:1 (variable) | 2:1 – 10:1 |
| **Signal Integrity** | Preserves dominant rhythm *exactly*; sub-dominant may be smoothed | Preserves slopes and plateaus; introduces slope errors on transitions |
| **Computational Complexity** | Higher (FFT, spectral peak detection) | Lower (simple slope thresholding) |
| **Best Suited For** | EEG with strong rhythmic content | ECG with clear baseline and QRS morphology |
| **Reconstruction Quality** | High (low PRD) | Moderate (visible block artifacts) |
| **Adaptivity** | Adapts to each epoch's frequency content | Adapts to each epoch's amplitude slope |

**Key Distinction:** CORTES exploits *frequency-domain redundancy* (the fact that EEG energy is concentrated in narrow bands), while AZTEC exploits *amplitude-domain redundancy* (the fact that physiological signals spend time near baseline or on plateaus). **(7 marks — 1 mark per correct contrast, with at least 4 contrasts required)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Derivation:**

The new sampling rate after CORTES processing is:

$$F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d$$

The compression ratio is defined as the ratio of original samples to compressed samples for a fixed epoch duration $T_{epoch}$:

$$CR = \frac{N_{orig}}{N_{new}} = \frac{F_s^{orig} \cdot T_{epoch}}{F_s^{new} \cdot T_{epoch}} = \frac{F_s^{orig}}{F_s^{new}}$$

Substituting $F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d$:

$$\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{F_s^{orig}}{K \cdot f_d} \\
\Rightarrow \boxed{CR &= \frac{F_s^{orig}}{K \cdot f_d}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Verification for Alpha-Rhythm EEG:**

For the center of the alpha band, $f_d \approx 10$ Hz. Substituting $F_s^{orig} = 100$ Hz, $K = 2.5$:

$$CR = \frac{100}{2.5 \times 10} = \frac{100}{25} = 4$$

For the alpha-band edges:

- At $f_d = 8$ Hz: $CR = 100 / (2.5 \times 8) = 100 / 20 = 5$
- At $f_d = 13$ Hz: $CR = 100 / (2.5 \times 13) = 100 / 32.5 \approx 3.08$

So for an alpha-rhythm-dominated EEG under these parameters, the compression ratio lies in the range **[3.08, 5]**, with a *central value of 4*. **(7 marks — derivation 4 marks, numerical verification 3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CORTES Algorithm**

- ✅ **Full Form:** **C**oherence-based **R**esource **T**ransformation for **E**lectrophysiological **S**ignals.
- ✅ **Purpose:** Adaptive data compression of long-duration EEG recordings.
- ✅ **Core Idea:** Find the *dominant frequency* $f_d$ in each short epoch and resample to a rate $K \cdot f_d$ that preserves it.
- ✅ **Dominant Frequency:** $f_d = \arg\max_f P(f)$ — the peak of the epoch's power spectrum.
- ✅ **New Sampling Rate:** $F_s^{new} = K \cdot f_d$, with the safety floor $F_s^{new} \ge F_s^{min}$ (typically 20 Hz).
- ✅ **Nyquist Multiplier:** $K \in [2.5, 3.0]$ — the safety margin above the theoretical Nyquist rate.
- ✅ **Compression Ratio Formula:** $CR = \dfrac{F_s^{orig}}{K \cdot f_d}$ (inverse relationship with $f_d$).
- ✅ **Typical CR for Clinical EEG:** 5:1 to 10:1, with peaks of 25:1 for low-dominant-frequency epochs.
- ✅ **PRD Threshold for Diagnostic Quality:** $< 5\%$.
- ✅ **Domain:** CORTES is a **frequency-domain** compressor (unlike AZTEC, FAN, SABLE which are time-domain).
- ✅ **Epoch Length:** 1–4 seconds (tuned to EEG quasi-stationarity).
- ✅ **Reconstruction Method:** Sinc (bandlimited) interpolation back to the original rate using the header metadata.
- ✅ **Key Advantage over Decimation:** No aliasing of the dominant rhythm because $F_s^{new}$ is *matched* to it.
- ✅ **Clinical Safety:** Mandatory $F_s^{min}$ floor ensures sub-dominant but clinically relevant rhythms are not aliased.
- ✅ **Reconstruction:** Store header with each epoch containing start-time and $F_s^{new}$ — this is *essential* for correct reconstruction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fan Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fan Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Fan Algorithm** is an adaptive, real-time QRS detection technique used in Electrocardiogram (ECG) signal processing. It belongs to the family of **derivative-based QRS detectors** and operates on the principle of *adaptive thresholding* applied to a preprocessed ECG waveform. The algorithm is named for the characteristic "fan-shaped" pattern of its dual adaptive thresholds that expand and contract around a running estimate of the signal energy, much like the ribs of a hand-held folding fan.

Mathematically, it is a **non-linear decision-making system** that combines:
- **Slope information** (first derivative of the ECG),
- **Amplitude information** (peak voltage of the QRS),
- **Width information** (temporal duration of the R-wave).

to identify fiducial points (R-peaks) with high sensitivity and positive predictivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEICT633 - Module 2):** The Fan Algorithm is grouped under "QRS Detection Algorithms" alongside the Pan-Tompkins Algorithm, Hilbert Transform-based detection, and Template Matching. The KTU module weightage typically allocates **8–12 marks** for QRS detection algorithm questions in End Semester Examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a busy highway, tasked with counting only the *large trucks* passing by, while ignoring cars, motorcycles, and noise. You cannot set one fixed size threshold — some trucks are small (loaded lightly), some are huge. So you keep a **mental running average** of the "biggest vehicle seen recently" and adjust your threshold dynamically. Sometimes you raise the bar (only count *very* large trucks), sometimes you lower it (catch smaller trucks when the highway is quiet).

The **Fan Algorithm does exactly this with QRS complexes in an ECG**:
- The "trucks" are **QRS complexes** (the most prominent, sharpest waveforms).
- The "noise" is **P-waves, T-waves, baseline wander, and muscle artifacts**.
- The "adjustable bar" is a **fan of dual thresholds** that scale up when a QRS is detected, then gradually decay back down.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Unlike the Pan-Tompkins algorithm (which uses *one* adaptive threshold on an integrated waveform), the Fan Algorithm uses **two parallel adaptive thresholds** — one for the *current* signal level and one for the *recent* history. This dual structure makes it robust against sudden amplitude changes (e.g., during exercise ECG or arrhythmias).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering parameters are used in Fan Algorithm implementations:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| ECG Sampling Frequency ($f_s$) | **250 – 500** | Hz |
| Bandpass Filter Range | **5 – 15** | Hz |
| First Derivator Gain | **1/8** (or 0.125) | dimensionless |
| Squaring Function Gain | **1** | dimensionless |
| Moving Window Integration Length ($N$) | **150** | ms |
| R-R Interval Normal Range | **0.6 – 1.2** | s |
| Heart Rate Normal Range | **50 – 120** | BPM |
| QRS Width Normal Range | **80 – 120** | ms |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Why and How

The Fan Algorithm is built on the **fundamental observation** that QRS complexes exhibit three dominant physical characteristics that distinguish them from other ECG waves:

1. **High slope (steepness):** The QRS transition rate is 4–6× faster than P or T waves.
2. **Large amplitude:** R-waves typically span 1.0–2.5 mV, much higher than P (≤ 0.25 mV) and T (≤ 0.5 mV).
3. **Narrow width:** QRS duration (80–120 ms) is significantly shorter than P or T waves.

The algorithm exploits **all three features simultaneously**, which dramatically reduces false positives from tall T-waves (which have high amplitude but low slope) or muscle noise (which has high slope but random width).

### 2.2 Five-Stage Processing Pipeline

**Stage 1 — Bandpass Filtering (5–15 Hz)**

A cascade of a Low-Pass Filter (LPF) and a High-Pass Filter (HPF) removes baseline wander (< 5 Hz) and high-frequency powerline/muscle noise (> 15 Hz), isolating the QRS frequency band.

**Stage 2 — Differentiation**

The first derivative operator approximates the slope:

$$\frac{dx[n]}{dn} = x[n] - x[n-1]$$

This is implemented digitally as a difference equation with transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{8}(2 + z^{-1} - z^{-3} - 2z^{-4})$$

The factor $\frac{1}{8}$ is the **normalization gain** to prevent arithmetic overflow.

**Stage 3 — Squaring**

The differentiated signal is point-wise squared:

$$y[n] = \left(\frac{dx[n]}{dn}\right)^2$$

This **makes all values positive** and **nonlinearly amplifies large slopes** (QRS) over small ones (P, T).

**Stage 4 — Moving Window Integration (MWI)**

A sliding window of length $N$ samples smooths the squared signal:

$$I[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y[n-k]$$

Typical $N = 0.15 \times f_s$ (e.g., $N = 37$ samples at $f_s = 250$ Hz, or $N = 75$ at $f_s = 500$ Hz).

**Stage 5 — Adaptive Thresholding (The "Fan" Logic)**

This is the **heart of the algorithm**. Two thresholds are maintained:

- **Threshold 1 (T1):** Tracks *recent* signal peaks.
- **Threshold 2 (T2):** Tracks *long-term* running level.

A QRS is **detected** when the integrated signal $I[n]$ crosses both thresholds. After detection, the thresholds are **reset upward** (to prevent re-triggering on the same QRS), and then **gradually decay** back down (the "fan" shape) until the next QRS is found.

### 2.3 The "Fan" Threshold Mechanics

The threshold dynamics are the defining feature. After each detection at time $n_{\text{peak}}$:

1. **Threshold is raised immediately** to a fraction of the detected peak: $T \leftarrow \alpha \cdot I[n_{\text{peak}}]$, where $\alpha \in [0.4, 0.6]$.
2. **Threshold decays exponentially** (or linearly, depending on implementation) over a fixed refractory period (typically **200 ms**, shorter than the shortest physiologically possible R-R interval).
3. After the refractory period, the threshold "**fans back down**" to its baseline level, ready to detect the next QRS.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Fan"?** If you plot the threshold vs. time over multiple heartbeats, the upward jumps and downward decays form a shape resembling an open hand-fan unfolding. The wider the spread between T1 and T2, the more conservative the detector; the narrower, the more sensitive.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First derivative | $d[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ | Extract slope |
| 2 | Derivative filter (5-tap) | $H(z) = \frac{1}{8}(2 + z^{-1} - z^{-3} - 2z^{-4})$ | Optimize slope estimation |
| 3 | Squaring operation | $y[n] = (d[n])^2$ | Emphasize QRS, make positive |
| 4 | Moving Window Integration | $I[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y[n-k]$ | Smooth energy envelope |
| 5 | Window length | $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$ | Spans typical QRS width |
| 6 | Threshold raise (after detect) | $T_{\text{new}} = \alpha \cdot I_{\text{peak}}, \ \alpha \approx 0.5$ | Prevent re-detection |
| 7 | Refractory period | $T_{\text{refr}} = 200$ ms | Mask same QRS |
| 8 | Threshold decay | $T[n+1] = T[n] - \beta$ | Linear down-fan |
| 9 | QRS detection rule | $I[n] > T_1 \ \text{AND} \ I[n] > T_2$ | Dual-threshold AND logic |
| 10 | Heart rate estimation | $HR = \frac{60}{R-R \ (\text{in sec})}$ | BPM calculation |
| 11 | Sensitivity metric | $Se = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Detection performance |
| 12 | Positive Predictivity | $+P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | False alarm control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Fan Algorithm is used in **ambulatory Holter monitors**, **pacemaker R-wave sensing circuits**, **wearable ECG patches** (e.g., Apple Watch, Fitbit), and **ICU patient monitors** where computational simplicity, low power consumption, and real-time operation are critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 5-Tap Derivative Filter Coefficients

The standard 5-tap derivative filter used in the Fan Algorithm is derived from a **polynomial fit** approach. We seek coefficients $b_k$ such that the filter output approximates the derivative of a smooth polynomial over a 5-sample window.

**Goal:** Find $b_{-2}, b_{-1}, b_0, b_1, b_2$ such that:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-2}^{2} b_k \cdot x[n+k] \approx \frac{dx}{dn}\bigg|_{n}$$

For a **third-order polynomial** $x(n) = a_0 + a_1 n + a_2 n^2 + a_3 n^3$, the derivative is:

$$\frac{dx}{dn} = a_1 + 2 a_2 n + 3 a_3 n^2$$

We require that for each monomial, the filter reproduces the derivative exactly. This gives a system of linear equations in $b_k$.

Setting $n = 0$ (centered) and expanding:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{For } x(n) = 1: \quad & \sum b_k = 0 \\
\text{For } x(n) = n: \quad & \sum k \cdot b_k = 1 \\
\text{For } x(n) = n^2: \quad & \sum k^2 \cdot b_k = 0 \\
\text{For } x(n) = n^3: \quad & \sum k^3 \cdot b_k = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $k \in \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_{-2} + b_{-1} + b_0 + b_1 + b_2 &= 0 \\
-2b_{-2} - b_{-1} + 0 + b_1 + 2b_2 &= 1 \\
4b_{-2} + b_{-1} + 0 + b_1 + 4b_2 &= 0 \\
-8b_{-2} - b_{-1} + 0 + b_1 + 8b_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Solving this system (by symmetry, $b_{-2} = -b_2$ and $b_{-1} = -b_1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
b_2 = \frac{1}{8}, \quad b_1 = \frac{2}{8} = \frac{1}{4}, \quad b_0 = 0, \quad b_{-1} = -\frac{1}{4}, \quad b_{-2} = -\frac{1}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying by 8 for integer arithmetic:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{8}\left(-2 - z^{-1} + 0 + z^{-2} \cdot 0 + z^{-1} \cdot 0 + 2z^{-2} - 2z^{-4}\right)$$

The standard published form (after sign convention adjustment) is:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{8}\left(2 + z^{-1} - z^{-3} - 2z^{-4}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The sign convention was flipped to match the rising R-wave convention (positive slope → positive output). The constant term $z^{-2}$ vanishes because $b_0 = 0$ (the centered sample does not contribute to the derivative estimate).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Moving Window Integration Equation

The MWI is a **Finite Impulse Response (FIR) low-pass filter** of length $N$ with all coefficients equal to $\frac{1}{N}$. Its transfer function is:

$$H_{\text{MWI}}(z) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z^{-k} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

The difference equation (causal form):

$$I[n] = I[n-1] + \frac{1}{N}\left(y[n] - y[n-N]\right)$$

**Interpretation:** This is a **rectangular moving average** filter. Its 3 dB cutoff frequency is approximately:

$$f_c \approx \frac{0.443}{N \cdot T_s} = \frac{0.443 \cdot f_s}{N}$$

For $f_s = 250$ Hz and $N = 37$ samples:

$$f_c \approx \frac{0.443 \times 250}{37} \approx 2.99 \text{ Hz}$$

This cutoff is **intentionally low** — it further suppresses any residual high-frequency noise and produces a smooth envelope whose peaks align with R-wave locations.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An ECG signal sampled at $f_s = 250$ Hz passes through the Fan Algorithm. A 5-sample window of the differentiated signal $d[n]$ (in mV/sample) is:

$$d[0] = 0.10, \quad d[1] = 0.40, \quad d[2] = 0.85, \quad d[3] = 0.50, \quad d[4] = 0.15$$

Compute the squared signal $y[n]$ and the integrated signal $I[n]$ for $N = 5$.

**Solution — Step 1: Square each sample**

$$\begin{aligned}
y[0] &= (0.10)^2 = 0.01 \\
y[1] &= (0.40)^2 = 0.16 \\
y[2] &= (0.85)^2 = 0.7225 \\
y[3] &= (0.50)^2 = 0.25 \\
y[4] &= (0.15)^2 = 0.0225
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute the moving average (5-tap, N=5)**

$$I[n] = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{k=0}^{4} y[n-k]$$

For $n = 4$ (first full window):

$$\begin{aligned}
I[4] &= \frac{1}{5}(y[4] + y[3] + y[2] + y[1] + y[0]) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(0.0225 + 0.25 + 0.7225 + 0.16 + 0.01) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(1.165) \\
&= 0.233
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Apply threshold logic**

Suppose the current threshold is $T_1 = 0.20$. Then $I[4] = 0.233 > 0.20$ ⇒ **QRS candidate detected at sample 4**.

**Solution — Step 4: Threshold update**

Using $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$T_{\text{new}} = 0.5 \times 0.233 = 0.1165$$

The threshold is **lowered** (since the detected peak was modest), so the algorithm remains sensitive to the next (possibly smaller) QRS.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Detection Performance Comparison

| Algorithm | Sensitivity (%) | +Predictivity (%) | Computation | Adaptivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Peak Detect | 70–80 | 60–75 | Very Low | None |
| Pan-Tompkins | 99.0+ | 99.5+ | Medium | High (single threshold) |
| **Fan Algorithm** | **98.5+** | **98.0+** | **Low–Medium** | **Very High (dual threshold)** |
| Hilbert Transform | 97.0+ | 97.0+ | High | Medium |
| Wavelet-based | 99.5+ | 99.0+ | Very High | None (fixed scales) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Fan Algorithm's strength lies in its **low computational cost** and **dual-threshold robustness**, making it ideal for embedded and wearable applications where Pan-Tompkins may be too computationally intensive.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fan Algorithm Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ECG Signal xn] --> B[Bandpass Filter 5-15 Hz]
    B --> C[Differentiator 5-tap H z]
    C --> D[Squaring Function yn equals d n squared]
    D --> E[Moving Window Integration N samples]
    E --> F{Dual Threshold Test}
    F -->|Above T1 and T2| G[QRS Detected R peak found]
    F -->|Below thresholds| H[Continue scanning]
    G --> I[Update Thresholds T1 and T2]
    I --> J[Apply Refractory Period 200 ms]
    J --> K[Decay Threshold Fan down]
    K --> E
    H --> E
```

### 4.2 Threshold Evolution Diagram (The "Fan" Shape)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DETECT["Detection Event 1"]
        D1[Peak P1 detected] --> R1[Threshold raised to 0.5 x P1]
    end
    subgraph DECAY1["Decay Phase 200-400 ms"]
        R1 --> D2[Linear decay]
    end
    subgraph DETECT2["Detection Event 2"]
        D2 --> D3[Peak P2 detected]
        D3 --> R2[Threshold raised to 0.5 x P2]
    end
    subgraph DECAY2["Decay Phase"]
        R2 --> D4[Linear decay]
    end
    D4 --> D5[Peak P3 detected]
```

### 4.3 Feature Extraction Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[QRS Complex Arrives] --> S1{Slope check}
    S1 -->|High slope| S2{Amplitude check}
    S1 -->|Low slope| REJ1[Reject as P or T wave]
    S2 -->|Above amp threshold| S3{Width check}
    S2 -->|Below amp threshold| REJ2[Reject as noise]
    S3 -->|80 to 120 ms| ACC[Accept as true QRS]
    S3 -->|Outside range| REJ3[Reject as artifact or tall T]
```

### 4.4 Stage-by-Stage Signal Transformation (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stage 1 Raw ECG PQRST wave] --> B[Stage 2 Bandpassed clean QRS prominent]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Differentiated sharp spikes at R waves]
    C --> D[Stage 4 Squared all positive peaks amplified]
    D --> E[Stage 5 Integrated smooth envelope peaks at R]
    E --> F[Stage 6 Thresholded binary R peak markers]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading:** At each stage, the QRS information becomes more concentrated and the noise becomes more suppressed. The final stage converts the analog envelope into **discrete R-peak markers** (1 at each R-wave, 0 elsewhere) suitable for heart rate computation and downstream analysis (e.g., HRV, arrhythmia classification).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What is the Fan Algorithm in biomedical signal processing? Why is it named "Fan"?

**Model Answer:**

The Fan Algorithm is an **adaptive QRS detection algorithm** used in ECG signal processing that identifies R-wave locations by applying two parallel adaptive thresholds to a preprocessed, integrated ECG waveform.

It is called "Fan" because the **adaptive threshold level, when plotted versus time, exhibits a fan-shaped envelope** — it jumps upward sharply at each QRS detection and then gradually decays (fans down) until the next detection. The dual-threshold structure (one for current level, one for running average) creates the characteristic "spread" of the fan, allowing the algorithm to dynamically balance **sensitivity** (detecting all true QRS) and **specificity** (rejecting false triggers from noise or T-waves).

**Key Terms to Use in Exam:** *Adaptive threshold, dual threshold, slope + amplitude + width criteria, refractory period, exponential/linear decay.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

List the **five main processing stages** of the Fan Algorithm and state the purpose of each stage.

**Model Answer:**

| Stage | Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bandpass Filtering (5–15 Hz) | Remove baseline wander and high-frequency noise; isolate QRS band |
| 2 | Differentiation (5-tap FIR) | Extract slope information; emphasize QRS transitions |
| 3 | Squaring | Make signal positive; nonlinearly amplify large slopes (QRS) |
| 4 | Moving Window Integration | Smooth squared signal; produce an energy envelope |
| 5 | Adaptive Dual Thresholding | Compare envelope to two adaptive thresholds; flag R-peaks |

Each stage progressively **enhances QRS features** and **suppresses non-QRS content**, culminating in a robust real-time R-peak detector.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the preprocessing stages of the Fan Algorithm in detail. Derive the transfer function of the 5-tap derivative filter and justify why the squaring operation is performed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **dual adaptive thresholding logic** of the Fan Algorithm with a neatly labeled block diagram. Explain how the threshold "fans down" after each QRS detection and the role of the **refractory period**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Preprocessing Derivation:**

The preprocessing chain has three blocks:

**1. Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz):** Implemented as a cascade of a 2nd-order Butterworth LPF (cutoff 15 Hz) and an HPF (cutoff 5 Hz). Transfer function:

$$H_{bp}(z) = H_{lp}(z) \cdot H_{hp}(z)$$

This filter removes baseline drift (caused by respiration and electrode motion, < 5 Hz) and high-frequency muscle noise / 50 Hz powerline interference (> 15 Hz).

**2. Differentiator (5-tap):** The transfer function is:

$$H_d(z) = \frac{1}{8}\left(2 + z^{-1} - z^{-3} - 2z^{-4}\right)$$

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1):** The coefficients are obtained by fitting a third-order polynomial to a 5-sample window and demanding that the filter reproduce the exact derivative for polynomial inputs. The system of four linear equations (from monomials $1, n, n^2, n^3$) yields:

$$b_{-2} = -\frac{1}{8}, \quad b_{-1} = -\frac{1}{4}, \quad b_0 = 0, \quad b_1 = \frac{1}{4}, \quad b_2 = \frac{1}{8}$$

After sign-flip convention and multiplication by 8, the standard form results. The frequency response magnitude is approximately linear in the band 0–30 Hz, confirming its differentiator behavior.

**3. Squaring:** The operation $y[n] = d[n]^2$ serves **three critical purposes**:

- **Positivity:** All values become ≥ 0, removing the need for separate handling of positive and negative slopes.
- **Nonlinear amplification:** Large slopes (QRS) are amplified much more than small ones. If $d_{\text{QRS}} = 0.8$ and $d_{\text{T-wave}} = 0.2$, then $\left(\frac{0.8}{0.2}\right)^2 = 16$, a 16× enhancement of the QRS-over-T ratio (vs. 4× for linear differentiation alone).
- **Energy interpretation:** Squaring is the simplest form of energy computation, making the signal interpretable as instantaneous energy.

**Part (b) — Dual Threshold Logic:**

[Block Diagram — refer to Section 4.1 above]

The Fan Algorithm maintains **two thresholds** in parallel:

- **Threshold 1 (T1):** "Current peak level" — updated to a fraction ($\alpha \approx 0.5$) of the most recent detected peak. This is the **immediate, high-confidence threshold**.
- **Threshold 2 (T2):** "Running level" — slowly tracks the long-term average of the integrated signal. This is the **baseline reference threshold**.

**Detection Rule (AND logic):** A QRS is declared only when the integrated signal $I[n]$ exceeds **both** T1 and T2 simultaneously:

$$I[n] > T_1 \ \text{AND} \ I[n] > T_2$$

This dual condition prevents false positives in two ways:
- A tall T-wave may exceed T2 but not T1 (because T1 was just raised by the previous QRS).
- Sudden noise spike may exceed T1 momentarily but not the long-term T2.

**Fan-Down Decay:** Immediately after a QRS is detected at sample $n^*$:

$$T_1[n^*] \leftarrow \alpha \cdot I[n^*]$$

Then, sample-by-sample, the threshold decays linearly (or exponentially):

$$T_1[n+1] = T_1[n] - \beta, \quad \beta > 0$$

until the next QRS is detected. This **decay trajectory forms the "fan" shape** when plotted over time across multiple beats.

**Refractory Period:** For 200 ms following a detection, the algorithm is "blind" — no new QRS can be declared. This serves two purposes:
- Prevents re-triggering on the **same QRS complex** (e.g., on the S-wave rebound).
- Enforces the **physiological lower bound** on heart rate (300 BPM, well above any human heart rate), so any detection within 200 ms is physiologically impossible and therefore noise.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete Fan Algorithm for QRS detection in ECG. Compare its performance with the Pan-Tompkins algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ECG signal is sampled at $f_s = 360$ Hz. Compute:
  (i) The length $N$ of the moving window integration filter.
  (ii) The 3 dB cutoff frequency of this MWI filter.
  (iii) The refractory period in number of samples.
  (iv) For a detected R-R interval of 0.8 s, compute the heart rate in BPM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Comparison:**

[Block Diagram — refer to Section 4.1]

The Fan Algorithm is structurally similar to the Pan-Tompkins algorithm in the first four stages (bandpass → differentiate → square → integrate), but differs fundamentally in the **thresholding stage**:

| Feature | Fan Algorithm | Pan-Tompkins |
|---|---|---|
| Number of thresholds | **Two (dual)** | One |
| Threshold update rule | Fraction of *current* peak + running average | Fraction of *running* peak only |
| Threshold decay | Explicit **fan-down** mechanism | Gradual forgetting via moving average |
| Sensitivity to sudden amplitude changes | **High** (dual threshold adapts faster) | Moderate |
| Computational cost | Slightly lower | Slightly higher |
| Detection rule | AND of two thresholds | Single threshold + slope + amplitude checks |

**Key advantage of Fan Algorithm:** The dual threshold creates a **dynamic sensitivity region** that widens and narrows automatically. When the signal is noisy, the fan widens (more conservative); when the signal is clean, the fan narrows (more sensitive).

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Moving Window Integration Length $N$:**

Standard formula:

$$N = 0.15 \times f_s = 0.15 \times 360 = 54 \text{ samples}$$

**(ii) 3 dB Cutoff Frequency of MWI:**

The moving average filter's approximate 3 dB cutoff is:

$$f_c \approx \frac{0.443 \cdot f_s}{N} = \frac{0.443 \times 360}{54} = \frac{159.48}{54} \approx 2.95 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Refractory Period in Samples:**

$$N_{\text{refr}} = T_{\text{refr}} \times f_s = 0.200 \times 360 = 72 \text{ samples}$$

**(iv) Heart Rate Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R\text{-}R \text{ interval} &= 0.8 \text{ s} \\
HR &= \frac{60}{R\text{-}R} = \frac{60}{0.8} \\
HR &= 75 \text{ BPM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Summary of computed values:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $N$ (MWI length) | 54 samples |
| $f_c$ (MWI cutoff) | 2.95 Hz |
| $N_{\text{refr}}$ (refractory) | 72 samples |
| $HR$ (heart rate) | 75 BPM |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the squaring gain factor or normalization:** Always mention that the 5-tap differentiator has a $\frac{1}{8}$ gain factor. Skipping this is a 1-mark deduction in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing the moving window integration cutoff formula:** The 0.443 factor is the **3 dB point of a rectangular moving average filter**, not 0.5. Using $f_c = f_s / (2N)$ is **wrong** and shows a fundamental misunderstanding.
> 3. **Mixing up Fan and Pan-Tompkins:** Many students incorrectly claim Fan Algorithm uses only **one** threshold. KTU examiners specifically test the **dual-threshold** knowledge — this is the most frequently tested differentiator.
> 4. **Wrong units in numerical problems:** Refractory period must be expressed in **both seconds and samples** when $f_s$ is given. Missing the sample conversion loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the "why" — KTU 2024 scheme awards marks for reasoning,** not just formulas. Always explain *why* each stage is performed (e.g., "squaring is done to nonlinearly amplify QRS slopes because the QRS-over-T-wave amplitude ratio must be increased to improve detectability").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fan Algorithm**
>
> ✅ **Core identity:** Adaptive QRS detector with **dual adaptive thresholds** arranged in a "fan" pattern.
>
> ✅ **Five stages:** Bandpass (5–15 Hz) → Differentiate (5-tap, $\frac{1}{8}$ gain) → Square → Integrate (MWI, $N = 0.15 f_s$) → Dual threshold + refractory.
>
> ✅ **Key formula — 5-tap differentiator:** $H(z) = \frac{1}{8}(2 + z^{-1} - z^{-3} - 2z^{-4})$.
>
> ✅ **Key formula — MWI length:** $N = 0.15 \cdot f_s$.
>
> ✅ **Key formula — MWI cutoff:** $f_c = \frac{0.443 \cdot f_s}{N}$.
>
> ✅ **Refractory period:** **200 ms** (physiologically enforced lower bound on R-R interval).
>
> ✅ **Threshold update:** $T_{\text{new}} = \alpha \cdot I_{\text{peak}}$, with $\alpha \approx 0.5$.
>
> ✅ **Detection rule:** $I[n] > T_1$ **AND** $I[n] > T_2$.
>
> ✅ **HR formula:** $HR = \frac{60}{R-R \text{ (in sec)}}$ in BPM.
>
> ✅ **Why "Fan"?** Threshold jumps up after detection, then linearly/exponentially decays — the plot looks like an unfolding fan.
>
> ✅ **Vs. Pan-Tompkins:** Fan = dual threshold + explicit fan-down decay; Pan-Tompkins = single threshold + moving average.
>
> ✅ **Best application:** Real-time, low-power, wearable/embedded ECG monitors (Holter, pacemaker, smartwatch).
>
> ✅ **Performance:** Sensitivity ~98.5%, Positive Predictivity ~98% (comparable to Pan-Tompkins, lower complexity).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Huffman Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Huffman Coding # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Huffman Coding** is an optimal, lossless, prefix-free statistical data compression algorithm developed by **David A. Huffman in 1952** (published in his MIT term paper). It constructs a binary tree by repeatedly combining the two least-probable symbols into a parent node whose probability is the sum of the two children's probabilities. The algorithm guarantees that the resulting variable-length code minimizes the **expected (average) code length** $\bar{L}$ for a given discrete source with known symbol probabilities, subject to the **prefix condition** (no codeword is a prefix of another codeword). In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing**, Huffman coding is a textbook application of **Information Theory** (a Module 2 core topic) and is widely used in compressing ECG, EEG, and medical imaging data (e.g., within JPEG2000 for DICOM images) where lossless archival is mandated. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > The module explicitly demands the design of optimal **statistical codes** for a discrete memoryless source. Huffman coding is the canonical answer because it minimizes the average codeword length and is provably optimal among all prefix codes. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Exam Marks Shortcut" Imagine you are a professor with a stack of **100 answer sheets** to grade and file. You observe the grade distribution: - **Grade A** (distinction): 40 sheets — appears very often - **Grade B** (first class): 20 sheets - **Grade C** (second class): 20 sheets - **Grade D** (pass): 10 sheets - **Grade F** (fail): 10 sheets Now you must tag each sheet with a short, unambiguous label. A **fixed-length** code (e.g., all 3-letter tags: A, B, C, D, F → AAA, BBB, CCC, DDD, FFF) wastes space because Grade A (the most common) gets the same long tag as Grade F (the rarest). A smarter scheme — the **Huffman idea** — gives: - Grade A → tag "**0**" (one character — saves space on 40 sheets) - Grade B → tag "**10**" (two characters) - Grade C → tag "**110**" (three characters) - Grade D → tag "**1110**" (four characters) - Grade F → tag "**1111**" (four characters) The frequent grades get *short* codes, and the rare grades get *long* codes. The total tagging effort is minimized on average. This is precisely the principle of **entropy-weighted variable-length coding**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway:** Huffman coding is **entropy-driven**. It exploits the statistical redundancy of a source (probability distribution) to produce an *optimal* variable-length binary code that approaches the theoretical compression limit — the **Shannon Entropy** $H(S)$ — but never goes below it. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Recap (used throughout the notes) - **Entropy** $H(S)$ : minimum average bits/symbol (theoretical lower bound). - **Average code length** $\bar{L}$ : actual average bits/symbol used by the code. - **Coding efficiency** $\eta$ : ratio $H(S) / \bar{L}$, expressed in **%**. - **Redundancy** $R$ : $1 - \eta$, expressed in **%**. These four quantities are the **favourite marks-fetching quartet** of KTU examiners for any source-coding problem. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Information Theory For a **Discrete Memoryless Source (DMS)** emitting symbols $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n$ with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ such that $\sum p_i = 1$: ### Step 1 — Information Content (Self-Information) Each symbol carries an information content proportional to the *surprise* it carries: $$I(s_i) = -\log_2(p_i) \quad \text{bits/symbol}$$ A rare symbol ($p_i \to 0$) carries high information; a certain symbol ($p_i = 1$) carries zero information. ### Step 2 — Shannon Entropy (Average Information) The expected information per symbol is the **entropy**: $$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2(p_i) \quad \text{bits/symbol}$$ This is the **theoretical lower bound** on the average code length — no code, however cleverly designed, can beat this on average. ### Step 3 — Average Code Length If symbol $s_i$ is assigned a codeword of length $l_i$ bits, the expected length is: $$\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \, l_i \quad \text{bits/symbol}$$ ### Step 4 — Coding Efficiency & Redundancy $$\eta = \frac{H(S)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\,\%$$ $$R = 1 - \eta = 1 - \frac{H(S)}{\bar{L}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 highlight:** A well-designed Huffman code for a DMS always satisfies $H(S) \le \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$, i.e., the efficiency is always $\ge 50\%$ and can approach **100%** for highly skewed probability distributions. ## 2.2 The Huffman Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **List** all source symbols and their probabilities in **descending order**. 2. **Combine** the two symbols with the **smallest probabilities** into a single composite node whose probability is their sum. 3. **Re-sort** the list (new composite node + remaining symbols) in descending order. 4. **Repeat** steps 2 and 3 until only **two nodes** remain; assign one a binary **'0'** and the other a **'1'**. 5. **Trace** the binary labels from the root to each leaf — the path is the Huffman codeword. > [!IMPORTANT] > The algorithm produces a **binary tree** where leaves are the source symbols. The depth of each leaf equals the length of its codeword. The tree is **not unique** when probabilities are equal — different optimal trees of equal $\bar{L}$ can arise, but the *minimum* $\bar{L}$ is invariant. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Unit / Notes** | |---|---|---| | Self-Information of $s_i$ | $I(s_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol | | Entropy of source $S$ | $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol | | Average code length | $\bar{L} = \sum p_i \, l_i$ | bits/symbol | | Coding efficiency | $\eta = (H(S) / \bar{L}) \times 100\,\%$ | dimensionless, in % | | Redundancy | $R = 1 - \eta$ | dimensionless, in % | | Kraft's Inequality (prefix codes) | $\sum 2^{-l_i} \le 1$ | necessary condition | | Variance of code length | $\sigma^2 = \sum p_i (l_i - \bar{L})^2$ | bits$^2$, for buffer design | | Lower bound on $\bar{L}$ | $H(S) \le \bar{L}$ | Shannon's noiseless coding theorem | | Upper bound on $\bar{L}$ | $\bar{L} < H(S) + 1$ | for Huffman codes on DMS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on table syntax:** Per the engine rules, mathematical magnitude bars are written using `\vert` and `\le` symbols to keep the markdown table parser intact. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ECG / EEG Compression:** Lossless transmission of Holter recordings for telemedicine. - **Medical Imaging (DICOM / JPEG-LS / JPEG 2000):** Huffman stage in the entropy coder of compression pipelines. - **Genomics (FASTA / BAM files):** Compressing DNA base calls. - **Communication Systems:** Source coding stage before channel coding in any digital transmitter. - **File Formats:** ZIP, GZIP, PNG, MP3 (frame headers) — all rely on Huffman-style or related entropy coding. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example ## 3.1 Worked Example — Full Huffman Construction **Given:** A discrete source $S$ produces 5 symbols $\{s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5\}$ with the following probability distribution: | Symbol | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $s_4$ | $s_5$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Probability $p_i$ | **0.40** | **0.20** | **0.15** | **0.15** | **0.10** | | $I(s_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ (bits) | 1.3219 | 2.3219 | 2.7370 | 2.7370 | 3.3219 | **Required:** (a) Construct the Huffman code. (b) Find $\bar{L}$, $H(S)$, $\eta$ and $R$. ### Step 1 — Verify Probability Closure $$\sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i = 0.40 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Entropy $H(S)$ $$ \begin{aligned} H(S) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i \log_2 p_i \\[4pt] &= -\big[0.40 \log_2 0.40 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 \\ &\qquad + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10\big] \\[4pt] &= -\big[0.40 \cdot (-1.3219) + 0.20 \cdot (-2.3219) + 0.15 \cdot (-2.7370) \\ &\qquad + 0.15 \cdot (-2.7370) + 0.10 \cdot (-3.3219)\big] \\[4pt] &= -\big[-0.5288 - 0.4644 - 0.4106 - 0.4106 - 0.3322\big] \\[4pt] &= 2.1466 \text{ bits/symbol} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Each $p_i \log_2 p_i$ is negative (since $p_i < 1$), so the leading negative sign makes the whole sum positive — the entropy is a positive quantity by definition. ### Step 3 — Build the Huffman Tree (Reduction Table) **Iteration 1** — Combine the two smallest probabilities ($s_5 = 0.10$ and $s_4 = 0.15$): | Symbol | Probability | |---|---| | $s_1$ | 0.40 | | $s_2$ | 0.20 | | $s_3$ | 0.15 | | **$N_1 = (s_4, s_5)$** | **0.25** | **Iteration 2** — Combine the two smallest ($s_3 = 0.15$ and $N_1 = 0.25$): | Symbol | Probability | |---|---| | $s_1$ | 0.40 | | $s_2$ | 0.20 | | **$N_2 = (s_3, N_1)$** | **0.40** | **Iteration 3** — Combine the two smallest ($s_2 = 0.20$ and $N_2 = 0.40$): | Symbol | Probability | |---|---| | $s_1$ | 0.40 | | **$N_3 = (s_2, N_2)$** | **0.60** | **Iteration 4** — Final combination of the two remaining nodes $s_1 = 0.40$ and $N_3 = 0.60$ gives the **root** with probability **1.00**. ### Step 4 — Assign Binary Labels and Read Codewords Tracing from the root (assigning **'0'** to the upper branch and **'1'** to the lower branch at each split): | Symbol | Path (root → leaf) | Codeword | Length $l_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | 0 | **0** | 1 | | $s_2$ | 1 0 | **10** | 2 | | $s_3$ | 1 1 0 | **110** | 3 | | $s_4$ | 1 1 1 0 | **1110** | 4 | | $s_5$ | 1 1 1 1 | **1111** | 4 | ### Step 5 — Verify the Prefix Condition (Kraft's Inequality) $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=1}^{5} 2^{-l_i} &= 2^{-1} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-4} + 2^{-4} \\ &= 0.5000 + 0.2500 + 0.1250 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 \\ &= 1.0000 \;\le\; 1 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > The prefix condition holds (in fact it is *tight* — the sum equals 1, meaning the code uses the entire binary tree). ### Step 6 — Compute Average Code Length $\bar{L}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{L} &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i \, l_i \\ &= (0.40)(1) + (0.20)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.15)(4) + (0.10)(4) \\ &= 0.40 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.60 + 0.40 \\ &= 2.25 \text{ bits/symbol} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Compute Efficiency $\eta$ and Redundancy $R$ $$ \eta = \frac{H(S)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.1466}{2.2500} \times 100\% = 95.40\,\% $$ $$ R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9540 = 0.0460 = 4.60\,\% $$ ### Step 8 — Final Result Summary | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Entropy $H(S)$ | **2.1466 bits/symbol** | | Average code length $\bar{L}$ | **2.25 bits/symbol** | | Efficiency $\eta$ | **95.40 %** | | Redundancy $R$ | **4.60 %** | | Compression ratio (vs. fixed 3-bit code) | $3/2.25 = 1.333$ : 1 | > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The Huffman code uses on average **2.25 bits/symbol**, very close to the theoretical minimum of 2.1466 bits/symbol. Compared to a naive fixed-length 3-bit code (for 5 symbols, $\lceil \log_2 5 \rceil = 3$), Huffman saves **25 %** of the bits on average. ## 3.2 Verification — Shannon's Noiseless Coding Theorem The bound $H(S) \le \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$ must hold: $$2.1466 \le 2.25 < 2.1466 + 1 = 3.1466 \quad \checkmark$$ The code is therefore **valid and optimal** for this source. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Huffman Binary Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD nodeRoot["ROOT p=1.00"] nodeA["s1 p=0.40"] nodeB["p=0.60"] nodeB0["s2 p=0.20"] nodeB1["p=0.40"] nodeB1a["s3 p=0.15"] nodeB1b["p=0.25"] nodeB1bA["s4 p=0.15"] nodeB1bB["s5 p=0.10"] nodeRoot ---|"0"| nodeA nodeRoot ---|"1"| nodeB nodeB ---|"0"| nodeB0 nodeB ---|"1"| nodeB1 nodeB1 ---|"0"| nodeB1a nodeB1 ---|"1"| nodeB1b nodeB1b ---|"0"| nodeB1bA nodeB1b ---|"1"| nodeB1bB classDef leaf fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000; classDef internal fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000; class nodeA,nodeB0,nodeB1a,nodeB1bA,nodeB1bB leaf; class nodeRoot,nodeB,nodeB1,nodeB1b internal; ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The root splits into `s1` (left, label '0') and the right subtree (label '1'). - Following the branch labels from root to leaf yields the **codeword** for that symbol. - **Leaf depths** = code lengths: $s_1$ at depth 1, $s_2$ at depth 2, $s_3$ at depth 3, $s_4$ and $s_5$ at depth 4. ## 4.2 End-to-End Biomedical Source-Coding Pipeline ```mermaid graph LR src["Biomedical Signal
ECG or EEG"] samp["Sampling and
Quantization"] sym["Discrete Symbol
Stream"] prob["Probability
Estimation"] tree["Huffman Tree
Builder"] enc["Entropy
Encoder"] ch["Channel
Encoder"] tx["Transmission
or Storage"] src --> samp --> sym prob --> tree --> enc sym --> enc enc --> ch --> tx classDef io fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000; classDef proc fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000; class src,tx io; class samp,sym,prob,tree,enc,ch proc; ``` > **Pipeline roles:** > - The *sampler-quantizer* converts the analog biomedical signal into discrete symbols. > - The *probability estimator* computes symbol statistics (often from a training set of past recordings). > - The *Huffman tree builder* generates the optimal codebook. > - The *entropy encoder* replaces each symbol with its variable-length codeword. > - The *channel encoder* then adds redundancy for error protection before transmission. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Define **Huffman coding**. State the **prefix condition** and explain why it is essential for unambiguous decoding. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Huffman coding is a statistical, lossless compression technique that assigns variable-length binary codewords to source symbols based on their probabilities of occurrence, so that more probable symbols get shorter codes. It is constructed by repeatedly merging the two least-probable symbols into a composite node. - **(1 mark)** The **prefix condition** states that no codeword should be the prefix of any other codeword in the code set. For example, if `'0'` is a codeword, then no other codeword can begin with `'0'` (i.e., `'01'`, `'00'`, etc., are forbidden). - **(1 mark)** The prefix condition is essential because it allows **instantaneous, unambiguous decoding** using a simple left-to-right scan without the need to look ahead. The decoder can identify the end of a codeword the moment a leaf is reached in the code tree. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** Differentiate between **fixed-length coding** and **Huffman (variable-length) coding** in terms of average code length and decoding complexity. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Fixed-Length Code | Huffman Code | |---|---|---| | Codeword length | Constant for all symbols ($l = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$) | Variable, depends on $p_i$ | | Average length $\bar{L}$ | Higher; equals $l$ for all messages | Lower; approaches $H(S)$ | | Decoding complexity | Simple — block-boundary known | Slightly higher — needs tree traversal | | Compression efficiency | Low for skewed sources | High for skewed sources | | Robustness to bit errors | A bit error usually corrupts only 1 symbol | A bit error may desynchronize the entire stream | | Optimality | Not optimal in $\bar{L}$ | Provably optimal among prefix codes | **(1 mark each, total 3 marks.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]** A discrete memoryless source emits five symbols $A, B, C, D, E$ with probabilities $P(A) = 0.40$, $P(B) = 0.20$, $P(C) = 0.15$, $P(D) = 0.15$, $P(E) = 0.10$. **(a)** Design a **Huffman code** for this source. Show the reduction steps clearly. **(b)** Compute the **average code length** $\bar{L}$, **entropy** $H(S)$, **coding efficiency** $\eta$ and **redundancy** $R$. Verify Shannon's noiseless coding theorem. --- #### Model Solution to Q-A **Part (a) — Huffman Tree Construction (7 marks)** We follow the standard reduction procedure. At each step we merge the two lowest probabilities and form a new node with their sum. The detailed reduction table is shown below: | Iteration | Symbol list (sorted ↓) | Merged node | New probability | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.40, 0.20, 0.15, **0.15, 0.10** | $N_1 = D + E$ | **0.25** | | 2 | 0.40, 0.20, **0.15, 0.25** | $N_2 = C + N_1$ | **0.40** | | 3 | 0.40, **0.20, 0.40** | $N_3 = B + N_2$ | **0.60** | | 4 | **0.40, 0.60** | ROOT = A + N_3 | **1.00** | Assigning **'0'** to the upper branch and **'1'** to the lower branch at every split, the resulting codewords are: | Symbol | Probability $p_i$ | Codeword | Length $l_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | A | 0.40 | **0** | 1 | | B | 0.20 | **10** | 2 | | C | 0.15 | **110** | 3 | | D | 0.15 | **1110** | 4 | | E | 0.10 | **1111** | 4 | **(1 mark for each correct reduction step × 4 = 4 marks; 3 marks for tracing codewords correctly.)** **Part (b) — Performance Metrics (7 marks)** **(i) Average code length $\bar{L}$ (2 marks):** $$ \bar{L} = \sum p_i l_i = 0.40(1) + 0.20(2) + 0.15(3) + 0.15(4) + 0.10(4) = 2.25 \text{ bits/symbol} $$ **(ii) Entropy $H(S)$ (2 marks):** $$ H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i = 2.1466 \text{ bits/symbol} $$ **(iii) Coding efficiency $\eta$ and redundancy $R$ (2 marks):** $$ \eta = \frac{2.1466}{2.25} \times 100\% = 95.40\,\%, \quad R = 1 - 0.9540 = 4.60\,\% $$ **(iv) Verification of Shannon's theorem (1 mark):** $$2.1466 \le 2.25 < 2.1466 + 1 = 3.1466 \quad \checkmark$$ The code is **valid and optimal** among all prefix codes for this source. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model — Internal Choice]** **(a)** Define **information content** and **entropy** of a discrete source. Compute the entropy of a source with symbol set $\{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}$ having probabilities $0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125$ respectively. **(b)** For the same source, design a **Huffman code**, compute the **average code length** and **coding efficiency**. Comment on the result. --- #### Model Solution to Q-B **Part (a) — Definitions and Entropy Computation (7 marks)** - **Information content (1 mark):** The information content of a symbol $x_i$ is defined as $I(x_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ bits, where $p_i$ is the probability of the symbol. It quantifies the *surprise* associated with the symbol's occurrence. - **Entropy (1 mark):** Entropy $H(S)$ of a discrete memoryless source is the *expected* information content per symbol: $H(S) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ bits/symbol.
