It represents the **minimum possible average bits/symbol** achievable by any lossless coding scheme. - **Numerical evaluation (5 marks):** With $p_1 = 0.5, p_2 = 0.25, p_3 = 0.125, p_4 = 0.125$: $$ \begin{aligned} H(S) &= -\big[0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125\big] \\ &= -\big[0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.25 \cdot (-2) + 0.125 \cdot (-3) + 0.125 \cdot (-3)\big] \\ &= -\big[-0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375\big] \\ &= 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Huffman Code, $\bar{L}$, $\eta$ (7 marks)** **Huffman reduction (4 marks):** | Iteration | Sorted list | Merged node | New probability | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.500, 0.250, **0.125, 0.125** | $N_1 = x_3 + x_4$ | **0.250** | | 2 | 0.500, **0.250, 0.250** | $N_2 = x_2 + N_1$ | **0.500** | | 3 | **0.500, 0.500** | ROOT = $x_1 + N_2$ | **1.000** | Codewords (assigning '0' to upper, '1' to lower at each split): | Symbol | $p_i$ | Codeword | $l_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | $x_1$ | 0.500 | **0** | 1 | | $x_2$ | 0.250 | **10** | 2 | | $x_3$ | 0.125 | **110** | 3 | | $x_4$ | 0.125 | **111** | 3 | **Average code length (2 marks):** $$ \bar{L} = 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.125(3) + 0.125(3) = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol} $$ **Efficiency (1 mark):** $$ \eta = \frac{1.75}{1.75} \times 100\% = 100\,\% $$ **Comment (extra credit in valuation):** The efficiency is **exactly 100 %**, meaning the Huffman code achieves the absolute theoretical optimum. This happens because the source has a **dyadic probability distribution** ($p_i$ are all powers of $1/2$), which is the only case where $\bar{L} = H(S)$ exactly. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones (from KTU valuation reports):** > 1. **Forgetting to re-sort** after every merge — examiners specifically look for sorted lists at every iteration. Unsorted merges → 0 marks for the construction. > 2. **Wrong codeword tracing** — codeword '10' is **not** the same as '01'; the bit assigned to upper vs. lower branch matters for the *code*, but the *lengths* (and hence $\bar{L}$) are invariant. KTU awards marks for the code table, not just the lengths. > 3. **Not verifying the prefix condition** (Kraft's inequality) — losing 1 mark that is otherwise free. > 4. **Forgetting the unit 'bits/symbol'** in the final $\bar{L}$ and $H(S)$ answers. Examiners *do* deduct half-a-mark for missing units in the strict marking scheme. > 5. **Skipping the "verify Shannon's theorem" step** — a 1-mark gift question; skipping it indicates incomplete understanding. > 6. **Confusing Huffman with Shannon-Fano coding** — Shannon-Fano is a top-down split, Huffman is bottom-up merging. They are *not* the same algorithm, and the question often tests this distinction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Huffman coding** is the *optimal prefix code* for a known symbol probability distribution. - **Algorithm:** bottom-up; repeatedly merge the **two smallest** probabilities. - **Output:** a full binary tree whose leaves are the source symbols; codeword = path from root (0 = left, 1 = right). - **Information content** of $s_i$: $I(s_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ bits. - **Entropy** (theoretical minimum): $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ bits/symbol. - **Average code length**: $\bar{L} = \sum p_i l_i$ bits/symbol. - **Efficiency**: $\eta = (H(S) / \bar{L}) \times 100\,\%$. - **Redundancy**: $R = 1 - \eta$. - **Shannon's bound**: $H(S) \le \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$ for any DMS Huffman code. - **Kraft's inequality**: $\sum 2^{-l_i} \le 1$ is the necessary & sufficient condition for the *existence* of a prefix code with lengths $l_i$. - **Optimality guarantee:** Huffman achieves the *minimum* $\bar{L}$ over **all** prefix codes for the given source. - **Limitations:** (i) Requires the probability distribution to be known in advance; (ii) sensitive to bit-errors (a single bit-flip desynchronizes the stream — channel coding must follow); (iii) the codebook must be transmitted/stored alongside the data. - **Biomedical relevance:** Used in ECG, EEG, EMG, and medical image compression (DICOM, JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000) where **lossless** archival is legally and clinically mandatory. - **Variants worth knowing:** Adaptive Huffman (Vitter algorithm), Canonical Huffman (used in JPEG/MPEG for compact codebook storage), and $m$-ary Huffman (for non-binary alphabets). - **Exam mantra:** *Always sort, always label, always compute $\bar{L}$, always verify Kraft & Shannon, and always carry the unit 'bits/symbol'.*

topics.title: Cardiological Signal Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cardiological Signal Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cardiological Signal Processing?

**Cardiological Signal Processing (CSP)** is the sub-discipline of biomedical signal processing concerned with the acquisition, conditioning, analysis, and interpretation of electrical, acoustic, and mechanical signals originating from the human heart. The principal signal of interest is the **Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)**, which represents the time-varying bioelectric potential generated by the depolarization and repolarization of cardiac muscle cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Cardiological Signal Processing refers to the application of digital signal processing (DSP) techniques — including filtering, transform-domain analysis, feature extraction, and pattern recognition — to the Electrocardiogram (ECG) and other cardiovascular bio-signals for the purpose of detecting, characterizing, and diagnosing cardiac abnormalities.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart as an Orchestra"

Imagine the human heart as a **120-piece symphony orchestra** performing inside a concert hall (the chest cavity):

| Cardiac Analogy | Engineering / DSP Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Conductor raising the baton | SA Node firing the depolarization pulse |
| Different sections (strings, brass) playing in sequence | Atrial (P) then Ventricular (QRS-T) depolarization |
| The microphone capturing the sound | Surface electrodes on the skin |
| The audio engineer cleaning the recording | Analog + Digital filtering |
| The musicologist analyzing the score | Feature extraction, QRS detection, HRV analysis |
| Detecting a wrong note | Arrhythmia / pathology detection |

> [!NOTE]
> The ECG is essentially a **non-invasive "audio recording" of the heart's electrical performance**. Just as a sound engineer removes 50 Hz hum and noise from a music track, a biomedical engineer applies a **50/60 Hz notch filter**, **baseline wander removal**, and **bandpass filtering** to obtain a clean ECG for diagnostic interpretation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Clinical Metrics & Constants

The following **standardized physiological constants** form the bedrock of cardiological signal processing:

- **Normal Resting Heart Rate:** **60 – 100 beats per minute (bpm)**
- **Standard ECG Paper Speed:** **25 mm/s**
- **Amplitude Calibration:** **10 mm/mV** (i.e., 1 mV input → 10 mm pen deflection)
- **Sampling Frequency (clinical):** **500 Hz** (Nyquist for QRS spectral content)
- **Sampling Frequency (Holter/ambulatory):** **125 – 250 Hz**
- **Standard Lead Configuration:** **12-Lead ECG** (10 electrodes, 12 views)
- **Dominant QRS Frequency Band:** **5 – 40 Hz**
- **Powerline Interference:** **50 Hz (India/Europe) / 60 Hz (USA)**
- **Mains Noise Bandwidth:** Typically **±1 Hz** around powerline frequency

---

## 1.4 Why is Cardiological Signal Processing Important?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cardiovascular Diseases (CVDs) are the **leading cause of mortality globally (WHO, ~17.9 million deaths/year)**. Cardiological signal processing enables:
> 1. **Early detection** of arrhythmias (e.g., Atrial Fibrillation, Ventricular Tachycardia).
> 2. **Remote monitoring** via wearable/ambulatory devices (Holter, patch monitors).
> 3. **Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD)** reducing cardiologist workload.
> 4. **Quantitative metrics** like Heart Rate Variability (HRV) for autonomic nervous system assessment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the ECG Waveform

The ECG trace for a single cardiac cycle comprises five cardinal waves, each representing a distinct electrophysiological event:

| Wave / Complex | Physiological Origin | Duration (ms) | Amplitude (mV) | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (SA → AV node) | 80 – 110 | 0.25 | Atrial enlargement, ectopic foci |
| **PR interval** | AV nodal conduction delay | 120 – 200 | — | First-degree AV block |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 80 – 120 | 1.0 – 1.5 (R) | Bundle branch blocks, MI |
| **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (repolarization onset) | 80 – 120 | Isoelectric | Ischemia, infarction, electrolyte imbalance |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 160 | 0.1 – 0.5 | Hyperkalemia, ischemia |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular depolarization + repolarization | 350 – 450 (HR-dependent) | — | Torsades de Pointes, drug toxicity |
| **U wave** (sometimes) | Purkinje repolarization | — | < 0.1 | Hypokalemia |

> [!NOTE]
> The **RR interval** (time between successive R-peaks) is the fundamental period for Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis.

---

## 2.2 ECG Lead Systems

### 2.2.1 Einthoven's Triangle (Bipolar Limb Leads)
- **Lead I:** LA (+) – RA (−)
- **Lead II:** LL (+) – RA (−) → **Most commonly used for rhythm analysis**
- **Lead III:** LL (+) – LA (−)

**Einthoven's Law:**

$$V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$$

### 2.2.2 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger)
- **aVR:** Augmented Vector Right
- **aVL:** Augmented Vector Left
- **aVF:** Augmented Vector Foot

### 2.2.3 Precordial (Chest) Leads — Wilson's Central Terminal
- **V1, V2:** Septal view
- **V3, V4:** Anterior wall
- **V5, V6:** Lateral wall

### 2.2.4 Frank Lead System (Vectorcardiography — VCG)
- Uses **7 electrodes** to reconstruct the 3 orthogonal leads (X, Y, Z) representing the **cardiac vector** in 3-D space.
- Critical for spatial analysis of ventricular depolarization.

---

## 2.3 Sources of Noise in ECG

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ECG is notoriously noisy. Five primary contaminants must be removed before any diagnostic algorithm runs:

1. **Powerline Interference (50/60 Hz):** Capacitive/inductive coupling from mains wiring. Removed by a **narrowband notch filter** (e.g., IIR notch at $f_0 = 50$ Hz).
2. **Baseline Wander (0 – 0.5 Hz):** Caused by respiration, patient motion, electrode polarization. Removed by a **highpass filter** with cutoff $\approx 0.5$ Hz.
3. **Muscle (EMG) Artifact (5 – 500 Hz):** Skeletal muscle activity. Reduced by lowpass filtering and adaptive filters.
4. **Motion Artifact:** Abrupt baseline shifts. Tackled by **adaptive LMS filters** using auxiliary motion reference.
5. **Instrumentation Noise (Thermal/Shot):** Broadband white Gaussian noise. Reduced by **bandpass filtering** and **ensemble averaging**.

---

## 2.4 The ECG Signal Processing Pipeline

A typical CSP chain follows this hierarchy:

$$\text{Acquisition} \rightarrow \text{Amplification} \rightarrow \text{Analog Filtering} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Digital Filtering} \rightarrow \text{QRS Detection} \rightarrow \text{Feature Extraction} \rightarrow \text{Classification}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{HR (bpm)} = \dfrac{60}{RR_{sec}}$ | Instantaneous heart rate from RR interval | bpm |
| 2 | $\text{Normalized HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_R}$, where $T_R$ is mean RR | Used in HRV studies | bpm |
| 3 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 4 | $H_{notch}(z) = \dfrac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | IIR notch filter at $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s$ | $r \approx 0.9$ (pole radius) |
| 5 | $H_{HP}(z) = \dfrac{1 - z^{-M}}{M}$ | Moving-average highpass for baseline wander | M = $f_s / f_c$ |
| 6 | $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ | Einthoven's law | Volts |
| 7 | $\text{QT}_c = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | Bazett's corrected QT (HR-normalized) | ms |
| 8 | $\text{SDNN} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(RR_i - \overline{RR})^2}$ | Standard deviation of NN intervals (HRV time-domain) | ms |
| 9 | $\text{RMSSD} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2}$ | Root mean square of successive differences (parasympathetic HRV) | ms |
| 10 | $P(f) = \lim_{T\to\infty} \dfrac{\vert X_T(f)\vert^2}{T}$ | Power Spectral Density (Welch/FFT method) | ms²/Hz |
| 11 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 12 | $\text{CMMR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common Mode Rejection Ratio (typical > 80 dB) | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **Pan-Tompkins QRS detector**, five cascaded digital filters are used: **(1)** bandpass (5–15 Hz) → **(2)** differentiator → **(3)** squaring → **(4)** moving-window integrator → **(5)** adaptive thresholding.

---

## 2.6 Heart Rate Variability (HRV) — The Cardiac Fingerprint

HRV quantifies the **beat-to-beat variation in RR intervals** and is a powerful, non-invasive marker of the **Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)** balance.

| HRV Domain | Method | Physiological Correlate | Typical Indices |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Domain** | Statistical on NN intervals | Overall variability | SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50 |
| **Frequency Domain** | Power Spectral Density | Sympathovagal balance | LF (0.04–0.15 Hz), HF (0.15–0.4 Hz) |
| **Non-Linear** | Poincaré plot, Sample Entropy, DFA | Chaos, complexity | SD1, SD2, ApEn, SampEn, $\alpha_1$, $\alpha_2$ |
| **Geometric** | HRV triangular index, TINN | Geometric shape of NN distribution | HRV index, TINN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LF/HF Ratio** $\downarrow$ → Parasympathetic dominance (rest).
> **LF/HF Ratio** $\uparrow$ → Sympathetic dominance (stress, exercise).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heart Rate from RR Interval

The instantaneous heart rate is derived by inverting the cardiac period.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cardiac} &= RR_{seconds} \\
\text{Beats per second} &= \frac{1}{RR_{sec}} \\
\text{Beats per minute} &= \frac{60}{RR_{sec}} \\
\therefore \boxed{HR_{bpm} = \frac{60}{RR_{sec}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Multiply by 60 because 1 second = 1/60 minute.

### Numerical Example
Given $RR = 0.8$ s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HR_{bpm} &= \frac{60}{0.8} = 75 \text{ bpm} \quad (\text{Normal sinus rhythm})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Notch Filter Design for 50 Hz Mains Removal

A 2nd-order IIR notch filter removes a specific frequency $f_0$ while preserving all others.

**Step 1 — Convert frequency to digital radian frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_0 &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_0}{f_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $f_0 = 50$ Hz and $f_s = 500$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_0 = 2\pi \cdot \frac{50}{500} = \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define transfer function with zeros on the unit circle (notch) and poles inside (for selectivity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Zeros on the unit circle create **infinite attenuation** at $\omega_0$. Poles with $r < 1$ (typically $r = 0.9$) lie just inside, controlling the **notch bandwidth**.

**Step 3 — Substitute $\omega_0 = \pi/5$ and $r = 0.9$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{5}\right) &= 0.8090 \\
2\cos(\omega_0) &= 1.6180 \\
H(z) &= \frac{1 - 1.6180\,z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.4562\,z^{-1} + 0.81\,z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This filter achieves $\geq 40$ dB attenuation at **50 Hz** with negligible distortion in the QRS band.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection — Filter Cascade

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985) is the **gold standard** for real-time QRS detection. The cascaded processing is derived as follows:

### Step A — Bandpass Filter (5 – 15 Hz)
Cascade of a **2nd-order lowpass** ($f_c = 11$ Hz) and a **2nd-order highpass** ($f_c = 5$ Hz). The transfer function is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{LP}(z) &= \frac{(1 - z^{-32})^2}{(1 - z^{-1})^2} \quad \text{(int form)} \\
H_{HP}(z) &= \frac{-1 + 32 z^{-16} + z^{-17} - z^{-32}}{1 - z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Differentiator (Sharpens QRS slope)
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{diff}(z) &= \frac{1}{8 T_s} \left(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z^{1} + z^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Squaring (Emphasizes large slopes, suppresses P/T waves)
$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= (x[n])^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Moving Window Integrator (Smooths multiple QRS peaks)
$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k], \quad N = 0.125 \cdot f_s
\end{aligned}
$$

For $f_s = 200$ Hz, $N = 25$ samples (i.e., 125 ms window — covers the widest QRS).

### Step D — Adaptive Thresholding
Two thresholds $THR_1$ (signal level) and $THR_2$ (noise level) are updated each beat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
THR_1 &= NPK + 0.25 \cdot (SPK - NPK) \\
THR_2 &= 0.5 \cdot THR_1
\end{aligned}
$$

where **SPK** = running peak signal level and **NPK** = running peak noise level.

> [!NOTE]
> Pan-Tompkins achieves **> 99.3% sensitivity** and **> 99.7% positive predictivity** on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database — the KTU benchmark.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Spectral Density of HRV (Welch Method)

The PSD reveals frequency components of HRV. Welch's method divides the RR tachogram into overlapping segments, windows them, computes FFTs, and averages.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}_{xx}(f) &= \frac{1}{M \cdot U} \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_m[n] \cdot w[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $M$ = number of overlapping segments
- $L$ = segment length (typically 256 samples)
- $w[n]$ = window (Hann/Hamming)
- $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2[n]$ = window energy normalization factor

**Interpretation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF_{power} &= \int_{0.04}^{0.15} \hat{P}_{xx}(f)\, df \quad (\text{Sympathetic + Baroreflex}) \\
HF_{power} &= \int_{0.15}^{0.40} \hat{P}_{xx}(f)\, df \quad (\text{Parasympathetic / Respiration}) \\
\text{LF/HF Ratio} &= \frac{LF_{power}}{HF_{power}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Ensemble Averaging for SNR Enhancement

When the ECG is buried in noise, **synchronous (coherent) averaging** of $N$ beats aligned on the R-peak enhances SNR by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{out} &= \text{SNR}_{in} \cdot \sqrt{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reasoning:** Signal is deterministic and correlated → adds linearly ($N$ times). Noise is random and uncorrelated → adds in power ($N$ times) → $\sqrt{N}$ in amplitude. Net SNR gain = $\sqrt{N}$.

For $N = 100$ beats: **SNR improves by 20 dB** (factor of 10).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of QRS Detection Algorithms

| Algorithm | Year | Sensitivity (%) | Specificity (%) | Computational Cost | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pan-Tompkins** | 1985 | 99.3 | 99.7 | Low | Real-time, Holter |
| **Hamilton-Tompkins** | 1986 | 99.6 | 99.5 | Medium | Real-time, robust |
| **Wavelet Transform (Mallat)** | 1989 | 99.5 | 99.6 | High | Noisy, ambulatory |
| **Hilbert Transform** | 2001 | 99.4 | 99.4 | Medium | Real-time |
| **Deep Learning (1D-CNN)** | 2019+ | 99.8+ | 99.8+ | Very High (GPU) | Offline CAD |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ECG Signal Processing Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **entire CSP pipeline** from skin electrodes to diagnostic decision:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Skin Electrodes] --> B[Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR > 80 dB]
    B --> C[Analog Anti-Aliasing LPF fc = 150 Hz]
    C --> D[16-bit ADC fs = 500 Hz]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processing Core]
    E --> F1[50 Hz IIR Notch Filter]
    E --> F2[Highpass 0.5 Hz Baseline Wander Removal]
    E --> F3[Lowpass 40 Hz EMG Suppression]
    F1 --> G[Pan-Tompkins QRS Detector]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Feature Extraction Block]
    H --> H1[RR Interval Series]
    H --> H2[QRS Duration]
    H --> H3[ST Segment Level]
    H --> H4[QT Interval]
    H1 --> I[HRV Analysis Module]
    I --> I1[Time Domain SDNN RMSSD]
    I --> I2[Frequency Domain Welch PSD]
    I --> I3[Non Linear Poincare Plot]
    H2 --> J[Arrhythmia Classifier]
    H3 --> J
    H4 --> J
    I1 --> J
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Diagnostic Output Beat Classification]
    K --> K1[Normal Sinus Rhythm]
    K --> K2[Atrial Fibrillation]
    K --> K3[Ventricular Tachycardia]
    K --> K4[Myocardial Ischemia]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Function Summary:**
> - **B** (Instrumentation Amp): Rejects common-mode 50 Hz pickup from the body.
> - **C** (Anti-Alias LPF): Prevents frequency aliasing — mandatory before ADC.
> - **F1–F3** (Digital Filters): Form a 0.5–40 Hz bandpass — the QRS sweet spot.
> - **G** (QRS Detector): Outputs R-peak times → enables all HRV computations.
> - **J** (Classifier): Maps extracted features to disease categories (e.g., using SVM, Random Forest, or CNN).

---

## 4.2 Detailed Pan-Tompkins Filter Cascade (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw ECG Signal] --> B[Bandpass Filter 5 to 15 Hz]
    B --> C[Differentiator Slope Enhancer]
    C --> D[Squaring Function Non Linear Amplifier]
    D --> E[Moving Window Integrator Width 150 ms]
    E --> F[Adaptive Thresholding Dual Level]
    F --> G[QRS Detected Output Pulse]
    G --> H[RR Interval Timer]
    H --> I[Heart Rate Display]
```

> **Stage Rationale:**
> - **B** isolates QRS energy (P/T waves suppressed by ~10×).
> - **C** highlights the steep QRS slope.
> - **D** makes everything positive and amplifies large slopes.
> - **E** merges Q, R, S into a single peak per beat.
> - **F** auto-adapts to changing noise and QRS amplitudes.

---

## 4.3 HRV Frequency Band Allocation (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RR Tachogram x t] --> B[Welch Power Spectral Density]
    B --> C[Total Power 0 to 0.4 Hz]
    B --> D[Very Low Frequency VLF 0 to 0.04 Hz]
    B --> E[Low Frequency LF 0.04 to 0.15 Hz]
    B --> F[High Frequency HF 0.15 to 0.4 Hz]
    E --> G[Sympathetic plus Baroreflex Activity]
    F --> H[Parasympathetic plus Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia]
    G --> I[LF over HF Ratio Autonomic Balance Index]
    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B, Q1b]`
**Define the term "Electrocardiogram". List the five standard waves of a normal ECG complex in their temporal order and state the physiological event each represents.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a non-invasive graphical recording of the time-varying electrical potentials generated by the depolarization and repolarization of the cardiac muscle, captured via surface electrodes placed on the body.

> **Five waves in temporal order (2 marks):**

| # | Wave | Physiological Event |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (impulse spreads from SA node through atria) |
| 2 | **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization (masking atrial repolarization) |
| 3 | **ST segment** | Isoelectric period — all ventricular cells depolarized (plateau phase) |
| 4 | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization |
| 5 | **U wave** (optional) | Purkinje fiber repolarization or papillary muscle repolarization |

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper, Q2a]`
**What is Heart Rate Variability (HRV)? Mention any two time-domain HRV indices and their physiological significance.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** **HRV** is the physiological variation in the time interval between successive heartbeats (RR intervals), reflecting the dynamic balance between the sympathetic and parasympathetic branches of the Autonomic Nervous System (ANS).

> **Two time-domain indices (2 marks):**

- **SDNN (Standard Deviation of NN intervals):** Reflects **overall HRV** and **total autonomic activity**. A reduced SDNN is a strong predictor of post-MI mortality.
- **RMSSD (Root Mean Square of Successive Differences):** Reflects **short-term, high-frequency variability** mediated by the **parasympathetic (vagal) nervous system**. A higher RMSSD indicates better vagal tone and recovery.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Convention:** Module 3 questions typically offer a choice between two questions, each divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. We provide both choices with full model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3, Q11]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the various stages involved in the processing of an ECG signal. Discuss the role of each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is a notch filter? Derive the transfer function of a 2nd-order IIR notch filter used for 50 Hz powerline interference removal in ECG. Assume sampling frequency $f_s = 250$ Hz.

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

The processing of an ECG signal involves the following stages, depicted in the block diagram:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Skin Electrodes} \rightarrow \text{Pre-Amp} \rightarrow \text{Filtering} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{DSP} \rightarrow \text{QRS Detection} \rightarrow \text{Classification}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage-wise explanation:**

1. **Electrode Interface (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl surface electrodes transduce ionic skin currents into electronic voltages. Proper skin preparation (abrasion, gel) minimizes contact impedance ($< 5$ k$\Omega$).

2. **Instrumentation Amplifier (1 mark):** A 3-op-amp topology provides high **CMRR (> 80 dB)** to reject common-mode 50 Hz interference picked up by both electrodes equally. Typical gain = 1000.

3. **Analog Filtering (1 mark):**
   - **Highpass ($\approx 0.05$ Hz)** blocks DC electrode offset.
   - **Lowpass ($\approx 150$ Hz)** anti-aliases before ADC.

4. **ADC (1 mark):** A 12–16 bit ADC samples at $f_s \geq 500$ Hz (Nyquist for 150 Hz analog LPF). Quantization resolution $\Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^N$.

5. **Digital Filtering (1 mark):** Cascaded operations:
   - 50 Hz IIR notch filter → removes powerline interference.
   - 0.5 Hz highpass → eliminates baseline wander.
   - 40 Hz lowpass → suppresses EMG noise.

6. **QRS Detection (1 mark):** The Pan-Tompkins algorithm locates R-peaks using bandpass → derivative → square → integrate → adaptive threshold.

7. **Feature Extraction & Classification (1 mark):** RR intervals, QRS width, ST level, QT interval are extracted. These features are fed to a classifier (SVM / Random Forest / CNN) to output a diagnostic label (Normal, AF, VT, etc.).

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A **notch filter** is a band-stop filter with a very narrow, sharp rejection band designed to remove a single specific frequency (e.g., 50 Hz mains interference) while preserving all surrounding frequencies.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute digital frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_0 &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_0}{f_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{50}{250} = \frac{2\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Place zeros on the unit circle at $\omega_0$:**

For real coefficients, conjugate zeros must be paired:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{1,2} &= e^{\pm j\omega_0} = e^{\pm j 2\pi/5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the numerator polynomial:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(z) &= (1 - e^{j\omega_0} z^{-1})(1 - e^{-j\omega_0} z^{-1}) \\
&= 1 - (e^{j\omega_0} + e^{-j\omega_0}) z^{-1} + z^{-2} \\
&= 1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Place poles inside the unit circle at $r \cdot e^{\pm j\omega_0}$:**

Using $r = 0.9$ for a sharp, stable notch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(z) &= (1 - r e^{j\omega_0} z^{-1})(1 - r e^{-j\omega_0} z^{-1}) \\
&= 1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final transfer function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) &= \frac{N(z)}{D(z)} = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical substitution** $\left(\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{5}\right) = 0.3090, \; r = 0.9\right)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = \frac{1 - 0.6180\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5562\, z^{-1} + 0.81\, z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This notch filter provides **$\geq 40$ dB attenuation at 50 Hz** with negligible phase distortion in the diagnostic QRS band (5–40 Hz).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, Module 3, Q12]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for real-time QRS detection in ECG. List its main processing stages and discuss why squaring is essential.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define Heart Rate Variability (HRV). Compute the LF/HF ratio from the following RR-interval (NN) tachogram (5-min recording) and interpret the result:
Sample PSD values: $LF_{power} = 1240 \; ms^2$, $HF_{power} = 415 \; ms^2$. Mean RR = 850 ms.

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

> **Introduction (1 mark):** The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** (1985) is a real-time, derivative-based QRS detection method that achieves > 99% accuracy on the MIT-BIH database. It works on a cascade of digital filters and adaptive thresholding.

**Main Stages (5 marks):**

1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz):** Isolates QRS energy and suppresses P, T, baseline, and 50/60 Hz noise.

2. **Differentiator:** Approximates the derivative, emphasizing the **steep slopes** of the QRS (dQRS/dt) and attenuating flat waves:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \frac{1}{8T_s}\!\left(-x[n-2] - 2x[n-1] + 2x[n+1] + x[n+2]\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Squaring Function:** $y[n] = (x[n])^2$. This is a **non-linear operation** that:
   - Makes all values **positive** (rectifying).
   - **Amplifies large values** disproportionately (since $x^2$ grows faster than $|x|$).
   - **Suppresses small P and T waves** relative to the high-amplitude QRS.
   - **Enhances SNR** nonlinearly.

4. **Moving Window Integrator:** Smooths the squared signal over a 150 ms window to produce a single peak per QRS, avoiding detection of multiple peaks within one beat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x^2[n-k]
\end{aligned}
$$

5. **Adaptive Thresholding:** Two dynamic thresholds (signal level & noise level) auto-adjust to the patient's QRS amplitude, enabling robust detection across varying morphologies.

> **Why squaring is essential (1 mark):** Squaring is a non-linear transformation that **converts the QRS detection problem from a difficult amplitude-discrimination task into a high-amplitude-to-low-amplitude ratio task**. Since the QRS has the highest slope in the ECG, squaring amplifies it far more than the P and T waves, making subsequent thresholding extremely reliable.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

> **Definition (1 mark):** **HRV** is the variation in the time interval between consecutive heartbeats (RR intervals), serving as a non-invasive marker of autonomic nervous system function.

**Step 1 — Compute LF/HF ratio (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{LF}{HF} = \frac{1240 \; ms^2}{415 \; ms^2} = 2.99 \approx 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Normalize RR interval (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{RR} &= 850 \; ms = 0.85 \; s \\
HR &= \frac{60}{0.85} \approx 70.6 \text{ bpm} \quad (\text{Normal sinus rhythm})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (3 marks):**

> A **LF/HF ratio of ~3.0** falls within the **normal physiological range (1 – 4)** for a resting adult.

- **LF (0.04–0.15 Hz)** power (1240 ms²) reflects **baroreflex-mediated sympathetic and parasympathetic activity**.
- **HF (0.15–0.40 Hz)** power (415 ms²) reflects **respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA)** — pure **parasympathetic (vagal)** activity.
- The moderate LF/HF ratio indicates a **balanced sympathovagal state** with a slight sympathetic predominance, consistent with a relaxed, awake, supine adult at normal heart rate.

> **Clinical caveat:** Markedly elevated LF/HF (> 6) suggests sympathetic hyperactivity (stress, heart failure). Markedly reduced LF/HF (< 1) suggests parasympathetic dominance orvagal overcompensation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cardiological Signal Processing Answers:**

1. **❌ Forgetting the order of waves:** Students often write "Q, R, S" in a single letter, but the examiner expects the **complex as a whole (QRS)** with each component defined. Always draw the waveform with the **isoelectric baseline** marked.
2. **❌ Confusing PR interval with PR segment:** PR **interval** = P onset to QRS onset (120–200 ms). PR **segment** = P end to QRS onset (isoelectric).
3. **❌ Wrong notch filter formula:** Forgetting to square the pole radius. The denominator must have $r^2$, not $r$. Examiner deducts 2 marks.
4. **❌ Not stating $f_s$:** Any filter design must explicitly state the sampling frequency and the digital radian frequency. Stating only $f_0$ is incomplete.
5. **❌ Mixing LF and HF bands:** LF = 0.04–0.15 Hz, HF = 0.15–0.4 Hz. The boundary at **0.15 Hz** is the most-tested number in KTU exams.
6. **❌ SDNN vs RMSSD confusion:** SDNN = overall HRV; RMSSD = short-term vagal HRV. Examiner expects you to state what each one specifically measures.
7. **❌ Pan-Tompkins without squaring justification:** Writing "squaring is done" earns partial credit. Writing **why** (non-linear amplification of large slopes) earns full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Cardiological Signal Processing:**

- ☐ **ECG** = time-varying bioelectric potential of the heart; recorded at **25 mm/s, 10 mm/mV**.
- ☐ **Five waves:** P, Q, R, S, T (sometimes U); each has distinct origin, duration, and amplitude.
- ☐ **Einthoven's Law:** $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$.
- ☐ **12-Lead ECG** = 3 bipolar (I, II, III) + 3 augmented (aVR, aVL, aVF) + 6 precordial (V1–V6).
- ☐ **Five noise sources:** 50/60 Hz mains, baseline wander, EMG, motion artifact, instrumentation noise.
- ☐ **Bandpass for ECG:** **0.5 – 40 Hz** (clinical) preserves QRS while rejecting noise.
- ☐ **Notch filter** = bandstop with very narrow stopband; 2nd-order IIR uses zeros on unit circle + poles at $r \cdot e^{\pm j\omega_0}$.
- ☐ **Pan-Tompkins** = Bandpass → Differentiator → **Square** → Integrate → Adaptive threshold. Squaring is the **non-linear amplifier**.
- ☐ **HR = 60 / RR (seconds)**; given in **bpm**.
- ☐ **HRV Time Domain:** SDNN (overall), RMSSD (vagal), pNN50.
- ☐ **HRV Frequency Domain:** LF (0.04–0.15 Hz) = sympathetic + baroreflex; HF (0.15–0.4 Hz) = parasympathetic; **LF/HF ratio** = autonomic balance.
- ☐ **Ensemble averaging** improves SNR by $\sqrt{N}$ for N synchronous beats.
- ☐ **Welch PSD** uses overlapping windowed FFT segments averaged in power.
- ☐ **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** is the **KTU gold-standard benchmark** for any ECG algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG):** A non-invasive bioelectric recording that captures the time-varying potential differences generated by the depolarization and repolarization of the cardiac muscle (myocardium) as sensed through surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest.

The ECG is a **quasi-periodic, deterministic biomedical signal** whose morphology encodes the sequential electrical activation of the atria and ventricles. From a signal-processing perspective, the ECG belongs to the class of **electro-physiological signals**, occupying a clinical bandwidth of approximately **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** with a diagnostic amplitude range of roughly **0.5 mV – 5 mV**.

The original term stems from the German "**Elektro-kardiogramm**," coined by **Willem Einthoven** (physiologist, Nobel laureate, 1924), who is also credited with inventing the string galvanometer and standardizing the modern ECG waveform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Einthoven's Law (String Galvanometer, 1903):** The instantaneous algebraic sum of the potentials recorded in standard leads I, II, and III equals zero at every instant of time:
> Lead I + Lead III = Lead II
> This foundational principle permits redundancy checks and signal integrity verification in clinical instrumentation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Heart's Electrical Symphony":**
> Imagine the heart as a large orchestra with thousands of cells (cardiomyocytes). Before each contraction, a "conductor" (the SA node) sends an electrical wave that sweeps through the cardiac tissue like ripples in a pond. The ECG electrodes are like **microphones placed on the chest**, picking up the sum of all these tiny electrical ripples. When the wave travels *toward* a microphone, the trace goes **up**; when it travels *away*, it goes **down**; when the entire heart is in a quiet electrical state, the trace returns to the **isoelectric baseline**.

The fundamental three-stage cycle of every heartbeat is:
1. **Atrial depolarization** → produces the **P wave** (small bump).
2. **Ventricular depolarization** → produces the **QRS complex** (large spike).
3. **Ventricular repolarization** → produces the **T wave** (medium dome).

This **P-QRS-T** triplet is the *signature* of one cardiac cycle and is the unit of analysis in virtually every ECG-based diagnostic algorithm.

## 1.3 Standard Clinical Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical Numerical Constants — Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Standard ECG amplitude:** $10 \text{ mm} = 1 \text{ mV}$ (calibration pulse)
> - **Standard paper speed:** $25 \text{ mm/s}$
> - **Standard heart rate range (adult):** **60 – 100 bpm**
> - **Bradycardia threshold:** **< 60 bpm**
> - **Tachycardia threshold:** **> 100 bpm**
> - **QRS amplitude (normal):** **0.5 mV – 2.5 mV**
> - **P wave duration:** **< 120 ms**
> - **QRS duration:** **80 – 120 ms**
> - **QT interval:** **350 – 440 ms** (rate-corrected: QTc ≤ 440 ms in males, ≤ 460 ms in females)
> - **PR interval:** **120 – 200 ms**

## 1.4 Bioelectric Origin of the ECG Signal

Every cardiac cell maintains a **resting membrane potential** of approximately **$-90$ mV** (inside negative). Upon excitation, voltage-gated fast Na⁺ channels open, causing rapid depolarization (**Phase 0**) to about **$+20$ mV**. The sequential wave of depolarization across millions of cells generates an externally measurable **dipole vector**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dipole Theory:** The heart, considered as a syncytium of dipoles, can be modeled as a single time-varying **equivalent cardiac vector** $\vec{H}(t)$ whose magnitude and direction change throughout the cardiac cycle. The surface ECG is the **scalar projection** of $\vec{H}(t)$ along the lead axis.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cardiac Conduction System (Anatomical Substrate of the ECG)

The ECG morphology is entirely dictated by the **specialized conduction pathway** of the heart:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequential Activation Pathway (Sino-atrial to Purkinje):**
> 1. **SA Node** (Sino-Atrial Node) — located in the right atrial wall near the SVC. *Intrinsic rate ≈ 100 bpm* — the natural pacemaker.
> 2. **Internodal Tracts** (Bachmann, Wenckebach, Thorel) — conduct the impulse across both atria → produces **P wave**.
> 3. **AV Node** (Atrio-Ventricular Node) — introduces a critical **~100 ms delay** that allows complete atrial emptying before ventricular contraction. AV block pathology alters PR interval.
> 4. **Bundle of His** — sole electrical bridge between atria and ventricles.
> 5. **Right & Left Bundle Branches** — distribute the impulse down the interventricular septum.
> 6. **Purkinje Fibers** — rapidly (≈ 4 m/s) deliver the impulse to the ventricular myocardium → produces **QRS complex**.
> 7. **Ventricular Myocardium** — depolarizes in a coordinated fashion from endocardium → epicardium.
> 8. **Repolarization phase** — ventricles recover → produces **T wave** (repolarization of atria is hidden inside the QRS).

## 2.2 Detailed ECG Waveform Decomposition

| Wave/Segment | Electrical Event | Amplitude (mV) | Duration (ms) | Frequency (Hz) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | 0.05 – 0.25 | 60 – 110 | 0.5 – 10 |
| **PR Segment** | AV nodal conduction delay (isoelectric) | 0 | 50 – 120 | — |
| **PR Interval** | Onset of P to onset of QRS | — | 120 – 200 | 0.5 – 5 |
| **Q Wave** | Initial septal depolarization (downward) | < 0.25 (25% of R) | < 30 | — |
| **R Wave** | Main ventricular depolarization (upward peak) | 0.5 – 2.5 | — | — |
| **S Wave** | Late ventricular depolarization (downward) | < 0.6 | < 30 | — |
| **QRS Complex** | Total ventricular depolarization | — | **80 – 120** | **10 – 50** |
| **ST Segment** | Ventricular plateau (isoelectric) | 0 | 80 – 120 | — |
| **T Wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 0.1 – 0.5 | 100 – 250 | 0.5 – 10 |
| **U Wave** | Purkinje repolarization (sometimes) | < 0.1 | — | — |
| **QT Interval** | Total ventricular systole | — | 350 – 440 (HR-dependent) | — |
| **RR Interval** | One complete cardiac cycle | — | 600 – 1000 (HR-dependent) | 0.5 – 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> The entire QRS energy is concentrated in the **10 – 50 Hz** band. This is critical for filter design: a **0.5 – 40 Hz** band-pass filter is the engineering sweet-spot used in clinical ECG monitors, preserving morphology while rejecting baseline wander and high-frequency muscle artifact.

## 2.3 The 12-Lead ECG System Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lead Classification:**
> - **3 Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven's Triangle):** Lead I, Lead II, Lead III — measure potential difference between two limbs.
> - **3 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger):** aVR, aVL, aVF — measure potential at one limb relative to a reference of the other two.
> - **6 Precordial (Chest) Unipolar Leads (Wilson):** V1, V2, V3, V4, V5, V6 — placed on the chest wall over the heart.

Although there are **12 leads**, only **10 electrodes** are physically attached to the patient (the right-leg electrode is a driven-right-leg ground, not a true sensing lead).

### Einthoven's Triangle (Geometric Interpretation)

The three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) form the vertices of an equilateral triangle (Einthoven's triangle) on the body's frontal plane. The cardiac vector $\vec{H}$ projects onto the three sides of this triangle to produce Leads I, II, III.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical** for KTU PEICT633 — practice deriving each one.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{HR (bpm)} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}}$ | Instantaneous heart rate from R-R interval $T_{RR}$ | bpm |
| 2 | $\text{HR (bpm)} = \dfrac{1500}{\text{R-R in mm (at 25 mm/s)}}$ | Quick paper-strip estimation | bpm |
| 3 | $\text{HR (bpm)} = \dfrac{300}{\text{large-boxes between R waves}}$ | "300-rule" — large box = 5 mm = 0.20 s | bpm |
| 4 | $f_{\text{heart}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{RR}}$ | Fundamental frequency of ECG | Hz |
| 5 | $\text{QTc} = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | Bazett's heart-rate-corrected QT interval | ms |
| 6 | $A_x = \text{Lead I amplitude (mV)}$ | X-axis projection of cardiac vector | mV |
| 7 | $A_y = \text{aVF amplitude (mV)}$ | Y-axis projection of cardiac vector | mV |
| 8 | $\theta_{\text{axis}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{A_y}{A_x}\right)$ | Mean Electrical Axis (MEA) of the heart | degrees (°) |
| 9 | $\text{Mean Vector: } \vec{H} = H_x \hat{i} + H_y \hat{j}$ | 2-D frontal-plane cardiac vector | mV |
| 10 | $\text{Lead III} = \text{Lead II} - \text{Lead I}$ | Einthoven's Law (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law) | mV |
| 11 | $V_{\text{recorded}} = \vec{H}(t) \cdot \hat{n}_{\text{lead}}$ | Lead projection of cardiac vector onto lead axis | mV |
| 12 | $\Delta t_{\text{paper}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{mm}}}{v_{\text{paper}}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{mm}}}{25}$ | Time = distance ÷ paper speed | seconds |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiac pacemakers and ICDs**: Use intra-cardiac ECGs (IECG) to detect arrhythmia and deliver synchronized therapy.
- **Holter monitors (24-h ambulatory ECG)**: Apply digital filters (Notch at 50/60 Hz, HPF 0.5 Hz, LPF 40 Hz) — direct application of biomedical DSP.
- **AI-based ECG analysis** (e.g., convolutional neural networks): Automated detection of myocardial infarction, atrial fibrillation, and long QT syndrome.
- **Wearable fitness bands** (Apple Watch, Fitbit): Single-lead ECG using wavelet-based denoising.
- **Heart-Rate Variability (HRV) analysis**: Spectral analysis of RR intervals yields LF/HF ratio — an index of autonomic nervous system balance.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heart Rate from R-R Interval

> **Given:** R-R interval measured as $N$ large boxes on standard ECG paper (each large box = **5 mm = 0.20 s**).

**Step 1 — Convert large boxes to time (seconds):**

$$
T_{RR} = N \times 0.20 \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to beats per minute (bpm):**

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{60 \text{ s}}{T_{RR} \text{ (s)}} = \dfrac{60}{0.20 \times N}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the constant:**

$$
\text{HR (bpm)} = \dfrac{300}{N}
$$

This is the famous **"300 Rule"** used by every KTU student in viva voce.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — QRS Duration & Frequency Content

> **Given:** A QRS complex spans **4 small boxes** on a paper recorded at **25 mm/s**, with each small box = **1 mm**.

**Step 1 — Distance in mm:**

$$
d = 4 \text{ small boxes} \times 1 \text{ mm/box} = 4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Time duration using the paper-speed relation:**

$$
T_{QRS} = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{4 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
T_{QRS} = 0.16 \text{ s} = 160 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Since the normal QRS duration is 80–120 ms, this patient's QRS is **prolonged (160 ms)**, which clinically suggests a **bundle-branch block** (BB). For signal processing purposes, the dominant QRS frequency is:

$$
f_{QRS} \approx \dfrac{1}{T_{QRS}} = \dfrac{1}{0.16} = 6.25 \text{ Hz (fundamental)}
$$

But because QRS has sharp edges (steep slopes), its harmonic content extends up to **40–50 Hz**. This is why **low-pass filtering at 40 Hz** is essential to remove high-frequency noise without distorting QRS morphology.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Mean Electrical Axis (MEA)

The **Mean Electrical Axis** is the average direction of depolarization throughout the ventricles, projected onto the frontal plane.

**Step 1 — Net QRS area in Lead I (horizontal axis, $x$-component):**

$$
A_x = \int_{QRS} V_I(t) \, dt \;\; (\text{mV}\cdot\text{s})
$$

**Step 2 — Net QRS area in Lead aVF (vertical axis, $y$-component):**

$$
A_y = \int_{QRS} V_{aVF}(t) \, dt \;\; (\text{mV}\cdot\text{s})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the resultant vector magnitude:**

$$
\vert \vec{H} \vert = \sqrt{A_x^{2} + A_y^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the axis angle:**

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{A_y}{A_x}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Quadrant correction (clinical ranges):**

- Normal axis: $-30^{\circ}$ to $+90^{\circ}$
- Right axis deviation: $+90^{\circ}$ to $+180^{\circ}$ (e.g., right ventricular hypertrophy)
- Left axis deviation: $-30^{\circ}$ to $-90^{\circ}$ (e.g., left anterior fascicular block)

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Bazett's Corrected QT Interval (QTc)

The raw QT interval shortens at high heart rates. To compare QT values across patients, **Bazett's formula** normalizes QT to a heart rate of **60 bpm**:

**Step 1 — Measure the RR interval (in seconds):**

$$
T_{RR} = \dfrac{60}{\text{HR}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bazett's square-root correction:**

$$
QT_c = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{T_{RR}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**

> Given: $QT = 0.40 \text{ s}$, HR = 75 bpm
>
> First, $T_{RR} = 60/75 = 0.80 \text{ s}$
>
> Then,
>
> $$
> QT_c = \dfrac{0.40}{\sqrt{0.80}} = \dfrac{0.40}{0.8944} = 0.447 \text{ s} = 447 \text{ ms}
> $$
>
> Since $447 \text{ ms} > 440 \text{ ms}$, this is **borderline prolonged QTc** — a known risk factor for Torsades de Pointes.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Cardiac Vector Projection on a Lead Axis

Consider an arbitrary lead placed along unit vector $\hat{n}_{\text{lead}}$. The voltage recorded by this lead is the **scalar projection** of the cardiac dipole vector $\vec{H}(t)$ onto $\hat{n}_{\text{lead}}$:

**Step 1 — Define the dipole vector in 3-D space:**

$$
\vec{H}(t) = H_x(t) \hat{i} + H_y(t) \hat{j} + H_z(t) \hat{k}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the lead unit vector:**

$$
\hat{n}_{\text{lead}} = \cos\alpha \, \hat{i} + \cos\beta \, \hat{j} + \cos\gamma \, \hat{k}
$$

where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are direction cosines between the lead axis and the $x, y, z$ body axes.

**Step 3 — The lead voltage (scalar product):**

$$
V_{\text{lead}}(t) = \vec{H}(t) \cdot \hat{n}_{\text{lead}} = H_x \cos\alpha + H_y \cos\beta + H_z \cos\gamma
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum voltage occurs when $\hat{n}_{\text{lead}}$ is parallel to $\vec{H}$:**

$$
V_{\max} = \vert \vec{H} \vert
$$

This result explains why **different leads record different amplitudes** of the same cardiac event — each lead "sees" a different projection of the same 3-D cardiac vector.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cardiac Conduction System (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SA Node Sinoatrial Pacemaker] -->|Internodal Tracts| B[Atrial Myocardium]
    B -->|Atrial Depolarization| C[P Wave on ECG]
    B -->|AV Delay 100 ms| D[AV Node Atrioventricular Gate]
    D -->|Bundle of His| E[Right and Left Bundle Branches]
    E -->|Purkinje Fibers| F[Ventricular Myocardium]
    F -->|Ventricular Depolarization| G[QRS Complex on ECG]
    F -->|Ventricular Repolarization| H[T Wave on ECG]
```

## 4.2 ECG Waveform Component Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[P Wave Atrial Depolarization] --> PR[PR Segment AV Delay Isoelectric]
    PR --> PRI[PR Interval 120 to 200 ms]
    PRI --> Q[Q Wave Initial Septal Downstroke]
    Q --> R[R Wave Main Ventricular Peak]
    R --> S[S Wave Late Ventricular Downstroke]
    S --> QRS[QRS Complex 80 to 120 ms]
    QRS --> ST[ST Segment Isoelectric Plateau]
    ST --> T[T Wave Ventricular Repolarization]
    T --> U[U Wave Optional Purkinje Recovery]
    U --> NEXT[Next Cardiac Cycle]
```

## 4.3 12-Lead ECG System Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    HEART[Cardiac Dipole Vector H of t] --> LA[Left Arm Electrode]
    HEART --> RA[Right Arm Electrode]
    HEART --> LL[Left Leg Electrode]
    HEART --> RL[Right Leg Driven Ground]
    HEART --> V1[V1 Chest Electrode]
    HEART --> V2[V2 Chest Electrode]
    HEART --> V3[V3 Chest Electrode]
    HEART --> V4[V4 Chest Electrode]
    HEART --> V5[V5 Chest Electrode]
    HEART --> V6[V6 Chest Electrode]
    LA --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]
    RA --> AMP
    LL --> AMP
    V1 --> AMP2[Chest Lead Amplifier]
    V2 --> AMP2
    V3 --> AMP2
    V4 --> AMP2
    V5 --> AMP2
    V6 --> AMP2
    AMP --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter 12 bit at 500 Hz]
    AMP2 --> ADC
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processing Filter Bank 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    DSP --> DISP[Display on Monitor and Plotter]
```

## 4.4 Standard ECG Signal Processing Pipeline (Block Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw ECG with Noise] --> B[High Pass Filter at 0.5 Hz Removes Baseline Wander]
    B --> C[Low Pass Filter at 40 Hz Removes EMG and High Freq Noise]
    C --> D[Notch Filter at 50 Hz Removes Powerline Interference]
    D --> E[QRS Detection Pan Tompkins Algorithm]
    E --> F[R R Interval Computation]
    F --> G[Heart Rate and HRV Calculation]
    G --> H[Display and Alarm]
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

> **Q:** Define Electrocardiogram. List the standard amplitude calibration and paper speed used in clinical ECG recording.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** The Electrocardiogram (ECG) is a graphical recording of the electrical potentials generated by the cardiac muscle during depolarization and repolarization, obtained using surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest.

2. **Amplitude Calibration (1 mark):** The standard vertical calibration is **$10 \text{ mm} = 1 \text{ mV}$**. Hence, each small box (1 mm) = 0.1 mV.

3. **Paper Speed (1 mark):** The standard horizontal paper speed is **$25 \text{ mm/s}$**. Hence, each small box = 0.04 s and each large box = 0.20 s.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** Explain the significance of the P wave, QRS complex, and T wave in a normal ECG trace.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **P wave (1 mark):** Represents **atrial depolarization**, originating from the SA node and spreading across both atria. Normal duration < 120 ms, amplitude < 0.25 mV.

2. **QRS complex (1 mark):** Represents **ventricular depolarization**. Duration 80–120 ms, amplitude 0.5–2.5 mV. The atrial repolarization (Ta wave) is masked inside the QRS.

3. **T wave (1 mark):** Represents **ventricular repolarization**. Asymmetric, rounded, normally in the same direction as the QRS. Inversion of T wave is a sensitive marker of myocardial ischemia.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> When answering 14-mark questions, students commonly lose 2–3 marks by:
> 1. **Confusing PR interval with PR segment** — interval includes the P wave; segment does not.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the heart-rate formula** — examiners explicitly hunt for the constant **300**.
> 3. **Drawing the cardiac cycle backwards** (T before QRS) — always memorize **P → QRS → T** order.
> 4. **Skipping the cardiac conduction pathway** — Einthoven, His, Purkinje terms earn easy marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

> **Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the ECG signal acquisition system. Explain the role of each stage. (7 marks)
>
> **Q (b)** A patient's ECG shows an R-R interval of 4 large boxes on a paper recorded at standard speed. Calculate the heart rate in bpm and the cardiac frequency in Hz. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks:**

The ECG acquisition system consists of the following stages:

1. **Electrodes (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl disposable electrodes placed on the limbs (RA, LA, LL, RL ground) and chest (V1–V6). They transduce ionic skin currents into electronic signals via the half-cell potential (~+0.22 V for Ag/AgCl).

2. **Patient Cable & Defibrillator Protection (1 mark):** Provides electrical isolation, protects patient from leakage currents (< 10 µA), and incorporates high-energy surge protection for defibrillation.

3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (1 mark):** High CMRR (> 100 dB), gain ≈ 1000, rejects common-mode 50/60 Hz interference. Key IC examples: AD620, INA128.

4. **Band-Pass Filter (1 mark):** HPF (0.05–0.5 Hz) removes baseline drift; LPF (40–150 Hz) removes EMG and high-frequency noise.

5. **Notch Filter (1 mark):** Tuned to 50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (USA) to remove powerline interference.

6. **ADC (1 mark):** Resolution 12-bit (or higher); sampling rate 250–1000 Hz; per Nyquist must satisfy $f_s > 2 f_{\max}$.

7. **Display / Recorder (1 mark):** CRT/LCD monitor or thermal-array plotter; also computer display for digital storage.

#### **Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** R-R interval = **4 large boxes**.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Recall standard constants:
- 1 large box = 5 mm = 0.20 s (at 25 mm/s paper speed).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Convert R-R distance to time:

$$
T_{RR} = 4 \times 0.20 = 0.80 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply heart-rate formula:

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}} = \dfrac{60}{0.80} = 75 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the "300 rule" cross-check:

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{300}{N} = \dfrac{300}{4} = 75 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Calculate cardiac fundamental frequency:

$$
f_{\text{heart}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{RR}} = \dfrac{1}{0.80} = 1.25 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Clinical interpretation:

The heart rate of 75 bpm falls within the **normal sinus rhythm** range (60–100 bpm).

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Spectral consequence:

Therefore, the dominant ECG periodicity is **1.25 Hz**, with significant harmonics up to 40 Hz corresponding to QRS edges.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q (a)** Explain the Einthoven's Triangle and derive the relationship between Leads I, II, and III. (7 marks)
>
> **Q (b)** With a clear diagram, describe the standard 12-lead ECG system and explain the difference between bipolar and unipolar leads. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**

Einthoven's triangle is an **equilateral triangle** whose vertices are the three limb electrodes: Right Arm (RA), Left Arm (LA), and Left Leg (LL). It is assumed to lie in the body's **frontal plane**. The cardiac vector $\vec{H}(t)$ projects onto each side of this triangle, producing the three bipolar limb leads.

**Step 2 — Bipolar Lead Definitions (2 marks):**

- Lead I = $V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ (horizontal axis, +ve to the left)
- Lead II = $V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ (diagonal axis, +ve downwards)
- Lead III = $V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ (diagonal axis, +ve upwards to the left)

**Step 3 — Derivation of Einthoven's Law (2 marks):**

Adding Lead I and Lead III:

$$
\text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})
$$

The $V_{LA}$ terms cancel:

$$
\text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}
$$

But by definition, $V_{LL} - V_{RA} = \text{Lead II}$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{Lead II} = \text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III}}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation (1 mark):**

This is essentially **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the closed triangle loop, confirming the redundancy of the lead system — only two of the three bipolar leads carry independent information.

#### **Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Classification (2 marks):**

The 12 standard ECG leads are classified into three groups:
- **3 Bipolar Limb Leads:** Lead I, Lead II, Lead III (Einthoven)
- **3 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads:** aVR, aVL, aVF (Goldberger)
- **6 Precordial (Chest) Unipolar Leads:** V1, V2, V3, V4, V5, V6 (Wilson)

**Step 2 — Bipolar Lead Definition (1 mark):**

A **bipolar lead** measures the **potential difference between two exploring electrodes**, both of which contribute to the recorded signal. Example: Lead II = $V_{LL} - V_{RA}$.

**Step 3 — Unipolar Lead Definition (1 mark):**

A **unipolar lead** measures the potential at a single exploring electrode relative to a **central terminal** (Wilson's CT, or Goldberger's modified CT), which serves as a near-zero reference.

**Step 4 — Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (1 mark):**

Goldberger's augmentation: the reference terminal is formed from the *other two* limb electrodes through high-value resistors. The recorded voltage is therefore **augmented by 50%** compared to Wilson's unipolar limb leads. Hence the prefix "**a**" in aVR, aVL, aVF.

**Step 5 — Precordial Lead Placement (1 mark):**

- V1: 4th intercostal space, right sternal border
- V2: 4th intercostal space, left sternal border
- V4: 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line
- V3: midway between V2 and V4
- V5: anterior axillary line (same level as V4)
- V6: mid-axillary line (same level as V4)

**Step 6 — Clinical Significance (1 mark):**

The 12-lead system provides **12 different "viewing angles"** of the same 3-D cardiac vector, allowing precise localization of ischemia, infarction, and conduction blocks across the 17 segments of the LV myocardium.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Twice Before Every KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **ECG definition:** Recording of cardiac electrical activity (depolarization + repolarization).
- [ ] **Standard calibration:** **10 mm = 1 mV vertical**, **25 mm/s horizontal**.
- [ ] **1 small box** = 1 mm = 0.04 s = 0.1 mV; **1 large box** = 5 mm = 0.20 s = 0.5 mV.
- [ ] **P wave** = Atrial depolarization (< 120 ms, < 0.25 mV).
- [ ] **PR interval** = Onset of P to onset of QRS (**120–200 ms**); reflects AV conduction.
- [ ] **QRS complex** = Ventricular depolarization (**80–120 ms**, 10–50 Hz dominant).
- [ ] **ST segment** = Isoelectric plateau — elevation/depression is a hallmark of ischemia/infarction.
- [ ] **T wave** = Ventricular repolarization; inversion = ischemia.
- [ ] **QT interval** = Total ventricular systole; rate-corrected by **Bazett's** formula.
- [ ] **Heart rate formula:** $\text{HR} = 300/N$ (N = large boxes between R waves).
- [ ] **Cardiac frequency:** $f = 1/T_{RR}$ in Hz.
- [ ] **Einthoven's Law:** Lead II = Lead I + Lead III.
- [ ] **12 leads** = 3 bipolar (I, II, III) + 3 augmented (aVR, aVL, aVF) + 6 precordial (V1–V6).
- [ ] **Cardiac conduction sequence:** SA → Atria → AV (100 ms delay) → Bundle of His → Bundle branches → Purkinje → Ventricles.
- [ ] **Bandwidth:** Clinical ECG = 0.05–150 Hz; filtered monitor = 0.5–40 Hz.
- [ ] **Sampling rate:** ≥ 250 Hz (Nyquist requires > 2 × 50 Hz = 100 Hz minimum, but 250 Hz is KTU-standard).
- [ ] **Standard adult HR:** **60–100 bpm**; bradycardia < 60; tachycardia > 100.
- [ ] **Mean Electrical Axis (MEA):** $\theta = \arctan(A_y/A_x)$; normal range $-30^{\circ}$ to $+90^{\circ}$.
- [ ] **Always state units** in numerical answers (bpm, mV, ms, Hz) — examiners deduct marks otherwise.

---

*End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 3, Topic: Basics of ECG*

topics.title: Anatomy of the Heart and Electrical Activity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Anatomy of the Heart and Electrical Activity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cardiac Anatomy & Electrical Activity (KTU Syllabus Definition):** > The heart is a four-chambered, dual-pump muscular organ that circulates blood through the pulmonary and systemic circuits. Its function is governed by an intrinsic **cardiac conduction system** comprising specialized nodal tissues (SA node, AV node), bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers that generate and propagate **cardiac action potentials**, producing the surface bioelectric signal known as the **Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)**. ### 1.2 Heart — The Dual Mechanical-Electrical Pump The human heart is a **hollow, cone-shaped, four-chambered muscular organ** approximately the size of a fist, weighing roughly **300 g (males)** / **250 g (females)**. It is located in the **mediastinum**, slightly left of the midline, between the lungs and resting on the diaphragm. **Structural Divisions:** - **Atria (Upper chambers):** Right Atrium (RA) and Left Atrium (LA) — receiving chambers - **Ventricles (Lower chambers):** Right Ventricle (RV) and Left Ventricle (LV) — pumping chambers - **Septum:** Interatrial and Interventricular septa separating left and right sides - **Valves:** Four valves ensuring unidirectional blood flow > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **Left Ventricle (LV)** has the thickest wall (~8–15 mm) because it must generate high pressure (~120 mmHg systolic) to drive blood through the entire systemic circulation. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Two-Story House with Plumbing" Imagine the heart as a **two-storey house with a single set of plumbing on each side**: | House Component | Heart Equivalent | Function | |----------------|------------------|----------| | Upper-floor rooms (receiving) | Right & Left Atria | Collect incoming blood | | Ground-floor rooms (pumping) | Right & Left Ventricles | Push blood out | | Doors between floors | AV Valves (Tricuspid, Mitral) | One-way flow downstairs | | Exit doors to outside | Semilunar Valves (Pulmonary, Aortic) | One-way flow to arteries | | Electrical wiring system | SA Node → AV Node → His → Purkinje | Triggers coordinated contraction | | Backup generator | Ectopic pacemaker sites | Backup rhythm if main fails | The "electrical wiring" fires automatically, the "doors" (valves) snap open/shut based on pressure, and the "rooms" (chambers) squeeze in sequence — this is the heart's operation in plain English. ### 1.4 Standard Cardiac Metrics (Memorize for KTU Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Normal Cardiac Parameters:** > - **Resting Heart Rate (HR):** **60–100 beats per minute (bpm)** > - **Stroke Volume (SV):** **~70 mL/beat** > - **Cardiac Output (CO):** **~5 L/min** at rest, up to **~25 L/min** during exercise > - **Ejection Fraction (EF):** **55–70%** (clinical gold standard) > - **Action Potential Duration (ventricular):** **~250–300 ms** > - **Conduction Velocity (Purkinje):** **~2–4 m/s** (fastest in body) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Chambers — Functional Anatomy #### 2.1.1 Right Side of the Heart (Pulmonary Circuit) - **Right Atrium (RA):** Receives deoxygenated blood from **Superior Vena Cava (SVC)**, **Inferior Vena Cava (IVC)**, and **Coronary Sinus**. Contains the **SA node** in its upper posterolateral wall. - **Right Ventricle (RV):** Pumps blood to the lungs via the **Pulmonary Artery**. Wall thickness ~3–5 mm. Contains the **moderator band** carrying part of the right bundle branch. #### 2.1.2 Left Side of the Heart (Systemic Circuit) - **Left Atrium (LA):** Receives oxygenated blood from the four **Pulmonary Veins**. - **Left Ventricle (LV):** Pumps blood to the entire body via the **Aorta**. Wall thickness ~8–15 mm (3× thicker than RV). ### 2.2 The Four Heart Valves | Valve | Location | Type | Function | |-------|----------|------|----------| | **Tricuspid Valve** | Between RA and RV | Right AV valve | Prevents backflow into RA during ventricular systole | | **Mitral (Bicuspid) Valve** | Between LA and LV | Left AV valve | Prevents backflow into LA during ventricular systole | | **Pulmonary Valve** | RV → Pulmonary Artery | Semilunar | Prevents backflow into RV during diastole | | **Aortic Valve** | LV → Aorta | Semilunar | Prevents backflow into LV during diastole | > [!NOTE] > **Auscultation Landmarks (KTU Clinical Pearl):** > - **S1 ("Lub"):** Closure of AV valves → marks beginning of systole > - **S2 ("Dub"):** Closure of semilunar valves → marks beginning of diastole > - **S3 / S4:** Pathological (S3 = rapid ventricular filling; S4 = atrial kick against stiff ventricle) ### 2.3 Cardiac Conduction System — The "Electrical Wiring" The heart contains **specialized autorhythmic cells** that generate action potentials spontaneously — this property is called **automaticity** or **pacemaker activity**. #### 2.3.1 Sequence of Electrical Activation 1. **Sinoatrial (SA) Node** — Located in upper RA near SVC opening. **Primary pacemaker** at ~**70 bpm** (60–100 bpm normal). Spontaneously depolarizes via "funny current" (If). 2. **Atrial Conduction Pathways** — Internodal tracts and **Bachmann's bundle** spread depolarization across both atria → produces **P wave** on ECG. 3. **Atrioventricular (AV) Node** — Located at base of right atrium near interatrial septum. **Delay of ~100 ms** allows atrial contraction to complete ventricular filling. 4. **Bundle of His (AV bundle)** — The only electrical connection between atria and ventricles (penetrates fibrous skeleton). 5. **Right & Left Bundle Branches** — Travel down the interventricular septum. 6. **Purkinje Fibers** — Spread depolarization rapidly throughout ventricular myocardium → produces **QRS complex**. #### 2.3.2 Pacemaker Hierarchy (Critical for KTU) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intrinsic Pacemaker Rates (Hierarchy of Automaticity):** > - **SA Node:** 60–100 bpm (primary) > - **AV Node/Junction:** 40–60 bpm (escape rhythm) > - **Ventricular (Purkinje/ventricular cells):** 20–40 bpm (idioventricular) If the SA node fails, the AV node takes over at a slower rate. If both fail, ventricles depolarize at their intrinsic rate (often incompatible with life). ### 2.4 Cardiac Action Potential — The Cellular Basis The ventricular myocyte action potential has **5 distinct phases (0–4)**, unlike the simple neuronal AP: | Phase | Event | Ion Movement | ECG Correlation | |-------|-------|--------------|-----------------| | **Phase 4** (Resting) | Stable resting membrane potential (~**−90 mV**) | K⁺ leak dominates | TP segment | | **Phase 0** (Depolarization) | Rapid upstroke | Fast Na⁺ influx (voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open) | QRS complex | | **Phase 1** (Initial repolarization) | Brief dip | Na⁺ inactivation + transient K⁺ efflux (Ito) | J point | | **Phase 2** (Plateau) | Sustained depolarization | Ca²⁺ influx (L-type Ca²⁺ channels) balances K⁺ efflux | ST segment | | **Phase 3** (Repolarization) | Return to resting | Massive K⁺ efflux (delayed rectifier channels) | T wave | | **Phase 4** (Return) | Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase restores gradients | Active transport | TP interval | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is the cardiac action potential so long (~250–300 ms)?** > The **Phase 2 plateau** due to L-type Ca²⁺ channels prevents tetanic contraction of cardiac muscle. This **long absolute refractory period** ensures the heart cannot be re-stimulated until it has relaxed — vital for proper filling and ejection. ### 2.5 The Electrocardiogram (ECG) — Surface Manifestation The ECG is the **summed vector of all cardiac action potentials** recorded from the body surface. It does **NOT** measure mechanical contraction. #### 2.5.1 ECG Wave Components | Wave/Segment | Origin | Duration | Amplitude | Significance | |--------------|--------|----------|-----------|--------------| | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | <120 ms | <2.5 mm | Atrial enlargement if tall/wide | | **PR interval** | Onset P → onset QRS | 120–200 ms | — | Conduction delay if prolonged | | **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 80–120 ms | 5–25 mm | Bundle branch block if wide | | **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (Phase 2) | 80–120 ms | Isoelectric | Ischemia/infarction if elevated/depressed | | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 160 ms | <5 mm (V6) | Hyperkalemia if peaked | | **QT interval** | Total ventricular depolarization + repolarization | 350–440 ms (rate-dependent) | — | Risk of arrhythmia if prolonged | #### 2.5.2 Einthoven's Triangle and Lead Relationships Willem Einthoven established the **standard 12-lead ECG** in 1903 (Nobel Prize 1924). The **bipolar limb leads (I, II, III)** form **Einthoven's triangle** (an equilateral triangle with the heart at center). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Einthoven's Law (KCL applied to the body):** > $$II = I + III$$ > This fundamental equation is testable on KTU exams. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cardiac Equations) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\text{CO} = \text{HR} \times \text{SV}$ | Cardiac Output | L/min | | 2 | $\text{SV} = \text{EDV} - \text{ESV}$ | Stroke Volume (ends-diastolic minus end-systolic volume) | mL | | 3 | $\text{EF} = \dfrac{\text{SV}}{\text{EDV}} \times 100\%$ | Ejection Fraction | % | | 4 | $\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \dfrac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP})$ | Mean Arterial Pressure | mmHg | | 5 | $\text{II} = \text{I} + \text{III}$ | Einthoven's Law | dimensionless | | 6 | $\text{Cardiac Cycle Time} = \dfrac{60}{\text{HR}}$ | Time per beat | seconds | | 7 | $\text{QTc} = \dfrac{\text{QT}}{\sqrt{\text{RR}}}$ | Bazett's corrected QT | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{PP interval} = \text{RR interval} = \dfrac{60}{\text{HR}}$ | Heart period | seconds | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - The ECG is the cornerstone signal processed in **PEICT633 — Biomedical Signal Processing**. Filtering the ECG (e.g., 50/60 Hz notch, baseline wander removal, QRS detection via Pan-Tompkins) requires understanding its physiological origin. > - Pacemakers (implantable devices) deliver precisely-timed electrical pulses based on detected P/R waves. > - Defibrillators exploit the **absolute refractory period** to reset chaotic electrical activity. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Einthoven's Law ($II = I + III$) This derivation demonstrates that the three Einthoven limb lead voltages are linearly dependent — a **Kirchhoff's Current Law** equivalent applied to the body as a volume conductor. #### Step 1: Define Lead Potentials - **Lead I** measures the potential difference between **Left Arm (LA)** and **Right Arm (RA)**: $$V_{\text{I}} = V_{\text{LA}} - V_{\text{RA}}$$ - **Lead II** measures the potential difference between **Left Leg (LL)** and **Right Arm (RA)**: $$V_{\text{II}} = V_{\text{LL}} - V_{\text{RA}}$$ - **Lead III** measures the potential difference between **Left Leg (LL)** and **Left Arm (LA)**: $$V_{\text{III}} = V_{\text{LL}} - V_{\text{LA}}$$ #### Step 2: Add Lead I and Lead III $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{I}} + V_{\text{III}} &= (V_{\text{LA}} - V_{\text{RA}}) + (V_{\text{LL}} - V_{\text{LA}}) \\ &= V_{\text{LA}} - V_{\text{RA}} + V_{\text{LL}} - V_{\text{LA}} \\ &= V_{\text{LL}} - V_{\text{RA}} \\ &= V_{\text{II}} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3: Conclusion $$ \boxed{V_{\text{II}} = V_{\text{I}} + V_{\text{III}}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Only **two of the three limb leads carry independent information**. The third is redundant. This is also why modern digital ECG systems only require 4 electrodes (RA, LA, LL, ground/reference) to compute all 12 leads. ### 3.2 Derivation: Cardiac Output (Fick Principle Simplified) #### Step 1: Define Variables - Let **CO** = Cardiac Output (mL/min) - Let **HR** = Heart Rate (beats/min) - Let **SV** = Stroke Volume (mL/beat) #### Step 2: Setup By definition, cardiac output is the volume of blood pumped by **one ventricle** in **one minute**. If each beat ejects SV mL, and there are HR beats per minute: $$ \text{CO} = \underbrace{\text{SV}}_{\text{mL/beat}} \times \underbrace{\text{HR}}_{\text{beats/min}} $$ #### Step 3: Calculate Example For a resting adult: HR = 72 bpm, SV = 70 mL $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CO} &= 70 \text{ mL/beat} \times 72 \text{ beats/min} \\ &= 5040 \text{ mL/min} \\ &\approx 5.04 \text{ L/min} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) #### Step 1: Recognize Cardiac Cycle The heart spends approximately **1/3 of its cycle in systole** and **2/3 in diastole** (at rest). Therefore, MAP is weighted more toward diastolic pressure. #### Step 2: Apply Time-Weighted Average $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MAP} &= \text{DBP} \times \dfrac{2}{3} + \text{SBP} \times \dfrac{1}{3} \\ &= \text{DBP} + \dfrac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP}) \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3: Numerical Example For BP = 120/80 mmHg: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MAP} &= 80 + \dfrac{1}{3}(120 - 80) \\ &= 80 + \dfrac{40}{3} \\ &= 80 + 13.33 \\ &\approx 93.3 \text{ mmHg} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Clinical Rule of Thumb:** MAP must exceed **~60 mmHg** to maintain adequate organ perfusion. Below this, risk of ischemia. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conduction Velocities & AP Durations | Tissue | Resting Potential (mV) | Conduction Velocity | AP Duration | Primary Ion for Phase 0 | |--------|------------------------|---------------------|-------------|------------------------| | SA Node | ~ −50 to −60 | ~0.05 m/s | ~150 ms | Ca²⁺ (slow upstroke) | | Atrial Muscle | ~ −80 to −90 | ~1.0 m/s | ~100–150 ms | Na⁺ (fast upstroke) | | AV Node | ~ −60 to −70 | ~0.05 m/s (slowest) | ~100–150 ms | Ca²⁺ | | His-Purkinje | ~ −90 | ~2–4 m/s (fastest) | ~150–200 ms | Na⁺ | | Ventricular Muscle | ~ −90 | ~0.3–1.0 m/s | ~250–300 ms (longest) | Na⁺ | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** AV node is **slowest** (causing PR delay); Purkinje is **fastest** (ensures synchronized ventricular contraction). The slow AV nodal conduction uses **Ca²⁺** channels, which is why **Ca²⁺ channel blockers** (verapamil, diltiazem) slow AV conduction — a frequent KTU pharmacology correlation. ### 3.5 ECG Vector Analysis (The Concept of Mean Electrical Axis) Each lead "sees" the cardiac vector from a different angle. The **mean electrical axis** of the heart in the frontal plane is normally between **−30° and +90°**. #### Step 3.5.1: Compute Axis from Lead I and aVF $$ \tan(\theta) = \dfrac{\text{Net deflection in aVF}}{\text{Net deflection in Lead I}} $$ #### Step 3.5.2: Example If Lead I = +10 mm, aVF = +8 mm (both positive → normal axis quadrant): $$ \begin{aligned} \tan(\theta) &= \dfrac{8}{10} = 0.8 \\ \theta &= \arctan(0.8) \\ \theta &\approx 38.7^\circ \end{aligned} $$ This is in the **normal axis range (0° to +90°)**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Heart Anatomy — Chamber and Flow Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SYSTEMIC["SYSTEMIC CIRCULATION (Body)"] BODY["Body Tissues
Deoxygenated Blood Returns"] end subgraph RIGHT_SIDE["RIGHT HEART (Pulmonary Pump)"] SVC["Superior Vena Cava"] IVC["Inferior Vena Cava"] RA["Right Atrium
Contains SA Node"] TV["Tricuspid Valve"] RV["Right Ventricle
Wall ~3-5 mm"] PV["Pulmonary Valve"] end subgraph LUNGS["LUNGS"] LUNG["Pulmonary Capillaries
Gas Exchange"] end subgraph LEFT_SIDE["LEFT HEART (Systemic Pump)"] PV2["Pulmonary Veins (x4)"] LA["Left Atrium"] MV["Mitral Valve"] LV["Left Ventricle
Wall ~8-15 mm"] AV["Aortic Valve"] AORTA["Aorta"] end SVC --> RA IVC --> RA RA -->|"Fills"| TV TV -->|"Opens during systole"| RV RV -->|"Pumps to lungs"| PV PV --> LUNG LUNG --> PV2 PV2 --> LA LA -->|"Fills"| MV MV -->|"Opens during systole"| LV LV -->|"Pumps to body"| AV AV --> AORTA AORTA --> BODY BODY --> SVC BODY --> IVC style RA fill:#ff9999 style RV fill:#ff6666 style LA fill:#99ccff style LV fill:#3399ff style LUNG fill:#ccffcc style BODY fill:#ffffcc ``` ### 4.2 Cardiac Conduction System — Electrical Pathway ```mermaid flowchart LR SANODE["SA Node
70 bpm
Primary Pacemaker"] ATRIA["Atrial Myocardium
Produces P Wave"] AVNODE["AV Node
100 ms Delay
40-60 bpm Backup"] HIS["Bundle of His
Only AV Conduction Path"] RBB["Right Bundle Branch"] LBB["Left Bundle Branch"] PURK_R["Purkinje Fibers RV
2-4 m/s"] PURK_L["Purkinje Fibers LV
2-4 m/s"] VENT["Ventricular Myocardium
Produces QRS and T Wave"] SANODE -->|"Internodal tracts"| ATRIA ATRIA -->|"Bachmann bundle"| AVNODE AVNODE --> HIS HIS --> RBB HIS --> LBB RBB --> PURK_R LBB --> PURK_L PURK_R --> VENT PURK_L --> VENT style SANODE fill:#ff4444,color:#fff style AVNODE fill:#ff8800,color:#fff style HIS fill:#ffcc00 style PURK_R fill:#00cc00 style PURK_L fill:#00cc00 style VENT fill:#cc66ff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Ventricular Action Potential Phases (Phases 0–4) vs. ECG Waves ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AP["VENTRICULAR ACTION POTENTIAL"] P4A["Phase 4
Resting -90 mV
K+ leak"] P0["Phase 0
Depolarization
Na+ INFLUX
upstroke to +30 mV"] P1["Phase 1
Initial Repolarization
Na+ off, K+ out"] P2["Phase 2
PLATEAU
Ca2+ IN equals K+ OUT
~200-250 ms"] P3["Phase 3
Repolarization
