K+ EFFLUX massive"] P4B["Phase 4
Return to -90 mV
Na/K ATPase"] end subgraph ECG["SURFACE ECG CORRELATION"] QRS["QRS Complex
Ventricular Depol"] ST["ST Segment
Plateau Phase 2"] TWAVE["T Wave
Repolarization"] TP["TP Segment
Phase 4 Resting"] end P4A --> P0 P0 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4B P0 -.-> QRS P2 -.-> ST P3 -.-> TWAVE P4B -.-> TP style P0 fill:#ff0000,color:#fff style P2 fill:#ffaa00 style P3 fill:#0066ff,color:#fff style QRS fill:#ff0000,color:#fff style ST fill:#ffaa00 style TWAVE fill:#0066ff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 ECG Waveform Anatomy (P-QRS-T with Intervals) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Baseline
TP interval"] --> B["P Wave
Atrial Depolarization
Duration < 120 ms"] B --> C["PR Segment
AV Nodal Delay"] C --> D["QRS Complex
Ventricular Depol
80-120 ms"] D --> E["ST Segment
Ventricular Plateau
Isoelectric"] E --> F["T Wave
Ventricular Repolar
Asymmetric"] F --> G["Baseline again"] style B fill:#ffff00 style D fill:#ff0000,color:#fff style F fill:#00cc00 ``` ### 4.5 Block Diagram: ECG Signal Acquisition Pipeline (Engineering View) ```mermaid flowchart LR HEART["Heart
Cardiac Action Potentials"] BODY["Body Volume Conductor
Tissue Impedance"] ELECTRODES["Surface Electrodes
Ag/AgCl"] PREAMP["Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain ~1000"] HPF["High-Pass Filter
0.05 Hz"] LPF["Low-Pass Filter
150 Hz"] NOTCH["Notch Filter
50 or 60 Hz"] ADC["Analog to Digital Converter
~500 Hz sampling"] DSP["Digital Signal Processor
QRS Detection
HR Variability"] DISPLAY["Display / Storage"] HEART --> BODY BODY --> ELECTRODES ELECTRODES --> PREAMP PREAMP --> HPF HPF --> LPF LPF --> NOTCH NOTCH --> ADC ADC --> DSP DSP --> DISPLAY style HEART fill:#ff6666 style PREAMP fill:#99ccff style DSP fill:#cc99ff style DISPLAY fill:#66ff66 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the following with respect to the cardiac conduction system:* *(a) SA Node (1 mark)* *(b) AV Node (1 mark)* *(c) Bundle of His (1 mark)* **Model Answer:** *(a) **SA Node (Sinoatrial Node):** A small, crescent-shaped cluster of specialized autorhythmic cardiac muscle cells located in the upper posterolateral wall of the right atrium, near the opening of the superior vena cava. It is the **primary pacemaker of the heart**, generating impulses at 60–100 bpm via spontaneous Phase 4 depolarization (the "funny current" If, mediated by Na⁺).* *(b) **AV Node (Atrioventricular Node):** A small mass of specialized conductive tissue located in the lower posterior part of the interatrial septum, near the opening of the coronary sinus. It provides the **only normal electrical connection between atria and ventricles** via the Bundle of His, and introduces a critical **~100 ms delay** to allow complete ventricular filling before systole. Intrinsic rate 40–60 bpm.* *(c) **Bundle of His (AV Bundle):** A band of specialized cardiac muscle fibers that originates from the AV node, pierces the fibrous skeleton of the heart (the only place where the AV groove is electrically crossed), and travels down the interventricular septum before dividing into the right and left bundle branches. It rapidly conducts impulses (~1.5–2 m/s) to the ventricles.* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the significance of the absolute refractory period in cardiac muscle. (3 marks)* **Model Answer:** The **absolute refractory period (ARP)** in cardiac muscle spans Phases 0, 1, 2, and most of Phase 3 of the action potential (total ~250 ms). Its significance is threefold: 1. **Prevents Tetanic Contraction:** Because Na⁺ channels remain inactivated, cardiac muscle **cannot be re-stimulated** until repolarization is nearly complete. This is unlike skeletal muscle and ensures the heart alternates strictly between contraction and relaxation. 2. **Allows Proper Filling & Ejection:** The ARP guarantees a relaxation phase, during which ventricles refill with blood — essential for generating adequate stroke volume. 3. **Protects Against Arrhythmias:** The long ARP prevents the establishment of **circus re-entry circuits** (a common cause of arrhythmias such as atrial flutter and ventricular tachycardia). The long ARP is enabled by the **Phase 2 plateau** (sustained Ca²⁺ influx) — a feature unique to cardiac myocytes. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** — answer ANY ONE from the two sub-choices (A or B). Each sub-choice has parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — July 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the anatomy of the human heart, labeling all four chambers, the four valves, and the great vessels. Explain the systemic and pulmonary circulations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the cardiac conduction system in detail, explaining the sequence of electrical activation, the intrinsic pacemaker rates at each level, and the ionic basis of the SA node action potential. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Heart Anatomy (4 marks):** The human heart is a four-chambered, double-pump muscular organ located in the mediastinum. The chambers are: - **Right Atrium (RA):** Receives deoxygenated blood via SVC, IVC, and coronary sinus. - **Right Ventricle (RV):** Pumps blood to the pulmonary artery; wall thickness 3–5 mm. - **Left Atrium (LA):** Receives oxygenated blood from four pulmonary veins. - **Left Ventricle (LV):** Pumps blood to the aorta; wall thickness 8–15 mm (thickest, due to high systemic pressure). **The Four Valves:** - **Tricuspid valve:** Right AV valve (RA → RV); has 3 cusps. - **Mitral (Bicuspid) valve:** Left AV valve (LA → LV); has 2 cusps. - **Pulmonary valve:** Semilunar; at RV outflow. - **Aortic valve:** Semilunar; at LV outflow. **Great Vessels:** Aorta, Pulmonary trunk, SVC, IVC, four Pulmonary veins. **2. Two Circulations (3 marks):** - **Pulmonary Circulation (Right Heart):** Deoxygenated blood from body → SVC/IVC → RA → RV → Pulmonary artery → Lungs (oxygenation) → Pulmonary veins → LA. This is a **low-pressure** circuit (~25/10 mmHg). - **Systemic Circulation (Left Heart):** Oxygenated blood from LA → LV → Aorta → Body tissues → SVC/IVC → RA. This is a **high-pressure** circuit (~120/80 mmHg). The two circuits operate in **series** but are functionally and anatomically separated by the pulmonary and systemic vascular beds. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **1. Conduction System (4 marks):** The cardiac conduction system consists of **specialized autorhythmic cells** that generate and propagate action potentials: **Step 1 — SA Node:** Located in the upper RA wall. Spontaneously depolarizes due to "funny current" (If) — Na⁺ influx through HCN channels during Phase 4. Threshold reached at ~−40 mV → fires action potential. **Rate: 70–80 bpm** (normal sinus rhythm). **Step 2 — Atrial Conduction:** Depolarization spreads across atria via internodal tracts and **Bachmann's bundle** (to LA). Causes atrial contraction — produces the **P wave** on ECG. Conduction velocity ~1 m/s. **Step 3 — AV Node:** Slow conduction (~0.05 m/s) creates a **~100 ms delay** (PR interval on ECG). This allows atrial contraction to complete ventricular filling. Uses **Ca²⁺**-dependent slow upstroke. **Step 4 — Bundle of His → Bundle Branches → Purkinje Fibers:** His bundle penetrates fibrous skeleton, divides into right and left bundle branches, which terminate in **Purkinje fibers**. Conduction velocity is highest here (~2–4 m/s), ensuring near-simultaneous ventricular depolarization → produces **QRS complex**. **2. Pacemaker Hierarchy (2 marks):** | Pacemaker | Rate (bpm) | |-----------|-----------| | SA Node | 60–100 (Primary) | | AV Junction | 40–60 (Junctional escape) | | Ventricular | 20–40 (Idioventricular — life-threatening if SA + AV both fail) | **3. Ionic Basis of SA Node AP (1 mark):** - **Phase 4 (slow depolarization):** "Funny current" (If) — Na⁺ influx via HCN channels; later T-type Ca²⁺ channels open. - **Phase 0 (depolarization):** L-type Ca²⁺ channels (NOT Na⁺) — slow upstroke characteristic of nodal cells. - **Phase 3 (repolarization):** K⁺ efflux via delayed rectifier channels. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A part b):** > Students often incorrectly state that the SA node uses Na⁺ for Phase 0 upstroke. **SA and AV nodes use L-type Ca²⁺ channels** (slow upstroke). Only **non-nodal cardiac tissue** (atrial/ventricular muscle, Purkinje) uses fast Na⁺ channels. This is a 2-mark deduction in valuation. --- ### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Draw and explain the five phases of the ventricular myocyte action potential. Correlate each phase with the corresponding ECG waveform. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define Stroke Volume and Cardiac Output. A patient has a heart rate of 75 bpm, end-diastolic volume of 120 mL, and end-systolic volume of 50 mL. Calculate the stroke volume, cardiac output, and ejection fraction. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Ventricular Action Potential — 5 Phases (5 marks):** | Phase | Event | Ion Movement | ECG Correlation | |-------|-------|--------------|-----------------| | **Phase 4** | Resting membrane potential at ~**−90 mV** | K⁺ leak (Inward Rectifier IK1) maintains RMP | **TP segment** (isoelectric) | | **Phase 0** | Rapid depolarization to ~+30 mV | Fast **Na⁺ influx** (voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open) | **QRS complex** (ventricular depolarization) | | **Phase 1** | Brief initial repolarization (~0 mV) | Na⁺ channel inactivation + transient K⁺ efflux (Ito) | **J point** (junction between QRS and ST) | | **Phase 2** | Plateau phase (~−10 to 0 mV, 200–250 ms) | **Ca²⁺ influx** (L-type Ca²⁺ channels) balanced by K⁺ efflux | **ST segment** (isoelectric — all ventricular cells in plateau) | | **Phase 3** | Repolarization to −90 mV | Massive **K⁺ efflux** (delayed rectifier IK, IK1) | **T wave** (asymmetric repolarization) | **Correlation with ECG (2 marks):** The surface ECG represents the **summation of all ventricular myocyte action potentials**. Because the **Phase 2 plateau** is synchronous across all ventricular cells, no net vector change occurs → **isoelectric ST segment**. The T wave is **asymmetric** (slower upstroke) because repolarization is heterogeneous — epicardium repolarizes *later* than endocardium (opposite of depolarization direction). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note:** The Phase 2 Ca²⁺ influx is what triggers **calcium-induced calcium release** from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, causing myocyte contraction. This is why **Ca²⁺ channel blockers** are negatively inotropic (reduce contractility). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **1. Definitions (2 marks):** - **Stroke Volume (SV):** The volume of blood ejected by **one ventricle in one beat**, measured in mL/beat. $$\text{SV} = \text{EDV} - \text{ESV}$$ - **Cardiac Output (CO):** The volume of blood pumped by **one ventricle in one minute**, measured in L/min. $$\text{CO} = \text{SV} \times \text{HR}$$ - **Ejection Fraction (EF):** The fraction of end-diastolic volume ejected per beat, expressed as a percentage. $$\text{EF} = \dfrac{\text{SV}}{\text{EDV}} \times 100\%$$ **2. Given Data:** - HR = 75 bpm - EDV = 120 mL - ESV = 50 mL **3. Step-by-Step Calculation (5 marks):** **Step 1: Stroke Volume** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SV} &= \text{EDV} - \text{ESV} \\ &= 120 \text{ mL} - 50 \text{ mL} \\ &= 70 \text{ mL/beat} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Cardiac Output** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CO} &= \text{SV} \times \text{HR} \\ &= 70 \text{ mL/beat} \times 75 \text{ beats/min} \\ &= 5250 \text{ mL/min} \\ &= 5.25 \text{ L/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Ejection Fraction** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EF} &= \dfrac{\text{SV}}{\text{EDV}} \times 100\% \\ &= \dfrac{70 \text{ mL}}{120 \text{ mL}} \times 100\% \\ &= 58.33\% \end{aligned} $$ **4. Clinical Interpretation:** - SV = 70 mL → **Normal** (range 60–100 mL) - CO = 5.25 L/min → **Normal** (range 4–8 L/min at rest) - EF = 58.33% → **Normal** (range 55–70%) This patient has **normal systolic function**. An EF < 40% indicates **systolic heart failure (HFrEF)**. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B part b):** > **Common Mistakes:** > 1. Forgetting to convert mL/min to L/min in CO (lose 1 mark). > 2. Confusing **stroke volume** with **cardiac output** — SV is per beat, CO is per minute. > 3. Not including **units** in final answer — KTU deducts marks even for correct numerical values without proper units. > 4. EF must be expressed as a **percentage**; some students write it as a decimal (0.58) — partial credit loss. --- ### 5.3 KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Concepts — Quick Recall Matrix | # | Concept | Key Number / Value | Frequency in KTU | |---|---------|---------------------|------------------| | 1 | Normal resting HR | **60–100 bpm** | Very High | | 2 | Normal cardiac output (rest) | **~5 L/min** | Very High | | 3 | Stroke volume (rest) | **~70 mL** | High | | 4 | Ejection fraction normal | **55–70%** | High | | 5 | LV wall thickness | **8–15 mm** | Medium | | 6 | SA node rate | **70–80 bpm** | Very High | | 7 | AV node delay | **~100 ms** | High | | 8 | Purkinje conduction velocity | **2–4 m/s** | High | | 9 | PR interval normal | **120–200 ms** | Very High | | 10 | QRS duration normal | **80–120 ms** | Very High | | 11 | QT interval normal | **350–440 ms** | High | | 12 | MAP formula | $DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP-DBP)$ | Very High | | 13 | Einthoven's law | $II = I + III$ | Very High | --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **🔖 Rapid-Fire Recall — Anatomy of the Heart and Electrical Activity** **Heart Anatomy (4 chambers + 4 valves):** - ☐ **RA** receives deoxygenated blood (SVC, IVC, coronary sinus) — contains SA node - ☐ **RV** pumps to lungs (pulmonary artery) — thin wall (~3–5 mm) - ☐ **LA** receives oxygenated blood (4 pulmonary veins) - ☐ **LV** pumps to body (aorta) — **thickest wall (~8–15 mm)** - ☐ Valves: **Tricuspid** (R-AV), **Mitral** (L-AV), **Pulmonary** + **Aortic** (Semilunar) **Conduction Pathway (Memorize the sequence):** - ☐ **SA Node** (Primary, 70 bpm) → Atria (P wave) → **AV Node** (100 ms delay, 40–60 bpm) → **Bundle of His** → Bundle Branches → **Purkinje Fibers** (2–4 m/s) → Ventricles (QRS complex) **Action Potential (5 phases) — Ventricular Myocyte:** - ☐ **Phase 4:** Resting −90 mV (K⁺ leak) - ☐ **Phase 0:** Na⁺ INFLUX (QRS) — **fast upstroke** - ☐ **Phase 1:** Na⁺ off, K⁺ out (J point) - ☐ **Phase 2:** **Ca²⁺ influx** (plateau — ST segment) — *prevents tetanus* - ☐ **Phase 3:** K⁺ efflux (T wave) **ECG Waveform — Origins:** - ☐ **P wave** → Atrial depolarization - ☐ **QRS** → Ventricular depolarization - ☐ **T wave** → Ventricular repolarization - ☐ **Atrial repolarization is HIDDEN** inside the QRS complex (low amplitude, buried) **Key Engineering Concepts:** - ☐ ECG is a **surface voltage measurement** (mV range) — does NOT measure contraction - ☐ ECG is the **vector sum** of all myocardial action potentials - ☐ Standard 12-lead ECG uses 10 electrodes, 12 views of the heart - ☐ **Einthoven's Triangle:** I + III = II - ☐ SA node uses **Ca²⁺** for Phase 0 (not Na⁺) — slow upstroke - ☐ Cardiac cells have **long absolute refractory period** (~250 ms) — prevents arrhythmia **Must-Memorize Formulas:** - ☐ $CO = HR \times SV$ - ☐ $SV = EDV - ESV$ - ☐ $EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\%$ - ☐ $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ - ☐ $II = I + III$ (Einthoven's Law) - ☐ $QTc = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ (Bazett's formula) **Pacemaker Hierarchy (Automatricity Fall-back):** - ☐ SA Node (60–100) → AV Node (40–60) → Ventricles (20–40) > [!WARNING] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In ESE questions asking for ECG correlations, **always draw the AP curve alongside the ECG** with arrows linking Phases 0/1/2/3 to QRS/J-point/ST/T. Examiners award full marks only when both physiological and signal-processing perspectives are shown. Also remember — the ECG signal is what PEICT633 is fundamentally about: filtering, denoising, feature extraction (QRS detection), and HRV analysis all depend on the **anatomical and electrical basis** covered in this module.

topics.title: Basics of ECG Waveform and Lead Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basics of ECG Waveform and Lead Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is an ECG? **Electrocardiography (ECG or EKG)** is the process of recording the **electrical activity of the heart** over a period of time using electrodes placed on the skin. The resulting trace is called an **electrocardiogram**, which is a quasi-periodic, low-frequency bioelectric signal. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** An ECG is the graphical representation of the time-varying electrical potentials generated by the **depolarization and repolarization** of the cardiac muscle (myocardium), captured non-invasively via surface electrodes and displayed as a voltage-versus-time waveform. | Parameter | Standard Value (Clinical) | |---|---| | Signal Amplitude | **0.5 mV to 5 mV** (calibrated to **10 mm = 1 mV**) | | Frequency Content | **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (diagnostic bandwidth) | | Paper/Display Speed | **25 mm/sec** (1 small box = 0.04 s, 1 large box = 0.20 s) | | Sampling Rate (digital) | ≥ **500 Hz** (per AHA recommendations) | ### 1.2 The Cardiac Electrophysiological Origin The heart's natural pacemaker, the **Sino-Atrial (SA) node**, located in the right atrium, initiates each heartbeat. The wave of depolarization spreads through: $$\text{SA Node} \rightarrow \text{Atria} \rightarrow \text{AV Node} \rightarrow \text{Bundle of His} \rightarrow \text{Bundle Branches} \rightarrow \text{Purkinje Fibers} \rightarrow \text{Ventricles}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ECG does **NOT** record mechanical contraction directly. It records the **summed electrical vectors** of all myocardial cells firing in synchrony. It is a *superposition* of action potentials. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Heart as a Dipole in a Bucket of Saline Imagine the heart as a **battery (dipole)** suspended in a conductive tank of saline (the human torso). As the depolarization wave sweeps across the myocardium, the instantaneous **cardiac vector** (a magnitude-and-direction arrow) changes orientation and length. Surface electrodes, like voltmeters placed around the tank, "see" projections of this rotating dipole. - The **arrow (vector)** rotates as the wave of depolarization moves (atria → ventricles). - Each **lead** is just a *voltmeter* viewing this arrow from a different angle. - The **ECG tracing** is the *projection* of the arrow onto that lead's axis, plotted versus time. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Just as a clock's hands can be read differently from the front, side, or back, the same cardiac event looks different in Lead I, Lead II, or V1. That is the entire reason for the **multi-lead system**. ### 1.4 The Genesis of the ECG Waveform — Wave-by-Wave The standard ECG cycle (one cardiac cycle) consists of the following key deflections and intervals: | Deflection / Interval | Physiological Event | Typical Duration | Amplitude | |---|---|---|---| | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | **< 120 ms** (0.12 s) | **< 2.5 mm** (0.25 mV) | | **PR segment** | Conduction delay at AV node | ~50 ms | Isoelectric | | **PR interval** | Onset of P to onset of QRS | **120 – 200 ms** | — | | **Q wave** | Initial septal depolarization (negative) | < 30 ms | < 25 % of R | | **R wave** | Main ventricular depolarization (positive) | — | Dominant | | **S wave** | Late ventricular depolarization (negative) | — | — | | **QRS complex** | Total ventricular depolarization | **< 120 ms** | — | | **ST segment** | End of depolarization to start of repolarization | ~80–120 ms | Isoelectric | | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | ~160 ms | Asymmetric, rounded | | **U wave** | Purkinje repolarization (or mech-electrical feedback) | — | Small, low-frequency | | **QT interval** | Total ventricular activity (dep + rep) | **350 – 440 ms** (rate dependent) | — | | **TP segment** | Resting (diastolic) baseline | Variable | Truly zero potential | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of Each Waveform Component #### 2.1.1 The P Wave - Represents **atrial depolarization**, beginning in the SA node and spreading leftward and inferiorly through both atria. - Because the depolarization vector points roughly **+60°** in the frontal plane, P waves are normally **upright in Lead II** and **inverted in aVR**. - Duration > 120 ms or amplitude > 2.5 mm suggests **atrial enlargement** (P mitrale, P pulmonale). #### 2.1.2 The PR Interval - From the **beginning of the P wave to the beginning of the QRS complex**. - Reflects the time taken for the impulse to travel from atria → AV node → bundle of His → ventricles. - The PR segment (after P, before QRS) is **isoelectric** because the AV node delay produces no net movement of charge. #### 2.1.3 The QRS Complex - Represents **ventricular depolarization**, the largest deflection in the ECG. - Components: * **Q wave:** first negative deflection *before* any R wave. * **R wave:** any positive deflection. * **S wave:** negative deflection *after* an R wave. - Normally **< 120 ms** in adults. A wide QRS indicates bundle branch block or ventricular origin. #### 2.1.4 The ST Segment - From the **end of the S wave (J point) to the beginning of the T wave**. - Represents the **plateau phase (Phase 2)** of the ventricular action potential when all myocytes are depolarized — net vector is near zero, so the trace is isoelectric. - ST elevation/depression is the classic hallmark of **myocardial ischemia/infarction**. #### 2.1.5 The T Wave - Represents **ventricular repolarization**. - Asymmetric: **slow upstroke, faster downstroke** (because repolarization is not synchronous). - Normally upright in I, II, V3–V6; inverted in aVR. #### 2.1.6 The U Wave - Small, low-frequency deflection following the T wave. - Origin debated: **Purkinje fiber repolarization** or **mechano-electrical feedback** from ventricular wall stretch. - Prominent U wave → hypokalemia. #### 2.1.7 The QT Interval - **Total time of ventricular depolarization + repolarization.** - Heart-rate dependent. Corrected using **Bazett's formula**: $$QT_c = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$$ where RR is the interval between successive R waves in seconds. Normal $QT_c$ ≤ **440 ms** (male) / 460 ms (female). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **must-memorize formula bank** for KTU exams on this topic. | # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Einthoven's Law (Vector sum) | $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ | Voltages are signed | | 2 | Heart Rate (large-box method) | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{300}{N}$ | $N$ = number of large boxes between R–R | | 3 | Heart Rate (small-box method) | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{1500}{n}$ | $n$ = number of small boxes between R–R | | 4 | Heart Rate (6-sec method) | $\text{HR} = (\text{R waves in 6 s}) \times 10$ | Used in irregular rhythms | | 5 | Time per small box | $t_{small} = 25 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.04 \text{ s} = 0.04 \text{ s}$ | Calibration constant | | 6 | Time per large box | $t_{large} = 5 \times t_{small} = 0.20 \text{ s}$ | 1 large = 5 small | | 7 | Voltage calibration | $1 \text{ mV} = 10 \text{ mm}$ | Vertical gain | | 8 | Corrected QT (Bazett) | $QT_c = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | RR in seconds | | 9 | Mean Electrical Axis (frontal) | $\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{\text{Net QRS in aVF}}{\text{Net QRS in Lead I}}$ | Net = R − (Q + S) | | 10 | Cardiac Vector Magnitude | $\vec{M} = \sqrt{M_x^2 + M_y^2}$ | From 2 orthogonal leads | | 11 | Lead-augmented gain (Goldberger) | $V_{aV_x} = V_x - \dfrac{V_y + V_z}{2}$ | For x = R, L, F | | 12 | Wilson Central Terminal (WCT) | $V_{WCT} = \dfrac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$ | Reference for unipolar leads | | 13 | Sampling theorem for ECG | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{max} \approx 150$ Hz ⇒ $f_s \geq 500$ Hz | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Clinical diagnostics:** Arrhythmia detection, myocardial infarction localization, electrolyte imbalance assessment. - **Wearable/ambulatory monitoring:** Holter monitors, smartwatches — driven by the same lead-theory principles, often with **1-lead (Lead I or II)** derivative configurations. - **Signal processing design:** QRS detection (Pan-Tompkins, wavelet-based), baseline wander removal (0.5 Hz HPF), 50/60 Hz notch filtering for powerline interference. - **Machine learning / AI:** Convolutional networks trained on 12-lead ECGs to detect > 15 cardiac conditions. ### 2.4 Why Multi-Lead? — The Vectorial Viewpoint A single ECG lead gives a **1-D projection** of a **2-D (or 3-D) cardiac vector**. With $N$ leads at known angles, we can reconstruct the vector's orientation. The 12-lead system provides redundancy for: 1. **Localization of infarction** (anterior, inferior, lateral, septal, posterior). 2. **Axis determination** (hemiblocks, fascicular blocks). 3. **Chamber enlargement detection.** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Any derivation question on leads assumes the heart behaves as a **time-varying equivalent dipole** at the center of the torso (the *Einthoven image surface* model). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Einthoven's Law $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ **Einthoven's postulate** assumes the body is a homogeneous, linear, isotropic volume conductor and the heart is a fixed-location time-varying dipole at its center. Let the three limb electrode potentials be: - $V_{RA}$ = potential at Right Arm - $V_{LA}$ = potential at Left Arm - $V_{LL}$ = potential at Left Leg By KVL/KCL around the triangle, the lead voltages are defined as: $$ \begin{aligned} V_I &= V_{LA} - V_{RA} \\ V_{II} &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\ V_{III} &= V_{LL} - V_{LA} \end{aligned} $$ Now compute $V_I + V_{III}$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_I + V_{III} &= (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\ &= V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA} \\ &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\ &= V_{II} \end{aligned} $$ **Therefore:** $\boxed{V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}}$ (Einthoven's Law) ∎ This is a **Kirchhoff-conservation** result and holds for any instantaneous ECG sample. It is the single most-tested KTU relation on this topic. ### 3.2 Derivation: Wilson Central Terminal (WCT) A **unipolar (V) lead** measures the potential at an exploring electrode relative to a *reference* that should be electrically neutral (zero potential). Wilson defined this reference by tying the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) together through **equal high-value resistors** (typically 5 kΩ each) to suppress loading. The potential at the junction is the average of the three limb potentials: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{WCT} &= \frac{1}{3} \left( V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL} \right) \end{aligned} $$ This is the **reference terminal** for the **unipolar precordial (chest) leads** $V_1$ through $V_6$. ### 3.3 Derivation: Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger) Goldberger observed that the unipolar limb lead $V_R$ (Right arm) had a small amplitude. He proposed **augmented** versions by **disconnecting the exploring limb from the WCT** so the reference becomes the *average of the other two* limbs. For example, for **aVR**, the reference is the average of LA and LL: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{aVR} &= V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Using the WCT relation $V_{WCT} = \frac{1}{3}(V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL})$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{aVR} &= V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2} \\ &= \frac{2V_{RA} - V_{LA} - V_{LL}}{2} \\ &= \frac{3}{2}\left( V_{RA} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3} \right) \\ &= \frac{3}{2} (V_{RA} - V_{WCT}) \end{aligned} $$ Thus the **augmentation factor is 3/2 (1.5×)** — hence the name *augmented*. By cyclic permutation: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{aVR} &= V_{RA} - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{LA} + V_{LL}) \\ V_{aVL} &= V_{LA} - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{RA} + V_{LL}) \\ V_{aVF} &= V_{LL} - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{RA} + V_{LA}) \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Derivation: Mean Electrical Axis (Frontal Plane) The **mean electrical axis** $\theta$ is the average direction of the QRS vector over its duration, projected onto the **frontal plane**. We use the **net QRS deflection**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net}_I &= R_I - (Q_I + S_I) \\ \text{Net}_{aVF} &= R_{aVF} - (Q_{aVF} + S_{aVF}) \end{aligned} $$ Because Lead I lies on the **0° (horizontal) axis** and aVF lies on the **+90° axis**: $$ \begin{aligned} \tan(\theta) &= \frac{\text{Net}_{aVF}}{\text{Net}_I} \\ \theta &= \arctan\left( \frac{\text{Net}_{aVF}}{\text{Net}_I} \right) \end{aligned} $$ > **Sign-convention correction:** If $\text{Net}_I < 0$, add 180° to bring the angle into the correct quadrant (use `atan2` instead of `atan` for software). | Axis Range | Interpretation | |---|---| | **−30° to +90°** | **Normal axis** | | **−30° to −90°** | **Left Axis Deviation (LAD)** | | **+90° to +180°** | **Right Axis Deviation (RAD)** | | **−90° to +180°** | **Extreme / Indeterminate** | ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: The 12-Lead ECG System | Lead Group | Type | # Leads | Reference | Polarity / Angle | Clinical Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Standard / Bipolar limb** (Einthoven) | Bipolar | 3 (I, II, III) | One limb vs another | I = 0°, II = +60°, III = +120° | Rhythm, axis, inferior MI | | **Augmented unipolar limb** (Goldberger) | Unipolar-aug | 3 (aVR, aVL, aVF) | Avg of other 2 limbs | aVR = −150°, aVL = −30°, aVF = +90° | Axis, lateral / high-lateral MI | | **Precordial / Chest** (Wilson) | Unipolar | 6 (V1–V6) | Wilson CT | V1=4th ICS RSB, V2=4th ICS LSB, V3 mid V2–V4, V4=5th ICS MCL, V5 ant axillary, V6 mid-axillary | Anterior / septal / lateral MI, RV strain, Brugada | ### 3.6 Calibration & Quantitative Analysis — Heart Rate **Method 1 — Large-box method** (for regular rhythms): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Period } T &= N \times 0.20 \text{ s} \\ \text{HR} &= \frac{60}{T} = \frac{60}{0.20 \times N} = \frac{300}{N} \text{ bpm} \end{aligned} $$ **Method 2 — Small-box method** (more precision): $$ \begin{aligned} T &= n \times 0.04 \text{ s} \\ \text{HR} &= \frac{60}{0.04 \times n} = \frac{1500}{n} \text{ bpm} \end{aligned} $$ **Method 3 — Six-second method** (for irregular rhythms): count R waves in 6 seconds of strip and multiply by 10. ### 3.7 Worked Example (KTU-Style) > An ECG strip has the following net QRS deflections: Lead I = **+8 mm**, aVF = **+6 mm**. Standard calibration: 10 mm/mV. Find the **mean electrical axis** and the **approximate cardiac vector magnitude**. **Step 1 — Convert to mV:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net}_I &= +8 \text{ mm} \times \frac{1 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mm}} = +0.8 \text{ mV} \\ \text{Net}_{aVF} &= +6 \text{ mm} \times \frac{1 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mm}} = +0.6 \text{ mV} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute axis angle:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arctan\left( \frac{\text{Net}_{aVF}}{\text{Net}_I} \right) = \arctan\left( \frac{0.6}{0.8} \right) = \arctan(0.75) \\ &= 36.87^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute vector magnitude:** $$ \begin{aligned} |\vec{M}| &= \sqrt{0.8^2 + 0.6^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 0.36} = \sqrt{1.00} = 1.00 \text{ mV} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Axis ≈ **+37°** (Normal); $|\vec{M}|$ = **1.0 mV**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — ECG Acquisition Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Heart
Dipole Source] --> B[Body Tissue
Volume Conductor] B --> C1[Electrode RA] B --> C2[Electrode LA] B --> C3[Electrode LL] B --> C4[Electrodes V1 to V6] C1 --> D[Lead Network
Einthoven / Goldberger / Wilson] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D D --> E[Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain 100 to 1000] E --> F[Bandpass Filter
0.05 to 150 Hz] F --> G[Notch Filter
50 / 60 Hz] G --> H[ADC
fs >= 500 Hz, 12 to 16 bit] H --> I[Digital Signal Processing
Baseline / QRS detection] I --> J[Display / Storage / Diagnosis] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — ECG Waveform Components ```mermaid flowchart LR A[One Cardiac Cycle] --> B[P Wave] A --> C[PR Segment] A --> D[QRS Complex] A --> E[ST Segment] A --> F[T Wave] A --> G[U Wave] A --> H[TP Segment] B --> B1[Atrial Depol] C --> C1[AV Node Delay] D --> D1[Vent Depol] E --> E1[Plateau Phase] F --> F1[Vent Repol] G --> G1[Purkinje Repol] H --> H1[Diastolic Baseline] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Lead Network Architecture (12-Lead) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Limb3[Bipolar Limb Leads - Einthoven] L1[Lead I: LA minus RA] L2[Lead II: LL minus RA] L3[Lead III: LL minus LA] end subgraph LimbA[Augmented Limb Leads - Goldberger] LA1[aVR: RA minus avg of LA LL] LA2[aVL: LA minus avg of RA LL] LA3[aVF: LL minus avg of RA LA] end subgraph Precord[Precordial Leads - Wilson] P1[V1 to V6: Exploring minus WCT] end RA[Right Arm] --> L1 LA[Left Arm] --> L1 RA --> L2 LL[Left Leg] --> L2 LA --> L3 LL --> L3 RA --> LA1 LA --> LA1 LL --> LA1 RA --> LA2 LA --> LA2 LL --> LA2 RA --> LA3 LA --> LA3 LL --> LA3 RA --> P1 LA --> P1 LL --> P1 C1[Chest V1] --> P1 C2[Chest V2] --> P1 C3[Chest V3] --> P1 C4[Chest V4] --> P1 C5[Chest V5] --> P1 C6[Chest V6] --> P1 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic — Einthoven's Triangle with Lead Vectors ```mermaid flowchart LR A[RA] --- B[LA] B --- C[LL] A --- C A -->|Lead I axis 0 deg| A1[Direction: Right to Left] B -->|Lead II axis +60 deg| B1[Direction: RA to LL] C -->|Lead III axis +120 deg| C1[Direction: LA to LL] A -.->|aVR -150 deg| A2 B -.->|aVL -30 deg| B2 C -.->|aVF +90 deg| C2 ``` ### 4.5 Block Diagram — QRS Detection Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw ECG] --> B[Bandpass 5 to 15 Hz] B --> C[Derivative Operator] C --> D[Squaring Function] D --> E[Moving Window Integration] E --> F[Adaptive Thresholding] F --> G[R Peak Detected] G --> H[RR Interval / HR Calculation] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the PR interval. What is its clinical significance and normal duration? **Model Answer:** The **PR interval** is the time interval from the **beginning of the P wave to the beginning of the QRS complex** on the ECG. It represents the time taken for the electrical impulse to travel from the atria, through the AV node, the bundle of His, bundle branches, and the Purkinje fibers to the ventricular myocardium. - **Normal duration:** **120 ms to 200 ms** (i.e., 3 to 5 small boxes at 25 mm/s). - **Clinical significance:** A PR interval > 200 ms indicates **first-degree AV block**; a PR < 120 ms suggests pre-excitation (e.g., WPW syndrome) or junctional rhythm. --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the **Wilson Central Terminal (WCT)**? Why is it used in ECG lead systems? **Model Answer:** The **Wilson Central Terminal (WCT)** is a reference electrode formed by connecting the three limb electrodes (Right Arm, Left Arm, Left Leg) through **three equal high-value resistors (typically 5 kΩ)** to a common node. Its potential is the average of the three limb potentials: $$V_{WCT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$$ It is used as the **electrically neutral reference** for the **unipolar precordial (chest) leads V1 to V6**, allowing them to record the *true* cardiac potential at the exploring chest electrode rather than relative to another limb. The high-value resistors prevent any limb from being short-circuited and preserve the bipolar limb leads (I, II, III) simultaneously. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper Style] **(a)** With neat diagrams and necessary derivations, explain the **Einthoven's Triangle and Einthoven's Law** in detail. Show how the three standard limb leads are obtained. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **augmented unipolar limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF)**. Derive the augmentation factor of **1.5** for the Goldberger leads. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Einthoven's Triangle and Law:** Einthoven (1913) proposed that the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) form the vertices of an **equilateral triangle** inscribed in the body (a torso approximation). The heart sits at the centroid and acts as a **time-varying electrical dipole** with a vector $\vec{M}$. **Lead definitions** (potential differences): $$ \begin{aligned} V_I &= V_{LA} - V_{RA} \\ V_{II} &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\ V_{III} &= V_{LL} - V_{LA} \end{aligned} $$ **Derivation of Einthoven's Law:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_I + V_{III} &= (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\ &= V_{LL} - V_{RA} = V_{II} \end{aligned} $$ Hence, $\boxed{V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}}$. This is a **KCL/KVL identity** that holds for *every* instantaneous sample, making it a self-consistency check on any ECG machine. **Einthoven's Triangle properties:** - The triangle is *equilateral* in the **abstract** (Einthoven) plane, but not in the actual body. - The lead axes point along the sides: Lead I = 0°, Lead II = +60°, Lead III = +120° (in the frontal plane). - The cardiac vector $\vec{M}$ projects onto each lead axis to give the recorded voltage. **(b) Augmented Unipolar Leads — Goldberger's Derivation:** The standard unipolar limb lead $V_R$ measures $V_{RA}$ relative to the WCT. Its amplitude is small. **Goldberger** proposed to **augment** it by 50 % by disconnecting the exploring limb from the WCT. For aVR: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{aVR} &= V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Using $V_{WCT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{aVR} &= V_{RA} - \frac{1}{2}(V_{LA} + V_{LL}) \\ &= \frac{2V_{RA} - V_{LA} - V_{LL}}{2} \\ &= \frac{3}{2}\left[ V_{RA} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3} \right] \\ &= \frac{3}{2} (V_{RA} - V_{WCT}) = 1.5 \, V_R \end{aligned} $$ **Augmentation factor = 1.5 (50 % larger amplitude).** The same derivation cyclically permutes for $aVL$ and $aVF$. The lead axes are oriented at **−150° (aVR)**, **−30° (aVL)**, and **+90° (aVF)** in the frontal plane. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Draw and label a **typical ECG waveform** for one cardiac cycle. Identify and describe the significance of the **P wave, QRS complex, T wave, and U wave**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ECG strip recorded at 25 mm/s paper speed shows **net QRS deflections** of **+10 mm in Lead I** and **−4 mm in Lead aVF**. Compute the **mean electrical axis** of the heart. Standard calibration is **10 mm/mV**. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) ECG Waveform Identification:** A normal ECG (one cycle) contains: **P wave, PR segment, PR interval, QRS complex, ST segment, T wave, U wave, TP segment, QT interval**. | Component | Description | Significance | |---|---|---| | **P wave** | Small, smooth, rounded, upright in II | Atrial depolarization originating in SA node. Absent → atrial fibrillation; inverted → junctional rhythm. | | **QRS complex** | Tall, sharp, three deflections (Q, R, S); largest feature | Ventricular depolarization. Wide QRS (> 120 ms) → bundle branch block. Pathological Q → prior MI. | | **T wave** | Asymmetric, slower upstroke, faster downstroke | Ventricular repolarization. Inverted T → ischemia. Tall peaked T → hyperkalemia. | | **U wave** | Small, low-frequency, follows T | Purkinje repolarization (or mechano-electrical feedback). Prominent U → hypokalemia. | **(b) Mean Electrical Axis Calculation:** **Step 1 — Convert net deflections to mV:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net}_I &= +10 \text{ mm} \div 10 \text{ mm/mV} = +1.0 \text{ mV} \\ \text{Net}_{aVF} &= -4 \text{ mm} \div 10 \text{ mm/mV} = -0.4 \text{ mV} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply axis formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arctan\left( \frac{\text{Net}_{aVF}}{\text{Net}_I} \right) \\ &= \arctan\left( \frac{-0.4}{+1.0} \right) \\ &= \arctan(-0.4) \\ &= -21.8^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Quadrant check:** $\text{Net}_I > 0$ and $\text{Net}_{aVF} < 0$ → **quadrant IV** (lower right of frontal plane). Since $\arctan$ already gives a value in $(-90°, 0°)$ and $\text{Net}_I > 0$, the angle is $\boxed{\theta \approx -22^{\circ}}$. **Step 4 — Interpretation:** $-22^{\circ}$ is within the **normal axis range (−30° to +90°)** → **Normal axis**. No axis deviation; LAD/RAD can be ruled out. **Vector magnitude:** $$ \begin{aligned} |\vec{M}| &= \sqrt{1.0^2 + (-0.4)^2} = \sqrt{1.16} \approx 1.077 \text{ mV} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls (where students lose marks):** > 1. **Confusing $V_{III}$ with $V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$** — the polarity is critical; check the KTU board's lead definitions. > 2. **Forgetting the augmentation factor** (1.5) when deriving Goldberger leads. Many students only state $V_{aVR} = V_{RA} - V_{WCT}$ instead of the augmented form. > 3. **Misapplying Bazett's formula** — the $RR$ must be in **seconds**, not milliseconds or boxes. > 4. **Quadrant error in axis determination** — always use `atan2(N_aVF, N_I)` (i.e., 4-quadrant inverse tangent), not 2-quadrant `atan`. > 5. **Confusing QRS duration (≤ 120 ms)** with QT interval. QRS is *only* depolarization; QT is *depolarization + repolarization*. > 6. **Wrong calibration:** $1 \text{ mV} = 10 \text{ mm}$ vertically; $1 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ mm}$ (5 large boxes) horizontally. Mixing these up wrecks numerical answers. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — ECG Basics & Lead Systems):** > > - **ECG** records electrical (not mechanical) activity of the heart as a function of time. > - **Standard calibration:** 10 mm/mV vertical, 25 mm/s horizontal. > - **Waves:** **P** (atria), **QRS** (ventricles), **T** (ventricular repol), **U** (Purkinje repol). > - **Intervals:** PR = 120–200 ms, QRS < 120 ms, QT = 350–440 ms, QTc ≤ 440 ms. > - **Einthoven's Law:** $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ — must be derivable from $V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$. > - **Lead system = 12 leads:** 3 bipolar (I, II, III) + 3 augmented (aVR, aVL, aVF) + 6 precordial (V1–V6). > - **Bipolar leads (Einthoven):** measure between two limb electrodes. > - **Augmented unipolar limb leads (Goldberger):** $\times 1.5$ augmentation factor, reference = average of the other two limbs. > - **Precordial leads (Wilson):** reference = Wilson Central Terminal $V_{WCT} = (V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL})/3$. > - **Mean Electrical Axis:** $\theta = \arctan(\text{Net}_{aVF}/\text{Net}_I)$ using `atan2`; normal range **−30° to +90°**. > - **Heart rate (regular):** $\text{HR} = 300 / N$ (large boxes between R–R). > - **Frequency content of ECG:** 0.05–150 Hz; sample at $f_s \geq 500$ Hz. > - **Cardiac dipole model:** heart = time-varying vector at the centroid of Einthoven's triangle. > - **Einthoven axes (frontal):** I = 0°, II = +60°, III = +120°, aVR = −150°, aVL = −30°, aVF = +90°.

topics.title: Methods and Devices for ECG Signal Acquisition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods and Devices for ECG Signal Acquisition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrocardiogram (ECG) Signal Acquisition** is the systematic process of detecting, transducing, conditioning, and digitizing the bioelectric potentials generated by the depolarization and repolarization of cardiac muscle tissue, for the purpose of clinical diagnosis and physiological monitoring.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 3), ECG acquisition encompasses the **complete front-end signal chain**: from the biopotential electrode interfacing with the human body, through analog signal conditioning circuitry, to the analog-to-digital conversion stage. The standard diagnostic ECG bandwidth is **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**, with a dynamic range of approximately **±5 mV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The cardiac action potential propagates through specialized conduction tissue (SA node → AV node → Bundle of His → Purkinje fibers), generating surface potentials measurable at the skin. ECG acquisition is fundamentally a *surface biopotential measurement* problem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart as a Battery's Echo"

Imagine the heart as a **weak battery** floating inside a tub of conductive saltwater (the body). Each heartbeat creates a momentary electrical dipole (a tiny vector with magnitude and direction). The ECG system acts like placing **two voltage probes (electrodes)** at different points on the "tub's" surface and measuring the *voltage difference* between them.

Just as the voltage reading depends on **where you place the probes and the orientation of the battery inside**, the ECG waveform shape depends entirely on the **electrode placement** (lead configuration). This is why a standard **12-lead system** uses 10 electrodes in fixed anatomical positions to capture the heart's electrical activity from 12 different "angles."

### 1.3 Key Physiological & Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| ECG Signal Amplitude (R-peak) | **1.0 mV – 2.5 mV** |
| Diagnostic Bandwidth | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** |
| Skin–Electrode Impedance | **< 5 kΩ** (after gel preparation) |
| Half-Cell Potential (Ag/AgCl) | **+0.223 V** (vs. SHE) |
| Mains Interference | **50 Hz** (India) / **60 Hz** (US) |
| ADC Resolution | **≥ 12 bits** (typical 16-bit) |
| Patient Leakage Current Limit | **10 µA** (Type CF, Class I) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** Willem Einthoven, the father of electrocardiography, introduced the famous triangle (Einthoven's Triangle) in 1903 and won the Nobel Prize in 1924 for this work. The modern 12-lead ECG evolved from his original 3-lead string galvanometer system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ECG Acquisition Signal Chain — Operational Breakdown

The ECG acquisition system follows a strict cascaded architecture. Each block serves a critical purpose, and skipping any stage compromises diagnostic quality.

**Stage 1 — Electrode Interface**
- Converts ionic current in tissue → electronic current in wire
- Establishes electrochemical half-cell potential
- Sources of error: polarization, motion artifact, gel drying

**Stage 2 — Patient Cable & Safety Network**
- Carries microvolt-level signals in a hostile EM environment
- Includes current-limiting resistors for defibrillation protection
- Implements *isolation barrier* (galvanic/optical) for patient safety

**Stage 3 — Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)**
- Provides high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR ≥ 100 dB)
- Differential gain typically **G = 10 to 1000**
- High input impedance (> 10 GΩ) prevents electrode loading

**Stage 4 — Analog Filtering**
- High-pass filter (HPF) at **0.05 Hz** → removes baseline wander (DC offset, respiration)
- Low-pass filter (LPF) at **150 Hz** → removes EMG interference and HF noise
- 50 Hz notch filter → suppresses powerline interference

**Stage 5 — Right Leg Drive (RLD) Circuit**
- Active feedback loop that *inverts* and *re-injects* the common-mode voltage into the patient's right leg
- Reduces common-mode voltage to < 1 V (from several volts passively)

**Stage 6 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)**
- Sampling rate (per Nyquist): **f_s ≥ 500 Hz** (practical: **360 Hz – 1000 Hz**)
- Resolution: **12 bits minimum** (4096 levels over 10 mV range → ~2.4 µV/LSB)
- Anti-aliasing analog filter placed *before* ADC

### 2.2 The Electrode-Skin Interface — Three-Layer Model

The electrode–electrolyte–skin interface is electrically modeled as a **distributed RC network** with a half-cell DC source.

$$V_{\text{measured}} = V_{\text{hc}} + V_{\text{polarization}} + V_{\text{motion}} + i(t) \cdot Z_{\text{interface}}$$

The half-cell potential arises from the redox reaction at the metal-electrolyte junction, governed by the **Nernst Equation**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU exams.**

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{\text{ox}}}{a_{\text{red}}} \right)$$ | Nernst Equation (Half-cell potential) | Volts (V); $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96485$ C/mol |
| $$V_{II} = V_{F} - V_{R}$$ | Einthoven's Law (Lead II = Lead I + Lead III) | Volts; algebraic sum |
| $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} \right)$$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | Decibels (dB); $A_d$ = differential gain, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain |
| $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}} \right)$$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Decibels (dB) |
| $$Z_{\text{input}} \gg Z_{\text{source}}$$ | Loading Rule (10× rule) | Impedance mismatch |
| $$f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$$ | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | Hz; for ECG: $\geq 300$ Hz |
| $$Q = \frac{V_{\text{LSB}}}{V_{\text{FSR}}} = \frac{1}{2^{N}}$$ | Quantization Step | $N$ = ADC bits; $V_{\text{FSR}}$ = full-scale range |
| $$V_{\text{half-cell}}(\text{Ag/AgCl}) = +0.223 \text{ V}$$ | Reference value at body temperature (37 °C) | Volts (V) vs. SHE |
| $$i_{\text{leak}} \leq 10 \text{ µA}$$ | Patient safety leakage limit (Type CF) | Microamperes (µA) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Diagnosis**: Detection of arrhythmias (AF, VT, MI), ischemia, conduction blocks
- **Holter Monitoring**: 24-hour ambulatory ECG acquisition with on-board flash storage
- **Wearable/IoT Devices**: Smart watches with single-lead ECG (Apple Watch, AliveCor KardiaMobile)
- **Defibrillators**: ECG acquisition integrated with shock delivery circuitry
- **Telemedicine**: Real-time streaming of digitized ECG over networks for remote diagnosis

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Half-Cell Potential via Nernst Equation (Ag/AgCl Electrode)

**Problem Context:** The silver/silver-chloride (Ag/AgCl) electrode is the gold standard for ECG. Derive its half-cell potential at body temperature (37 °C = 310.15 K) for the redox reaction:

$$\text{AgCl}_{(s)} + e^{-} \leftrightarrow \text{Ag}_{(s)} + \text{Cl}^{-}_{(aq)}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the standard redox potential.**
The standard potential for this half-reaction at 25 °C is:
$$E^{0}_{\text{AgCl/Ag}} = +0.2223 \text{ V vs. SHE}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nernst Equation in general form.**
For the reaction $\text{AgCl} + e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^{-}$, with $n = 1$ electron:
$$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( a_{\text{Cl}^{-}} \right)$$

> (Note: The activity of pure solids Ag and AgCl is 1, so they drop out of the logarithmic term.)

**Step 3 — Convert temperature and substitute constants.**
At $T = 310.15$ K (body temperature):
$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310.15}{96485} = 0.02673 \text{ V} = 26.73 \text{ mV}$$

For physiological saline, $a_{\text{Cl}^{-}} \approx 0.85$ (or use molarity-based approximation):
$$\ln(0.85) = -0.1625$$

**Step 4 — Compute the half-cell potential.**
$$E = 0.2223 - (0.02673)(1)(-0.1625)$$
$$E = 0.2223 + 0.004345$$
$$\boxed{E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}}(37^{\circ}\text{C}) \approx +0.223 \text{ V vs. SHE}}$$

**Interpretation for KTU Exam:** This ~223 mV offset appears at *every* electrode. Since both electrodes have nearly identical offsets, a **differential measurement** cancels them out — which is precisely why the ECG uses differential amplifiers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Einthoven's Law from Vector Superposition

**Problem Context:** Derive the fundamental Einthoven relationship $V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{II}$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the lead voltages as potential differences.**
Using $V_R$, $V_L$, $V_F$ for potentials at Right arm, Left arm, and Left foot:
$$V_{I} = V_{L} - V_{R}$$
$$V_{II} = V_{F} - V_{R}$$
$$V_{III} = V_{F} - V_{L}$$

**Step 2 — Add $V_I$ and $V_{III}$.**
$$V_{I} + V_{III} = (V_{L} - V_{R}) + (V_{F} - V_{L})$$
$$V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{F} - V_{R}$$
$$V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{II}$$

$$\boxed{V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{II}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *mathematical proof* that the cardiac vector forms a closed triangle. It's a favorite KTU 2-mark short answer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — CMRR of a Practical Instrumentation Amplifier

**Problem Context:** An INA has differential gain $A_d = 1000$ and common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.01$. Find the CMRR and the output when $V_{d} = 1$ mV and $V_{cm} = 1$ V.

**Step 1 — Apply the CMRR formula (linear scale first).**
$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} = \frac{1000}{0.01} = 100{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Convert to decibels.**
$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(100{,}000) = 20 \times 5 = 100 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the output voltage.**
$$V_{\text{out}} = A_{d} \cdot V_{d} + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$$
$$V_{\text{out}} = (1000)(1 \text{ mV}) + (0.01)(1 \text{ V})$$
$$V_{\text{out}} = 1 \text{ V} + 0.01 \text{ V} = 1.01 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR} = 100 \text{ dB}, \quad V_{\text{out}} = 1.01 \text{ V}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Without the INA, the 1 V common-mode (50 Hz pickup) would dominate the 1 mV signal by **60 dB** (factor of 1000). The 100 dB CMRR ensures clean differential extraction.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Electrode Types & Characteristics

| Electrode Type | Material | Application | Impedance | Motion Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Disposable Ag/AgCl (gel) | Silver/Silver Chloride w/ KCl gel | Resting ECG, Holter | Low (~1 kΩ) | Low |
| Reusable plate | Stainless steel | Emergency/short-term | Moderate | Moderate |
| Suction electrode | Ag/AgCl w/ bulb | Stress test | Moderate | Moderate |
| Needle electrode | Stainless steel/platinum | Intracardiac, intraop | Very low | Very low |
| Dry electrode | Ag/AgCl-coated polymer | Wearables, long-term | High (100 kΩ) | High |
| Capacitive (non-contact) | Insulated plate | Through-clothing monitoring | Reactive (capacitive) | Low |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — ADC Quantization Error Analysis

**Problem Context:** A 12-bit ADC samples an ECG signal with ±5 mV range. Find the resolution and the worst-case quantization error.

**Step 1 — Total number of quantization levels.**
$$L = 2^{N} = 2^{12} = 4096$$

**Step 2 — Step size (LSB weight).**
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^{N}} = \frac{10 \text{ mV}}{4096} = 2.441 \text{ µV/LSB}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum quantization error.**
$$E_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm 1.22 \text{ µV}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Resolution} = 2.441 \text{ µV/LSB}, \quad E_{q,\max} = \pm 1.22 \text{ µV}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often confuse *resolution* (the LSB value) with *accuracy* (which includes gain, offset, and linearity errors). The quantization error is only ONE component of total ADC error.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — ECG Acquisition System (End-to-End Signal Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Body - Cardiac Dipole] --> B[Electrodes x10 - Ag/AgCl]
    B --> C[Patient Cable with Defib Protection]
    C --> D[Isolation Barrier - Optical/Coupling]
    D --> E[Instrumentation Amplifier - INA]
    E --> F[High-Pass Filter 0.05 Hz]
    F --> G[Low-Pass Filter 150 Hz]
    G --> H[50 Hz Notch Filter]
    H --> I[ADC - 12 to 16 bit]
    I --> J[Digital Signal Processing]
    J --> K[Display and Storage]
    E -.Feedback Loop.-> L[Right Leg Drive Circuit]
    L -.Active CM Re-injection.-> A
```

### 4.2 Diagram — Electrode-Skin Interface Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InterfaceModel[Three Layer Electrode Skin Interface]
        E1[Metal Ag AgCl Electrode] -->|E_half_cell DC Source| R1[R_d - Charge Transfer Resistance]
        R1 --> C1[C_d - Double Layer Capacitance]
        C1 --> GEL[KCl Electrolyte Gel Layer]
        GEL --> R2[R_s - Skin Stratum Corneum Resistance]
        R2 --> C2[C_s - Skin Capacitance]
        C2 --> TISS[Living Tissue - Deep Dermis]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Instrumentation Amplifier Topology (3-Op-Amp)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input V1] --> A1[Op-Amp A1 Buffer]
    IN2[Input V2] --> A2[Op-Amp A2 Buffer]
    A1 --> RG[Gain Resistor RG]
    A2 --> RG
    RG --> A1
    RG --> A2
    A1 --> A3[Op-Amp A3 Difference Stage]
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> OUT[Output Vout Differential]
    A3 -.Feedback.-> A1
    A3 -.Feedback.-> A2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The gain of this 3-Op-Amp INA is set by a single external resistor: $A_d = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}$. The matched resistor pair $R_1$ in the input buffers is what enables the extremely high CMRR (> 100 dB).

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Standard 12-Lead ECG Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Limbs[Limbs - 4 Electrodes]
        RA[RA - Right Arm - Red]
        LA[LA - Left Arm - Yellow]
        LL[LL - Left Leg - Green]
        RL[RL - Right Leg - Black - Reference]
    end

    subgraph Bipolar[Bipolar Limb Leads - 3]
        L1[Lead I = LA minus RA]
        L2[Lead II = LL minus RA]
        L3[Lead III = LL minus LA]
    end

    subgraph Augmented[Augmented Leads - 3]
        AVR[aVR = RA minus LA plus LL]
        AVL[aVL = LA minus RA plus LL]
        AVF[aVF = LL minus RA plus LA]
    end

    subgraph Precordial[Precordial Chest Leads - 6]
        V1[V1 - 4th ICS Right Sternal Border]
        V2[V2 - 4th ICS Left Sternal Border]
        V3[V3 - Midway V2 to V4]
        V4[V4 - 5th ICS Midclavicular]
        V5[V5 - Anterior Axillary Line V4 Level]
        V6[V6 - Midaxillary Line V4 Level]
    end

    RA --> L1
    LA --> L1
    RA --> L2
    LL --> L2
    LA --> L3
    LL --> L3
    RA --> AVR
    LA --> AVR
    LL --> AVR
    RA --> AVL
    LA --> AVL
    LL --> AVL
    RA --> AVF
    LA --> AVF
    LL --> AVF
    V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 --> V5 --> V6
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Right Leg Drive Circuit Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BODY[Patient Body] -->|Common Mode Voltage Vcm| AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> CMOP[Common Mode Detector - Summing Junction]
    CMOP --> INV[Inverting Amplifier Gain ~ 100]
    INV --> CURR[Current Limiting Resistor 1 MΩ]
    CURR --> RLL[Right Leg Electrode]
    RLL --> BODY
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The RLD circuit *actively cancels* the 50 Hz interference by re-injecting an inverted version of the common-mode voltage into the body. This reduces CM voltage from several volts to < 1 V, making the INA's job dramatically easier.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "half-cell potential" in the context of biopotential electrodes. Why is Ag/AgCl preferred over pure silver for ECG electrodes?*

**Model Answer:**
The half-cell potential is the **DC voltage generated at the metal-electrolyte interface** due to the electrochemical redox reaction, quantified by the **Nernst Equation**. For Ag/AgCl, $E \approx +0.223$ V vs. SHE at 37 °C.

Ag/AgCl is preferred because:
- **Non-polarizable**: Current passes via reversible redox ($\text{AgCl} + e^{-} \leftrightarrow \text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^{-}$) rather than ion displacement → stable offset, low artifact.
- **Low offset voltage drift** (< 1 mV over hours vs. several mV for pure Ag).
- **Low electrode-skin impedance** with KCl gel interface.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three essential specifications of an ECG instrumentation amplifier.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **High CMRR** (≥ 100 dB) to reject 50 Hz mains pickup
2. **High input impedance** (> 10 GΩ) to avoid signal attenuation by electrode impedance
3. **Low input bias current** (< 1 nA) and **low input-referred noise** (< 1 µV RMS in 0.05–150 Hz band)
4. (Bonus) Adjustable gain (10 to 10,000), bandwidth ≥ 1 kHz, isolation ≥ 5000 V RMS

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete ECG signal acquisition system**. Discuss the role of the **Right Leg Drive (RLD) circuit** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **half-cell potential** of an Ag/AgCl electrode at body temperature using the **Nernst Equation**. State the boundary conditions and assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — ECG Acquisition System

**Block Diagram:**

The system comprises the following cascaded stages:

1. **Electrodes (10 for 12-lead, or 3 for simplified):** Ag/AgCl surface electrodes convert ionic currents in tissue to electronic signals.
2. **Patient Cable:** Shielded, with current-limiting resistors (≥ 10 kΩ) for defibrillator protection.
3. **Isolation Amplifier:** Provides galvanic isolation (typically via opto-couplers or transformers) with breakdown voltage ≥ 5000 V, protecting the patient from ground faults.
4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** Three-op-amp topology, $A_d = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}$, CMRR > 100 dB.
5. **Bandpass Filter:** HPF at 0.05 Hz (eliminates DC and baseline wander) + LPF at 150 Hz (anti-aliasing and EMG rejection).
6. **Notch Filter:** Twin-T or active notch at 50 Hz with Q ≈ 10.
7. **ADC:** ≥ 12-bit, ≥ 500 samples/sec.
8. **DSP & Display:** R-peak detection, HR calculation, waveform display.

**Right Leg Drive Circuit — Detailed Role:**
- The patient's body picks up 50 Hz common-mode voltage from capacitive coupling to AC mains.
- A summing amplifier detects the common-mode voltage (average of all electrode signals).
- An inverting amplifier (gain ≈ -100) reverses and amplifies this voltage.
- The output is re-injected into the patient's **right leg** through a current-limiting resistor (≥ 1 MΩ).
- This active feedback **reduces V_cm from several volts to < 1 V**.
- Safety: The feedback resistor limits maximum current to **< 10 µA**, ensuring patient safety under single-fault conditions.

---

#### Part (b) — Half-Cell Potential Derivation

**Assumptions:**
- Activity coefficients ≈ 1 (dilute KCl gel)
- Body temperature $T = 310.15$ K
- $a_{\text{Ag}} = a_{\text{AgCl}} = 1$ (pure solids)
- $a_{\text{Cl}^{-}} = 0.85$ (physiological saline approximation)

**Derivation:**

For the reaction $\text{AgCl}_{(s)} + e^{-} \leftrightarrow \text{Ag}_{(s)} + \text{Cl}^{-}_{(aq)}$ with $n = 1$:

$$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( a_{\text{Cl}^{-}} \right)$$

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 310.15$ K, $F = 96485$ C/mol, $E^{0} = 0.2223$ V, $a_{\text{Cl}^{-}} = 0.85$:

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310.15}{96485} = 0.02673 \text{ V}$$

$$E = 0.2223 - (0.02673) \ln(0.85)$$

$$E = 0.2223 - (0.02673)(-0.1625)$$

$$E = 0.2223 + 0.00434$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = 0.2266 \text{ V} \approx +0.223 \text{ V vs. SHE}}$$

**Boundary Conditions:** Valid for non-current-carrying equilibrium; departures occur under polarization.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **Einthoven's Triangle** and derive Einthoven's Law algebraically. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **sources of noise and artifacts** in ECG acquisition. How are they mitigated? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Einthoven's Triangle and Law

**Definition:** Einthoven's Triangle is an **equilateral triangle** formed by connecting the three limb electrodes: **RA (Right Arm), LA (Left Arm), LL (Left Leg)**. The heart is assumed to be at the **centroid**, acting as a fixed electric dipole source.

**Algebraic Derivation of Einthoven's Law:**

Define potentials at each vertex as $V_R$, $V_L$, $V_F$ (where F denotes Foot/LL).

The three bipolar lead voltages are:
$$V_I = V_L - V_R$$
$$V_{II} = V_F - V_R$$
$$V_{III} = V_F - V_L$$

Adding $V_I$ and $V_{III}$:
$$V_I + V_{III} = (V_L - V_R) + (V_F - V_L) = V_F - V_R = V_{II}$$

$$\boxed{V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}}$$

**Physical Significance:** The cardiac dipole vector lies in the plane of the triangle, and its projections onto the three sides are the three lead voltages. This redundancy allows **verification of signal integrity** during acquisition (any deviation from the law indicates lead misplacement or electrode failure).

---

#### Part (b) — Noise Sources and Mitigation

| Noise Source | Frequency | Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Powerline Interference** | 50 Hz (India) | Capacitive coupling to AC mains | Notch filter, RLD circuit, shielded cables |
| **Baseline Wander** | < 0.5 Hz | Respiration, electrode gel drying, patient motion | HPF at 0.05 Hz, proper skin prep |
| **Motion Artifact** | 0.1 – 10 Hz | Skin-electrode impedance change due to movement | Ag/AgCl gel, secure electrode adhesion |
| **EMG Interference** | 20 – 500 Hz | Skeletal muscle activity (especially in stressed patients) | LPF at 150 Hz, patient relaxation |
| **Thermal Noise (Johnson)** | Broadband | Random electron motion in electrode resistance | Low-noise INA, minimal cable length |
| **Quantization Noise** | Broadband | ADC discretization | Higher ADC bits (≥ 12-bit, ideal 16-bit) |
| **RF Interference** | > 1 MHz | Mobile phones, electrosurgery | Shielded enclosure, EMI filters |
| **DC Offset** | 0 Hz | Half-cell potential differences | AC coupling (HPF), differential measurement |

**Best-Practice Mitigation Strategy (KTU Exam Emphasis):**
1. *Skin preparation* — abrade stratum corneum with alcohol
2. *Use Ag/AgCl electrodes* — low polarization
3. *Use high-CMRR INA* — > 100 dB
4. *Active RLD* — reduce common-mode voltage
5. *Bandpass filter* — 0.05 to 150 Hz
6. *Notch filter at 50 Hz* — narrow Q ≈ 10
7. *Shielded patient cable* — minimize capacitive pickup

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> - **Half-cell potential is a DC offset** — students mistakenly treat it as noise. It's a *deterministic* offset, canceled by differential measurement.
> - **HPF cutoff for ECG is NOT 0.5 Hz** — that's a common misconception. The standard is **0.05 Hz** for diagnostic ECG. Setting it at 0.5 Hz *distorts the ST segment*, which is clinically critical for ischemia detection.
> - **The Right Leg Drive is NOT just a reference.** It is an *active feedback loop*. Many students write "RLD is the ground reference" — wrong; RL is connected to the body through an active *drive* amplifier.
> - **Don't confuse CMRR formula.** Some textbooks use $20 \log$ (voltage), others use $10 \log$ (power). For voltage ratios, **always use 20**.
> - **Einthoven's Law sign convention matters.** Use $V_{II} = V_{F} - V_{R}$ consistently; mixing signs gives the wrong derivation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — ECG Acquisition**

**Core Concepts (Must Know):**
- ☐ ECG is a *surface biopotential measurement* with 0.05–150 Hz bandwidth and ~1 mV amplitude.
- ☐ **Ag/AgCl electrode** is the gold standard; half-cell potential ≈ +0.223 V at 37 °C.
- ☐ **Three-layer model** of electrode-skin interface: metal → electrolyte gel → skin (stratum corneum + living tissue) — each layer modeled as parallel RC.
- ☐ **Standard 12-lead ECG** uses 10 electrodes: 4 limb + 6 precordial (V1–V6).
- ☐ **Einthoven's Triangle** (RA, LA, LL) gives 3 bipolar leads; **Einthoven's Law**: $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$.
- ☐ **Augmented leads** (aVR, aVL, aVF) are unipolar with reference to a central terminal.
- ☐ **Precordial leads** (V1–V6) are unipolar chest leads measuring the horizontal plane of the heart.

**Front-End Hardware (Must Know):**
- ☐ **Instrumentation amplifier** with CMRR ≥ 100 dB and input impedance > 10 GΩ.
- ☐ **Right Leg Drive (RLD)** active circuit reduces CM voltage (from volts to < 1 V).
- ☐ **Isolation amplifier** (opto/transformer) for patient safety — leakage < 10 µA.
- ☐ **HPF at 0.05 Hz** removes baseline wander — *never set higher, ST segment matters*.
- ☐ **LPF at 150 Hz** removes EMG and high-frequency noise.
- ☐ **50 Hz notch filter** (Twin-T topology) for India mains interference.
- ☐ **ADC sampling rate ≥ 500 Hz**, resolution ≥ 12 bits.

**Mathematical Tools (Must Know):**
- ☐ **Nernst Equation** for half-cell potential derivation.
- ☐ **CMRR formula** in dB: $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$.
- ☐ **Nyquist theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$.
- ☐ **Quantization step**: $\Delta = V_{\text{FSR}} / 2^N$.

**Noise & Artifacts (Must Know):**
- ☐ 50 Hz interference → notch + RLD + shielding.
- ☐ Baseline wander → HPF + skin prep.
- ☐ Motion artifact → Ag/AgCl gel + secure adhesion.
- ☐ EMG → LPF + patient relaxation.

**Exam Traps (Avoid):**
- ☐ Do not confuse *half-cell potential* (deterministic offset) with *noise*.
- ☐ Do not write RLD as a *passive ground*.
- ☐ Do not set HPF above 0.05 Hz for diagnostic ECG.
- ☐ Use $20 \log$ (not $10 \log$) for voltage CMRR calculations.

**Practical Engineering Insight:**
Modern wearable ECG devices (smart watches) face the exact challenges of traditional ECG systems — dry electrodes with high impedance, motion artifacts, miniaturization — and solve them through aggressive DSP (adaptive filtering, template matching, wavelet denoising) layered on top of fundamentally the same analog front-end principles covered above.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise and Artifacts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Noise and Artifacts # 📘 MODULE 3 — NOISE AND ARTIFACTS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Noise** and **Artifacts** are the two principal classes of unwanted components that contaminate every practical biomedical signal. Distinguishing between them is the first step in any biomedical signal processing pipeline. ### 1.1 Formal Definition — Noise In the context of biomedical signals, **noise** is defined as an unwanted, random, stochastic disturbance that is **uncorrelated with the underlying physiological signal of interest**, and which arises from physical processes of thermal, electronic, or quantum origin. Mathematically, if $x(t)$ is the true physiological signal, the **observed measurement** is: $$ y(t) = x(t) + n(t) $$ where $n(t)$ denotes the additive noise process. Noise is **inherently unpredictable** in any single realization but is fully characterized by its **statistical descriptors** (mean, variance, power spectral density). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Noise is *random* by definition — its instantaneous value cannot be predicted, but its ensemble statistics (mean, variance, PSD) are well-defined and time-invariant (stationary). ### 1.2 Formal Definition — Artifacts **Artifacts** are deterministic or quasi-deterministic contaminations in the recorded biomedical signal that arise either from the **measurement apparatus (instrumentation artifacts)** or from **other physiological activities of the patient that are not the signal of interest (physiological artifacts)**. Artifacts have a **structured waveform** (e.g., sinusoidal 50 Hz hum, baseline drift, motion spikes) and are often **correlated** with identifiable events. $$ y(t) = x(t) + s(t) + n(t) $$ where $s(t)$ represents the structured artifact component and $n(t)$ the random noise. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Analogy — The Stethoscope in a Crowded Room:** > Imagine a doctor trying to listen to a patient's heartbeat (the *signal of interest*) using a stethoscope in a crowded marketplace. The patient's heartbeat is the true physiological signal. The random chattering of the crowd is **noise** — unpredictable, with no fixed pattern, but with a measurable *average loudness*. Meanwhile, a baby crying nearby, the honking of a car horn, and a passing street musician are **artifacts** — they have a recognizable pattern, and you can in principle identify and remove them if you know their source. > > * **Noise** = the random crowd chatter (statistical, stationary) > * **Artifacts** = the crying baby or honking horn (structured, often identifiable source) > * The doctor's "filtering" = cupping hands around the stethoscope, asking the crowd to be quiet (signal processing!) ### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Biomedical Noise Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > Key quantitative descriptors used throughout this module: > > | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit | > |---|---|---| > | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{SNR}$ | **0 – 60 dB** (clinical ECG/EEG) | > | **Power Spectral Density** | $S_n(f)$ | $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$ | > | **Mean Squared Value** | $E[n^2(t)]$ | $\text{V}^2$ | > | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma_n$ | $\text{V}$ (or $\mu\text{V}$) | > | **Noise Power** | $P_n$ | $\text{W}$ or $\text{V}^2$ | > | **Common-mode Rejection Ratio** | $\text{CMRR}$ | **80 – 120 dB** | The benchmark for *good* signal quality in clinical recordings is **SNR ≥ 20 dB** for diagnostic-grade ECG and **SNR ≥ 10 dB** for routine EEG. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Noise in Biomedical Signals Biomedical noise is classified along two orthogonal axes: **(a) source of origin**, and **(b) spectral character**. ### 2.1.1 Classification by Source * **Thermal (Johnson–Nyquist) Noise** — generated by random thermal motion of charge carriers in any resistive element. Universal and unavoidable. * **Shot Noise** — arises from the discrete (quantized) nature of charge carriers crossing a potential barrier (e.g., diode, transistor). * **Flicker Noise (1/f Noise)** — dominant at very low frequencies (below ~10 Hz); origin is not fully quantum but is tied to device imperfections. * **Environmental (Interference) Noise** — coupled from external electromagnetic sources (50/60 Hz power line, RF, mobile phones). * **Quantization Noise** — produced by the A/D converter due to finite bit resolution. ### 2.1.2 Classification by Spectral Character * **White Noise** — flat power spectral density across all frequencies; samples are uncorrelated. * **Colored Noise** — non-flat PSD; examples: **pink noise** (1/f), **brown/red noise** (1/f²), **blue noise** (f²). * **Burst Noise (Popcorn Noise)** — discrete random jumps in amplitude; observed in some semiconductor devices. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common two-mark question asks, *"Why is thermal noise called white noise?"* The answer is the analogy to white light — equal intensity at all frequencies, hence a **constant PSD**. ## 2.2 Taxonomy of Artifacts in Biomedical Signals ### 2.2.1 Physiological Artifacts These originate from the patient's own bodily processes that are not the signal under study. | Artifact | Source Signal | Contaminates | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | **ECG artifact** | Cardiac electrical activity | EEG, EMG, EOG | Adaptive filtering / gating | | **EMG artifact** | Muscle activity | EEG, ECG, EOG | Patient relaxation / filtering | | **EOG artifact** | Eye movement / blink | EEG (frontal) | Regression / ICA | | **Respiratory artifact** | Breathing | ECG baseline, EEG | Chest belt / baseline correction | | **Pulse artifact** | Mechanical pulse near electrode | EEG, MEG | Repositioning | ### 2.2.2 Instrumentation (Non-Physiological) Artifacts | Artifact | Cause | Mitigation | |---|---|---| | **Power-line interference (50/60 Hz)** | Mains pickup | Notch filter, driven-right-leg | | **Baseline wander** | Electrode–skin impedance, respiration | High-pass filter (0.5 – 5 Hz cutoff) | | **Motion artifact** | Electrode / cable movement | Restriction, adaptive filters | | **DC offset** | Electrode half-cell potential | AC coupling, high-pass | | **Saturation / clipping** | Amplifier overload | Gain reduction, input protection | ## 2.3 Statistical Characterization of Random Noise A stationary random noise process $n(t)$ is fully described by: * **Mean:** $\mu_n = E[n(t)]$ * **Variance:** $\sigma_n^2 = E[(n(t) - \mu_n)^2]$ * **Autocorrelation Function:** $R_{nn}(\tau) = E[n(t) \, n(t+\tau)]$ * **Power Spectral Density (PSD):** $S_n(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{nn}(\tau)\}$ The **Wiener–Khinchin theorem** establishes the Fourier-transform duality between the autocorrelation function and the PSD. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master these equations — they are the high-yield items that recur every KTU examination cycle for this module. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_n = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} \vert n(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Average noise power | $\text{V}^2$ | | 2 | $P_n = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_n(f) \, df$ | Total noise power from PSD | $\text{V}^2$ | | 3 | $S_n(f) = \dfrac{N_0}{2}$ (white noise) | Flat PSD | $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$ | | 4 | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio (dB) | **dB** | | 5 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{imp}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} + 10 \log_{10}(M)$ | Improvement from $M$ averages | **dB** | | 6 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | **dB** | | 7 | $V_n^2 = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$ | **Johnson–Nyquist thermal noise** (rms squared) | $\text{V}^2$ | | 8 | $f_{\text{notch}} = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (India) / $60 \text{ Hz}$ (USA) | Power-line interference frequency | **Hz** | | 9 | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Effective number of A/D bits | bits | | 10 | $\sigma_q = \dfrac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | Quantization noise std. dev. | **V** | > **Physical constants used:** Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$; room temperature $T \approx 300 \text{ K}$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility These noise/artifact models are foundational to: * **Clinical ECG monitors** — must achieve $\text{SNR} \geq 40$ dB for arrhythmia detection. * **EEG-based Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — physiological artifact removal is the single biggest preprocessing challenge. * **Implantable pacemakers** — operate at $\text{SNR}$ often below 10 dB; sophisticated template matching is required. * **Fetal ECG extraction from abdominal recordings** — maternal ECG is the dominant artifact; must be canceled by $\geq 30$ dB. * **Wearable health monitors (smartwatches)** — motion artifact is the limiting factor for ambulatory ECG/PPG. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermal (Johnson–Nyquist) Noise Power The **Johnson–Nyquist noise** is the noise voltage generated across any resistor $R$ due to thermal agitation of electrons. Derivation proceeds from the equipartition theorem applied to an RC low-pass network. **Step 1.** Consider an RC low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$. The mean squared noise voltage across the capacitor, from the equipartition theorem, is: $$ \frac{1}{2} C \langle v^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{2} k_B T $$ This gives $\langle v^2 \rangle = \dfrac{k_B T}{C}$. **Step 2.** For a low-pass RC filter, the noise-equivalent bandwidth is $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{4RC}$.
