The PSD of the noise across the resistor is white: $S_v(f) = \dfrac{\overline{v_n^2}}{\Delta f}$. **Step 3.** Substitute $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{4RC}$ and $\overline{v_n^2} = \dfrac{k_B T}{C}$: $$ S_v(f) = \frac{k_B T / C}{1/(4RC)} = 4 k_B T R $$ **Step 4.** The mean squared noise voltage measured across the resistor in bandwidth $\Delta f$ is: $$ \overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \, \Delta f $$ This is the **Johnson–Nyquist noise formula** (often called the *thermal noise equation*). > **Engineering use:** For a $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ resistor at $T = 300$ K observed in $\Delta f = 10 \text{ kHz}$ bandwidth, the rms noise is: $$ v_n = \sqrt{4 \cdot 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 300 \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^4} \approx 1.29 \ \mu\text{V} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ensemble Averaging Improves SNR **Problem setup:** We wish to recover a deterministic (repetitive) signal $s(t)$ from $M$ noisy recordings $x_i(t) = s(t) + n_i(t)$, where the $n_i(t)$ are independent realizations of zero-mean noise with variance $\sigma_n^2$. **Step 1.** The ensemble average of $M$ recordings is: $$ \bar{x}(t) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} x_i(t) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \big[ s(t) + n_i(t) \big] $$ **Step 2.** Since $s(t)$ is identical in every recording: $$ \bar{x}(t) = s(t) + \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} n_i(t) $$ **Step 3.** The expected value of the averaged noise term is: $$ E\!\left[ \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} n_i(t) \right] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} E[n_i(t)] = 0 $$ **Step 4.** The variance of the averaged noise, since the $n_i$ are statistically independent, is: $$ \text{Var}\!\left[ \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} n_i \right] = \frac{1}{M^2} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \text{Var}[n_i] = \frac{M \sigma_n^2}{M^2} = \frac{\sigma_n^2}{M} $$ **Step 5.** The standard deviation (rms) of the residual noise is: $$ \sigma_{\bar{n}} = \frac{\sigma_n}{\sqrt{M}} $$ **Step 6.** The signal power is unchanged ($P_s = \sigma_s^2$ remains identical). The noise power is reduced by a factor of $M$. The SNR improvement is: $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{P_s}{P_{\bar{n}}} = \frac{P_s}{P_n / M} = M \cdot \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} $$ **Step 7.** Converting to decibels: $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{imp}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(M) \ \text{dB} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "3-dB Rule":** Each time you double the number of averages $M$, SNR improves by exactly **3 dB**. This is the gold-standard result for evoked-potential (EP) recording in clinical neurology. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PSD of White Noise and Its Autocorrelation **Step 1.** A *white noise* process has, by definition, a flat power spectral density: $$ S_n(f) = \frac{N_0}{2} \quad \text{for all } f $$ The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ accounts for the two-sided spectrum. **Step 2.** The total noise power in a band $\Delta f$ is: $$ P_n = \int_{-\Delta f/2}^{\Delta f/2} S_n(f) \, df = \frac{N_0}{2} \cdot 2 \Delta f = N_0 \Delta f $$ **Step 3.** The autocorrelation function is the inverse Fourier transform of $S_n(f)$ (Wiener–Khinchin theorem): $$ R_{nn}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_n(f) \, e^{j 2 \pi f \tau} \, df = \frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2 \pi f \tau} \, df $$ **Step 4.** Using the Fourier identity $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2 \pi f \tau} df = \delta(\tau)$ (the Dirac delta), we obtain: $$ R_{nn}(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2} \, \delta(\tau) $$ **Step 5.** Conclusion: For white noise, $R_{nn}(\tau) = 0$ for any $\tau \neq 0$. This means **successive samples of white noise are uncorrelated** — the defining statistical property used in derivations such as ensemble averaging above. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Quantization Noise Variance **Step 1.** A uniform mid-tread A/D converter with step size $\Delta$ produces a quantization error $e_q$ uniformly distributed in $\left[ -\dfrac{\Delta}{2}, \dfrac{\Delta}{2} \right]$. **Step 2.** The mean is $E[e_q] = 0$ by symmetry. **Step 3.** The variance (mean squared error) is: $$ \sigma_q^2 = E[e_q^2] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, d e_q $$ **Step 4.** Evaluating the integral: $$ \sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 (\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} $$ **Step 5.** The rms quantization noise is therefore: $$ \sigma_q = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.289 \Delta $$ > **Engineering use:** For a 12-bit A/D converter with range $\pm 5 \text{ V}$, $\Delta = 10/4096 \approx 2.44 \text{ mV}$, so $\sigma_q \approx 0.7 \text{ mV}$. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Problem | Recommended Method | Limitation | |---|---|---| | 50/60 Hz interference | **Notch (band-stop) filter** | Removes legitimate signal at that frequency | | Random broadband noise | **Ensemble averaging** | Requires repeatable signal | | Baseline wander | **High-pass filter (≤ 0.5 Hz)** | May distort ST segment | | Motion artifact | **Adaptive filter** | Needs reference input | | ECG-in-EEG | **ICA / Adaptive filter** | Computationally intensive | | Quantization noise | **Oversampling + dithering** | Requires faster A/D | | Single-trial SNR boost | **Wavelet denoising / Kalman filter** | Choice of basis/state model matters | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Biomedical Signal Acquisition Chain with Noise/Artifact Sources The diagram below shows the standard acquisition pipeline. At every stage, a different category of noise or artifact may be injected. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Body] --> B[Bioelectric Source] B --> C[Electrodes and Skin Interface] C --> D[Preamplifier] D --> E[Amplifier and Filter] E --> F[A to D Converter] F --> G[Digital Signal Processor] G --> H[Display or Storage] N1[(Thermal Noise
4 kT R df)] -.-> D N2[(Shot Noise
2 q I df)] -.-> D N3[(Flicker Noise
1 over f)] -.-> D N4[(Powerline Interference
50 Hz)] -.-> E N5[(Baseline Wander
less than 0.5 Hz)] -.-> C N6[(Motion Artifact)] -.-> C N7[(ECG or EMG Crosstalk)] -.-> B N8[(Quantization Noise)] -.-> F N9[(Electrode Half Cell
Potential DC Offset)] -.-> C ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Noise and Artifact Removal Pipeline This flowchart shows the order in which noise/artifact reduction techniques are typically applied in a digital signal processor. ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Raw Recorded Signal] --> S1{Baseline Drift Present?} S1 -- Yes --> S2[High Pass Filter Cutoff 0.5 Hz] S1 -- No --> S3 S2 --> S3{Powerline Interference 50 Hz Present?} S3 -- Yes --> S4[Notch Filter 50 Hz Q equals 30] S3 -- No --> S5 S4 --> S5{High Frequency Noise Present?} S5 -- Yes --> S6[Low Pass Filter Cutoff 40 to 150 Hz] S5 -- No --> S7 S6 --> S7{Physiological Crosstalk Present?} S7 -- Yes --> S8[Adaptive Filter or ICA] S7 -- No --> S9 S8 --> S9[Clean Diagnostic Signal] S9 --> S10[Feature Extraction and Diagnosis] ``` ## 4.3 Block Architecture — Adaptive Noise Cancellation (ANC) for Biomedical Signals Adaptive filtering is the most powerful technique when a *reference* noise signal is available. The classic Widrow-Hoff ANC architecture is shown below. ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Primary Input: s t plus n t] --> A[Summing Junction] A --> ADAP[Adaptive Filter
Wiener or LMS] REF[Reference Input: n prime t] --> ADAP ADAP --> Y[Estimated Noise y hat t] Y --> SUB[Subtract from Primary] P --> SUB SUB --> E[Error Signal e t
Approx s t] E --> ADAP ``` > [!TIP] > **How it works in clinical practice:** The primary input is the contaminated EEG. The reference input is a separately recorded EOG (eye-blink) or ECG signal. The adaptive filter models the coupling path from the reference into the primary and subtracts its contribution, leaving the clean EEG. ## 4.4 Schematic of Notch Filter Frequency Response (50 Hz Rejection) A textual rendering of the magnitude response $|H(f)|$ of a 50 Hz IIR notch filter with quality factor $Q = 30$. ```mermaid flowchart LR F0[Frequency Axis f in Hz] --> F1[0 to 45 Hz: Passband Gain 0 dB] F1 --> F2[49 to 51 Hz: Deep Notch minus 40 dB] F2 --> F3[55 to 500 Hz: Passband Gain 0 dB] ``` For a 50 Hz notch filter with $Q = 30$, the **−3 dB bandwidth** is: $$ \text{BW} = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{50}{30} \approx 1.67 \text{ Hz} $$ This narrow rejection band ensures that legitimate EEG/ECG frequency content near 50 Hz is preserved. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define "noise" and "artifact" in the context of biomedical signal processing. Give two examples of each. **Model Answer:** **Noise:** A random, stochastic, statistically describable unwanted signal that is uncorrelated with the physiological signal of interest. It is characterized by ensemble statistics (mean, variance, PSD) rather than an instantaneous waveform. *Examples:* (i) Thermal (Johnson–Nyquist) noise in the input resistor of the preamplifier, (ii) Shot noise in the front-end transistor. **Artifact:** A structured, often deterministic, unwanted signal component caused by either the measurement system or other physiological processes. It typically has a recognizable waveform shape and a known source. *Examples:* (i) 50 Hz power-line interference coupled into an ECG recording, (ii) ECG signal leaking into an EEG recording (ballistocardiographic artifact). **Distinguishing feature:** Noise is *random* and *unstructured*; artifacts are *structured* and *identifiable* in origin. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State and briefly explain the Johnson–Nyquist thermal noise equation. Why is this noise unavoidable? **Model Answer:** The **Johnson–Nyquist thermal noise** equation gives the mean squared noise voltage generated across a resistor $R$ at absolute temperature $T$ in a measurement bandwidth $\Delta f$: $$ \overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \, \Delta f $$ where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ is Boltzmann's constant. **Why unavoidable:** It arises from the fundamental thermal agitation of charge carriers (electrons) in any conducting medium, which is a direct consequence of the laws of statistical mechanics (equipartition theorem). Even at $T = 0$ K, quantum-mechanical zero-point fluctuations persist. Therefore, no electronic circuit can be entirely free of thermal noise. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Adaptive Filtering / Ensemble Averaging **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **adaptive noise canceller (ANC)** used for removing physiological artifacts from biomedical signals. Define the LMS update rule. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **SNR improvement** obtained by ensemble averaging $M$ independent noisy recordings of a deterministic signal. Show that doubling $M$ improves SNR by **3 dB**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Adaptive Noise Canceller:** The ANC architecture (Widrow-Hoff, 1975) requires two inputs: * **Primary input** $d(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ — the contaminated signal. * **Reference input** $x(t) = n'(t)$ — a recording of the noise only, ideally uncorrelated with $s(t)$. The adaptive filter $W(z)$ processes $x(t)$ to produce an estimate $\hat{n}(t)$ of the noise in the primary. The error signal is: $$ e(t) = d(t) - \hat{n}(t) = s(t) + n(t) - \hat{n}(t) $$ Minimizing $E[e^2(t)]$ forces $\hat{n}(t) \to n(t)$, leaving $e(t) \to s(t)$. The **LMS (Least Mean Squares) update rule** for an FIR adaptive filter of order $N$ is: $$ \mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + 2 \mu \, e(k) \, \mathbf{x}(k) $$ where $\mu$ is the step size (learning rate), $e(k)$ is the instantaneous error, and $\mathbf{x}(k) = [x(k), x(k-1), \ldots, x(k-N+1)]^T$ is the reference input vector. **Stability condition:** $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$ where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix. **Clinical application:** Removing maternal ECG from abdominal fetal ECG recordings; the reference is a chest-lead maternal ECG. **Part (b) — SNR Improvement from Ensemble Averaging:** This derivation is shown in full in Section 3.2. Key results: $$ \sigma_{\bar{n}} = \frac{\sigma_n}{\sqrt{M}} \quad \text{(rms noise reduced by } \sqrt{M}\text{)} $$ $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = M \cdot \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} $$ $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{imp (dB)}} = 10 \log_{10}(M) $$ For $M = 2$ (doubling): $\text{SNR}_{\text{imp}} = 10 \log_{10}(2) = 10 \times 0.301 = 3.01 \text{ dB} \approx 3 \text{ dB}$. ✔ Verified. **Numerical example:** If single-trial SNR = 0 dB, then averaging 100 trials gives SNR = 20 dB — a clinically useful evoked potential. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Power Line Interference & Filtering **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the sources and characteristics of **power-line interference** in ECG recordings. Design a **50 Hz second-order IIR notch filter** with $Q = 30$. Write the difference equation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **notch filtering**, **adaptive filtering**, and **wavelet denoising** as methods for removing power-line interference from biomedical signals. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 50 Hz Notch Filter Design:** **Sources of power-line interference in ECG:** * Capacitive coupling between the mains wiring and electrode leads. * Inductive coupling from nearby transformers. * Improper grounding creating ground loops. * High electrode–skin impedance imbalance (poor CMRR). **Notch filter design:** A digital IIR notch filter has a transfer function: $$ H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}} $$ For notch at $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $F_s = 500$ Hz: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 50}{500} = 0.6283$ rad/sample. So $\cos(\omega_0) = \cos(0.6283) \approx 0.3090$. Pole radius $r$ related to quality factor $Q$ by: $r = 1 - \dfrac{\pi \cdot \text{BW}}{F_s} = 1 - \dfrac{\pi f_0}{Q F_s} = 1 - \dfrac{\pi \cdot 50}{30 \cdot 500} \approx 0.9895$. Numerator (zeros on the unit circle): $b = [1, -0.6180, 1]$ (since $2 \cos \omega_0 = 0.6180$). Denominator (poles inside unit circle): $a = [1, -0.6118, 0.9791]$ (since $2r\cos\omega_0 = 0.6118$, $r^2 = 0.9791$). Difference equation: $$ y(n) = 0.6118 \, y(n-1) - 0.9791 \, y(n-2) + x(n) - 0.6180 \, x(n-1) + x(n-2) $$ **Part (b) — Comparison Table:** | Criterion | Notch Filter | Adaptive Filter | Wavelet Denoising | |---|---|---|---| | **Requires reference** | No | Yes (reference noise) | No | | **Distortion of signal** | Yes (rejects 50 Hz content) | Minimal | Minimal | | **Time-varying interference** | No (fixed frequency) | Yes (adapts) | Yes | | **Computational cost** | Low | Medium | High | | **Harmonics (100, 150 Hz)** | Need multiple notches | Handled automatically | Handled automatically | | **Best for** | Stationary interference | Non-stationary interference | Non-stationary + transient artifacts | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > 1. **Confusing noise and artifact:** Many students write "thermal noise is an artifact" — it is not. Noise is random; artifacts are structured. > 2. **Forgetting the ½ factor in white-noise PSD:** Always state $S_n(f) = N_0/2$ for the *two-sided* spectrum. Writing $N_0$ alone is marked down. > 3. **Units mismatch in SNR:** A common error is using $\text{SNR} = P_s/P_n$ (linear) but writing the answer in dB. Conversion: $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$ for power, $20 \log_{10}(\cdot)$ for amplitude. > 4. **Sign error in LMS update:** The LMS rule is $\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + 2\mu e(k)\mathbf{x}(k)$ — note the **plus** sign (steepest descent), not minus. > 5. **Adaptive filter requirement:** Forgetting to mention that an adaptive canceller requires a **reference input** is the single most common 2-mark deduction. > 6. **Indian vs US frequency:** India uses **50 Hz**; the US uses **60 Hz**. Always specify which one your design targets. > 7. **Quantization noise unit:** $\sigma_q$ is the *standard deviation*, not the peak-to-peak value (which is $\Delta$). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision checklist — Module 3: Noise and Artifacts** * **Definitions:** Noise is *random* and *stochastic*; artifacts are *structured* and *deterministic/quasi-deterministic*. * **Johnson–Nyquist noise:** $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \, \Delta f$ — thermal, white, Gaussian-distributed, unavoidable. * **Shot noise:** Poisson statistics; $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I \, \Delta f$. * **Flicker (1/f) noise:** dominant at low frequencies (< 10 Hz); cannot be removed by averaging; needs chopper stabilization. * **White noise PSD:** $S_n(f) = N_0/2$ (two-sided), $R_{nn}(\tau) = (N_0/2)\delta(\tau)$ — uncorrelated samples. * **SNR definition (dB):** $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$. * **Ensemble averaging gain:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{imp}} = 10 \log_{10}(M)$; **doubling $M$ gives +3 dB**. * **Quantization noise:** $\sigma_q = \Delta / (2\sqrt{3})$; $\text{SNR}_{q} \approx 6.02 N + 1.76$ dB for an $N$-bit A/D. * **Power-line interference:** **50 Hz** in India, **60 Hz** in the US; remove with notch filter (Q ≈ 30). * **Baseline wander:** < 0.5 Hz; remove with high-pass filter. * **Physiological artifacts:** ECG, EMG, EOG, respiration — remove with adaptive filtering or ICA. * **Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC):** Widrow-Hoff LMS algorithm; needs a reference input; update rule $\mathbf{w}_{k+1} = \mathbf{w}_k + 2\mu e_k \mathbf{x}_k$. * **CMRR:** Should be $\geq 80$ dB for clinical-grade bioamplifiers. * **Order of preprocessing:** (1) Remove DC offset / baseline wander → (2) Remove power-line interference → (3) Low-pass filter → (4) Remove physiological crosstalk → (5) Detect features. * **Wiener–Khinchin theorem:** $S_n(f) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} R_{nn}(\tau)$ — connects time-domain correlation to frequency-domain power. * **Equipartition theorem:** Foundation of Johnson–Nyquist noise; gives $\frac{1}{2} C \langle v^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{2} k_B T$. * **Most-tested formula in KTU:** the $4 k_B T R \Delta f$ equation, followed by the $10 \log_{10}(M)$ ensemble-averaging result. * **Practical SNR targets:** ECG ≥ 40 dB; EEG ≥ 10 dB; EMG ≥ 20 dB for diagnostic recordings.

topics.title: Preprocessing of ECG Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Preprocessing of ECG Signals ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Preprocessing of ECG (Electrocardiogram) Signals** is defined as the systematic application of digital signal processing (DSP) techniques to remove or attenuate noise contamination, correct baseline deviations, and enhance the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the raw ECG waveform acquired from surface electrodes, prior to any clinical feature extraction, morphological analysis, or automated diagnostic interpretation. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, preprocessing forms the **front-end conditioning stage** of the cardiac signal processing chain and is mapped to **Course Outcome CO3** of PEICT633 (Application of DSP techniques to biomedical signals). The fundamental processing goals are: 1. **Noise Suppression** — Removal of powerline interference, EMG (electromyogram) artifacts, baseline wander, and motion artifacts. 2. **Signal Enhancement** — Bandwidth limitation and morphological sharpening of the QRS complex. 3. **Baseline Restoration** — Removal of low-frequency drift (< 0.5 Hz) caused by respiration and electrode polarization. ### 1.2 Standard ECG Signal Characteristics | Parameter | Standard Value | KTU Reference | |---|---|---| | **Heart Rate (HR)** | **60 – 100 bpm** (resting) | 0.5 – 40 Hz bandwidth | | **QRS Duration** | **0.06 – 0.10 s** | Critical for detection | | **P Wave Duration** | **0.06 – 0.11 s** | Atrial depolarization | | **T Wave Duration** | **0.10 – 0.25 s** | Ventricular repolarization | | **Sampling Frequency** | **360 – 1000 Hz** | Clinical standard | | **Signal Amplitude** | **0.5 – 5 mV** | Surface electrodes | > [!NOTE] > **Why is ECG Preprocessing Critical?** > Raw ECG signals are inherently corrupted by multiple noise sources. A study by the **American Heart Association (AHA)** indicates that without proper preprocessing, automated arrhythmia detection algorithms suffer a **misclassification rate increase of 18 – 32%**. Preprocessing is therefore the *non-negotiable foundation* upon which all subsequent clinical interpretations are built. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Stethoscope at a Rock Concert"** > Imagine you are a doctor trying to listen to a patient's heart using a stethoscope in the middle of a rock concert. The actual heartbeat sound (your "ECG signal") is completely drowned by the loud music (noise), the moving crowd shifting you around (motion artifacts), and the bass-rhythm of the speakers causing your hand to vibrate (baseline wander). > > **ECG preprocessing is your digital "noise-cancelling headphone"** for cardiac signals. It mathematically isolates the true heart's electrical activity by: > - Cutting out the constant drone of the air-conditioner (high-pass filter removes DC drift). > - Blocking the high-pitched microphone hiss (low-pass filter removes EMG noise). > - Cancelling the 50 Hz electrical hum of the stage lights (notch filter removes powerline interference). > > Only after this digital "auditory cleanup" can a clean heartbeat pattern be recorded and analyzed. ### 1.4 Types of Noise in ECG Signals ``` ECG_signal(t) = pure_ECG(t) + n_powerline(t) + n_baseline(t) + n_EMG(t) + n_motion(t) ``` | Noise Type | Frequency Band | Primary Source | Clinical Impact | |---|---|---|---| | **Powerline Interference** | **50/60 Hz** | Mains electricity coupling | False QRS detection | | **Baseline Wander** | **< 0.5 Hz** | Respiration, electrode motion | ST-segment distortion | | **EMG / Muscle Noise** | **20 – 500 Hz** | Skeletal muscle activity | Obscures low-amplitude waves | | **Motion Artifacts** | **1 – 10 Hz** | Patient movement | Transient spikes | | **High-Frequency Noise** | **> 100 Hz** | Instrumentation electronics | False R-peak detection | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Decomposition of the Preprocessing Pipeline The ECG preprocessing pipeline is a **cascaded multi-stage filtering architecture**. Each stage targets a specific noise band using the **linear time-invariant (LTI)** filter theory, where the impulse response $h(n)$ and transfer function $H(z)$ fully characterize the system behavior. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KTU 2024 Core Principle:** Preprocessing must be performed in the **sequence: Notch → High-Pass → Low-Pass → Adaptive**. This order prevents the introduction of new artifacts and ensures monotonic SNR improvement. #### Stage 1: Powerline Interference Removal (Notch Filter) The **notch filter** is a second-order IIR band-stop filter designed with a sharp null at the powerline frequency. Its transfer function in the Z-domain is: $$ H_{notch}(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}} $$ where the **resonance angular frequency** is: $$ \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{F_s} $$ with $f_0$ being the interference frequency (**50 Hz in India, 60 Hz in USA**) and $F_s$ the sampling frequency. > [!NOTE] > **Why the $r$ (pole-radius) parameter?** The parameter $r < 1$ controls the bandwidth of the notch. A value of $r = 0.9$ produces a narrow notch with 3-dB bandwidth of approximately **5 Hz**, preserving ECG spectral content at adjacent frequencies. #### Stage 2: Baseline Wander Removal (High-Pass Filter) Baseline wander (BW) is a low-frequency artifact typically below **0.5 Hz** caused by respiration (≈ 0.25 Hz) and electrode-skin polarization. A first-order high-pass IIR filter suffices: $$ H_{HP}(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}} $$ where $\alpha$ is the pole location, chosen such that the **3-dB cutoff frequency** is: $$ f_c = \frac{F_s}{2\pi} \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{2\alpha}{1 + \alpha^2}\right) $$ #### Stage 3: High-Frequency Noise Suppression (Low-Pass Filter) A **Butterworth low-pass filter** of order 4–8 is preferred due to its maximally flat passband response. The continuous-time transfer function is: $$ \vert H_{LP}(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}} $$ where $N$ is the filter order and $\Omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ is the cutoff frequency. For ECG, $f_c = 40$ Hz is the standard clinical choice. #### Stage 4: Adaptive Filtering (Wiener / LMS / NLMS) Adaptive filters are essential when noise characteristics are **non-stationary**, as in motion artifacts. The **Least Mean Squares (LMS) algorithm** updates the filter coefficients as: $$ w(n+1) = w(n) + \mu \cdot e(n) \cdot x(n) $$ where: - $w(n)$ = filter coefficient vector - $\mu$ = step-size (learning rate) - $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ = error signal - $x(n)$ = reference noise input - $d(n)$ = primary (corrupted) input > [!TIP] > **Stability Condition for LMS:** The step-size must satisfy $0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{max}}$, where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the autocorrelation matrix of $x(n)$. Violating this causes the filter to **diverge**, producing amplified noise instead of removing it. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 BOARD EXAM FOCUS** | # | Concept | Formula | Key Variable Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Notch Resonance Frequency | $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{F_s}$ | $f_0$ = 50/60 Hz | | 2 | Notch Transfer Function | $H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | $r < 1$ | | 3 | HPF Cutoff Frequency | $f_c = \frac{F_s}{2\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha^2}\right)$ | $\alpha \in [0.9, 0.99]$ | | 4 | LPF Butterworth Magnitude | $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}}$ | $N$ = order | | 5 | LMS Update Rule | $w(n+1) = w(n) + \mu e(n) x(n)$ | $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{max}$ | | 6 | Wiener-Hopf Solution | $w_{opt} = R^{-1} p$ | $R$ = autocorrelation matrix | | 7 | Pan-Tompkins Squaring | $y(n) = [x(n)]^2$ | Non-linear amplification | | 8 | Moving Average | $y(n) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} x(n-k)$ | $M$ = window length | | 9 | Wavelet Thresholding | $\hat{w}_{j,k} = \text{sign}(w_{j,k}) \cdot \max(\vert w_{j,k}\vert - \lambda, 0)$ | Soft threshold | | 10 | SNR (dB) | $\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Quality metric | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE:** When writing $H(z)$ in exam answers, ALWAYS show the difference equation form $y(n) = \sum a_k x(n-k) - \sum b_k y(n-k)$ alongside it. Examiners specifically look for the difference equation derivation (4 marks out of 7) when the transfer function is asked. ### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility ECG preprocessing is not merely academic — it is the cornerstone of: - **Implantable Pacemakers and ICDs** — Resource-constrained chips require ultra-efficient noise cancellation. - **Wearable Fitness Bands (Apple Watch, Fitbit)** — Real-time motion artifact removal in ambulatory settings. - **Telemedicine Cloud Diagnostics** — Clean signals enable remote ML-based arrhythmia detection. - **Holter Monitoring** — 24-hour continuous recording demands robust baseline correction. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Notch Filter Transfer Function from First Principles **Objective:** Derive $H_{notch}(z)$ for removing 50 Hz interference with sampling rate $F_s = 500$ Hz. **Step 1 — Determine the normalized digital frequency** The angular frequency of the interference is $\Omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi (50) = 100\pi$ rad/s. Converting to digital frequency: $$ \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{F_s} = \frac{2\pi (50)}{500} = \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample} $$ This places the notch at the normalized frequency $\omega_0 = 0.2\pi$ on the unit circle. **Step 2 — Place the zeros on the unit circle** A notch filter requires two zeros exactly on the unit circle at angles $\pm \omega_0$ to create nulls in the magnitude response. The zeros are: $$ z_{1,2} = e^{\pm j\omega_0} $$ The numerator polynomial is constructed from these zeros: $$ N(z) = (z - e^{j\omega_0})(z - e^{-j\omega_0}) = z^2 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z + 1 $$ **Step 3 — Normalize the numerator to monic form with $z^{-1}$ terms** Dividing by $z^2$ and multiplying by $z^{-1}$ to align with standard DSP convention: $$ N(z^{-1}) = 1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2} $$ **Step 4 — Place the poles inside the unit circle for selectivity** To create a sharp, narrow notch, poles are placed at radius $r < 1$ along the same angles: $$ p_{1,2} = r e^{\pm j\omega_0} $$ The denominator polynomial becomes: $$ D(z) = (z - r e^{j\omega_0})(z - r e^{-j\omega_0}) = z^2 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z + r^2 $$ **Step 5 — Construct the final transfer function** $$ H_{notch}(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}} $$ Substituting $\omega_0 = \pi/5$ and choosing $r = 0.9$: $$ H_{notch}(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\pi/5) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.8\cos(\pi/5) z^{-1} + 0.81 z^{-2}} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to Difference Equation form** Cross-multiplying $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$: $$ Y(z)[1 - 1.8\cos(\pi/5) z^{-1} + 0.81 z^{-2}] = X(z)[1 - 2\cos(\pi/5) z^{-1} + z^{-2}] $$ Taking the inverse Z-transform yields the time-domain difference equation: $$ y(n) = 1.8\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{5}\right) y(n-1) - 0.81 y(n-2) + x(n) - 2\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{5}\right) x(n-1) + x(n-2) $$ This equation is the **practical implementable form** of the 50 Hz notch filter. ### 3.2 Derivation: LMS Adaptive Filter Convergence Analysis **Step 1 — Define the cost function** The LMS algorithm minimizes the **Mean Square Error (MSE)**: $$ J(n) = E[e^2(n)] = E[(d(n) - w^T(n) x(n))^2] $$ **Step 2 — Apply gradient descent** The weight update uses the steepest descent principle: $$ w(n+1) = w(n) - \frac{1}{2}\mu \nabla J(n) $$ **Step 3 — Use the instantaneous gradient estimate** Using the LMS instantaneous estimate $\hat{\nabla} J(n) = -2 e(n) x(n)$: $$ w(n+1) = w(n) + \mu e(n) x(n) $$ **Step 4 — Stability bound derivation** For the algorithm to converge in the mean, the step-size must satisfy: $$ 0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{max}} $$ where $\lambda_{max}$ is the **largest eigenvalue of the autocorrelation matrix** $R = E[x(n) x^T(n)]$. This bound is fundamental to KTU questions on adaptive filtering. ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Filter Selection for ECG Noise | Noise Type | Recommended Filter | Order/Cutoff | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | 50 Hz Interference | **IIR Notch (r=0.9)** | 2nd order | Removes 50 Hz, preserves ECG | | Baseline Wander | **1st order HPF** | $f_c = 0.5$ Hz | Risk of ST-segment distortion | | Muscle Noise (EMG) | **Low-pass Butterworth** | $N=4$, $f_c = 40$ Hz | Slight QRS smoothing | | Motion Artifacts | **Adaptive LMS Filter** | $\mu = 0.01$ | Requires reference channel | | Combined Noise | **Wavelet Thresholding (db6)** | 8 levels | Computationally heavy | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ECG Preprocessing Pipeline Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A[Raw ECG Acquisition
Surface Electrodes] --> B[Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain 1000] B --> C[Anti-Aliasing LPF
fc 150 Hz] C --> D[ADC
Fs 500 Hz] D --> E[Notch Filter
50 Hz Removal] E --> F[High-Pass Filter
fc 0.5 Hz] F --> G[Low-Pass Filter
fc 40 Hz] G --> H[Adaptive Filter
LMS / NLMS] H --> I[Wavelet Denoising
Optional Stage] I --> J[Clean ECG Output
For Feature Extraction] J --> K[QRS Detection
Pan-Tompkins] J --> L[ST Segment Analysis] J --> M[HRV Computation] ``` ### 4.2 Adaptive Filter Architecture for Motion Artifact Cancellation ```mermaid graph LR A[Primary Input d n
Corrupted ECG] --> S[Summing Junction] R[Reference Input x n
Accelerometer Signal] --> AF[Adaptive FIR Filter w n] AF --> Y[Filter Output y n] Y --> S S --> E[Error Signal e n] E --> WU[Weight Update
LMS Algorithm] WU --> AF E --> C[Clean ECG Output] ``` ### 4.3 Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Algorithm Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[Preprocessed ECG x n] --> B[Bandpass Filter
5-15 Hz] B --> C[Derivative Operator] C --> D[Squaring Function] D --> E[Moving Window Integration] E --> F[Adaptive Thresholding] F --> G{R Peak Detected?} G -->|Yes| H[Record R-R Interval] G -->|No| I[Search Continuation] H --> J[Update Threshold] I --> F J --> F ``` ### 4.4 Spectral Content of ECG and Major Noise Sources ```mermaid graph LR A0[DC Component
0 Hz] A1[P T U Waves
Below 5 Hz] A2[QRS Complex
10 40 Hz] A3[High Freq Detail
40 100 Hz] A4[EMG Noise
20 500 Hz] A5[Powerline
50 Hz] A6[Baseline Wander
Below 0.5 Hz] A7[Motion Artifacts
1 10 Hz] A0 ~~~ A1 ~~~ A2 ~~~ A3 ~~~ A4 ~~~ A5 ~~~ A6 ~~~ A7 ``` ### 4.5 Notch Filter Pole-Zero Plot Schematic ```mermaid graph TD Z1[Zero on Unit Circle at angle omega0] Z2[Second Zero at angle minus omega0] P1[Pole at radius r 0.9 angle omega0] P2[Pole at radius r 0.9 angle minus omega0] Z1 --> R1[Notch Response 50 Hz null] Z2 --> R1 P1 --> R1 P2 --> R1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Pole-Zero Diagram:** The two zeros (circles) sit ON the unit circle at $\pm \omega_0$, forcing the magnitude response to **zero** at that frequency. The poles (crosses) are pulled **inward** (radius $r = 0.9$) to create a sharp, narrow notch without affecting the rest of the frequency spectrum. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** List any **three sources of noise** in ECG signals and specify the **frequency range** over which each noise source predominantly occurs. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | # | Noise Source | Frequency Range | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Powerline Interference** | **50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA)** | | 2 | **Baseline Wander** | **Below 0.5 Hz** | | 3 | **Electromyogram (EMG) Noise** | **20 – 500 Hz** | (1 mark per correctly identified noise source with its frequency range.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the **purpose of using a notch filter** in ECG preprocessing? Mention the **transfer function** of a second-order IIR notch filter. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **notch filter** is used to **remove powerline interference** (50 Hz in India, 60 Hz in USA) from the corrupted ECG signal without significantly affecting the surrounding spectral content. The transfer function is: $$ H_{notch}(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}} $$ (1 mark for purpose, 2 marks for transfer function with parameter explanation.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Adaptive Filter Focus** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the **LMS adaptive filter algorithm** used for ECG noise cancellation. Derive the weight update equation and state the condition for convergence. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 50 Hz powerline interference is corrupting an ECG signal sampled at $F_s = 250$ Hz. Design a **second-order IIR notch filter** with pole-radius $r = 0.95$ to remove this interference. Derive the transfer function, difference equation, and plot the pole-zero locations. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — LMS Adaptive Filter (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Architecture and Objective (2 Marks):** The LMS adaptive filter aims to cancel noise from a primary input $d(n)$ (the corrupted ECG) using a reference input $x(n)$ (correlated noise, e.g., from an accelerometer or additional electrode). The filter output $y(n)$ is subtracted from $d(n)$ to produce the error $e(n)$, which is the estimated clean ECG. $$ y(n) = w^T(n) x(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n) x(n-k) $$ $$ e(n) = d(n) - y(n) $$ **Step 2 — Cost Function (1 Mark):** The objective is to minimize the mean-square error: $$ J(n) = E[e^2(n)] $$ **Step 3 — LMS Update Rule Derivation (3 Marks):** Using steepest descent with an instantaneous gradient estimate $\hat{\nabla}J(n) = -2e(n)x(n)$: $$ w(n+1) = w(n) - \frac{\mu}{2}\hat{\nabla}J(n) = w(n) + \mu e(n) x(n) $$ **Step 4 — Stability Condition (1 Mark):** $$ 0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{max}} $$ where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix. **Part (b) — Notch Filter Design (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Compute Normalized Frequency (1 Mark):** $$ \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{F_s} = \frac{2\pi(50)}{250} = \frac{2\pi}{5} = 0.4\pi \text{ rad/sample} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Trigonometric Values (1 Mark):** $$ \cos(\omega_0) = \cos(0.4\pi) = \cos(72°) \approx 0.309 $$ **Step 3 — Transfer Function (2 Marks):** $$ H_{notch}(z) = \frac{1 - 2(0.309)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2(0.95)(0.309)z^{-1} + (0.95)^2 z^{-2}} $$ $$ H_{notch}(z) = \frac{1 - 0.618 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.587 z^{-1} + 0.9025 z^{-2}} $$ **Step 4 — Difference Equation (2 Marks):** $$ y(n) = 0.587 y(n-1) - 0.9025 y(n-2) + x(n) - 0.618 x(n-1) + x(n-2) $$ **Step 5 — Pole-Zero Plot (1 Mark):** - **Zeros** at $z = e^{\pm j0.4\pi}$ — on the unit circle. - **Poles** at $z = 0.95 e^{\pm j0.4\pi}$ — inside the unit circle, close to the zeros. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **NEVER use the vertical pipe `|` inside a markdown table cell** for absolute value notation — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead to avoid table-breaking. KTU answer scripts are evaluated digitally. > 2. When designing filters, **always state the assumed value of $r$ explicitly**. Leaving it as a generic variable without a numerical value costs **2 marks**. > 3. The **LMS derivation is often incomplete** — students write the update rule but forget the **convergence bound**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Filter Design Focus** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** Discuss the **various sources of noise** corrupting the ECG signal with their frequency ranges. Explain the role of **baseline wander** removal using a high-pass filter and derive the transfer function. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for QRS complex detection. List its five main processing stages and write the mathematical expression for the **moving window integrator** stage. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Noise Sources and HPF Design (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Noise Sources Table (3 Marks):** | Noise Source | Frequency Range | Physiological Origin | |---|---|---| | **Baseline Wander** | **< 0.5 Hz** | Respiration, electrode motion | | **Powerline Interference** | **50/60 Hz** | Mains electricity | | **EMG Noise** | **20 – 500 Hz** | Muscle contractions | | **Motion Artifacts** | **1 – 10 Hz** | Patient movement | **Step 2 — Role of HPF for Baseline Removal (2 Marks):** Baseline wander causes ST-segment distortion and false QRS detection. A high-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = 0.5$ Hz removes this drift while preserving the P-QRS-T morphology. **Step 3 — HPF Transfer Function Derivation (2 Marks):** A first-order IIR HPF has the form: $$ H_{HP}(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}} $$ The 3-dB cutoff frequency is: $$ f_c = \frac{F_s}{2\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{2\alpha}{1 + \alpha^2}\right) $$ For $F_s = 360$ Hz and $f_c = 0.5$ Hz, solving for $\alpha$ gives $\alpha \approx 0.9956$. **Part (b) — Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Algorithm Overview (1 Mark):** The Pan-Tompkins algorithm is a **real-time QRS detection method** that operates in five cascaded stages to robustly identify R-peaks. **Step 2 — Five Stages (4 Marks):** 1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz):** Isolates QRS energy. 2. **Derivative Operator:** Emphasizes the steep slopes of the QRS: $$ y(n) = \frac{1}{8}[2x(n) + x(n-1) - x(n-3) - 2x(n-4)] $$ 3. **Squaring Function:** Non-linear amplification of large values: $$ y(n) = [x(n)]^2 $$ 4. **Moving Window Integration:** Smooths the squared signal: $$ y(n) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(n-k) $$ 5. **Adaptive Thresholding:** Dynamic peak detection with refractory period enforcement. **Step 3 — Mathematical Expression for Integration (2 Marks):** The moving window integrator over a window of $N$ samples (typically $N = 30$ for $F_s = 200$ Hz): $$ y(n) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(n-k) $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Confusing high-pass with low-pass filter design** — students often mix up the transfer function form. Always double-check: HPF blocks DC (z=1 zero), LPF blocks high frequencies. > 2. **Forgetting the refractory period** in Pan-Tompkins — without the 200 ms refractory window, T-waves get falsely detected as R-peaks. Examiners specifically look for this clinical detail (1 mark). > 3. **Writing derivative equation incorrectly** — the Pan-Tompkins derivative uses a **5-point filter** with specific coefficients, not a simple first-order difference. Memorize the exact form shown above. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU PEICT633 Module 3** **Core Concepts:** - ECG preprocessing involves **four cascaded stages**: Notch → High-Pass → Low-Pass → Adaptive. - The **notch filter** is the most critical stage for powerline interference removal at **50 Hz** (India). - **Baseline wander** is a low-frequency artifact (< 0.5 Hz) removed by a first-order HPF. - **LMS adaptive filter** uses the update rule $w(n+1) = w(n) + \mu e(n) x(n)$ with **stability bound** $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{max}$. - The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** uses five stages: Bandpass → Derivative → Squaring → Moving Integration → Adaptive Thresholding. **Must-Memorize Formulas:** - Notch filter: $H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ - HPF cutoff: $f_c = \frac{F_s}{2\pi}\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha^2}\right)$ - LMS rule: $w(n+1) = w(n) + \mu e(n) x(n)$ - Moving integrator: $y(n) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(n-k)$ **High-Yield Exam Traps:** - **Always compute numerical values** for $\cos(\omega_0)$ — generic symbolic answers lose 2 marks. - **State the sampling rate** in design problems — it is needed for frequency normalization. - **Mention the refractory period** (200 ms) in Pan-Tompkins answers to avoid T-wave false positives. - **Use `\vert` not `|`** in tables for absolute value notation to preserve markdown formatting. **Clinical Context to Include in Every Answer:** - ECG bandwidth: **0.5 – 40 Hz** (clinical standard). - QRS duration: **0.06 – 0.10 s**. - Sampling rate: **360 Hz** (clinical) to **1000 Hz** (research). - Reference databases: **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** for performance evaluation.

topics.title: Noise Removal and Baseline Correction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise Removal and Baseline Correction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Noise Removal — Formal Definition

In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing (PEICT633)**, **noise removal** refers to the systematic suppression of unwanted, non-physiological signal components that contaminate a recorded bio-electric or bio-mechanical signal, such as the **ECG (Electrocardiogram)**, **EEG (Electroencephalogram)**, or **EMG (Electromyogram)**, in order to recover the true underlying physiological waveform for clinical interpretation and automated feature extraction.

Formally, the observed signal $x[n]$ is modeled as:

$$
x[n] = s[n] + \eta[n] + b[n]
$$

where $s[n]$ is the clean physiological signal, $\eta[n]$ is the additive stochastic or sinusoidal noise, and $b[n]$ represents the low-frequency **baseline wander** term.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Noise removal in biomedical signals is a two-stage engineering task: **(i) High-frequency noise suppression** (powerline interference, EMG crosstalk, thermal noise) and **(ii) Low-frequency baseline wander removal** (respiration drift, motion artifacts). Both stages must preserve the diagnostic morphology of the waveform (e.g., QRS complex in ECG).

## 1.2 Baseline Correction — Formal Definition

**Baseline correction (or baseline wander removal)** is the specific sub-problem of estimating and subtracting the low-frequency drift component $b[n]$ from $x[n]$, producing a zero-mean, flat-baseline output $y[n] = x[n] - \hat{b}[n]$ that preserves the higher-frequency diagnostic features of the signal.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Cleaning a Muddy Audio Recording"

Imagine recording a beautiful violin concert using a cheap microphone placed next to a busy highway. What you hear is the **violin (your signal $s[n]$)** completely drowned out by the **traffic rumble (low-frequency baseline drift)**, the **hiss of the amplifier (high-frequency thermal noise)**, and the **50 Hz electrical hum of the recording equipment (powerline interference)**.

- **High-pass filtering** is like applying a "rumble filter" that rolls off the lowest frequencies (the truck engines).
- **Low-pass filtering** is like turning down the treble to suppress the hiss.
- **Notch filtering** is like surgically cutting out the exact 50 Hz electrical hum without affecting nearby violin frequencies.
- **Baseline correction** is like digitally "re-anchoring" the recording so the violinist's volume fluctuations (drift) are flattened, allowing the music to be heard cleanly.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations

- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** measured in **dB (decibels)**
- **Sampling Frequency ($F_s$):** typically **250 Hz to 1000 Hz** for ECG, **256 Hz to 512 Hz** for EEG
- **Powerline Interference Frequency:** **50 Hz (India/Europe)** or **60 Hz (USA)**
- **QRS Spectral Band:** approximately **0.5 Hz to 40 Hz**
- **Baseline Wander Band:** below **0.5 Hz**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Biomedical Noise Frequencies to Memorize:**
> - Powerline interference: **50 Hz / 60 Hz** (notch filter required)
> - EMG crosstalk: **> 100 Hz** (low-pass filter required)
> - Baseline wander: **< 0.5 Hz** (high-pass filter / median filter required)
> - Thermal/Instrumentation noise: broadband (low-pass filter required)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Noise in Biomedical Signals

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to classify the noise source, identify its spectral location, and choose an *appropriate* filter. The taxonomy is:

1. **Powerline Interference (PLI):** A narrow-band sinusoidal interference at the mains frequency (50 Hz in India). It arises due to electromagnetic coupling with the power grid and poor grounding of the subject. It may also include its harmonics at **100 Hz, 150 Hz, 200 Hz**.
2. **Baseline Wander (BW):** A very low-frequency drift (< 0.5 Hz) caused by respiration, subject movement, perspiration, or slow changes in electrode-skin impedance.
3. **High-Frequency Muscle (EMG) Artifact:** Broadband noise from neighboring muscle activity, typically occupying **20 Hz to several kHz**.
4. **Instrumentation Noise:** Broadband thermal noise from electronic components, electrode contact noise, and quantization noise from the ADC.
5. **Motion Artifact:** Transient, large-amplitude baseline excursions caused by electrode displacement.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Filtering Stage

- **Why a Notch Filter for 50 Hz?** A standard low-pass filter would distort the QRS complex because the QRS energy extends up to 40 Hz. A **notch filter** provides extremely high attenuation (typically **> 40 dB**) only at 50 Hz while leaving neighboring frequencies (40 Hz, 60 Hz) untouched.
- **Why a High-Pass Filter for Baseline?** Baseline wander shares the low-frequency band with the **P-wave and T-wave**. A simple first-order high-pass filter at 0.5 Hz is the standard clinical choice (per AHA recommendations).
- **Why Median Filtering for Baseline?** Median filters are *non-linear* and preserve sharp QRS edges, unlike linear high-pass filters that introduce ringing distortion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Key Parameters / Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Noisy signal model | $x[n] = s[n] + \eta[n] + b[n]$ | $s$: signal, $\eta$: noise, $b$: baseline |
| 2 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_\eta}\right)$ | $P_s$, $P_\eta$ are signal and noise powers; result in **dB** |
| 3 | Butterworth Magnitude Response | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1+\left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$ | $N$: filter order, $\omega_c$: cutoff in **rad/s** |
| 4 | First-Order High-Pass Cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F, $f_c$ in **Hz** |
| 5 | Moving Average (FIR) Filter | $y[n] = \dfrac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} x[n-k]$ | $M$: window length, must be odd for symmetric response |
| 6 | Moving Average Frequency Response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{1}{M}\cdot\dfrac{\sin(M\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}\,e^{-j\omega(M-1)/2}$ | Magnitude is a Dirichlet kernel |
| 7 | Median Filter | $y[n] = \text{median}\{x[n-M+1], \dots, x[n]\}$ | $M$: window length (odd), non-linear operator |
| 8 | Notch Filter (IIR, second order) | $H(z) = \dfrac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | $\omega_0$: notch frequency, $r < 1$: pole radius |
| 9 | Polynomial Baseline Fit | $p(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{K} a_k n^k$, minimize $\sum (x[n] - p(n))^2$ | Order $K$ typically 3 to 5; solved via least-squares |
| 10 | ECG Median Baseline Algorithm | $b[n] = M_{200}\,\bigl(M_{600}(x[n])\bigr)$ | 200 ms + 600 ms cascaded median filters |
| 11 | Window Length to Time | $M = F_s \cdot T_w$ | $T_w$ in seconds, $F_s$ in **Hz** |
| 12 | Pole Radius from Bandwidth | $r \approx 1 - \pi \cdot \dfrac{B}{F_s}$ | $B$: 3-dB notch bandwidth in **Hz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Note:** In the table, the absolute value / magnitude bars are written using `\vert` or `\mid` syntax (e.g., `\vert H(j\omega) \vert`) so as not to break the Markdown table. In your answer script, you may freely use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation since it is rendered by the typesetter, not Markdown.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The noise removal and baseline correction chain is the **first signal-conditioning block** in every commercial biomedical instrument:
- **Patient Monitors (Philips, GE Healthcare):** Real-time 50 Hz notch + 0.5 Hz HPF applied to ECG before QRS detection.
- **Holter Recorders:** 24-hour ambulatory ECG requires aggressive baseline correction because subjects walk, run, and sleep.
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** EEG signals require 50/60 Hz notch + 1–40 Hz bandpass before feature extraction.
- **Wearable Fitness Bands (Apple Watch, Fitbit):** Photoplethysmography (PPG) signals require motion-artifact cancellation via adaptive filtering.
- **Polysomnography (Sleep Labs):** Multi-channel EEG/EOG/EMG recordings require a unified denoising pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnitude Response of the Butterworth Low-Pass Filter

The Butterworth filter is maximally flat in the passband. We derive its squared magnitude response starting from the requirement that the first $2N-1$ derivatives of $\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2$ vanish at $\omega = 0$.

**Step 1:** Define the squared magnitude response as a polynomial in $\omega^2$:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + B_{2N}(\omega^2)}
$$

**Step 2:** To achieve maximal flatness at the origin, the polynomial $B_{2N}$ must be a **monomial** of degree $N$ in $\omega^2$. We therefore set:

$$
B_{2N}(\omega^2) = \left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^{2N}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting back yields the standard Butterworth magnitude response:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1+\left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^{2N}}
$$

**Step 4:** At the cutoff frequency $\omega = \omega_c$, the response is:

$$
\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1+1} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Converting to decibels (this is the **3-dB cutoff point**):

$$
20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = -3.0103 \text{ dB} \approx -3 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 6 (Filter Order Selection):** Given a desired stopband attenuation $A_s$ at frequency $\omega_s$, the required filter order is:

$$
N \geq \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(10^{A_s/10} - 1\right)}{2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_s}{\omega_c}\right)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Higher $N$ gives a sharper roll-off but introduces more phase delay and potential ringing near the QRS complex.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency Response of the M-Point Moving Average Filter

The moving average is the simplest low-pass FIR filter. We derive its frequency response to understand its limitation as a noise smoother.

**Step 1 (Time Domain):** The output is the average of the last $M$ samples:

$$
y[n] = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} x[n-k]
$$

**Step 2 (Z-Transform):** Taking the Z-transform of both sides:

$$
Y(z) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} X(z) = \frac{1}{M}\left(\frac{1 - z^{-M}}{1 - z^{-1}}\right) X(z)
$$

**Step 3 (Transfer Function):**

$$
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1}{M}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-M}}{1 - z^{-1}}
$$

**Step 4 (Frequency Response):** Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$
H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{M}\cdot\frac{1 - e^{-jM\omega}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}
$$

**Step 5 (Simplify using Euler's identity):** Factor out $e^{-jM\omega/2}$ from the numerator and $e^{-j\omega/2}$ from the denominator:

$$
H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{M}\cdot\frac{e^{-jM\omega/2}\left(e^{jM\omega/2} - e^{-jM\omega/2}\right)}{e^{-j\omega/2}\left(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}\right)}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply $\sin\theta = \dfrac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$:

$$
H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{M}\cdot\frac{\sin(M\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}\,e^{-j\omega(M-1)/2}
$$

**Step 7 (Magnitude):** The magnitude is the **normalized Dirichlet kernel**:

$$
\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{M}\left\vert \frac{\sin(M\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert
$$

**Step 8 (Zeros):** Zeros occur at $\omega = \pm\dfrac{2\pi k}{M}$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, M-1$.

**Engineering Insight:** For ECG with $F_s = 360$ Hz, choosing $M = 5$ gives a moving average with first spectral null at $f = 72$ Hz — excellent for removing high-frequency EMG noise while preserving the QRS.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Cascaded Median Filter for ECG Baseline Removal

The most cited baseline-removal technique (used in the Pan-Tompkins algorithm) is the **200 ms + 600 ms cascaded median filter**. We derive why this works.

**Step 1 (Problem Setup):** The QRS complex is approximately 100–200 ms wide. The baseline drift is slower than 0.5 Hz (i.e., its half-period is > 1 second). We must design a non-linear filter that destroys the baseline but preserves the QRS.

**Step 2 (Median Filter Definition):** The median of an odd-length window $\{x[n-M+1], \dots, x[n]\}$ is the middle value when sorted. We define the $T$-millisecond median filter as:

$$
M_T(x)[n] = \text{median}\{x[n - W + 1], \dots, x[n]\}, \quad W = F_s \cdot T
$$

**Step 3 (200 ms Median):** With $F_s = 360$ Hz, $W_1 = 200 \times 360/1000 = 72$ samples. A 200 ms median completely **erases the QRS** (which is 100–200 ms wide), replacing it with the surrounding baseline.

$$
b_1[n] = M_{200}(x)[n]
$$

**Step 4 (600 ms Median):** With $W_2 = 600 \times 360/1000 = 216$ samples. The 600 ms median smooths the result of Step 3, removing any residual sharp transitions from the previous stage.

$$
b_2[n] = M_{600}(b_1)[n] = M_{600}\!\left(M_{200}(x)\right)[n]
$$

**Step 5 (Subtraction):** The corrected signal is:

$$
y[n] = x[n] - b_2[n]
$$

**Step 6 (Why it works):**
- The 200 ms median **destroys** any feature narrower than 200 ms (the QRS, motion spikes) → output is purely baseline.
- The 600 ms median **smooths** the recovered baseline, removing high-frequency residuals.
- Subtraction removes the baseline while **perfectly preserving the QRS** (which is nonlinearly reconstructed, not filtered linearly).

> [!NOTE]
> **The 200 ms / 600 ms choice is not arbitrary.** It is calibrated to the **biophysical duration of the QRS complex** (≤ 200 ms) and the **minimum period of respiration** (~3 seconds → half-period ~1.5 s ≫ 600 ms). The cascaded median is **immune to linear phase distortion**, which is its key advantage over the IIR high-pass filter.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Notch Filter for 50 Hz Powerline Interference

A second-order IIR notch filter has the transfer function:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}
$$

**Step 1 (Zeros on Unit Circle):** The numerator roots are at $z = e^{\pm j\omega_0}$, placing zeros exactly on the unit circle at the notch frequency $\omega_0$. This gives **infinite attenuation** at $\omega_0$.

**Step 2 (Poles Inside Unit Circle):** The denominator has poles at $z = r e^{\pm j\omega_0}$ with $r < 1$ to maintain stability. The closer $r$ is to 1, the narrower the notch.

**Step 3 (Digital Frequency):** The notch angular frequency is:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{F_s}
$$

For $f_0 = 50$ Hz and $F_s = 500$ Hz:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{500} = \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample}
$$

**Step 4 (Bandwidth Selection):** The 3-dB notch bandwidth $B$ (in Hz) is approximately:

$$
B \approx \frac{F_s (1-r)}{\pi}
$$

For a desired bandwidth of 2 Hz at $F_s = 500$ Hz:

$$
r = 1 - \frac{\pi B}{F_s} = 1 - \frac{\pi \times 2}{500} \approx 0.9874
$$

**Step 5 (Substitute Coefficients):** With $\cos(\pi/5) \approx 0.8090$ and $r \approx 0.9874$:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1 - 1.618 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.598 z^{-1} + 0.975 z^{-2}}
$$

This filter provides **> 40 dB attenuation at 50 Hz** with minimal distortion at 40 Hz and 60 Hz — ideal for ECG.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Question:** An ECG signal sampled at $F_s = 250$ Hz contains 50 Hz PLI. Design a second-order Butterworth low-pass filter with cutoff 40 Hz, and a notch filter at 50 Hz. Compute the SNR improvement when the noise power is reduced to 1/10 of its original.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Butterworth Order:** Using the formula from Section 3.1, with $f_c = 40$ Hz, $f_s = 60$ Hz (desired stopband start), $A_s = 20$ dB:

$$
N \geq \frac{\log_{10}(10^{2} - 1)}{2 \log_{10}(60/40)} = \frac{\log_{10}(99)}{2 \log_{10}(1.5)} = \frac{1.9956}{0.3522} \approx 5.67
$$

Therefore, $N = 6$ (next integer) suffices.

**Part (b) — SNR Improvement:** If noise power reduces by a factor of 10:

$$
\Delta \text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(10) = 10 \text{ dB}
$$

The output SNR is **10 dB higher** than the input SNR.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Biomedical Signal Denoising Pipeline

The figure below maps the end-to-end signal-conditioning chain applied to a raw biomedical signal in a typical patient monitor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RAW BIO SIGNAL x of n] --> B[PRE AMPLIFIER]
    B --> C[HIGH PASS FILTER FC 0.5 HZ]
    C --> D[LOW PASS FILTER FC 40 HZ]
    D --> E[NOTCH FILTER 50 HZ]
    E --> F[ADC SAMPLING]
    F --> G[DIGITAL BASELINE CORRECTION]
    G --> H[MEDIAN FILTER 200 MS]
    H --> I[MEDIAN FILTER 600 MS]
    I --> J[SUBTRACT BASELINE]
    J --> K[CLEAN SIGNAL y of n]
    L[RESPIRATION] -.->|Couples into| A
    M[POWERLINE 50 HZ] -.->|Couples into| A
    N[MUSCLE EMG] -.->|Couples into| A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Adaptive Filter for Motion Artifact Cancellation

Adaptive filters (e.g., LMS, NLMS, RLS) are used when the noise reference is available, such as an accelerometer reference for removing motion artifacts from a PPG signal.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT
        P[PRIMARY SIGNAL x of n = s of n plus n of n]
        Q[REFERENCE NOISE r of n from accelerometer]
    end
    subgraph ADAPTIVE_CORE
        R[ADAPTIVE FILTER W of n]
        S[ERROR ESTIMATOR e of n]
        T[WEIGHT UPDATE LMS ALGORITHM]
    end
    P --> S
    Q --> R
    R -->|y hat of n| S
    S -->|e of n approximates s of n| U[CLEAN SIGNAL OUTPUT]
    T -.->|Updates weights| R
    S -.->|Drives adaptation| T
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Filter

The flowchart below is a **decision aid** that the KTU examiner often expects in 14-mark design questions. It maps the noise type to the recommended filter.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START IDENTIFY NOISE TYPE] --> B{FREQUENCY RANGE}
    B -->|< 0.5 HZ BASELINE WANDER| C[USE HIGH PASS OR MEDIAN FILTER]
    B -->|AT 50 OR 60 HZ PLI| D[USE IIR NOTCH FILTER]
    B -->|> 40 HZ EMG OR THERMAL| E[USE LOW PASS BUTTERWORTH OR MOVING AVERAGE]
    B -->|TRANSIENT SPIKES MOTION| F[USE MEDIAN FILTER OR WAVELET THRESHOLDING]
    C --> G[FINAL OUTPUT CLEAN SIGNAL]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Frequency-Domain Masking View

This diagram explains, in a single picture, *where* in the frequency domain each filter acts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPECTRUM [FREQUENCY DOMAIN OF x of n]
        A1[0 TO 0.5 HZ BASELINE] --> A2[0.5 TO 40 HZ QRS ECG EEG]
        A2 --> A3[40 TO 50 HZ USEFUL P WAVE TAIL]
        A3 --> A4[50 HZ PLI SPIKE]
        A4 --> A5[50 TO 1000 HZ EMG NOISE]
    end
    subgraph ACTIONS [FILTER ACTIONS]
        B1[HIGH PASS REMOVES A1] --> B2[LOW PASS REMOVES A5]
        B2 --> B3[NOTCH REMOVES A4]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define baseline wander in ECG. List two of its causes and the standard high-pass cutoff frequency used to remove it.**

**Model Answer:**

Baseline wander is the low-frequency drift component (typically below 0.5 Hz) superimposed on the ECG signal, causing the isoelectric line to deviate from zero. **Two causes:** (i) Respiration — chest expansion alters electrode position, modulating the ECG amplitude. (ii) Body movement / electrode-skin impedance changes due to perspiration. The **standard high-pass cutoff frequency** used in clinical practice is **0.5 Hz** (as recommended by the American Heart Association AHA), which preserves the P and T waves while removing the respiratory drift.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is a notch filter? Why is it preferred over a low-pass filter for removing 50 Hz powerline interference from an ECG signal?**

**Model Answer:**

A notch filter is a **band-stop filter** with a very narrow stopband centered on a specific frequency (e.g., 50 Hz) and unity gain elsewhere. It is **preferred over a low-pass filter** because the QRS complex in ECG contains energy up to approximately 40 Hz, and the P/T waves extend to about 10–15 Hz. A low-pass filter with cutoff below 50 Hz would severely distort the diagnostic morphology of the QRS. In contrast, a notch filter provides high attenuation (typically > 40 dB) **only at 50 Hz**, leaving 40 Hz and 60 Hz (the diagnostic band edges) intact, thereby preserving the QRS complex while eliminating the powerline interference.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** the *order* of the filter with the *type* (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic). State the type explicitly.
> - **Always specify units** — frequencies must be in Hz and angular frequencies in rad/s. Marks are deducted for unit ambiguity.
> - For 14-mark design questions, the KTU scheme requires **(i) specification table, (ii) transfer function, (iii) frequency response sketch, (iv) justification of design choices.** Missing any one of these forfeits 2–3 marks.
> - Do not write the moving average as `|x|`-like notation; use the explicit summation form $y[n] = \frac{1}{M}\sum x[n-k]$.
> - For the median filter, **mention that it is a non-linear operator** and **justify the window length** in milliseconds (e.g., 200 ms > QRS width).

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the different sources of noise in an ECG signal. For each noise source, specify its frequency range and the appropriate filter to remove it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a second-order IIR notch filter to remove 50 Hz powerline interference from an ECG signal sampled at $F_s = 250$ Hz. The desired 3-dB notch bandwidth is 4 Hz. Compute the filter coefficients and write its transfer function.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sources of Noise in ECG:**

| Noise Source | Frequency Range | Origin | Appropriate Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Baseline wander | < 0.5 Hz | Respiration, motion | High-pass filter (0.5 Hz) or median filter |
| Powerline interference | 50 Hz (and harmonics 100, 150 Hz) | Electromagnetic coupling to mains | IIR Notch filter at 50 Hz |
| EMG crosstalk | > 20 Hz (broadband) | Adjacent muscle activity | Low-pass filter (< 40 Hz) |
| Thermal/instrumentation | Broadband (white) | Electronic components, ADC | Low-pass filter |
| Motion artifact | 0.1 to 10 Hz transient | Electrode displacement | Adaptive filter / median filter / wavelet |

**Block diagram of the noise coupling:**

$$
x_{\text{raw}}(t) = s_{\text{ecg}}(t) + n_{\text{baseline}}(t) + n_{\text{PLI}}(t) + n_{\text{EMG}}(t)
$$

The recorded ECG is therefore a sum of the clean cardiac signal $s_{\text{ecg}}(t)$ and three additive noise sources, each occupying a distinct spectral band. This separation in frequency justifies a **multi-stage filter cascade**:
1. **Stage 1:** High-pass at 0.5 Hz → removes baseline wander.
2. **Stage 2:** Low-pass at 40 Hz → removes EMG and thermal noise.
3. **Stage 3:** Notch at 50 Hz → removes PLI.

The order matters: high-pass first prevents baseline saturation of the ADC in subsequent stages; notch must be applied carefully to avoid distorting the QRS tail.

**Part (b) — Notch Filter Design:**

**Step 1 — Specification:**
- Notch frequency: $f_0 = 50$ Hz
- Sampling rate: $F_s = 250$ Hz
- 3-dB bandwidth: $B = 4$ Hz

**Step 2 — Digital Notch Frequency:**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{F_s} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{250} = \frac{2\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample}
$$

Therefore, $\cos\omega_0 = \cos(2\pi/5) \approx 0.3090$.

**Step 3 — Pole Radius from Bandwidth:**

$$
r = 1 - \frac{\pi B}{F_s} = 1 - \frac{\pi \times 4}{250} = 1 - 0.0503 = 0.9497
$$

**Step 4 — Transfer Function:**

$$
H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Coefficients:**

$$
b_0 = 1, \quad b_1 = -2\cos\omega_0 = -0.6180, \quad b_2 = 1
$$

$$
a_1 = 2r\cos\omega_0 = 2 \times 0.9497 \times 0.3090 = 0.5870
$$

$$
a_2 = r^2 = (0.9497)^2 = 0.9019
$$

**Step 6 — Final Difference Equation:**

$$
y[n] = 0.5870\, y[n-1] - 0.9019\, y[n-2] + x[n] - 0.6180\, x[n-1] + x[n-2]
$$

**Step 7 — Verification:** At $\omega = \omega_0$, $|H(e^{j\omega_0})| \approx 0$ (infinite attenuation), and at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi$, $|H| = 1$ (unity gain). The filter is stable since $r = 0.9497 < 1$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the cascaded median filter technique for ECG baseline wander removal. Justify the choice of 200 ms and 600 ms window lengths with reference to the physiological duration of the QRS complex and the respiratory cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An EEG signal is sampled at $F_s = 256$ Hz and is contaminated by 60 Hz powerline interference. Design a first-order IIR notch filter using the transfer function:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-2}}{1 - r z^{-2}}
$$

Choose an appropriate value of $r$ (with $0.9 \le r \le 0.99$) and justify. Plot (describe) the pole-zero pattern.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Cascaded Median Filter:**

The median filter is a **non-linear spatial filter** that replaces the center sample of a sliding window with the middle value of the sorted samples in that window. Unlike linear filters, it does not introduce phase distortion or ringing.

**Why 200 ms?** The QRS complex of a normal human ECG has a duration of **80 to 120 ms** (it can extend up to 200 ms in pathological cases). A 200 ms median filter replaces every sample in any 200 ms interval with the median of that interval. Since the QRS is at most 200 ms wide, the median filter **completely eliminates the QRS** from its output, leaving only the baseline drift and any features wider than 200 ms (e.g., T waves may be partially preserved, but on average they are smoothed out by the cascaded second stage).

**Why 600 ms?** The 600 ms median is applied to the output of the 200 ms median to **smooth out the sharp transitions** introduced at the QRS boundaries. The 600 ms window is also chosen to be **much shorter than the respiratory period** (typically 3 to 5 seconds), ensuring the baseline drift is preserved. After this cascade, the output is a clean estimate of the baseline drift:

$$
\hat{b}[n] = M_{600}\!\left(M_{200}(x[n])\right)
$$

**Final corrected signal:**

$$
y[n] = x[n] - \hat{b}[n]
$$

**Advantages over linear high-pass filtering:**
1. The QRS morphology is **perfectly preserved** (no ringing).
2. There is **no phase distortion** (zero-phase operation by design).
3. **Transient motion artifacts** that exceed the median are clipped, not smeared.

**Part (b) — Notch Filter Design for EEG:**

**Step 1 — Digital Frequency:**

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{F_s} = \frac{2\pi \times 60}{256} = \frac{15\pi}{32} \text{ rad/sample}
$$

**Step 2 — Design Rationale:** The transfer function $H(z) = (1 - z^{-2}) / (1 - r z^{-2})$ has zeros at $z = \pm 1$ on the unit circle, which correspond to $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi$, **not** at 60 Hz. Hence this is **not the right form** for a 60 Hz notch. (This sub-question tests whether the student can recognize an incorrectly specified prototype.) The correct form for a notch at $\omega_0$ is:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Recomputing for EEG (60 Hz):** $\cos(15\pi/32) \approx 0.0980$. Choosing $r = 0.95$ for a narrow notch:

$$
a_1 = 2r\cos\omega_0 = 2(0.95)(0.0980) \approx 0.1862
$$

$$
a_2 = r^2 = 0.9025
$$

$$
H(z) = \frac{1 - 0.1960 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.1862 z^{-1} + 0.9025 z^{-2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Pole-Zero Pattern:**
- **Zeros:** $z = e^{\pm j\omega_0} = e^{\pm j\,15\pi/32}$ — on the unit circle, close to $z = 1$ but slightly rotated counter-clockwise.
- **Poles:** $z = r e^{\pm j\omega_0} = 0.95\, e^{\pm j\,15\pi/32}$ — inside the unit circle at the same angles as the zeros.
- The pole-zero pairing creates a sharp "notch" exactly at $\omega_0 = 15\pi/32$ rad/sample (i.e., 60 Hz).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Noise in biomedical signals is additive** and is modeled as $x[n] = s[n] + \eta[n] + b[n]$.
- [x] **Baseline wander** is the low-frequency drift (< 0.5 Hz) caused mainly by respiration and motion; it is removed by a **0.5 Hz high-pass filter** or a **cascaded median filter** (200 ms + 600 ms).
- [x] **Powerline interference (50 Hz India / 60 Hz USA)** is removed by a **second-order IIR notch filter** with a pole-radius $r \approx 1 - \pi B / F_s$.
- [x] **EMG and thermal noise** (high-frequency) are removed by a **low-pass filter** with cutoff 40 Hz for ECG; use **Butterworth** for maximal passband flatness.
- [x] **The Butterworth magnitude response** is $\vert H(j\omega)\vert^2 = 1 / (1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N})$ — flat in passband, monotonic roll-off.
- [x] **Moving average filter** has magnitude $\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert = \frac{1}{M}\left\vert \frac{\sin(M\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert$ — zeros at $\omega = 2\pi k/M$.
- [x] **The median filter is non-linear** and preserves sharp edges (QRS), unlike linear high-pass filters.
- [x] **Filter order $N$** is computed from the stopband attenuation and transition band: $N \geq \log_{10}(10^{A_s/10} - 1) / (2 \log_{10}(\omega_s / \omega_c))$.
- [x] **SNR improvement in dB** = $10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{noise, in}} / P_{\text{noise, out}})$.
- [x] **Standard clinical cutoffs (memorize for KTU):** ECG: 0.5–40 Hz bandpass + 50 Hz notch; EEG: 1–40 Hz bandpass + 50 Hz notch; EMG: 20–500 Hz bandpass.
- [x] **Order of filter cascade matters:** apply **high-pass (baseline) first**, then **low-pass (EMG)**, then **notch (PLI)** last.
- [x] **Adaptive filtering (LMS, NLMS, RLS)** is used when a noise **reference signal** is available (e.g., accelerometer for motion artifact in PPG).
- [x] **Phase distortion** is a critical issue with IIR filters; for offline processing, use **zero-phase filtering** (forward-backward filtering) to avoid QRS morphology distortion.
- [x] **Always specify units** (Hz for frequency, rad/s for angular frequency, dB for attenuation) in KTU answers to avoid losing marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feature Extraction - Detection of P and T Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feature Extraction - Detection of P and T Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**P-wave and T-wave detection** is a fundamental stage in the automated analysis of the Electrocardiogram (ECG) signal, falling under the broader discipline of *Feature Extraction* in biomedical signal processing. It involves the localization of the **onset, peak, and offset** of the P-wave and T-wave components of the ECG, which represent atrial and ventricular repolarization respectively.

In the KTU 2024 framework, this topic is studied under Module 3 (Pattern Classification and Diagnostic Decision), where students are expected to understand how low-amplitude, low-frequency waves embedded in a noisy ECG can be isolated and characterized using time-domain, frequency-domain, and time-frequency techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **ECG Wave Hierarchy (Amplitudes & Frequencies)**
> - **P-wave**: Amplitude $\leq$ **0.25 mV**, Duration 80–120 ms, Frequency 0.67–5 Hz
> - **QRS Complex**: Amplitude 1.0–1.5 mV, Frequency 5–40 Hz
> - **T-wave**: Amplitude 0.1–0.5 mV, Duration 160–200 ms, Frequency 0.7–4 Hz
> - **Sampling rate (standard)**: 360 Hz (MIT-BIH) or 500 Hz (modern)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are listening to a busy orchestra (the ECG signal) and trying to isolate the soft sound of the **viola (P-wave)** and **cello (T-wave)** from the dominant **trumpet blast (QRS complex)**. The viola and cello have lower pitch (frequency) and lower volume (amplitude) compared to the trumpet.

To hear them, you would:
1. **Reduce the trumpet's volume** (bandpass filtering to remove QRS energy).
2. **Listen over a longer time interval** (averaging/integration) to enhance the soft tones.
3. **Apply a "selective hearing" filter** (matched filter or wavelet) tuned to the specific timbre of each instrument.

This is precisely what P and T wave detectors do — they **suppress the dominant QRS energy**, **enhance the low-frequency components**, and use **shape- or derivative-based logic** to pinpoint wave boundaries.

## 1.3 Why P and T Wave Detection is Hard

| Challenge | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Low amplitude** | P/T waves are 5–10× smaller than R-wave |
| **Low frequency overlap** | P and T share the 0.5–5 Hz band |
| **Morphological variability** | P and T shapes change with pathology, respiration, lead placement |
| **Baseline wander** | Low-frequency noise masks the slow waves |
| **Noise** | EMG, motion artifacts, 50/60 Hz powerline interference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The course expects students to understand the **mathematical basis** of detection algorithms (derivatives, energy functions, wavelets, matched filters, Hilbert transform) and the **physiological justification** for each step — *not* the implementation of a particular software tool.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Pipeline for P and T Wave Detection

A robust P/T-wave detector follows a structured pipeline. Each step has a clear "**Why**" and "**How**":

### Step 1: Preprocessing (Baseline Removal)
- **Why:** P and T waves sit on a wandering baseline; without correction, peaks shift falsely.
- **How:** Apply a highpass filter with cutoff **$\approx$ 0.5 Hz** (Butterworth order 2) or subtract a moving median.

### Step 2: Bandpass Filtering (QRS Suppression)
- **Why:** The QRS complex is **8–10× larger** in amplitude; it must be attenuated to expose P/T waves.
- **How:** Use a **lowpass filter (≤ 15 Hz)** for P-wave emphasis, or **bandpass 0.5–10 Hz** for T-wave.

### Step 3: Differentiation (Slope-Based Detection)
- **Why:** P and T waves have characteristic slopes different from noise and ST-segment.
- **How:** Apply first or second derivative to locate steepest ascent/descent points.

### Step 4: Nonlinear Transformation (Energy Extraction)
- **Why:** Amplify large slopes and suppress small fluctuations.
- **How:** **Squaring** ($x^2$) or **absolute value** operation.

### Step 5: Moving Window Integration (Smoothing)
- **Why:** Spreads the energy over time to create detectable peaks.
- **How:** Average over a window of **N samples** (e.g., N = 30 at 360 Hz ≈ 83 ms).

### Step 6: Adaptive Thresholding
- **Why:** P/T wave amplitudes vary across patients and leads.
- **How:** Compute a threshold as a fraction (e.g., 0.4–0.6) of the local R-peak energy.

### Step 7: Search Window Logic
- **Why:** P-wave appears **200 ms BEFORE QRS**; T-wave appears **300 ms AFTER QRS** (in normal sinus rhythm).
- **How:** Define **temporal search windows** relative to the detected R-peak.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation / Symbol** | **Expression** | **Purpose / Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| First difference | $y(n) = x(n) - x(n-1)$ | Slope detection |
| Five-point derivative | $y(n) = \dfrac{1}{8}\left[2x(n) + x(n-1) - x(n-2) - 2x(n-3)\right]$ | Reduces low-frequency noise, enhances QRS |
| Squaring function | $y(n) = \left[x(n)\right]^2$ | Nonlinear amplification of large slopes |
| Moving average | $y(n) = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(n-k)$ | Smoothing for energy envelope |
| Pan-Tompkins threshold | $THR_{\text{level2}} = 0.5 \cdot THR_{\text{level1}}$ | Adaptive detection |
| Continuous Wavelet Transform | $CWT(a,b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\psi^{*}\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right)dt$ | Multi-scale analysis of waves |
| Hilbert transform | $\hat{x}(t) = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\,PV\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\dfrac{x(\tau)}{t-\tau}\,d\tau$ | Envelope extraction |
| Envelope (analytic signal) | $A(t) = \sqrt{x^2(t) + \hat{x}^2(t)}$ | P/T amplitude profile |
| Cross-correlation (matched filter) | $R_{xy}(\tau) = \sum_{n} x(n)\,y(n-\tau)$ | Template-based P/T detection |
| Bandpass for T-wave | **0.5 Hz – 10 Hz** (Butterworth 2nd order) | T-wave isolation |
| Bandpass for P-wave | **5 Hz – 15 Hz** (Butterworth 2nd order) | P-wave isolation |
| Standard sampling rate | **$f_s = 360$ Hz or 500 Hz** | Per MIT-BIH / IEC 60601-2-51 |
| Pan-Tompkins window length | **N = 30** (at 360 Hz) | Standard integration window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations power **Holter monitors, ICDs, pacemakers, and AI-based cardiac diagnostics (e.g., Apple Watch ECG, AliveCor KardiaMobile)**. Without robust P/T detection, conditions like **atrial fibrillation, hyperkalemia, Brugada syndrome, and Long-QT syndrome** cannot be diagnosed automatically.

## 2.3 Why These Specific Methods?
