- **Derivative-based methods** are computationally cheap → suitable for **wearable/embedded systems**.
- **Wavelet methods** offer **multi-resolution analysis** → ideal for **morphologically variable** T-waves (e.g., in ischemia).
- **Matched filtering** works well when a **template** of the patient's own P/T morphology is available.
- **Hilbert transform** provides an **instantaneous envelope**, useful for T-wave alternans (TWA) detection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Five-Point Derivative for ECG Slope Detection

The standard first difference $y(n) = x(n) - x(n-1)$ is sensitive to high-frequency noise. To overcome this, the **five-point central derivative** is derived using the Taylor series expansion of $x(n)$ about $n$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider the Taylor expansion of $x(n+k)$ for small $k$:

$$x(n+1) = x(n) + x'(n) + \dfrac{x''(n)}{2!} + \dfrac{x'''(n)}{3!} + O(h^4)$$

$$x(n-1) = x(n) - x'(n) + \dfrac{x''(n)}{2!} - \dfrac{x'''(n)}{3!} + O(h^4)$$

$$x(n+2) = x(n) + 2x'(n) + \dfrac{4x''(n)}{2!} + \dfrac{8x'''(n)}{3!} + O(h^4)$$

$$x(n-2) = x(n) - 2x'(n) + \dfrac{4x''(n)}{2!} - \dfrac{8x'''(n)}{3!} + O(h^4)$$

**Subtract symmetric pairs to eliminate even-order terms:**

$$x(n+1) - x(n-1) = 2x'(n) + \dfrac{x'''(n)}{3} + O(h^4)$$

$$x(n+2) - x(n-2) = 4x'(n) + \dfrac{4x'''(n)}{3} + O(h^4)$$

**Combine with weights (1 and -2) to cancel the $x'''(n)$ term:**

$$2[x(n+1) - x(n-1)] - [x(n+2) - x(n-2)] = 4x'(n) - 4x'(n) = 0 \quad \text{(gives 0, not useful)}$$

**Recompute with weights $(1, -8, 0, 8, -1)$ from the standard 5-point stencil:**

$$y(n) = \dfrac{1}{8}\Big[-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)\Big]$$

This is the **forward-looking** Pan-Tompkins five-point derivative. It approximates $x'(n)$ while **suppressing low-frequency baseline wander** (differentiator with a highpass notch effect) and **attenuating 50/60 Hz interference**.

> **Conversion Logic:** The coefficients $[-1, -2, 0, 2, 1] / 8$ are obtained by solving a linear system that forces zero gain at DC (eliminates baseline) and unity gain near 15–25 Hz (where QRS energy lies).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Moving Window Integration (MWI) Energy

After squaring, the signal becomes positive. The MWI computes a **local energy estimate** over a window of N samples.

$$y(n) = \dfrac{1}{N}\left[x^2(n) + x^2(n-1) + \dots + x^2(n-N+1)\right]$$

**Choosing N (window length):**
- For QRS detection: $N \approx 0.12 \cdot f_s$ (i.e., 150 ms window).
- For P/T detection: $N \approx 0.20 \cdot f_s$ (longer window, ~200 ms) to capture slow waves.
- At $f_s = 360$ Hz, **N = 72** is typical for T-wave energy integration.

> **Conversion Logic:** The MWI converts **spike-like QRS energy** into a **smooth hump**, while **broader P/T waves** produce **lower, wider bumps**. This is the basis for distinguishing P, QRS, and T from a single energy waveform.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hilbert Transform for T-Wave Envelope

The **analytic signal** $z(t)$ of a real signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$z(t) = x(t) + j\hat{x}(t)$$

where $\hat{x}(t)$ is the Hilbert transform:

$$\hat{x}(t) = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\,PV\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\dfrac{x(\tau)}{t-\tau}\,d\tau$$

**The instantaneous envelope (amplitude) is:**

$$A(t) = \vert z(t) \vert = \sqrt{x^2(t) + \hat{x}^2(t)}$$

**The instantaneous phase is:**

$$\phi(t) = \arctan\!\left[\dfrac{\hat{x}(t)}{x(t)}\right]$$

**The instantaneous frequency is:**

$$f(t) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\dfrac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$$

### Application to T-Wave Detection
- The T-wave is a **broad, smooth oscillation** in the 0.5–4 Hz band.
- Applying the Hilbert transform to a **bandpass-filtered ECG (0.5–10 Hz)** yields a smooth envelope $A(t)$ that has a clear maximum aligned with the T-wave peak.
- This is widely used in **T-wave alternans (TWA) detection** — alternation in $A(t)$ between beats indicates repolarization instability (a precursor to arrhythmia).

> **Conversion Logic:** The Hilbert transform is a **90° phase shifter**. By adding it in quadrature to $x(t)$, we obtain a **complex signal** whose magnitude is the **true instantaneous amplitude**, immune to the sign changes of the original T-wave.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) for P-Wave Localization

The CWT at scale $a$ and translation $b$ is:

$$C(a,b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\psi^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right)dt$$

where $\psi(t)$ is the **mother wavelet** (commonly **Mexican hat** or **Daubechies-4 (db4)** for ECG).

### Modulus Maxima Method (Mallat, 1992)
A P-wave peak corresponds to a **modulus maximum line** across adjacent scales in the CWT. Detection rule:

1. Compute $C(a,b)$ for scales $a = 2^1, 2^2, 2^3, 2^4$.
2. Find local maxima in $b$ for each $a$.
3. A wave peak exists if maxima **propagate** from coarse to fine scales.
4. The **zero-crossing** of $C(a,b)$ between two opposite-sign maxima gives the **wave boundary**.

### Scale-to-Frequency Mapping (Mexican Hat Wavelet)

$$f_{\text{central}} = \dfrac{f_c}{a \cdot \Delta t}$$

where $f_c \approx 0.25$ Hz is the center frequency of the Mexican hat. For a 50 Hz P-wave component detected at scale $a = 8$ with $\Delta t = 1/360$:

$$f_{\text{central}} = \dfrac{0.25}{8 \cdot (1/360)} = \dfrac{0.25 \cdot 360}{8} = 11.25 \text{ Hz}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Small scales $a$ → high frequencies (QRS). Large scales $a$ → low frequencies (P/T waves). The CWT acts as a **mathematical microscope** with **zoom-in/zoom-out** capability.

## 3.5 Summary Table: Which Method When?

| **Method** | **Best For** | **Limitation** | **Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|
| First derivative | Quick QRS detection | Noise-sensitive | **Low** |
| Pan-Tompkins (5-pt) | QRS + coarse P/T | Needs clean signal | **Low** |
| Squaring + MWI | Energy envelope | Loses phase info | **Low** |
| Hilbert envelope | T-wave amplitude, TWA | Boundary artifacts | **Medium** |
| CWT (modulus maxima) | P/T onset, offset, peak | Computationally heavy | **High** |
| Matched filter | Single-lead clean ECG | Needs patient-specific template | **Medium** |
| Phasor transform | Robust to noise | Recent, less standard | **Medium** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram: P and T Wave Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ECG Signal x n] --> B[Baseline Wander Removal Highpass 0.5 Hz]
    B --> C[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 10 Hz for T or 5 to 15 Hz for P]
    C --> D[Five Point Derivative Slope Enhancement]
    D --> E[Squaring Function x squared]
    E --> F[Moving Window Integration N equals 72 at 360 Hz]
    F --> G[Adaptive Threshold Block]
    G --> H{Is peak within search window?}
    H -->|P window 200 ms before R| I[P wave Detected]
    H -->|T window 300 ms after R| J[T wave Detected]
    H -->|Outside search window| K[Discard as Noise]
    I --> L[Feature Vector Onset Peak Offset Amplitude Duration]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Output to Classifier KTU Module 4]
```

## 4.2 CWT-Based Detection Architecture (P-Wave)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ECG Lead II Input] --> B[Preprocessing Median Filter M equals 200 ms]
    B --> C[CWT Decomposition db4 wavelet scales 2 4 8 16 32]
    C --> D[Modulus Maxima Detection across scales]
    D --> E[Zero Crossing Analysis Onset and Offset]
    E --> F{Temporal Location Check}
    F -->|Before QRS 80 to 200 ms| G[P wave Peak Located]
    F -->|After QRS 80 to 400 ms| H[T wave Peak Located]
    G --> I[P wave Features Output]
    H --> J[T wave Features Output]
```

## 4.3 Adaptive Threshold Decision Logic (Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize SPK equals 0 and NPK equals 0] --> B[Read MWI output y n]
    B --> C{y n greater than THR level2}
    C -->|Yes| D[R peak candidate]
    D --> E{SPK is initialized}
    E -->|No| F[Set SPK equals y n and THR1 equals 0.4 x SPK]
    E -->|Yes| G[Update SPK equals 0.125 x y n + 0.875 x SPK]
    G --> H[Set THR1 equals NPK + 0.25 x SPK minus NPK]
    F --> I[Continue search]
    H --> I
    C -->|No| J{y n between THR1 and THR2}
    J -->|Yes| K[Search back for local maximum in 200 ms window]
    K --> L[Mark as QRS peak]
    L --> M[Open P search window 250 ms before R and T window 350 ms after R]
    M --> N[Apply lowpass 10 Hz and repeat derivative to detect P and T]
    J -->|No| O[Update NPK]
    O --> P[Recompute THR2 equals NPK + 0.5 x SPK minus NPK]
    P --> B
```

## 4.4 Wave Morphology Annotation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Beat Start] --> B[P wave Onset]
    B --> C[P wave Peak]
    C --> D[P wave Offset]
    D --> E[PR Segment]
    E --> F[Q wave]
    F --> G[R wave Peak]
    G --> H[S wave]
    H --> I[J point ST Segment Start]
    I --> J[ST Segment Middle]
    J --> K[T wave Onset]
    K --> L[T wave Peak]
    L --> M[T wave Offset]
    M --> N[Beat End]
    style C fill:#ffe5e5
    style L fill:#ffe5e5
    style G fill:#e5f5ff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Schematics:** Red-filled nodes (P-peak, T-peak) are the **detection targets** in this module. The blue-filled R-peak is the **reference anchor** from which search windows are defined.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1.iii]**
Define the P-wave and T-wave. State their typical amplitude, duration, and clinical significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **P-wave** (1 mark): Represents **atrial depolarization**. Typical amplitude **≤ 0.25 mV**, duration **80–120 ms**. Clinical significance: its absence or abnormal shape indicates atrial fibrillation, atrial enlargement, or ectopic atrial rhythm.
- **T-wave** (1 mark): Represents **ventricular repolarization**. Typical amplitude **0.1–0.5 mV**, duration **160–200 ms**. Clinical significance: T-wave inversion suggests myocardial ischemia; tall peaked T-waves suggest hyperkalemia; flattened T-waves suggest hypokalemia.
- **Distinguishing factor (1 mark):** P-wave precedes QRS by ~160 ms (PR interval); T-wave follows QRS by ~300 ms (ST-T segment). P-wave is higher in frequency content (5–15 Hz) compared to T-wave (0.7–4 Hz).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Q1.v]**
Why is P-wave detection more challenging than QRS detection in automated ECG analysis?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Low amplitude (1 mark):** P-wave amplitude is **5–10× smaller** than the R-wave, making it easily masked.
- **Low frequency overlap (1 mark):** P-wave (0.67–5 Hz) overlaps with baseline wander, T-wave, and motion artifacts.
- **Morphological variability (1 mark):** P-wave shape varies between patients and even between beats in conditions like atrial fibrillation, where P-wave is replaced by fibrillatory waves.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Module 3 — Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Q8.a-b]**

**(a)** Derive the five-point derivative filter used in the Pan-Tompkins algorithm and explain how it enhances the QRS complex while suppressing baseline wander and high-frequency noise. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the role of squaring and moving window integration (MWI) in P and T wave detection. For an ECG sampled at $f_s = 360$ Hz, justify the choice of window length $N = 30$ for QRS and $N = 72$ for T-wave energy estimation. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Five-Point Derivative (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The first difference operator $y(n) = x(n) - x(n-1)$ is sensitive to high-frequency noise and low-frequency baseline wander.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Taylor series expansions of $x(n+k)$ for $k = \pm 1, \pm 2$:

$$x(n+1) = x(n) + x'(n) + \dfrac{x''(n)}{2} + \dfrac{x'''(n)}{6} + O(h^4)$$

$$x(n-1) = x(n) - x'(n) + \dfrac{x''(n)}{2} - \dfrac{x'''(n)}{6} + O(h^4)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Construct the linear combination to suppress DC and the first two even-order derivatives while emphasizing the slope $x'(n)$:

$$y(n) = \dfrac{1}{8}\Big[2x(n) + x(n-1) - x(n-2) - 2x(n-3)\Big]$$

This is the **standard Pan-Tompkins five-point derivative** (using samples at $n, n-1, n-2, n-3$ — actually, depending on the version, this is the 4-tap form; the canonical version uses 5 taps as derived in Section 3.1).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Frequency response: This filter has **zero gain at DC** (suppresses baseline wander) and **unity gain near 15–25 Hz** (passes QRS energy). The 50/60 Hz notch effect is achieved by proper choice of coefficients.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The output $y(n)$ is proportional to the slope of the ECG. The R-wave (steepest slope) produces the largest output; the gentler P and T waves produce smaller peaks. This is the foundation for subsequent energy-based detection.

#### Part (b) — Squaring and MWI (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Squaring $y(n) \to y^2(n)$:
- Makes the signal **strictly positive** (rectifying the slope).
- **Amplifies large values** nonlinearly (squared → emphasizes QRS).
- Preserves sign information lost in absolute value.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Mathematical form of MWI:

$$z(n) = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y^2(n-k)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** For QRS detection at $f_s = 360$ Hz:
- Typical QRS duration = 100 ms.
- $N = 0.10 \times 360 = 36 \approx 30$ samples.
- Window $N = 30$ covers **~83 ms**, sufficient to capture QRS energy but short enough to prevent merging with T-wave.
- If $N$ is too large (e.g., 100), QRS and T-wave energies merge → false detection.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** For T-wave detection:
- Typical T-wave duration = 160–200 ms.
- $N = 0.20 \times 360 = 72$ samples.
- This **~200 ms window** captures the full T-wave energy, allowing the threshold to detect the broader, lower T-wave hump.
- For P-wave: similar logic, $N = 50$–$60$ samples (140–170 ms) is typical.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The MWI output $z(n)$ is then compared with an adaptive threshold:

$$THR = NPK + 0.5 \cdot (SPK - NPK)$$

where SPK and NPK are running estimates of signal and noise peak levels. This is the **Pan-Tompkins adaptive thresholding** that enables robust detection across varying signal amplitudes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary, Q8.a-b]**

**(a)** Explain the use of the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) for the detection of P and T waves. Derive the relationship between wavelet scale and frequency, and state the choice of mother wavelet for ECG analysis. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the matched filter approach for T-wave detection. What is the cross-correlation function and how is it used to localize the T-wave peak? List two limitations of the matched filter method. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — CWT for P and T Wave Detection (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Definition of CWT:

$$C(a,b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\psi^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right)dt$$

where $a$ = scale, $b$ = translation, $\psi$ = mother wavelet.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Scale-frequency relationship for the Mexican hat wavelet (the 2nd derivative of a Gaussian):

$$f_{\text{central}} = \dfrac{f_c}{a \cdot \Delta t}$$

with $f_c \approx 0.25$ Hz (center frequency of Mexican hat).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Numerical example: at $a = 8$ and $\Delta t = 1/360$ s, $f_{\text{central}} = \dfrac{0.25 \cdot 360}{8} = 11.25$ Hz — covers the P-wave band.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Choice of mother wavelet: **Daubechies-4 (db4)** is most commonly used for ECG because:
- It is **compactly supported** (good time localization).
- It is **approximately symmetric** (preserves wave shape).
- It has **vanishing moments** that suppress polynomial trends (baseline wander).

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Modulus maxima method for P/T wave localization:
- A P-wave peak corresponds to a **pair of opposite-sign modulus maxima** (one for the rising edge, one for the falling edge).
- The **zero-crossing** of $C(a,b)$ between them gives the **wave peak**.
- At **scales $a = 2^1$ to $2^3$**, the QRS is dominant; at **$a = 2^4$ to $2^5$**, P and T waves become prominent because their energy is concentrated at lower frequencies (longer durations).

#### Part (b) — Matched Filter for T-Wave Detection (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The matched filter correlates the incoming ECG with a **template** $h(n)$ of the expected T-wave shape:

$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\,y(n-\tau)$$

or in continuous form:

$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,y(t-\tau)\,dt$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** When the template $h(n)$ matches the T-wave in $x(n)$, the correlation output $R_{xh}(\tau)$ reaches a **maximum** at the lag $\tau$ corresponding to the T-wave position. This maximum is the **detection statistic**.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Template construction: A patient's T-wave template is typically built by **averaging T-waves from 5–10 consecutive normal beats** (coherent averaging reduces noise by $\sqrt{N}$).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The peak of $R_{xh}(\tau)$ in the post-R search window (200–400 ms after R) gives the T-wave peak location. The width of $R_{xh}(\tau)$ at half-maximum gives the **T-wave duration**.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Limitations of matched filter:
- **Template dependence:** Requires a representative template; morphologically variable T-waves (e.g., in ischemia) cause mismatch and reduced correlation.
- **Patient-specificity:** A template from one patient cannot be used for another; cross-patient templates perform poorly.
- **Computational cost** (1 mark for either): $O(NM)$ for $N$ samples and $M$ lags — heavier than derivative-based methods.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing P and T wave polarity:** In standard Lead II, both P and T are normally **upright** (positive). In lead aVR, both are **inverted**. Many students write "P is positive, T is negative" — this is **wrong** for most leads. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Mixing up window lengths:** Pan-Tompkins $N = 30$ is for **QRS**, not P/T. Writing $N = 30$ for T-wave detection is a common error. (Lose 0.5–1 mark.)
> 3. **Forgetting the scale factor $1/\sqrt{a}$ in CWT:** This is required for **energy normalization** across scales. Omitting it gives biased coefficients.
> 4. **Writing the matched filter output as a peak without the lag variable $\tau$:** The correlation function is a function of $\tau$ — students often write "correlation = maximum" without specifying **at which lag**.
> 5. **Using 50 Hz for powerline interference in India:** The correct value is **50 Hz** (not 60 Hz). Examiners in Kerala specifically mark this.
> 6. **Confusing PR interval (160 ms) with P-wave duration (100 ms):** The PR interval includes the AV nodal delay.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Fire Revision Checklist

- **P-wave**: Atrial depolarization, 80–120 ms, ≤ 0.25 mV, frequency 0.67–5 Hz. **Precedes QRS by 120–200 ms**.
- **T-wave**: Ventricular repolarization, 160–200 ms, 0.1–0.5 mV, frequency 0.7–4 Hz. **Follows QRS by 200–400 ms**.
- **P-wave search window**: 80–250 ms **before** the detected R-peak.
- **T-wave search window**: 200–400 ms **after** the detected R-peak.
- **Pan-Tompkins pipeline** (5 steps): **Bandpass → Derivative → Squaring → MWI → Adaptive Threshold**.
- **Five-point derivative**: $y(n) = \dfrac{1}{8}\left[2x(n) + x(n-1) - x(n-2) - 2x(n-3)\right]$.
- **MWI for QRS**: $N = 30$ (at 360 Hz) → **83 ms window**.
- **MWI for T-wave**: $N = 72$ (at 360 Hz) → **200 ms window**.
- **CWT scale-to-frequency**: $f_{\text{central}} = \dfrac{f_c}{a \cdot \Delta t}$. Higher $a$ = lower frequency (slower waves).
- **Preferred mother wavelet for ECG**: **db4 (Daubechies-4)** or **Mexican hat (Ricker)**.
- **Hilbert envelope**: $A(t) = \sqrt{x^2(t) + \hat{x}^2(t)}$ — used for **T-wave alternans** detection.
- **Matched filter peak** = T-wave location; correlation maximum gives **best alignment** with template.
- **Standard sampling rate**: **360 Hz** (MIT-BIH) or **500 Hz** (modern IEC standard).
- **Powerline interference in India**: **50 Hz** (not 60 Hz).
- **Lowest amplitude wave = P-wave** (5–10× smaller than R-wave).
- **Adaptive threshold formula**: $THR = NPK + 0.5(SPK - NPK)$.
- **Clinical flags from P/T morphology**:
  - Inverted T → ischemia
  - Tall peaked T → hyperkalemia
  - Absent P → atrial fibrillation
  - Wide notched P → atrial enlargement

### Key Takeaway for KTU 2024 Exam

The detection of P and T waves is fundamentally a **low-amplitude, low-frequency feature extraction** problem. Master three mathematical tools — **(1) derivatives, (2) energy functions, and (3) time-frequency representations (CWT/Hilbert)** — and you can answer **any** KTU question on this topic. Always relate the math to the **physiology** (P = atrial, T = ventricular repolarization) to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heart Rate Variability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heart Rate Variability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** is the physiological phenomenon of variation in the time interval between consecutive heartbeats, specifically measured as the variation in the **R-R intervals** (inter-beat intervals, IBI) of the **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** signal. It quantifies the autonomic nervous system's (ANS) regulatory influence on the sinoatrial (SA) node, providing a non-invasive marker of cardiac autonomic modulation.

Formally, given a sequence of successive R-peak fiducial points $\{R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_N\}$ detected from an ECG recording, the **R-R tachogram** is constructed as:

$$RR_i = R_{i+1} - R_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

The instantaneous heart rate is then given by:

$$HR_i = \frac{60}{RR_i} \quad [\text{beats per minute, bpm}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Measurement Units:**
> - **R-R Interval:** milliseconds (ms)
> - **Heart Rate:** beats per minute (bpm)
> - **Recording Duration Standards:** **Short-term** (5 min — clinical standard), **Long-term** (24 h — Holter-based)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart's Jazz Solo"

Imagine your heart is not a metronome (rigid, fixed tempo) but rather a **Jazz drummer**. A metronome beats at exactly 60 BPM constantly — boring, predictable, and mechanical. A jazz drummer, however, **varies** the timing between beats slightly, responding to the music's mood, the audience, and the band. That variation is *not a flaw* — it is **skill, responsiveness, and adaptability**.

- A **healthy, well-conditioned heart** = the *expert jazz drummer*. It speeds up when you inhale, slows when you exhale, accelerates under stress, and recovers quickly after exercise. It is **flexible**.
- An **unhealthy, rigid heart** = a *broken metronome stuck at 75 BPM*. It cannot adapt. It pumps at a constant rate regardless of demand, which is a sign of poor autonomic regulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-Intuitive Truth:** Higher HRV is GOOD. Lower HRV is BAD (associated with mortality post-MI, diabetic neuropathy, and chronic stress). Athletes have notably high HRV.

---

## 1.3 Physiological Origin of HRV

HRV originates from the continuous tug-of-war between the two branches of the autonomic nervous system (ANS):

| Branch | Neurotransmitter | Effect on HRV | Frequency Band |
|--------|------------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Sympathetic Nervous System (SNS)** | Norepinephrine | Accelerates heart rate (decreases R-R) | **LF (0.04–0.15 Hz)** |
| **Parasympathetic Nervous System (PNS) / Vagus** | Acetylcholine | Decelerates heart rate (increases R-R) | **HF (0.15–0.4 Hz)** |

The instantaneous balance of these two opposing signals, modulated by respiration, baroreflexes, thermoregulation, and renin-angiotensin activity, **writes the variability pattern** into the R-R interval sequence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of HRV Analysis

HRV is analyzed using **three principal methodological domains**, each capturing a different physiological dimension.

### 2.1.1 Pillar I — Time-Domain Analysis

Statistical descriptors computed directly on the R-R interval series. Simple, robust, and widely used in clinical cardiology.

**Key Statistical Metrics:**

1. **Mean R-R interval (MeanRR):**
The arithmetic mean of all normal-to-normal (NN) intervals.

$$\overline{RR} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} RR_i$$

2. **SDNN — Standard Deviation of NN intervals:**
Reflects **all** cyclic components responsible for variability in the recording period. A "global" HRV index.

$$SDNN = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\left(RR_i - \overline{RR}\right)^2}$$

3. **SDSD — Standard Deviation of Successive Differences:**
Measures beat-to-beat variability.

$$SDSD = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-2}\sum_{i=1}^{N-2}\left(RR_{i+1} - RR_i\right)^2}$$

4. **RMSSD — Root Mean Square of Successive Differences:**
**The gold standard** for short-term, high-frequency (parasympathetic) variation.

$$RMSSD = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-2}\sum_{i=1}^{N-2}\left(RR_{i+1} - RR_i\right)^2}$$

5. **pNN50 — Percentage of successive NN intervals differing by more than 50 ms:**
Pure vagal tone marker.

$$pNN50 = \frac{\text{count}\{\vert RR_{i+1} - RR_i \vert > 50\,\text{ms}\}}{N-2} \times 100\%$$

6. **Geometric Indices:** HRV Triangular Index, TINN — derived from the histogram of R-R intervals.

---

### 2.1.2 Pillar II — Frequency-Domain (Power Spectral Density) Analysis

Decomposes the R-R tachogram into its constituent frequency components, mapping them to **specific autonomic branches**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pre-processing Rule:** The R-R tachogram is a *non-uniformly sampled* signal. It MUST be converted into an evenly time-sampled signal via **interpolation** (cubic spline, linear) or **Lomb-Scargle Periodogram** before FFT.

**Spectral Estimation Methods:**

- **Non-parametric:** Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) — applied to the interpolated tachogram.
- **Parametric:** Autoregressive (AR) modeling — provides smoother PSD with better frequency resolution for short records.

**Power Spectral Density Bands (Task Force, 1996):**

| Band | Frequency Range | Physiological Correlate | Marker Of |
|------|----------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **VLF** (Very Low Frequency) | 0.0033 – 0.04 Hz | Thermoregulation, renin-angiotensin, slow humoral mechanisms | Long-term regulation |
| **LF** (Low Frequency) | 0.04 – 0.15 Hz | Baroreflex, sympathetic + parasympathetic mix | Mixed autonomic (Baroreceptor Sensitivity) |
| **HF** (High Frequency) | 0.15 – 0.4 Hz | Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA) | **Parasympathetic (Vagal) tone** |
| **LF/HF Ratio** | — | Sympathovagal Balance | Stress/Recovery Index |

**Total Power** (TP) = VLF + LF + HF, integrated over the 5-min recording (units: **ms²**).

---

### 2.1.3 Pillar III — Non-Linear (Geometric / Dynamical) Analysis

Recognizes that the R-R series is the output of a **non-linear, chaotic** system. Two pivotal techniques:

1. **Poincaré Plot (Return Map):**
A scatter plot of $RR_{i+1}$ vs $RR_i$. The shape of this "cloud" carries diagnostic information.

2. **SD1 and SD2 — Ellipse-fitting descriptors:**
Derived from the Poincaré plot, representing the ellipse's minor and major semi-axes.

$$SD1 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\,SDSD^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot SDSD$$

$$SD2 = \sqrt{2 \cdot SDNN^2 - \frac{1}{2}\,SDSD^2}$$

**SD1** captures short-term, beat-to-beat variability (correlates strongly with RMSSD, vagal).
**SD2** captures long-term variability (correlates with SDNN).

3. **Sample Entropy (SampEn):** Quantifies the irregularity/predictability of the R-R series.
4. **Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA):** Yields scaling exponents $\alpha_1$ (short-term) and $\alpha_2$ (long-term).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table for rapid revision before the KTU ESE. Note the deliberate use of `\vert` for absolute values to prevent table corruption.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physiological Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | Mean RR | $\overline{RR} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum RR_i$ | ms | Average cardiac cycle length |
| 2 | Mean HR | $\overline{HR} = \frac{60}{\overline{RR}}$ | bpm | Average heart rate |
| 3 | SDNN | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum(RR_i-\overline{RR})^2}$ | ms | Global HRV |
| 4 | SDSD | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-2}\sum(RR_{i+1}-RR_i)^2}$ | ms | Beat-to-beat variability |
| 5 | RMSSD | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-2}\sum(RR_{i+1}-RR_i)^2}$ | ms | Short-term vagal HRV |
| 6 | pNN50 | $\frac{\#\{\vert RR_{i+1}-RR_i\vert > 50\}}{N-2}\cdot 100$ | % | Vagal tone |
| 7 | Total Power | $\int_{0.0033}^{0.4} PSD(f)\,df$ | ms² | Total spectral power |
| 8 | LF Power | $\int_{0.04}^{0.15} PSD(f)\,df$ | ms² | Mixed autonomic |
| 9 | HF Power | $\int_{0.15}^{0.4} PSD(f)\,df$ | ms² | Parasympathetic |
| 10 | LF/HF Ratio | $LF / HF$ | dimensionless | Sympathovagal balance |
| 11 | SD1 | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\text{Var}(RR_{i+1}-RR_i)}$ | ms | Short-term variability |
| 12 | SD2 | $\sqrt{2\,SDNN^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}SDSD^2}$ | ms | Long-term variability |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Diagnostics:** Predictor of mortality after myocardial infarction, severity of diabetic autonomic neuropathy.
- **Stress & Fatigue Monitoring:** Wearables (Apple Watch, Fitbit) expose RMSSD and SDNN to consumers.
- **Sports Physiology:** HRV-guided training — athletes avoid high-intensity training on days of low RMSSD.
- **Mental Health:** Reduced HRV is correlated with PTSD, anxiety, and depression.
- **Drug Safety Pharmacology:** HRV is a biomarker for assessing drug-induced autonomic effects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between SD1, SD2, SDNN, and SDSD

The Poincaré plot rotates the coordinate system by 45°. The line $y = x$ becomes the new longitudinal axis, and the perpendicular line $y = -x$ becomes the new transverse axis. Let us derive SD1 and SD2 rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define the Successive Difference

$$d_i = RR_{i+1} - RR_i$$

Its variance is:

$$\text{Var}(d) = \frac{1}{N-2}\sum_{i=1}^{N-2}(d_i - \bar{d})^2 = SDSD^2$$

### Step 2 — SD1 (Width of the Ellipse along the perpendicular axis)

The short-axis projection of the Poincaré cloud is the standard deviation of the perpendicular distance of each point from the $y = x$ line. By linear algebra, this projection equals $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ times the standard deviation of $d_i$. Thus:

$$SD1 = \frac{SDSD}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Expanding:

$$SD1 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\,SDSD^2}$$

### Step 3 — SD2 (Length of the Ellipse along the longitudinal axis)

The long-axis projection of the Poincaré cloud is related to both $SDNN$ and $SDSD$ via the following identity (Brennan et al., 2001):

$$SD2 = \sqrt{2\,SDNN^2 - \frac{1}{2}\,SDSD^2}$$

**Derivation Outline:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{RR_{i+1} + RR_i}{2}\right) &= \frac{1}{2}\left[\text{Var}(RR_{i+1}) + \text{Var}(RR_i) + 2\,\text{Cov}(RR_{i+1},RR_i)\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\left[2\,SDNN^2 + 2\,(\text{Cov})\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The covariance between $RR_{i+1}$ and $RR_i$ can be expressed using the identity:

$$\text{Cov}(RR_{i+1}, RR_i) = SDNN^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}SDSD^2$$

Substituting back:

$$SD2^2 = 2\,SDNN^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}SDSD^2$$

This confirms the formula in the KTU cheat sheet.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Calibrated Problem

> **Problem:** Five successive R-R intervals are recorded as: **800, 820, 790, 810, 805 ms**. Compute (a) Mean HR, (b) SDNN, (c) RMSSD, (d) pNN50.

### (a) Mean R-R and Mean HR

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{RR} &= \frac{800 + 820 + 790 + 810 + 805}{5} \\
&= \frac{4025}{5} = 805 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\overline{HR} &= \frac{60\,000 \text{ ms/min}}{805 \text{ ms}} = 74.53 \text{ bpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) SDNN

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviations:} &\quad -5,\; +15,\; -15,\; +5,\; 0 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Squared:} &\quad 25,\; 225,\; 225,\; 25,\; 0 \\
\text{Sum:} &\quad 500 \\
SDNN &= \sqrt{\frac{500}{4}} = \sqrt{125} \approx 11.18 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (c) RMSSD

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Successive Differences:} &\quad 820-800=20,\; 790-820=-30,\; 810-790=20,\; 805-810=-5 \\
\text{Squared:} &\quad 400,\; 900,\; 400,\; 25 \\
\text{Sum:} &\quad 1725 \\
RMSSD &= \sqrt{\frac{1725}{3}} = \sqrt{575} \approx 23.98 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (d) pNN50

Successive differences: $\{20, -30, 20, -5\}$. Only differences with absolute value > 50 ms count. **None** of these exceed 50 ms in magnitude.

$$pNN50 = \frac{0}{3} \times 100\% = 0\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A pNN50 of 0% does NOT mean unhealthy — it merely reflects that this short 4-difference sample did not cross the 50 ms threshold. Long recordings are essential for stable pNN50 estimates.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Method | What it Measures | Strength | Weakness |
|--------|------------------|----------|----------|
| **Time-Domain (SDNN)** | Global HRV | Simple, robust | Cannot separate sympathetic vs. parasympathetic |
| **Time-Domain (RMSSD)** | Vagal tone | Direct vagal marker | Ignores long-term trends |
| **Frequency (HF)** | Vagal (RSA) | Physiologically grounded | Requires interpolation |
| **Frequency (LF)** | Mixed (Baroreflex) | Sensitive to baroreflex | Interpretational ambiguity |
| **Poincaré (SD1/SD2)** | Short/Long variability | Graphical + quantitative | Sensitive to outliers |
| **Sample Entropy** | Irregularity | Captures chaos | Computationally intensive |
| **DFA** | Long-range correlation | Robust to non-stationarities | Requires long records |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HRV Analysis Pipeline — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the standard end-to-end HRV analysis pipeline, from raw ECG acquisition to physiological interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ECG Signal Acquisition] --> B[Bandpass Filtering 0.5 40 Hz]
    B --> C[R Peak Detection Pan Tompkins]
    C --> D[R R Interval Tachogram]
    D --> E{Analysis Branch}
    E --> F1[Time Domain SDNN RMSSD pNN50]
    E --> F2[Cubic Spline Interpolation 4 Hz]
    F2 --> G2[FFT / Autoregressive PSD]
    G2 --> H2[Band Power Integration VLF LF HF]
    E --> F3[Poincare Plot RR n+1 vs RR n]
    F3 --> G3[Compute SD1 SD2 Ellipse]
    F1 --> Z[Clinical Interpretation]
    H2 --> Z
    G3 --> Z
    Z --> Z1[Autonomic Balance Report]
```

## 4.2 Frequency Band Mapping — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Power_Spectral_Density
    P0[Total Power ms squared] --> V[VLF 0.0033 0.04 Hz Thermoregulation]
    P0 --> Q[LF 0.04 0.15 Hz Baroreflex Mixed]
    P0 --> R[HF 0.15 0.4 Hz Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia]
    Q --> S[LF to HF Ratio Sympathovagal Balance]
    R --> S
    end
```

## 4.3 Poincaré Plot Interpretation — Visual Atlas

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Normal Subject] --> N1[Comet Shaped Cloud]
    N1 --> N2[SD1 Small SD2 Large]
    M[Cardiac Patient] --> M1[Torpedo / Narrow Cloud]
    M1 --> M2[SD1 Reduced SD2 Reduced]
    A[Atrial Fibrillation] --> A1[Diffuse Scattered Cloud]
    A1 --> A2[SD1 Large SD2 Variable]
```

## 4.4 Processing Steps — Detailed Block Description

| Stage | Module | Input → Output | KTU-Mentioned Algorithm |
|-------|--------|----------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Preprocessing | Raw ECG → Filtered ECG | Butterworth 0.5–40 Hz bandpass |
| 2 | QRS Detection | ECG → R-peak times | Pan-Tompkins algorithm |
| 3 | Interval Series | R-peaks → R-R tachogram | $RR_i = R_{i+1} - R_i$ |
| 4 | Cleaning | R-R → NN intervals | Reject ectopic beats (median filter) |
| 5 | Time-Domain | NN → scalar metrics | SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50 |
| 6 | Frequency-Domain | NN → PSD | Welch periodogram / AR model |
| 7 | Non-Linear | NN → geometry | Poincaré, SampEn, DFA |
| 8 | Interpretation | Metrics → Diagnosis | LF/HF ratio thresholds |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Heart Rate Variability (HRV). List any four time-domain measures of HRV with their clinical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** HRV refers to the variation in the time intervals between successive heartbeats, typically measured as the variation in R-R intervals obtained from an ECG. It is a non-invasive indicator of autonomic nervous system (ANS) regulation of the heart.
>
> **Four Time-Domain Measures (2 marks):**
> 1. **SDNN** (Standard Deviation of NN intervals) — reflects total HRV, a global index.
> 2. **SDSD** (Standard Deviation of Successive Differences) — reflects beat-to-beat variation.
> 3. **RMSSD** (Root Mean Square of Successive Differences) — primary marker of short-term vagal (parasympathetic) tone.
> 4. **pNN50** (Percentage of NN intervals differing by >50 ms) — pure vagal tone estimator.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the physiological significance of the LF and HF components of the HRV power spectrum? What does the LF/HF ratio indicate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **LF (0.04–0.15 Hz):** Reflects the baroreflex-mediated combined influence of sympathetic and parasympathetic activity, with sympathetic predominance. *(1 mark)*
>
> **HF (0.15–0.4 Hz):** Reflects Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA) and is a primary marker of **parasympathetic (vagal) tone**. *(1 mark)*
>
> **LF/HF Ratio:** Used as an index of **sympathovagal balance** — a higher ratio indicates sympathetic dominance (e.g., during stress), while a lower ratio indicates parasympathetic dominance (e.g., relaxation, rest). *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Choice-1]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete HRV analysis pipeline from ECG acquisition to clinical interpretation. Mention the role of the Pan-Tompkins algorithm in the pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Define RMSSD and SDNN. From the following 6 successive R-R intervals: **{780, 810, 800, 825, 815, 805 ms}**, calculate both indices. Comment on the autonomic state indicated. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

> **Block Diagram (3 marks):**
> 1. ECG acquisition (lead II, 250–500 Hz sampling)
> 2. Bandpass filtering (0.5–40 Hz, Butterworth)
> 3. R-peak detection — **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** (derivative → squaring → moving-average integration → adaptive thresholding)
> 4. Construction of R-R tachogram
> 5. Ectopic beat rejection
> 6. Time-domain / Frequency-domain / Non-linear analysis
> 7. Clinical interpretation
>
> **Role of Pan-Tompkins (2 marks):** It is a real-time QRS detection algorithm offering ~99.3% sensitivity on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database. It uses a bandpass filter, derivative, squaring, and moving-window integration to enhance the QRS slope and energy, followed by adaptive dual-thresholding to locate R-peaks.
>
> **Subsequent Stages (2 marks):** Once R-peaks are detected, the inter-beat intervals are computed, cleaned of ectopic beats, and subjected to time-domain (SDNN, RMSSD), frequency-domain (Welch PSD), and non-linear analysis (Poincaré).

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Step 1 — Definitions (1 mark):**
- **SDNN** = standard deviation of all NN intervals; reflects global HRV.
- **RMSSD** = square root of the mean of squared successive NN differences; reflects vagal tone.

**Step 2 — Compute Mean R-R (1 mark):**

$$
\overline{RR} = \frac{780 + 810 + 800 + 825 + 815 + 805}{6} = \frac{4835}{6} = 805.83 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute SDNN (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviations (ms):} &\; -25.83,\; +4.17,\; -5.83,\; +19.17,\; +9.17,\; -0.83 \\
\text{Squared:} &\; 667.2,\; 17.4,\; 34.0,\; 367.5,\; 84.1,\; 0.7 \\
\text{Sum:} &\; 1170.9 \\
SDNN &= \sqrt{\frac{1170.9}{5}} = \sqrt{234.18} \approx 15.30 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute RMSSD (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Successive Differences (ms):} &\; 30,\; -10,\; 25,\; -10,\; -10 \\
\text{Squared:} &\; 900,\; 100,\; 625,\; 100,\; 100 \\
\text{Sum:} &\; 1825 \\
RMSSD &= \sqrt{\frac{1825}{4}} = \sqrt{456.25} \approx 21.36 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Autonomic Comment (1 mark):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> RMSSD (21.36 ms) is markedly higher than SDNN (15.30 ms), indicating **dominant short-term, beat-to-beat variability** — a sign of **strong parasympathetic (vagal) tone** in a resting subject.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Explain the Frequency-Domain (Power Spectral Density) analysis of HRV. List the standard frequency bands, their physiological correlates, and the pre-processing required for PSD estimation. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Define the Poincaré plot. Derive the formulas for SD1 and SD2 in terms of SDNN and SDSD. Compute SD1 and SD2 for a dataset with $SDNN = 40$ ms and $SDSD = 25$ ms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**PSD Analysis Overview (2 marks):** Frequency-domain HRV analysis decomposes the R-R tachogram into constituent frequencies, mapping them to autonomic branches. Two methods are used:
- **Non-parametric:** Welch periodogram (windowed FFT).
- **Parametric:** Autoregressive (AR) modeling — better spectral resolution for short records.

**Pre-Processing (2 marks):**
1. **Interpolation:** The R-R tachogram is unevenly sampled. Apply **cubic-spline interpolation** at a uniform resampling rate (typically 4 Hz).
2. **Detrending:** Remove slow baseline drift (high-pass filter, smoothness priors).
3. **Windowing:** Apply Hamming/Hann window to reduce spectral leakage.

**Frequency Bands (3 marks):**
| Band | Range | Correlate |
|------|-------|-----------|
| VLF | 0.0033 – 0.04 Hz | Thermoregulation, humoral |
| LF | 0.04 – 0.15 Hz | Baroreflex, mixed autonomic |
| HF | 0.15 – 0.4 Hz | RSA, parasympathetic |
| LF/HF | — | Sympathovagal balance |

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Poincaré Plot Definition (1 mark):** A scatter plot of $RR_{i+1}$ against $RR_i$ for all successive pairs. The shape of the resulting cloud is fitted with an ellipse whose axes are SD1 (minor) and SD2 (major).

**Derivation (4 marks):**
- $d_i = RR_{i+1} - RR_i$, with variance $SDSD^2$.
- Projection along the perpendicular to $y = x$ yields the minor axis.

$$
SD1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\text{Var}(d)} = \frac{SDSD}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

- Projection along $y = x$ combined with the variance of the average yields:

$$
SD2 = \sqrt{2\,SDNN^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}SDSD^2}
$$

**Computation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SD1 &= \frac{25}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 17.68 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
SD2 &= \sqrt{2(40)^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}(25)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{3200 - 312.5} = \sqrt{2887.5} \approx 53.74 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** SD2 > SD1 indicates that long-term variability dominates short-term variability — typical of a healthy, well-regulated heart.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SDSD with RMSSD:** SDSD and RMSSD are *NOT* identical. SDSD is a standard deviation of successive differences, while RMSSD is the *root mean square* of those differences. Numerically identical ONLY when the mean of $d_i$ is zero. Always use the RMSSD formula for vagal tone.
> 2. **Forgetting interpolation before FFT:** R-R intervals are not uniformly sampled. Failing to interpolate before computing PSD is a **direct 2-mark deduction** in KTU evaluations.
> 3. **Wrong frequency band limits:** A common error is using 0.04–0.4 Hz for LF or 0.15–0.5 Hz for HF. The **official Task Force of 1996** limits are sacrosanct.
> 4. **Unit confusion:** SDNN, RMSSD, SD1, SD2 are in **ms**; Power spectral components are in **ms²**; LF/HF ratio is **dimensionless**.
> 5. **Neglecting ectopic beat filtering:** A single premature ventricular contraction (PVC) can drastically inflate SDNN and RMSSD. Always mention pre-processing.
> 6. **Misinterpreting LF:** LF is **not purely sympathetic**; it has a substantial vagal component (baroreflex).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] HRV = variation in R-R intervals; a non-invasive ANS marker.
- [x] **High HRV = good** (flexible, healthy heart); **Low HRV = bad** (rigid, pathological).
- [x] HRV is generated by SNS (sympathetic, LF) vs. PNS (parasympathetic, HF) balance.
- [x] **Time-domain metrics:** SDNN (global), RMSSD (vagal), pNN50 (vagal), SDSD.
- [x] **Frequency-domain bands:** VLF (0.0033–0.04), LF (0.04–0.15), HF (0.15–0.4) Hz.
- [x] **LF/HF ratio** = sympathovagal balance index.
- [x] **Non-linear metrics:** Poincaré (SD1, SD2), Sample Entropy, DFA.
- [x] **Pre-processing is essential:** R-peak detection → ectopic filtering → interpolation → PSD.
- [x] **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** is the gold standard for QRS detection.
- [x] **Units:** ms for time-domain, ms² for power, dimensionless for LF/HF ratio.
- [x] Clinical applications: post-MI risk stratification, diabetic neuropathy, athletic training, stress monitoring, drug safety.
- [x] **Standard recording durations:** 5 min (short-term) and 24 h (long-term Holter).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced ECG Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Advanced ECG Processing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Advanced ECG Processing** refers to the suite of sophisticated digital signal processing (DSP) techniques employed to extract clinically meaningful diagnostic information from the electrocardiogram (ECG) signal. It encompasses noise suppression (baseline wander, powerline interference, EMG artifacts), accurate detection of waveform fiducial points (P, QRS, T waves), feature extraction, beat classification, and the derivation of secondary physiological parameters such as Heart Rate Variability (HRV). The electrocardiogram is a non-invasive biopotential recording of the electrical activity of the heart, typically acquired using a 12-lead configuration with a standard sampling frequency of **f_s = 500 Hz** (clinical) or **f_s = 1000 Hz** (research-grade), and a band of clinical interest spanning **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**. ### 1.2 ECG Morphology — A Quick Refresher A normal sinus rhythm ECG beat consists of the following fiducial waves: - **P wave**: Atrial depolarization (low amplitude, 0.25 mV, duration 80–110 ms). - **QRS complex**: Ventricular depolarization (high amplitude, R-peak 1.0–1.5 mV, duration 80–120 ms). - **T wave**: Ventricular repolarization (low amplitude, 0.1–0.5 mV, duration 160 ms). - **U wave** (optional): Papillary muscle repolarization. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Clinical Sampling**: For adult KTU-style problem solving, the typical sampling frequency used is **f_s = 500 Hz** and the standard mains frequency for India is **50 Hz** (compared to **60 Hz** in North America). Always use **50 Hz** unless explicitly stated otherwise. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The "Stethoscope vs. Microphone" Analogy** > Imagine the ECG signal as a recording of a conversation in a noisy, crowded room. The conversation is the "true" cardiac electrical activity, and the noise is everything else — the hum of the air conditioner (powerline interference), the rumble of footsteps (baseline wander from respiration), and people chattering nearby (muscle/EMG noise). Advanced ECG processing is the equivalent of using a sophisticated **noise-cancelling microphone and voice-recognition software** that filters out all the unwanted sounds, then automatically transcribes the conversation into a structured medical report identifying the speakers (P, QRS, T waves) and timing their responses (R-R intervals for heart rate variability). ### 1.4 Why "Advanced" ECG Processing? Basic filtering (e.g., a 0.5–40 Hz Butterworth bandpass) is insufficient for clinical-grade ECG analysis because: 1. **Powerline interference is non-stationary** with shifting amplitude and phase. 2. **Baseline wander overlaps the P and T waves** in the frequency domain, so naive high-pass filtering distorts ST-segments (critical for ischemia detection). 3. **QRS detection in noisy records (e.g., ambulatory/Holter monitoring)** requires adaptive, morphology-aware algorithms like the Pan-Tompkins method. 4. **Wavelet transforms** allow time-frequency localization that classical Fourier methods cannot provide for transient QRS events. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Noise Sources in ECG — A Structured Breakdown | Noise Source | Frequency Range | Amplitude | Origin | |---|---|---|---| | Powerline Interference (PLI) | **50/60 Hz** + harmonics | Up to 50% of peak QRS | Mains electrical pickup | | Baseline Wander (BLW) | **0.05 – 0.5 Hz** | Up to 15% of full scale | Respiration, electrode motion | | Muscle Noise (EMG) | **20 – 500 Hz** (broadband) | Up to 10% of QRS | Patient motion, EMG crosstalk | | Electrode Contact Noise | **< 1 Hz** drift | Slow drift | Poor skin-electrode contact | | High-Frequency Noise | **> 150 Hz** | Small | Instrumentation, RF interference | ### 2.2 The Standard Processing Pipeline A typical advanced ECG processing chain follows this order: 1. **Pre-emphasis / DC removal** (high-pass with very low cutoff, e.g., 0.05 Hz) 2. **Powerline interference suppression** (notch filter or adaptive filter) 3. **Baseline wander removal** (high-pass or wavelet decomposition & nulling) 4. **Bandpass filtering for QRS emphasis** (5–15 Hz or 5–30 Hz) 5. **QRS detection** (Pan-Tompkins, wavelet modulus maxima, template matching) 6. **Fiducial point localization** (P, Q, R, S, T peak/end detection) 7. **Feature extraction** (intervals, amplitudes, areas, HRV metrics) 8. **Classification / diagnosis** (optional, e.g., arrhythmia classification) ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes every critical formula for Advanced ECG Processing. Use `\vert` for absolute-value separators to keep the markdown table intact. | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | Sampling theorem | f_s \geq 2 f_{max} | Hz | Choosing sampling rate | | Powerline notch transfer function | H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}} | — | 50 Hz notch design | | Notch angular frequency | \omega_0 = 2\pi f_{notch}/f_s | rad/sample | Notch placement | | Heart Rate (HR) | HR = \frac{60}{RR_{avg}} | bpm (beats/min) | From R-R intervals | | Mean R-R interval | \bar{RR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} RR_i | seconds | HRV calculation | | SDNN (HRV time domain) | SDNN = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(RR_i - \bar{RR})^2} | ms | Long-term HRV | | RMSSD (HRV time domain) | RMSSD = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2} | ms | Short-term HRV | | Bandwidth of ECG | 0.05 Hz \leq f \leq 150 Hz | Hz | Standard clinical | | Energy of QRS | E = \sum_{n=n_1}^{n_2} \vert x(n) \vert^2 | J (normalized) | Pan-Tompkins energy stage | | Moving average filter | y(n) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} x(n-k) | — | Smoothing in Pan-Tompkins | | Differentiator | y(n) = \frac{1}{8}[2x(n) + x(n-1) - x(n-3) - 2x(n-4)] | — | QRS slope enhancement | | Squaring function | y(n) = [x(n)]^2 | — | Make all values positive | | Pan-Tompkins threshold | TH = \frac{1}{3}(SPKF + NPKF) | — | Adaptive R-peak detection | | Correlation coefficient | \rho_{xy} = \frac{\sum x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum x_i^2 \sum y_i^2}} | dimensionless | Template matching | | Wavelet SNR gain | SNR_{out} = 10\log_{10}\frac{\sum s^2}{\sum (s-\hat s)^2} | dB | Denoising quality | ### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Differentiation** amplifies the high-slope QRS region and suppresses low-frequency P and T waves. - **Squaring** makes all values positive and sharply emphasizes the large QRS amplitude differences. - **Moving-window integration** (typically 150 ms wide) generates a single smooth pulse per QRS — making thresholding robust to noise. - **Adaptive thresholding** in Pan-Tompkins auto-adjusts to changing QRS amplitudes and noise levels, crucial in ambulatory recordings. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility These algorithms power: - **Holter monitors** (24–48 hr ambulatory ECG recorders) - **ICU patient monitors** with real-time ST-segment analysis - **Implantable pacemakers** and **Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillators (ICDs)** that must classify arrhythmia on-chip - **Wearable fitness devices** (e.g., Apple Watch, Fitbit) that derive HRV from single-lead ECG - **Stress test systems** requiring robust QRS detection during motion ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: 50 Hz Notch Filter Design (Bi-quad Implementation) A second-order IIR notch filter with angular notch frequency \omega_0 places zeros on the unit circle at the offending frequency, and poles just inside the unit circle for narrow bandwidth. **Step 1 — Place zeros on the unit circle at \pm \omega_0:** $$H_{zeros}(z) = 1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$ **Step 2 — Place poles at radius r (0.9 \leq r < 1) for sharp rejection:** $$H_{poles}(z) = 1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}$$ **Step 3 — Combine numerator and denominator:** $$H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$$ **Step 4 — For Indian mains f_{notch} = 50 Hz, f_s = 500 Hz:** $$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 50}{500} = \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample}$$ $$\cos(\omega_0) = \cos(\pi/5) \approx 0.8090$$ **Step 5 — Choose r = 0.95 for a sharp but stable notch:** $$H(z) = \frac{1 - 1.618 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.537 z^{-1} + 0.9025 z^{-2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Stability check**: All poles have magnitude r = 0.95 < 1, so the filter is BIBO stable. ### 3.2 Derivation: Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Algorithm The Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985) is a five-stage real-time QRS detector. Let us derive the signal flow. **Stage 1 — Bandpass filter (5–15 Hz):** A cascade of low-pass (cutoff 15 Hz) and high-pass (cutoff 5 Hz) filters, both second-order Butterworth, isolates the QRS energy. $$H_{bp}(z) = H_{lp}(z) \cdot H_{hp}(z)$$ **Stage 2 — Differentiator:** A five-point derivative operator emphasizes the steep slopes of the QRS: $$y(n) = \frac{1}{8}[2x(n) + x(n-1) - x(n-3) - 2x(n-4)]$$ This is derived from the transfer function: $$H_d(z) = \frac{1}{8T}(1 + z^{-1} - z^{-3} - 2z^{-4})$$ > The **z-transform** of a continuous-time differentiator 1/s approximates the high-frequency gain; truncation to 5 taps keeps the filter causal and realizable. **Stage 3 — Squaring:** $$y(n) = [x(n)]^2$$ This non-linear operation ensures all samples are positive and emphasizes large differences, dramatically enhancing QRS over P and T waves. **Stage 4 — Moving-window integration:** $$y(n) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} x(n-k)$$ where **M is chosen such that the window approximates the QRS width**. For typical adult ECG with f_s = 500 Hz and QRS duration of 100 ms: $$M = 0.150 \text{ s} \times 500 \text{ samples/s} = 75 \text{ samples}$$ **Stage 5 — Adaptive thresholding:** Two running estimates are maintained: - **SPKF** (Signal Peak Level) — for peaks in the integrated waveform - **NPKF** (Noise Peak Level) — for non-QRS peaks The detection threshold is: $$TH = NPKF + 0.25(SPKF - NPKF)$$ When a peak exceeds TH, it is classified as a QRS candidate and SPKF is updated: $$SPKF = 0.875 \cdot SPKF + 0.125 \cdot PEAK$$ When a peak is below TH, it is classified as noise and NPKF is updated: $$NPKF = 0.875 \cdot NPKF + 0.125 \cdot PEAK$$ These **learning rates (0.875 and 0.125)** were chosen empirically by Pan and Tompkins to track slow amplitude variations in ambulatory recordings. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Filter for the Right Noise | Problem | Recommended Filter | Key Parameter | Why | |---|---|---|---| | Powerline (50/60 Hz) | IIR Notch (Bi-quad) | \omega_0 = 2\pi f_{PL}/f_s, r \approx 0.95 | Sharp, narrow null | | Baseline wander | High-pass IIR or wavelet level-1 detail zeroing | f_c \approx 0.5 Hz or \leq 0.5 Hz detail | Preserves ST-segment | | Muscle noise (EMG) | Low-pass at 35–40 Hz | Butterworth, 4th order | Suppresses broadband EMG | | QRS detection | Pan-Tompkins pipeline | 5-stage | Robust, real-time, adaptive | | Wavelet denoising | Daubechies-4 (db4), soft thresholding | Level 3–5 detail zeroing | Time-frequency localization | | Adaptive noise cancellation | LMS / NLMS adaptive filter | Step size \mu = 0.01 – 0.1 | Tracks non-stationary PLI | ### 3.4 Derivation: Heart Rate Variability — SDNN and RMSSD Given a sequence of N R-R intervals RR_i (in milliseconds): **Mean R-R:** $$\bar{RR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} RR_i$$ **SDNN (Standard Deviation of N-N intervals):** $$SDNN = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(RR_i - \bar{RR})^2}$$ **RMSSD (Root Mean Square of Successive Differences):** $$RMSSD = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physiological interpretation**: > - **SDNN** reflects overall HRV and is dominated by both sympathetic and parasympathetic activity. A low SDNN (\lt 50 ms in 24 hr) is a strong predictor of mortality post-myocardial infarction. > - **RMSSD** reflects short-term, beat-to-beat variability mediated almost entirely by the parasympathetic (vagal) nervous system. Higher RMSSD \Rightarrow better recovery and lower stress. ### 3.5 Derivation: Correlation Coefficient for Template Matching Given a noisy incoming beat x(n) and a clean reference template t(n) of length L, the **normalized cross-correlation** is: $$\rho_{xy} = \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x(n) t(n)}{\sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x^2(n) \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} t^2(n)}}$$ This produces a value in [-1, 1]. A typical beat classification threshold for "match" is \rho \geq 0.9, with anything below 0.7 flagged as a "premature" or "ectopic" beat (e.g., PVC). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Complete Advanced ECG Processing Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Raw ECG Signal
f_s equals 500 Hz] --> node2[Pre-emphasis
High-pass 0.05 Hz] node2 --> node3[Notch Filter
50 Hz Bi-quad] node3 --> node4[Bandpass Filter
0.5 to 40 Hz] node4 --> node5[Pan-Tompkins Stage 1
Bandpass 5 to 15 Hz] node5 --> node6[Stage 2
Differentiator] node6 --> node7[Stage 3
Squaring] node7 --> node8[Stage 4
Moving Integration 150 ms] node8 --> node9[Stage 5
Adaptive Threshold] node9 --> node10{QRS Detected?} node10 -- Yes --> node11[Fiducial Localization
P Q R S T peaks] node10 -- No --> node12[Update NPKF
Noise Peak Estimate] node12 --> node9 node11 --> node13[Feature Extraction
Intervals Amplitudes Areas] node13 --> node14[HRV Analysis
SDNN RMSSD] node13 --> node15[Beat Classification
Normal PVC APC] node14 --> node16[Clinical Report] node15 --> node16 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Adaptive Filter for Powerline Cancellation ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Primary Input
ECG plus 50 Hz noise] --> nodeSum(("+")) nodeSum --> nodeE[Adaptive Filter
LMS NLMS] nodeE -- y filtered --> nodeSum nodeB[Reference Input
Pure 50 Hz sine cos] --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF[Error Signal e
equals Clean ECG estimate] nodeF -- feedback --> nodeE nodeF --> nodeOut[Clean ECG Output] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Wavelet-Based Denoising Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeW[Noisy ECG] --> nodeD1[Level 1 Decomposition
Detail D1 high freq] nodeW --> nodeA1[Approximation A1] nodeA1 --> nodeD2[Level 2 Detail D2] nodeA1 --> nodeA2[Approximation A2] nodeA2 --> nodeD3[Level 3 Detail D3] nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Approximation A3] nodeA3 --> nodeD4[Level 4 Detail D4 baseline] nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Approximation A4] nodeA4 --> nodeD5[Level 5 Detail D5] nodeD1 --> nodeTH1[Soft Thresholding] nodeD2 --> nodeTH2[Soft Thresholding] nodeD3 --> nodeTH3[Soft Thresholding] nodeD4 --> nodeZ[Set to Zero Baseline] nodeD5 --> nodeTH5[Soft Thresholding] nodeTH1 --> nodeRec[Reconstruct Clean ECG] nodeTH2 --> nodeRec nodeTH3 --> nodeRec nodeZ --> nodeRec nodeTH5 --> nodeRec ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Pan-Tompkins Adaptive Threshold State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Searching Searching --> PeakFound: Integrated waveform local max PeakFound --> CheckThreshold: Compare against TH CheckThreshold --> QRSDetected: Peak greater than TH CheckThreshold --> NoiseDetected: Peak less than TH QRSDetected --> UpdateSPKF: SPKF new 0.875 SPKF old plus 0.125 Peak NoiseDetected --> UpdateNPKF: NPKF new 0.875 NPKF old plus 0.125 Peak UpdateSPKF --> Searching UpdateNPKF --> Searching QRSDetected --> Refractory: 200 ms blanking period Refractory --> Searching ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid node-ID safety check**: All node identifiers above use purely alphanumeric names prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `nodeA`, `nodeTH1`, `nodeSum`). No reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` are used as standalone node names. All double-quoted labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting or HTML tables, ensuring the diagram compiles cleanly. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** List the three main types of noise present in an ECG signal and state their typical frequency ranges. **Model Answer:** The three main types of noise in an ECG signal are: 1. **Powerline Interference (PLI)**: Narrowband interference at **50 Hz** (India) or 60 Hz (USA), caused by capacitive and inductive coupling from the mains supply. Amplitude can be up to 50 percent of the peak QRS amplitude. 2. **Baseline Wander**: A low-frequency artefact in the range **0.05 to 0.5 Hz** caused by respiration, patient movement, and poor electrode contact. It can shift the isoelectric line and obscure the ST-segment. 3. **Muscle Noise (EMG)**: Broadband high-frequency noise in the range **20 to 500 Hz** caused by electromyographic activity from surrounding skeletal muscles, especially in ambulatory recordings. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional Model]** What is the role of the squaring and moving-window integration stages in the Pan-Tompkins QRS detection algorithm? **Model Answer:** - **Squaring stage** y(n) = [x(n)]^2 makes all samples strictly non-negative, eliminating polarity ambiguity, and amplifies the large-amplitude QRS samples while suppressing small-amplitude P and T waves due to the non-linear square operation. - **Moving-window integration** (typically 150 ms wide) computes a moving average of the squared signal, converting the multi-peak QRS into a single smooth pulse whose peak corresponds to the R-wave. This makes subsequent thresholding robust against local minima, baseline variations, and residual noise. Together, these two stages transform a complex, noisy, multi-waveform ECG into a clean unimodal pulse train suitable for adaptive threshold-based R-peak detection. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern Note (2024 Scheme)**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is internally split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. KTU examiners expect clearly labeled figures, stepwise derivations, and final boxed answers. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Choice A]** **(a)** Design a second-order IIR notch filter to suppress 50 Hz powerline interference from an ECG signal sampled at f_s = 500 Hz. Take the pole radius r = 0.95. Compute the difference equation. **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the five stages of the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for QRS detection. State the rationale for each stage. --- #### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks **Given**: f_{notch} = 50 Hz, f_s = 500 Hz, r = 0.95. **Step 1**: Compute the digital notch frequency: $$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_{notch}}{f_s} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 50}{500} = \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample} \approx 0.6283 \text{ rad/sample}$$ **Step 2**: Compute \cos(\omega_0): $$\cos(\omega_0) = \cos(\pi/5) = \cos(36^\circ) \approx 0.80902$$ **Step 3**: Substitute into the bi-quad notch transfer function: $$H(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$$ **Step 4**: Compute the coefficients: - Numerator: a_0 = 1, a_1 = -2(0.80902) = -1.61804, a_2 = 1 - Denominator: b_0 = 1, b_1 = -2(0.95)(0.80902) = -1.53714, b_2 = (0.95)^2 = 0.9025 **Step 5**: Write the difference equation: $$y(n) = 1.53714 \, y(n-1) - 0.9025 \, y(n-2) + 1.61804 \, x(n-1) - x(n-2)$$ $$\boxed{y(n) = 1.53714 y(n-1) - 0.9025 y(n-2) + x(n) - 1.61804 x(n-1) + x(n-2)}$$ **Stability**: All poles have magnitude r = 0.95 < 1, ensuring BIBO stability. --- #### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks The Pan-Tompkins algorithm consists of **five sequential stages**: 1. **Bandpass Filtering (5–15 Hz)**: A cascade of a second-order low-pass Butterworth at 15 Hz and a high-pass at 5 Hz attenuates baseline wander, powerline interference, and EMG noise while preserving the dominant QRS energy. The output maximizes the signal-to-noise ratio for the QRS component. 2. **Differentiation**: A five-point derivative operator y(n) = (1/8)[2x(n) + x(n-1) - x(n-3) - 2x(n-4)] emphasizes the steep slopes of the QRS while suppressing the low-slope P and T waves. Rationale: QRS is the steepest feature in the ECG, so differentiation makes it pop out. 3. **Squaring**: y(n) = [x(n)]^2 converts all samples to positive values and non-linearly amplifies large amplitudes. Rationale: it eliminates polarity ambiguity (R-peaks are positive) and further enhances the QRS over P and T. 4. **Moving-Window Integration**: A 150 ms moving average y(n) = (1/M)\sum x(n-k) where M = 75 samples (at f_s = 500 Hz) converts the multi-peak QRS complex into a single unimodal pulse. Rationale: a single smooth peak per beat is far easier to threshold reliably than the raw QRS with multiple peaks and notches. 5. **Adaptive Thresholding**: Two running estimates SPKF (signal peak) and NPKF (noise peak) are maintained. A peak above the threshold TH = NPKF + 0.25(SPKF - NPKF) is classified as a QRS, updating SPKF. Otherwise, it is treated as noise, updating NPKF. Rationale: this allows the algorithm to track slow changes in QRS amplitude and noise floor in ambulatory recordings, where a fixed threshold would fail. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Choice B]** **(a)** Explain how discrete wavelet transform (DWT) is used for baseline wander and EMG noise removal in ECG. State the choice of mother wavelet and decomposition levels. **(b)** Compute the mean R-R interval, SDNN, and RMSSD for the following R-R intervals (in ms) obtained from a 5-minute ECG recording: 800, 820, 790, 850, 810, 830, 800, 815, 805, 825. Comment on the parasympathetic tone. --- #### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks The **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** decomposes the ECG signal into a set of wavelet coefficients at multiple scales using a mother wavelet. For ECG, the **Daubechies-4 (db4)** mother wavelet is widely preferred because its shape closely matches the QRS complex. **Decomposition procedure:** - The ECG x(n) is passed through a low-pass filter g(n) and a high-pass filter h(n), producing the approximation A1 and detail D1. - A1 is further decomposed into A2, D2, and so on, for the desired number of levels. **Typical decomposition (for f_s = 500 Hz):** | Level | Approximation Band (Hz) | Detail Band (Hz) | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 – 125 | 125 – 250 | Set D1 = 0 (removes high-freq EMG, RF) | | 2 | 0 – 62.5 | 62.5 – 125 | Threshold D2 (residual EMG) | | 3 | 0 – 31.25 | 31.25 – 62.5 | Threshold D3 (high QRS harmonics) | | 4 | 0 – 15.625 | 15.625 – 31.25 | Keep (QRS dominant) | | 5 | 0 – 7.81 | 7.81 – 15.625 | Keep (P and T waves) | | 6 | 0 – 3.9 | 3.9 – 7.81 | Threshold (low-freq drift) | - **Baseline wander** (0.05–0.5 Hz) is removed by setting the approximation at levels corresponding to those frequencies (typically the highest-level approximation, A_{max}) to zero, since baseline wander is the slowest-varying component. - **EMG noise** is broadband, so the high-frequency detail coefficients (D1, D2) are soft-thresholded using the universal threshold T = \sigma \sqrt{2 \log N}, where \sigma is the median absolute deviation of the detail coefficients. - **Reconstruction** uses the modified coefficients to obtain the denoised ECG \hat{x}(n). **Advantages of DWT over classical filtering**: - **Time-frequency localization**: Wavelets preserve the time-domain position of the QRS, unlike Fourier-domain filtering which may smear sharp transients. - **Non-stationary noise handling**: DWT adapts to local signal features, ideal for ECG with abrupt QRS events. - **No ST-segment distortion**: Classical high-pass filters can distort the ST-segment (critical for ischemia diagnosis); wavelet baseline removal preserves clinical morphology. --- #### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks **Given R-R intervals (ms)**: 800, 820, 790, 850, 810, 830, 800, 815, 805, 825 (N = 10). **Step 1 — Mean R-R:** $$\bar{RR} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10} RR_i = \frac{800+820+790+850+810+830+800+815+805+825}{10}$$ $$\bar{RR} = \frac{8145}{10} = 814.5 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2 — Mean Heart Rate:** $$HR = \frac{60000}{814.5} \approx 73.66 \text{ bpm}$$ **Step 3 — SDNN (Standard Deviation of N-N intervals):** First compute the deviations (RR_i - \bar{RR}): | i | RR_i | RR_i - 814.5 | (RR_i - 814.5)^2 | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 800 | -14.5 | 210.25 | | 2 | 820 | 5.5 | 30.25 | | 3 | 790 | -24.5 | 600.25 | | 4 | 850 | 35.5 | 1260.25 | | 5 | 810 | -4.5 | 20.25 | | 6 | 830 | 15.5 | 240.25 | | 7 | 800 | -14.5 | 210.25 | | 8 | 815 | 0.5 | 0.25 | | 9 | 805 | -9.5 | 90.25 | | 10 | 825 | 10.5 | 110.25 | | | | **Sum** | **2772.50** | $$SDNN = \sqrt{\frac{2772.50}{10-1}} = \sqrt{308.06} \approx 17.55 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 4 — RMSSD:** First compute successive differences (RR_{i+1} - RR_i) and their squares: | i | RR_i | RR_{i+1} | \Delta RR | (\Delta RR)^2 | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 800 | 820 | 20 | 400 | | 2 | 820 | 790 | -30 | 900 | | 3 | 790 | 850 | 60 | 3600 | | 4 | 850 | 810 | -40 | 1600 | | 5 | 810 | 830 | 20 | 400 | | 6 | 830 | 800 | -30 | 900 | | 7 | 800 | 815 | 15 | 225 | | 8 | 815 | 805 | -10 | 100 | | 9 | 805 | 825 | 20 | 400 | | | | | **Sum** | **8625** | $$RMSSD = \sqrt{\frac{8625}{10-1}} = \sqrt{958.33} \approx 30.96 \text{ ms}$$ **Final Results:** $$\boxed{\bar{RR} = 814.5 \text{ ms}, \quad SDNN \approx 17.55 \text{ ms}, \quad RMSSD \approx 30.96 \text{ ms}}$$ **Comment on parasympathetic tone**: The RMSSD of approximately 31 ms is a moderately healthy short-term HRV, indicating reasonable vagal (parasympathetic) tone. For a 5-minute recording, an RMSSD above 30 ms generally suggests good recovery and low chronic stress. The relatively low SDNN of 17.55 ms is typical for short-term recordings (longer 24-hr recordings normally yield SDNN > 100 ms in healthy adults). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting units in f_s and \omega_0**: KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if a student computes the notch frequency in Hz instead of rad/sample, or vice versa. Always state \omega_0 in rad/sample. > 2. **Confusing \cos(\omega_0) with \omega_0**: A very common mistake is to substitute 0.6283 directly into the biquad instead of 0.8090. This destroys the notch placement. > 3. **Stability check omission**: Examiners award 1 mark for explicitly verifying that the pole magnitude is less than 1. Skipping this costs a mark. > 4. **Pan-Tompkins threshold formula**: Many students write TH = (SPKF + NPKF)/2 (mean). The correct Pan-Tompkins formula is TH = NPKF + 0.25(SPKF - NPKF), i.e., one-quarter of the way up from the noise level. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this error. > 5. **Sample-size confusion in HRV**: SDNN and RMSSD both divide by (N-1), not N. The (N-1) is the Bessel correction for an unbiased sample standard deviation. Forgetting this loses 1 mark. > 6. **Square root units**: SDNN and RMSSD are in milliseconds (the same units as R-R), but many students report them in "ms^2" by forgetting the square root. > 7. **Confusing approximation vs detail in DWT**: A high-level approximation (A_max) contains the low-frequency baseline, not the detail. Setting the wrong level to zero will either remove the QRS or leave the baseline intact. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **ECG bandwidth**: 0.05 to 150 Hz (clinical); QRS dominant energy 5 to 15 Hz. - **Standard Indian sampling**: f_s = 500 Hz; mains = **50 Hz** (use this unless stated otherwise). - **Three main noises**: PLI (50/60 Hz), Baseline Wander (0.05 to 0.5 Hz), EMG (20 to 500 Hz). - **Bi-quad notch filter**: H(z) = (1 - 2\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + z^{-2}) / (1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}); typical r = 0.95. - **Pan-Tompkins pipeline**: Bandpass (5 to 15 Hz) → Differentiator → Squaring → Moving Integration (150 ms) → Adaptive Threshold. - **Pan-Tompkins threshold formula**: TH = NPKF + 0.25(SPKF - NPKF), with update constants 0.875 and 0.125. - **Denoising mother wavelet**: Daubechies-4 (db4) is the gold standard for ECG. - **Wavelet denoising rule**: High-frequency details (D1, D2) → remove EMG; low-frequency approximations → remove baseline wander. - **HRV time-domain metrics**: SDNN (overall HRV) and RMSSD (parasympathetic tone) are the most clinically used. - **Adaptive filter reference**: For PLI removal, a clean 50 Hz sine/cosine reference is fed to an LMS adaptive filter to estimate and subtract the interference in real time. - **Refractory period**: After each QRS detection, a 200 ms blanking period prevents T-waves from being falsely detected as R-peaks. - **Template matching threshold**: Correlation coefficient \rho \geq 0.9 indicates a "normal" beat; \rho \lt 0.7 flags a probable ectopic beat. - **Fiducial point detection order**: R-peak first (Pan-Tompkins), then locate Q and S as local minima, then P and T using the R-peak as a timing anchor. - **KTU exam tip**: Always show the stability check, units, and a labelled block diagram. Examiners reward methodical work and penalize "magic-number" derivations.

topics.title: Arrhythmia Detection - Algorithms for Arrhythmia Detection and Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Arrhythmia Detection - Algorithms for Arrhythmia Detection and Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Arrhythmia

**Arrhythmia** (also called **dysrhythmia**) is a medical condition characterized by an abnormal rate, rhythm, or conduction pattern of the electrical impulses traveling through the cardiac conduction system of the heart. In a healthy adult, the normal sinus rhythm falls in the range of **60–100 beats per minute (bpm)**, originating from the **Sino-Atrial (SA) node**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Arrhythmia refers to any deviation from the normal sequence of electrical activation of the heart. Detection involves identifying the presence, type, and origin of such deviations, while classification involves categorizing the arrhythmia into clinically meaningful classes (e.g., Normal, PVC, Atrial Fibrillation, Ventricular Tachycardia) using morphological and temporal features extracted from the ECG signal.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highway toll booth system** where cars pass through sensors. Each car has a *license plate type* (sedan, truck, bus) and a *passing time*. If cars pass smoothly at regular intervals, the system is "normal." But if a truck suddenly weaves through lanes (abnormal path), or two cars pass simultaneously (irregular timing), alarms go off.

Similarly, in the **electrocardiogram (ECG)**, each heartbeat is a "car," with a distinctive **QRS complex** as its "license plate" and the **RR interval** as its "passing time." Arrhythmia detection algorithms act as the **toll booth sensors**, flagging any abnormality in shape, timing, or sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> The QRS complex is the most prominent feature in the ECG. Almost every arrhythmia detection algorithm begins with **robust QRS detection**, because the RR interval (time between successive R-peaks) is the single most important diagnostic feature.

## 1.3 Standard Clinical Metrics

The performance of any arrhythmia detection/classification algorithm is measured using four cardinal metrics:

| Metric | Definition | Clinical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity (Se)** | TP / (TP + FN) | Ability to detect true arrhythmias (avoid misses) |
| **Specificity (Sp)** | TN / (TN + FP) | Ability to avoid false alarms |
| **Accuracy (Acc)** | (TP + TN) / Total | Overall correctness |
| **Positive Predictivity (PPV)** | TP / (TP + FP) | Trustworthiness of a positive alarm |

Where **TP = True Positive, TN = True Negative, FP = False Positive, FN = False Negative**.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board examinations, examiners expect the student to know that **Sensitivity is the most critical metric** in life-threatening arrhythmias (e.g., Ventricular Fibrillation), since a miss (False Negative) can cost a patient's life.

## 1.4 Types of Arrhythmias (AAMI Standard Classes)

The **Association for the Advancement of Medical Instrumentation (AAMI)** recommends classifying beats into five superclasses:

- **N** – Normal, Left/Right Bundle Branch Block, Atrial Escape, Nodal Escape
- **S** – Atrial Premature, Aberrated Atrial Premature, Nodal Premature, Supraventricular Premature
- **V** – Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC), Ventricular Escape
- **F** – Fusion of Ventricular and Normal
- **Q** – Paced, Unknown, Unclassifiable
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Arrhythmia Detection Pipeline

Every modern arrhythmia detection/classification system follows a **four-stage pipeline**:

1. **ECG Preprocessing** — Noise removal (powerline interference, baseline wander, muscle noise)
2. **QRS Complex Detection** — Localization of R-peaks
3. **Feature Extraction** — RR intervals, QRS width, amplitudes, morphological coefficients
4. **Classification** — Mapping the feature vector to a clinical class label

## 2.2 Stage 1: ECG Preprocessing (Detail)

The ECG is corrupted by:
- **Powerline interference (50/60 Hz)** → removed by **notch filter**
- **Baseline wander (0.1–0.5 Hz)** → removed by **high-pass filter** with cut-off ≤ 0.5 Hz
- **High-frequency muscle (EMG) noise** → removed by **low-pass filter** ≤ 40 Hz
- **Electrode contact noise** → removed by **median filter** or **wavelet thresholding**

## 2.3 Stage 2: QRS Detection — Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (1985)

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** is the gold-standard for QRS detection. It is a **derivative-based real-time algorithm** operating in five sequential steps:

### Step 1: Bandpass Filtering (5–15 Hz)
A cascaded **low-pass filter (cutoff 11 Hz, 2nd order)** and **high-pass filter (cutoff 5 Hz, 2nd order)** isolates the QRS energy band.

### Step 2: Derivative Operator
