BCI applications"] F2 --> APP F3 --> APP F4 --> APP F5 --> APP ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define Resting Membrane Potential. Explain the role of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump in maintaining it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** is the steady-state voltage difference across the plasma membrane of a neuron in the absence of stimulation, typically **−70 mV** (inside negative with respect to outside). The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** maintains the RMP by actively transporting **3 Na⁺ ions out** of the cell and **2 K⁺ ions into** the cell per cycle, hydrolyzing one ATP molecule. This: 1. Establishes the **concentration gradients** essential for the Nernst potentials (high K⁺ inside, high Na⁺ outside). 2. Is **electrogenic** — net export of one positive charge per cycle contributes approximately **−5 mV** to the RMP directly. | Allocation | Marks | |---|---| | Definition of RMP with value −70 mV | 1 | | Stoichiometry of pump (3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in) | 1 | | Functional role (gradient + electrogenic contribution) | 1 | --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Why are cortical pyramidal neurons considered the primary generators of scalp EEG?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Cortical pyramidal neurons are the primary generators of scalp EEG because: 1. **Geometric alignment:** Their apical dendrites are arranged **perpendicular to the cortical surface**, so the postsynaptic dipoles they generate are aligned in parallel, enabling **constructive vector summation** rather than cancellation. 2. **Large population size:** Millions of these neurons fire simultaneously, and their summed dipoles produce potentials large enough to traverse the brain, skull, and scalp. 3. **Synchronous activity:** When cortical neurons fire in synchrony (e.g., during alpha rhythm), the summed extracellular field is strong enough to be detected by scalp electrodes (10–300 µV). Other neurons (e.g., stellate cells) are randomly oriented, so their fields cancel. Thus, pyramidal cells are uniquely suited as scalp EEG generators. | Allocation | Marks | |---|---| | Perpendicular alignment of apical dendrites | 1 | | Vector summation producing measurable field | 1 | | Population-level synchronization | 1 | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks):** > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]** > **(a)** Derive the Nernst equation starting from the electrochemical equilibrium condition. Use it to compute the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37°C, given $[K^+]_{out} = 4 \text{ mM}$ and $[K^+]_{in} = 140 \text{ mM}$. > > **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchical generation of scalp EEG from ionic currents to scalp electrodes. Discuss why pyramidal neurons dominate this process. #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Step 1 — Write the electrochemical potential inside and outside.** (1 mark) $$\tilde{\mu}_{in} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [K^+]_{in} + zF V_{in}$$ $$\tilde{\mu}_{out} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [K^+]_{out} + zF V_{out}$$ **Step 2 — Apply equilibrium condition Δμ̃ = 0.** (1 mark) $$RT \ln [K^+]_{in} + zF V_{in} = RT \ln [K^+]_{out} + zF V_{out}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the membrane potential Vₘ = V_in − V_out.** (1 mark) $$V_m = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \left(\dfrac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Convert to base-10 logarithm using the body-temperature constant 61.5 mV.** (1 mark) $$V_m = \dfrac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \left(\dfrac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.** (1 mark) $$E_K = \dfrac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4}{140}\right) = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.02857)$$ **Step 6 — Evaluate.** (1 mark) $$\log_{10}(0.02857) = -1.544 \Rightarrow E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.544) \approx -95 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 7 — State the result.** (1 mark) $$\boxed{E_K \approx -95 \text{ mV} \text{ (consistent with the standard } -90 \text{ mV reference value)}}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` Ionic Current → Post-Synaptic Potential → Dipole Formation → Population Summation → Volume Conduction → Scalp Electrode ``` Each stage should be explained: 1. **Ionic currents** at the synapse generate local extracellular currents. (0.5 marks) 2. **EPSPs/IPSPs** develop in the dendrites, creating a current sink at the synapse and a passive return current along the dendrite. (0.5 marks) 3. **Dipole moment p** is established between sink and source. (0.5 marks) 4. **Population summation** of thousands of synchronously active pyramidal neurons — their parallel alignment allows vector addition, giving V_scalp ∝ N · p. (1 mark) 5. **Volume conduction** through brain tissue, CSF, skull, and scalp attenuates the signal by a factor of ~1/1000. (0.5 marks) 6. **Scalp electrodes** (10-20 system) detect the resulting 10–300 µV potential difference. (0.5 marks) **Why pyramidal neurons dominate (1 mark):** Their apical dendrites are aligned **perpendicular to the cortical surface**, so their postsynaptic dipoles are co-oriented, enabling constructive summation. Randomly oriented interneurons (e.g., stellate cells) cancel out. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Pitfall — KTU Valuation Penalty:** > Students frequently forget to state the **threshold potential value of −55 mV** or the typical **EEG amplitude range of 10–300 µV** explicitly. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing such standard numerical values. Always memorize the standard clinical/physiological numbers: RMP = −70 mV, threshold = −55 mV, E_K = −90 mV, E_Na = +60 mV, EEG = 10–300 µV, EEG bandwidth = 0.5–100 Hz. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]** > **(a)** Describe the structure of a typical neuron with a labelled diagram. Differentiate between chemical and electrical synapses. > > **(b)** Explain the phases of an action potential with a phase diagram. State the ionic basis of each phase and the significance of the threshold potential. #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Neuron Structure (4 marks):** A typical neuron consists of: - **Soma (Cell Body):** Contains the nucleus, organelles; integrates incoming synaptic inputs. - **Dendrites:** Branched, tree-like extensions that receive synaptic inputs from thousands of other neurons. - **Axon Hillock:** The trigger zone; site of action potential initiation. - **Axon:** Long, slender projection that conducts action potentials away from the soma; may be myelinated. - **Myelin Sheath:** Formed by oligodendrocytes (CNS) or Schwann cells (PNS); increases conduction velocity via saltatory conduction. - **Nodes of Ranvier:** Gaps in the myelin where voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are concentrated; enable saltatory AP propagation. - **Synaptic Boutons / Axon Terminals:** Release neurotransmitters at the synapse. **Chemical vs. Electrical Synapses (3 marks):** | Feature | Chemical Synapse | Electrical Synapse | |---|---|---| | **Cleft width** | ~20–40 nm (wide) | ~2–4 nm (very narrow) | | **Transmission mechanism** | Neurotransmitter release | Direct ionic current flow via gap junctions | | **Directionality** | Unidirectional (pre → post) | Often bidirectional | | **Speed** | Slower (~1 ms synaptic delay) | Virtually instantaneous (<0.1 ms) | | **Plasticity** | Highly plastic; basis of learning | Limited plasticity | | **Location in CNS** | Predominant | Rare; e.g., some retinal and hippocampal circuits | | **Signal type** | Can be excitatory (EPSP) or inhibitory (IPSP) | Always excitatory/passive depolarization | --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Phases of the Action Potential (5 marks):** 1. **Resting State (Vₘ = −70 mV):** Na⁺ channels closed; K⁺ leak channels maintain RMP. (0.5 mark) 2. **Depolarization to Threshold (Vₘ reaches −55 mV):** Stimulus opens some Na⁺ channels; regenerative positive feedback begins. (0.5 mark) 3. **Rising Phase (Rapid Depolarization to +30 mV):** Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open en masse; Na⁺ influx drives Vₘ toward E_Na ≈ +60 mV. (1 mark) 4. **Peak (+30 mV):** Na⁺ channels inactivate; K⁺ channels open. (0.5 mark) 5. **Repolarization (Falling Phase):** K⁺ efflux drives Vₘ back toward E_K. (1 mark) 6. **Hyperpolarization (Undershoot, Vₘ ≈ −80 mV):** K⁺ channels close slowly; Vₘ briefly dips below RMP. (0.5 mark) 7. **Return to Resting:** Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump restores ionic gradients. (0.5 mark) 8. **Refractory Periods:** - **Absolute Refractory Period:** No new AP can be elicited regardless of stimulus strength (Na⁺ channels inactivated). (0.25 mark) - **Relative Refractory Period:** A stronger-than-normal stimulus can elicit an AP (some Na⁺ channels recovered, K⁺ still open). (0.25 mark) **Significance of Threshold Potential (2 marks):** - The **threshold (~−55 mV)** is the **all-or-none trigger**; sub-threshold stimuli produce graded local potentials (EPSPs/IPSPs) that decay with distance, while supra-threshold stimuli always produce a full-amplitude AP. - It is determined by the **density of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** at the axon hillock — highest in the neuron, ensuring APs initiate here. - It enforces **digital-like fidelity** of neural coding: information is encoded in **AP frequency and pattern**, not amplitude. - **Clinical relevance:** Anesthetics and toxins (e.g., tetrodotoxin from pufferfish) raise the threshold, blocking AP generation and causing paralysis. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating EPSP and Action Potential:** EPSPs are graded, decaying, sub-threshold events; APs are all-or-none, propagated, and uniform in amplitude. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for confusing the two. > 2. **Forgetting the Ionic Basis:** Students often say "Na⁺ enters" without specifying **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels**. Always state the *channel type* responsible for each ionic flux. > 3. **Missing the "Why" of Threshold:** The threshold is not arbitrary — it is the membrane voltage at which the **inward Na⁺ current exceeds the outward K⁺ current**, initiating regenerative depolarization. > 4. **Confusing Hyperpolarization with Afterpotential:** The undershoot is *hyperpolarization* (below RMP), distinct from a normal action potential's afterpotential. > 5. **Ignoring the Na⁺/K⁺ Pump's Role:** The pump does not generate APs but is essential for *restoring* the gradients. Examiners often ask: "Can APs be sustained indefinitely without the pump?" Answer: **No** — the pump's continuous action is required to maintain the ionic gradients underlying every subsequent AP. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Brain Anatomy and Electrical Activity** **A. Brain Anatomy** - Brain = **Cerebrum** (cognition, motor, sensory) + **Cerebellum** (coordination) + **Brainstem** (autonomic, relay). - Cerebrum has **4 lobes**: Frontal, Parietal, Temporal, Occipital. - CNS = Brain + Spinal Cord; PNS = Somatic + Autonomic. - **~86 billion neurons** in the human brain; roughly equal number of glial cells. - Cortex thickness: **2–4 mm** of gray matter. **B. Neuron Structure** - **Soma** (integration) → **Dendrites** (input) → **Axon Hillock** (trigger) → **Axon** (conduction) → **Synaptic Bouton** (output). - **Myelin** (oligodendrocytes in CNS, Schwann cells in PNS) insulates axons. - **Nodes of Ranvier** enable saltatory conduction. **C. Membrane Potentials — Critical Numbers** - **RMP = −70 mV**; **Threshold = −55 mV**; **E_K ≈ −90 mV**; **E_Na ≈ +60 mV**. - Na⁺/K⁺ pump: **3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP**; electrogenic. - **Nernst equation (37°C):** $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ mV. - **GHK equation** gives realistic Vₘ as weighted sum of Nernst potentials (weights = relative permeabilities). **D. Action Potential — Phases** - Resting → Threshold → **Depolarization** (Na⁺ in) → **Peak +30 mV** → **Repolarization** (K⁺ out) → **Hyperpolarization** (undershoot) → Resting. - All-or-none, ~1 ms duration, propagated. - **Absolute refractory period:** no AP possible. **Relative refractory period:** stronger stimulus needed. - Information encoded in **frequency and timing**, not amplitude. **E. Synaptic Transmission** - Chemical synapses dominate the CNS; one-way (pre → post), ~1 ms delay. - **EPSP** (excitatory, depolarization, e.g., glutamate, Na⁺ influx) vs. **IPSP** (inhibitory, hyperpolarization, e.g., GABA, Cl⁻ influx). - Sequence: AP → Ca²⁺ influx → vesicle fusion → neurotransmitter release → receptor binding → ion channel opening → PSP. **F. EEG Signal Origin** - **Source:** Summed postsynaptic potentials of cortical **pyramidal neurons**. - **Pyramidal alignment** is essential (perpendicular to cortex). - **Volume conduction** attenuates signal ~1000× through brain → CSF → skull → scalp. - **Scalp EEG:** 10–300 µV, 0.5–100 Hz. - **Frequency bands:** Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30) Hz. **G. Engineering / BSP Connections** - EEG bandwidth dictates **filter design** (bandpass 0.5–100 Hz). - Volume conduction causes **low spatial resolution** → motivates **inverse source localization** (e.g., LORETA). - Artifact removal (EMG, EOG, ECG) is critical — they share frequency/amplitude ranges with EEG. - Understanding biophysics is essential for **valid feature extraction** in BCI, seizure detection, and neurological diagnostics. **H. KTU Board Exam Quick-Fire Formulas** - $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ mV at 37°C. - $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$ (length constant). - $V_{scalp} \propto N \cdot p_0$ (coherent aligned population). - Na⁺/K⁺ pump stoichiometry: **3 out / 2 in**.

topics.title: Basics of EEG Waveform and Electrode Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Basics of EEG Waveform and Electrode Placement** # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Electroencephalography (EEG): Formal Definition **Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique that records the summed postsynaptic electrical activity of large populations of cortical neurons (specifically pyramidal cells of the cerebral cortex) using electrodes placed on the scalp. The resulting time-series voltage signal, typically in the microvolt ($\mu V$) range, is called the **Electroencephalogram**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** EEG is the spontaneous, rhythmic electrical activity of the brain measured from the scalp surface, used clinically for the diagnosis of neurological disorders such as epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies, and brain death. The signal originates from **excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs and IPSPs)** of cortical pyramidal neurons aligned perpendicularly to the cortical surface. When thousands of these neurons fire synchronously, the resulting dipole generates a measurable potential difference on the scalp. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "Listening to a Stadium Crowd" Imagine you are standing outside a massive football stadium with 100,000 people inside. - **The Stadium (Brain):** The brain is the stadium, full of electrically active cells. - **The Crowd (Neurons):** Each person is a neuron, murmuring and cheering. - **Microphones on the Roof (EEG Electrodes):** You cannot hear individual whispers, but if the crowd chants in unison (synchronized firing), the microphones pick up a rhythmic, collective hum. - **The Recording (EEG Signal):** This collective hum is the EEG waveform. - **The Mood of the Crowd (Brain State):** A sleepy crowd (Delta waves), a relaxed daydreaming crowd (Alpha waves), an alert crowd (Beta waves) — different rhythms tell you about the brain's state. Just as the stadium hum changes with the game, the EEG waveform changes with sleep, attention, disease, and consciousness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constants for EEG:** > - **Signal Amplitude:** $10$ to $100\ \mu V$ (typically) > - **Frequency Range:** $0.1$ to $100\ Hz$ (clinically relevant: $0.5$–$30\ Hz$) > - **Electrode Impedance:** Should be kept **below $5\ k\Omega$** to minimize noise > - **Number of Standard Channels (Clinical):** $21$ channels (10-20 system) to $256$ channels (High-density research systems) ## 1.3 Why EEG Matters in Biomedical Engineering EEG is **cheap, non-invasive, and has excellent temporal resolution** (millisecond range), making it indispensable for: | Application | Use Case | |---|---| | **Clinical Diagnostics** | Epilepsy, sleep disorders, tumors, encephalopathies | | **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** | Motor imagery classification, P300 spellers | | **Neuromarketing / Cognitive Research** | Attention, emotion, workload measurement | | **Anesthesia Depth Monitoring** | BIS (Bispectral Index) | | **Polysomnography** | Sleep stage scoring | | **Neurofeedback Therapy** | ADHD, anxiety, rehabilitation | > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** EEG amplitude is approximately **1000 times smaller** than ECG. Hence EEG acquisition requires much higher gain amplifiers and superior Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR > $80\ dB$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin of the EEG Signal: The Neuronal Dipole The EEG signal does **not** arise from action potentials (which are too brief and asynchronous). It arises from: 1. **Synchronized postsynaptic potentials** (EPSPs/IPSPs) of cortical pyramidal neurons. 2. These neurons are arranged in **palisade-like columns** with apical dendrites aligned perpendicularly to the cortical surface. 3. This geometric alignment creates an **equivalent current dipole** that can be detected at the scalp. **Volume Conductor Problem:** The skull acts as a **low-pass spatial filter**, smearing and attenuating the signal as it passes through the brain, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), skull, and scalp (a 4-layered volume conductor). ## 2.2 Classification of EEG Rhythms (The "Five Waves") The EEG signal is decomposed into five classical frequency bands. Each rhythm correlates with a specific brain state, age, and pathology. | Rhythm | Frequency Band | Amplitude ($\mu V$) | Associated Brain State | Location | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Delta ($\delta$)** | $0.5$ – $4\ Hz$ | $20$ – $200$ | Deep sleep (Stage 3/4 NREM), infants, pathological | Frontal in adults, posterior in children | | **Theta ($\theta$)** | $4$ – $8\ Hz$ | $20$ – $100$ | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation, creativity | Temporal/parietal | | **Alpha ($\alpha$)** | $8$ – $13\ Hz$ | $20$ – $60$ | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed, calm | Occipital (posterior dominant) | | **Beta ($\beta$)** | $13$ – $30\ Hz$ | $2$ – $20$ | Active thinking, focus, anxiety, alertness | Frontal/central | | **Gamma ($\gamma$)** | $> 30\ Hz$ (up to $100\ Hz$) | Very low | Higher cognitive processing, perception binding | Frontal, somatosensory | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Alpha Blocking (Alpha Attenuation):** When a subject opens their eyes or performs mental arithmetic, the alpha rhythm is **replaced** by desynchronized low-amplitude beta activity. This phenomenon is called the **Berger Effect** (after Hans Berger, the inventor of EEG). ## 2.3 Other Important EEG Patterns (Clinical) - **Mu Rhythm ($\mu$):** $8$ – $13\ Hz$ over the **motor cortex (C3, C4)**, attenuated by movement or motor imagery. Critical for BCI. - **Vertex Sharp Waves (V-waves):** Seen in Stage 1 sleep. - **Sleep Spindles:** $12$ – $14\ Hz$ bursts in Stage 2 sleep. - **K-Complexes:** Sharp high-amplitude waves in Stage 2 sleep. - **Lambda Waves:** Sharp transients in occipital region during visual scanning. - **Pathological Patterns:** Spike-and-wave ($3\ Hz$, absence epilepsy), Polyspike, Periodic complexes. ## 2.4 The International 10-20 Electrode Placement System The **10-20 System** (developed by Herbert Jasper in 1958) is the universal standard for placing scalp electrodes. It is based on: - **Four anatomical landmarks:** Nasion (Nz), Inion (Iz), Left Pre-auricular (A1), Right Pre-auricular (A2). - **Distances** are measured as percentages (10% or 20%) of the total skull perimeter or the nasion-inion / pre-auricular distances. ### 2.4.1 Electrode Nomenclature - **Odd numbers ($1, 3, 5, 7$):** Left hemisphere. - **Even numbers ($2, 4, 6, 8$):** Right hemisphere. - **"z" suffix (e.g., Fz, Cz, Pz):** Midline electrodes. ### 2.4.2 Standard Electrode Sites (21-Channel Clinical Setup) | Site Prefix | Cortical Region | |---|---| | **Fp (Fp1, Fp2)** | Frontopolar (prefrontal) | | **F (F3, F4, F7, F8)** | Frontal | | **T (T3, T4, T5, T6)** | Temporal | | **C (C3, C4, Cz)** | Central (motor/sensory strip) | | **P (P3, P4, Pz)** | Parietal | | **O (O1, O2)** | Occipital (visual cortex) | | **A (A1, A2)** | Auricular (earlobe references) | ## 2.5 Montages: How Electrodes Are Connected A **montage** is the specific arrangement of electrode pairs used to display EEG. | Montage Type | Description | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Referential (Monopolar)** | Each active electrode is referenced to a single common reference (e.g., A1, A2, or linked-ears) | Localizing abnormalities | | **Bipolar** | Each channel is the difference between two adjacent active electrodes | Detecting phase reversals (e.g., spikes) | | **Average Reference** | All electrodes averaged and used as reference | Reduces bias of single reference | | **Laplacian (Source Derivation)** | Reference is the weighted average of surrounding electrodes | Enhances local activity, reduces volume conduction | ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | $f_{max} \approx 100\ Hz$ for EEG, so $f_s \geq 200\ Hz$; clinically $f_s = 256$ or $500\ Hz$ is used | | **EEG Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}} \right)$ | dB | $V_{signal} \sim 10$–$100\ \mu V$, $V_{noise}$ includes line noise ($50/60\ Hz$) | | **Electrode-Skin Impedance** | $Z \leq 5\ k\Omega$ | $\Omega$ | Achieved via skin abrasion and conductive gel | | **CMRR Requirement** | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right)$ | dB | Must be $> 80\ dB$ (typically $100\ dB$) | | **10-20 Distance — Nasion to Inion** | Total $N$–$I$ distance | cm | Marked at $10\%$, $20\%$, $20\%$, $20\%$, $20\%$, $10\%$ from nasion | | **10-20 Distance — Pre-auricular to Pre-auricular** | Total $T_3$–$T_4$ distance | cm | Marked similarly for circumferential placement | | **Vertex (Cz) Location** | Intersection of $50\%$ Nasion-Inion and $50\%$ LPA-RPA lines | — | Central reference point | | **Power Spectral Density** | $P(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{T}$ | $\mu V^2 / Hz$ | Used to identify dominant EEG rhythms | | **Wavelength in Brain Tissue** | $\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$, where $v \approx 6$–$10\ m/s$ | m | $v$ is conduction velocity in tissue | > [!NOTE] > **Conduction Velocity in Brain Tissue:** $v \approx 6$ to $10\ m/s$ (much slower than peripheral nerves at $50$–$120\ m/s$ because EEG source is postsynaptic, not axonal action potential). ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility The 10-20 system is the **foundation of every clinical EEG machine** and modern BCI headsets (e.g., Emotiv EPOC, OpenBCI, NeuroSky). Reducing the system to standardized coordinates enables: - **Reproducibility** across hospitals and research labs. - **Source localization** (e.g., LORETA, sLORETA) — solving the **inverse problem** of EEG. - **Comparison with fMRI/PET** for multimodal brain mapping. - **Standardized BCI motor-imagery datasets** (e.g., BCI Competition IV-2a, 22 electrodes at 10-20 positions). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Sampling Rate for EEG To faithfully capture all clinically relevant EEG rhythms (including high-frequency gamma bursts up to $f_{max} = 100\ Hz$), we apply the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**. **Step 1: State the Theorem.** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ **Step 2: Identify the Maximum EEG Frequency.** From the KTU formula sheet, the highest clinically relevant EEG rhythm (Gamma) extends up to: $$f_{max} = 100\ Hz$$ **Step 3: Apply the Inequality.** $$\begin{aligned} f_s &\geq 2 \cdot f_{max} \\ f_s &\geq 2 \cdot 100\ Hz \\ f_s &\geq 200\ Hz \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Choose the Practical Sampling Rate.** In practice, an **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** (typically 4th-order Butterworth) is set at $f_c \approx 70$–$100\ Hz$, and the ADC samples at a standard rate of $f_s = 256\ Hz$, $500\ Hz$, or $1000\ Hz$ to provide a guard band. $$\boxed{f_s = 256\ Hz \quad \text{(typical clinical)}}$$ **Step 5: Justify the Margin.** The excess bandwidth ($\sim 28\%$ above Nyquist) accommodates: - Non-ideal anti-aliasing filter roll-off. - Sharp transients (spikes, K-complexes) that contain higher spectral content. - Phase linearity preservation in digital filters. ## 3.2 Derivation: Location of Vertex (Cz) in the 10-20 System The vertex electrode **Cz** is the intersection of two imaginary lines: - The **sagittal line** from Nasion (Nz) to Inion (Iz). - The **coronal line** from Left Pre-Auricular (A1) to Right Pre-Auricular (A2). **Step 1: Measure the Total Distances.** Let $D_{NI}$ = distance from Nasion to Inion. Let $D_{AA}$ = distance from A1 to A2. **Step 2: Compute the 50% Mark on Each Line.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Sagittal midpoint} &= D_{NI} \times 0.50 \\ \text{Coronal midpoint} &= D_{AA} \times 0.50 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Intersection defines Cz.** $$\boxed{Cz = \left(0.50 \cdot D_{NI}, \;\; 0.50 \cdot D_{AA}\right)}$$ **Example Calculation:** Suppose $D_{NI} = 36\ cm$ and $D_{AA} = 34\ cm$. Then: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Cz location from Nasion} &= 0.50 \times 36 = 18\ cm \\ \text{Cz location from A1} &= 0.50 \times 34 = 17\ cm \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: 10-20 System Percentage Markings on the Sagittal Line The sagittal line (Nasion → Inion) is divided into **five segments** with ratios **10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10%** summing to 100%. **Step 1: List the electrode positions in order from Nasion:** - Fpz (at $10\%$) - Fz (at $10\% + 20\% = 30\%$) - Cz (at $10\% + 20\% + 20\% = 50\%$) - Pz (at $10\% + 20\% + 20\% + 20\% = 70\%$) - Oz (at $10\% + 20\% + 20\% + 20\% + 20\% = 90\%$) - Iz (at $100\%$) **Step 2: Generalize.** For a total distance $D_{NI}$, the position of the $n$-th electrode from Nasion is: $$P_n = D_{NI} \cdot s_n, \quad \text{where } s_n \in \{0.10,\ 0.30,\ 0.50,\ 0.70,\ 0.90,\ 1.00\}$$ **Step 3: Numerical Example.** For $D_{NI} = 36\ cm$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{Fpz} &= 36 \times 0.10 = 3.6\ cm \\ P_{Fz} &= 36 \times 0.30 = 10.8\ cm \\ P_{Cz} &= 36 \times 0.50 = 18.0\ cm \\ P_{Pz} &= 36 \times 0.70 = 25.2\ cm \\ P_{Oz} &= 36 \times 0.90 = 32.4\ cm \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation: SNR of an EEG Recording **Step 1: Define signal and noise powers.** $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} \right) = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{rms,signal}}{V_{rms,noise}} \right)$$ **Step 2: Typical Values.** - $V_{rms,signal} \approx 50\ \mu V$ (mid-range EEG amplitude). - $V_{rms,noise} \approx 5\ \mu V$ (after shielding and $50\ Hz$ notch filter). **Step 3: Compute SNR.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{SNR}_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{50\ \mu V}{5\ \mu V} \right) \\ &= 20 \log_{10}(10) \\ &= 20 \times 1 \\ &= 20\ dB \end{aligned}$$ A well-designed clinical EEG system achieves **SNR $\geq 20\ dB$**, while research-grade systems target **$30$–$40\ dB$**. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EEG vs ECG vs EMG | Parameter | EEG | ECG | EMG | |---|---|---|---| | **Origin** | Cortical postsynaptic potentials | Cardiac depolarization | Muscle fiber action potentials | | **Amplitude** | $10$–$100\ \mu V$ | $0.5$–$5\ mV$ | $0.1$–$5\ mV$ | | **Frequency** | $0.5$–$100\ Hz$ | $0.05$–$150\ Hz$ | $20$–$2000\ Hz$ | | **Channels (Standard)** | $21$–$256$ | $12$ (clinical) | $1$–$4$ per muscle | | **Reference** | Linked-ears, average | Wilson's Central Terminal | Belly-tendon | | **Sampling Rate** | $256$–$1000\ Hz$ | $500\ Hz$ | $1000$–$2000\ Hz$ | | **Key Artifact** | Eye blinks, muscle | Motion, $50\ Hz$ | Crosstalk, motion | ## 3.6 Common EEG Artifacts and Their Frequency Signatures | Artifact | Frequency | Amplitude | Removal Method | |---|---|---|---| | **Eye Blink (EOG)** | $< 4\ Hz$ | $> 200\ \mu V$ | Regression, ICA | | **Muscle (EMG)** | $> 20\ Hz$ | Variable | ICA, low-pass filtering | | **Line Noise ($50/60\ Hz$)** | Exactly $50/60\ Hz$ | $5$–$50\ \mu V$ | Notch filter, notch at $f_{line}$ | | **Cardiac (ECG)** | $1$–$2\ Hz$ | $5$–$20\ \mu V$ | ICA, average heartbeat subtraction | | **Sweat / Drift** | $< 0.5\ Hz$ | High | High-pass filter at $0.1$–$0.5\ Hz$ | | **Electrode Pop** | Broadband transient | Very high | Automatic rejection, interpolation | > [!TIP] > **KTU Engineering Insight:** The $50\ Hz$ (India/Europe) or $60\ Hz$ (USA) powerline interference is the **single largest noise source** in EEG. A **notch filter** (IIR with very narrow bandwidth) or **adaptive filtering** using a reference channel is mandatory. # 4. High Cognition] Delta --> Use1[Clinical: Sleep staging tumor detection] Theta --> Use2[Clinical: Drowsiness meditation ADHD] Alpha --> Use3[Clinical: Relaxation BCI Baseline] Beta --> Use4[Clinical: Alertness anxiety motor planning] Gamma --> Use5[Research: Perception binding consciousness] ``` ## 4.4 Montage Configurations Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RefMontage[Referential Monopolar Montage] E1[Active Electrode F3] --> Diff1[(Differential Amplifier)] E2[Active Electrode C3] --> Diff1 E3[Active Electrode P3] --> Diff1 Ref1[Reference A1 Linked Ears] --> Diff1 Diff1 --> Out1[Output Channel] end subgraph BiMontage[Bipolar Montage] E4[Fp1] --> Diff2[(Diff Amp 1)] E5[F3] --> Diff2 Diff2 --> Diff3[(Diff Amp 2)] E5 --> Diff3 E6[C3] --> Diff3 end subgraph AvgMontage[Average Reference Montage] E7[Fp1] --> Sum[(Average of All Electrodes)] E8[F3] --> Sum E9[C3] --> Sum E10[P3] --> Sum E11[O1] --> Sum Sum --> Out2[Common Reference Signal] end ``` ## 4.5 EEG Acquisition System Architecture (Block Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Subject[Subject] Brain[Brain Cortex Neurons] end subgraph Acquisition[Acquisition Hardware] Electrodes[Electrode Array 21 to 256 Channels] Amp[Multichannel Amplifier] Filter[Analog Filter Bank] ADC[Analog to Digital Converter] end subgraph Processing[Digital Signal Processing] Artifact[Artifact Removal ICA or Regression] Filter2[Digital Bandpass 0.5 to 50 Hz] Feature[Feature Extraction Power Band Raties] Classify[Classifier BCI or Diagnostic] end subgraph Output[Output] Display[Real Time Display] Storage[Data Storage EDF Format] Alert[Clinical Alert Seizure Detection] end Brain --> Electrodes Electrodes --> Amp Amp --> Filter Filter --> ADC ADC --> Artifact Artifact --> Filter2 Filter2 --> Feature Feature --> Classify Classify --> Display Classify --> Storage Classify --> Alert ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model] **Define EEG. List the five standard EEG rhythms with their frequency ranges.** **Model Answer:** **Electroencephalogram (EEG):** It is a non-invasive recording of the spontaneous electrical activity of the brain obtained from electrodes placed on the scalp. The signal amplitude is typically $10$–$100\ \mu V$ and the frequency range is $0.5$–$100\ Hz$. **Five Standard EEG Rhythms:** | Rhythm | Frequency Range | |---|---| | Delta ($\delta$) | $0.5$ – $4\ Hz$ | | Theta ($\theta$) | $4$ – $8\ Hz$ | | Alpha ($\alpha$) | $8$ – $13\ Hz$ | | Beta ($\beta$) | $13$ – $30\ Hz$ | | Gamma ($\gamma$) | $> 30\ Hz$ | --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model] **Explain the 10-20 electrode placement system. Why is it called "10-20"?** **Model Answer:** The **10-20 system** is the international standard for placing EEG electrodes on the scalp. It is based on four anatomical landmarks: Nasion (Nz), Inion (Iz), Left Pre-auricular point (A1), and Right Pre-auricular point (A2). **Why "10-20"?** The distances between adjacent landmarks are divided into **intervals of 10% and 20%** of the total skull perimeter. Electrodes are placed at these percentage marks: - $10\%$ from each landmark. - $20\%$ intervals between subsequent electrodes. This ensures **reproducibility**, **uniform coverage** of the scalp, and **standardization** across clinical centers worldwide. The 21-electrode standard clinical montage uses positions such as Fp1, Fp2, F3, F4, C3, C4, P3, P4, O1, O2, F7, F8, T3, T4, T5, T6, Fz, Cz, Pz, A1, A2. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model] **(a) [7 Marks] Describe the origin of the EEG signal. Explain how the rhythmic activity of the brain is recorded and what determines the amplitude and frequency of the EEG waveform.** **(b) [7 Marks) With a neat block diagram, explain the EEG acquisition system. Mention the role of CMRR and electrode impedance in EEG recording.** #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Origin of EEG Signal:** The EEG signal originates from the **postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs and IPSPs)** of the **pyramidal neurons** in the cerebral cortex. These neurons are aligned perpendicularly to the cortical surface in a palisade arrangement. **Key Reasons EEG is Detectable:** 1. **Synchronous firing** of thousands of pyramidal neurons creates a measurable dipole. 2. The **apical dendrites** are aligned, summing their dipole moments. 3. The **cortical layers** generate a closed current loop with the scalp. **Volume Conductor:** The signal travels through brain tissue, CSF, skull, and scalp (a 4-layered inhomogeneous medium) before reaching the electrodes. The skull acts as a **low-pass spatial filter**, attenuating and smearing the signal. **Determinants of Amplitude:** - **Degree of Synchronization:** Synchronous activity → high amplitude (e.g., Delta in deep sleep). - **Number of Active Neurons:** More neurons → higher amplitude. - **Distance from Source:** Deeper sources produce smaller scalp signals. - **Orientation of Dipole:** Tangential dipoles are poorly seen; radial dipoles are best seen. **Determinants of Frequency:** - **Brain State:** Sleep produces slow waves (Delta), wakefulness produces faster waves (Beta). - **Thalamocortical Oscillations:** The thalamus acts as a pacemaker, generating rhythmic bursts. - **Neuronal Membrane Time Constant:** Affects the resonance frequency of cortical circuits. - **Neurotransmitter Balance:** GABAergic inhibition shapes rhythm frequency. #### Part (b) — Model Solution **EEG Acquisition System Block Diagram:** ``` Scalp Electrodes → Differential Amplifier → Bandpass Filter → Notch Filter (50 Hz) → ADC → Digital Processing → Display ``` **Stages Explained:** 1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Convert ionic currents into electronic signals. Placed using the 10-20 system with conductive gel to reduce skin-electrode impedance. 2. **Differential Amplifier:** Amplifies the voltage difference between two electrodes. Provides high gain ($10^4$–$10^5$) and rejects common-mode noise. 3. **Bandpass Filter:** Passes $0.1$–$100\ Hz$, attenuates DC drift and high-frequency noise. 4. **Notch Filter:** Rejects $50\ Hz$ powerline interference (Q-factor $\sim 30$). 5. **ADC:** Samples at $f_s = 256$–$1000\ Hz$ with 16–24 bit resolution. 6. **Digital Processing:** Artifact removal (ICA), averaging, spectral analysis. **Role of CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio):** $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right)$$ For EEG, the desired signal ($\mu V$) is much smaller than environmental noise (mV). **CMRR must exceed $80\ dB$** (typically $100\ dB$) to reject common-mode $50\ Hz$ interference and other environmental noise picked up by both electrodes. **Role of Electrode Impedance:** - High electrode-skin impedance (>$10\ k\Omega$) increases **thermal noise** (Johnson-Nyquist noise): $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$. - Mismatched impedance between two electrodes converts common-mode noise into differential noise, defeating CMRR. - **Standard practice:** Keep impedance **below $5\ k\Omega$** and **balanced** between electrode pairs (mismatch $< 1\ k\Omega$). --- ### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model] **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the International 10-20 system of electrode placement with a diagram. List the locations of all 21 electrodes and their corresponding brain regions.** **(b) [7 Marks) Compare and contrast the different types of EEG montages. Discuss the clinical significance of alpha rhythm and the Berger effect.** #### Part (a) — Model Solution **International 10-20 System:** The 10-20 system, standardized by the International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN), uses four anatomical landmarks: - **Nasion (Nz):** Frontal depression between forehead and nose. - **Inion (Iz):** Bump at the back of the skull. - **Left Pre-auricular Point (A1):** In front of left ear. - **Right Pre-auricular Point (A2):** In front of right ear. **Measurement Procedure:** 1. Measure the **Nasion-to-Inion** distance ($D_{NI}$). 2. Measure the **A1-to-A2** distance (circumferential, $D_{AA}$). 3. Mark points at **10% and 20% intervals** along these lines. 4. The **Vertex (Cz)** is at the intersection of the $50\%$ sagittal and coronal lines. **21 Standard Electrodes and Their Brain Regions:** | Electrode | Brain Region | Hemisphere | |---|---|---| | Fp1, Fp2 | Frontopolar (prefrontal) | Left, Right | | F3, F4 | Dorsolateral prefrontal | Left, Right | | F7, F8 | Inferior frontal (Broca's area) | Left, Right | | T3, T4 | Mid-temporal | Left, Right | | T5, T6 | Posterior temporal | Left, Right | | C3, C4 | Sensorimotor cortex | Left, Right | | P3, P4 | Parietal | Left, Right | | O1, O2 | Occipital (visual cortex) | Left, Right | | Fz, Cz, Pz | Midline frontal, central, parietal | — | | A1, A2 | Earlobe references | Left, Right | **Naming Convention:** Odd numbers = Left; Even numbers = Right; "z" = Midline. #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Comparison of EEG Montages:** | Feature | Referential | Bipolar | Average Reference | |---|---|---|---| | **Reference** | Single common electrode (e.g., A1+A2) | Adjacent active electrodes | Average of all electrodes | | **Amplitude** | High (absolute voltages) | Lower (differences) | Moderate | | **Best For** | Localizing lesions, amplitude asymmetries | Detecting phase reversals (spikes) | Reducing reference bias | | **Limitation** | Reference contamination | Less accurate localization | Loses absolute amplitude info | | **Clinical Use** | Standard clinical EEG | Epilepsy monitoring | Research, source localization | **Alpha Rhythm ($\alpha$):** - **Frequency:** $8$–$13\ Hz$. - **Amplitude:** $20$–$60\ \mu V$. - **Location:** Maximal over the **occipital region** (O1, O2) in awake, relaxed adults. - **Conditions for Occurrence:** Eyes closed, relaxed wakefulness, dark room. - **Clinical Significance:** Loss of alpha is seen in cortical damage; asymmetric alpha suggests focal lesions; excessive alpha during effort indicates attention deficits. **Berger Effect (Alpha Blocking / Alpha Desynchronization):** Discovered by **Hans Berger** (the inventor of EEG) in 1929. When a subject with eyes closed (producing strong alpha rhythm) is presented with a stimulus (light, sound, mental task) or simply **opens their eyes**, the alpha rhythm is **abruptly replaced by desynchronized low-amplitude beta activity**. **Mechanism:** Sensory input activates the **reticular activating system** → desynchronizes thalamocortical oscillations → abolishes the synchronous alpha rhythm. **Clinical Significance:** Absent or reduced Berger effect may indicate **encephalopathy, coma, or deep anesthesia** (graded by the **BIS Index**). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing Frequency Bands:** Many students incorrectly write Alpha as $13$–$30\ Hz$ (mixing with Beta). Memorize: **Delta $0.5$–$4$, Theta $4$–$8$, Alpha $8$–$13$, Beta $13$–$30$, Gamma $> 30$ Hz**. > 2. **Forgetting "z" Suffix:** Midline electrodes are Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz, Fpz — do not write them as F1, F2 etc. > 3. **10-20 Percentage Error:** The system is called 10-20 because intervals are 10% AND 20%, not just 20%. Some students write the wrong ratios. > 4. **CMRR vs SNR Confusion:** CMRR rejects *common-mode* noise (environmental); SNR is the *signal quality* after all processing. > 5. **Sampling Theorem Misapplication:** For EEG, $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 200\ Hz$ minimum, not $2000\ Hz$ (that is for EMG). > 6. **Skipping Diagrams:** In 14-mark questions, always include a labeled block diagram or 10-20 layout — KTU awards 2–3 marks for neat sketches. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** ### A. Core Concepts - EEG = recording of summed postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons. - Amplitude: $10$–$100\ \mu V$; Frequency: $0.5$–$100\ Hz$. - EEG is **not** action potentials — they are too fast and asynchronous. - The skull is a **spatial low-pass filter** (volume conductor). - Hans Berger (1924) recorded the first human EEG. ### B. The Five Rhythms (Memorize in Order) - **Delta** ($0.5$–$4\ Hz$): Deep sleep, infants, tumors. - **Theta** ($4$–$8\ Hz$): Drowsiness, meditation, ADHD. - **Alpha** ($8$–$13\ Hz$): Relaxed, eyes closed, occipital. - **Beta** ($13$–$30\ Hz$): Alert, thinking, anxiety, frontal. - **Gamma** ($> 30\ Hz$): Cognition, perception binding. ### C. 10-20 System - Landmarks: **Nasion, Inion, A1, A2**. - Cz = intersection of 50% sagittal and 50% coronal lines. - **Odd = Left, Even = Right, z = Midline.** - 21 standard clinical electrodes. ### D. Montages - **Referential:** Each channel vs. a common reference. - **Bipolar:** Adjacent electrode differences (phase reversal detection). - **Average Reference:** All-electrode average as reference. - **Laplacian:** Spatial second derivative (local activity). ### E. Acquisition Requirements - Electrode impedance $< 5\ k\Omega$ (mismatch $< 1\ k\Omega$). - CMRR $> 80\ dB$ (ideally $100\ dB$). - Bandpass: $0.1$–$100\ Hz$; Notch at $50\ Hz$ (India). - Sampling rate: $f_s = 256$–$1000\ Hz$; Resolution: $16$–$24$ bit. ### F. Key Artifacts & Removal - **EOG (eye blink):** $< 4\ Hz$, remove via ICA or regression. - **EMG (muscle):** $> 20\ Hz$, remove via ICA or low-pass filter. - **$50\ Hz$ line noise:** Notch filter or adaptive filter. - **ECG artifact:** ICA or template subtraction. - **Electrode pop:** Interpolation, automatic rejection. ### G. Clinical Conditions Detected by EEG - **Epilepsy:** Spike-and-wave, polyspike, focal spikes. - **Sleep disorders:** Sleep staging, sleep apnea. - **Encephalopathy:** Generalized slowing (Delta/Theta dominance). - **Brain death:** Electrocerebral silence. - **Tumors:** Focal slowing, asymmetry. ### H. KTU Board Exam Key Points - Always draw the **10-20 layout** in 14-mark questions. - Always include a **block diagram of EEG acquisition**. - Always state **frequency AND amplitude** for each rhythm. - Always mention **CMRR and electrode impedance** in amplifier questions. - Always state **Nyquist theorem** when discussing sampling. > [!TIP] > **Final Memory Aid:** *"D T A B G"* — **D**elta, **T**heta, **A**lpha, **B**eta, **G**amma — going from slow to fast, deep sleep to peak cognition. Remember: **Slow = Synchronous = High Amplitude**, **Fast = Desynchronized = Low Amplitude**.

topics.title: Methods and Devices for EEG Signal Acquisition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Methods and Devices for EEG Signal Acquisition # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of EEG **Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique that records the electrical activity of the brain by placing multiple electrodes along the scalp. The EEG signal represents the **summed postsynaptic dendritic potentials** of large populations of cortical neurons (primarily pyramidal cells oriented perpendicularly to the cortical surface), captured through the intervening layers of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), skull, and scalp. From the KTU 2024 syllabus perspective, **EEG Signal Acquisition** refers to the complete instrumentation chain — from electrode placement, through differential amplification, to analog-to-digital conversion — required to faithfully capture and digitize these microvolt-level bioelectric potentials for diagnostic and research purposes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The EEG signal amplitude ranges from **1 µV to 100 µV** (typical: **10–50 µV**) with a usable frequency band of **0.1 Hz to 100 Hz** (clinical interest concentrated in **0.5–30 Hz**). The signal is therefore **5–10 times smaller** than ECG and **100 times smaller** than EMG, demanding exceptional noise rejection. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Listening to a Whisper in a Stadium" Imagine you are inside a packed cricket stadium (the human head) where **70,000 crore neurons** are firing in chaotic patterns. Standing outside the stadium (on the scalp), you want to listen to a *single whispered conversation* (the EEG signal of ~20 µV). Around you: - A **jet engine is roaring** (50/60 Hz mains interference at ~220 V across the body). - **Drums are beating randomly** (muscle artifacts — EMG, up to several mV). - **Rain is pattering on the roof** (thermal noise from electrode–skin impedance). - People are **shifting in their seats** (eye-blink and movement artifacts). Your job as the EEG engineer is to build a microphone (electrode + amplifier) so sensitive it picks up the whisper, so smart it ignores the roar, and so stable it doesn't add its own hiss. That is what **EEG signal acquisition** is all about. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference:** The internationally accepted electrode placement convention is the **International 10–20 System** (Jasper, 1958), which uses anatomical landmarks (nasion, inion, pre-auricular points) to position **21 standard electrodes** at 10% or 20% of skull arc distances. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Signal Amplitude | **1–100 µV** | Extremely low; demands low-noise design | | Frequency Band | **0.1–100 Hz** | Spans DC to high-frequency cognitive rhythms | | Clinical Bandwidth | **0.5–30 Hz** | Where dominant rhythms (δ, θ, α, β) live | | Electrode–Scalp Impedance | **< 5 kΩ** (clinical) | Critical for low-noise pickup | | Common-Mode Voltage | Up to **±1 V** (mains-coupled) | Rejected via high CMRR | | Required CMRR | **≥ 100 dB** (>10⁵) | At 50/60 Hz mains frequency | | ADC Resolution | **≥ 16 bits** | To resolve sub-µV steps | | Sampling Rate | **≥ 200 Hz** (clinical); 500 Hz (research) | Nyquist-compliant for 100 Hz band | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The EEG Signal — Origin and Characteristics The scalp EEG is generated by **excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs/IPSPs)** in the dendrites of cortical pyramidal neurons. These neurons are aligned in parallel columns, perpendicular to the cortical surface, so their summed dipoles create an open electrical field that propagates through: $$\text{Neuron} \rightarrow \text{CSF} \rightarrow \text{Skull} \rightarrow \text{Scalp} \rightarrow \text{Electrode}$$ Each layer acts as a **spatial low-pass filter**, smearing the intracranial activity and limiting the spatial resolution of scalp EEG to roughly **2–3 cm**. ## 2.2 EEG Rhythm Bands (The "Alphabet" of EEG) The EEG signal is conventionally decomposed into five canonical rhythm bands, each associated with distinct physiological and cognitive states: | Rhythm | Frequency Range | Amplitude (µV) | Behavioral / Clinical State | |---|---|---|---| | **Delta (δ)** | **0.5–4 Hz** | 20–200 | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, pathological in awake adults | | **Theta (θ)** | **4–8 Hz** | < 30 | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation, hippocampal activity | | **Alpha (α)** | **8–13 Hz** | 20–60 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed (posterior dominant) | | **Beta (β)** | **13–30 Hz** | < 20 | Active thinking, focused attention, anxiety | | **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30 Hz** | < 5 | Sensory binding, higher cognitive processing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this clinically useful?** Disorders like epilepsy show **paroxysmal spike-and-wave discharges**, sleep disorders are diagnosed via altered **delta/theta profiles**, and encephalopathies flatten or slow the background rhythm. Every subsequent acquisition design must preserve these bands faithfully. ## 2.3 The EEG Acquisition Chain — Step-by-Step Logic A complete EEG acquisition system comprises six functional stages: 1. **Electrodes** — transduce ionic (brain) currents into electronic (voltage) signals at the scalp. 2. **Electrode–Skin Interface** — establishes a stable **half-cell potential** via electrolytic gel. 3. **Differential Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** — amplifies the small differential EEG while rejecting the large common-mode mains interference; provides high input impedance and high CMRR. 4. **Bandpass Filter (0.1–100 Hz)** — removes DC offset and high-frequency muscle/line noise. 5. **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz)** — specifically attenuates residual power-line interference. 6. **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA) + Anti-Aliasing Filter + ADC** — scales the signal to the ADC input range, removes aliasing frequencies, and digitizes the waveform. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---| | Half-cell potential (Ag/AgCl) | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(a_{\text{Ag}^+})$ | $E \approx +0.223$ V vs SHE | | Electrode-skin impedance | $Z_e = R_s \Vert \left(\dfrac{1}{j\omega C_d}\right)$ | $R_s$: gel resistance; $C_d$: double-layer capacitance | | Differential gain of INA | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | Set by external gain resistor $R_g$ | | CMRR (in dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Required $\geq$ 100 dB at 50/60 Hz | | Common-mode to differential conversion | $V_{\text{out,cm}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cm}}}{\text{CMRR}}$ | Mains $\sim$1 V; CMRR $=10^5 \Rightarrow V_{\text{out,cm}} = 10\,\mu$V | | Total input-referred noise | $v_n = \sqrt{e_n^2 + i_n^2 Z_e^2 + 4kTR_e}$ | $e_n$ V/√Hz; $i_n$ A/√Hz | | Thermal (Johnson) noise | $v_n = \sqrt{4kTR\Delta f}$ | $k = 1.38\times10^{-23}$ J/K | | Nyquist sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | $f_s \geq 200$ Hz for EEG | | ADC quantization step | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^N}$ | $N = 16 \Rightarrow \Delta \approx 1.5\,\mu$V @ $\pm$5 V range | | ENOB from SNR | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Effective bits | | Notch filter (twin-T) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Centered at 50/60 Hz | | -3 dB cutoff (1st-order HPF) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | High-pass for DC blocking | | -3 dB cutoff (1st-order LPF) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Low-pass for high-frequency roll-off | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** The same EEG acquisition chain — with minor parameter tweaks — is used clinically for **long-term epilepsy monitoring (EMU)**, in **BCI (Brain–Computer Interface)** devices for prosthetic control, in **polysomnography** for sleep staging, and in **consumer neurotech** (e.g., Muse, OpenBCI). The principles generalize to any microvolt-level bioelectric signal. ## 2.5 Why Each Stage Is Non-Negotiable - **Differential (not single-ended) amplification** is essential because both electrodes sit on a body that is capacitively coupled to the **220 V/50 Hz** (or 110 V/60 Hz) AC mains; the resulting common-mode voltage can be 1000× larger than the EEG itself. - **High input impedance (> 100 MΩ)** prevents the amplifier from loading the high-impedance electrode interface, which would otherwise attenuate and distort the microvolt signal. - **Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit** actively drives the body to a known reference potential, reducing the common-mode voltage that the INA must reject — a feedback technique borrowed from ECG. - **Notch filtering at 50/60 Hz** is mandatory in any country with mains power; without it, the spectrum is dominated by a single huge line. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required CMRR to Make Common-Mode Mains Invisible **Given:** - Common-mode mains voltage coupled into the patient: $V_{\text{cm}} = 1\,\text{V}$ (worst-case body-coupled mains pickup) - Maximum allowed residual mains contribution at the ADC input: $V_{\text{resid}} = 1\,\mu\text{V}$ (so it does not dominate a 20 µV EEG signal) - Mains frequency: $f_{\text{mains}} = 50\,\text{Hz}$ **Find:** Minimum acceptable CMRR (in dB). **Step 1 — State the relationship between input common-mode voltage and its differential-mode equivalent after imperfect rejection:** $$V_{\text{resid}} = \frac{V_{\text{cm}}}{\text{CMRR}_{\text{ratio}}}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for the CMRR ratio:** $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{V_{\text{cm}}}{V_{\text{resid}}} = \frac{1\,\text{V}}{1\,\mu\text{V}} = \frac{1}{10^{-6}} = 10^6$$ **Step 3 — Convert to decibels (the KTU/industry standard form):** $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(\text{CMRR}_{\text{ratio}}) = 20 \log_{10}(10^6) = 20 \times 6 = 120\,\text{dB}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** A differential amplifier with CMRR of **120 dB at 50 Hz** will reduce a 1 V common-mode mains pickup to a residual **1 µV** — small enough that the EEG signal (typically 20–50 µV) remains the dominant content. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering reality check:** Most commercial INAs (e.g., AD8221, INA128, ADS1299) achieve **>110 dB CMRR at 50 Hz** with careful board layout and active grounding (DRL). A relaxed specification of 80 dB would leave 100 µV of mains residue — burying the EEG signal completely. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: ADC Quantization Resolution Required for Sub-µV EEG **Given:** - ADC full-scale range: $V_{\text{FSR}} = \pm 5\,\text{V}$ (bipolar, so span = 10 V) - Smallest EEG feature we wish to resolve: $\Delta V_{\min} = 0.5\,\mu\text{V}$ **Find:** Minimum number of ADC bits $N$. **Step 1 — Write the quantization step formula:** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FSR, span}}}{2^N}$$ **Step 2 — Set $\Delta \leq \Delta V_{\min}$ and solve for $N$:** $$2^N \geq \frac{V_{\text{FSR, span}}}{\Delta V_{\min}} = \frac{10}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^7$$ **Step 3 — Apply $\log_2$ to both sides:** $$N \geq \log_2(2 \times 10^7) = \log_2(2) + 7\log_2(10) = 1 + 7 \times 3.3219 = 1 + 23.253 = 24.25$$ **Step 4 — Round up to nearest integer (always round up, never down):** $$N_{\min} = 25\,\text{bits}$$ **Step 5 — Practical interpretation:** Although 25 bits is theoretically required, KTU-grade EEG systems use **24-bit Σ–Δ ADCs** (e.g., ADS1299 has 24-bit resolution at 250 Hz) paired with a **PGA gain of 24** so that the input-referred noise and quantization step are both sub-µV. The Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) after noise is typically **18–21 bits**, sufficient for clinical EEG. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Input-Referred Noise at the EEG Amplifier **Given:** - Voltage noise density of INA: $e_n = 8\,\text{nV}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ - Current noise density: $i_n = 0.8\,\text{fA}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ - Electrode impedance: $Z_e = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ (real, worst-case clinical limit) - Source resistance: $R_e = Z_e = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ - Bandwidth: $\Delta f = 100\,\text{Hz}$ (DC to 100 Hz) - Temperature: $T = 310\,\text{K}$ (37 °C body) **Find:** Total RMS input-referred noise. **Step 1 — Compute the Johnson (thermal) noise of the source resistance:** $$v_{n,R} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R_e \Delta f} = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 \times 5000 \times 100}$$ $$v_{n,R} = \sqrt{8.55 \times 10^{-15}} \approx 9.25 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{V} = 0.0925\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{rms}}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the INA's voltage noise contribution over the bandwidth:** $$v_{n,e} = e_n \sqrt{\Delta f} = 8 \times 10^{-9} \times \sqrt{100} = 8 \times 10^{-9} \times 10 = 8 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{V} = 0.08\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{rms}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the INA's current-noise-converted voltage noise across $Z_e$:** $$v_{n,i} = i_n Z_e \sqrt{\Delta f} = 0.8 \times 10^{-15} \times 5000 \times 10 = 4 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{V} = 0.00004\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{rms}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute total RMS noise (assuming uncorrelated sources, RSS):** $$v_{n,\text{total}} = \sqrt{v_{n,R}^2 + v_{n,e}^2 + v_{n,i}^2} = \sqrt{(0.0925)^2 + (0.08)^2 + (0.00004)^2}$$ $$v_{n,\text{total}} \approx \sqrt{0.00856 + 0.00640 + \text{negligible}} = \sqrt{0.01496} \approx 0.1223\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{rms}}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to peak-to-peak (approx. 6σ for Gaussian noise):** $$v_{n,\text{pp}} \approx 6 \times 0.1223 \approx 0.73\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{pp}}$$ **Interpretation:** The total system noise floor is **below 1 µV**, comfortably allowing discrimination of 5 µV EEG features. Note that **electrode thermal noise and INA voltage noise dominate equally** — improving only the INA without reducing $Z_e$ will not halve the noise floor. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Minimum Sampling Rate to Avoid Aliasing **Given:** - Highest EEG frequency of interest: $f_{\max} = 100\,\text{Hz}$ (research-grade, including gamma) - Anti-aliasing filter roll-off slope: 80 dB/octave (4th-order Butterworth) **Find:** Minimum sampling frequency $f_s$. **Step 1 — Nyquist criterion (idealized, no anti-alias filter):** $$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max} = 200\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Practical guard-band factor.** Because real filters cannot brick-wall reject frequencies just above $f_s/2$, we apply a 2.5× oversampling margin (clinical standard): $$f_s \geq 2.5 \times f_{\max} = 250\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 3 — Verify with the chosen 4th-order Butterworth.** At $f = f_s/2 = 125\,\text{Hz}$, the gain is 0 dB by definition. At $f = 200\,\text{Hz}$ (one octave above Nyquist), attenuation is **−80 dB**. Aliasing artifacts would be attenuated by 10,000× — clinically acceptable. **Step 4 — Final design choice:** $f_s = \mathbf{256\,\text{Hz}}$ or **500 Hz**, both common in KTU-referenced EEG systems. Some research systems sample at **1024 Hz or higher** to capture high-frequency oscillations (HFOs) up to 500 Hz. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Instrumentation Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain **Given:** Standard 3-op-amp INA with $R_1 = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$. **Step 1 — Write the standard 3-amp INA gain formula:** $$A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 25\,000}{1\,000}\right)\frac{50\,000}{50\,000} = (1 + 50)(1) = 51$$ **Step 3 — Convert to dB if needed:** $$A_d (\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(51) \approx 34.16\,\text{dB}$$ **Step 4 — Combined chain gain.** With a subsequent PGA of gain 10 and ADC full-scale $\pm 5\,\text{V}$, the smallest detectable input is: $$V_{\text{in,min}} = \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{A_{\text{total}} \cdot 2^N} = \frac{10}{51 \times 10 \times 2^{24}} = \frac{10}{51 \times 10 \times 1.68 \times 10^7}$$ $$V_{\text{in,min}} \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{V} = 0.117\,\mu\text{V}$$ **Interpretation:** The combined analog + digital chain resolves **sub-µV** EEG features, well within clinical EEG amplitude ranges. --- ## 3.6 Summary Matrix of Key Numbers | Specification | Required Value | Justification | |---|---|---| | CMRR @ 50 Hz | **≥ 110 dB** | Suppress 1 V mains to ≤ 3 µV | | Input impedance | **≥ 100 MΩ** | Avoid loading 5 kΩ electrode | | Input-referred noise | **≤ 1 µV rms** | Allow 5 µV feature detection | | Bandwidth | **0.1 – 100 Hz** | Cover δ through γ bands | | Notch attenuation | **≥ 40 dB @ 50 Hz** | Reduce residual mains | | ADC resolution | **≥ 16 bits (effective)** | Sub-µV digitization | | Sampling rate | **≥ 250 Hz** | Nyquist + 2.5× margin | | Common-mode range | **± 5 V minimum** | Survive defibrillation (EEG post-event) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 EEG Acquisition Chain — Functional Block Diagram The following Mermaid diagram captures the entire end-to-end EEG signal flow from scalp to digital storage, including all the critical analog and digital conditioning stages. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SCALP EEG SOURCE
10 to 100 microvolt signal
0.1 to 100 Hz band"] --> B["ELECTRODE ARRAY
Ag-AgCl disc electrodes
10-20 system placement"] B --> C["ELECTRODE-SKIN INTERFACE
Electrolyte gel
Half-cell potential 0.22 V
Impedance less than 5 kohm"] C --> D["ESD AND PROTECTION NETWORK
Input clamping diodes
Current limiting resistors
Defibrillation protection"] D --> E["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER
3 opamp topology
Gain 51 approx 34 dB
CMRR greater than 110 dB"] E --> F["DRIVEN RIGHT LEG CIRCUIT
Active body grounding
Reduces common mode voltage"] F -.feedback.-> C E --> G["HIGHPASS FILTER
Cutoff 0.1 Hz
Removes DC electrode offset"] G --> H["LOWPASS ANTI ALIAS FILTER
Cutoff 100 Hz
4th order Butterworth"] H --> I["NOTCH FILTER
50 Hz or 60 Hz
Twin T topology
Attenuation 40 dB"] I --> J["PROGRAMMABLE GAIN AMP
Gain 1 to 64 selectable
Matches ADC input range"] J --> K["24 BIT SIGMA DELTA ADC
Sample rate 250 to 500 Hz
Effective 18 to 21 ENOB"] K --> L["DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSOR
Artifact rejection
Bandpass digital filtering
Feature extraction"] L --> M["DISPLAY AND STORAGE
Time domain waveform
Spectrogram
Topographic maps"] ``` ## 4.2 International 10-20 Electrode Placement — Topological Map The 10-20 system defines 21 standard scalp positions relative to four anatomical landmarks: **nasion (Nz)**, **inion (Iz)**, and the **left/right pre-auricular points (A1/A2)**. Odd-numbered electrodes are on the **left**, even on the **right**, and "z" denotes midline. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Frontal["FRONTAL REGION"] Fp1["Fp1
Left frontopolar"] Fpz["Fpz
Midline frontopolar"] Fp2["Fp2
Right frontopolar"] F7["F7
Left inferior frontal"] F3["F3
Left frontal"] Fz["Fz
Midline frontal"] F4["F4
Right frontal"] F8["F8
Right inferior frontal"] end subgraph Central["CENTRAL REGION"] T3["T3
Left mid temporal"] C3["C3
Left central
Motor cortex"] Cz["Cz
Midline central
Vertex"] C4["C4
Right central"] T4["T4
Right mid temporal"] end subgraph Parietal["PARIETAL AND POSTERIOR"] T5["T5
Left posterior temporal"] P3["P3
Left parietal"] Pz["Pz
Midline parietal"] P4["P4
Right parietal"] T6["T6
Right posterior temporal"] O1["O1
Left occipital
Visual cortex"] O2["O2
Right occipital"] end subgraph Reference["REFERENCE ELECTRODES"] A1["A1
Left ear"] A2["A2
Right ear"] Cz_ref["Cz
Common reference"] end Fp1 --- Fpz --- Fp2 F7 --- F3 --- Fz --- F4 --- F8 T3 --- C3 --- Cz --- C4 --- T4 T5 --- P3 --- Pz --- P4 --- T6 O1 --- O2 ``` ## 4.3 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Topology (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INA["3 OPAMP INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"] V1["V1 input
Electrode 1"] --> R1a["R1"] V2["V2 input
Electrode 2"] --> R1b["R1"] R1a --> OA1["Opamp A1
Non inverting buffer"] R1b --> OA2["Opamp A2
Non inverting buffer"] OA1 --- Rg["Rg external
gain setting"] Rg --- OA2 OA1 --> R2a["R2"] OA2 --> R2b["R2"] R2a --> OA3["Opamp A3
Difference amp"] R2b --> OA3 OA3 --> R3a["R3"] OA3 --> R3b["R3"] R3a --> OUT["Vout differential"] R3b --> REF["Reference pin"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Node ID Rule Compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`node1`-style naming), no reserved Mermaid keywords are used as standalone identifiers, and all labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **List the five standard EEG rhythm bands and state the dominant behavioral state associated with each.** **Model Answer:** | Rhythm | Frequency | Associated State | |---|---|---| | Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep slow-wave sleep | | Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, light sleep | | Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed | | Beta (β) | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, focused attention | | Gamma (γ) | > 30 Hz | Higher cognitive processing, perception | (3 marks: 1 mark band list, 1 mark frequencies, 1 mark states) --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]` **Why is a differential amplifier (rather than single-ended) mandatory in EEG acquisition?** **Model Answer:** A differential amplifier is mandatory because **(1)** the EEG signal is differential in nature — measured between two scalp electrodes that share a large common-mode voltage (up to ±1 V from mains coupling through the body); **(2)** single-ended amplification would saturate on this common-mode voltage and clip the microvolt EEG; **(3)** the differential topology, when implemented with high CMRR (>100 dB), rejects the common-mode mains interference while amplifying only the small differential EEG (10–100 µV), giving an effective dynamic range of >10⁵. Additionally, **(4)** differential inputs reject even-order harmonics and electrode-matched noise that affect both inputs equally. (3 marks: 1 mark differential nature, 1 mark CMRR requirement, 1 mark saturation argument) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete EEG signal acquisition chain. Explain the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An EEG acquisition system uses a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier with $R_1 = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 500\,\Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$. The common-mode mains interference coupled into the patient is 1.5 V (peak) at 50 Hz. Compute **(i)** the differential gain of the INA, **(ii)** the minimum CMRR (in dB) needed to reduce this mains pickup to below 5 µV at the INA output, and **(iii)** the required ADC resolution (in bits) to digitize an EEG feature as small as 1 µV with a $\pm 5$ V full-scale ADC. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The complete EEG acquisition chain consists of the following stages (describe each block, 1 mark per block except the INA which carries 2 marks): 1. **Electrode Array (Ag/AgCl):** Transduces ionic brain currents into electronic voltage. Placed on scalp using the 10-20 system. Provides a stable half-cell potential of ~+0.223 V. 2. **Electrode–Skin Interface (Electrolyte Gel):** Establishes a low-impedance (<5 kΩ) ionic bridge between the scalp and the metal electrode. Reduces contact noise and motion artifacts. 3. **Input Protection Network:** ESD diodes and current-limiting resistors protect the front-end from electrostatic discharge and defibrillator shocks. Critical for patient safety (IEC 60601-1). 4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** Provides high input impedance (>100 MΩ), high CMRR (>110 dB at 50 Hz), and adjustable differential gain (typically 50–100). Built using the 3-op-amp topology with external $R_g$. 5. **Driven Right Leg (DRL) Circuit:** Actively drives the patient body to a quiet reference potential, reducing the common-mode voltage the INA must reject. A feedback loop that inverts the common-mode signal and reinjects it through a third electrode. 6. **High-Pass Filter (0.1 Hz cutoff):** Blocks DC offset caused by electrode half-cell potentials and amplifier bias. 7. **Low-Pass / Anti-Aliasing Filter (100 Hz cutoff, 4th-order Butterworth):** Removes high-frequency EMG interference and prevents aliasing of out-of-band noise. 8. **Notch Filter (50 Hz):** A narrow-band twin-T filter providing ≥40 dB attenuation of residual mains interference. 9. **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA):** A second-stage amplifier with software-selectable gain (1–64) to scale the conditioned signal to the ADC's full-scale range. 10. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (24-bit Σ–Δ):** Digitizes the conditioned EEG waveform at 250–500 Hz with >18 ENOB. 11. **Digital Signal Processor:** Performs artifact rejection (eye blink, muscle), digital bandpass filtering, and feature extraction (e.g., band power, Hjorth parameters). #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Differential gain of INA:** $$A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2} = \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 20\,000}{500}\right)\frac{40\,000}{40\,000}$$ $$A_d = (1 + 80)(1) = 81 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_d(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(81) \approx 38.17\,\text{dB}$$ **(ii) Required CMRR to reduce 1.5 V mains to ≤ 5 µV:** $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{ratio}} \geq \frac{V_{\text{cm}}}{V_{\text{resid}}} = \frac{1.5\,\text{V}}{5\,\mu\text{V}} = \frac{1.5}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 3 \times 10^5$$ $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(3 \times 10^5) = 20[\log_{10}(3) + 5] = 20[0.477 + 5] \approx 109.54\,\text{dB}$$ **(iii) Required ADC resolution for 1 µV step in ±5 V range:** $$2^N \geq \frac{V_{\text{FSR, span}}}{\Delta V_{\min}} = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{V}} = 10^7$$ $$N \geq \log_2(10^7) = 7 \times \log_2(10) = 7 \times 3.3219 \approx 23.25$$ $$N_{\min} = 24\,\text{bits}$$ **Final answer:** Gain = 81 (~38.17 dB); CMRR ≥ 109.54 dB; ADC resolution ≥ 24 bits. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain the **International 10-20 System** for EEG electrode placement. State the anatomical landmarks used and describe the rules for naming electrode positions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **total input-referred noise** of an EEG front-end given: INA voltage noise $e_n = 10\,\text{nV}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$, current noise $i_n = 1\,\text{fA}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$, electrode impedance $Z_e = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, bandwidth $\Delta f = 100\,\text{Hz}$, body temperature $T = 310\,\text{K}$. Also explain why **the Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit** is essential for EEG (and not just ECG) systems. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The **International 10-20 System** (developed by Jasper, 1958) is the standardized method for placing scalp electrodes in EEG recordings. It uses four anatomical landmarks: - **Nasion (Nz):** the depression at the bridge of the nose, between the eyes. - **Inion (Iz):** the bony prominence at the back of the skull. - **Left pre-auricular point (A1):** the small bony bump in front of the left ear. - **Right pre-auricular point (A2):** the small bony bump in front of the right ear. **Placement rules:** 1. The skull circumference along the **sagittal plane (Nz–Cz–Iz)** is divided at **10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10%** intervals. The 10% end-segments correspond to **Fp1/Fp2** (frontopolar) and **O1/O2** (occipital); the 20% intervals place **F3/F4, C3/C4, P3/P4**. 2. The skull circumference along the **coronal plane (A1–Cz–A2)** places **T3/T4, C3/C4, T5/T6** analogously. 3. The **lateral (circumferential)** arcs from Fp1 to O1 (and Fp2 to O2) are divided at 10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10% to place **F7/F8, T3/T4, T5/T6** and **F3/F4, C3/C4, P3/P4**. 4. **Naming convention:** letters denote the underlying lobe (Fp = frontopolar, F = frontal, T = temporal, C = central, P = parietal, O = occipital); odd numbers = **left hemisphere**, even numbers = **right hemisphere**, "z" = **midline**. 5. A total of **21 standard electrodes** are defined, plus optional intermediate positions (e.g., FC1, FC2, CP1, CP2) in the extended 10-10 system. This systematic placement ensures **reproducibility across patients, labs, and clinical sites**, enabling quantitative comparison and longitudinal studies. (Marks: 1 mark landmarks, 2 marks division rules, 2 marks naming, 1 mark reproducibility, 1 mark diagram reference) #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Johnson noise of the electrode source resistance:** $$v_{n,R} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f} = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 \times 10\,000 \times 100}$$ $$v_{n,R} = \sqrt{1.711 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 1.308 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{V} = 0.1308\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{rms}}$$ **Step 2 — Voltage noise contribution of the INA over the bandwidth:** $$v_{n,e} = e_n \sqrt{\Delta f} = 10 \times 10^{-9} \times \sqrt{100} = 10^{-7}\,\text{V} = 0.1\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{rms}}$$ **Step 3 — Current-noise converted voltage noise across $Z_e$:** $$v_{n,i} = i_n Z_e \sqrt{\Delta f} = 1 \times 10^{-15} \times 10\,000 \times 10 = 10^{-10}\,\text{V} = 0.0001\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{rms}}$$ **Step 4 — Total RMS noise (RSS of uncorrelated sources):** $$v_{n,\text{total}} = \sqrt{(0.1308)^2 + (0.1)^2 + (0.0001)^2} = \sqrt{0.01711 + 0.01 + \text{neg.}}$$ $$v_{n,\text{total}} = \sqrt{0.02711} \approx 0.1647\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{rms}}$$ **Step 5 — Peak-to-peak estimate (6σ):** $$v_{n,\text{pp}} \approx 6 \times 0.1647 \approx 0.99\,\mu\text{V}_{\text{pp}}$$ **Why DRL is essential for EEG:** The **Driven Right Leg (DRL)** circuit is essential — and arguably *more* critical for EEG than for ECG — for three reasons: 1. **Higher source impedance of EEG electrodes:** EEG electrodes have impedance up to 5 kΩ each, and many electrodes are used simultaneously. Without active grounding, the body floats at an unpredictable potential relative to the amplifier ground, allowing large 50 Hz common-mode voltages to develop. 2. **Longer electrode leads and higher mains coupling:** EEG electrodes are typically placed on the head, where capacitive coupling to overhead power lines and lighting fixtures is strong. The induced common-mode voltage can exceed ±1 V. 3. **Smaller signal of interest:** EEG is 5–10× smaller than ECG. Even residual common-mode leakage of a few hundred microvolts (post-CMRR) can swamp the EEG. DRL actively drives the body to a known reference, reducing the common-mode voltage by 20–40 dB before it reaches the INA, easing the burden on the CMRR specification. The DRL works by averaging the two electrode voltages, inverting the result, and feeding it back through a third electrode on the body (e.g., on the forehead or earlobe). This active feedback loop is essentially a "common-mode noise cancellation servo." (7 marks: 2 marks Johnson derivation, 2 marks INA noise derivation, 1 mark total RSS, 2 marks DRL explanation) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing EEG amplitude with ECG:** A common error is stating EEG is "millivolts." Always write **microvolts (µV)**. Marks deducted: 1. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $2R_1/R_g$:** The 3-op-amp INA formula has a $2R_1/R_g$ term, not $R_1/R_g$. Marks deducted: 1–2. > 3. **CMRR in dB vs. linear ratio:** Many students write CMRR = $10^5$ instead of $20\log_{10}(10^5) = 100$ dB. Always specify which form. Marks deducted: 1. > 4. **Forgetting the round-up rule for ADC bits:** Always round $N$ **up** to the next integer; rounding down under-resolves the smallest signal. Marks deducted: 1. > 5. **Ignoring Johnson noise:** Students often compute only INA voltage noise and forget the thermal noise of the source resistance $R_e$. With $R_e = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, Johnson noise is comparable to the INA's own noise. Marks deducted: 1–2. > 6. **Writing "high-pass filter" instead of "bandpass filter":** EEG acquisition requires a *bandpass* (HPF + LPF), not just an HPF. Marks deducted: 0.5. > 7. **Confusing "Driven Right Leg" with "Right Leg Drive":** The terminology is **DRL** (Driven Right Leg); do not abbreviate as "RLD" — that's the older terminology. Marks deducted: 0.5. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **EEG amplitude = 1–100 µV** (clinical: 10–50 µV); **bandwidth = 0.1–100 Hz** (clinical interest: 0.5–30 Hz). - **Five rhythm bands:** Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30) Hz. - **Acquisition chain:** Electrode → Protection → INA → HPF → LPF (anti-alias) → Notch → PGA → ADC → DSP. - **3-op-amp INA gain:** $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$. - **CMRR required ≥ 100 dB (typical 110–120 dB)** at 50/60 Hz to reject mains-coupled common-mode. - **Input impedance ≥ 100 MΩ** to avoid loading 5 kΩ electrode–scalp interface. - **Electrode impedance < 5 kΩ** is the clinical gold standard (prep with abrasive gel + alcohol). - **Ag/AgCl electrodes** preferred due to stable half-cell potential (~+0.223 V) and low offset drift. - **10-20 system:** 21 electrodes, 4 landmarks (Nz, Iz, A1, A2); odd = left, even = right, "z" = midline. - **Notch filter (twin-T):** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ centered at 50/60 Hz, attenuation ≥ 40 dB. - **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2.5 f_{\max} \Rightarrow f_s = 250$ Hz is the clinical minimum; 500 Hz for research. - **ADC:** 16-bit minimum, 24-bit Σ–Δ preferred; quantization step ≤ 0.5 µV at full-scale. - **DRL circuit:** active body grounding; reduces common-mode voltage by 20–40 dB. - **Key safety standards:** IEC 60601-1 (medical electrical equipment), patient isolation via optocouplers or isolation transformers. - **Common artifacts to reject:** 50/60 Hz line noise, ECG artifact (especially on ear references), EOG (eye blinks), EMG (muscle), motion artifact, sweat artifact (slow baseline drift). > [!TIP] > **Last-minute mnemonic — "ESD INA HPL NPA":** **E**lectrodes → **S**afety/ESD → **D**RL → **I**NA → **N**otch → **A**nti-aliasing → **H**PF → **P**GA → **L**PF → **A**DC. Use this to recall the EEG acquisition chain in the exam hall.

topics.title: Detection of EEG Rhythms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Detection of EEG Rhythms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are EEG Rhythms?

An **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** is a non-invasive recording of the brain's electrical activity, captured via electrodes placed on the scalp. The resulting quasi-periodic waveform is a superposition of several underlying oscillatory components, conventionally termed **EEG Rhythms** (or *brain waves*). Each rhythm occupies a characteristic frequency band, has a distinct physiological origin, and reflects a specific cognitive or behavioral state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** EEG rhythms are the *frequency-band-limited oscillatory components* of the scalp-recorded EEG signal that correlate with distinct neurological and behavioral states. The standard classification divides them into **five canonical bands**: Delta (δ), Theta (θ), Alpha (α), Beta (β), and Gamma (γ).

## 1.2 The Five Canonical EEG Bands

| Band | Frequency Range (Hz) | Typical Amplitude (µV) | Associated State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 | 20 – 200 | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, pathological coma |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 | < 30 | Drowsiness, light sleep, emotional stress, meditation |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 | 20 – 60 | Relaxed wakefulness with eyes closed |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 | < 20 | Active thinking, focus, anxiety, REM sleep |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 | Very low | Higher cognitive processing, perception binding |

> [!NOTE]
> The upper boundary of gamma is often taken as **100 Hz** in modern high-resolution EEG systems, although for clinical scalp EEG the practical upper limit is closer to **~45 Hz** due to attenuation by the skull (a phenomenon called *volume conduction*).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestra of the Brain"

Imagine the EEG signal as a **live orchestral recording** captured by a single microphone placed at the back of the concert hall.
