The microphone does not isolate individual instruments — instead, it picks up the *mixture* of violin (delta), cello (theta), flute (alpha), trumpet (beta), and piccolo (gamma) sections all playing simultaneously. The job of the **biomedical engineer** is to act as the audio engineer who applies a bank of electronic filters and spectral analyzers to *separate out* each instrument (rhythm) so the neurologist can study the contribution of each section (brain state) to the overall performance (cognition).

In DSP terms: the EEG is a **multi-component non-stationary signal**, and rhythm detection is a **frequency-band decomposition problem**.

## 1.4 Why is Detection Difficult?

> [!WARNING]
> **Volume Conduction & Signal Mixing:** The skull and scalp act as a *spatial low-pass filter*, smearing and attenuating higher frequencies. Additionally, the EEG is **non-stationary** — its spectral content changes moment-to-moment as the patient transitions between sleep stages or cognitive tasks. This makes simple FFT over the whole recording fundamentally inadequate.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Sampling rate for clinical EEG: **fs ≥ 256 Hz** (typically 256, 512, or 1024 Hz)
- Electrode placement standard: **10-20 International System**
- Reference: earlobe (A1/A2) or mastoid, often with linked-ear average
- Impedance maintained below **5 kΩ** to prevent 50/60 Hz line-noise contamination
- Notch filter: **50 Hz** (India/Europe) or **60 Hz** (USA) to remove power-line interference
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Detection Pipeline — Block Diagram Concept

A canonical EEG rhythm detection system follows this DSP chain:

1. **Acquisition** → Scalp electrodes + differential amplifier (gain ≈ 10⁴)
2. **Pre-processing** → Bandpass (0.5–45 Hz) + Notch (50 Hz) + Artefact rejection
3. **Decomposition** → Separate the signal into the five frequency bands
4. **Feature extraction** → Compute *band power* or *relative power* for each band
5. **Decision/Visualization** → Classify or display

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Emphasis:** Module 4 specifically focuses on the **Decomposition + Feature Extraction** stages. The two dominant paradigms are the **Filter-Bank (Fourier) approach** and the **Time-Frequency (Wavelet/Hilbert) approach**.

## 2.2 Method 1 — Band-Pass Filter Bank Approach

This is the classical, most intuitive method. A bank of band-pass filters — one for each rhythm — is applied in parallel to the EEG signal. The output of each filter is a time-domain waveform representing the activity in that specific band.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

For an EEG signal $x(t)$, the band-limited component for band $k$ is:

$$x_k(t) = h_k(t) * x(t)$$

where $h_k(t)$ is the impulse response of the $k$-th band-pass filter, and $*$ denotes convolution.

**Filter Design Specifications (for KTU):**

| Band | Passband (Hz) | Stopband (Hz) | Typical Filter Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delta | 0.5 – 4 | < 0.1, > 5 | 4th-order Butterworth (zero-phase via filtfilt) |
| Theta | 4 – 8 | < 3, > 9 | 4th-order Butterworth |
| Alpha | 8 – 13 | < 7, > 14 | 4th-order Butterworth |
| Beta | 13 – 30 | < 12, > 32 | 4th-order Butterworth |
| Gamma | 30 – 45 | < 29, > 50 | 4th-order Butterworth |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Butterworth?** It has a *maximally flat magnitude response* in the passband — critical for not distorting the relative amplitudes between rhythms, which carry clinical meaning.

The *band power* for band $k$ over an epoch of length $T$ is then computed as the mean-square of the filtered signal:

$$P_k = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x_k^2(t)\,dt \quad \text{(µV}^2\text{)}$$

## 2.3 Method 2 — Power Spectral Density (PSD) Approach

Since the EEG is non-stationary, we compute the **short-term PSD** over sliding windows of length $L$ (typically 1–2 seconds), then integrate the PSD over each frequency band to obtain the band power.

The PSD estimate can be obtained using:

**(a) Periodogram (Welch's Method):**

$$P_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{N \cdot U} \left\vert X(f) \right\vert^2$$

where $X(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot w[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi fn}$, and $U$ is the window energy normalization factor.

**(b) Autoregressive (AR) Parametric Method:**

The AR model of order $p$ expresses the current sample as a linear combination of past samples:

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x[n-k] + e[n]$$

The PSD is then:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi fk} \right\vert^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AR for EEG?** The AR model provides **smoother spectral estimates with lower variance** than the periodogram, especially for short data segments. It is therefore preferred in clinical EEG rhythm detection. Typical AR order: $p = 6$ to $p = 20$ (Burg or Yule-Walker estimation).

## 2.4 Method 3 — Wavelet Transform (Time-Frequency Approach)

Because EEG rhythms are **non-stationary** (e.g., alpha bursts appear and disappear), a pure frequency-domain method loses temporal information. The **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** decomposes the signal into time-frequency atoms:

$$C(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)dt$$

where $a$ is the scale (inversely related to frequency) and $b$ is the time shift.

By selecting a *mother wavelet* whose frequency response aligns with the EEG bands (commonly the **Daubechies-4 (db4)** or **Morlet** wavelet), the coefficients $C(a,b)$ directly yield the time-localized energy in each band.

**Approximate scale-to-frequency mapping (for Morlet, fs = 256 Hz):**

| Band | Center Frequency (Hz) | Scale $a$ |
|---|---|---|
| Delta | 2 | ~ 60 |
| Theta | 6 | ~ 20 |
| Alpha | 10 | ~ 12 |
| Beta | 20 | ~ 6 |

## 2.5 Method 4 — Hilbert-Huang Transform (HHT)

A modern, fully data-adaptive method:

1. **Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD)** decomposes $x(t)$ into *Intrinsic Mode Functions* (IMFs), each of which is approximately mono-component.
2. The **Hilbert Transform** is applied to each IMF to obtain its instantaneous frequency and amplitude.

$$y(t) = \mathcal{H}\{x(t)\} = \frac{1}{\pi} \text{P.V.} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x(\tau)}{t-\tau} d\tau$$

The analytical signal is $z(t) = x(t) + jy(t) = A(t)e^{j\phi(t)}$, and the instantaneous frequency is:

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$$

Each IMF can then be assigned to the appropriate EEG band based on its mean instantaneous frequency.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $P_k = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x_k^2(t)\,dt$ | Band power via filter bank | µV² |
| $P_{band} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} P_{xx}(f)\,df$ | Band power via PSD integration | µV²/Hz × Hz |
| $RP_k = \frac{P_k}{\sum_{i} P_i}$ | Relative power (normalized) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $C(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int x(t)\psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)dt$ | Continuous Wavelet Transform | — |
| $f_c \approx \frac{f_s}{a \cdot \Delta}$ | Scale-to-frequency (Morlet, $\Delta \approx 1$) | Hz |
| $P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi fk} \right\vert^2}$ | AR-based PSD | µV²/Hz |
| $f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$ | Instantaneous frequency (Hilbert) | Hz |
| $\bar{f}_{IMF} = \frac{\int f \cdot A^2(f) df}{\int A^2(f) df}$ | Mean freq of an IMF (HHT) | Hz |
| $E_k = \sum_{n} \left\vert C_k[n] \right\vert^2$ | Wavelet band energy | (µV)² |
| $SE_k = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy of band (complexity) | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** EEG rhythm detection is the cornerstone of **sleep staging (polysomnography)**, **epilepsy monitoring (spike detection)**, **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)**, and **depth-of-anesthesia monitoring (BIS index)**. Mastering these formulas is mandatory for KTU Module 4.

## 2.7 Engineering Significance

- The **alpha rhythm** is used in BCI spellers (P300, motor imagery).
- The **theta/beta ratio** is a clinical biomarker for **ADHD**.
- The **delta/alpha ratio** is used in **anesthesia depth** monitoring.
- **Gamma band synchronization** is being studied as a biomarker for **schizophrenia and Alzheimer's**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Band Power from Filtered EEG

**Problem:** Given an EEG epoch $x(t)$ of duration $T = 2$ seconds sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz, derive the *alpha band power* using a zero-phase 4th-order Butterworth band-pass filter (8–13 Hz) applied via the `filtfilt` algorithm. Then compute the *relative alpha power*.

### Step 1 — Define the filter specifications

The 4th-order Butterworth band-pass filter has the squared magnitude response:

$$\left\vert H(f) \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{B(f)}{B(f_c)}\right)^{2N}}$$

where $N = 4$ is the filter order, $f_c = \sqrt{8 \times 13} \approx 10.2$ Hz is the geometric center frequency, and $B(f)$ is a frequency-dependent function of the band-edge design.

### Step 2 — Apply the filter to the EEG

The filtered alpha signal is:

$$x_\alpha(t) = h_{BP}(t) * x(t)$$

Using the `filtfilt` (zero-phase) approach, the signal is filtered forward then backward, giving a net phase delay of zero and a doubled filter order of **8**. The squared magnitude response of the effective filter is:

$$\left\vert H_{eff}(f) \right\vert^2 = \left\vert H_{BP}(f) \right\vert^4$$

### Step 3 — Compute the alpha band power

By Parseval's theorem, the average power in the alpha band equals the integral of the PSD over the band:

$$P_\alpha = \int_{8}^{13} P_{xx}(f)\, df = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x_\alpha^2(t)\, dt$$

In discrete form (with $N$ samples in the epoch):

$$P_\alpha = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_\alpha^2[n]$$

### Step 4 — Compute the total power

The total EEG power is the sum of band powers across all five bands:

$$P_{total} = P_\delta + P_\theta + P_\alpha + P_\beta + P_\gamma$$

### Step 5 — Compute relative alpha power

$$RP_\alpha = \frac{P_\alpha}{P_{total}}$$

This dimensionless quantity (range: 0 to 1, or 0% to 100%) is *less sensitive to inter-subject variability* in absolute amplitude and is therefore the **clinically preferred** metric.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: AR-based PSD for a Simulated EEG Epoch

**Problem:** Given an EEG segment modeled as an AR(4) process with coefficients $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.8$, $a_3 = -0.2$, $a_4 = 0.1$ and noise variance $\sigma_e^2 = 1$, derive the PSD expression and identify the dominant rhythm (i.e., the spectral peak).

### Step 1 — Write the AR model

$$x[n] = 1.5\,x[n-1] - 0.8\,x[n-2] + 0.2\,x[n-3] - 0.1\,x[n-4] + e[n]$$

(Note the sign convention: $a_k$ appears with a leading minus in the time-domain model, so $x[n] = -\sum a_k x[n-k] + e[n]$.)

### Step 2 — Derive the transfer function

The transfer function from $e[n]$ to $x[n]$ is:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{4} a_k z^{-k}}$$

Substituting:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.8 z^{-2} - 0.2 z^{-3} + 0.1 z^{-4}}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the PSD on the unit circle

Setting $z = e^{j2\pi f / f_s}$ (with $f_s = 256$ Hz, normalized frequency $f_n = f/f_s \in [0, 0.5]$):

$$P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_e^2 \left\vert H(e^{j2\pi f / f_s}) \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{\left\vert 1 - 1.5 e^{-j2\pi f_n} + 0.8 e^{-j4\pi f_n} - 0.2 e^{-j6\pi f_n} + 0.1 e^{-j8\pi f_n} \right\vert^2}$$

### Step 4 — Find the spectral peak (dominant rhythm)

The denominator $\left\vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi k f_n} \right\vert^2$ is minimized (and therefore $P_{AR}$ is maximized) when the numerator term inside the modulus vanishes — i.e., when $z = e^{j2\pi f_n}$ is approximately equal to a pole of $H(z)$.

Poles are roots of the denominator polynomial $1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.8 z^{-2} - 0.2 z^{-3} + 0.1 z^{-4} = 0$. Numerically (using a root-finder), the dominant conjugate pole pair typically has a magnitude close to 1 (resonance) and angle $\theta_p$ such that:

$$f_{peak} = \frac{f_s}{2\pi} \theta_p$$

For the coefficients given above, the dominant resonance typically falls in the **alpha range (8–13 Hz)**, confirming that the model represents a typical relaxed-wakefulness EEG.

### Step 5 — Compute band power from the AR PSD

$$P_{alpha} = \int_{8}^{13} P_{AR}(f)\, df$$

This is evaluated numerically (trapezoidal rule or Simpson's rule) by sampling $P_{AR}(f)$ at a dense grid of frequencies.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Method | Temporal Resolution | Frequency Resolution | Robustness to Noise | Computation | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Filter bank | Excellent | Fixed (bandwidth-dependent) | Moderate | Low | Real-time BCI |
| Welch PSD | Poor (epoch-averaged) | Good | High (windowing reduces variance) | Low | Sleep staging, clinical |
| AR model | Moderate | Excellent for short data | High for low SNR | Moderate | Short epochs, single-trial BCI |
| Wavelet (CWT) | Excellent | Variable (good at low freq, poor at high) | Moderate | High | Spike detection, transient analysis |
| HHT (EMD) | Excellent | Adaptive (no fixed resolution) | Moderate (mode-mixing problem) | Moderate | Non-stationary, seizure analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 Takeaway:** The **method choice is dictated by the signal property you wish to preserve** — temporal (use wavelets/HHT), spectral (use Welch/AR), or both (use STFT or wavelet packet decomposition).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EEG Rhythm Detection — Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw EEG Signal x t] --> B[Pre-processing Block]
    B --> B1[Bandpass 0.5 to 45 Hz]
    B --> B2[Notch 50 Hz]
    B --> B3[Artefact Rejection EOG EMG]
    B1 --> C[Decomposition Engine]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D1[Filter Bank Method]
    C --> D2[Welch PSD Method]
    C --> D3[AR Model Method]
    C --> D4[Wavelet CWT Method]
    C --> D5[Hilbert Huang Method]
    D1 --> E[Feature Extractor]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> E1[Band Power Pk]
    E --> E2[Relative Power RPk]
    E --> E3[Band Energy Ek]
    E --> E4[Spectral Entropy SEk]
    E1 --> F[Classifier Visualizer]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> F1[Rhythm Identification]
    F --> F2[Clinical Output]
    F --> F3[BCI Command]
```

## 4.2 Frequency-Band Decomposition Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Input EEG x t] --> S1{Filter Bank}
    S1 --> P1[BPF 0.5 to 4 Hz]
    S1 --> P2[BPF 4 to 8 Hz]
    S1 --> P3[BPF 8 to 13 Hz]
    S1 --> P4[BPF 13 to 30 Hz]
    S1 --> P5[BPF 30 to 45 Hz]
    P1 --> Q1[Delta Component x delta t]
    P2 --> Q2[Theta Component x theta t]
    P3 --> Q3[Alpha Component x alpha t]
    P4 --> Q4[Beta Component x beta t]
    P5 --> Q5[Gamma Component x gamma t]
    Q1 --> R[Power Calculator Pk equals mean of xk squared]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    Q5 --> R
    R --> T[Band Power Vector P delta P theta P alpha P beta P gamma]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Method Selection (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is real time processing required?] --> Q1A{Yes or No}
    Q1A -->|Yes| A1[Use Filter Bank Method]
    Q1A -->|No| Q2[Is the epoch very short less than 1 s?]
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Use AR Model Method]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3[Are transients or spikes of interest?]
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Use Wavelet CWT]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4[Is the signal highly non stationary e.g. seizure?]
    Q4 -->|Yes| A4[Use Hilbert Huang Transform]
    Q4 -->|No| A5[Use Welch PSD for standard clinical reporting]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks "which method is best for *short data segments*?" — the correct answer is **AR model**. When it asks about *preserving temporal information* of a *non-stationary* signal — the answer is **wavelet transform**. These are high-frequency viva and 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Define the five canonical EEG rhythms. List their frequency ranges and one clinical state associated with each.**

**Model Answer:**

The five canonical EEG rhythms are the frequency-band-limited oscillatory components of the scalp EEG, classified as:

- **Delta (δ):** 0.5 – 4 Hz — Deep slow-wave sleep
- **Theta (θ):** 4 – 8 Hz — Drowsiness and light sleep
- **Alpha (α):** 8 – 13 Hz — Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed
- **Beta (β):** 13 – 30 Hz — Active thinking, alert state
- **Gamma (γ):** > 30 Hz — Higher cognitive processing

**KTU Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Frequency ranges (1 mark) + Clinical states (1 mark).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Why is the simple FFT of an entire EEG recording inadequate for rhythm detection? What alternative is preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

The EEG is a **non-stationary signal** — its spectral content changes over time (e.g., during transitions from wakefulness to sleep, or during an epileptic seizure). Applying a single FFT to the whole recording yields only a *time-averaged spectrum* in which transient bursts (such as alpha spindles or sleep spindles) are smeared and lost. The preferred alternative is a **time-frequency method** such as:

- The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** with sliding windows
- The **Welch periodogram** for a smoothed PSD
- The **Wavelet Transform (CWT/DWT)** for multi-resolution time-frequency analysis

**KTU Mark Distribution:** Explanation of non-stationarity (2 marks) + Preferred method (1 mark).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Explain the **filter-bank method** for detecting EEG rhythms. With a neat block diagram, describe the design specifications of a Butterworth band-pass filter used to isolate the **alpha band (8–13 Hz)** from an EEG signal sampled at 256 Hz. Derive the expression for the **band power** in the alpha rhythm. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **Autoregressive (AR) model-based PSD** approach for EEG rhythm detection. Derive the PSD expression, state the recommended model order, and explain why AR is preferred over the periodogram for short EEG epochs. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
The filter-bank method uses a parallel bank of band-pass filters, each tuned to one canonical EEG band. The output of each filter is the time-domain waveform of that rhythm. The squared-and-averaged output gives the band power.

**Step 2 — Filter Specification (2 marks):**

For the alpha band (8–13 Hz) with $f_s = 256$ Hz, a **4th-order Butterworth** band-pass filter is designed with:
- Passband edges: 8 Hz and 13 Hz
- Passband ripple: ≤ 3 dB (maximally flat)
- Stopband attenuation: ≥ 20 dB
- Zero-phase implementation using `filtfilt` (forward-backward filtering) effectively doubles the order to 8.

The squared magnitude response is:

$$\left\vert H(f) \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left( \frac{f/f_c - f_c/f}{B} \right)^{2N}}$$

where $f_c = \sqrt{8 \times 13} \approx 10.2$ Hz, $B = (13-8)/2 = 2.5$ Hz, and $N = 4$.

**Step 3 — Band Power Derivation (3 marks):**

The filtered alpha signal is:

$$x_\alpha(t) = h_{BP}(t) * x(t)$$

The alpha band power over an epoch of duration $T$ is:

$$P_\alpha = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x_\alpha^2(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_\alpha^2[n]$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — AR Model Definition (2 marks):**
An AR model of order $p$ expresses the current sample as a weighted sum of past samples plus white noise:

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x[n-k] + e[n]$$

**Step 2 — PSD Derivation (3 marks):**
Taking the Z-transform:

$$X(z) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k} X(z) + E(z)$$

Solving for the transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{X(z)}{E(z)} = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

Evaluating on the unit circle $z = e^{j2\pi f / f_s}$:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_e^2 \left\vert H(e^{j2\pi f / f_s}) \right\vert^2 = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k / f_s} \right\vert^2}$$

**Step 3 — Practical Recommendations (2 marks):**
- Recommended model order: $p = 6$ to $p = 20$ (typically $p = 8$ for routine EEG)
- Coefficients estimated via **Burg algorithm** or **Yule-Walker equations**
- The dominant peak of $P_{AR}(f)$ identifies the prevailing rhythm

**Step 4 — Why AR is preferred (2 marks):**
The periodogram has high variance ($\sim$ equal to the square of the true PSD), and this variance does *not decrease* with longer data — it is inconsistent. The AR model, being a *parametric method*, smooths the spectrum and is **consistent**: variance decreases as data length increases. For short EEG epochs (e.g., 1-second windows used in BCI), AR yields far smoother and more reliable spectral peaks than the periodogram.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper Style — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Wavelet Transform-based method** for EEG rhythm detection. Explain how the **scale parameter** of the Continuous Wavelet Transform is related to frequency, and derive the wavelet band-energy expression. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Hilbert-Huang Transform (HHT)** for EEG rhythm detection. Describe the **Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD)** algorithm and derive the **instantaneous frequency** expression using the Hilbert transform. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
The CWT decomposes the EEG into a time-frequency representation using scaled and shifted versions of a mother wavelet. Unlike the STFT, it provides **multi-resolution analysis** — high time-resolution at high frequencies and high frequency-resolution at low frequencies, which matches the EEG rhythm structure (slow delta waves need finer frequency resolution than fast gamma waves).

**Step 2 — CWT Definition (2 marks):**

$$C(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)dt$$

where $a$ is the scale (inversely related to frequency), $b$ is the time-shift, and $\psi$ is the mother wavelet.

**Step 3 — Scale-to-Frequency Relation (1 mark):**
For the Morlet wavelet, the pseudo-frequency is:

$$f_a = \frac{f_c \cdot f_s}{a}$$

where $f_c$ is the center frequency of the wavelet (≈ 1 Hz for Morlet) and $f_s$ is the sampling rate. Thus, for $f_s = 256$ Hz:

- Delta (2 Hz) → $a \approx 128$
- Alpha (10 Hz) → $a \approx 25.6$

**Step 4 — Wavelet Band Energy (2 marks):**
For band $k$ covering the scale range $[a_{k,1}, a_{k,2}]$, the energy is:

$$E_k = \sum_{b} \sum_{a=a_{k,1}}^{a_{k,2}} \left\vert C(a,b) \right\vert^2$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — EMD Concept (2 marks):**
EMD is a *data-adaptive* decomposition that splits the EEG into a finite number of Intrinsic Mode Functions (IMFs), each satisfying two conditions: (i) the number of extrema and zero-crossings differ by at most one, and (ii) the mean of the upper and lower envelopes is zero.

**Step 2 — Sifting Algorithm (1 mark):**
1. Identify all local maxima and minima of $x(t)$.
2. Construct upper envelope $e_{up}(t)$ (cubic-spline through maxima) and lower envelope $e_{low}(t)$ (through minima).
3. Compute the mean $m(t) = (e_{up} + e_{low})/2$.
4. Extract the candidate $h(t) = x(t) - m(t)$.
5. If $h(t)$ satisfies the IMF conditions, set $\text{IMF}_1 = h(t)$ and repeat on the residual $r(t) = x(t) - \text{IMF}_1$.
6. Stop when the residual has ≤ 2 extrema.

**Step 3 — Hilbert Transform (2 marks):**
For an IMF $c(t)$:

$$\tilde{c}(t) = \mathcal{H}\{c(t)\} = \frac{1}{\pi} \text{P.V.} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{c(\tau)}{t-\tau} d\tau$$

The analytical signal:

$$z(t) = c(t) + j\tilde{c}(t) = A(t) e^{j\phi(t)}$$

where the instantaneous amplitude is $A(t) = \sqrt{c^2(t) + \tilde{c}^2(t)}$ and the instantaneous phase is $\phi(t) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\tilde{c}(t)}{c(t)}\right)$.

**Step 4 — Instantaneous Frequency (2 marks):**

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$$

The mean frequency of each IMF is:

$$\bar{f}_{IMF} = \frac{\int f \cdot A^2(f)\, df}{\int A^2(f)\, df}$$

Each IMF is then mapped to its corresponding EEG rhythm based on $\bar{f}_{IMF}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in AR model:** Students frequently forget that the coefficients in the time-domain AR model have an *inherent minus sign*. Writing $x[n] = \sum a_k x[n-k]$ (without the minus) is a 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Wavelet scale confusion:** Students write $f = a \cdot f_c$ instead of $f = f_c \cdot f_s / a$. Marks are deducted for the wrong direction of proportionality.
> 3. **Hilbert transform applied to the raw EEG directly:** HHT requires EMD *first*, then Hilbert on each IMF. Applying Hilbert directly to the raw EEG gives meaningless instantaneous frequency (the signal is not mono-component).
> 4. **Confusing power with amplitude:** Band *power* is in µV², not µV. Students writing "alpha power = 30 µV" lose marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the AR order:** Always mention $p$ and the estimation algorithm (Burg / Yule-Walker) for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — EEG Rhythm Detection**

- ✅ **Five canonical bands:** Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30 Hz).
- ✅ **Alpha rhythm** is the most prominent resting rhythm and the most clinically studied.
- ✅ **Volume conduction** smears and attenuates high frequencies — gamma needs special care.
- ✅ **Band power** $P_k = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x_k^2(t)\,dt$ is the primary feature; **relative power** normalizes for inter-subject variability.
- ✅ **Filter-bank method:** simple, real-time, but fixed bandpass design.
- ✅ **Welch PSD:** averaged periodogram, good for stationary epochs, low variance.
- ✅ **AR model:** parametric, best for **short epochs**, lower variance, order $p = 6$–$20$.
- ✅ **Wavelet (CWT):** multi-resolution, **best for non-stationary** signals, scale $a$ inversely related to frequency.
- ✅ **Hilbert-Huang (HHT):** fully data-adaptive via EMD, gives instantaneous frequency $f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi}{dt}$.
- ✅ **Method choice depends on:** data length, stationarity, real-time requirement, and noise level.
- ✅ **Clinical uses:** sleep staging, epilepsy, BCI, anesthesia depth, ADHD/AD biomarkers.
- ✅ **Sign convention for AR:** $x[n] = -\sum a_k x[n-k] + e[n]$ (note the minus sign).
- ✅ **Standard sampling rate:** $f_s \geq 256$ Hz; notch at 50 Hz for India.
- ✅ **10-20 electrode system** is the international placement standard.
- ✅ **Butterworth filter** is preferred for its maximally flat passband.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Mantra:** When asked to "explain" a method — give **(i) block diagram, (ii) key equation, (iii) one clinical/engineering use, (iv) one limitation.** This 4-part structure guarantees full marks in KTU ESE answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Template Matching for EEG Spike and Wave Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Template Matching for EEG Spike and Wave Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Template Matching for EEG Spike and Wave Detection** is a deterministic pattern recognition technique in which a pre-recorded, clinically validated **reference waveform prototype (template)** — representing the morphological signature of an epileptiform transient (e.g., the classic 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharge of absence seizures) — is systematically compared against a continuous, windowed segment of the multi-channel EEG signal using a mathematical **similarity metric** (typically cross-correlation or Euclidean distance). A detection event is declared when the computed similarity score exceeds an empirically or statistically derived **decision threshold**, thereby enabling automated localization, counting, and classification of paroxysmal discharges in long-term EEG recordings.

In the vocabulary of the KTU 2024 scheme (aligned to **Course Outcome CO4** of PEICT633 — *"Apply adaptive and template-based techniques for the detection of events in biomedical signals"*), template matching is classified under the broader umbrella of **Model-Based Detection**, sitting alongside matched filtering, parametric estimation, and Kalman filtering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fingerprint in a Crowd" Metaphor

Imagine an airport security officer (the **detector**) holding a high-resolution photograph of a known suspect's face (the **template**). As thousands of travelers stream past a camera (the **continuous EEG stream**), the officer's brain performs a continuous, sub-second **pattern comparison** — measuring how closely each passing face resembles the photograph in terms of geometric features (eye spacing, nose length, jawline). When the resemblance crosses a subjective "alarm" threshold, the officer pulls the traveler aside (a **detection event**).

Translating this analogy to EEG signal processing:

| Airport Analogy | EEG Template Matching Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Photograph of suspect | Stored spike-and-wave prototype (e.g., 1-second window) |
| Travelers walking past | Continuous EEG samples $x(n)$ |
| Officer's brain comparison | Cross-correlation $r_{xy}(\tau)$ |
| Subjective "match" feeling | Numerical correlation coefficient $\rho$ |
| Pulling traveler aside | Threshold crossing $\rho \geq T_{dec}$ |
| False alarm (wrong person) | False positive detection |
| Suspect walks past unseen (poor angle) | False negative (missed discharge) |

### 1.3 Why Spike-and-Wave Detection Matters Clinically

The **spike-and-wave complex (SWC)** is the pathognomonic electrographic hallmark of **generalized epileptic discharges**, most prominently:

- **Absence seizures (Petit Mal):** Bilateral, symmetric **3 Hz** spike-and-wave bursts lasting 5–10 seconds
- **Lennox-Gastaut syndrome:** Slower (1.5–2.5 Hz) SWC with polyspike components
- **Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy:** Generalized 4–6 Hz polyspike-and-wave discharges

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Clinical Metrics** that any spike-detection algorithm (template-matching based or otherwise) must attempt to optimize:
> - **Sensitivity** (True Positive Rate): $\geq 90\%$ in clinically viable systems
> - **Specificity** (True Negative Rate): $\geq 95\%$
> - **False Detection Rate (FDR):** $\leq 0.5$ per minute for 24-hour ambulatory recordings
> - **Detection Latency:** $\leq 0.5$ seconds for closed-loop neurostimulation applications

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Template Matching System

The detection pipeline is implemented as a sequence of six logical stages. Each stage is a design decision point that directly influences the final sensitivity-specificity trade-off.

1. **Signal Acquisition & Preprocessing**
   - EEG is sampled at $f_s \geq 200$ Hz (clinical standard: $f_s = 256$ Hz or $f_s = 512$ Hz to preserve sharp spike edges)
   - Bandpass filtering: typically **0.5 Hz – 40 Hz** (or **1 Hz – 70 Hz** for intracranial EEG)
   - **50/60 Hz notch filter** to suppress powerline interference
   - Optional baseline removal via **polynomial detrending** or **median subtraction**

2. **Template Library Construction**
   - Templates are extracted from **expert-annotated** EEG segments by an epileptologist
   - Each template $t_k(n)$ of length $L$ samples is **normalized** to unit energy: $\sum_n t_k^2(n) = 1$
   - A clinically robust library contains $K$ templates ($K = 5$ to $20$) to capture morphological variability (e.g., different patients, different seizure foci, different propagation patterns)

3. **Windowed Segmentation of the Input EEG**
   - Continuous signal $x(n)$ is partitioned into overlapping windows of length $L$ with hop size $H$
   - Overlap ratio: $R_{ov} = 1 - H/L$ (typically $R_{ov} = 0.5$ or $0.75$ to avoid missing transients at window boundaries)

4. **Similarity Computation**
   - For each window $x_w(n)$ and each template $t_k(n)$, the similarity metric is evaluated
   - Output: a time-varying score $S_k(w)$ for window index $w$ and template index $k$

5. **Threshold-Based Decision**
   - Detection at window $w$ if $\max_k S_k(w) \geq T_{dec}$
   - Threshold is set via **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** analysis on a labeled training set

6. **Post-Processing (Refractory Period & Clustering)**
   - A **refractory period** of 200–500 ms suppresses redundant adjacent detections of the same discharge
   - **Clustering** merges fragmented detections of a single sustained burst into one clinical event

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** required to solve template-matching problems in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). All symbols are SI-consistent; $n$ denotes discrete sample index and $\tau$ denotes discrete lag.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Domain & Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cross-Correlation (deterministic, finite-length) | $r_{xy}(\tau) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x(n)\, y(n+\tau)$ | $\tau \in [-(L-1), L-1]$; Lags where index falls outside signal length are **zero-padded** |
| 2 | Cross-Correlation (random process form) | $r_{xy}(\tau) = \mathrm{E}[x(n)\, y(n+\tau)]$ | $\mathrm{E}[\cdot]$ is the expectation operator |
| 3 | Cross-Covariance | $c_{xy}(\tau) = \mathrm{E}[(x(n)-\mu_x)(y(n+\tau)-\mu_y)]$ | Removes DC (mean) bias — **preferred for EEG** since mean offset is non-physiological |
| 4 | Pearson Normalized Correlation Coefficient | $\rho_{xy}(\tau) = \dfrac{c_{xy}(\tau)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}$ | $\rho \in [-1, +1]$; $\rho = 1 \Rightarrow$ perfect positive match |
| 5 | Energy of a discrete signal | $E_x = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2$ | L2 norm squared |
| 6 | Cross-Correlation in terms of energies | $r_{xy}(\tau) = \dfrac{\sum_n x(n)\, y(n+\tau)}{\sqrt{E_x \cdot E_y}}$ | **Normalizes for amplitude** — critical for EEG where waveform amplitude varies between patients |
| 7 | Matched Filter SNR Gain | $\mathrm{SNR}_{out} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$ | $E_s$ = signal energy, $N_0$ = noise PSD. Maximum achievable by any linear filter |
| 8 | Matched Filter Impulse Response | $h(n) = s(L-1-n)$ | Time-reversed and shifted version of template $s(n)$ |
| 9 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) distance | $d_{MSE}(w) = \dfrac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} [x_w(n) - t(n)]^2$ | Lower $d_{MSE} \Rightarrow$ better match (opposite polarity of correlation) |
| 10 | Cosine Similarity (vector form) | $S_{cos} = \dfrac{\mathbf{x}_w \cdot \mathbf{t}}{\Vert \mathbf{x}_w \Vert \, \Vert \mathbf{t} \Vert}$ | Equivalent to Pearson $\rho$ when means are zero |
| 11 | ROC Sensitivity | $\mathrm{Se} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | True Positive Rate |
| 12 | ROC Specificity | $\mathrm{Sp} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | True Negative Rate |
| 13 | Optimal Bayesian Threshold (equal priors) | $T^* = \dfrac{\mu_{H_0} + \mu_{H_1}}{2}$ | Minimizes total error when distributions are Gaussian and equiprobable |
| 14 | Neyman-Pearson Threshold | $T_{NP}$ such that $P_{FA} = \alpha$ | Used in KTU problems to satisfy a fixed false-alarm constraint |
| 15 | Sample Mean of a window | $\mu_x = \dfrac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x(n)$ | Used to demean the window before correlation |

### 2.3 Physical & Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why cross-correlation works for spike-and-wave detection — the underlying physical intuition:**
> A spike-and-wave discharge is a **quasi-deterministic waveform** with a characteristic morphology: a sharp negative deflection (spike, ~70 ms) immediately followed by a slower positive dome (wave, ~150–200 ms). This morphology is the result of synchronized post-synaptic potentials in cortical pyramidal neurons during a paroxysmal depolarizing shift. Because the underlying neuronal generator is the same from one discharge to the next (within a patient), the *shape* of the waveform is preserved even when its *amplitude* fluctuates. Cross-correlation rewards shape similarity regardless of amplitude, making it the natural detector for such quasi-deterministic transients.

In contrast, the **matched filter** is the **optimal linear detector** for a known signal in additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN), maximizing the output signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). For a template $s(n)$ of known shape, the matched filter's output is mathematically equivalent to the cross-correlation $r_{xs}(\tau)$, evaluated at the lag $\tau = 0$. This is why template matching is often called **"quasi-matched filtering"** in the clinical literature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Cross-Correlation to the Normalized Similarity Score

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Given a discrete EEG window $\mathbf{x} = [x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(L-1)]$ and a stored template $\mathbf{t} = [t(0), t(1), \ldots, t(L-1)]$, derive the **normalized cross-correlation score** $S_{xy}$ that is invariant to the amplitude scaling of the input window. Show every algebraic transition.

**Derivation:**

We begin with the **un-normalized** finite-length cross-correlation, evaluated at zero lag ($\tau = 0$):

$$r_{xy}(0) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x(n)\, t(n)$$

This is the dot product of two L-dimensional vectors: $r_{xy}(0) = \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{t}$.

If the EEG window is amplitude-scaled by a factor $A$ (e.g., due to varying electrode-skin impedance or amplifier gain), the observation becomes $\tilde{x}(n) = A \cdot x(n)$. The cross-correlation then scales linearly:

$$\tilde{r}_{xy}(0) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} A\,x(n)\, t(n) = A \cdot r_{xy}(0)$$

This linear dependence on $A$ means the *raw* correlation cannot distinguish between a **strong-amplitude genuine spike** and a **weak-amplitude background fluctuation that coincidentally matches the template direction**. To remove this amplitude dependence, we **divide by the L2 norms** of both vectors:

$$S_{xy} = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{t}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{t} \Vert}$$

where the L2 norm is defined as $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_n x^2(n)}$. Substituting the explicit sums:

$$S_{xy} = \dfrac{\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x(n)\, t(n)}{\sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x^2(n)} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} t^2(n)}}$$

The score $S_{xy}$ is now **bounded** as $S_{xy} \in [-1, +1]$ by the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** applied to the inner product:

$$\vert \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{t} \vert \le \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{t} \Vert$$

The upper bound $S_{xy} = +1$ is achieved **if and only if** $\mathbf{x} = c \cdot \mathbf{t}$ for some positive scalar $c$ (i.e., the window is a positively-scaled, perfectly aligned copy of the template). The lower bound $S_{xy} = -1$ corresponds to a sign-inverted copy. This is the **Cosine Similarity** form of template matching and is the most clinically deployed variant.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalence of Matched Filter and Cross-Correlation

**Problem Statement:**
Show that the output of a **matched filter** $h(n)$ convolved with input $x(n)$ is mathematically equivalent to the **cross-correlation** of $x(n)$ with the template $s(n)$ at a specific lag, and use this to derive the maximum output SNR.

**Derivation:**

A matched filter is, by definition, a linear time-invariant system whose impulse response is the **time-reversed, time-shifted** version of the known signal $s(n)$:

$$h(n) = s(L-1-n)$$

The filter output is the convolution of the input $x(n)$ with $h(n)$:

$$y(m) = (x * h)(m) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x(n)\, h(m-n) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x(n)\, s(L-1-(m-n))$$

Let us introduce the substitution $\tau = m - (L-1)$, which represents the lag. The output at sample $m = L-1$ (i.e., $\tau = 0$) becomes:

$$y(L-1) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x(n)\, s(n) = r_{xs}(0)$$

This proves that **the matched filter output at the synchronization instant is exactly the cross-correlation at zero lag.** For $\tau \neq 0$, the matched filter output equals the cross-correlation evaluated at lag $\tau$.

To derive the **maximum output SNR**, let the input be $x(n) = s(n) + w(n)$, where $w(n)$ is white Gaussian noise with two-sided power spectral density $N_0/2$. The signal component at the output is:

$$y_s(L-1) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} s(n)\, s(n) = E_s$$

The noise variance at the output is computed via the Parseval/convolution property:

$$\sigma_w^2 = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} s^2(n) = \dfrac{N_0}{2} E_s$$

The output SNR is therefore:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{out} = \dfrac{y_s^2(L-1)}{\sigma_w^2} = \dfrac{E_s^2}{(N_0/2)E_s} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$$

This result, $\mathrm{SNR}_{out} = 2E_s/N_0$, is the **fundamental matched-filter theorem** — no other linear filter can produce a higher output SNR for a known signal in AWGN.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Typical Style)

**Given:**
- Template (one period of a 3 Hz spike-and-wave, sampled at $f_s = 250$ Hz, hence $L = 84$ samples covering 336 ms):
  $\mathbf{t} = [0,\, 5,\, 30,\, 60,\, 20,\, -10,\, -40,\, -20,\, 0]$ (simplified, length 9 for pedagogical clarity)
- Observed EEG window:
  $\mathbf{x} = [0.1,\, 0.5,\, 3.0,\, 6.0,\, 2.0,\, -1.0,\, -4.0,\, -2.0,\, 0.2]$

**Required:** Compute (a) the un-normalized cross-correlation, (b) the normalized cosine similarity, and (c) the MSE distance. Decide whether a spike is detected at threshold $T_{dec} = 0.85$.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Un-normalized correlation:**
$$r_{xt}(0) = (0)(0.1) + (5)(0.5) + (30)(3.0) + (60)(6.0) + (20)(2.0) + (-10)(-1.0) + (-40)(-4.0) + (-20)(-2.0) + (0)(0.2)$$
$$r_{xt}(0) = 0 + 2.5 + 90 + 360 + 40 + 10 + 160 + 40 + 0 = 702.5$$

**Step (b) — Energy normalizations:**
$$E_t = 0^2 + 5^2 + 30^2 + 60^2 + 20^2 + (-10)^2 + (-40)^2 + (-20)^2 + 0^2 = 0 + 25 + 900 + 3600 + 400 + 100 + 1600 + 400 + 0 = 7025$$
$$E_x = 0.1^2 + 0.5^2 + 3.0^2 + 6.0^2 + 2.0^2 + (-1.0)^2 + (-4.0)^2 + (-2.0)^2 + 0.2^2 = 0.01 + 0.25 + 9.0 + 36.0 + 4.0 + 1.0 + 16.0 + 4.0 + 0.04 = 70.30$$

**Cosine similarity:**
$$S_{xy} = \dfrac{702.5}{\sqrt{7025 \times 70.30}} = \dfrac{702.5}{\sqrt{493{,}857.5}} = \dfrac{702.5}{702.75} \approx 0.9996$$

**Step (c) — MSE distance:**
$$d_{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{9}\sum_{n=0}^{8} [x(n) - t(n)]^2$$
Computing each term: $0.01, 20.25, 729, 2916, 324, 81, 1296, 324, 0.04$ → sum $\approx 5690.30$
$$d_{MSE} = 632.26$$

**Decision:** Since $S_{xy} = 0.9996 \ge T_{dec} = 0.85$, a **SPIKE DETECTION IS DECLARED.** Note how the cosine similarity correctly identified the match despite the $\sim 10\times$ amplitude scaling of $\mathbf{x}$ relative to $\mathbf{t}$, which would have made the raw correlation value (702.5) difficult to threshold across patients.

---

### 3.4 Design Trade-off Matrix (Analytical Summary)

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a high-yield KTU revision tool. Each cell captures the effect of one design parameter on a clinical performance metric.

| Design Parameter $\uparrow$ | Sensitivity | Specificity | Computation | Robustness to Morphology Variation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of templates $K$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (initially) | $\uparrow \propto K$ | $\uparrow$ |
| Window overlap $R_{ov}$ | $\uparrow$ | $\approx$ flat | $\uparrow$ | flat |
| Decision threshold $T_{dec}$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | flat | flat |
| Template length $L$ | $\uparrow$ (initially) | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ (overfitting risk) |
| Sampling rate $f_s$ | $\uparrow$ for sharp spikes | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| Refractory period $\tau_{ref}$ | $\downarrow$ (may miss back-to-back) | $\uparrow$ | flat | flat |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Block Diagram of a Template-Matching EEG Spike Detector

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the entire signal flow from raw EEG acquisition to clinical event annotation, with explicit functional labels on every block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Multi-channel EEG] --> B[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    B --> C[50 Hz Notch Filter]
    C --> D[Windowed Segmentation Length L, Hop H]
    D --> E{Demean Window Subtract Mean}
    E --> F[Template Library T1 to TK]
    F --> G[Cross Correlation Engine dot product per lag]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Normalize by Energy sqrt of Ex times Et]
    H --> I[Maximum Score Selection max over K]
    I --> J{Threshold T dec}
    J --> K[Detection Event Spike Flag]
    J --> L[No Detection Background]
    K --> M[Refractory Period Filter 200 to 500 ms]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Cluster Adjacent Detections]
    N --> O[Clinical Annotation Output]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for a Single Window

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New window xw arrives] --> comp[Compute Sxy for all K templates]
    comp --> smax[Find Smax = max over k of Sxy]
    smax --> thresh{Smax greater than or equal to T dec}
    thresh --> yes[Flag detection increment counter]
    thresh --> no[Skip window advance by H]
    yes --> ref[Apply refractory period check]
    ref --> output[Update clinical output log]
    no --> output
```

### 4.3 Template Library Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Long-term EEG recording] --> annot[Expert epileptologist marks spike events]
    annot --> extract[Extract epochs length L around each mark]
    extract --> align[Time-align epochs to spike peak]
    align --> norm[Normalize amplitude to unit energy]
    norm --> cluster[Cluster similar epochs k-means]
    cluster --> proto[Select K cluster centroids as templates]
    proto --> lib[Store Template Library T1 to TK]
    lib --> deploy[Deploy to runtime detector]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Every node in the Mermaid blocks above is a functionally independent stage. The two decision diamonds (threshold and refractory) are the only points at which the binary "detection / no detection" outcome is finalized. The energy-normalization block (node H) is the **key feature** that distinguishes robust cosine-similarity-based template matching from naive raw-correlation approaches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define template matching in the context of EEG spike-and-wave detection. List any two clinical applications where this technique is deployed.*

**Model Answer:**
Template matching is a model-based pattern recognition technique in which a pre-recorded, normalized reference waveform (template) of a known EEG transient — such as a 3 Hz spike-and-wave complex of absence seizures — is compared against windowed segments of a continuous EEG signal using a similarity metric (typically normalized cross-correlation). A detection is declared when the similarity score exceeds a decision threshold.

**Clinical applications:**
1. **Long-term ambulatory EEG monitoring** for seizure counting in refractory epilepsy patients (e.g., 24-hour Holter-EEG studies).
2. **Closed-loop neurostimulation** systems (e.g., Responsive Neurostimulation / RNS by NeuroPace) where spike detection within 0.5 s latency triggers abortive stimulation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*State the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality and explain its role in bounding the normalized cross-correlation coefficient used in template matching.*

**Model Answer:**
The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality states that for any two real sequences $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{t}$ of equal length $L$, the absolute value of their inner product is bounded by the product of their L2 norms:
$$\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x(n)\, t(n) \vert \le \sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x^2(n)} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} t^2(n)}$$

**Role in template matching:** Dividing both sides by the right-hand product yields $\rho \in [-1, +1]$. The value $\rho = +1$ occurs only when the input window is a positively-scaled, perfectly aligned copy of the template, and $\rho = -1$ for a sign-inverted copy. This boundedness makes the similarity score **thresholdable in a patient-independent manner** — a clinical prerequisite for any deployable detector.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Format** — KTU allows the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions below carry sub-parts (a) [7 marks] and (b) [7 marks].

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Computational and Threshold Design Focus*

**(a)** For a 2-second EEG window sampled at $f_s = 250$ Hz, a clinician has extracted a single spike-and-wave template $\mathbf{t}$ of length $L = 100$ samples. The template and an observed window $\mathbf{x}$ (also $L = 100$ samples) yield an un-normalized cross-correlation $r_{xt}(0) = 480$ at zero lag. The window energy is $E_x = 6400$ and the template energy is $E_t = 900$.
**(i)** Compute the **normalized cosine similarity score** $S_{xy}$.
**(ii)** State whether a detection is declared if the threshold is $T_{dec} = 0.9$.
**(iii)** Briefly justify why the normalized score is preferred over the raw $r_{xt}(0)$ in clinical EEG monitoring. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** Apply the normalized cross-correlation formula:
$$S_{xy} = \dfrac{r_{xt}(0)}{\sqrt{E_x \cdot E_t}} = \dfrac{480}{\sqrt{6400 \times 900}} = \dfrac{480}{\sqrt{5{,}760{,}000}} = \dfrac{480}{2400} = 0.20$$

**(ii)** Since $S_{xy} = 0.20 < T_{dec} = 0.9$, **no detection is declared** for this window. The window is classified as background activity.

**(iii)** The raw $r_{xt}(0) = 480$ has units of $(\mu V)^2$ and is sensitive to amplitude scaling: a high-amplitude muscle artifact could produce a large raw correlation purely due to its magnitude, not its shape, causing a false positive. The normalized score $S_{xy} \in [-1, +1]$ is dimensionless and **invariant to amplitude scaling**, isolating *morphological* similarity from *energy* similarity. This permits a single fixed threshold to be used across patients with widely varying EEG amplitudes, which is the key clinical usability requirement.

---

**(b)** A team is designing a matched filter for spike-and-wave detection. The template is $s(n) = [1, 3, 2, -1, -3, -2, 0]$ (length $L = 7$, arbitrary units).
**(i)** Derive the impulse response $h(n)$ of the matched filter.
**(ii)** Compute the maximum output SNR of this filter if the input noise has two-sided PSD $N_0/2 = 0.5$ units.
**(iii)** If the input is $x(n) = s(n) + 0.1\,w(n)$ where $w(n)$ is zero-mean WGN, conceptually explain why matched filtering improves detection reliability. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** The matched filter impulse response is the time-reversed template:
$$h(n) = s(L-1-n) = s(6-n) \quad \text{for } n = 0,1,\ldots,6$$
Enumerating: $h(0) = s(6) = 0$, $h(1) = s(5) = -2$, $h(2) = s(4) = -3$, $h(3) = s(3) = -1$, $h(4) = s(2) = 2$, $h(5) = s(1) = 3$, $h(6) = s(0) = 1$.
$$\boxed{h(n) = [0,\ -2,\ -3,\ -1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 1]}$$

**(ii)** Compute the template energy:
$$E_s = \sum_{n=0}^{6} s^2(n) = 1 + 9 + 4 + 1 + 9 + 4 + 0 = 28 \text{ units}$$
Maximum output SNR:
$$\mathrm{SNR}_{out} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0} = \dfrac{2 \times 28}{2 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{56}{1} = 56 \text{ (linear, dimensionless)}$$
In decibels: $\mathrm{SNR}_{out,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(56) \approx 17.48$ dB.

**(iii)** The matched filter coherently accumulates the signal energy at the synchronization instant (zero lag) because the template is *time-aligned* with the input signal component $s(n)$. Meanwhile, the noise $w(n)$ — being white and uncorrelated — does not coherently accumulate, and its variance grows only proportionally to $\sqrt{E_s}$ rather than $E_s$. The result is that the signal-to-noise ratio at the matched filter output is **maximized** at the lag corresponding to the spike location, producing a sharp detection peak that rises well above the noise floor, making thresholding reliable and the false-alarm rate low.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Algorithmic Design and Clinical Performance Focus*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete end-to-end pipeline** of a template-matching-based EEG spike-and-wave detection system. Label each block clearly. Discuss the role of:
**(i)** Bandpass and notch filtering.
**(ii)** Energy normalization of templates.
**(iii)** Refractory period post-processing. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The block diagram is as shown in SECTION_4 above. The role of each stage is described below.

**(i) Bandpass and notch filtering:** EEG is contaminated by slow baseline drifts (<0.5 Hz, from electrode polarization and subject motion) and high-frequency muscle (EMG) artifact (>40 Hz). A **bandpass filter (0.5–40 Hz)** preserves the frequency content of spike-and-wave complexes (whose spectral energy lies between 1 Hz and 25 Hz) while rejecting these artifacts. The **50/60 Hz notch filter** specifically suppresses powerline interference, which would otherwise produce spurious correlation peaks in mains-polluted recordings.

**(ii) Energy normalization of templates:** Spike amplitudes in human EEG vary from ~20 µV (deep foci) to >500 µV (cortical foci) across patients, and even within a single patient across sleep-wake cycles. If templates are *not* energy-normalized, the cosine-similarity calculation (which normalizes by both window and template energies) would still work, but the **computational dynamic range** of the intermediate raw correlation would be huge, risking numerical underflow in fixed-point hardware. More importantly, **unit-energy templates** allow direct comparison of similarity scores across the entire template library, simplifying threshold calibration.

**(iii) Refractory period post-processing:** A single sustained 3 Hz spike-and-wave burst lasting 3 seconds would produce ~9 separate detections when windowed with 50% overlap. Clinically, these represent **one** epileptiform event. The refractory period filter (typically 200–500 ms) suppresses redundant detections within this window, counting each burst as a single clinical event and dramatically reducing the false-positive rate that would otherwise inflate the apparent "spike count."

---

**(b)** A clinical research group has recorded 10 hours of EEG from an epileptic patient. A template-matching detector produces the following contingency table when compared against expert ground-truth annotation:

| | Ground Truth Positive | Ground Truth Negative | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detector Positive** | 270 (TP) | 50 (FP) | 320 |
| **Detector Negative** | 30 (FN) | 5650 (TN) | 5680 |
| **Total** | 300 | 5700 | 6000 |

Compute:
**(i)** Sensitivity, **(ii)** Specificity, **(iii)** False Detection Rate per hour, and **(iv)** Positive Predictive Value (PPV). Comment on whether the detector meets the clinical performance target of Se $\geq 90\%$ and FDR $\leq 1$/hour. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sensitivity (True Positive Rate):**
$$\mathrm{Se} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{270}{270 + 30} = \dfrac{270}{300} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

**(ii) Specificity (True Negative Rate):**
$$\mathrm{Sp} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP} = \dfrac{5650}{5650 + 50} = \dfrac{5650}{5700} \approx 0.9912 = 99.12\%$$

**(iii) False Detection Rate (per hour):**
$$\mathrm{FDR}_{hr} = \dfrac{FP}{\text{Total duration in hours}} = \dfrac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ per hour}$$

**(iv) Positive Predictive Value:**
$$\mathrm{PPV} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{270}{320} = 0.84375 = 84.375\%$$

**Clinical Interpretation:**
- **Sensitivity (90%)** meets the clinical target of $\geq 90\%$ — **marginally acceptable**, as a 10% miss rate means 30 clinically significant spikes were missed.
- **FDR (5/hour)** is **5 times worse** than the target of $\leq 1$/hour — the detector is generating excessive false alarms, which is consistent with the modest PPV of 84.4%.
- **Conclusion:** The detector's *sensitivity* is at the lower bound of acceptability, but its *specificity* is inadequate for unattended clinical deployment. Engineering recommendation: **raise the decision threshold $T_{dec}$** to suppress false positives (which will trade some sensitivity for better specificity), and re-evaluate on a held-out validation set. Additional artifact-rejection preprocessing (e.g., EMG-burst detection) is also recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the cross-correlation output for a probability:** Students often write "$S_{xy} = 0.95$ means there is a 95% chance the window contains a spike." This is **wrong** — the score is a *geometric similarity metric*, not a probability. Probabilities require Bayesian or likelihood-ratio frameworks.
> 2. **Forgetting the energy normalization:** A 7-mark sub-question explicitly asking for the *normalized* score is frequently answered with the *raw* dot product, leading to a 4-mark deduction.
> 3. **Conflating matched filter SNR with input SNR:** The matched filter's output SNR of $2E_s/N_0$ is a *gain* over the input SNR — students must not equate it to the input SNR, which is typically $E_s/(N_0 \cdot L)$ for a window of $L$ samples.
> 4. **Ignoring the refractory period:** Many students assume each detection is a unique clinical event. Real EEG bursts produce 5–15 overlapping window detections per event, and omitting the refractory-period explanation costs easy marks.
> 5. **Computing FDR as a probability instead of a rate per unit time:** The clinical metric is **per hour** (or per 24 hours for ambulatory monitoring), not a dimensionless ratio — both forms will appear in KTU questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Re-read this box 5 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **Template matching = pattern recognition by shape similarity** — robust to amplitude variations because of energy normalization.
- ✅ **Cosine similarity** is the *preferred* metric: $S_{xy} = \frac{\sum x(n)t(n)}{\sqrt{E_x E_t}}$, bounded in $[-1, +1]$.
- ✅ **Matched filter is mathematically equivalent to cross-correlation** at zero lag: $y_{MF}(L-1) = r_{xs}(0)$.
- ✅ **Matched filter SNR gain** $= 2E_s/N_0$ — this is the **maximum** achievable by any linear filter (AWGN assumption).
- ✅ **Pipeline stages** in order: Acquire → Bandpass → Notch → Segment → Demean → Correlate → Normalize → Threshold → Refractory → Cluster → Annotate.
- ✅ **Threshold selection**: Bayesian minimum-error (equal priors) uses $T^* = (\mu_{H_0} + \mu_{H_1})/2$; Neyman-Pearson fixes $P_{FA} = \alpha$ and is the KTU-preferred clinical paradigm.
- ✅ **Sample rate** must be $\geq 200$ Hz to preserve sharp spike edges (Nyquist for a 70 ms spike demands $f_s > 14$ Hz, but clinical oversampling is mandatory).
- ✅ **Window overlap** $R_{ov} = 50\%$ to $75\%$ prevents missing transients at window boundaries.
- ✅ **Clinical KPIs**: Se $\geq 90\%$, Sp $\geq 95\%$, FDR $\leq 0.5$/min, latency $\leq 0.5$ s.
- ✅ **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** guarantees the boundedness of normalized correlation — quote it whenever justifying the threshold scheme.
- ✅ **Refractory period** (200–500 ms) merges fragmented detections of a single sustained burst into one clinical event.
- ✅ **Template library** should contain 5–20 normalized prototypes to capture inter-patient and intra-patient morphological variability.
- ✅ **Failing to normalize** the template energy leads to a template-length-dependent bias in the similarity score — a classic KTU trap.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preprocessing of EEG Signals - Noise Removal and Artifact Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Preprocessing of EEG Signals - Noise Removal and Artifact Reduction --- # Module 4 — Preprocessing of EEG Signals: Noise Removal and Artifact Reduction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **EEG Preprocessing** is the systematic sequence of signal conditioning operations applied to raw scalp-recorded electroencephalographic (EEG) signals *before* feature extraction, in order to **enhance the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**, suppress physiological and non-physiological contaminants (artifacts), and extract the neural activity of interest within the diagnostically/clinically relevant band (typically **0.5 Hz – 30 Hz**, sometimes extended to 100 Hz). The **electroencephalogram (EEG)** measures the summed post-synaptic electrical activity of cortical pyramidal neurons via surface electrodes placed according to the international **10–20 system**. Because the cortical signal is heavily attenuated by the skull and scalp, the measurable amplitude at the scalp is extremely small (**10 – 100 µV** peak-to-peak), making the EEG one of the most fragile biosignals recorded in clinical practice. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Whispering Gallery" Imagine trying to **hear a single soft-spoken student (the brain signal) in a noisy school assembly hall**. Around him: - A ceiling fan (**thermal/Johnson noise**, broadband hiss), - A flickering tube light at 50 Hz (**power-line interference**), - A classmate tapping a pen rhythmically (**muscle/EMG artifact**), - Someone waving a hand in front of the speaker (**eye-blink/EOG artifact**), - The student himself shifting in his seat (**electrode/motion artifact**). **EEG preprocessing is the audio engineer's job** — he applies: 1. **High-pass filter** → removes the rumble of the building vibrating (baseline wander), 2. **Low-pass filter** → suppresses the high-pitched pen tapping, 3. **Notch filter at 50 Hz** → eliminates the precise hum of the tube light, 4. **Smart noise-cancelling algorithm (ICA / Adaptive filter)** → detects and subtracts the hand-waving and pen-tapping patterns while preserving the student's voice. After all this, the original whisper becomes audibly clean for analysis. ### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Raw EEG | Parameter | Typical Value | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | **Amplitude** | **10 – 100 µV** | Amplification gain ≈ 10,000–100,000 required | | **Frequency band** | **0.5 – 30 Hz** (clinical) / up to 100 Hz (research) | Sets filter passband | | **Sampling rate** | **256 Hz – 2048 Hz** | Per **Nyquist theorem**, must be ≥ 2 × f_max | | **Number of channels** | 16 / 32 / 64 / 128 / 256 | Drives multi-channel algorithms (ICA, CAR) | | **Reference** | Linked ears, average, mastoid | Choice affects preprocessing pipeline | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024, students must clearly distinguish between **noise** (unwanted random signals, e.g., thermal) and **artifacts** (structured unwanted signals of physiological/external origin, e.g., blinks, EMG). Noise removal ≠ Artifact reduction; both are preprocessing goals. ### 1.4 Classification of EEG Contaminants **A. Non-Physiological (External/Instrumental) Noise:** - **Power-line interference** at **50 Hz (India/Europe)** or **60 Hz (USA)**. - **Electrode contact / impedance noise** (loose electrodes, gel drying). - **Cable movement / motion artifacts** (capacitive pickup, microphonic effects). - **Thermal (Johnson) noise** — broadband, Gaussian; irreducible floor. - **Quantization noise** of the ADC. **B. Physiological Artifacts:** - **EOG (electro-oculogram)** — eye blinks & saccades (largest, 100× EEG amplitude). - **EMG (electromyogram)** — scalp/neck muscle activity (broadband, > 20 Hz). - **ECG (electrocardiogram)** — cardiac pulse artifact in earlobe/channels near carotids. - **GSR / sweat-induced slow drifts** — low-frequency baseline wandering. - **Glossokinetic potentials** — tongue movements. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Preprocessing Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic) The standard EEG preprocessing chain is executed in this canonical order: **Step 1 → Data Import & Channel Inspection** - Reject flat / saturated channels (variance ≈ 0 or > 200 µV). - Verify sampling rate consistency. **Step 2 → Filtering** - **High-pass filter (0.5 – 1 Hz)** to remove DC drift and slow sweat artifacts. - **Low-pass filter (30 – 40 Hz)** to suppress EMG, high-frequency noise. - **Notch filter at 50 Hz** (Q ≈ 30) to remove power-line interference. - *Filter choice*: **FIR** (zero-phase, linear, stable) preferred over IIR (cheaper but phase-distorting). **Step 3 → Bad Channel Interpolation / Re-referencing** - Compute **Common Average Reference (CAR)**: $$V_{i}^{CAR}(n) = V_{i}(n) - \frac{1}{M}\sum_{j=1}^{M} V_{j}(n)$$ - Interpolate bad channels using **spherical spline interpolation** of neighbouring electrodes. **Step 4 → Artifact Detection & Removal** - **Amplitude thresholding** (± 75–100 µV) for blink detection. - **ICA / PCA** decomposition for blind source separation. - **Adaptive filtering** (LMS / RLS) when a reference channel (e.g., VEOG) exists. - **Wavelet thresholding** for non-stationary denoising. **Step 5 → Segmentation (Epoching)** - Cut continuous EEG into **epochs of 1–4 s** for trial-based analysis. **Step 6 → Baseline Correction** - Subtract the mean of a pre-stimulus baseline window: $$V_{corrected}(n) = V(n) - \bar{V}_{baseline}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind the Order:** Filtering is done *first* because ICA assumes relatively stationary sources; if DC drift dominates, ICA cannot separate blink components from drift. Artifact removal precedes segmentation because artifacts must be evaluated in continuous data (a blink spanning epoch boundaries is harder to handle). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **SNR Definition** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Power in **W** or **µV²** | Healthy EEG: SNR ≈ **0 to 10 dB** | | 2 | **Common Average Reference** | $V_{i}^{CAR}(n) = V_{i}(n) - \dfrac{1}{M}\sum_{j=1}^{M} V_{j}(n)$ | $M$ = number of channels | Assumes brain sources are uncorrelated and average out | | 3 | **Notch Filter (2nd-order IIR)** | $H(z) = \dfrac{1 - 2\cos\omega_{0}\,z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_{0}\,z^{-1} + r^{2}z^{-2}}$ | $\omega_{0} = 2\pi f_{0}/f_{s}$, $r \approx 0.9$ for sharp notch | $f_{0}$ = 50 Hz in India | | 4 | **LMS Adaptive Filter Update** | $w(n+1) = w(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\, x(n)$ | $\mu$ = step-size, $e(n)$ = error | Convergence: $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ | | 5 | **LMS Output** | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | $\mathbf{w},\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ | $N$ = filter order | | 6 | **RLS Recursion (gain)** | $k(n) = \dfrac{P(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)P(n-1)\mathbf{x}(n)}$ | $\lambda$ = forgetting factor (0.95–0.99) | Faster convergence than LMS | | 7 | **Wavelet Thresholding** | $\hat{c}_{j,k} = \begin{cases} \text{sign}(c_{j,k})(\vert c_{j,k}\vert - \lambda), & \vert c_{j,k}\vert \ge \lambda \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $\lambda = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$ (universal threshold) | **Soft thresholding** for smooth denoising | | 8 | **ICA Goal (Independence)** | Maximize non-Gaussianity of $y = \mathbf{W}x$ | $y$ statistically independent components | Sources assumed non-Gaussian | | 9 | **PCA Variance Retained** | $\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_{i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{M}\lambda_{i}} \ge 0.95$ | $\lambda_{i}$ = eigenvalues of covariance | Keep top $k$ components | | 10 | **EOG Regression (Gratton)** | $V_{corr}(n) = V_{EEG}(n) - \beta\, V_{EOG}(n - \tau)$ | $\beta$ = propagation coefficient, $\tau$ = delay | Requires dedicated EOG channel | | 11 | **Surface Laplacian** | $V_{Laplacian} \approx \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}(V_{i} - V_{0})$ | $k$ = neighbouring electrodes | Sharpens spatial resolution | | 12 | **Powerline Interference Model** | $v_{pl}(n) = A\sin(2\pi f_{0}n/f_{s} + \phi)$ | $A$ = amplitude, $f_{0}$ = 50 Hz | Narrowband, periodic | ### 2.3 Filtering Strategies — Deeper Discussion #### A. FIR vs IIR for EEG | Property | FIR (Finite Impulse Response) | IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) | |---|---|---| | **Phase** | **Linear phase** (no distortion) | Non-linear (warps waveform) | | **Stability** | Always stable | Conditionally stable | | **Order needed** | High (50–100 taps) | Low (4–8 taps) | | **Use case** | **Preferred for clinical EEG** | Real-time low-power devices | Zero-phase filtering is achieved by **filtfilt()** — running the filter forward then backward. This is **not causal**, hence not for real-time, but excellent for offline KTU-lab analysis. #### B. Adaptive Filtering — When Reference Channels Exist When an **auxiliary EOG/EMG channel** is recorded simultaneously, adaptive filtering is optimal: - **Reference input** $x(n)$: EOG channel. - **Primary input** $d(n)$: Contaminated EEG channel. - **Output** $y(n)$: Estimate of EOG leakage. - **Error** $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$: Cleaned EEG. The **LMS (Least Mean Squares)** algorithm iteratively minimizes $E[e^{2}(n)]$ using the simple update: $$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$ **Convergence requirement:** $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})}$, where $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ is the autocorrelation matrix of the reference. #### C. Wavelet Denoising (Non-stationary approach) EEG is **non-stationary** (transient spikes, bursts). Classical Fourier filters assume stationarity. **Wavelet transform** offers time–frequency localization: 1. Decompose EEG using **Daubechies-4 (db4)** wavelet to 5–6 levels. 2. Apply **threshold** to detail coefficients (universal: $\lambda = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$). 3. Reconstruct. This is excellent for **EMG artifact** which is broadband, since wavelets capture its sharp transients at fine scales. #### D. ICA — The Gold Standard for Ocular Artifact Removal **Independent Component Analysis (ICA)** decomposes the multichannel EEG $\mathbf{x}(n) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{s}(n)$ into maximally independent sources $\mathbf{s}(n)$. Blinks produce a **spatially fixed, temporally stereotyped component** identifiable by: - Topographic map concentrated **frontally**, - Time-course with sharp, periodic 200–400 ms deflections every 3–10 s. After identification, that component is zeroed and the signal reconstructed: $\mathbf{x}_{clean} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{W}^{-1}\mathbf{s}_{clean}$. The widely used algorithm is **Infomax (Bell & Sejnowski)** or **FastICA (Hyvärinen)**. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Clinical neurology**: Clean EEG is mandatory for diagnosing **epilepsy** (spike detection fails on artifact-corrupted data). - **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface)**: P300 spellers, motor-imagery wheelchairs — every 1 dB of SNR improvement lifts classification accuracy by **2–5%**. - **Sleep medicine (polysomnography)**: Sleep-stage scoring (REM, N1–N3) is impossible without EMG/EOG removal. - **Neurofeedback / cognitive neuroscience**: Source-localization algorithms (LORETA, sLORETA) are highly sensitive to ocular contamination. - **Wearable EEG (consumer headsets like Muse, Emotiv)**: Heavily rely on aggressive ICA + adaptive filtering for dry-electrode robustness. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the LMS Adaptive Filter Update Rule The LMS algorithm is derived from the steepest-descent minimization of the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** cost function. **Step 1 — Define the cost function.** Let $\mathbf{w}(n) = [w_{0}(n), w_{1}(n), \ldots, w_{N-1}(n)]^{T}$ be the adaptive filter coefficient vector, and $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-N+1)]^{T}$ the tapped reference input. The filter output is: $$y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_{k}(n)\, x(n-k)$$ **Step 2 — Define the error signal.** The error (cleaned EEG) is the difference between the primary input $d(n)$ and the adaptive output: $$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$ **Step 3 — Instantaneous squared error (LMS uses this instead of true MSE for simplicity).** $$J(n) = e^{2}(n)$$ **Step 4 — Gradient of $J$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$.** $$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = \frac{\partial\, e^{2}(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2 e(n) \frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}}$$ From Step 2: $$\frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -\mathbf{x}(n)$$ Therefore: $$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = -2\, e(n)\, \mathbf{x}(n)$$ **Step 5 — Apply steepest descent update.** The standard gradient descent is: $$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n)$$ Substituting: $$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \big[-2\, e(n)\, \mathbf{x}(n)\big]$$ $$\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\, \mathbf{x}(n)}$$ **Step 6 — Convergence condition.** Taking expectations and analysing the mean-weight recursion, the LMS converges in the mean if: $$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$$ where $\lambda_{max}$ is the **largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)]$**. In practice, a normalized step-size $\mu_{NLMS} = \dfrac{\mu}{\epsilon + \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)}$ is used to avoid numerical issues. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the 50 Hz Notch Filter (2nd-Order IIR, Form II) A notch filter places a **zero on the unit circle** at the interfering frequency to cancel it, and a **pole inside the unit circle** close to the zero to limit cancellation to a narrow band. **Step 1 — Set the notch angular frequency.** $$\omega_{0} = \frac{2\pi f_{0}}{f_{s}} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{f_{s}}$$ For $f_{s} = 250$ Hz (common in EEG): $$\omega_{0} = \frac{100\pi}{250} = 0.4\pi = 1.2566 \text{ rad/sample}$$ **Step 2 — Place zeros on the unit circle at $e^{\pm j\omega_{0}}$.** Numerator polynomial: $$N(z) = (1 - e^{j\omega_{0}} z^{-1})(1 - e^{-j\omega_{0}} z^{-1}) = 1 - 2\cos\omega_{0}\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$ For $\omega_{0} = 0.4\pi$: $\cos(0.4\pi) = \cos(72°) = 0.3090$. Hence numerator: $$N(z) = 1 - 0.618\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$ **Step 3 — Place poles radially inside at radius $r < 1$.** For a sharp notch, $r \approx 0.9$: $$D(z) = 1 - 2r\cos\omega_{0}\, z^{-1} + r^{2} z^{-2} = 1 - 0.5562\, z^{-1} + 0.81\, z^{-2}$$ **Step 4 — Normalize gain to 1 at DC and Nyquist.** A gain-correction factor: $$K = \frac{1 - 2r\cos\omega_{0} + r^{2}}{1 - 2\cos\omega_{0} + 1} = \frac{1 - 0.5562 + 0.81}{2 - 0.618} = \frac{1.2538}{1.382} = 0.9073$$ **Step 5 — Final transfer function.** $$H(z) = K\,\frac{1 - 0.618\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5562\, z^{-1} + 0.81\, z^{-2}}$$ This notch filter has a 3-dB bandwidth of $\Delta f \approx \dfrac{f_{s}}{\pi}\arccos\!\left[\dfrac{2r}{1+r^{2}}\right]$, giving $\Delta f \approx 4.4$ Hz at $f_{s} = 250$ Hz — ideal for surgical removal of 50 Hz without distorting surrounding neural bands. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Denoising Method | Artifact Type | Frequency Range | Amplitude | Best Method | Why | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Power-line 50 Hz** | Narrowband at 50 Hz | Up to mV | **Notch filter (IIR/FIR)** | Periodic, single frequency, narrow band | | **Baseline wander** | < 0.5 Hz | Tens of µV | **High-pass FIR (0.5–1 Hz)** | Removes DC drift & sweat | | **Eye blink (EOG)** | Broadband, slow, **200–400 ms** | **200–800 µV** | **ICA** or **regression** (VEOG) | Spatially stereotyped frontal pattern | | **Eye movement (saccade)** | Broadband, slow, **> 400 ms** | 100–300 µV | **ICA** | Distinct lateral frontal topography | | **Muscle (EMG)** | **> 20 Hz**, broadband | Variable | **Low-pass at 30–40 Hz** + **wavelet thresholding** | Broadband, high-frequency | | **Cardiac (ECG)** | 0.5–2 Hz, **pulsatile** | 50–200 µV in earlobe channels | **ICA** or template subtraction | Rhythmic, distinct temporal signature | | **Electrode pop** | Step discontinuities | Very large | **Amplitude threshold + interpolation** | Abrupt DC shifts | | **Thermal/Johnson noise** | **Broadband, Gaussian** | < 5 µV RMS | **Low-pass filtering** (limited gain) | Theoretical floor, irreducible | --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — SNR Improvement A 32-channel EEG has clean brain signal RMS = **8 µV** and total noise RMS = **12 µV** (unfiltered). **Step 1 — Compute pre-filtering SNR.** $$\text{SNR}_{in} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{8^{2}}{12^{2}} = \frac{64}{144} = 0.444$$ $$\text{SNR}_{in,dB} = 10\log_{10}(0.444) = -3.52 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 2 — After 0.5–30 Hz bandpass + 50 Hz notch, noise RMS drops to 3 µV** (artifacts suppressed). $$\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{8^{2}}{3^{2}} = \frac{64}{9} = 7.111$$ $$\text{SNR}_{out,dB} = 10\log_{10}(7.111) = 8.52 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 3 — SNR improvement.** $$\Delta \text{SNR} = 8.52 - (-3.52) = 12.04 \text{ dB}$$ This ≈ **16× power improvement**, which is typical of well-tuned EEG preprocessing pipelines. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Canonical EEG Preprocessing Flow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Scalp EEG
32-256 Channels
10-100 uV] --> B[Channel Inspection
Reject Flat/Saturated] B --> C[High-Pass Filter
0.5-1 Hz FIR] C --> D[Low-Pass Filter
30-40 Hz FIR] D --> E[Notch Filter
50 Hz IIR] E --> F[Common Average
Re-Reference CAR] F --> G{Bad Channel
Detected?} G -->|Yes| H[Spherical Spline
Interpolation] G -->|No| I[ICA Decomposition
FastICA Algorithm] H --> I I --> J[Manual / Automatic
Component Rejection] J --> K[Reconstruct Clean EEG
x_clean = A W^-1 s_clean] K --> L[Epoching
1-4 s segments] L --> M[Baseline Correction
Subtract pre-stimulus mean] M --> N[Preprocessed EEG
Ready for Analysis] N --> O[Feature Extraction
ERP, PSD, Connectivity] N --> P[Classification
BCI, Seizure Detection] ``` ### 4.2 Adaptive Noise Canceller Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Primary P1[Contaminated EEG
d n = s n + n0 n] end subgraph Reference R1[EOG / EMG Channel
x n = n1 n correlated with n0 n] end P1 --> Sum1((+)) Sum1 --> Error[e n = d n - y n] Error --> Clean[Cleaned EEG Output] R1 --> AF[Adaptive Filter
LMS w n+1 = w n + 2u e n x n] AF --> Output[y n = wTxn] Output --> Sum1 Error --> AF ``` ### 4.3 Wavelet Denoising Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Noisy EEG Signal
x n] --> B[Discrete Wavelet Transform
db4, 5-6 levels] B --> C1[Approximation Coeffs
cA5 - low frequency] B --> C2[Detail Coefficients
cD1 to cD5] C2 --> D[Soft Thresholding
lambda = sigma sqrt 2 ln N] D --> E[Thresholded Details] C1 --> F[Combine cA5 + Thresholded Details] E --> F F --> G[Inverse DWT] G --> H[Reconstructed
Denoised EEG] ``` ### 4.4 ICA Decomposition Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Multichannel EEG
x in R MxT] --> B[Whitening
Sphering] B --> C[ICA Algorithm
FastICA or Infomax] C --> D[Mixing Matrix A] C --> E[Independent Sources
s in R NxT] E --> F[Identify Artifactual ICs
blinks, muscle, ECG] F --> G[Zero Bad Components
s_clean] G --> H[Reconstruct
x_clean = A W^-1 s_clean] H --> I[Clean EEG
Ready for Analysis] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB` style) with descriptive upper-case quoted labels and no special markdown inside the quoted labels — fully compatible with Mermaid's strict parser. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** What are the main sources of artifacts in EEG signals?
