List any four with their typical amplitude and frequency characteristics. **Model Answer:** EEG artifacts are of two types: 1. **External (non-physiological)**: Power-line interference (50 Hz, up to mV), electrode contact noise (broadband, ~50 µV), cable motion artifacts. 2. **Internal (physiological)**: EOG eye blinks (200–800 µV, 200–400 ms duration, low-frequency dominated), EMG muscle activity (broadband, > 20 Hz, 10–100 µV), ECG cardiac artifact (0.5–2 Hz, ~50–200 µV in earlobe channels), sweat/GSR baseline drift (< 0.5 Hz, tens of µV). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Explain the principle of a 50 Hz notch filter used in EEG preprocessing. Why is the Q-factor typically chosen high (≈ 30)? **Model Answer:** A 50 Hz notch filter is a **band-stop filter** designed to reject the narrow band centred at 50 Hz (power-line frequency in India) while passing all other frequencies. It is implemented using a 2nd-order IIR with zeros on the unit circle at $e^{\pm j\omega_{0}}$ ($\omega_{0} = 2\pi \cdot 50/f_{s}$) and poles at radius $r \approx 0.9$ slightly inside. A high Q-factor (~30) ensures: - The **notch is sharp and narrow** (bandwidth ≈ 4 Hz), preserving neural activity in adjacent bands (e.g., 40–60 Hz gamma rhythms). - Minimum distortion of the EEG waveform outside the 50 Hz band. - Insensitive to slight frequency drift of the mains supply. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternatives are provided — students answer **either** set. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU Model Paper — Module 4] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of an adaptive noise canceller** for removing EOG artifacts from EEG using the LMS algorithm. Derive the LMS weight update equation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (textual):** The adaptive noise canceller has two inputs: - **Primary input** $d(n) = s(n) + n_{0}(n)$ — contaminated EEG (neural signal $s(n)$ + EOG leakage $n_{0}(n)$). - **Reference input** $x(n)$ — EOG channel recording, correlated with $n_{0}(n)$ but not with $s(n)$. The adaptive filter (FIR of order $N$) processes $x(n)$ to produce an estimate $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ of the EOG leakage. The **error signal** $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ is the cleaned EEG and is also fed back to update the filter weights. **LMS Derivation (as in Section 3.1):** Cost function: $J(n) = e^{2}(n)$ where $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$. $$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = -2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$$ Applying steepest descent: $$\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}$$ **Convergence:** $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})$. **Key assumptions:** (1) Reference is correlated only with artifact; (2) EEG and reference are statistically independent; (3) Step-size small enough for stable convergence. **(b)** Compare **FIR and IIR filters** for EEG preprocessing. Why is the **Common Average Reference (CAR)** used, and what is its limitation? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | FIR | IIR | |---|---|---| | Phase | Linear (no distortion) | Non-linear | | Stability | Always stable | Conditionally stable | | Order | High (50–100) | Low (4–8) | | Computational cost | High | Low | | Group delay | Constant | Frequency-dependent | | EEG suitability | **Preferred for clinical** | Real-time wearables | **Common Average Reference (CAR):** $$V_{i}^{CAR}(n) = V_{i}(n) - \frac{1}{M}\sum_{j=1}^{M} V_{j}(n)$$ **Why used:** Removes any reference-electrode bias common to all channels; provides a "neutral" reference. **Limitations:** 1. If a single channel contains large noise, it contaminates the average and leaks into all channels. 2. Assumes all channels sample brain activity symmetrically — not true for focal sources. 3. Reduces but does not remove artifact (blinks are picked up by most frontal channels, biasing the average). 4. Loses absolute voltage information. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU Model Paper — Module 4] **(a)** Explain the **Independent Component Analysis (ICA)** method for removal of ocular artifacts from EEG. Mention its assumptions and limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Principle:** ICA decomposes multichannel EEG $\mathbf{x}(n) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{s}(n)$ into statistically independent source components $\mathbf{s}(n)$, given by $\mathbf{s} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}$, where $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}$ is the unmixing matrix. It assumes: 1. Sources are **statistically independent**. 2. Sources have **non-Gaussian** distributions (Central Limit Theorem basis). 3. Mixing is **linear and instantaneous**. **Procedure:** 1. Apply bandpass filter (1–30 Hz) to EEG. 2. Run ICA (FastICA or Infomax) to obtain $N$ independent components. 3. Inspect each component's **topographic map** and **time-course**. 4. Blink components show: frontal topographic map (high weight over Fp1, Fp2), sharp 200–400 ms deflections every 3–10 s. 5. Mark these components as artifacts, set their time-courses to zero. 6. Reconstruct: $\mathbf{x}_{clean} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{W}^{-1}\mathbf{s}_{clean}$. **Limitations:** - Manual component rejection is **subjective** (inter-rater variability). - If blink component also contains neural activity (rare but possible), removing it loses information. - Requires sufficient channels ($N \ge 32$ recommended; theoretically ≥ number of sources). - Cannot separate two simultaneous blinks on different spatial locations if they perfectly correlate. **(b)** Describe **wavelet-based denoising** of EEG. How is the threshold $\lambda$ chosen? Why is **Daubechies-4 (db4)** a common choice? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Wavelet Denoising Steps:** 1. **Decomposition:** Apply Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) using db4 to 5–6 levels, producing one approximation coefficient set $cA_{L}$ and detail sets $cD_{1}, \ldots, cD_{L}$. 2. **Thresholding:** Apply soft-threshold to detail coefficients: $$\hat{c}_{j,k} = \text{sign}(c_{j,k})\max(\vert c_{j,k}\vert - \lambda,\, 0)$$ 3. **Reconstruction:** Apply inverse DWT with thresholded details + original approximation. **Threshold choice:** - **Universal threshold (Donoho):** $\lambda = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$, where $\sigma$ = median absolute deviation of finest detail coefficients / 0.6745. - **Minimax threshold:** minimises the mean squared error risk. - **Sure threshold:** adaptive per level. **Why Daubechies-4 (db4):** - db4 has **4 vanishing moments**, allowing it to efficiently represent polynomial trends of degree ≤ 3 (including slow EEG drifts). - **Compact support** (4–7 coefficients) provides good time-localization for sharp transients like spikes and EMG. - Approximately **symmetric phase response** minimises distortion. - Good trade-off between smoothness and sharpness for EEG morphology. **Advantage over classical filters:** Handles **non-stationary** artifacts (transient muscle bursts) without smearing. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board):** > 1. **Conflating noise and artifacts** — they are NOT the same; noise is random, artifacts are structured. > 2. **Forgetting to mention the reference for ICA** — ICA needs a multichannel setup; for a single channel it is undefined. > 3. **Writing "FIR is better than IIR"** without justification — Board wants comparison along phase, stability, order, distortion. > 4. **Skipping the convergence condition** for LMS ($0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$) — a 1-mark deduction. > 5. **Using $f_{s} = 100$ Hz** when given an EEG sampled at 250 Hz — incorrect notch frequency computation. > 6. **No diagram in adaptive filter / ICA question** — minimum 2-mark deduction. > 7. **Calling ICA = PCA** — partial credit only; independence ≠ uncorrelated. > 8. **Not mentioning zero-phase filtfilt** for offline EEG analysis. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **EEG amplitude** = 10–100 µV; **band** = 0.5–30 Hz (clinical) — these set all preprocessing parameters. - [x] **Preprocessing order**: Filter → Re-reference → Artifact removal → Epoch → Baseline correction. - [x] **50 Hz notch** (India) is mandatory; **60 Hz** for USA/Japan EEG. - [x] **FIR filters** preferred for clinical EEG (linear phase, no waveform distortion). - [x] **LMS update rule**: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$. - [x] **Convergence of LMS**: $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}(\mathbf{R}_{xx})$. - [x] **ICA** is the gold standard for ocular artifacts; needs ≥ 32 channels in practice. - [x] **ICA assumptions**: linear mixing, statistical independence, non-Gaussian sources. - [x] **PCA ≠ ICA**: PCA finds uncorrelated (orthogonal) components; ICA finds independent components. - [x] **CAR** = subtract channel-by-channel mean; useful but not artifact-free. - [x] **EOG blinks** are 200–400 ms deflections, frontal topography, 200–800 µV — 10× EEG. - [x] **Wavelet thresholding** uses $\lambda = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$ (universal); db4 is common wavelet. - [x] **Baseline correction** = subtract mean of pre-stimulus window. - [x] **SNR improvement** of ~10–15 dB achievable with well-tuned pipeline. - [x] Always mention **sampling rate** when discussing any digital filter (notch, bandpass, etc.).

topics.title: Feature Extraction - Time-Frequency Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feature Extraction - Time-Frequency Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Time-Frequency Analysis (TFA)** is a branch of signal processing that simultaneously describes a signal in both the **time domain** and the **frequency domain**. It overcomes the fundamental limitation of the classical Fourier Transform, which provides only global frequency information while discarding all temporal localization of those frequencies.

For non-stationary biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG, etc.), the spectral content varies with time. TFA generates a two-dimensional representation $TFR(t, f)$ in which the signal energy or power is mapped as a function of both time $t$ and frequency $f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Time-Frequency Analysis refers to the class of techniques used to compute the joint time-frequency distribution $TFR(t,f)$ of a signal, enabling the extraction of features such as instantaneous frequency, spectral evolution, and energy localization — all of which are critical for the diagnosis of pathologies from biomedical signals.

The mathematical foundation is built upon a **kernel function** $\phi(\tau, \nu)$ that acts on the local autocorrelation function $R_x(t, \tau)$ of the signal:

$$
TFR_x(t, f) = \iiint e^{j2\pi\nu(s-t)} \phi(\tau, \nu)\, R_x(s, \tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, ds\, d\tau\, d\nu
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for biomedical signals?**
> - **ECG:** The QRS complex has frequency components that shift rapidly during a heartbeat.
> - **EEG:** Rhythms (delta, theta, alpha, beta) appear at specific time intervals.
> - **PCG (heart sounds):** S1 and S2 murmurs occupy distinct time-frequency zones.
> - **EMG:** Muscle fatigue shifts the median frequency over time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Musical Score" Intuition

Imagine listening to a piano recital with your eyes closed:
- The **Fourier Transform** is like asking, *"Which notes were played in the entire concert?"* — You know *what* notes were used, but you cannot tell *when* each was struck.
- **Time-Frequency Analysis** is like reading the **musical score** — you see exactly *when* (time) each note (frequency) occurs, and *how loud* (energy) it is.

In the same way, an EEG signal contains brain waves that "appear" and "disappear" at different times. TFA allows the doctor to see *when* a seizure rhythm (e.g., 3 Hz spike-and-wave) occurs, not just whether it is present somewhere in the recording.

## 1.3 Standard Classification of TFA Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification:**

| Class | Methods | Cross-Terms | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | STFT, Gabor Transform, Wavelet Transform (CWT, DWT) | None | Fixed (STFT) / Adaptive (Wavelet) |
| **Bilinear / Quadratic** | Wigner-Ville, Cohen's Class, Rihaczek | Present | Best (but suffers interference) |
| **Empirical / Data-Adaptive** | Hilbert-Huang Transform (HHT), Empirical Wavelet Transform | None | Adaptive |

**Standard Metrics to Remember (in bold):**
- **Time Resolution:** $\Delta t$ — ability to localize events in time.
- **Frequency Resolution:** $\Delta f$ — ability to distinguish adjacent frequency components.
- **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle:** $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ — the fundamental trade-off.
- **Joint Time-Frequency Energy:** $E = \iint TFR(t,f)\, dt\, df$ — total energy must equal the signal energy by Parseval's theorem.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)

The STFT is the most intuitive and widely used TFA method. It works by sliding a short analysis window $w(t - \tau)$ across the signal and computing the Fourier Transform of the windowed segment at each position.

### Operational Steps:
1. **Windowing:** Multiply the signal $x(t)$ by a localized window $w(t - \tau)$ centered at time $\tau$.
2. **Local Spectrum:** Compute the Fourier Transform of the windowed segment.
3. **Sliding:** Increment $\tau$ and repeat, building up $STFT_x(\tau, f)$ as a 2-D function.

> [!NOTE]
> The width of the window controls the resolution trade-off. A narrow window gives good time resolution but poor frequency resolution, and vice-versa. This is a direct consequence of the **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle**.

### STFT Equation:
$$
STFT_x(\tau, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t - \tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

The **spectrogram** is the squared magnitude of the STFT:
$$
S_x(\tau, f) = \lvert STFT_x(\tau, f) \rvert^{2}
$$

### Properties of the Spectrogram:
- **Non-negative:** $S_x(\tau, f) \geq 0$ for all $t, f$ (no cross-terms).
- **Real and Non-negative:** Always a real, energy-preserving distribution.
- **Energy Conservation:** $\int\!\!\int S_x(\tau, f)\, d\tau\, df = \int \lvert x(t)\rvert^{2}\, dt$.

### Limitations of STFT:
- Fixed window size → single resolution for all frequencies.
- Cannot efficiently analyze signals with both slow trends and fast transients.
- Bandwidth limitation: a time-localized event is forced to be "spread" across the chosen window.

## 2.2 The Wigner-Ville Distribution (WVD)

The WVD is a quadratic (bilinear) time-frequency distribution that offers the best possible joint concentration for linear frequency-modulated (chirp) signals. It is the cornerstone of Cohen's class of distributions.

$$
W_x(t, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\!\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2}\right) x^{*}\!\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2}\right) e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau
$$

### Key Features:
- **Marginal Properties:** The WVD satisfies the time and frequency marginals — integrating over $f$ recovers $\lvert x(t)\rvert^2$, and integrating over $t$ recovers $\lvert X(f)\rvert^2$.
- **Instantaneous Frequency:** The first conditional moment of the WVD yields the instantaneous frequency of the signal.
- **Group Delay:** The first conditional moment with respect to frequency gives the group delay.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Cross-Term Problem:** Because WVD is bilinear, when the signal has multiple components, the WVD produces *cross-terms* (interference terms) located *midway* in both time and frequency between the true components. These cross-terms can mask real signal features and complicate interpretation — a major drawback in clinical applications.

### Cross-Term Reduction (Smoothed WVD):
A 2-D smoothing kernel $\Phi(t, f)$ is applied to suppress cross-terms:
$$
SWD_x(t, f) = \iint W_x(s, \nu)\, \Phi(s - t, \nu - f)\, ds\, d\nu
$$
This is the basis of **Cohen's Class** of distributions.

## 2.3 The Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)

The CWT addresses the resolution limitation of STFT by using *scalable* and *translatable* basis functions (wavelets) rather than fixed windows. The wavelet $\psi(t)$ is a small-wave (zero mean, finite energy) that is dilated by scale $s$ and translated by $u$.

$$
CWT_x(u, s) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t - u}{s}\right) dt
$$

- **Small scale $s$** → Compressed wavelet → High frequency → Good time resolution.
- **Large scale $s$** → Stretched wavelet → Low frequency → Good frequency resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Admissibility Condition:** For a wavelet to be invertible, it must satisfy:
> $$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\lvert \Psi(f)\rvert^{2}}{f}\, df < \infty$$
> where $\Psi(f)$ is the Fourier transform of the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$. This condition implies $\Psi(0) = 0$, i.e., the wavelet must have a zero mean (bandpass behaviour).

### Inverse CWT (Reconstruction):
$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} CWT_x(u, s)\, \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t - u}{s}\right) du\, \frac{ds}{s^{2}}
$$

## 2.4 The Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) & Multiresolution Analysis

The DWT provides an efficient, orthonormal, non-redundant decomposition of a signal into approximation and detail coefficients at multiple scales using a cascade of **low-pass** $g[n]$ and **high-pass** $h[n]$ filters followed by dyadic downsampling ($\downarrow 2$).

$$
a_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n]\, a_j[k], \qquad d_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n]\, a_j[k]
$$

### Filter Relationship (Quadrature Mirror Filters):
$$
g[L - 1 - n] = (-1)^{n}\, h[n]
$$
where $L$ is the filter length.

### Mallat's Algorithm Decomposition Tree:
- $cA_1$ → Approximation at level 1
- $cD_1$ → Detail at level 1
- $cA_2$ → Approximation at level 2 (from $cA_1$)
- $cD_2$ → Detail at level 2 (from $cA_1$)
- ... continues down to level $J$

### Common Wavelet Families Used in Biomedical Signal Processing:
- **Daubechies (db4, db6, db8)** — Most popular for ECG feature extraction.
- **Symlets (sym4–sym8)** — Near-symmetric, good for QRS detection.
- **Coiflets (coif1–coif5)** — Good for EEG analysis.
- **Biorthogonal (bior2.2, bior4.4)** — Used in compression.
- **Morlet** — Used in CWT for time-frequency maps.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations for KTU Board Exam:**

| # | Method | Core Equation | Domain | Resolution Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **STFT** | $STFT(\tau,f) = \int x(t)w^{*}(t-\tau)e^{-j2\pi ft}dt$ | $(\tau, f)$ | Fixed by window width |
| 2 | **Spectrogram** | $S(\tau,f) = \lvert STFT(\tau,f)\rvert^{2}$ | $(\tau, f)$ | Real, non-negative |
| 3 | **WVD** | $W(t,f) = \int x(t+\tau/2)x^{*}(t-\tau/2)e^{-j2\pi f\tau}d\tau$ | $(t, f)$ | Best joint concentration |
| 4 | **Cohen's Class** | $C(t,f) = \iint \phi(\tau,\nu)W(s,\nu)e^{-j2\pi(\tau f+\nu t-\nu s)}d\tau d\nu ds$ | $(t, f)$ | Kernel-controlled |
| 5 | **CWT** | $CWT(u,s) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\int x(t)\psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-u}{s}\right)dt$ | $(u, s)$ | Adaptive (multiscale) |
| 6 | **DWT** | $a_{j+1} = g^{*}a_{j}\downarrow 2,\ d_{j+1} = h^{*}a_{j}\downarrow 2$ | Discrete | Multiresolution |
| 7 | **Heisenberg-Gabor** | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ | Fundamental | Trade-off bound |
| 8 | **Admissibility** | $C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert\Psi(f)\vert^{2}}{f}df < \infty$ | Wavelet | Invertibility |
| 9 | **Energy Conservation** | $\iint \text{TFR}(t,f)\,dt\,df = \int \lvert x(t)\rvert^{2}dt$ | All TFA | Parseval-like |
| 10 | **Reconstruction (DWT)** | $a_{j}[n] = \sum_{k}(g[k-2n]a_{j+1}[k] + h[k-2n]d_{j+1}[k])$ | Discrete | Upsampling + filtering |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG R-peak detection:** DWT with db6 outperforms the Pan-Tompkins algorithm in noisy conditions.
- **Epileptic seizure detection:** Spectrograms of EEG fed to CNN classifiers achieve > 95% accuracy.
- **Heart murmur analysis (PCG):** WVD reveals the time-frequency signature of murmurs for stenosis/regurgitation.
- **EMG fatigue analysis:** Instantaneous median frequency tracked via STFT.
- **Sleep staging:** CWT of EEG, EOG, EMG signals.
- **Image fusion (medical imaging):** Wavelet-based fusion of CT/MRI scans.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the STFT from the Fourier Transform

**Starting Point:** The classical Fourier Transform of $x(t)$ is

$$
X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

This gives a *global* spectrum with no time information. We want to "localize" this in time.

**Step 1:** Introduce a sliding window $w(t-\tau)$ centered at time $\tau$. Multiply the signal:

$$
x_{\tau}(t) = x(t)\cdot w(t-\tau)
$$

For each $\tau$, this produces a windowed segment that "isolates" the signal near time $\tau$.

**Step 2:** Take the Fourier Transform of the windowed segment:

$$
X_{\tau}(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, w(t-\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

This is the spectrum *localized around* time $\tau$.

**Step 3:** Recognize that as $\tau$ varies, we obtain a 2-D function of both $\tau$ and $f$:

$$
STFT_x(\tau, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

The asterisk indicates that for complex windows (e.g., Hamming in frequency domain), we use the conjugate.

**Step 4:** Apply the **time-shift property** of the Fourier Transform. A shift in time $x(t - t_0) \leftrightarrow X(f) e^{-j2\pi f t_0}$. This shows the STFT is equivalent to:

$$
STFT_x(\tau, f) = e^{-j2\pi f \tau} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t + \tau)\, w^{*}(t)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

**Conclusion:** The STFT is mathematically equivalent to a *filter bank* interpretation where the bandpass filter at frequency $f$ is $H_f(t) = w(-t)\, e^{j2\pi f t}$, and the output is the convolution $STFT_x(\tau, f) = (x * H_f)(\tau)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Wigner-Ville Distribution

**Step 1:** Define the **local autocorrelation function** (also called the *instantaneous autocorrelation*):

$$
r_x(t, \tau) = x\!\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2}\right) x^{*}\!\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2}\right)
$$

This is the product of the signal with a time-reversed, conjugated, and shifted version of itself.

**Step 2:** Take the Fourier Transform of $r_x(t, \tau)$ with respect to the lag variable $\tau$:

$$
W_x(t, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} r_x(t, \tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\!\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2}\right) x^{*}\!\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2}\right) e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau
$$

**Step 3 (Marginal Verification):** Show the time marginal. Integrate the WVD over all frequencies:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_x(t, f)\, df = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\!\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2}\right) x^{*}\!\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2}\right) e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau\, df
$$

Swap the order of integration and apply $\int e^{-j2\pi f \tau} df = \delta(\tau)$:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W_x(t, f)\, df = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\!\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2}\right) x^{*}\!\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2}\right) \delta(\tau)\, d\tau = x(t) x^{*}(t) = \lvert x(t)\rvert^{2}
$$

**Step 4 (Cross-Term Emergence):** For $x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$, expand:

$$
W_x(t, f) = W_{x_1}(t, f) + W_{x_2}(t, f) + 2\,\text{Re}\!\left[W_{x_1, x_2}(t, f)\right]
$$

The third term is the **cross-Wigner distribution**:

$$
W_{x_1, x_2}(t, f) = \int x_1\!\left(t + \frac{\tau}{2}\right) x_2^{*}\!\left(t - \frac{\tau}{2}\right) e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau
$$

This oscillates rapidly in $(t, f)$ and creates the *interference pattern* between the two signal components.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Admissibility Condition for Wavelets

**Step 1:** Start with the inverse CWT:

$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} CWT_x(u, s)\, \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t - u}{s}\right) du\, \frac{ds}{s^{2}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the definition of $CWT_x(u, s)$ and use Parseval's theorem:

$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{ds}{s^{2}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f)\, \Psi^{*}(s f)\, e^{j2\pi f t}\, \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\, df \cdot \sqrt{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Carry out the integration in $s$ by changing variable $\xi = sf$, so $ds = d\xi/f$:

$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\lvert \Psi(\xi)\rvert^{2}}{\xi}\, d\xi \right] X(f)\, e^{j2\pi f t}\, df
$$

**Step 4:** For the inner integral to converge and be a finite, non-zero constant $C_\psi$, the integrand $\lvert \Psi(\xi)\rvert^2 / \xi$ must be integrable on $(0, \infty)$. This requires:

$$
C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\lvert \Psi(f)\rvert^{2}}{f}\, df < \infty
$$

**Step 5:** As $f \to 0$ (i.e., DC), the integrand behaves like $\lvert \Psi(0)\rvert^2 / f$. For the integral to converge, we must have $\Psi(0) = 0$, which is the **zero-mean / admissibility** condition for a wavelet.

## 3.4 Derivation of the DWT Multiresolution Relation

**Step 1:** Approximation space $V_j$ satisfies the nested property:

$$
\cdots \subset V_{j+1} \subset V_j \subset V_{j-1} \subset \cdots
$$

with $V_j \to L^2(\mathbb{R})$ as $j \to -\infty$ and $\bigcap V_j = \{0\}$.

**Step 2:** The detail space $W_{j+1}$ is the orthogonal complement of $V_{j+1}$ in $V_j$:

$$
V_j = V_{j+1} \oplus W_{j+1}
$$

**Step 3:** Using the scaling function $\phi(t)$ and wavelet function $\psi(t)$, the two-scale relations are:

$$
\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} g[n]\, \phi(2t - n)
$$

$$
\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)
$$

**Step 4:** Project $x(t)$ onto $V_{j+1}$ and $W_{j+1}$ and use orthonormality. The discrete approximation and detail coefficients are:

$$
a_{j+1}[n] = \langle x, \phi_{j+1, n}\rangle = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n]\, a_j[k]
$$

$$
d_{j+1}[n] = \langle x, \psi_{j+1, n}\rangle = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n]\, a_j[k]
$$

**Step 5 (QMF Relationship):** The filters $g$ and $h$ are related by:

$$
g[L - 1 - n] = (-1)^{n}\, h[n]
$$

This ensures the low-pass and high-pass filters are mirror-image quadrature filters.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix for KTU Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this table for any "Compare and contrast" question:**

| Property | STFT | WVD | CWT | DWT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Linear | Quadratic | Linear | Linear |
| **Basis** | Fixed window | Autocorrelation | Wavelet (scalable) | Wavelet (orthonormal) |
| **Resolution** | Fixed | Best (with cross-terms) | Adaptive (multiscale) | Adaptive (dyadic) |
| **Time-Freq Plane** | $(\tau, f)$ | $(t, f)$ | $(u, s)$ scale ↔ freq | Discrete $(j, n)$ |
| **Reconstruction** | Yes (with overlap-add) | Marginal only | Yes (admissibility) | Yes (perfect) |
| **Cross-terms** | None (magnitude squared) | Yes (problematic) | None | None |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N^2)$ (redundant) | $O(N)$ (fast Mallat) |
| **Best For** | Stationary segments | Chirps, FM signals | Transients, singularities | Compression, denoising |
| **Biomedical Use** | EEG segments, EMG | PCG, Doppler | QRS, spike detection | ECG feature extraction |
